Writing "BLE uses FHSS" without specifying *adaptive* frequency hopping loses 1 mark. > 2. **Wrong channel count:** Some students write "BLE has 79 channels" — this is **classic Bluetooth**. BLE has **40 channels** (3 advertising + 37 data). Examiners will deduct marks. > 3. **Forgetting GFSK modulation index:** Many students write "GFSK" but omit that the modulation index is **0.45–0.55**, which is sometimes asked. > 4. **Mixing up GATT client/server:** The **server holds the data** (smart meter), the **client reads it** (phone). Reversing this in a 7-mark question loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Path Loss formula unit mismatch:** FSPL formula uses $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**. Forgetting this and using meters/Hz will give a wildly wrong answer. > 6. **Ignoring practical derating in battery life:** Examiners expect a sentence acknowledging that theoretical battery life is degraded by self-discharge, temperature, and internal resistance growth. > 7. **Missing "Bluetooth Mesh"** as a feature of BLE 5.0+ — frequently asked as a 2-mark question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist ✅ - **BLE = Bluetooth Low Energy**, standardized under **IEEE 802.15.1 / BT Core Spec 4.0+**, in the **2.4 GHz ISM** band. - **40 RF channels**: **3 advertising (37, 38, 39) + 37 data (0–36)**. - **Modulation:** **GFSK** with modulation index **h = 0.45–0.55**. - **PHY Modes:** **LE 1M (1 Mbps), LE 2M (2 Mbps), LE Coded (125/500 kbps, S=2/8)** for long range. - **Three Roles:** **Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central** (GAP-defined). - **GATT = Data Model:** **Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value**. - **Standardized Services include:** Environmental Sensing 0x181A, Device Information 0x180A, Battery 0x180F. - **State Machine:** Standby → Advertising / Scanning → Initiating → Connection. - **Frequency Hopping:** Adaptive, governed by $f_{n+1} = (f_n + h) \mod 37$, with **CSA #2** in BT 5.0+. - **Smart Grid Role:** **HAN layer** — smartphone-native, ultra-low-power connectivity for IHDs, smart plugs, smart meters, field service tools. - **Key Advantage over Wi-Fi/Zigbee:** Lowest power consumption + smartphone-native. - **Key Limitation:** Limited mesh scalability in pre-5.0 versions; addressed by **Bluetooth Mesh** (flooding, managed flooding) in **BT 5.0+**. - **Bluetooth 5.4 (2023):** Added **PAwR (Periodic Advertising with Responses)** — enables star topology with thousands of energy-autonomous endpoints (ideal for **electronic shelf labels** and large-scale AMI). - **Battery Life Formula:** $T = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$, where $I_{avg} = I_{tx} \cdot D + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-D)$. - **Free-Space Path Loss Formula:** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$. - **Range ≈ 10–30 m indoor (LE 1M) / up to 1.5 km outdoor (LE Coded S=8)**. - **Receiver Sensitivity:** **−97 dBm (LE 1M)**, **−103 dBm (LE Coded S=2)**, **−104 dBm or lower (LE Coded S=8)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "low-power," "battery-operated," or "HAN," immediately think **BLE**. Whenever it mentions "broadband backhaul," "video surveillance," or "substation LAN," think **Wi-Fi/Fiber**. Whenever it mentions "large-scale mesh with thousands of nodes," think **Zigbee or BLE Mesh (5.0+)**. This triage is half the battle in KTU Smart Grid exams.

topics.title: Li-Fi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Li-Fi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Li-Fi (Light Fidelity)** is a bidirectional, high-speed, fully networked wireless communication technology based on **Visible Light Communication (VLC)** that uses the visible spectrum (380 nm – 750 nm), infrared, and near-ultraviolet light emitted by LED luminaires to transmit data at extremely high speeds.

It was first conceptualized and demonstrated by Professor **Harald Haas** (University of Edinburgh) in a TED Global Talk in **2011**, where he coined the term "Li-Fi" and showed that an ordinary LED bulb could be modulated at very high speeds to transmit data.

In the context of **Smart Grid Technologies**, Li-Fi is positioned as a complementary (and in certain substation scenarios, a superior) alternative to Radio Frequency (RF) based wireless systems such as Wi-Fi, ZigBee, and LTE, primarily because of its **immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI)** which is rampant inside High-Voltage substations and power generation switchyards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Li-Fi is a Visible Light Communication (VLC) system that uses intensity modulation of LED light sources and direct detection using photodiodes to achieve wireless data transmission, where the data rate is proportional to the switching speed (modulation bandwidth) of the LED."*

**Standard Governing Body:** The IEEE has codified VLC under **IEEE 802.15.7 — Short-Range Optical Wireless Communications** standard, which is the primary reference for Li-Fi PHY and MAC layers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Talking Light Bulb"

Imagine you are sitting in a room lit by a normal LED bulb. Now imagine that the LED is flickering — but so fast (millions of times per second) that your human eye perceives it as a *steady glow*. To your eye, the light is "ON." But to a special receiver (a photodiode or solar panel), each flicker represents a binary **'1'** or **'0'**.

> 🎯 **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a **Morse code lighthouse**. The lighthouse keeper blinks the lamp in long and short pulses (dah-dit-dah) to send messages to ships at sea. Ships with telescopes can decode the message, but people standing on the beach cannot tell whether the light is blinking or steady, because the flashes are too fast. **Li-Fi works exactly the same way** — the LED bulb is the "lighthouse," and the photodiode-equipped device is the "ship."

In a **smart home** equipped with Li-Fi, every LED ceiling light becomes a wireless access point, capable of streaming high-definition video, transmitting smart meter data, or relaying control commands to smart appliances — all while the room is being illuminated!

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle:** Li-Fi modulates the **intensity** of light (not the color or frequency in the RF sense). Since LEDs are semiconductors, their light output is **linearly proportional to the forward current** in the linear region, which makes them perfect for intensity modulation. The photodiode at the receiver end converts this varying light intensity back into a proportional electrical current, which is then demodulated to recover the original data stream.

---

## 1.3 Why Li-Fi Matters in Smart Grids — The "EMI Problem"

Traditional power system environments like **EHV substations (220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV)**, **HVDC converter stations**, and **switchgear rooms** are saturated with intense electromagnetic fields produced by:
- Bus bar corona discharges
- SF₆ gas circuit breakers (during operation)
- Transformer leakage flux
- Partial discharge events
- HVDC thyristor valve switching (which generates harmonics up to several kHz)

RF-based wireless systems like **Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz / 5 GHz)** suffer severe **co-channel interference** and signal degradation in these environments. Li-Fi, being an **optical** technology, is **completely immune to EMI** because the visible light spectrum is 10,000× – 1,000,000× higher in frequency than RF, and light does not interact with electromagnetic fields in the same way radio waves do.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Use Case:** The U.S. Department of Energy (DoE) and several European TSOs have piloted Li-Fi inside 400 kV substations to enable wireless communication between IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices), protection relays, and SCADA gateways without EMI-induced false tripping or data corruption.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Values)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Spectrum used** | 400 THz – 800 THz (visible), 1 THz – 400 THz (full optical) |
| **Wavelength range** | **380 nm – 750 nm** |
| **Theoretical peak data rate** | **> 10 Gbps** (lab demonstrated) |
| **Commercial data rate** | 1 Mbps – 224 Mbps (IEEE 802.15.7) |
| **Range (Line of Sight)** | up to 10 m indoor |
| **Security** | Confined to room (light does not pass through walls) |
| **Photobiological safety** | IEC 62471 compliant (no UV, no laser) |
| **Latency** | < 5 ms (comparable to Wi-Fi) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Li-Fi Communication Chain — Five Functional Blocks

The Li-Fi system can be functionally decomposed into the following sequential stages:

### **Block 1: Data Source & Modulator (Transmitter Side)**
- The input digital data (e.g., smart meter reading, video stream, protection command) is fed into a **driver circuit**.
- The data is encoded using one of the modulation schemes: **OOK (On-Off Keying)**, **VPPM (Variable Pulse Position Modulation)**, **CSK (Color Shift Keying)**, or **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)**.
- The encoded signal is superimposed as a high-frequency current variation on the LED's DC bias current.

### **Block 2: LED as the Optical Transmitter**
- The LED's optical output intensity is **linearly proportional** to the forward current: $P_{optical} = \eta_{LED} \cdot I_{forward}$
- A typical phosphor-coated white LED has a **modulation bandwidth of 2–20 MHz**.
- A micro-LED (µLED) or laser diode (LD) can support **>1 GHz bandwidth**, enabling Gbps-class transmission.

### **Block 3: The Optical Wireless Channel**
- This is the air/vacuum path between LED and photodetector.
- It can be modeled as a **Lambertian source** with both a **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** component and **diffuse reflections** from walls and ceilings.
- Channel is characterized by the **DC channel gain $H(0)$**.

### **Block 4: Photodetector (Receiver Side)**
- A **PIN photodiode**, **avalanche photodiode (APD)**, or even a **solar panel** is used to convert the incident light intensity into a proportional electrical current.
- The conversion efficiency is captured by the **responsivity $R$** (in A/W).

### **Block 5: Demodulator & Data Sink**
- The photogenerated current is amplified by a **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)**.
- The signal is then equalized, demodulated, and decoded back into the original data stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind each step:**
> - Why modulate intensity? Because photodiodes respond to **optical power**, not optical phase. So we cannot use phase/frequency modulation directly; we must encode information in the **instantaneous intensity**.
> - Why LEDs? Because LEDs are **fast-switching semiconductor devices** (nanosecond rise times) that can be modulated at MHz–GHz rates while still functioning as illumination sources.
> - Why a photodiode? It is the **only practical device** that can convert the rapidly oscillating light intensity back into a proportional electrical signal at sufficient bandwidth.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — All Equations You Must Memorize

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{optical} = \eta_{LED} \cdot I_F$ | $P_{optical}$ = emitted light power (W), $\eta_{LED}$ = LED efficacy (W/A), $I_F$ = forward current (A) | LED linearity check |
| 2 | $H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{(m+1) \cdot A_{PD}}{2\pi d^{2}} \cdot \cos^{m}(\phi) \cdot T_s(\psi) \cdot g(\psi) \cdot \cos(\psi)$ | $H_{LOS}(0)$ = DC channel gain, $A_{PD}$ = photodetector area, $d$ = distance, $\phi$ = angle of irradiance, $\psi$ = angle of incidence, $T_s$ = optical filter transmission, $g$ = optical concentrator gain, $m$ = Lambertian order | Optical link budget |
| 3 | $m = -\dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(\cos \Phi_{1/2})}$ | $\Phi_{1/2}$ = half-power semi-angle of LED | Beam shape characterization |
| 4 | $P_r = H(0) \cdot P_t$ | $P_r$ = received optical power, $P_t$ = transmitted optical power | Received power calculation |
| 5 | $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_r$ | $I_{ph}$ = photogenerated current, $R$ = responsivity (A/W) | Receiver current |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{q \cdot \eta_{q}}{h \cdot f} = \dfrac{\eta_{q} \cdot \lambda}{1240 \text{ W·nm/A}}$ | $q$ = electron charge, $\eta_q$ = quantum efficiency, $h$ = Planck's constant, $f$ = optical frequency, $\lambda$ = wavelength (nm) | Photodiode design |
| 7 | $SNR = \dfrac{(R \cdot P_r)^{2}}{N_0 \cdot B}$ | $N_0$ = noise PSD, $B$ = modulation bandwidth | Link performance |
| 8 | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}(1 + SNR)$ | $C$ = channel capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) | Maximum data rate |
| 9 | $P_{e,OOK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{R \cdot P_r}{2 \cdot N_0 \cdot B}}\right)$ | $P_e$ = bit error probability, $Q$ = Q-function | BER analysis |
| 10 | $R_{b,max} = 2 \cdot B \cdot \log_2 M$ (for M-PAM) | $R_b$ = bit rate, $B$ = bandwidth, $M$ = PAM order | Spectral efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU numericals, you will typically be given values of $P_t$, $d$, $\Phi_{1/2}$, $\psi$, $\phi$, $A_{PD}$, and asked to compute $P_r$, $I_{ph}$, or $SNR$. Memorize equations 2, 4, 5, and 7 in particular.

---

## 2.3 Modulation Schemes in Li-Fi — KTU-Favorite Comparison

| Scheme | Full Name | Spectral Efficiency | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OOK** | On-Off Keying | 1 b/s/Hz | Low | Low-data smart sensors |
| **VPPM** | Variable Pulse Position Modulation | 1 b/s/Hz | Low | Dimming control + data |
| **CSK** | Color Shift Keying | 1–4 b/s/Hz | Medium | RGB-LED high data rate |
| **OFDM (DCO-OFDM / ACO-OFDM)** | Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing | 4–8 b/s/Hz | High | Gbps Li-Fi, 5G NR-Light |
| **DCO-OFDM** | DC-biased Optical OFDM | High | High | DC-coupled photodiodes |
| **ACO-OFDM** | Asymmetrically Clipped Optical OFDM | High | High | AC-coupled photodiodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **OFDM is preferred in modern Li-Fi** because it converts a frequency-selective optical channel into many flat-fading subcarriers, enabling simple one-tap equalization and high spectral efficiency. The "DC bias" in DCO-OFDM ensures the signal remains non-negative, which is required for intensity modulation.

---

## 2.4 Li-Fi vs Wi-Fi — The KTU Comparison Table (Frequently Asked)

| Parameter | Li-Fi | Wi-Fi |
|---|---|---|
| Spectrum | Visible light (400–800 THz) | RF (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz) |
| Bandwidth | **10,000× wider** | Limited, regulated |
| Speed (lab) | > 100 Gbps | ~10 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6/6E/7) |
| Range | ~10 m (LOS) | ~50–100 m |
| Security | Excellent (light does not pass through walls) | Lower (RF penetrates walls) |
| EMI immunity | **Total immunity** | Susceptible |
| Mobility handover | Hard (line-of-sight required) | Easy |
| Health safety | Photobiological safe (no RF exposure) | Debated (RF radiation concerns) |
| Cost | Low (uses existing LEDs) | Moderate |
| Smart grid use | Substation, indoor AMI, smart home | Last-mile AMI, outdoor backhaul |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lambertian DC Channel Gain $H_{LOS}(0)$

This derivation is the **most important KTU-style analytical problem** on Li-Fi. The setup is: a Lambertian LED source at origin, emitting into a hemisphere, illuminating a photodetector at distance $d$ oriented such that the normal to the photodetector makes an angle $\psi$ with the incident light direction.

### **Step 1: Define the Lambertian Radiation Pattern**

A Lambertian source radiates with intensity $I(\phi)$ (in W/sr) that follows a cosine law with respect to the angle of irradiance $\phi$ from the source normal:

$$I(\phi) = I_0 \cdot \cos^{m}(\phi)$$

where $I_0$ is the center-axis intensity and $m$ is the Lambertian order.

### **Step 2: Relate Total Transmitted Power to $I_0$**

The total emitted optical power $P_t$ equals the integral of intensity over the entire hemisphere (solid angle $2\pi$):

$$P_t = \int_{0}^{2\pi} I(\phi) \, d\Omega = \int_{0}^{2\pi} I_0 \cos^{m}(\phi) \sin(\phi) \, d\phi \, d\theta$$

Carrying out the integration over the azimuthal angle $\theta$ (from 0 to $2\pi$) and polar angle $\phi$ (from 0 to $\pi/2$):

$$P_t = 2\pi I_0 \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos^{m}(\phi) \sin(\phi) \, d\phi$$

Let $u = \cos(\phi)$, so $du = -\sin(\phi) d\phi$:

$$P_t = 2\pi I_0 \int_{0}^{1} u^{m} \, du = 2\pi I_0 \left[\dfrac{u^{m+1}}{m+1}\right]_{0}^{1} = \dfrac{2\pi I_0}{m+1}$$

Therefore:

$$I_0 = \dfrac{(m+1) P_t}{2\pi}$$

### **Step 3: Irradiance at the Receiver Plane**

The irradiance (incident optical power per unit area, W/m²) at distance $d$ on a plane perpendicular to the line joining source to detector is:

$$E_{incident} = \dfrac{I(\phi)}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{I_0 \cos^{m}(\phi)}{d^{2}}$$

### **Step 4: Account for the Detector's Effective Area**

The photodetector has physical area $A_{PD}$ and is tilted by angle $\psi$ relative to the incoming light. The **projected effective area** that actually captures light is $A_{PD} \cos(\psi)$. Substituting $I_0$:

$$P_{r,raw} = E_{incident} \cdot A_{PD} \cos(\psi) = \dfrac{(m+1) P_t}{2\pi d^{2}} \cos^{m}(\phi) \cos(\psi) A_{PD}$$

### **Step 5: Include Optical Filter and Concentrator**

The receiver typically has:
- An **optical bandpass filter** with transmission $T_s(\psi)$ (to reject ambient light, especially sunlight).
- A **non-imaging concentrator** (e.g., compound parabolic concentrator) with gain $g(\psi)$.

These modify the received power:

$$P_r = P_{r,raw} \cdot T_s(\psi) \cdot g(\psi)$$

### **Step 6: Define the DC Channel Gain**

By definition, the DC channel gain is the ratio of received to transmitted optical power:

$$H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{P_r}{P_t}$$

Equating with Step 5:

$$\boxed{H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{(m+1) A_{PD}}{2\pi d^{2}} \cos^{m}(\phi) \cdot T_s(\psi) \cdot g(\psi) \cdot \cos(\psi)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **signature equation** of every VLC/Li-Fi system. The inverse-square dependence on distance $d$ is what fundamentally limits Li-Fi to short-range (< 10 m) operation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Photodiode Responsivity Equation

Starting from the fundamental photoelectric conversion:

### **Step 1: Photon Energy**

A photon of wavelength $\lambda$ has energy:

$$E_{photon} = h f = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

### **Step 2: Number of Photons per Second in Incident Power $P_r$**

The number of photons per second incident on the photodetector is the power divided by photon energy:

$$N_{ph} = \dfrac{P_r}{E_{photon}} = \dfrac{P_r \lambda}{h c}$$

### **Step 3: Number of Electrons Generated per Second**

Only a fraction $\eta_q$ (the **quantum efficiency**, 0 < $\eta_q$ < 1) of the photons generate electron-hole pairs:

$$N_e = \eta_q \cdot N_{ph} = \eta_q \cdot \dfrac{P_r \lambda}{h c}$$

### **Step 4: Photocurrent**

Each electron carries charge $q$ (where $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C). The resulting current is:

$$I_{ph} = q N_e = \dfrac{q \eta_q P_r \lambda}{h c}$$

### **Step 5: Define Responsivity $R$**

$$R = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_r} = \dfrac{q \eta_q \lambda}{h c}$$

Substituting the universal constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, and $\lambda$ in nanometers (1 nm = $10^{-9}$ m):

$$R = \dfrac{\eta_q \lambda}{1240} \quad \text{[A/W]}$$

$$\boxed{R \,[\text{A/W}] = \dfrac{\eta_q \cdot \lambda_{[\text{nm}]}}{1240}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Shortcut for KTU:** If $\eta_q = 0.8$ and $\lambda = 1550$ nm (IR), then $R = 0.8 \times 1550 / 1240 = 1.0$ A/W. If $\lambda = 850$ nm (near-IR), then $R \approx 0.55$ A/W. This formula is a favorite 3-mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A Li-Fi link operates between an LED ceiling lamp and a desk-mounted photodetector. Given:
- Transmitted optical power $P_t = 10$ W
- LED half-power semi-angle $\Phi_{1/2} = 60°$
- Distance $d = 2$ m
- Angle of irradiance $\phi = 30°$
- Angle of incidence $\psi = 30°$
- Photodetector area $A_{PD} = 1 \text{ cm}^{2} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- Optical filter transmission $T_s = 1$
- Concentrator gain $g = 1$
- Operating wavelength $\lambda = 600$ nm
- Quantum efficiency $\eta_q = 0.7$
- Modulation bandwidth $B = 10$ MHz
- Noise PSD $N_0 = 10^{-22}$ A²/Hz

**Find:** (a) The DC channel gain $H_{LOS}(0)$.
(b) The received optical power $P_r$.
(c) The photocurrent $I_{ph}$.
(d) The SNR at the receiver.
(e) The maximum achievable data rate (Shannon limit).

### **Step (a): Compute the Lambertian order $m$**

$$m = -\dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(\cos 60°)} = -\dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(0.5)} = -\dfrac{0.6931}{-0.6931} = 1$$

### **Step (b): Compute the DC channel gain**

$$H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{(1+1)(1 \times 10^{-4})}{2\pi (2)^{2}} \cos^{1}(30°) \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \cos(30°)$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{25.133} \cdot (0.8660) \cdot (0.8660)$$

$$= (7.96 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 0.75 = 5.97 \times 10^{-6}$$

### **Step (c): Received optical power**

$$P_r = H_{LOS}(0) \cdot P_t = 5.97 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 = 5.97 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 59.7 \, \mu\text{W}$$

### **Step (d): Responsivity and photocurrent**

$$R = \dfrac{0.7 \times 600}{1240} = \dfrac{420}{1240} = 0.3387 \text{ A/W}$$

$$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_r = 0.3387 \times 5.97 \times 10^{-5} = 2.02 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 20.2 \, \mu\text{A}$$

### **Step (e): SNR**

$$SNR = \dfrac{(I_{ph})^{2}}{N_0 \cdot B} = \dfrac{(2.02 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}{10^{-22} \times 10^{7}}$$

$$= \dfrac{4.08 \times 10^{-10}}{10^{-15}} = 4.08 \times 10^{5} \quad (\text{or} \, 56.1 \text{ dB})$$

### **Step (f): Shannon capacity**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + SNR) = 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(1 + 4.08 \times 10^{5})$$

$$= 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(4.08 \times 10^{5}) = 10^{7} \times 18.64 = 1.864 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} \approx 186.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Interpretation:** A simple LED with just 10 W of optical power can deliver ~186 Mbps of error-free data at 2 m — this is the engineering marvel of Li-Fi. With better concentrators, micro-LEDs, and OFDM, this can be pushed beyond **1 Gbps**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Li-Fi Transmitter-Receiver Block Diagram

The diagram below shows the complete functional architecture of a Li-Fi communication system, including the transmitter section, optical channel, and receiver section. It also highlights the parallel illumination and data functions of the LED.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Data Source] --> A2[Source Encoder]
    A2 --> A3[Channel Encoder FEC]
    A3 --> A4[Modulator OOK OFDM VPPM CSK]
    A4 --> A5[LED Driver Circuit]
    A5 --> A6[High Power White LED or RGB LED]
    A6 -->|Visible Light Beam 380 to 750 nm| A7[Free Space Optical Channel LOS plus Diffuse]

    A7 --> B1[Photodetector PIN APD or Solar Cell]
    B1 --> B2[Optical Bandpass Filter]
    B2 --> B3[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    B3 --> B4[Equalizer]
    B4 --> B5[Demodulator]
    B5 --> B6[Channel Decoder FEC]
    B6 --> B7[Source Decoder]
    B7 --> B8[Output Data Sink]

    A6 -.Provides Illumination.-> A9[Room Space]
    A9 -.Also Illuminates.-> B1
```

## 4.2 Li-Fi Network Architecture in a Smart Grid Building

The second diagram illustrates how multiple Li-Fi access points (LED luminaires) form a cellular network inside a smart building, communicating with smart meters, IoT sensors, and the central energy management system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Smart_Building_Floor
        L1[LED Luminaire Room 1 Li-Fi AP 1]
        L2[LED Luminaire Room 2 Li-Fi AP 2]
        L3[LED Luminaire Room 3 Li-Fi AP 3]
        L4[LED Luminaire Room 4 Li-Fi AP 4]

        S1[Smart Meter with Photodiode]
        S2[HVAC Sensor with VLC Receiver]
        S3[Smart Appliance Refrigerator]
        S4[Smart Phone with Li-Fi Dongle]
        S5[Security Camera with VLC]

        L1 -.LOS VLC.-> S1
        L1 -.LOS VLC.-> S4
        L2 -.LOS VLC.-> S2
        L2 -.LOS VLC.-> S3
        L3 -.LOS VLC.-> S5
        L4 -.LOS VLC.-> S1
    end

    S1 --> GW[Li-Fi to Ethernet Gateway]
    S2 --> GW
    S3 --> GW
    S4 --> GW
    S5 --> GW
    GW --> BEMS[Building Energy Management System]
    BEMS --> CLOUD[Cloud SCADA and Analytics]
    CLOUD --> UTIL[Utility DSM Server]
```

## 4.3 Smart Grid Substation Deployment Architecture

This diagram shows how Li-Fi replaces conventional wired or RF communication links inside an EHV substation, where EMI is otherwise prohibitive.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EHV_Substation_400kV
        TR1[Power Transformer 400 by 220 kV]
        CB1[SF6 Circuit Breaker]
        CT1[Current Transformer]
        VT1[Voltage Transformer]
        IED1[Protection Relay IED 1]
        IED2[Protection Relay IED 2]
        IED3[Bay Controller Unit BCU]
        LED1[Substation LED Luminaire A with Li-Fi AP]
        LED2[Substation LED Luminaire B with Li-Fi AP]
        LED3[Substation LED Luminaire C with Li-Fi AP]

        CT1 --> IED1
        VT1 --> IED1
        CB1 --> IED1
        IED1 -.VLC Downlink.-> LED1
        IED1 -.VLC Uplink via IR.-> LED1
        LED1 -.LOS.-> IED2
        LED1 -.LOS.-> IED3
        LED2 -.LOS.-> IED2
        LED3 -.LOS.-> IED3
    end

    IED1 --> GWSUB[Substation Gateway Fiber Optic]
    IED2 --> GWSUB
    IED3 --> GWSUB
    GWSUB --> SAS[Substation Automation System SCADA]
    SAS --> CONTROL_CENTRE[Load Dispatch Centre LDC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The redundancy of multiple Li-Fi access points (LED1, LED2, LED3) provides spatial diversity. Even if one LED fails, communication to a given IED is maintained via another luminaire — a critical reliability feature for protection-grade communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Li-Fi. List any two advantages of Li-Fi over Wi-Fi in the context of smart grid communication.**

**Model Answer:**

**Li-Fi Definition:** Li-Fi (Light Fidelity) is a wireless optical communication technology that uses **visible light communication (VLC)** through intensity modulation of LED light sources and direct detection at the receiver using a photodiode to transmit data.

**Two Advantages over Wi-Fi in Smart Grids:**

1. **Complete immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI):** In high-voltage substations and switchgear rooms, RF-based Wi-Fi signals suffer from heavy interference due to corona, partial discharge, and switching transients. Li-Fi uses light waves, which are unaffected by these electromagnetic disturbances, making it ideal for IED-to-IED communication inside substations.

2. **Enhanced security:** Light waves cannot penetrate walls, so Li-Fi signals are physically confined to the room where the LED is located. This is critical for smart grid command-and-control data (e.g., protection trip signals) which must not leak outside the substation perimeter.

3. *(Optional third point)* Spectrum congestion: Wi-Fi shares limited RF bands (2.4/5/6 GHz) with Bluetooth, ZigBee, microwave ovens, etc. Li-Fi uses the 400–800 THz visible spectrum which is **10,000× wider** and currently unregulated.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of the IEEE 802.15.7 standard in Li-Fi technology? Mention the modulation schemes defined by it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of IEEE 802.15.15.7:** IEEE 802.15.7 is the international standard that defines the **Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers** for Short-Range Optical Wireless Communications using visible light. It provides a unified framework for VLC device interoperability, dimming support, and flicker-free operation, and it operates in the 380 nm to 750 nm wavelength range.

**Modulation Schemes defined by IEEE 802.15.7:**

1. **OOK (On-Off Keying)** — simplest binary modulation.
2. **VPPM (Variable Pulse Position Modulation)** — combines data and dimming.
3. **CSK (Color Shift Keying)** — uses RGB LEDs, maps symbols to color coordinates on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram.

The standard supports data rates from **11.67 kbps up to 96 Mbps** in its initial release, and enhancements have pushed it toward **224 Mbps** in the high-rate PHY mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 questions carry 14 marks, and students must answer ONE full question (out of typically 2–3 choices). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two alternative complete questions are provided below.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the working principle of a Li-Fi system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the LED and the photodetector in the system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An indoor Li-Fi link uses a white LED lamp with the following parameters:
- Transmitted optical power $P_t = 5$ W
- Lambertian order $m = 1$
- Distance between LED and photodetector $d = 2.5$ m
- Photodetector area $A_{PD} = 0.5 \text{ cm}^{2}$
- Angle of irradiance $\phi = 40°$
- Angle of incidence $\psi = 40°$
- Optical filter transmission $T_s = 0.9$
- Concentrator gain $g = 1$

Calculate: (i) DC channel gain $H_{LOS}(0)$, (ii) Received optical power $P_r$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Working Principle:** Li-Fi uses **intensity modulation** of an LED's light output to encode digital data. A constant DC bias current keeps the LED illuminated for normal lighting, while a high-frequency modulated current (carrying data) is superimposed on top. The LED's instantaneous optical output power varies in proportion to the modulated current. At the receiver, a photodiode converts these rapid light-intensity variations into a proportional electrical current, which is then amplified, equalized, and demodulated to recover the original data.

**Block Diagram:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Data source** | Digital information (e.g., smart meter data, video) |
| **Modulator** | Encodes data onto a carrier (OOK, OFDM, etc.) |
| **LED driver** | Converts the modulated signal into LED forward-current variation |
| **LED** | Emits intensity-modulated visible light |
| **Optical channel** | Free-space LOS + diffuse path |
| **Photodetector** | Converts optical power variation into electrical current |
| **TIA** | Transimpedance amplifier converts current to voltage |
| **Demodulator** | Recovers the data bits |

**Role of LED:**
- Functions as a **dual-purpose device** — provides illumination and acts as the optical transmitter simultaneously.
- LED's optical output is **linearly proportional to forward current** in the linear operating region, enabling faithful reproduction of the modulated signal.
- Switching speed (modulation bandwidth) of the LED **determines the maximum achievable data rate**. Standard phosphor white LEDs have ~2–20 MHz bandwidth; micro-LEDs and laser diodes support >1 GHz.

**Role of Photodetector:**
- Converts incident optical power $P_r$ into a proportional photocurrent $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_r$, where $R$ is the responsivity (A/W).
- A **PIN photodiode** offers good bandwidth and moderate sensitivity; an **avalanche photodiode (APD)** provides internal gain (10–100×) for high-speed, long-distance links.
- In some low-cost applications, a **solar panel** can also serve as a VLC receiver (relevant for outdoor Li-Fi-powered IoT sensors).

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** $P_t = 5$ W, $m = 1$, $d = 2.5$ m, $A_{PD} = 0.5 \text{ cm}^{2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $\phi = 40°$, $\psi = 40°$, $T_s = 0.9$, $g = 1$.

**Step 1 — DC channel gain formula:**

$$H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{(m+1) A_{PD}}{2\pi d^{2}} \cos^{m}(\phi) \cdot T_s(\psi) \cdot g(\psi) \cdot \cos(\psi)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{(1+1)(0.5 \times 10^{-4})}{2\pi (2.5)^{2}} \cos^{1}(40°) \cdot 0.9 \cdot 1 \cdot \cos(40°)$$

Compute each term:
- $(m+1) A_{PD} = 2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $2\pi d^{2} = 2 \times \pi \times 6.25 = 39.27$
- $\cos(40°) = 0.766$
- $\cos(\phi) \cdot \cos(\psi) = 0.766 \times 0.766 = 0.587$
- $T_s = 0.9$
- $g = 1$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{39.27} \times 0.587 \times 0.9 \times 1$$

$$= (2.546 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.5283 = 1.345 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Received optical power:**

$$P_r = H_{LOS}(0) \cdot P_t = 1.345 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 = 6.73 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 6.73 \, \mu\text{W}$$

**Final Answer:** $H_{LOS}(0) = 1.345 \times 10^{-6}$ (dimensionless), and $P_r = 6.73 \,\mu\text{W}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Compare Li-Fi and Wi-Fi technologies on the basis of spectrum, speed, security, EMI immunity, and suitability for smart grid applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how Li-Fi can be deployed in a 400 kV substation for protection and SCADA communication. Highlight why Li-Fi is preferred over Wi-Fi/ZigBee in this environment. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a): Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Li-Fi | Wi-Fi |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectrum** | Visible light (400–800 THz); 10,000× wider than RF | RF bands — 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz (regulated, crowded) |
| **Theoretical peak speed** | > 10 Gbps in lab; up to 224 Mbps commercial (IEEE 802.15.7) | Up to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6/6E); theoretical 46 Gbps (Wi-Fi 7) |
| **Security** | **Excellent** — light is confined to the room; cannot penetrate walls | **Lower** — RF signals leak outside the room, sniffable from outside |
| **EMI Immunity** | **Total immunity** — light unaffected by electromagnetic fields | **Highly susceptible** to corona, switching transients, partial discharge |
| **Range** | ~10 m LOS typical; limited by inverse-square law | ~50–100 m typical |
| **Mobility / Handover** | Difficult (requires LOS) | Seamless roaming supported |
| **Health & Safety** | Photobiological safety (IEC 62471); no RF exposure | Ongoing public concern over RF radiation |
| **Suitability for Smart Grid** | **Excellent** for: indoor AMI, substation IEDs, smart home, healthcare, EMI-sensitive zones | **Good** for: outdoor backhaul, customer-premises Wi-Fi, large-area AMI where mobility is needed |

**Synthesis:** Li-Fi and Wi-Fi are **complementary, not competitive**. In a smart grid, Wi-Fi is preferred for outdoor last-mile AMI and consumer broadband, whereas Li-Fi dominates in **indoor, EMI-hostile, and high-security zones** like EHV substations, hospitals, aircraft cabins, and defense installations.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b): Li-Fi in a 400 kV Substation**

**Deployment Architecture:**

A 400 kV substation has multiple switchgear bays, each containing:
- **Current Transformers (CTs)** and **Voltage Transformers (VTs)** for metering and protection
- **Circuit Breakers** (typically SF₆)
- **Disconnectors and earthing switches**
- **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)** — protection relays, bay controllers
- **SCADA Remote Terminal Units (RTUs)**

In a Li-Fi-based substation automation system:
1. **LED luminaires** (industrial-grade, high-lumen, vibration-resistant) are mounted on the substation structural steel at strategic locations. Each LED acts as a Li-Fi access point.
2. **Photodiodes** are integrated into the IEDs, BCUs, and SCADA gateways, pointing upward toward the LED ceiling.
3. **Bidirectional VLC links** carry protection trip commands, GOOSE messages, measurement values, and SCADA telemetry.
4. The LED luminaires are also powered by the substation's DC auxiliary supply, ensuring communication survives AC mains failure.
5. A **VLC-to-fiber gateway** aggregates data from multiple luminaires and forwards it to the Substation Automation System (SAS) server, which in turn communicates with the Load Dispatch Centre (LDC) over the utility's WAN.

**Why Li-Fi is Preferred over Wi-Fi/ZigBee in EHV Substations:**

1. **Total EMI Immunity:** 400 kV switchyards produce intense EM fields (corona, partial discharge, switching transients) that severely corrupt RF signals. Light is unaffected.
2. **No interference with protection signals:** Wi-Fi/ZigBee in the 2.4 GHz band can cross-couple into protection circuits and cause false tripping — a safety hazard. Li-Fi eliminates this risk.
3. **Cybersecurity:** Light signals cannot leak outside the substation perimeter, preventing wireless cyber-intrusion. A hacker cannot remotely sniff Li-Fi data from outside the boundary.
4. **Spectrum licensing:** Li-Fi uses the free, unregulated visible spectrum — no license fees, no spectrum auction costs.
5. **No health hazard to substation personnel** from prolonged RF exposure.

**Limitations to acknowledge:** Li-Fi requires LOS, so dense deployment of LEDs is needed; redundancy and hand-off between luminaires is more complex than Wi-Fi roaming.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Li-Fi Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine angles in $H_{LOS}(0)$:** A common mistake is to write only $\dfrac{(m+1)A}{2\pi d^2}$ and drop the $\cos^m(\phi) \cdot T_s(\psi) \cdot g(\psi) \cdot \cos(\psi)$ factor. This causes a loss of **3–4 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Wrong unit for photodetector area:** The formula demands SI units (m²). If $A_{PD}$ is given in cm², you **must** convert: $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Forgetting this gives a 100× error.
>
> 3. **Confusing Lambertian order $m$ with the angle $\Phi_{1/2}$:** Students often treat $m$ as a free parameter. Remember $m = -\ln 2 / \ln(\cos \Phi_{1/2})$, and it must be computed before $H_{LOS}(0)$ is evaluated.
>
> 4. **Mixing up angle of irradiance ($\phi$) and angle of incidence ($\psi$):** $\phi$ is measured at the **transmitter (LED)**, $\psi$ is measured at the **receiver (photodiode)**. They are usually equal only for aligned systems.
>
> 5. **Confusing Li-Fi with IR or fiber optics:** Li-Fi is a **subset of optical wireless communication** that primarily uses the **visible band**. Do not call it "infrared communication" — that's a different IEEE standard (802.11 IR).
>
> 6. **In modulation questions:** Writing only the names of OOK/OFDM without explaining the **non-negativity constraint** of intensity modulation loses a mark. Always mention that OFDM in Li-Fi requires **DCO-OFDM or ACO-OFDM** to ensure the signal is unipolar.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Li-Fi = Visible Light Communication (VLC)** — invented by Harald Haas (2011).
- [x] **Spectrum:** 400–800 THz visible band; **10,000× wider** than RF spectrum.
- [x] **IEEE Standard:** **IEEE 802.15.7** (PHY + MAC layers for short-range optical wireless).
- [x] **Working Principle:** Intensity modulation of LED + direct detection by photodiode.
- [x] **Key Components:** LED (transmitter), free-space channel, photodiode (receiver), TIA, demodulator.
- [x] **DC Channel Gain formula:** $H_{LOS}(0) = \dfrac{(m+1)A_{PD}}{2\pi d^2} \cos^m(\phi) T_s(\psi) g(\psi) \cos(\psi)$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- [x] **Lambertian order:** $m = -\ln 2 / \ln(\cos \Phi_{1/2})$ — must be calculated first.
- [x] **Responsivity:** $R = \eta_q \lambda / 1240$ A/W (with $\lambda$ in nm) — quick formula for KTU.
- [x] **SNR:** $SNR = (R P_r)^2 / (N_0 B)$ — square of photocurrent over noise.
- [x] **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ — maximum error-free bit rate.
- [x] **Modulation schemes:** OOK, VPPM, CSK (IEEE 802.15.7), and OFDM (DCO/ACO) for advanced systems.
- [x] **Smart grid applications:** Substation IED communication, indoor AMI, smart home automation, EV indoor positioning, HVDC valve hall data links.
- [x] **Advantages over Wi-Fi:** EMI immunity, security (no wall penetration), unregulated spectrum, no RF health hazard, dual illumination + communication.
- [x] **Limitations:** LOS requirement, short range (~10 m), uplink is challenging (since user devices don't emit light naturally; IR or RF uplink used), mobility handover complex.
- [x] **Photodiode area must be in m²** — never forget to convert from cm².
- [x] **Both irradiance angle $\phi$ and incidence angle $\psi$** are required in $H_{LOS}(0)$ — don't drop either.
- [x] **Remember the constants:** Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, electron charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
- [x] **DCO-OFDM = DC-biased Optical OFDM** — ensures non-negative signal for intensity modulation; **ACO-OFDM = Asymmetrically Clipped** — uses only odd subcarriers, suitable for AC-coupled photodiodes.
- [x] **Li-Fi is bidirectional:** Downlink via visible LED; Uplink typically via **infrared (IR) LED** on the user device (to avoid self-glare).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols in Smart Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Communication Protocols in Smart Grid # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Communication Protocols in Smart Grid** are standardized sets of rules, message formats, data structures, and transmission procedures that govern the exchange of information between intelligent electronic devices (IEDs), sensors, smart meters, control centers, distributed energy resources (DERs), and end-user appliances across the generation, transmission, distribution, and consumer domains of a power network. In the KTU 2024 scheme, the smart grid is treated as a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**. Communication protocols form the *cyber backbone* that allows the physical power infrastructure to be monitored (SCADA), protected (IED tripping), automated (substation automation), and optimized (demand response, AMI). Without these protocols, the components from different vendors cannot interoperate, and the grid cannot be "smart." The protocols are typically mapped onto the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) seven-layer model**, with the smart grid leveraging a hybrid stack combining **TCP/IP** (for enterprise/IP-based backhaul) and **fieldbus / industrial Ethernet** (for substation-level real-time traffic). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Human Nervous System:** > Think of the smart grid as a human body and communication protocols as the **nervous system**. > - **Power lines** = the *circulatory system* (carries blood = electricity). > - **Smart meters, sensors, IEDs** = sensory organs (eyes, skin) that gather data. > - **Communication protocols** = *nerves + brain signals* (decide, react, coordinate). > - **Control center (Utility DMS/EMS)** = the *brain* making decisions. > - **Breakers, inverters, transformers** = the *muscles* that act. > > Just as different nerves (motor, sensory, autonomic) carry different types of signals at different speeds, smart-grid protocols are specialized — some carry **fast trip signals** (Goose messages in IEC 61850, ~3 ms), others carry **slow meter readings** (MQTT, seconds). A "smart" body uses the right nerve for the right job — that is exactly what protocol selection does. ## 1.3 Why Smart Grids Need Standardized Communication - **Interoperability:** Devices from ABB, Siemens, GE, Schneider must "speak" the same language. - **Real-time Monitoring:** Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) stream data at **30–60 samples/second**. - **Demand Response:** Two-way price signals between utility and consumer. - **DER Integration:** Rooftop PV, EV chargers, and battery storage need dispatch commands. - **Self-Healing:** Fault detection, isolation, and restoration (FDIR) within **<500 ms**. - **Cybersecurity:** Encryption (TLS, AES-128/256) and authentication to prevent blackouts like the 2015 Ukraine attack. ## 1.4 Key Standard-Setting Bodies | Body | Role | |---|---| | **IEEE** | Wireless (802.15.4, 802.11), SEP 2.0 (IEEE 2030.5) | | **IEC** | IEC 61850 (substations), IEC 61968/61970 (CIM) | | **NIST** | Smart Grid Interoperability Reference Model (SGIRM) | | **DNP3 Users Group** | DNP3 for SCADA | | **IETF** | TCP/IP, MQTT, CoAP | | **3GPP** | Cellular 4G/5G for utility backhaul | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always map a protocol to its **layer in the OSI stack** and its **application domain** (HAN, NAN, WAN, Substation). This 2-axis mapping is the most common KTU framing. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Smart-Grid Communication Network Architecture (NIST SGIRM) The smart grid is divided into **three logical communication domains** (per the KTU 2024 syllabus & NIST SGIRM): | Domain | Full Form | Typical Protocols | Data Rate | Latency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **HAN** | Home Area Network | ZigBee, Wi-Fi, Z-Wave, BLE, Wi-SUN | 10–250 kbps | 1–5 s | | **NAN/FAN** | Neighbourhood/Field Area Network | RF Mesh (Wi-SUN), PLC, LTE-M, NB-IoT, LoRa | 10–1000 kbps | 1–15 s | | **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Fiber, 4G/5G, MPLS, Satellite, DNP3/IP, IEC 61850 | 1–1000 Mbps | 50–500 ms | | **Substation LAN** | Station Bus / Process Bus | IEC 61850 (MMS, GOOSE, SV), PRP/HSR | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | **<4 ms (GOOSE)** | ## 2.2 OSI Model Mapping for Smart-Grid Protocols $$\begin{aligned} \text{Layer 7 — Application} &: \text{ IEC 61850 (MMS), DNP3, Modbus, MQTT, IEEE 2030.5, OpenADR} \\ \text{Layer 4 — Transport} &: \text{ TCP (reliable) / UDP (low-latency GOOSE/SV)} \\ \text{Layer 3 — Network} &: \text{ IP, IPv6 (6LoWPAN for RF mesh)} \\ \text{Layer 2 — Data Link} &: \text{ Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), IEEE 802.15.4, PLC (IEEE 1901)} \\ \text{Layer 1 — Physical} &: \text{ Fiber, Copper, RF, Power Line, Cellular} \end{aligned}$$ ## 2.3 Classification of Communication Technologies **A. Wired Technologies** - **Power Line Communication (PLC):** Uses existing MV/LV conductors. Standards: **IEEE 1901, G3-PLC, PRIME**. - **Optical Fiber:** Highest reliability; used in backbone (OADM, OTN). - **Twisted Pair / Ethernet:** Substation LAN via IEC 61850-3 compliant switches. **B. Wireless Technologies** - **Short Range:** ZigBee, Bluetooth Low Energy, Z-Wave. - **Mesh:** Wi-SUN, RF Mesh (Itron). - **LPWAN:** LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT. - **Cellular:** 4G LTE, 5G (URLLC slice). - **Satellite:** SCADA in remote areas. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/s | | 2 | SNR (linear) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{rx}}{N_0 B}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | dB ; d in km, f in MHz | | 4 | Link Budget (Received Power) | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - L_{total}$ | dBm | | 5 | Latency (one-way) | $T_{lat} = T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{tx}$ | seconds | | 6 | Propagation Delay | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ ; $v_{fiber} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | seconds | | 7 | GOOSE Trip Time (IEC 61850) | $T_{GOOSE} \le 4$ ms (Type 1A) | ms | | 8 | Data Sampling Rate (PMU) | $f_s = 30 \text{–} 120$ samples/s per phasor | Hz | | 9 | Time Sync (IEEE 1588 PTP) | $\Delta t \le 1 \;\mu s$ for Process Bus | μs | | 10 | End-to-End DER Latency Budget | $T_{e2e} \le 1$ s (IEEE 2030.5) | s | > **Boundary condition:** For protection schemes using **GOOSE**, the latency must be $< 4$ ms; for metering, latency of 1–5 s is acceptable. This dictates the protocol choice. ## 2.5 Core Sub-Protocols — Engineering Insight ### 2.5.1 IEC 61850 (Substation Communication) - A **single standard** for substation design, configuration, and communication. - Defines an **SCL (Substation Configuration Language)** XML model. - Three core message types: - **MMS** (Manufacturing Message Specification) — client-server, slow, for SCADA. - **GOOSE** (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event) — peer-to-peer, multicast, **<4 ms trip**. - **Sampled Values (SV)** — streaming digitised CT/PT waveforms at **4800 samples/s** per IEC 61850-9-2. - Runs directly on **Layer 2 (Ethernet)** for GOOSE/SV, bypassing TCP/IP for speed. ### 2.5.2 DNP3 (Distributed Network Protocol) - Predominant in **North American SCADA** (water, oil & gas, electric). - Three layers: Application, Transport (pseudo-transport), Data Link. - Modern variant **DNP3-SAv5** adds **TLS 1.2 / AES-128/256** for security. ### 2.5.3 Modbus (RTU / TCP) - **Modbus RTU** over RS-232/485 — simple, master-slave, 19200 bps typical. - **Modbus TCP** wraps it over TCP port 502. Widely used in DER inverters. ### 2.5.4 MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) - Lightweight **publish/subscribe** protocol on top of TCP. - Uses a **broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ). - Quality of Service levels: QoS 0, 1, 2 — important for HAN/NAN AMI. ### 2.5.5 CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) - Like HTTP but for low-power IoT devices, uses UDP. - Ideal for **NB-IoT / 6LoWPAN** smart meters. ### 2.5.6 IEEE 2030.5 (Smart Energy Profile 2.0 / SEP 2.0) - Application layer for **HAN + DER** using REST/HTTP over TCP/IP. - Supports demand response, pricing, EV charging, energy usage info. ### 2.5.7 OpenADR 2.0 - Open **Automated Demand Response** — XML over HTTP, sends price/events to aggregators. ### 2.5.8 Power Line Communication (PLC) - Uses OFDM on 3–500 kHz (NB-PLC) or 1.8–86 MHz (BB-PLC). - **G3-PLC** supports IPv6 over power lines, ideal for AMI in dense urban areas. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Protection & Auto-Reclosing:** IEC 61850 GOOSE replaces copper hard-wiring, saving kilometres of cable and enabling adaptive logic. - **Outage Management (OMS):** AMI meters (via PLC/RF mesh) detect last-gasp messages to pinpoint outage zones. - **Distributed Energy Management (DERMS):** IEEE 2030.5 dispatches PV/battery inverters based on grid frequency. - **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G):** ISO 15118 + OCPP 2.0.1 negotiate charging over PLC on the EV charging cable. - **Wide-Area Monitoring (WAMS):** PMUs using IEEE C37.118 stream to PDC, enabling real-time oscillation detection (e.g., preventing 2012 India blackout). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Latency Budget Calculation for a Smart-Grid Link > **Problem:** A distribution substation is 25 km from the control center. The PMU data is sent over a fiber-optic link (refractive index n = 1.48). Compute the **one-way propagation delay**. If the PMU stream is 30 samples/s, each frame is 256 bytes, line rate is 1 Gbps. Compute the **transmission delay** and **total link latency**. ### Step 1 — Velocity in Fiber $$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{c}{n} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{1.48} \\ v &= 2.027 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Light slows inside fiber because of refractive index. We must use the *in-fibre* velocity, not the vacuum speed of light. ### Step 2 — Propagation Delay $$\begin{aligned} T_{prop} &= \frac{d}{v} = \frac{25\,000 \text{ m}}{2.027 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} \\ T_{prop} &= 1.233 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\ T_{prop} &\approx 123.3 \; \mu s \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* This is the irreducible physical delay due to signal travel. ### Step 3 — Transmission Delay (Serialization) $$\begin{aligned} \text{Frame size} &= 256 \text{ bytes} = 2048 \text{ bits} \\ T_{tx} &= \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2048 \text{ bits}}{10^{9} \text{ bps}} \\ T_{tx} &= 2.048 \; \mu s \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* 1 Gbps = $10^9$ bits/s. The serialiser takes 2.048 μs to push all 2048 bits onto the wire. ### Step 4 — Per-Frame Budget (Neglecting Queueing/Processing) $$\begin{aligned} T_{lat} &= T_{prop} + T_{tx} \\ T_{lat} &= 123.3 + 2.048 \\ T_{lat} &\approx 125.35 \; \mu s \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** This is well under the 4 ms GOOSE limit, so fiber is suitable for protection. For WAMS/PMU streaming, latency $< 50$ ms is required; we have ample margin. --- ## 3.2 Shannon Capacity for a NB-PLC Channel > **Problem:** A G3-PLC link uses a bandwidth **B = 50 kHz** over a noisy LV cable. Measured noise floor **N = -100 dBm**, received signal power **S = -60 dBm**. Compute the **maximum theoretical data rate**. ### Step 1 — Convert dBm to milliwatts $$\begin{aligned} P \;(\text{mW}) &= 10^{\frac{P_{\text{dBm}}}{10}} \\ N_{\text{mW}} &= 10^{\frac{-100}{10}} = 10^{-10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-13} \text{ W} \\ S_{\text{mW}} &= 10^{\frac{-60}{10}} = 10^{-6} \text{ mW} = 10^{-9} \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Power in dBm uses $10\log_{10}$, so we use the inverse formula. ### Step 2 — Compute SNR (linear) $$\begin{aligned} \text{SNR} &= \frac{S}{N} = \frac{10^{-9}}{10^{-13}} = 10^{4} \\ \text{SNR (dB)} &= 10 \log_{10}(10^{4}) = 40 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* This is a high-quality SNR, typical of good PLC conditions. ### Step 3 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem $$\begin{aligned} C &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\ C &= 50 \times 10^{3} \cdot \log_2(1 + 10^{4}) \\ C &= 50 \times 10^{3} \cdot \log_2(10001) \\ C &= 50 \times 10^{3} \cdot 13.288 \\ C &\approx 664.4 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* $\log_2(10001) = \ln(10001)/\ln(2) = 9.2104/0.6931 = 13.288$. This is the *upper bound*; real G3-PLC achieves ~30–50 kbps due to FEC overhead and channel impairments. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Smart-Grid Protocols | Protocol | Standard Body | OSI Layer | Topology | Typical Use | Security | Data Rate | Latency | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **IEC 61850 (GOOSE)** | IEC | App + Data Link (direct) | Peer-to-peer multicast | Substation protection | MAC + TLS (MMS) | 100 Mbps | $\le 4$ ms | | **IEC 61850-9-2 SV** | IEC | App + Data Link | Multicast streaming | Sampled Values (process bus) | MAC filtering | 100 Mbps | $\le 4$ ms | | **DNP3** | DNP3 Users Group | App + Data Link + Transport | Master-Slave | SCADA (NA) | DNP3-SA (TLS/AES) | 1–100 kbps | 100–500 ms | | **Modbus TCP** | Modbus Org | App (TCP/502) | Master-Slave | DER inverters | None (plain) | 10–100 Mbps | 10–50 ms | | **MQTT** | OASIS (IBM originated) | App (TCP/1883, TLS/8883) | Pub/Sub via Broker | AMI, IoT telemetry | TLS 1.2 + username/pwd | up to 100 Mbps | 1–5 s | | **CoAP** | IETF RFC 7252 | App (UDP/5683) | Request/Response | Constrained IoT | DTLS | 10–100 kbps | $< 1$ s | | **IEEE 2030.5** | IEEE | App (HTTP/TCP) | Client-Server | HAN, DER, EV | TLS + X.509 | up to 10 Mbps | 1 s | | **OpenADR 2.0** | OASIS | App (HTTP/XML) | Pull/Publish | Demand Response | TLS + signatures | low | 1–5 s | | **G3-PLC** | ITU-T G.9903 | PHY + Data Link | Mesh | AMI over LV lines | AES-128 CCM | $\le 200$ kbps | 50–500 ms | | **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | IEEE | PHY + MAC + App | Mesh (star/cluster) | HAN sensors/metering | AES-128 | 250 kbps | 1–5 s | | **LoRaWAN** | LoRa Alliance | PHY + MAC | Star-of-stars | LPWAN remote metering | AES-128 (AppKey) | 0.3–50 kbps | 1–15 s | | **NB-IoT / LTE-M** | 3GPP | Cellular | Star | Utility backhaul, AMI | 3GPP security suite | 200 kbps – 1 Mbps | 100 ms–2 s | | **5G URLLC** | 3GPP | Cellular | Any | V2G, real-time DER | 3GPP + slice isolation | up to 1 Gbps | **< 1 ms** | > **Reading the matrix:** For *protection*, choose IEC 61850 GOOSE (low latency, multicast). For *metering*, choose MQTT/CoAP/IEEE 2030.5 (low bandwidth, high node count). For *backhaul*, choose fiber + 4G/5G. The grid architect **composes** these — they are not competitors, they are complementary layers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Smart-Grid Communication Architecture (NIST SGIRM) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WAN["WAN Wide Area Network"] W1[Control Center EMS DMS] W2[Utility Cloud Historian] W3[Market Operator ISO RTO] end subgraph NAN["NAN Neighbourhood Area"] N1[Data Concentrator DCU] N2[Substation Gateway] N3[RF Mesh Repeater] end subgraph SUB["Substation LAN IEC 61850"] S1[IED Bay Controller] S2[Merging Unit SV] S3[Protection Relay GOOSE] S4[SCADA RTU] end subgraph HAN["HAN Home Area Network"] H1[Smart Meter] H2[PV Inverter] H3[EV Charger] H4[Battery EMS] H5[Home Display] end W1 <-->|IEC 60870 5 104 DNP3 IP| N1 W1 <-->|MPLS Fiber| N2 W2 <-->|HTTPS REST| W1 N1 <-->|PLC G3 OFDM| H1 N1 <-->|Wi SUN RF Mesh| H2 N2 <-->|IEC 61850 MMS| S1 S1 <-->|GOOSE multicast| S3 S2 -->|SV stream| S1 S3 -->|Trip command| S4 H1 <-->|ZigBee Wi-Fi| H5 H2 <-->|IEEE 2030.5| H4 H3 <-->|ISO 15118 PLC| W1 W1 <-->|OpenADR 2.0| W3 ``` *Logic of the diagram:* The Control Center (top) anchors the WAN. It exchanges data with NAN gateways, which in turn reach HAN devices and the Substation LAN. The Substation LAN uses IEC 61850 between IEDs, while the NAN uses PLC / RF mesh. The architecture shows that **different protocols operate at different tiers, simultaneously, in the same grid.** ## 4.2 IEC 61850 Messaging Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IED1 Bay Controller] -->|GOOSE Type 1A multicast| B[IED2 Protection Relay] A -->|GOOSE multicast| C[IED3 Breaker Controller] A -->|MMS TCP client server| D[SCADA HMI] E[CT VT] -->|Analog| F[Merging Unit] F -->|SV IEC 61850 9 2| A F -->|SV multicast| B B -->|Trip GOOSE| C D -->|Read Write| A D -->|Read Write| B ``` *Logic:* MMS is the slow SCADA channel (client-server over TCP). GOOSE is the fast trip channel (direct on Layer 2, no IP). SV is the streamed measurement channel from merging units. This **three-channel model** is the heart of IEC 61850. ## 4.3 OSI Layer Mapping for Smart-Grid Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB L7[Layer 7 Application
IEC 61850 MMS DNP3 Modbus MQTT IEEE 2030.5 OpenADR] L6[Layer 6 Presentation
ASN.1 XML SCL JSON] L5[Layer 5 Session
TLS 1.2 DTLS CoAP sessions] L4[Layer 4 Transport
TCP for SCADA AMI
UDP for GOOSE SV CoAP] L3[Layer 3 Network
IPv4 IPv6 6LoWPAN RPL] L2[Layer 2 Data Link
Ethernet 802.3 802.15.4 IEEE 1901 PLC] L1[Layer 1 Physical
Fiber Copper RF PLC Cellular] L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 ``` *Logic:* Each smart-grid protocol sits on top of the layered stack. GOOSE/SV "skip" Layers 3-4 (no IP) for speed, while MQTT/CoAP use the full stack. ## 4.4 Pub/Sub vs Request/Reply — Protocol Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PS[MQTT Pub Sub Model] P1[Smart Meter Publisher] -->|PUBLISH topic energy| BR[MQTT Broker Mosquitto] P2[PV Inverter Publisher] -->|PUBLISH topic pv| BR BR -->|PUSH to subscriber| S1[Utility Dashboard Subscriber] BR -->|PUSH| S2[Billing Server Subscriber] end subgraph RR[Modbus Request Reply Model] M1[SCADA Master] -->|Request Function 03| M2[DER Inverter Slave] M2 -->|Response register values| M1 end ``` *Logic:* MQTT decouples publishers from subscribers via a broker — ideal for thousands of meters. Modbus is tightly coupled master-slave — ideal for low-latency direct queries. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET861]` **Define the term "Communication Protocol" in the context of a smart grid. List any two protocols used in substation automation.** **Model Answer:** A communication protocol is a **standardized set of rules, data formats, and procedures** that governs the exchange of information between smart-grid devices (meters, IEDs, sensors, control centers), ensuring interoperability, deterministic behaviour, and security. Two protocols used in substation automation: 1. **IEC 61850** — for IED-to-IED protection, metering, and control. 2. **DNP3** — for SCADA telemetry between RTU and control center. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per protocol — total 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET861]` **Differentiate between GOOSE and MMS messages in IEC 61850 in terms of (i) transport layer, (ii) latency, and (iii) primary use.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **GOOSE** | **MMS** | |---|---|---| | (i) Transport | Direct on **Layer 2 (Ethernet)**, multicast, **no TCP/IP** | On **TCP/IP**, client-server | | (ii) Latency | $\le 4$ ms (Type 1A trip) | 100 ms – seconds | | (iii) Primary use | Fast **trip / interlocking** between protection IEDs | **SCADA monitoring & control** (read measurements, write setpoints) | *(1 mark per correct row — total 3 marks)* --- ## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEEET861]` **(a)** Explain the **OSI seven-layer model** as applied to smart-grid communication. With a neat block diagram, indicate the protocols operating at each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the architecture of **IEC 61850** for substation automation. Explain the roles of **MMS, GOOSE, and Sampled Values (SV)** in detail, and justify why GOOSE bypasses the TCP/IP stack. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A(a) The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model, ISO/IEC 7498-1, decomposes communication into seven abstraction layers. In the smart grid, each layer hosts specific protocols: | Layer | Function | Smart-Grid Protocols | |---|---|---| | 7 – Application | End-user services | IEC 61850 (MMS), DNP3, Modbus, MQTT, IEEE 2030.5, OpenADR | | 6 – Presentation | Encoding / encryption | ASN.1 (DNP3), XML (SCL, OpenADR), TLS, JSON | | 5 – Session | Dialog control | TLS/DTLS handshakes | | 4 – Transport | Reliable / fast delivery | **TCP** (MMS, MQTT, DNP3, IEEE 2030.5), **UDP** (CoAP), **none** (GOOSE/SV) | | 3 – Network | Addressing / routing | IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL | | 2 – Data Link | Framing / MAC | Ethernet (802.3), IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee), IEEE 1901 (PLC), PRP/HSR | | 1 – Physical | Bit transmission | Fiber, copper, RF (sub-GHz/2.4 GHz), PLC, cellular (4G/5G) | *Key insight:* A single end-to-end smart-grid flow (e.g., meter → DCU → control center) traverses **all 7 layers**, while a substation trip signal (GOOSE) traverses **only Layers 1-2 + 7**, skipping the network/transport overhead. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A(b) **Architecture of IEC 61850:** IEC 61850 is an **object-oriented, client-server + peer-to-peer standard** for substation automation. It has multiple parts: - **IEC 61850-3** – environmental & power quality requirements. - **IEC 61850-4** – system & project management. - **IEC 61850-5** – communication requirements (lists 7 conformance classes). - **IEC 61850-6** – **SCL (Substation Configuration Language)** – XML-based IED description. - **IEC 61850-7-x** – data model (Logical Nodes such as `MMXU`, `PDIS`, `XCBR`). - **IEC 61850-8-1** – MMS mapping (client-server over TCP/IP). - **IEC 61850-8-2** – GOOSE & SV mapping. - **IEC 61850-9-2** – Sampled Values over Ethernet. **Three Message Types:** 1. **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification):** - Client-server model: SCADA = client, IED = server. - Used for **configuration, setpoints, periodic measurement polling**. - Encapsulated in TCP/IP; latency **100 ms – seconds**. 2. **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event):** - **Peer-to-peer, multicast** at Layer 2. - Used for **trip commands, interlocking, breaker position, status changes**. - Latency **< 4 ms** (Type 1A); 1 ms achievable on dedicated networks. - Uses an **8-byte GOOSE header + status number (stNum/sqNum)**; retransmits with decreasing priority (back-off to next event). 3. **Sampled Values (SV):** - **Continuous streaming** of digitised CT/VT waveforms. - Up to **4800 samples/s** for 50 Hz systems (80 samples/cycle, IEC 61850-9-2LE). - Multicast Layer 2 to subscribers (protection/PMU). **Why GOOSE bypasses TCP/IP:** TCP/IP introduces: - 3-way handshake delays. - Acknowledgement and retransmission overhead. - Routing and stack processing (~ms-scale). A protection trip must fire within **< 4 ms** end-to-end. By mapping GOOSE **directly onto Ethernet multicast (Layer 2)**, IEC 61850 eliminates the IP/TCP overhead, achieving deterministic sub-millisecond transfer. Additionally, the **reliability** of GOOSE is preserved by **flood-and-rebroadcast** with `stNum/sqNum` — the receiver discards old/faulty frames. This makes the link **faster than a hard-wired copper trip** in many practical cases. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEEET861]` **(a)** Describe the **HAN, NAN/FAN, and WAN** domains of smart-grid communication. For each, state two representative protocols and their typical data rates. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the working principle of **Power Line Communication (PLC)** with reference to the **G3-PLC** and **IEEE 1901** standards. Discuss the advantages and limitations of PLC for AMI. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-B(a) The NIST Smart-Grid Interoperability Reference Model (SGIRM) partitions the smart-grid communication network into three domains: **1. Home Area Network (HAN)** - **Scope:** Inside customer premises — connects smart meter, smart appliances, rooftop PV inverter, EV charger, HEMS, in-home display. - **Scale:** 5–30 nodes per home. - **Data:** Energy usage, appliance on/off, pricing, DR signals. - **Representative Protocols:** - **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** – 250 kbps, 2.4 GHz, AES-128. - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11n/ac)** – up to 600 Mbps, 2.4/5 GHz. - *(also Z-Wave, BLE, Wi-SUN FAN at boundary)* **2. Neighbourhood / Field Area Network (NAN/FAN)** - **Scope:** Between customer premises and substation — connects thousands of meters to the data concentrator (DCU). - **Scale:** 1000–10000 meters per DCU. - **Data:** AMI readings, last-gasp outage notifications, firmware updates. - **Representative Protocols:** - **RF Mesh (Wi-SUN, Itron)** – 50–300 kbps, 900 MHz. - **G3-PLC / PRIME** – up to 200 kbps, 10–500 kHz band. **3. Wide Area Network (WAN)** - **Scope:** Utility backhaul from substations to control centers, market operators, neighbouring utilities. - **Scale:** Hundreds of km. - **Data:** SCADA, EMS telemetry, PMU streams, market signals. - **Representative Protocols:** - **Fiber / MPLS** – 1–100 Gbps. - **4G/5G cellular** – 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps; 5G URLLC < 1 ms latency. - *(also IEC 61850 MMS, DNP3-IP, IEC 60870-5-104)* **Data-rate summary:** - HAN: 10 kbps – 250 Mbps - NAN: 10 – 1000 kbps - WAN: 1 Mbps – 100 Gbps The **latency tolerance increases** as we go outward (HAN is latency-tolerant; WAN-substation segment is latency-critical for protection). --- #### Model Solution to Q-B(b) **Power Line Communication (PLC):** PLC reuses the **existing power cables** (LV 230/415 V, MV 11/22 kV) as a communication medium. A **coupling circuit** at the consumer premises injects a high-frequency signal (10 kHz – 86 MHz) onto the power conductor, and a **modem** at the substation extracts it. Two frequency bands are standardised: | Class | Band | Standard | Data Rate | |---|---|---|---| | NB-PLC (Narrowband) | 3 – 500 kHz | **IEEE 1901.2**, G3-PLC, PRIME | 5 – 500 kbps | | BB-PLC (Broadband) | 1.8 – 86 MHz | **IEEE 1901**, HomePlug AV/AV2/Green PHY | up to 1 Gbps (in-home) | **G3-PLC (ITU-T G.9903):** - Designed for **LV / MV AMI**, supports **IPv6** over PLC (6LoWPAN). - Uses **OFDM** with adaptive modulation (DBPSK → QAM). - Robust to impulsive noise via **ROBO mode** and FEC (Reed-Solomon + Convolutional). - Range: up to several km on MV lines, 100s of metres on LV. - Security: **AES-128** (CCM mode) at MAC layer. **IEEE 1901 (BB-PLC):** - Used for **in-home networking, EV charging (ISO 15118 uses Green PHY)**. - Up to 200 Mbps (HomePlug AV), 1 Gbps (HomePlug AV2). **Advantages of PLC for AMI:** 1. **No new wires** — uses existing infrastructure ⇒ low CAPEX. 2. Universal reach — every electrified home is "connected." 3. Mature, standardised, inter-vendor interoperability. 4. Hybrid with RF (RF-PLC mesh) for redundancy. 5. **EV charging cable** (ISO 15118 / Green PHY) is the only feasible way to do V2G signalling. **Limitations:** 1. **Channel is harsh:** impulsive noise from switching devices, narrowband interference, frequency-selective fading. 2. **Attenuation** rises with distance and load (transformers block signals; couplers needed). 3. **Impedance mismatch** at every junction causes reflections. 4. **Low data rate** compared to fiber — unsuitable for backhaul. 5. **EMC/EMI** regulatory constraints (must not interfere with licensed radio bands). 6. **Latency** 50–500 ms; not suitable for protection (only metering & DER setpoints). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing GOOSE with MMS:** Many students write "GOOSE is for SCADA" — that is **MMS**. GOOSE is for **trip / interlocking** and operates at **Layer 2 (no IP)**. (*–2 marks*) > 2. **Mixing up HAN / NAN / WAN:** Writing ZigBee in WAN or DNP3 in HAN. (*–1 mark per wrong mapping*) > 3. **Forgetting to mention IPv6 / security** when describing G3-PLC — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the *IP-based* and *secure* PLC variants. (*–1 mark*) > 4. **Writing "MQTT uses HTTP"** — it uses **TCP port 1883 (or 8883 with TLS)**, not HTTP. (*–1 mark*) > 5. **Skipping units in latency answers:** Always write "ms" or "μs" — examiners deduct for missing units. > 6. **Missing the "why"** in 14-mark questions: KTU 2024 requires the *engineering justification* (e.g., *why* GOOSE bypasses IP, *why* MQTT uses a broker). Don't just describe — **justify**. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - A **communication protocol** = set of rules for data exchange; essential for **interoperability** in a multi-vendor smart grid. - Smart-grid communication is partitioned into **HAN ↔ NAN/FAN ↔ WAN ↔ Substation LAN**. - **OSI layers** in smart grid: - Layer 7: **IEC 61850 MMS, DNP3, Modbus, MQTT, CoAP, IEEE 2030.5, OpenADR** - Layer 2: **Ethernet, IEEE 802.15.4, IEEE 1901 (PLC)** - Layer 1: **Fiber, RF, PLC, Cellular** - **IEC 61850** = the dominant **substation** standard; uses **MMS** (SCADA), **GOOSE** (trip, <4 ms, Layer-2 multicast), and **Sampled Values** (process bus, 80 samples/cycle). - **GOOSE bypasses IP/TCP** to achieve < 4 ms latency; uses retransmission with back-off for reliability. - **DNP3** = traditional **SCADA** protocol (esp. North America); DNP3-SAv5 adds **TLS + AES**. - **Modbus TCP** (port 502) is simple, master-slave, common in **DER inverters**; lacks native security. - **MQTT** = lightweight **publish/subscribe** over TCP (port 1883/8883); uses a **broker**; ideal for **IoT/AMI** telemetry. QoS levels: 0, 1, 2. - **CoAP** = HTTP-like over **UDP** (port 5683); for **constrained devices**; secured with **DTLS**. - **IEEE 2030.5 (SEP 2.0)** = HAN/DER application layer; uses **REST/HTTP** with **X.509 + TLS**. - **OpenADR 2.0** = automated demand-response; **XML/HTTP** between utility and aggregator. - **G3-PLC / IEEE 1901.2** = NB-PLC for **LV/MV AMI**; supports **IPv6**; uses **OFDM + AES-128**. - **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** = 250 kbps, 2.4 GHz, **AES-128**; common in **HAN**. - **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT / LTE-M** = **LPWAN** for remote metering; data rates < 50 kbps; latency 1–15 s. - **5G URLLC** = < 1 ms latency, enabler of **real-time V2G and grid-edge control**. - **PMU latency** (WAMS) ≈ 25–50 ms; **GOOSE** < 4 ms; **metering** 1–5 s. - **Cybersecurity** is now built-in: **TLS, DTLS, AES-128/256, X.509, MACsec** are mandatory per **IEC 62351**. - **Key formulas** to remember: - $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ - $\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ dB - $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - L_{total}$ - $T_{lat} = T_{prop} + T_{tx} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ - **Always mention**: latency budget, security, and standard body for any protocol answer. - **Trade-off principle:** No single protocol is "best" — the smart grid **stacks** them: **HAN protocols (ZigBee/Wi-Fi)** → **NAN protocols (RF mesh/PLC)** → **WAN protocols (Fiber/4G/5G)** → **Substation protocols (IEC 61850)**. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Protocol + OSI Layer + Domain (HAN/NAN/WAN/Substation) + Data Rate + Latency + Security = Full marks.*

topics.title: Introduction to IEC 61850 Standard and Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to IEC 61850 Standard and Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Introduction to IEC 61850 Standard and Benefits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61850** is the international standard titled *"Communication networks and systems for power utility automation"*, first published by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** in **2003**, with subsequent editions (Ed. 2 in 2011, Ed. 3 onwards). It defines a unified, object-oriented communication architecture for **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)** in electrical substations and, by extension, across the entire smart-grid ecosystem.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU-Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *IEC 61850 is a global, open, non-proprietary standard that provides a common framework for the interoperability of IEDs, enabling seamless data exchange, configuration, and coordinated control in substation automation systems (SAS) and beyond.*

The standard is not merely a "protocol"; it is a **complete ecosystem** comprising:

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| Data Modeling | Object-oriented modeling of substation equipment |
| Communication Services | Abstract Service Interface (ACSI) mapping to concrete protocols (MMS, etc.) |
| Configuration Language | SCL (Substation Configuration Language) based on XML |
| Conformance Testing | Mandatory testing for vendor interoperability |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The "Universal Language of the Substation"**
> 
> Imagine a multinational hospital where doctors, nurses, and labs all speak different native languages. Patient records get lost, prescriptions are misinterpreted, and critical alarms are ignored. The hospital eventually adopts a **single common medical language** with standardized forms, terminology, and procedures.
> 
> **IEC 61850 plays exactly that role in a substation:**
> - **Before IEC 61850:** Each IED (relay, breaker, transformer monitor) from different vendors (ABB, Siemens, GE, Schneider) used proprietary protocols (DNP3, Modbus, Profibus). Integration was expensive, slow, and error-prone.
> - **After IEC 61850:** Every IED "speaks" the same language, reports data in a standardized format, and reacts to events using common semantics.

### 1.3 Scope of the Standard

IEC 61850 is structured in **10 main parts** (annexed under multiple sub-parts):

- **Part 1:** Introduction and overview
- **Part 2:** Glossary
- **Part 3:** General requirements
- **Part 4:** System and project management
- **Part 5:** Communication requirements for functions and device models
- **Part 6:** Configuration description language for communication in electrical substations related to IEDs
- **Part 7:** Basic information and communication structure (Data models, ACSI)
- **Part 8:** Specific Communication Service Mappings (SCSM) – e.g., **MMS over TCP/IP**, **GSE/GOOSE** over Ethernet
- **Part 9:** Sampled values over ISO/IEC 8802-3
- **Part 10:** Conformance testing

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse IEC 61850 with IEC 60870-5 (DNP3 family). IEC 60870-5 is a **legacy telecontrol protocol**, while IEC 61850 is a **modern, object-oriented, Ethernet-based architecture** designed for high-speed substation automation.

### 1.4 Physical / Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **Data Rate:** **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps Ethernet** (vs. kbps for legacy serial protocols)
- **Latency for Trip Signals (Type 1A):** **< 3 ms** (GOOSE messages)
- **Latency for Sampled Values:** **< 3 ms**
- **Standardization Body:** **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** – Technical Committee **TC 57**
- **Reference Architecture:** **OSI 7-Layer Model** with simplified 3-layer mapping
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The IEC 61850 standard introduces a **3-layer functional architecture** (often called the **"Three-Level Substation Model"**):

1. **Process Level** – Sensors, CTs/PTs, actuators, breakers, merging units
2. **Bay Level** – IEDs (protection, control, metering) – *the heart of intelligence*
3. **Station Level** – SCADA gateway, HMI, engineering workstations, controllers

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three levels?** Each level has a well-defined responsibility, ensuring **functional decomposition** and **decoupled communication** – a key engineering principle that improves maintainability and scalability.

### 2.2 Core Engineering Innovations of IEC 61850

The standard introduces **four revolutionary engineering concepts** every KTU student must memorize:

#### (a) Object-Oriented Data Modeling

Each physical device is broken into **Logical Nodes (LNs)**. Example: A circuit breaker is the LN class **"XCBR"**, while its measurements are represented by **"MMXU"** (Measurement unit).

#### (b) Abstract Communication Service Interface (ACSI)

The standard defines *what* services are needed (e.g., report a data change, get a value) **independent of the protocol used**. This is mapped to concrete protocols in the **Specific Communication Service Mapping (SCSM)** layer.

#### (c) Substation Configuration Language (SCL)

An **XML-based** language that describes the substation **topology, IEDs, data models, and communication links** in a portable, vendor-neutral manner. Files have extensions like **.icd, .ssd, .scd, .iid, .cid**.

#### (d) High-Speed Peer-to-Peer Communication

- **GOOSE (Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event):** Multicast, high-priority, fast messages for trip/interlock signals.
- **GSE (Generic Substation Event):** Broader category; GOOSE is its fast Ethernet variant.
- **Sampled Values (SV):** Continuous streaming of digitized CT/PT waveforms.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Term | Definition / Standard Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| GOOSE Latency (Type 1A) | $t_{G} < 3$ | $\text{ms}$ |
| Sampled Value Latency | $t_{SV} < 3$ | $\text{ms}$ |
| Type 1A Transfer Time | $t_{tr} \leq 3$ | $\text{ms}$ (Trip command) |
| Type 1B Transfer Time | $t_{tr} \leq 20$ | $\text{ms}$ |
| Type 2 (Medium-speed) | $t_{tr} \leq 100$ | $\text{ms}$ |
| Type 3 (Low-speed) | $t_{tr} \leq 500$ | $\text{ms}$ |
| Type 4 (File transfer) | $t_{tr} \leq 1000$ | $\text{ms}$ |
| Ethernet Speed (typical) | $v_{eth} = 100 \text{ or } 1000$ | $\text{Mbps}$ |
| MMS over TCP/IP Port | $P_{MMS} = 102$ | $\text{TCP Port}$ |
| Min. Frame Size | $L_{min} = 64$ | $\text{bytes}$ |
| Max. Frame Size | $L_{max} = 1518$ | $\text{bytes}$ (Jumbo up to 9 KB) |
| IED Data Refresh Rate | $f_{r} \in \vert 48, 50, 60, 80, 256, 4800 \vert$ | $\text{frames/sec}$ (SV) |
| MAC Address Format | 48-bit (XX-XX-XX-XX-XX-XX) | Hexadecimal |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Memorize the **seven types of transfer time** (Type 1A to Type 7). They are frequently asked as 3-mark short questions!

### 2.4 The OSI-to-IEC 61850 Mapping

| OSI 7-Layer Model | IEC 61850 Simplified Stack |
|---|---|
| Application Layer | ACSI (MMS, GOOSE, SV) |
| Presentation Layer | (Integrated) |
| Session Layer | (Integrated) |
| Transport Layer | TCP/UDP (for MMS); direct Layer 2 (for GOOSE/SV) |
| Network Layer | IP (for MMS); Multicast MAC (for GOOSE/SV) |
| Data Link Layer | IEEE 802.1Q (VLAN), IEEE 802.1p (Priority), IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet) |
| Physical Layer | 100BASE-FX (Fiber), 100BASE-TX (Copper) |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Application | Benefit in Practice |
|---|---|
| **Substation Automation (SAS)** | Vendor-independent interoperability |
| **Wide Area Monitoring (WAMS)** | Synchrophasor communication over IEC 61850-90-5 |
| **Distributed Energy Resources (DER)** | Standardized inverter / controller communication |
| **EV Charging Infrastructure** | IEC 61850-90-8 for charger-to-grid messaging |
| **Hydro & Wind Plants** | IEC 61850-7-410 (hydro), 7-420 (wind) extensions |
