| **Power Quality Monitoring** | Real-time high-fidelity sampled values |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Step: Time-Critical Message Delay Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU Board Examination problem typically asks: *Calculate the maximum end-to-end delay of a Type 1A trip message in a 4-bay IEC 61850 substation with two switches, where each switch introduces a known processing delay.*

We derive a generalized formula for IEC 61850 message delay.

### 3.2 Derivation of End-to-End Transfer Time

**Given:**
- Processing delay at IED $i$: $d_{p,i}$ (in ms)
- Network propagation delay: $d_{prop}$ (in ms)
- Transmission delay of a frame of size $L$ over a link of bandwidth $B$:

$$
d_{tx} = \frac{L}{B}
$$

**Total transfer time across $N$ intermediate nodes:**

$$
t_{tr} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{p,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N+1} d_{tx,j} + \sum_{k=1}^{N+1} d_{prop,k}
$$

**Step-by-step logical expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tr} &= d_{p,1} + d_{p,2} + \cdots + d_{p,N} \\
&\quad + d_{tx,1} + d_{tx,2} + \cdots + d_{tx,N+1} \\
&\quad + d_{prop,1} + d_{prop,2} + \cdots + d_{prop,N+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Each network device (switch/router) contributes one processing delay.**

If we assume uniform processing delay $d_p$ for all $N$ devices:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{p,i} = N \cdot d_p
$$

**Step 2: Each network link contributes one transmission delay.** For a uniform link:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{N+1} d_{tx,j} = (N+1) \cdot \frac{L}{B}
$$

**Step 3: Each link contributes one propagation delay.** For uniform cable length $\ell$ and propagation speed $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ in fiber:

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{\ell}{v_p}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{N+1} d_{prop,k} = (N+1) \cdot \frac{\ell}{v_p}
$$

**Step 4: Combining the three results into the Master Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tr} &= N \cdot d_p + (N+1) \cdot \frac{L}{B} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{\ell}{v_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A GOOSE message of size $L = 256$ bytes is sent across $N = 2$ Ethernet switches. Each switch processing delay is $d_p = 0.5$ ms. Link bandwidth $B = 100$ Mbps. Link length $\ell = 100$ m. Fiber propagation speed $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute $t_{tr}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute transmission delay per link.**

$$
d_{tx} = \frac{256 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bit/s}} = \frac{2048}{10^8} = 2.048 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.02048 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute propagation delay per link.**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{100 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.0005 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the master equation with $N = 2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tr} &= N \cdot d_p + (N+1) \cdot d_{tx} + (N+1) \cdot d_{prop} \\
&= 2 \times 0.5 + 3 \times 0.02048 + 3 \times 0.0005 \\
&= 1.0 + 0.06144 + 0.0015 \\
&= 1.06294 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify against IEC 61850 Type 1A limit.**

$$
t_{tr} = 1.063 \text{ ms} \;\checkmark \;< 3 \text{ ms (Type 1A requirement)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The calculated transfer time (1.063 ms) is well within the **Type 1A** constraint of **3 ms**, confirming the network design meets the **most critical protection trip** requirement of IEC 61850.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Performance Classes Summary

| Performance Class | Transfer Time $t_{tr}$ | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Type 1A | $\le 3$ ms | Trip commands (fast protection) |
| Type 1B | $\le 20$ ms | Other protection functions |
| Type 2 | $\le 100$ ms | Control / Interlock |
| Type 3 | $\le 500$ ms | Event reporting |
| Type 4 | $\le 1000$ ms | File transfers (settings, logs) |
| Type 5 | $\le 1000$ ms (best effort) | Operator commands |
| Type 6 | $\le 3000$ ms | Operator messages |
| Type 7 | $\le 5000$ ms | Low-priority, telemetry |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEC 61850 Three-Level Substation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["STATION LEVEL - SCADA HMI Engineering Workstation Gateway Redundant Controller"]
    nodeB["BAY LEVEL - Bay 1 IED Bay 2 IED Bay 3 IED Bay 4 IED Protection Control Metering"]
    nodeC["PROCESS LEVEL - Current Transformers Voltage Transformers Circuit Breakers Disconnectors Merging Units Sensors Actuators"]

    nodeA -->|"Station Bus 100 Mbps Ethernet"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"Process Bus Sampled Values GOOSE Messages"| nodeC
    nodeA -.->|"MMS Reports Files Logs"| nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **Station Level** (top) supervises the entire substation. The **Bay Level** (middle) contains the IEDs that perform protection, control, and metering. The **Process Level** (bottom) interfaces with the actual physical power equipment.

## 4.2 IEC 61850 Protocol Stack Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Application ACSI Generic Substation Event GSE GOOSE Sampled Values SV Manufacturing Message Specification MMS"]
    node2["Transport TCP for MMS Direct Layer 2 for GOOSE and SV"]
    node3["Network IP for MMS Multicast MAC for GOOSE and SV"]
    node4["Data Link IEEE 802 1Q VLAN IEEE 802 1p Priority IEEE 802 3 Ethernet"]
    node5["Physical 100BASE FX Fiber 100BASE TX Copper"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
```

## 4.3 IEC 61850 Data Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["PHYSICAL DEVICE IED"]
    nodeB["LOGICAL DEVICE LD1 Protection LD2 Metering LD3 Control"]
    nodeC["LOGICAL NODE XCBR Circuit Breaker MMXU Measurement Unit PDIF Differential Protection PTRC Trip Logic"]
    nodeD["DATA OBJECTS Pos Status Oper Control"]
    nodeE["DATA ATTRIBUTES ctlVal stVal q quality t timestamp"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Insight:** This **5-tier hierarchy** (Physical Device → Logical Device → Logical Node → Data Object → Data Attribute) is the foundational object model of IEC 61850. Every KTU exam question on data modeling references this structure.

## 4.4 IEC 61850 SCL Configuration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Step 1 Vendor Tools Export ICD File IED Capability Description"]
    node2["Step 2 System Integrator Creates SSD Substation Description"]
    node3["Step 3 SCD Generation Substation Configuration Description"]
    node4["Step 4 IID Generation Instantiated IED Description"]
    node5["Step 5 Final CID Configured IED Description Uploaded to IED"]
    node6["Step 6 Communication Commissioning and Conformance Testing"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define IEC 61850. List any four of its key features. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> *IEC 61850 is an international standard published by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** for communication networks and systems in **power utility automation**, especially substation automation.*

**Four key features:**

1. **Object-Oriented Data Modeling** – Standardized logical nodes (e.g., XCBR, MMXU) representing physical devices.
2. **Abstract Communication Service Interface (ACSI)** – Protocol-independent service definition.
3. **Substation Configuration Language (SCL)** – XML-based vendor-neutral configuration.
4. **High-Speed Peer-to-Peer Messaging** – GOOSE and Sampled Values over Ethernet.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing only "it is a protocol" loses **1 mark**. Always mention *Communication networks and systems for power utility automation* explicitly.

---

### Q2. What is GOOSE in IEC 61850? Why is it important for protection? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **GOOSE (Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event)** is a high-speed, multicast, peer-to-peer communication service defined in **IEC 61850 Part 8-1** that operates directly over the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model, bypassing TCP/IP stack overhead.

**Importance in protection:**

- Delivers **trip/interlock signals in < 3 ms** (Type 1A performance class).
- Operates via **Layer 2 multicast**, so it is independent of IP address changes.
- Uses **VLAN tagging and IEEE 802.1p priority** to guarantee Quality of Service (QoS).
- Replaces **hard-wired copper interlocks** between panels, saving significant cabling cost.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for ESE Pattern]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **three-level substation architecture** of IEC 61850 with a neat functional block diagram. List the communication buses used at each level. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Discuss the **key benefits of IEC 61850 over conventional protocols** (DNP3, Modbus, Profibus). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 – Define the Three Levels:**

1. **Process Level:** Includes Current Transformers (CTs), Voltage Transformers (PTs), **Merging Units (MUs)**, primary switchgear (circuit breakers, isolators), and actuators. Analog signals (kV, kA) are digitized here.
2. **Bay Level:** Contains **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)** – one or more per bay. Functions include protection, control, measurement, monitoring, and recording.
3. **Station Level:** Houses the **SCADA gateway, HMI, and engineering station**. Supervisory control and alarm management happen here.

**Step 2 – Communication Buses:**

| Level | Bus Name | Communication Type | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process to Bay | **Process Bus** | Sampled Values (SV), GOOSE | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps |
| Bay to Station | **Station Bus** | MMS (client-server reports) | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps |

**Step 3 – Functional Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1).**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 – Conventional Protocol Limitations:**

- **Vendor Lock-in:** DNP3 / Modbus are **legacy serial protocols** with vendor-specific extensions.
- **Limited Data Modeling:** No semantic understanding of "circuit breaker" or "tap changer" – only raw registers.
- **Cabling Overhead:** Each trip signal requires **dedicated copper wiring**.
- **Slow Speed:** Serial links operate at **kbps**, not Gbps.

**Step 2 – Seven Major Benefits of IEC 61850:**

1. **Interoperability** – Multi-vendor IEDs integrate seamlessly.
2. **Reduced Cabling** – GOOSE replaces hardwiring, saving up to **40% copper**.
3. **High-Speed Communication** – Sub-3 ms trip delivery.
4. **Self-Description via SCL** – Configuration errors detected before commissioning.
5. **Standardized Data Models** – Logical nodes (XCBR, MMXU, PDIF) provide semantic clarity.
6. **Future-Proof Architecture** – Easy to extend from substations to **wind farms, EVs, DERs**.
7. **Advanced Diagnostics** – Continuous IED health monitoring via SCL and reporting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list benefits but **fail to compare with conventional protocols**. Always state *the problem* that IEC 61850 solves. Comparison earns the full 7 marks; mere listing earns only 4.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for ESE Pattern]`

> **(a)** Describe the **data model hierarchy of IEC 61850** with a suitable example. Explain the role of **Logical Nodes** using the **XCBR** (Circuit Breaker) class. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A **GOOSE trip message** must traverse **3 Ethernet switches** in a substation. The **frame size** is $L = 200$ bytes, link bandwidth $B = 100$ Mbps, fiber length between switches = 50 m, propagation speed $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, and switch processing delay $d_p = 0.4$ ms. Compute the **end-to-end transfer time** and verify whether it satisfies the **Type 1A requirement**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 – Data Model Hierarchy (5 levels):**

1. **Physical Device** – The IED itself (e.g., REF615 Relay).
2. **Logical Device (LD)** – A virtual sub-unit within the IED (e.g., `LD1 = Protection`, `LD2 = Metering`).
3. **Logical Node (LN)** – The smallest function entity. Each LN has standardized data.
4. **Data Object (DO)** – A named data item within an LN (e.g., `Pos`, `OpCnt`).
5. **Data Attribute (DA)** – The actual value with quality and timestamp (e.g., `stVal`, `q`, `t`).

**Step 2 – Example Using XCBR:**

> [!NOTE]
> **XCBR = Circuit Breaker Logical Node** (Class definition per IEC 61850-7-4).

Key data objects inside `XCBR`:
- `Pos` – The breaker position (Open / Closed / Intermediate).
- `OpCnt` – Operation counter (how many times it has tripped).
- `CBOpCap` – Operating capability (charge spring status).

A typical address might be:
`IEDName/Substation1/Bay1/LD0/XCBR1$ST$Pos$stVal`

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Compute transmission delay.**

$$
d_{tx} = \frac{L \times 8}{B} = \frac{200 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6} = \frac{1600}{10^8} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.016 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute propagation delay per link.**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{\ell}{v_p} = \frac{50}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.00025 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the master equation with $N = 3$ switches.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tr} &= N \cdot d_p + (N+1) \cdot d_{tx} + (N+1) \cdot d_{prop} \\
&= 3 \times 0.4 + 4 \times 0.016 + 4 \times 0.00025 \\
&= 1.2 + 0.064 + 0.001 \\
&= 1.265 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare against Type 1A requirement.**

$$
t_{tr} = 1.265 \text{ ms} \;\checkmark \;< 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** The GOOSE message satisfies the **Type 1A** performance class. The design is **compliant** with IEC 61850-5 transfer-time requirements.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors to Avoid:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **bytes → bits** in the transmission delay formula (8 multiplier).
> 2. Using $N$ instead of $N+1$ for links. There are **always one more link than switches** in a linear chain.
> 3. Confusing $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (fiber) with $v_p = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (vacuum). Fiber is approximately **67% of $c$**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 – IEC 61850):**

- IEC 61850 = **International standard** for power utility automation, published by **IEC TC 57**; first published **2003**.
- It is **not just a protocol** – it is a complete ecosystem: data models, services, configuration, and conformance.
- **Three Levels:** Process → Bay → Station; **Two Buses:** Process Bus (SV/GOOSE) and Station Bus (MMS).
- **Five-tier data hierarchy:** Physical Device → Logical Device → Logical Node → Data Object → Data Attribute.
- **Logical Node examples:** `XCBR` (breaker), `MMXU` (measurement), `PDIF` (differential), `PTRC` (trip logic), `TVTR` (voltage transformer).
- **GOOSE = Layer 2 multicast** for trips; **Sampled Values = continuous digital waveforms** from Merging Units.
- **MMS** = client-server reports/files over **TCP/IP port 102**.
- **SCL (Substation Configuration Language)** is **XML-based**; file types: `.icd`, `.ssd`, `.scd`, `.iid`, `.cid`.
- **Performance classes:** Type 1A $\le 3$ ms (trips), Type 1B $\le 20$ ms, Type 2 $\le 100$ ms, Type 3 $\le 500$ ms, Type 4 $\le 1000$ ms.
- **Master transfer-time equation:** $t_{tr} = N \cdot d_p + (N+1) \cdot \frac{L}{B} + (N+1) \cdot \frac{\ell}{v_p}$.
- **Key benefits:** Interoperability, reduced cabling, high speed, self-describing configuration, scalability to DER/EV/Wind, vendor-neutrality.
- **Standard Ethernet:** 100BASE-FX (fiber) or 100BASE-TX (copper), with VLAN (802.1Q) and Priority (802.1p) for QoS.
- **Propagation speed in fiber:** $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (not $3 \times 10^8$ m/s).
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** Confusing IEC 61850 with DNP3; forgetting $N+1$ links; using incorrect units in delay calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEC Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event (GOOSE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IEC Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event (GOOSE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event (GOOSE)** is a high-speed, peer-to-peer, multicast communication service defined under **IEC 61850** (specifically in **Part IEC 61850-8-1: Communication networks and systems for power utility automation – Part 8-1: Specific Communication Service Mapping (SCSM) – Mappings to MMS and ISO/IEC 8802-3**). GOOSE provides a deterministic and reliable mechanism for the transfer of time-critical, event-driven, binary status information (such as trip commands, interlocking signals, and breaker status) between Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) within a **Substation Automation System (SAS)**.

It operates over **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet)** local area networks, bypassing the heavy TCP/IP stack to achieve end-to-end transmission times as low as **4 milliseconds** for Type 1A (Trip) messages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** GOOSE is one of the **pillars of substation communication** mandated by the IEC 61850 standard. It belongs to the category of services that require *high-speed peer-to-peer communication* and replaces the legacy hard-wired copper interconnections between IEDs in protection panels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital emergency room. When a patient's heart monitor detects a fatal arrhythmia, the nurse **does not** send an email, fill out a form, and wait for the doctor to log in to a database. Instead, the monitor immediately broadcasts a **loud, repeated alarm** to every relevant piece of equipment in the ICU — defibrillators, central stations, doctor pagers — and the alarm is *repeated rapidly* a few times to ensure that no device misses it due to temporary bus contention.

**GOOSE works exactly the same way inside a digital substation:**

| Hospital Analogy | Substation GOOSE Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Heart monitor detects emergency | Protection IED detects a fault |
| Loud, repeated alarm | GOOSE message with rapid retransmissions (4 ms, 8 ms, 16 ms …) |
| Every ICU device hears the alarm | All subscribed IEDs in the process bus receive the multicast frame |
| Alarm contains a unique patient ID | GOOSE frame contains `goCBRef` (unique publisher identification) |
| System eventually stops alarming when patient stabilizes | Retransmission cadence stabilizes (resumes normal heartbeat) |

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (As per IEC 61850-5)

- **Type 1A — Trip Messages:** $\le \mathbf{4 \text{ ms}}$ end-to-end transfer time
- **Type 1 — Other Fast Messages:** $\le \mathbf{10 \text{ ms}}$
- **Type 2 — Medium Speed Messages:** $\le \mathbf{100 \text{ ms}}$
- **Type 3 — Low Speed Messages:** $\le \mathbf{500 \text{ ms}}$
- **Type 4 — Raw Data / File Transfer:** $\ge \mathbf{1000 \text{ ms}}$

> [!NOTE]
> The end-to-end transfer time includes **encoding, transmission, decoding, and IED processing**. For GOOSE specifically, the transfer time is bounded by the worst-case **3 ms** at the substation bus level (defined in IEC 61850-5, Table 2) and **4 ms** when crossing the station bus.

## 1.4 Why GOOSE was Designed

Before IEC 61850, substation protection schemes relied on **hard-wired copper control cables** between panels. This was:
1. **Expensive** (copper + trenching + termination)
2. **Difficult to test** and configure
3. **Inflexible** — every change required re-wiring
4. **Error-prone** — wiring mistakes caused misoperations

GOOSE, combined with **GSE (Generic Substation Event)**, allowed the **same physical Ethernet cable** to carry hundreds of virtual signals, drastically reducing copper usage and improving reliability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GOOSE Message Structure (Logical Model)

A GOOSE message is built as a structured **Application Protocol Data Unit (APDU)** under the GSE control block (GoCB). The structure is encoded using **ASN.1 BER (Abstract Syntax Notation One – Basic Encoding Rules)**.

**Logical Composition of a GOOSE APDU:**

| Field (ASN.1 Tag) | Data Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `gocbRef` (GoCB Reference) | VisibleString | Globally unique identifier of the publisher's control block |
| `timeAllowedtoLive` | INTEGER (ms) | Maximum time before next retransmission expected |
| `datSet` (DataSet Reference) | VisibleString | Reference to the data object class being transmitted |
| `goID` (GOOSE ID) | VisibleString | Application-level identifier of the message |
| `t` (Timestamp) | UTC TIME | Time of event (status change) occurrence |
| `stNum` (Status Number) | INTEGER | Increments on any *status change* in the dataset |
| `sqNum` ( Sequence Number) | INTEGER | Increments on *every transmission* (including retransmissions) |
| `simulation` | BOOLEAN | TRUE if message is from a simulator; IEDs must reject in test |
| `confRev` (Configuration Revision) | INTEGER | Configuration version number — used to detect configuration drift |
| `ndsCom` (Needs Commissioning) | BOOLEAN | Indicates the publisher is in commissioning mode |
| `numDatSetEntries` | INTEGER | Number of data items in the dataset |
| `allData` | ARRAY of Data | The actual data values (Boolean, integer, bit-string, etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-number trick to remember:** `stNum` changes **only on status change**; `sqNum` changes **on every transmission**. A receiving IED identifies a *new event* when `stNum` increments, and identifies a *retransmission* when only `sqNum` increments.

## 2.2 GOOSE Operational Philosophy — Step-by-Step Logic

The GOOSE mechanism follows a **Publisher–Subscriber model** built on **multicast Ethernet frames**. The operational logic can be broken down as follows:

1. **Event Detection:** A protection function inside an IED detects a state change (e.g., trip coil energization, breaker position change).
2. **Encoding:** The IED's **Logical Node (LN)** packs the relevant data (`Trip`, `Close`, `Pos`, `Interlock` status) into a `DataSet`.
3. **Trigger:** The GoCB (GOOSE Control Block) of the publisher is triggered, producing a new GOOSE APDU.
4. **Encoding (ASN.1 BER):** The APDU is encoded into a binary Ethernet frame and sent on the **process bus**.
5. **Initial Burst Transmission (Fastest Retransmission):** The publisher sends a *burst* of frames with progressively doubling intervals starting at the **minimum cycle time $T_{min}$** (typically 4 ms).
6. **Exponential Back-off:** Retransmissions continue with intervals doubling: $4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512 \text{ ms}, \dots$
7. **Steady State:** After the maximum back-off interval, the message is retransmitted at a slow "heartbeat" rate (e.g., every **5000 ms**) to confirm the publisher is still alive.
8. **Reception by Subscribers:** Every IED subscribed to that `gocbRef` and matching `confRev` decodes the message and updates its internal mirror of the data.
9. **Quality Validation:** If `confRev` does not match the subscriber's expected configuration, the message is **discarded** (configuration mismatch protection).
10. **Time-to-Live (TTL) Monitoring:** If a subscriber does not receive a GOOSE within `timeAllowedtoLive` ms after the last `stNum` change, it declares the publisher as **communication-failed** and acts per fallback logic.

## 2.3 GOOSE Retransmission Algorithm — The Exponential Back-off

The retransmission timing follows a deterministic **exponential back-off** algorithm defined in IEC 61850-7-2 (ACSI). The interval between successive retransmissions is given by:

$$
T_n = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)}
$$

Where:
- $T_n$ = Time interval (in ms) between the $(n-1)^{th}$ and the $n^{th}$ retransmission
- $T_{min}$ = Minimum cycle time (typically **4 ms** for type 1A trip messages)
- $n$ = Retransmission index, $n \in \{1, 2, 3, \dots, n_{max}\}$

After reaching the maximum back-off time $T_{max}$, the message continues to be retransmitted at a fixed **heartbeat interval $T_{hb}$**:

$$
T_{hb} \approx T_{max} = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n_{max}-1)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU 3-mark question, you are expected to **write the retransmission sequence** (4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 … ms) and state that the heartbeat continues every 5 seconds.

## 2.4 GOOSE Mapping on the Communication Stack

GOOSE bypasses the **TCP/IP stack** entirely. It uses:

| OSI Layer | Protocol Used for GOOSE | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 (Application) | GSE per IEC 61850-8-1 | GOOSE service definition |
| Layer 6 (Presentation) | ASN.1 BER Encoding | Compact, standardized binary encoding |
| Layer 5 (Session) | None (stateless) | Reduces latency |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | None (uses direct Ethernet) | Eliminates TCP handshake overhead |
| Layer 3 (Network) | None (multicast MAC only) | No IP routing latency |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | IEEE 802.1Q + Priority Tagging | Supports VLAN & QoS priority |
| Layer 1 (Physical) | 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps Ethernet (Fiber) | High noise immunity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KQ priority tagging:** GOOSE uses the **IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tag** with a configurable **Ethernet priority code point (PCP)** — typically set to **4 (or higher)** to ensure Quality of Service (QoS) on switched networks.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Retransmission Interval | $T_n = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)}$ | Time between $(n-1)^{th}$ and $n^{th}$ retransmission |
| Minimum Cycle Time (Type 1A) | $T_{min} = 4 \text{ ms}$ | Trip message minimum retransmission interval |
| Maximum Type 1A Transfer Time | $t_{transfer} \le 4 \text{ ms}$ | End-to-end latency budget |
| GOOSE Frame Format | Multicast 01-0C-CD-01-xx-xx | IEEE 802.1Q multicast MAC prefix for GOOSE |
| Heartbeat (Steady State) | $T_{hb} \approx 5 \text{ s}$ | Sends updates to confirm channel integrity |
| Configuration Mismatch Check | $\text{Receiver.confRev} = \text{Message.confRev}$ | Drop message if not equal (safety feature) |
| Status Change Detection | $\Delta(\text{stNum}) > 0$ | Indicates a new event (not a retransmission) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The exponential back-off is what makes GOOSE robust against transient bus collisions. If a switch momentarily drops a frame, the very next retransmission (within 4–8 ms) ensures the receiver still gets the data without the 4 ms deadline being violated. This is why GOOSE is used as the *digital equivalent* of trip-grade hard-wired logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Latency Across a Process Bus

Let us derive the **worst-case end-to-end transfer time** for a GOOSE trip message going from a Bay Controller (BC) to a Protection IED through a single managed Ethernet switch.

**Given Parameters:**
- Encoding time at publisher: $t_{enc} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$
- Propagation delay in 100 m fiber: $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v \cdot n}$
- Switch processing latency (cut-through): $t_{sw} = 0.4 \text{ ms}$
- Decoding time at subscriber: $t_{dec} = 0.4 \text{ ms}$
- Application processing time: $t_{app} = 1.0 \text{ ms}$

### Step 1 — Compute the fiber propagation delay

The speed of light in fiber $v \cdot n \approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (since refractive index $n \approx 1.5$).

$$
t_{prop} = \frac{100 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.0005 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Logic:** Fiber propagation is **negligible** (sub-microsecond) for substation distances (< 2 km). This confirms that fiber-optic Ethernet is a fast physical medium.

### Step 2 — Total serial latency (sum of all delays in series)

$$
t_{total} = t_{enc} + t_{prop} + t_{sw} + t_{dec} + t_{app}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$
t_{total} = 0.5 + 0.0005 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 1.0
$$

$$
t_{total} = 2.3005 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare against IEC 61850-5 Type 1A budget

$$
t_{total} = 2.3005 \text{ ms} \;\; \ll \;\; 4 \text{ ms (Type 1A budget)}
$$

### Step 5 — Margin calculation

$$
\text{Margin} = 4 \text{ ms} - 2.3005 \text{ ms} = 1.6995 \text{ ms} \approx 1.7 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The 1.7 ms margin is reserved for *store-and-forward* delay under congestion, *spanning tree reconfiguration*, and *priority queueing jitter*. As long as the cumulative delay stays under 4 ms, the **Type 1A trip performance** is satisfied.

$$
\boxed{\;t_{total} = 2.30 \text{ ms} \;\;<\;\; 4 \text{ ms (compliant)}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Retransmissions Within Type 1A Budget

Suppose a protection IED wants to ensure that its trip message is **guaranteed to be received** even if the first transmission is lost. Given:

- Minimum retransmission interval $T_{min} = 4 \text{ ms}$
- Back-off factor $k = 2$

### Step 1 — Recurrence relation

$$
T_n = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)} = 4 \cdot 2^{(n-1)} \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute successive intervals

$$
T_1 = 4 \cdot 2^0 = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_2 = 4 \cdot 2^1 = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_3 = 4 \cdot 2^2 = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_4 = 4 \cdot 2^3 = 32 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_5 = 4 \cdot 2^4 = 64 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Cumulative time after $n$ retransmissions

$$
t_{cum}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = T_{min} \cdot (2^n - 1)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $n$ such that $t_{cum} \le T_{critical}$ (assume $T_{critical} = 100 \text{ ms}$)

$$
T_{min} \cdot (2^n - 1) \le 100
$$

$$
4 \cdot (2^n - 1) \le 100
$$

$$
2^n - 1 \le 25
$$

$$
2^n \le 26
$$

$$
n \le \log_2(26) \approx 4.70
$$

### Step 5 — Largest integer $n$

$$
\boxed{\;n_{max} = 4 \text{ retransmissions within 100 ms}\;}
$$

> **Logic:** This explains why a publisher sends a *burst* of GOOSE frames at the moment of an event. If the subscriber's bus is congested, the second, third, or fourth retransmission (within tens of milliseconds) almost guarantees the message is delivered without violating the **4 ms Type 1A deadline** (since the *first* frame always lands at $t < 4 \text{ ms}$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: GOOSE vs. Other IEC 61850 Services

| Property | GOOSE | GSSE (Legacy UCA2) | MMS (Client/Server) | Sampled Values (SV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transfer Time Class | Type 1A (4 ms) | Type 1B (10 ms) | Type 4 / 5 (≥ 1000 ms) | Continuous (Type 4) |
| Transport | Direct Ethernet | Direct Ethernet | TCP/IP | Direct Ethernet |
| Encoding | ASN.1 BER | ASN.1 BER | ASN.1 BER | ASN.1 BER |
| Pattern | Publish/Subscribe | Publish/Subscribe | Client/Server | Publish/Subscribe |
| Primary Use | Trip/Interlock | Annunciator Alarms | Reports, Control | CT/VT digitized streams |
| Reliability Mechanism | Rapid retransmit | Rapid retransmit | TCP Retransmit | Reserved bandwidth |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: GOOSE Publisher–Subscriber Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PB[Process Bus 100 Mbps Ethernet]
        ETH((Ethernet Switch VLAN-id 1 Priority 4))
    end

    PUB[IED Publisher: Bay Controller BC-1]
    SUB1[IED Subscriber: Protection Relay PR-1]
    SUB2[IED Subscriber: Breaker IED BR-2]
    SUB3[IED Subscriber: SCADA Gateway GW-3]

    EVT((Trip Event Detected: Breaker Open))
    APDU[GOOSE APDU ASN.1 BER Encoded]

    EVT --> PUB
    PUB --> APDU
    APDU --> ETH
    ETH --> SUB1
    ETH --> SUB2
    ETH --> SUB3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A single event triggers the publisher, which multicasts the encoded APDU onto the process bus. Every subscribed IED subscribed to the same `gocbRef` decodes the frame and updates its internal mirror of the data. The Ethernet switch treats the frame with high priority (PCP 4 or higher).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: GOOSE Retransmission Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title GOOSE Burst Retransmission Cadence (Tmin = 4 ms)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %S ms

    section Initial Burst
    Frame 1 (stNum=5, sqNum=1) :a1, 0, 4ms
    Frame 2 (stNum=5, sqNum=2) :a2, 4, 8ms
    Frame 3 (stNum=5, sqNum=3) :a3, 12, 16ms
    Frame 4 (stNum=5, sqNum=4) :a4, 28, 32ms

    section Steady State
    Heartbeat Frame :crit, 1000, 4ms
    Heartbeat Frame Next :crit, 5000, 4ms
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The interval between frames doubles ($4, 8, 16, 32, \dots$ ms) until a steady "heartbeat" cadence is reached (typically every 5 seconds). The `stNum` is constant (since no new event) but `sqNum` keeps incrementing.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: GOOSE Frame on the OSI Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Layer: GSE Control Block GoCB] --> B[Presentation Layer: ASN.1 BER Encoding]
    B --> C[Data Link Layer: IEEE 802.1Q Tagged Frame Multicast MAC 01-0C-CD-01-xx-xx]
    C --> D[Physical Layer: 100BASE-FX / 1000BASE-LX Optical Fiber]
    D --> E[Substation Process Bus]
    E --> F[Subscriber IED Decoding]
    F --> G[Logic Discrimination: Trip Decision within 4 ms]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Configuration Mismatch & Quality Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R([Receive GOOSE Frame]) --> Q1{Does confRev match?}
    Q1 -- No --> DROP[Discard Frame: Configuration Mismatch Log Warning]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is ndsCom TRUE?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> DROP2[Discard: Publisher in Commissioning]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is simulation TRUE and IED in service?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> DROP3[Discard: Simulated Message Not Allowed]
    Q3 -- No --> ACC[Accept Frame: Update stNum, sqNum, t, allData]
    ACC --> TTL[Reset Time-to-Live Timer]
    TTL --> M[Mirror Data to Internal Logical Nodes]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Subscribers perform *four* safety checks before accepting a GOOSE message. This is what makes GOOSE **SIL-grade (Safety Integrity Level)** suitable for protection applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define GOOSE in the context of IEC 61850. List any two advantages of using GOOSE over traditional hard-wired interconnections in substation automation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
GOOSE (Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event) is a fast, peer-to-peer, multicast communication service defined in IEC 61850-8-1 used to exchange time-critical event-driven information (e.g., trip commands, breaker status, interlocking) between IEDs over an Ethernet substation bus.

**Two advantages over hard-wired cabling:**

1. **Drastic reduction in copper cabling:** A single Ethernet fiber can carry hundreds of virtual binary signals, replacing kilometers of control cable. This reduces cost, panel space, and installation time.
2. **Flexibility & reconfigurability:** Logic changes are made in software (configuration tools like IEDScout, PCM600) without re-wiring the panel. Engineers can re-route trip logic during commissioning using just a configuration file.
3. *Self-supervision:* GOOSE heartbeat frames continuously verify communication integrity, unlike a hard-wired contact whose disconnection might not be detected.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for two advantages. Mentioning 1 advantage + 1 supporting sentence = full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the role of `stNum` and `sqNum` in a GOOSE message. How does a receiving IED use these numbers to distinguish between a new event and a retransmission?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **`stNum` (Status Number):** Increments by 1 whenever a *status change* occurs in the data set being transmitted (e.g., a trip command is issued). It identifies *new events*.
- **`sqNum` (Sequence Number):** Increments by 1 on *every* transmission — including retransmissions of the same event.

**How an IED distinguishes:**
- If the incoming `stNum` is **higher** than the previously stored `stNum`, the IED recognizes a **new event** and updates its data mirror.
- If the incoming `stNum` is **equal** to the stored value but `sqNum` has increased, the IED recognizes a **retransmission** and does *not* re-trigger downstream logic (avoiding duplicate trips).

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark stNum meaning + 1 mark sqNum meaning + 1 mark for the new-vs-retransmission logic.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *(a)* Explain the GOOSE message structure as defined by IEC 61850-8-1. With a neat diagram, describe the publisher–subscriber model and the burst retransmission algorithm. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A digital substation has a protection IED and a breaker IED connected through a 100 Mbps managed Ethernet switch. The end-to-end delays are: encoding = 0.6 ms, propagation in 200 m of fiber = negligible, switch latency = 0.5 ms, decoding = 0.5 ms, application processing = 1.2 ms. Verify whether the system meets the **Type 1A performance class** (≤ 4 ms) and compute the **safety margin**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**1. GOOSE APDU Structure (4 marks):**
The GOOSE Application Protocol Data Unit (APDU) is encoded using ASN.1 BER and contains the following fields (as discussed in Section 2.1):

```
gocbRef | timeAllowedtoLive | datSet | goID | t | stNum |
sqNum | simulation | confRev | ndsCom | numDatSetEntries | allData[]
```

A neat block diagram representing the GOOSE message:

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|   Ethernet Header (with 802.1Q Tag, PCP = 4)             |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|   gocbRef       (Publisher's unique reference)           |
|   timeAllowedtoLive (TTL for subscribers)                |
|   datSet        (Data set name being transmitted)        |
|   goID          (Application-level ID)                   |
|   t             (UTC timestamp of event)                 |
|   stNum         (Status number – increments on change)   |
|   sqNum         (Sequence number – increments per tx)    |
|   simulation    (BOOLEAN)                                |
|   confRev       (Configuration version)                  |
|   ndsCom        (Needs commissioning flag)               |
|   numDatSetEntries                                      |
|   allData[]     (The actual data values)                 |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

**2. Publisher–Subscriber Model (2 marks):**
- **Publisher:** A single IED (e.g., Bay Controller) owns a GoCB and pushes a multicast Ethernet frame onto the process bus.
- **Subscribers:** Multiple IEDs listen to the multicast MAC address and decode frames matching their subscribed `gocbRef` and `confRev`.
- The model is *stateless* — there is no handshake, no acknowledgement, and no TCP session.

**3. Burst Retransmission Algorithm (1 mark):**
After an event, the publisher retransmits the same GOOSE frame at intervals $T_n = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)}$ — typically **4, 8, 16, 32, 64 … ms** — before settling to a heartbeat (e.g., 5 s). Only `sqNum` changes on retransmission; `stNum` remains constant.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $t_{enc} = 0.6$ ms
- $t_{prop} \approx 0$ ms (negligible for 200 m fiber)
- $t_{sw} = 0.5$ ms
- $t_{dec} = 0.5$ ms
- $t_{app} = 1.2$ ms

**Step 1 — Sum the latencies in series**

$$
t_{total} = 0.6 + 0 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 1.2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute**

$$
t_{total} = 2.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Type 1A budget**

$$
t_{total} = 2.8 \text{ ms} \;\; < \;\; 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute safety margin**

$$
\text{Margin} = 4.0 - 2.8 = 1.2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

The system **meets** the Type 1A performance class with a **1.2 ms safety margin**. This margin can absorb additional jitter, retransmission re-tries, or short-term network congestion without violating the trip-grade deadline.

$$
\boxed{\;t_{total} = 2.8 \text{ ms} \;\;\le \;\; 4 \text{ ms (Compliant, Margin} = 1.2 \text{ ms)}\;}
$$

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark listing given values + 1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark final answer + 1 mark comparison + 1 mark margin + 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *(a)* With a clear block diagram, illustrate how GOOSE bypasses the TCP/IP stack and maps directly onto the Ethernet (OSI Layer 2). Mention the role of IEEE 802.1Q VLAN priority tagging. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **GOOSE retransmission algorithm** with the help of a timing diagram. If $T_{min} = 4$ ms and 5 retransmissions occur, calculate the **cumulative time** consumed by the burst, and explain why this redundancy is essential for protection-grade reliability. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**1. Mapping below the IP Layer (4 marks):**

GOOSE deliberately **bypasses the TCP/IP stack** (Layers 3 and 4) to eliminate handshake, retransmission, and routing latency. The mapping is:

| OSI Layer | GOOSE Protocol |
|---|---|
| Layer 7 — Application | GSE Service (IEC 61850-8-1) |
| Layer 6 — Presentation | ASN.1 BER Encoding |
| Layer 5 — Session | None (stateless) |
| Layer 4 — Transport | None (no TCP/UDP) |
| Layer 3 — Network | None (no IP, no routing) |
| Layer 2 — Data Link | **IEEE 802.1Q with priority tag + Multicast MAC** |
| Layer 1 — Physical | 100BASE-FX / 1000BASE-LX optical fiber |

**Block Diagram:**

```
+--------------------------------+
|  GSE (IEC 61850-8-1)           |  Application
+--------------------------------+
|  ASN.1 BER Encoding            |  Presentation
+--------------------------------+
|  IEEE 802.1Q Frame             |  Data Link (no IP, no TCP)
+--------------------------------+
|  100BASE-FX Optical Fiber      |  Physical
+--------------------------------+
```

**2. Role of IEEE 802.1Q VLAN Priority Tagging (3 marks):**
- The GOOSE frame is an IEEE 802.1Q tagged frame. The **4-byte VLAN tag** contains a **3-bit Priority Code Point (PCP)** field.
- GOOSE frames are tagged with a **high PCP value (typically 4, 5, 6, or 7)**. This instructs all managed switches in the path to place the GOOSE frame in a **high-priority egress queue**.
- When congestion occurs, lower-priority traffic (e.g., MMS reports) is **dropped or delayed first**, ensuring GOOSE retains its **< 4 ms latency**.
- The tag also includes a **VLAN Identifier (VID)** that segregates the protection traffic from general substation traffic, providing *logical* isolation on a shared physical network.

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks mapping table/diagram + 2 marks explanation of bypass + 2 marks explanation of PCP/priority + 1 mark for VLAN isolation.)*

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**1. Description of the Retransmission Algorithm (3 marks):**

After an event (status change), the publisher sends a **burst of GOOSE frames** to maximize the probability of delivery across the bus. The interval between consecutive retransmissions follows an **exponential back-off**:

$$
T_n = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)}
$$

After the maximum back-off, a slow **heartbeat** is sent every ~5 s to confirm publisher liveness.

**Timing Diagram:**

```
Event detected at t = 0 ms
|---4 ms---|---8 ms---|---16 ms---|---32 ms---|---64 ms---| ... -> heartbeat
  F1         F2         F3          F4          F5
  (sqNum=1)  (sqNum=2)  (sqNum=3)   (sqNum=4)   (sqNum=5)
  stNum = 5  stNum = 5  stNum = 5   stNum = 5   stNum = 5
```

**2. Calculation of cumulative time (3 marks):**

For $T_{min} = 4$ ms and $n = 5$ retransmissions:

$$
t_{cum} = \sum_{n=1}^{5} T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)} = T_{min} \cdot (2^5 - 1)
$$

$$
t_{cum} = 4 \cdot (32 - 1) = 4 \cdot 31 = 124 \text{ ms}
$$

**3. Why this redundancy is essential (1 mark):**

In protection-grade applications, **missing a trip command is unacceptable**. The burst ensures that even if the *first* frame is lost due to bus collision or switch congestion, the second, third, fourth, or fifth transmission will succeed — *all within well under the critical fault clearing time* (typically 80–100 ms for HV systems). The receiver's `sqNum` logic prevents duplicate trip execution, while the rapid cadence guarantees the **first** frame still lands at the receiver well within the **4 ms Type 1A deadline**.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark timing diagram + 1 mark formula + 1 mark calculation + 1 mark cumulative time + 1 mark explanation of redundancy + 1 mark explanation of sqNum duplicate suppression + 1 mark connection to fault clearing time.)*

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in GOOSE questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `stNum` and `sqNum`.** Examiners often award 1 mark each for correct *meaning* and another 1 mark for the *new vs retransmission distinction*. Writing the correct definition but swapping their roles costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the burst retransmission sequence.** You must write the actual sequence — **4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 … ms** — not just "fast retransmission." Examiners specifically look for the doubling pattern.
> 3. **Writing "GOOSE uses TCP/IP."** This is the **single most common error** and will cost a full 2-mark component in any Part A question. GOOSE deliberately *bypasses* the TCP/IP stack. If you write TCP/IP, you are describing MMS reporting, not GOOSE.
> 4. **Omitting the 802.1Q priority tag.** Mentioning VLAN tagging is mandatory for full marks on any question about GOOSE QoS.
> 5. **Skipping the `confRev` (configuration revision) field.** Examiners expect at least one mention of how subscribers reject frames when their configuration does not match the publisher's — this is a safety check, not an optional feature.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GOOSE = Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event**, defined in **IEC 61850-8-1**.
- GOOSE is a **high-speed, peer-to-peer, multicast, publish–subscribe** service for time-critical protection data.
- It **bypasses the TCP/IP stack** and operates directly on **IEEE 802.1Q Ethernet** (Layer 2).
- GOOSE messages are encoded using **ASN.1 BER** and include the fields: `gocbRef`, `timeAllowedtoLive`, `datSet`, `goID`, `t`, `stNum`, `sqNum`, `simulation`, `confRev`, `ndsCom`, `numDatSetEntries`, `allData[]`.
- **Type 1A (Trip)** transfer time $\le \mathbf{4 \text{ ms}}$. Type 1 $\le$ 10 ms. Type 2 $\le$ 100 ms. Type 3 $\le$ 500 ms.
- **Burst retransmission** uses **exponential back-off**: $T_n = T_{min} \cdot 2^{(n-1)}$ starting from $T_{min} = 4$ ms, settling to a **5 s heartbeat**.
- **`stNum` = new event; `sqNum` = every transmission.** This dual-counter scheme prevents duplicate tripping.
- **`confRev` mismatch → frame discarded.** This is the configuration safety check.
- **IEEE 802.1Q Priority Tag (PCP ≥ 4)** is used to prioritize GOOSE frames in managed switches (QoS).
- **Multicast MAC** for GOOSE uses the IEEE-administered prefix **01-0C-CD-01-xx-xx** (Ethertype 0x88B8).
- **Time-to-Live (`timeAllowedtoLive`):** If the subscriber does not receive a fresh GOOSE within this window, it declares the publisher **communication-failed** and applies fallback logic.
- **Safety standards:** GOOSE is engineered for **SIL-3 / IEC 61508** protection applications, replacing hard-wired trip logic.
- **Difference from Sampled Values (SV):** SV carries *continuous* streams of digitized CT/VT measurements; GOOSE carries *event-driven* binary/analog status. Both bypass TCP/IP but SV uses a different Ethertype (0x88BA).
- **Real-world deployment:** Used in greenfield digital substations and retrofit brownfield projects worldwide; reference vendors include ABB, Siemens, GE, SEL, and Schneider with IEC 61850-3-certified IEDs and process-bus switches (e.g., RuggedCom, Hirschmann).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substation Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Substation Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Substation Model** is a mathematically and topologically structured representation of an electrical substation that captures its physical components (transformers, busbars, circuit breakers, isolators, instrument transformers, protection relays, and compensation devices), their electrical interconnections, the operational states of switching devices, and the communication/automation hierarchy that governs it. In the context of **Smart Grid Technologies**, the substation model extends beyond pure electro-mechanical representation to incorporate **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)**, **SCADA integration**, **IEC 61850 communication protocols**, and **phasor measurement data**, making the substation a fully cyber-physical node in the wider smart grid architecture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Module 1 of PEEET861), the substation model is treated as:
1. A **graph-theoretic / nodal model** for power-flow and short-circuit studies.
2. A **functional model** describing the hierarchical levels of substation automation (Process, Bay, Station, and Enterprise levels).
3. A **communication model** based on IEC 61850 abstract data modeling (Logical Nodes, Data Objects, and IID files).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PEEET861 expects students to understand the substation as a controllable, observable, and cyber-secured node. You must be able to draw the **single-line diagram**, identify **equipment rating**, and explain **substation automation architecture**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an electrical substation as the **"traffic intersection of a city's power highway"**:

- **High-voltage transmission lines** are like expressways carrying bulk traffic (electricity).
- The **substation** is a sophisticated traffic junction that **receives, redirects, steps down, and regulates** the power flow.
- **Transformers** are like elevators, lifting voltage down to safer usable levels.
- **Circuit breakers and switches** are the traffic signals — they can stop, reroute, or isolate faulty sections.
- **Protection relays and IEDs** are the traffic police — constantly monitoring, taking decisions in milliseconds, and sending signals.
- **SCADA and IEC 61850 communication** is the central control room that watches the entire intersection through CCTV cameras and remote sensors.
- In a **Smart Grid**, this traffic intersection becomes *intelligent* — it can predict traffic jams (overloads), communicate with other intersections (neighbouring substations), and even self-heal by rerouting power after a fault.

> [!NOTE]
> **Just as a smart traffic system reduces congestion and improves safety, a smart substation reduces losses, improves reliability, enables faster fault isolation, and supports bidirectional power flow from distributed energy resources (DERs).**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Standard / Metric | Value / Reference |
|---|---|
| **Standard Nominal Voltages** | **11 kV / 33 kV / 66 kV / 132 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV** |
| **Standard Frequency** | **50 Hz** (India), 60 Hz (USA) |
| **Base Power (S_base)** | **100 MVA** (typical for p.u. calculations) |
| **Short-circuit MVA** | Used to define bus fault levels |
| **IEC 61850 Edition** | Edition 2.1 / Edition 3.0 |
| **SCADA Latency** | ~1–2 seconds for control commands |
| **GOOSE Message Latency** | < 4 ms (Type 1A) |
| **Standard Lightning Impulse** | As per IEC 60060 |
| **Copper Conductivity** | **5.8 × 10⁷ S/m** |
| **Earth Resistivity (typical)** | **100 Ω·m** (clay, moist soil) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For all per-unit (p.u.) calculations in substation modelling, **S_base = 100 MVA** is the universally adopted convention in Indian power-system textbooks. Always convert equipment ratings to this base before any analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Modelling a Substation is Essential

A substation is not just a collection of hardware; it is a **multi-port electromechanical network** that interacts with transmission lines, loads, generators, and other substations. To perform:

- **Load flow analysis (Newton-Raphson, Gauss-Seidel)**
- **Short-circuit analysis (symmetrical components)**
- **Transient stability studies**
- **Optimal power dispatch**
- **State estimation in SCADA**

…we need a mathematically tractable model. The substation model reduces hundreds of components into an **admittance matrix (Y-bus)**, **impedance matrix (Z-bus)**, or **graph-theoretic adjacency structure** that a computer can solve efficiently.

## 2.2 Building Blocks of a Substation Model

A complete substation model comprises the following sub-models, each representing one functional layer:

1. **Topological Model (Graph):** Represents buses as nodes and lines/transformers as edges with switching states.
2. **Electrical Model (Network Equations):** Y-bus or Z-bus representation of the linear network.
3. **Equipment Model:** Transformer (π or T equivalent), Transmission line (π model, distributed parameter), Loads (ZIP model), Generators (Thevenin equivalent).
4. **Switching Model:** Circuit breaker, isolator, earth-switch represented as binary variables (OPEN = 0, CLOSED = 1).
5. **Protection Model:** Distance relay, overcurrent relay, differential relay with operating characteristics.
6. **Communication Model:** IEC 61850 Logical Nodes (LLN0, LPHD, XCBR, XSWI, MMXU, PDIF, PTOC, etc.).
7. **Automation Hierarchy Model:** Process → Bay → Station → Enterprise levels.

## 2.3 Single-Line Diagram (SLD) Conventions

In a substation single-line diagram (SLD), three-phase components are represented by single lines with standardized symbols:

- **Bus bar:** A thick horizontal line (single or double bus).
- **Power transformer:** Two overlapping circles with tap-changer symbol.
- **Circuit breaker:** A square with an X-cross or standard breaker symbol.
- **Isolator:** A knife-switch symbol.
- **Current Transformer (CT):** A two-circle symbol on the line.
- **Potential Transformer (PT):** A two-circle symbol connected between line and ground.
- **Lightning arrester:** A circle with a downward arrow.
- **Capacitor bank:** Two parallel lines representing plates.
- **Reactor:** A coil symbol.

## 2.4 Substation Topology Types

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Bus** | One main busbar; simple, low cost | Small 11/33 kV substations |
| **Double Bus** | Two busbars with bus coupler | Major EHV substations |
| **Main & Transfer Bus** | One main, one standby | 132 kV / 220 kV |
| **1 ½ Breaker Scheme** | Three breakers per 2 circuits | 400 kV, 765 kV (high reliability) |
| **Ring Main** | Looped busbars | 33 kV distribution |
| **Mesh Substation** | Mesh-connected breakers | Large generating stations |

## 2.5 Per-Unit System in Substation Modelling

The per-unit (p.u.) system normalises all quantities to a common base, eliminating the need to refer impedances across multiple voltage levels.

$$Z_{p.u.,new} = Z_{p.u.,old} \times \left( \frac{V_{base,old}}{V_{base,new}} \right)^{2} \times \left( \frac{S_{base,new}}{S_{base,old}} \right)$$

**Base Quantities:**

$$S_{base} = 100 \text{ MVA (assumed)}$$

$$V_{base} = \text{line-to-line nominal voltage at the bus}$$

$$I_{base} = \frac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{base}}$$

$$Z_{base} = \frac{(V_{base})^{2}}{S_{base}}$$

## 2.6 Symmetrical Component Model for Fault Analysis

During unsymmetrical faults (SLG, LL, LLG), the substation model is analysed using **Fortescue's symmetrical components**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_a \\ V_b \\ V_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a^{2} & a \\ 1 & a & a^{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_0 \\ V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $a = 1\angle 120°$ is the Fortescue operator.

The sequence networks (zero, positive, negative) are connected in specific ways depending on fault type:
- **SLG fault:** Series connection of all three sequence networks.
- **LL fault:** Parallel connection of positive and negative networks.
- **DLG fault:** All three in parallel.

## 2.7 Admittance Matrix (Y-bus) Formation

The Y-bus is the cornerstone of substation and power-system modelling:

$$Y_{bus} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} & \cdots & Y_{1n} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} & \cdots & Y_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ Y_{n1} & Y_{n2} & \cdots & Y_{nn} \end{bmatrix}$$

- **Diagonal element** $Y_{ii}$ = sum of all admittances connected to bus $i$.
- **Off-diagonal element** $Y_{ij}$ = negative of admittance between buses $i$ and $j$.

The power-flow equation at each bus:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} \cdot V_j$$

In polar form:

$$P_i + jQ_i = V_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}^{*} \cdot V_{j}^{*}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This nonlinear equation is solved iteratively using Newton-Raphson or Fast Decoupled Load Flow methods, which form the computational backbone of any substation model embedded in a larger grid.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_{base} = \dfrac{(kV_{base})^{2}}{MVA_{base}}$ | Base impedance | Ω |
| 2 | $I_{base} = \dfrac{MVA_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot kV_{base}}$ | Base current | kA |
| 3 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{prefault}}{Z_{th}}$ | Short-circuit current | kA |
| 4 | $MVA_{sc} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{sc}$ | Short-circuit MVA | MVA |
| 5 | $V_{p.u.,new} = V_{p.u.,old} \cdot \dfrac{V_{old}}{V_{new}}$ | Voltage p.u. conversion | — |
| 6 | $Y_{ii} = \sum_{k \neq i} y_{ik}$ | Y-bus diagonal entry | p.u. |
| 7 | $Y_{ij} = -y_{ij}$ | Y-bus off-diagonal | p.u. |
| 8 | $a = 1 \angle 120°$ | Fortescue operator | — |
| 9 | $P_i = \sum_{j} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert Y_{ij} \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j - \delta_{ij})$ | Real power injection | MW |
| 10 | $Q_i = \sum_{j} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert Y_{ij} \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j - \delta_{ij})$ | Reactive power injection | MVAR |
| 11 | $X/R \text{ ratio} = \tan(\phi)$ | Determines DC offset | — |
| 12 | $Z_{0}, Z_{1}, Z_{2}$ | Sequence impedances | p.u. |
| 13 | $V_{res} = \dfrac{V_0}{3R_f + Z_0 + Z_1 + Z_2}$ | SLG fault voltage | p.u. |
| 14 | $\Delta P / \Delta V$ | Voltage sensitivity | p.u./p.u. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Substation models are used by **Power System Operators (POSOCO in India)** for real-time contingency analysis, by **TRANSCO/DISCOMs** for planning augmentation, by **protection engineers** for relay coordination, and by **smart-grid researchers** for integrating DERs and microgrids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per-Unit Impedance Conversion Across a Transformer

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase transformer rated **15 MVA, 11 kV / 132 kV** has an impedance of **10%** on its own rating. Calculate its per-unit impedance on a **100 MVA, 11 kV** base (LV side) and **132 kV** base (HV side). Compute the equivalent impedance in ohms referred to both sides.

### Step 1 — Identify the Equipment Rating (Old Base)

$$S_{old} = 15 \text{ MVA}, \quad Z_{old,p.u.} = 0.10$$

### Step 2 — Identify the System Base (New Base)

$$S_{new} = 100 \text{ MVA}, \quad V_{base,LV} = 11 \text{ kV}, \quad V_{base,HV} = 132 \text{ kV}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Per-Unit Conversion Formula

$$Z_{p.u.,new} = Z_{p.u.,old} \times \frac{S_{new}}{S_{old}}$$

$$Z_{p.u.,new} = 0.10 \times \frac{100}{15} = 0.10 \times 6.6667 = 0.6667 \text{ p.u.}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Base Impedances on Each Side

Base impedance on LV side:

$$Z_{base,LV} = \frac{(V_{base,LV})^{2}}{S_{new}} = \frac{(11)^{2}}{100} = \frac{121}{100} = 1.21 \text{ Ω}$$

Base impedance on HV side:

$$Z_{base,HV} = \frac{(V_{base,HV})^{2}}{S_{new}} = \frac{(132)^{2}}{100} = \frac{17424}{100} = 174.24 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Equivalent Ohmic Impedances

On LV side:

$$Z_{LV} = Z_{p.u.,new} \times Z_{base,LV} = 0.6667 \times 1.21 = 0.8067 \text{ Ω}$$

On HV side:

$$Z_{HV} = Z_{p.u.,new} \times Z_{base,HV} = 0.6667 \times 174.24 = 116.16 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 6 — Verify Turns-Ratio Relationship

The ratio of HV to LV impedance should equal the square of the turns ratio:

$$\frac{Z_{HV}}{Z_{LV}} = \frac{116.16}{0.8067} \approx 144$$

$$\left( \frac{132}{11} \right)^{2} = (12)^{2} = 144 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The same physical transformer shows the *same* p.u. impedance regardless of the reference voltage level, but the *ohmic* impedance changes by the square of the turns ratio. This is the elegant property exploited in p.u. system analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Y-Bus Formation for a 3-Bus Substation

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a substation with 3 buses connected by three lines:

- Line 1–2: impedance $Z_{12} = j0.2$ p.u.
- Line 2–3: impedance $Z_{23} = j0.25$ p.u.
- Line 1–3: impedance $Z_{13} = j0.4$ p.u.

Generator at bus 1, transformer between bus 1–3, load at bus 2. Form the Y-bus matrix.

### Step 1 — Compute Line Admittances

$$y_{12} = \frac{1}{Z_{12}} = \frac{1}{j0.2} = -j5.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

$$y_{23} = \frac{1}{Z_{23}} = \frac{1}{j0.25} = -j4.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

$$y_{13} = \frac{1}{Z_{13}} = \frac{1}{j0.4} = -j2.5 \text{ p.u.}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Diagonal Elements

Each diagonal element is the sum of all admittances connected to that bus.

For bus 1:

$$Y_{11} = y_{12} + y_{13} = -j5.0 + (-j2.5) = -j7.5 \text{ p.u.}$$

For bus 2:

$$Y_{22} = y_{12} + y_{23} = -j5.0 + (-j4.0) = -j9.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

For bus 3:

$$Y_{33} = y_{13} + y_{23} = -j2.5 + (-j4.0) = -j6.5 \text{ p.u.}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Off-Diagonal Elements

Each off-diagonal element equals the negative of the admittance directly connecting the two buses.

$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -y_{12} = -(-j5.0) = +j5.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

$$Y_{13} = Y_{31} = -y_{13} = -(-j2.5) = +j2.5 \text{ p.u.}$$

$$Y_{23} = Y_{32} = -y_{23} = -(-j4.0) = +j4.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Y-Bus Matrix

$$Y_{bus} = \begin{bmatrix} -j7.5 & +j5.0 & +j2.5 \\ +j5.0 & -j9.0 & +j4.0 \\ +j2.5 & +j4.0 & -j6.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ p.u.}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Symmetry and Row Sum

The Y-bus must be symmetric: $Y_{ij} = Y_{ji}$ — confirmed.

Row sum of each row = total injected current if all voltages = 1 p.u.:

- Row 1 sum: $-j7.5 + j5.0 + j2.5 = 0$ (no shunt at bus 1) ✓
- Row 2 sum: $j5.0 - j9.0 + j4.0 = 0$ ✓
- Row 3 sum: $j2.5 + j4.0 - j6.5 = 0$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This Y-bus is the mathematical backbone for the Newton-Raphson load-flow solver that determines the voltage magnitudes and angles at every busbar in the substation — information crucial for VAR compensation, transformer tap-setting, and outage planning.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Single Line-to-Ground (SLG) Fault Current

**Problem Statement:**
A generator rated **50 MVA, 11 kV** with $X_1 = 0.2$ p.u., $X_2 = 0.15$ p.u., $X_0 = 0.05$ p.u., is connected to a substation through a transformer of $X_T = 0.1$ p.u. (all on 50 MVA base). Compute the SLG fault current at the substation bus if the neutral is solidly grounded (no fault impedance).

### Step 1 — Convert All to Common Base (Assume 50 MVA, already on base)

$$Z_1 = j(0.2 + 0.1) = j0.3 \text{ p.u. (positive sequence)}$$

$$Z_2 = j0.15 \text{ p.u. (negative sequence)}$$

$$Z_0 = j0.05 \text{ p.u. (zero sequence)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Fault Impedance for SLG

For SLG fault, sequence networks are connected in **series**:

$$Z_{eq} = Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 = j0.3 + j0.15 + j0.05 = j0.5 \text{ p.u.}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Fault Current in Per-Unit

Prefault voltage is assumed to be 1.0 p.u.

$$I_{f,p.u.} = \frac{3 \cdot V_{prefault}}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0} = \frac{3 \times 1.0}{j0.5} = \frac{3}{j0.5} = -j6.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

Magnitude: $\vert I_f \vert = 6.0$ p.u.

### Step 4 — Compute Base Current at Substation Bus

$$I_{base} = \frac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{base}} = \frac{50 \text{ MVA}}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \text{ kV}} = \frac{50}{19.052} = 2.624 \text{ kA}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Fault Current in kA

$$I_f = 6.0 \times 2.624 = 15.74 \text{ kA}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Fault MVA

$$MVA_{sc} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_f = \sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 15.74 = 299.86 \text{ MVA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The factor of 3 in step 3 arises because in SLG faults, current flows through all three sequence networks in series, but only phase-A current is the actual physical current. The other two phases (B and C) carry zero current during the SLG condition. This is a high-yield KTU concept frequently tested.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Substation Models Across Voltage Levels

| Voltage Level | Substation Type | Typical Topology | Key Equipment | Communication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **11 kV** | Distribution SS | Single Bus, Ring Main | 11/0.433 kV DT, OC relays | Basic SCADA / RTU |
| **33 kV** | Primary Distribution | Single Bus / Double Bus | 33/11 kV transformer, isolators | Modbus / DNP3 |
| **66 kV** | Secondary Transmission | Double Bus | 66/11 kV or 66/33 kV | IEC 60870-5-104 |
| **132 kV** | EHV Sub-transmission | Main & Transfer Bus | 132/33 kV ICTs, distance relays | IEC 61850 (MMS, GOOSE) |
| **220 kV** | EHV Transmission | Double Bus / 1½ CB | 220/132 kV ICT, bus reactors | IEC 61850, PMUs |
| **400 kV** | EHV Main Transmission | 1½ Breaker Scheme | 400/220 kV ICT, line reactors | IEC 61850, WAMS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
