# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Substation Topology** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** With a neat diagram, explain the **1½ breaker scheme** used in 400 kV substations. Why is it preferred for EHV applications?

**Model Answer:**

The **1½ (one-and-a-half) breaker scheme** uses three circuit breakers for two incoming/outgoing circuits, providing two independent paths for each feeder through two breakers in series, with a middle breaker common to both.

**Key Features:**
- Two main buses (Bus A and Bus B).
- Three breakers per two circuits: e.g., for circuits L1 and L2, breakers B1, B2, B3 are used where B1 is on L1 side, B3 is on L2 side, B2 is the middle (½) breaker shared.
- Any breaker can be removed for maintenance without interrupting either circuit.

**Advantages (EHV suitability):**
- **Maximum reliability** — fault on one breaker does not interrupt any feeder.
- **Flexible operation** — any breaker or bus can be taken out without load shedding.
- **Reduced outage** — 100% selectivity.
- **Preferred for 400 kV and above** in India where reliability outweighs higher cost.

> [!NOTE]
> 3-mark answers expect: neat diagram (1 mark), description (1 mark), reason for EHV suitability (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2: IEC 61850 Standard** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** List the **four levels of substation automation architecture** as per IEC 61850 and state the function of each level.

**Model Answer:**

| Level | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Process Level** | Direct interface with primary equipment (CTs, PTs, breakers, isolators); performs acquisition of analogue and digital signals. |
| **2. Bay Level** | Bay-level IEDs perform protection, control, and monitoring for a single bay (e.g., one transformer or one feeder). |
| **3. Station Level** | Substation automation server, HMI, gateway; performs coordinated control, event recording, and inter-bay logic. |
| **4. Enterprise / Network Level** | Control centre SCADA, EMS, DMS; provides remote supervision, dispatch, and grid-level optimisation. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat single-line diagram, explain the layout of a **220/66 kV substation** with **double busbar** arrangement. List the major equipment and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **per-unit system** used in substation modelling. A 3-phase transformer rated **20 MVA, 66 kV / 11 kV** has impedance of **8%**. Calculate its per-unit impedance on a **50 MVA base**. Also calculate the ohmic impedance referred to both HV and LV sides. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 220/66 kV Substation Layout

**Single-Line Diagram Description:**

A 220/66 kV substation with double busbar arrangement consists of:

1. **Two main busbars (Bus-A and Bus-B)** with a **bus coupler** circuit breaker connecting them.
2. **220 kV incoming lines** (typically 2–4) terminated through **line bays** containing CTs, PTs, isolators, and circuit breakers.
3. **Power Transformers (ICTs)** — usually 2 or 3 units of 100–160 MVA, 220/66 kV.
4. **66 kV outgoing feeders** (4 to 8) for distribution.
5. **Bus reactors / Line reactors** for reactive power control.
6. **Lightning arresters** at line entries.
7. **Station earthing** grid.

**Major Equipment and Functions:**

| Equipment | Function |
|---|---|
| Busbars (A & B) | Collect and distribute power; redundancy via bus coupler |
| Bus Coupler Breaker | Ties two busbars; allows transfer without outage |
| Circuit Breaker | Make/break load and fault currents |
| Isolators | Provide visible isolation for maintenance |
| CT | Step down current for metering/protection |
| PT (CVT) | Step down voltage for metering/protection |
| Power Transformer | Change voltage level (220 kV ↔ 66 kV) |
| Lightning Arrester | Divert lightning surges to earth |
| Protective Relays / IEDs | Detect faults and trip breakers |
| SCADA / SAS | Remote monitoring and control |

#### Part (b) — Per-Unit System

**Definition:**
The per-unit (p.u.) system expresses any electrical quantity (voltage, current, power, impedance) as a fraction of a chosen base value. It eliminates the need to refer quantities across different voltage levels in a multi-voltage substation.

**Old Base (Equipment Rating):**
$$S_{old} = 20 \text{ MVA}, \quad Z_{old,p.u.} = 0.08$$

**New Base (System):**
$$S_{new} = 50 \text{ MVA}, \quad V_{HV} = 66 \text{ kV}, \quad V_{LV} = 11 \text{ kV}$$

**Per-Unit Conversion:**
$$Z_{p.u.,new} = Z_{p.u.,old} \times \frac{S_{new}}{S_{old}} = 0.08 \times \frac{50}{20} = 0.08 \times 2.5 = 0.20 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Base Impedance on HV Side (66 kV):**
$$Z_{base,HV} = \frac{(66)^{2}}{50} = \frac{4356}{50} = 87.12 \text{ Ω}$$

**Base Impedance on LV Side (11 kV):**
$$Z_{base,LV} = \frac{(11)^{2}}{50} = \frac{121}{50} = 2.42 \text{ Ω}$$

**Ohmic Impedance:**

HV side:

$$Z_{HV} = 0.20 \times 87.12 = 17.424 \text{ Ω}$$

LV side:

$$Z_{LV} = 0.20 \times 2.42 = 0.484 \text{ Ω}$$

**Verification (Turns Ratio Test):**

$$\left( \frac{66}{11} \right)^{2} = (6)^{2} = 36$$

$$\frac{Z_{HV}}{Z_{LV}} = \frac{17.424}{0.484} = 36 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **SCADA-based substation automation architecture**. Describe the role of **RTU, IED, MTU, and communication gateway** with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **132/33 kV, 25 MVA** transformer has $Z = 12\%$ on its own base. A **3-phase short-circuit** occurs at the 33 kV bus. The upstream 132 kV grid has a short-circuit capacity of **1500 MVA**. Compute the **short-circuit current** and **fault MVA** at the 33 kV bus. Take base = **100 MVA**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — SCADA-Based Substation Automation

**Architecture (Block Diagram Description):**

A SCADA-based substation consists of the following hierarchy:

1. **Process Level:** Primary equipment — CTs, PTs, breakers, transformers.
2. **Bay Level:** IEDs (Bay Controllers, Protection Relays).
3. **Station Level:** RTU (Remote Terminal Unit), SAS, Communication Gateway.
4. **Control Centre (MTU — Master Terminal Unit):** SCADA master, EMS.

**Component Roles:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **IED (Intelligent Electronic Device)** | Microprocessor-based device for protection, metering, control, and event recording at bay level. |
| **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** | Aggregates data from multiple IEDs; transmits to control centre via protocols (IEC 60870-5-104, DNP3). |
| **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** | Located at the control centre; receives RTU data, displays on HMI, issues control commands. |
| **Communication Gateway** | Protocol converter between substation LAN (IEC 61850) and WAN (IEC 60870-5-104). |
| **SCADA Software** | Provides real-time monitoring, alarm management, trending, and historical archiving. |

**Block Diagram (ASCII representation for answer sheet):**

```
[CT/PT] → [IEDs] → [RTU/SAS] → [Gateway] → [MTU/SCADA]
   ↑          ↑          ↑           ↑            ↑
 Sensors    Bay ctrl  Station lvl  WAN link   Master ctrl
```

#### Part (b) — Short-Circuit Calculation at 33 kV Bus

**Step 1 — Convert Transformer Impedance to 100 MVA Base**

$$Z_{T,p.u.} = 0.12 \times \frac{100}{25} = 0.12 \times 4 = 0.48 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Source Impedance from Grid SC MVA**

For a 3-phase fault at the source:

$$Z_{S,p.u.} = \frac{S_{base}}{MVA_{sc,grid}} = \frac{100}{1500} = 0.0667 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 3 — Total Series Impedance to 33 kV Bus**

$$Z_{total,p.u.} = Z_{S} + Z_{T} = 0.0667 + 0.48 = 0.5467 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Fault Current in Per-Unit**

$$I_{f,p.u.} = \frac{V_{prefault}}{Z_{total,p.u.}} = \frac{1.0}{0.5467} = 1.829 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Base Current on 33 kV Side**

$$I_{base} = \frac{100 \text{ MVA}}{\sqrt{3} \times 33 \text{ kV}} = \frac{100}{57.157} = 1.7495 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 6 — Fault Current in kA**

$$I_f = 1.829 \times 1.7495 = 3.20 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 7 — Fault MVA**

$$MVA_{sc} = \sqrt{3} \times 33 \times 3.20 = 182.9 \text{ MVA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to **change the base** when computing the transformer impedance on a different MVA base, leading to a wrong answer. Always show the conversion step explicitly: $Z_{new,p.u.} = Z_{old,p.u.} \times (S_{new} / S_{old})$. Marks are awarded for the conversion step, not just the final number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Substation Model" — KTU Module 1:**

- ☐ **Substation** = node where voltage is transformed, power is switched, and faults are isolated.
- ☐ **Standard voltages in India:** 11 / 33 / 66 / 132 / 220 / 400 / 765 kV.
- ☐ **Single Bus** = simplest, cheapest, lowest reliability.
- ☐ **Double Bus** = high reliability, used in EHV substations.
- ☐ **1½ Breaker Scheme** = highest reliability, used in 400 kV+ substations.
- ☐ **Per-Unit Formula:** $Z_{new,p.u.} = Z_{old,p.u.} \times \dfrac{S_{new}}{S_{old}} \times \left( \dfrac{V_{old}}{V_{new}} \right)^{2}$.
- ☐ **Base impedance:** $Z_{base} = \dfrac{kV_{base}^{2}}{MVA_{base}}$; **Base current:** $I_{base} = \dfrac{MVA_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot kV_{base}}$.
- ☐ **Y-bus diagonal** = sum of admittances at that bus; **Off-diagonal** = negative of branch admittance.
- ☐ **SLG fault:** $I_f = \dfrac{3V_{pf}}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0}$.
- ☐ **3-phase fault:** $I_f = \dfrac{V_{pf}}{Z_1}$.
- ☐ **IEC 61850 levels:** Process → Bay → Station → Enterprise.
- ☐ **IEC 61850 messages:** MMS (client-server), GOOSE (fast peer-to-peer event), SV (sampled values).
- ☐ **Logical Nodes to remember:** XCBR (breaker), XSWI (switch), MMXU (metering), PDIF (differential), PTOC (overcurrent).
- ☐ **SCADA hierarchy:** Sensor → IED → RTU/SAS → Gateway → MTU.
- ☐ **GOOSE latency** < 4 ms; **SV latency** < 3 ms; **MMS latency** ~100 ms (acceptable).
- ☐ **Smart substation adds:** PMUs, WAMS, AI-based fault diagnosis, self-healing automation.
- ☐ **Always convert to a common 100 MVA base** in p.u. problems unless stated otherwise.
- ☐ **Fortescue operator** $a = 1 \angle 120°$; **$a^2 = 1 \angle 240°$; $1 + a + a^2 = 0$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Substation Levels — "Please Be Smart, Earn":**
> **P**rocess → **B**ay → **S**tation → **E**nterprise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Smart Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Smart Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Meter (as per KTU 2024 / IEC 62056 / IEEE 1701 standards):**
> A **Smart Meter** is an advanced, solid-state, electronic metering device that records real-time consumption of electrical energy (kWh), demand (kW), power quality parameters, and other operational data in fine-grained intervals (typically **15 minutes** or less) and supports **two-way secure communication** with the utility's central system (Head-End System / HES) for the purpose of automated billing, load management, demand response, outage management, and distribution automation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, the smart meter is positioned as the **customer-side endpoint of the Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** and acts as a real-time sensor–actuator node at the grid edge (typically the LV 230 V / 415 V consumer end).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Think of a **conventional electromechanical meter** as a *one-way mechanical odometer on a car*. It simply counts up as electricity flows, and a human must walk up, read the number, and report it to the utility.

A **smart meter** is more like a **modern smartphone with a built-in GPS that talks to a central server**:
- It counts electricity (records energy).
- It knows *when* you used it (time-of-use / TOU).
- It knows *how* you used it (power quality, voltage, current, frequency).
- It *talks back* to the utility automatically (two-way communication).
- It can receive commands (e.g., dynamic pricing, demand-response signals, remote connect/disconnect).
- It can *warn* you (tamper detection, outage notification).

So, in one line: **A smart meter = Conventional meter + Microprocessor + Communication module + Intelligence**.

### 1.3 Evolution: From Conventional to Smart

| Generation | Type | Communication | Reading | Data Granularity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen | Electromechanical (Ferraris disc) | None | Manual | Cumulative kWh only |
| 2nd Gen | Electronic / Hybrid (LCD) | None | Manual | kWh + max demand |
| 3rd Gen | AMR (Automatic Meter Reading) | One-way RF/PLC | Drive-by/Walk-by | Cumulative + cumulative TOU |
| 4th Gen | **Smart Meter (AMI)** | **Two-way RF / PLC / Cellular / RF Mesh** | Remote + Local | 15-min interval kWh, kVArh, V, I, Hz, PF, tamper, events |

### 1.4 Key Functional Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Capabilities of a Smart Meter (KTU High-Yield Must-Know List):**
> 1. **Bidirectional energy measurement** (import + export, critical for prosumers / rooftop solar).
> 2. **Time-of-Use (TOU) and Real-Time Pricing (RTP)** recording.
> 3. **Power quality monitoring** — voltage sag/swell, frequency deviation, harmonic content.
> 4. **Tamper detection and theft indication.**
> 5. **Remote connect / disconnect** (load limiting relay, typically **5 A to 100 A** ratings).
> 6. **Outage detection and last-gasp notification** to utility.
> 7. **Local consumer display (IHD – In-Home Display)** for energy awareness.
> 8. **HAN (Home Area Network) gateway** capability (Zigbee / Wi-Fi / Bluetooth).

### 1.5 Standard Reference Architecture

The smart meter sits at the intersection of three domains:
- **Field Area Network (FAN):** meter ↔ concentrator (DCU – Data Concentrator Unit).
- **Wide Area Network (WAN):** concentrator ↔ HES / utility server.
- **Home Area Network (HAN):** meter ↔ In-Home Display / smart appliances.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Smart Meter — Step by Step

A smart meter operates in a **sense → compute → communicate → actuate** loop:

1. **Sensing Stage (Analog Front End - AFE):**
   - Voltage is stepped down using a **voltage divider / potential transformer (PT)** to a low level (typically **0–5 V** or **0–1 V**).
   - Current is sensed using a **current shunt, current transformer (CT), or Rogowski coil**.
   - The signals are conditioned (anti-aliasing filter with cutoff $< f_s/2$) and fed to an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.

2. **Computation Stage (Digital Signal Processor / MCU):**
   - The ADC samples voltage $v(t)$ and current $i(t)$ synchronously at sampling rate $f_s$.
   - DSP computes RMS voltage, RMS current, active power, reactive power, apparent power, power factor, energy, frequency, and THD in real time.
   - Algorithms used: **Sample-by-sample multiplication (for power), integration over interval (for energy), DFT/Goertzel (for harmonics).**

3. **Storage Stage:**
   - Data is stored in **non-volatile memory (NVM / Flash / EEPROM)** with typical capacity of **128 kB to 4 MB** to retain interval load profile for **40 to 60 days** even during communication outages.

4. **Communication Stage:**
   - Interval data is pushed (or pulled) to the **Head-End System (HES)** through a defined protocol stack (DLMS/COSEM over TCP/IP via PLC or RF).

5. **Actuation Stage:**
   - On receiving a valid encrypted command (e.g., from MDM – Meter Data Management system), the **load switch (relay / triac)** opens or closes to connect/disconnect supply.

### 2.2 Why "Smart"?

The "smartness" comes from three pillars:

- **Computational Pillar:** Onboard DSP/MCU enables real-time analytics, not just counting.
- **Communication Pillar:** Two-way IP-based communication enables remote operation and integration with grid software.
- **Information Pillar:** High-resolution, time-stamped data enables new utility functions like **Time-of-Use billing, Demand Response, Outage Management, Distribution State Estimation, and Predictive Load Forecasting.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Smart Meter Computation**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous Active Power | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | W (watts) | Sampled and averaged |
| 2 | Average Active Power over $N$ samples | $P = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \, i[n]$ | W | $N$ = samples per fundamental cycle |
| 3 | RMS Voltage | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n]^{2}}$ | V | — |
| 4 | RMS Current | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} i[n]^{2}}$ | A | — |
| 5 | Apparent Power | $S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$ | VA | Geometric mean |
| 6 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{S^{2} - P^{2}}$ | VAr | Sign convention per IEEE 1459 |
| 7 | Power Factor | $PF = \dfrac{P}{S} = \cos \phi$ | dimensionless | $-1 \le PF \le 1$ |
| 8 | Active Energy over interval $T$ | $E = \sum_{k=0}^{T/\Delta t - 1} P[k] \cdot \Delta t$ | Wh | $\Delta t$ = interval, typically 15 min |
| 9 | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | $THD_{I} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_{h}^{2}}}{I_{1}} \times 100$ | % | $I_1$ = fundamental RMS |
| 10 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_{s} \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | For 50 harmonics, $f_s \ge 6.4$ kHz |
| 11 | Accuracy Class (IS 16444 / IEC 62053-22) | $\% \text{ error} = \dfrac{E_{m} - E_{t}}{E_{t}} \times 100$ | % | Class 0.5S / 0.2S for HT, Class 1.0 for LT |
| 12 | Communication Data Load (per meter per day) | $D = 96 \times L_{rec} \times f_{days}$ | bytes | 96 = 15-min intervals in 24 h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the exact code-level computations running inside the meter's DSP for every 50 Hz / 60 Hz cycle. They are also the basis for compliance testing under **BIS IS 16444 (India), IEC 62053-21/22, ANSI C12.20** standards.

### 2.4 Engineering Significance

In real utility practice, a smart meter is **not just a billing device** — it is a **distributed real-time sensor** on the LV network. Aggregated smart meter data enables:

- **Transformer Load Management (TLM)** — through sub-station level clustering.
- **Phase balancing** at distribution transformers.
- **Energy audit** for HT/LT consumers.
- **Renewable integration monitoring** (import–export net metering).
- **Theft detection** through statistical load anomaly analysis.
- **Peer-to-peer (P2P) energy trading** in transactive energy markets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Energy Recorded Over One Billing Interval

**Given:** Voltage $v(t)$ and current $i(t)$ at the load terminal, sampled at $f_s$ Hz.

**Goal:** Express the active energy consumed over a billing interval $T$ in terms of the meter's sampled data.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Power Definition**

By the physical definition of electrical power, the instantaneous power delivered to the load at any instant is the product of instantaneous voltage and instantaneous current.

$$
p(t) \;=\; v(t) \cdot i(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Energy as Time Integral of Power**

Energy is the integral of power over a duration. For a billing interval from $t = 0$ to $t = T$, the total active energy consumed is:

$$
E \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} p(t) \, dt \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt
$$

This is the continuous-time fundamental definition.

**Step 3 — Discretization for Digital Implementation**

The smart meter is a digital device — it cannot integrate continuously. It samples $v(t)$ and $i(t)$ at discrete instants $t_n = n \Delta t$ where $\Delta t = 1/f_s$. Replacing the integral by a Riemann sum (rectangular rule) over $N$ samples:

$$
E \;\approx\; \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \, i[n] \, \Delta t \;=\; \Delta t \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \, i[n]
$$

where $N = T \cdot f_s$.

**Step 4 — Average Active Power over the Interval**

Dividing the discrete energy expression by $T = N \Delta t$ gives the average active power that is also displayed as instantaneous demand:

$$
P_{avg} \;=\; \frac{E}{T} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \, i[n]
$$

**Step 5 — Energy in the Standard kWh Unit**

To report energy in **kilowatt-hours** (the standard billing unit), divide by $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ J:

$$
E_{kWh} \;=\; \frac{1}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \, i[n] \, \Delta t
$$

**Step 6 — Practical On-Meter Aggregation**

In practice, the meter's MCU does not store all raw samples. It integrates power in a running accumulator over the interval $\Delta t_{int} = 15$ min $= 900$ s, giving one energy value per interval:

$$
E_{int}[k] \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{N_{int}-1} v[n] \, i[n] \, \Delta t
$$

where $N_{int} = f_s \cdot 900$ is the number of samples per 15-min interval.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — RMS Voltage from Sampled Data

**Step 1 — Define RMS in Continuous Time**

The RMS (Root Mean Square) value of a periodic voltage is defined as the square root of the mean of the square of the signal over one period $T_0$.

$$
V_{rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} v(t)^{2} \, dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize over One Fundamental Cycle**

For a 50 Hz system, $T_0 = 20$ ms. The meter samples $N$ times per cycle. Replacing the integral by a discrete sum:

$$
V_{rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n]^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Required Number of Samples**

To accurately reconstruct the 50 Hz fundamental and up to the **50th harmonic (2.5 kHz)**, the Nyquist criterion requires:

$$
f_s \;\ge\; 2 \times 2500 \;=\; 5000 \text{ Hz}
$$

Typical smart meter DSPs sample at **6.4 kHz, 8 kHz, or 12.8 kHz** (i.e., 128, 160, or 256 samples per 50 Hz cycle).

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

**Step 1 — Express Current as Fourier Series**

A non-sinusoidal load current can be expanded into fundamental + harmonics:

$$
i(t) \;=\; I_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{H} I_{h} \sqrt{2} \sin(h \omega t + \phi_h)
$$

**Step 2 — RMS Value of the Harmonic Content**

By Parseval's theorem, the total RMS of the harmonic part (excluding DC) is:

$$
I_{h,rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_{h}^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define THD**

The ratio of harmonic RMS to fundamental RMS, expressed as a percentage, is the THD:

$$
THD_{I} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_{h}^{2}}}{I_{1}} \times 100 \;[\%]
$$

The DSP computes $I_h$ using a **256-point or 512-point DFT** per IEC 61000-4-7.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Smart Meter vs. Conventional Meter

| Feature | Conventional Meter | Smart Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement | Cumulative kWh only | kWh, kVArh, kVAh, kW, kVAr, kVA, V, I, Hz, PF, THD |
| Reading | Manual, monthly | Remote, **15-min interval** |
| Communication | None | Two-way (RF / PLC / Cellular / RF Mesh) |
| Tariff | Flat / single-rate | **TOU, RTP, Critical Peak Pricing (CPP)** |
| Outage Detection | None | **Last-gasp** alarm to HES |
| Tamper Detection | Mechanical seals | **Electronic event log** (cover open, magnetic, neutral disturbance) |
| Net Metering Support | Not available | **Bidirectional** (import + export) |
| Remote Connect/Disconnect | Manual | **Remote latching relay** |
| Consumer Awareness | None | **In-Home Display (IHD) + Mobile App** |
| Data Storage | None | **40–60 days interval data** in NVM |
| Cost (India, 2024) | ₹ 200 – 400 | ₹ 2,500 – 4,500 (single-phase whole current) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Smart Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LV Line Voltage 230V PT] --> B[Analog Front End Filter and ADC]
    A1[Load Current CT or Shunt] --> B
    B --> C[DSP Microcontroller Core]
    C --> D[Non Volatile Memory Flash and EEPROM]
    C --> E[RTC Real Time Clock with Battery]
    C --> F[LCD Display Module]
    C --> G[Optical Port IEC 62056 21]
    C --> H[Communication Module RF Mesh or PLC or Cellular]
    H --> I[Concentrator DCU]
    I --> J[Head End System HES]
    J --> K[Meter Data Management MDM]
    C --> L[HAN Zigbee or WiFi]
    L --> M[In Home Display IHD]
    C --> N[Load Switch Relay 5A to 100A]
    N --> A1
    C --> O[Tamper Detection and Security]
```

### 4.2 AMI Communication Stack — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Smart Meter at Consumer Premises] --> S2[RF Mesh or PLC Neighbourhood]
    S2 --> S3[Data Concentrator Unit DCU at DT]
    S3 --> S4[Cellular 4G or OFC Backhaul WAN]
    S4 --> S5[Head End System HES at Utility Data Centre]
    S5 --> S6[Meter Data Management System MDM]
    S6 --> S7[Billing System]
    S6 --> S8[Outage Management System OMS]
    S6 --> S9[Distribution Management System DMS]
    S5 --> S1
    S1 --> S10[In Home Display and Consumer App]
```

### 4.3 Information Flow Inside a Smart Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Voltage and Current Sensor Inputs] --> A1[Signal Conditioning and Anti Aliasing Filter]
    A1 --> A2[Analog to Digital Converter ADC at 6.4 kHz]
    A2 --> A3[DSP Engine]
    A3 --> A4[RMS Voltage and Current Calculation]
    A3 --> A5[Active Reactive Apparent Power]
    A3 --> A6[Energy Accumulation Wh and VArh]
    A3 --> A7[Frequency and Power Factor]
    A3 --> A8[Harmonic Analysis DFT up to 50th order]
    A4 --> A9[Interval Data Buffer 15 min]
    A5 --> A9
    A6 --> A9
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Non Volatile Memory Storage 40 to 60 days]
    A9 --> A11[Communication Processor DLMS COSEM Stack]
    A11 --> A12[Push to DCU over RF or PLC]
    A11 --> A13[HAN Broadcast to IHD]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define a Smart Meter. List any FOUR salient features that distinguish it from a conventional electromechanical energy meter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Smart Meter is an advanced solid-state electronic energy meter with two-way communication capability that records consumption of electrical energy in fine time intervals (typically 15 minutes) and exchanges data with the utility's central system for billing, monitoring, and control purposes, as per IEC 62056 standards.

> **Four distinguishing features (½ mark each):**
> 1. **Two-way communication** with the utility (RF / PLC / Cellular) versus one-way / no communication in conventional meters.
> 2. **Time-stamped interval data** (kWh, kVArh, demand) at 15-min granularity versus cumulative monthly reading only.
> 3. **Power quality monitoring** (voltage, current, frequency, THD, PF) versus kWh only.
> 4. **Remote connect/disconnect** of supply through an internal latching relay versus manual disconnection.
> 5. **Tamper detection and event logging** versus mechanical sealing only.

*(Any four well-explained features fetch full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is AMI? Differentiate between AMR and AMI in the context of smart metering.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **AMI – Advanced Metering Infrastructure (1 mark):** AMI is the complete system of smart meters, communication networks (FAN, WAN, HAN), and data management systems (HES, MDM) that enables two-way communication between the utility and the consumer for advanced functions like TOU billing, demand response, and outage management.

> **AMR vs AMI (2 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | AMR (Automatic Meter Reading) | AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Communication | **One-way** (meter → utility) | **Two-way** (bidirectional) |
> | Data | Cumulative readings | 15-min interval data + events |
> | Remote Control | **Not possible** (read-only) | **Possible** (connect/disconnect, DR signals) |
> | Time-of-Use | Limited | Full TOU, RTP, CPP support |
> | Outage Detection | Not supported | Last-gasp notification supported |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve **either Option A or Option B in full.** Examiners strictly allot 7 marks per sub-part.

---

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a single-phase smart meter. Identify and explain the role of each block in signal acquisition, computation, and communication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The functional architecture of a single-phase smart meter consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **Voltage Sensing Block (1 mark):** A resistor divider or miniature PT steps down the 230 V line voltage to a low-level signal (0–5 V). A surge arrester (MOV) provides HV transient protection.

2. **Current Sensing Block (1 mark):** A **CT (Current Transformer) or shunt resistor or Rogowski coil** senses the line current. CT-based sensing is preferred for galvanic isolation. The output is a small voltage proportional to current.

3. **Analog Front End (AFE) + ADC (1 mark):** Includes an anti-aliasing low-pass filter (cutoff ≤ 2.5 kHz for 50th harmonic), a programmable-gain amplifier, and a 16/24-bit sigma-delta ADC. Sampling rate is typically **3.2 kHz to 12.8 kHz**.

4. **DSP / MCU Core (1 mark):** A 32-bit microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0/M3) runs the metering firmware. It computes RMS V, RMS I, P, Q, S, PF, frequency, energy, and THD using DFT algorithms in real time per cycle.

5. **Non-Volatile Memory (1 mark):** 128 kB – 4 MB of Flash/EEPROM stores interval load profile (15-min × 96 = 96 records/day) for 40–60 days and tamper/event logs. RTC with battery backup keeps time-stamping accurate to ±0.5 s/day.

6. **Communication Module (1 mark):** A plug-in module supporting **RF Mesh (Zigbee SEP 2.0 / Wi-SUN), PLC (PRIME / G3), or Cellular (4G LTE / NB-IoT)**. Implements DLMS/COSEM protocol stack (IEC 62056) for data exchange with HES.

7. **Load Switch + Display + Tamper Detection (1 mark):** A latching relay (typically **60 A / 100 A**) for remote connect/disconnect, an LCD for local display, optical IEC 62056-21 port for local readout, and tamper sensors (magnetic, cover open, neutral missing, earth reference).

---

**(b)** Explain Time-of-Use (TOU) tariff implementation using smart meters. Show how a 24-hour TOU schedule with three tariff slots is recorded in the meter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Time-of-Use (TOU) tariff is a pricing structure in which the energy charge varies according to the time of day when energy is consumed. The smart meter maintains an internal **TOU clock and a tariff calendar** that allocates each 15-min interval to one of the predefined tariff slots. The Indian CEA's "Time of Day" tariff (2019 guidelines) specifies three slots:

| Slot | Time Window | Typical Multiplier | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peak (P)** | 06:00 – 09:00 hrs and 18:00 – 22:00 hrs | 1.20 × Base | High demand periods — discourage use |
| **Normal (N)** | 09:00 – 18:00 hrs | 1.00 × Base | Working hours |
| **Off-Peak (O)** | 22:00 – 06:00 hrs | 0.80 × Base | Encourage night use, valley filling |

**Recording Process in the Meter (3 marks):**

1. The meter's RTC triggers a check every 15 minutes.
2. The firmware looks up the **active tariff schedule** from non-volatile memory (e.g., Monday–Friday: P-N-O-P-N-O-P pattern; Sunday: uniform normal).
3. The energy consumed in that 15-min slot is **accumulated into the corresponding tariff register**: $E_{P}$, $E_{N}$, $E_{O}$ (in kWh).
4. The maximum demand in each slot is also tracked separately: $MD_{P}$, $MD_{N}$, $MD_{O}$.
5. At month-end, the HES / MDM reads all tariff registers and computes bill as: $\text{Bill} = E_{P} \cdot R_{P} + E_{N} \cdot R_{N} + E_{O} \cdot R_{O}$ where $R_P, R_N, R_O$ are the per-unit rates for each slot.

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
For a consumer with $E_P = 250$ kWh, $E_N = 400$ kWh, $E_O = 150$ kWh and rates $R_P = ₹ 8.00$, $R_N = ₹ 6.50$, $R_O = ₹ 4.50$:

$$
\text{Bill} = 250 \times 8.00 + 400 \times 6.50 + 150 \times 4.50
$$

$$
\text{Bill} = 2000 + 2600 + 675 = ₹ 5275
$$

This is the value the HES computes and pushes to the billing system.

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the differences between **AMR, AMI, and AMM**. Explain why AMI is the preferred architecture for a modern smart grid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **AMR – Automatic Meter Reading (1.5 marks):** A one-way communication system where the meter pushes cumulative consumption data to a mobile collector (drive-by / walk-by) or a fixed network collector. No remote control, no interval data. Used as a transitional step from manual to smart metering.

2. **AMI – Advanced Metering Infrastructure (2 marks):** A complete two-way system — meters, FAN (RF Mesh/PLC), WAN (cellular/fibre), HAN (Zigbee/Wi-Fi), HES, and MDM. Supports interval data, TOU/RTP, demand response, remote connect/disconnect, tamper detection, and outage management. AMI is the **foundation of all modern smart grid customer-side applications.**

3. **AMM – Automated Meter Management (1 mark):** Refers to the back-office software (MDM, billing integration, asset management) that manages the lifecycle of the metering fleet — commissioning, firmware upgrades, configuration changes, alarm handling, and decommissioning.

4. **Why AMI is Preferred (2.5 marks):**
   - Enables **Time-of-Use and Real-Time Pricing** for consumer choice.
   - Enables **Demand Response and Direct Load Control** for grid stability.
   - Provides **rich, time-stamped data** for distribution planning and forecasting.
   - Supports **Distributed Energy Resource (DER) integration** through bidirectional measurement.
   - Reduces Aggregate Technical & Commercial (AT&C) losses by enabling theft detection.
   - Cuts operational cost (no manual reading) and improves outage response time.

---

**(b)** A three-phase smart meter is rated at 230 V / 40 A per phase. The meter's MCU samples voltage and current at $f_s = 6.4$ kHz. Compute:
   (i) the average active power drawn by a balanced three-phase load if the phase voltage sampled over one cycle has $V_{rms} = 230$ V and the phase current has $I_{rms} = 25$ A at a power factor of 0.9 lag;
   (ii) the total energy in kWh consumed in 24 hours if the load remains constant;
   (iii) the number of samples per 50 Hz fundamental cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Average active power (2.5 marks):**

For a balanced three-phase load, total active power is:

$$
P_{3\phi} = 3 \, V_{ph,rms} \, I_{ph,rms} \, \cos \phi
$$

Substituting $V_{ph,rms} = 230$ V, $I_{ph,rms} = 25$ A, $\cos \phi = 0.9$:

$$
P_{3\phi} = 3 \times 230 \times 25 \times 0.9
$$

$$
P_{3\phi} = 3 \times 230 \times 22.5 = 15525 \text{ W} = 15.525 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Energy in 24 hours (2.5 marks):**

$$
E_{24h} = P_{3\phi} \times t = 15.525 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 372.6 \text{ kWh}
$$

**(iii) Samples per cycle (2 marks):**

$$
N_{cycle} = \frac{f_s}{f_{line}} = \frac{6400}{50} = 128 \text{ samples per cycle}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in three-phase power — students often use single-phase formula directly. *Loss: 1.5 marks.*
> 2. **Confusing "kW" with "kWh"** — power is the rate; energy is power × time. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Mixing per-phase and line values** in unbalanced calculations — for balanced 3-φ, $V_{L} = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$; if you use line values, use $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$. *Loss: 0.5 to 1 mark.*
> 4. **Not stating units** in final answers — examiners often deduct for "missing unit". *Loss: 0.5 mark.*
> 5. **Skipping the Nyquist reasoning** in sampling questions — write $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ explicitly before computing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smart Meter Introduction (Module 2)**
>
> - **Definition:** Solid-state, two-way communicating, interval-recording electronic energy meter.
> - **Standards:** IEC 62056 (DLMS/COSEM), IEC 62053-21/22, IS 16444 (India), ANSI C12.20.
> - **Key Functional Blocks:** Voltage sensor, Current sensor, AFE + ADC, DSP/MCU, NVM, RTC, Communication module, Load switch, Display, Tamper detection.
> - **Measured Quantities:** kWh (import/export), kVArh, kVAh, kW (MD), V, I, Hz, PF, THD.
> - **Interval:** Standard 15-minute logging → 96 records/day.
> - **Architecture Levels:** Field Area Network (FAN) → Wide Area Network (WAN) → Home Area Network (HAN).
> - **AMR vs AMI:** AMR = one-way, read-only; AMI = two-way, full control & analytics.
> - **TOU Tariff Slots:** Peak (P), Normal (N), Off-Peak (O) — each tracked in a separate register.
> - **Bidirectional Metering:** Mandatory for net-metered rooftop solar consumers.
> - **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 \times$ (highest harmonic of interest × 50 Hz).
> - **Accuracy Class:** Class 0.2S / 0.5S for HT industrial; Class 1.0 for LT domestic.
> - **Communication Technologies:** RF Mesh (Zigbee SEP 2.0 / Wi-SUN), PLC (PRIME / G3), Cellular (4G LTE / NB-IoT), LoRa (for sparse rural).
> - **AMI vs AMM:** AMI = hardware + network; AMM = back-office software (MDM).
> - **Roles in Smart Grid:** Demand response, outage management, distribution automation, net metering, theft detection, peer-to-peer trading.
> - **Three Domains:** Sensing, Computing, Communicating — all three together make a meter "smart".
> - **Regulatory Reference (India):** CEA (Standards of Performance of Intermediaries) Regulations + IS 16444:2015 + Rec. on smart meters by Forum of Regulators (FOR).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electricity Tariff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electricity Tariff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Smart Grid Technologies*, an **Electricity Tariff** is formally defined as the **rate structure** (or schedule of charges) fixed by a distribution licensee / utility for the supply of electrical energy to various categories of consumers. It encapsulates the financial mechanism by which the cost of electrical energy — comprising generation, transmission, distribution, fixed assets, fuel, and regulatory return on investment — is recovered from the end user, while simultaneously serving as a **price signal** that influences consumer load behavior.

Mathematically, a generalized tariff can be expressed as:

$$T = f(E, D, t, pf, C_f)$$

where $E$ is the energy consumed (kWh), $D$ is the maximum demand (kW or kVA), $t$ is the time-of-use, $pf$ is the power factor, and $C_f$ represents fixed cost components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A tariff is **not merely a rate per unit**; it is an *engineered economic instrument* that must simultaneously satisfy four goals — cost recovery, fair allocation, demand-side management, and incentivization of grid-friendly behavior (e.g., improved power factor, off-peak usage).

### 1.2 Key Objectives of an Ideal Tariff

An electricity tariff, as evaluated in KTU board examinations, must fulfill the following objectives:

- **Cost Recovery:** Total revenue must equal the total cost of generation, transmission, distribution, plus a reasonable return on capital investment.
- **Equitable Distribution:** The cost burden should be shared fairly among different consumer classes (domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural).
- **Simplicity & Transparency:** The consumer must be able to compute and predict their bill with minimal effort.
- **Load Factor Improvement:** The tariff should encourage consumers to spread their consumption and avoid peak-hour demand, thereby raising the system's overall **Load Factor**.
- **Demand-Side Management (DSM):** Modern smart-grid tariffs aim to flatten the load curve, defer capacity addition, and integrate renewable energy.
- **Power Factor Incentivization:** Penalize low-PF consumers and reward high-PF consumers to reduce line losses.
- **Economic Efficiency:** Encourage efficient utilization of electrical energy.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Bill:**
> Imagine a restaurant that charges its customers. A **flat-rate** restaurant charges ₹500 per person regardless of what they order (encourages over-ordering and unfair to light eaters). A **block-rate** restaurant charges ₹500 for the first 2 dishes, ₹400 for the next 2, and ₹300 thereafter (rewarding bulk eaters but still inefficient). A **two-part** restaurant charges a fixed entry fee of ₹1,000 (covering rent, staff, ambience) PLUS a per-dish charge (covering actual food cost). The entry fee is independent of consumption; the per-dish charge reflects variable costs. A **smart-grid dynamic** restaurant changes its per-dish price every hour based on kitchen capacity, ingredient freshness, and rush — much like **Time-of-Use** pricing.

Just as a well-designed restaurant menu balances fairness, profit, and customer flow, a well-designed electricity tariff balances utility economics, consumer equity, and grid stability.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Tariff Design

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Demand | MD | **kW** or **kVA** | Highest load drawn in a billing cycle |
| Energy Consumed | E | **kWh** (Units) | Active energy used |
| Load Factor | LF | **ratio (0–1)** | Average load / Peak load |
| Diversity Factor | DF | **ratio (>1)** | Sum of individual max / Coincident max |
| Power Factor | pf / cos φ | **ratio (0–1)** | Active power / Apparent power |
| Demand Factor | Df | **ratio (<1)** | Max demand / Connected load |
| Average Rate | AR | **₹ / kWh** | Total bill / Total units |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Electricity Tariffs

Tariffs are classified along two broad axes: **Conventional Tariffs** (legacy, bulk-billing) and **Smart-Grid Tariffs** (dynamic, time-variant). KTU board questions frequently test both, with a 2024-scheme emphasis on smart-grid variants.

#### 2.1.1 Flat Rate (Uniform Rate) Tariff
- A single rate (₹/kWh) is applied irrespective of consumption level, time, or demand.
- **Rate (R)** = Total annual cost of supply / Total energy sold
- **Merits:** Utter simplicity; trivial billing.
- **Demerits:** Penalizes low-consumption users; offers no incentive for load shifting; ignores demand-related costs (which are independent of energy).
- **KTU Note:** Almost never used in practice; appears in board questions as a *baseline for comparison*.

#### 2.1.2 Block Rate Tariff
- Consumption is divided into **slabs/blocks**, each with a different unit rate.
- **Two variants:**
  - *Cumulative (additive):* Each slab is charged at its own rate; the rate per unit *increases* with consumption (punitive).
  - *Non-cumulative (preferential):* The lowest slab rate applies to the *entire* consumption if it crosses the slab threshold (incentive for efficient use).
- **Merits:** Promotes energy conservation.
- **Demerits:** Discontinuous billing; can lead to consumer disputes at slab boundaries.

#### 2.1.3 Two-Part Tariff (Hopkinson Tariff)
This is the **most tested tariff in KTU papers**. The bill has two independent components:

$$\text{Total Bill} = (A \times kW_{MD}) + (B \times kWh)$$

where $A$ = fixed charge per kW of maximum demand, $B$ = energy charge per kWh.
- The fixed charge recovers capital cost (independent of consumption).
- The energy charge recovers running cost (proportional to consumption).
- **Merits:** Equitable — heavy users bear more burden; simple to apply.
- **Demerits:** Requires maximum-demand metering; a small consumer with a high instantaneous MD can be unfairly penalized.

#### 2.1.4 Three-Part Tariff
$$\text{Total Bill} = A \times kW + B \times kVA \times pf_{penalty} + C \times kWh$$

Some texts write it as: Fixed (₹/kVA of MD) + Semi-fixed (₹/kWh of energy) + Variable (fuel-adjustment charge ₹/kWh). It combines a **kVA-based demand charge** with both a base energy charge and a fuel-adjustment clause.

#### 2.1.5 Power Factor Tariffs (sub-types of demand-based tariffs)

- **(a) kVA Maximum Demand Tariff:** Fixed charge is based on *apparent* demand (kVA), not active demand (kW). This naturally penalizes low-PF consumers.
  - $\text{Fixed Charge} = A \times kVA_{MD} = A \times \dfrac{kW_{MD}}{pf}$

- **(b) Maximum Demand Tariff:** Fixed charge is per kW of MD, but a *power factor penalty clause* is added. Typically, if PF falls below a benchmark (say 0.8 lagging), a penalty of 1% of the energy bill is levied for every 0.01 drop.

- **(c) Sliding Scale (Average) Tariff:** The energy rate itself is *decreased* if the consumer's average load factor or load utilization increases beyond a threshold, or if PF is maintained above a benchmark.

- **(d) KW + KVAR Tariff:** Both components of apparent power (active and reactive) are billed separately. Reactive energy is metered by a kVARh meter and charged at a separate rate. This is the **most scientific** PF tariff.

#### 2.1.6 Smart-Grid Tariffs (2024-Syllabus Emphasis)

- **Time-of-Use (TOU) Tariff:** Day is divided into Peak, Shoulder, and Off-Peak periods. The unit rate differs across periods. *Static* TOU tariffs have fixed period definitions; *dynamic* TOU varies them seasonally.

- **Real-Time Pricing (RTP):** The unit rate is communicated to consumers (typically an hour ahead) and reflects the actual wholesale market price. Highest economic efficiency.

- **Critical Peak Pricing (CPP):** On a small number of "critical" days per year (e.g., extreme heat-wave days), the utility declares a critical event and charges a sharply higher peak rate to discourage demand.

- **Peak-Day Pricing (PDP):** A hybrid — TOU rates on most days, but with a much higher rate pre-declared for a few peak days.

- **Prepaid / Smart-Meter Tariff:** Consumer purchases energy credit; tariff is applied in real time; supply can be remotely curtailed when credit is exhausted.

- **Net-Metering Tariff (with Rooftop Solar):** Consumer exports surplus solar energy back to the grid; the exported kWh is offset against imported kWh, often at a different (typically lower) feed-in tariff.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Units Sold}}$ | Flat rate calculation |
| 2 | $\text{Bill} = A \cdot kW_{MD} + B \cdot kWh$ | **Two-part tariff (most asked)** |
| 3 | $kVA_{MD} = \dfrac{kW_{MD}}{pf}$ | Apparent demand from active demand |
| 4 | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}} = \dfrac{E \text{ (kWh)}}{MD \times \text{Hours}}$ | Load Factor |
| 5 | $LF = \dfrac{E}{MD \times 720}$ (for a 30-day month) | Practical LF formula |
| 6 | $\text{Average Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill}}{\text{Total kWh}}$ | Effective rate per unit |
| 7 | $\text{PF Penalty} = \dfrac{0.8 - pf}{0.01} \times 1\% \times \text{Energy Bill}$ | Standard 0.8-lag benchmark penalty |
| 8 | $E = MD \times T \times LF$ | Energy = MD × Time × LF |
| 9 | $MD = \dfrac{E}{T \times LF}$ | MD from energy, time and LF |
| 10 | $\text{Capacity Charge} = A \times kVA_{MD}$ | kVA MD tariff component |
| 11 | $\text{Reactive Charge} = C_R \times kVARh$ | KW + KVAR tariff |
| 12 | $\text{TOU Bill} = \sum_{i} B_i \times kWh_i$ | Sum across all time blocks |
| 13 | $\Delta kVA = kW \left(\dfrac{1}{pf_1} - \dfrac{1}{pf_2}\right)$ | kVA reduction from PF improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Units:** Always quote MD in **kW** (or **kVA** explicitly), energy in **kWh or units** (1 unit = 1 kWh), and charges in **₹ per kW (or kVA) per month** and **₹ per kWh**. Mixing up units is the most common error.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tariff design is the **economic backbone** of the smart grid. A well-engineered tariff:
- Decouples utility revenue from volumetric sales, allowing utilities to profit even when consumers conserve.
- Enables **demand response (DR)** — consumers shift loads in response to price signals, reducing peak demand by 10–30%.
- Facilitates **integration of distributed energy resources (DERs)** and **rooftop PV** via net-metering.
- Reduces the need for new peaking power plants, saving capital and emissions.
- In the KSEB (Kerala) context, tariffs are revised periodically by the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC)** and reflect these modern principles for HT consumers and prosumers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Multi-Tariff Comparison (14-mark standard)

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A factory has a maximum demand of **80 kW** at an average power factor of **0.8 lagging**, and consumes **24,000 kWh per month** (30 days). The utility offers the following tariff options:
- **Flat rate:** ₹6.00 per kWh
- **Two-part tariff:** ₹250 per kW of MD per month + ₹4.50 per kWh
- **kVA MD tariff:** ₹200 per kVA of MD per month + ₹4.50 per kWh
- **PF penalty clause:** If PF < 0.85 lag, a penalty of 2% of the energy bill is added for every 0.01 fall below 0.85.

**Find:** (a) The bill under each of the three tariffs. (b) Verify whether the PF penalty applies, and compute it. (c) Identify the cheapest scheme for the consumer.

#### Step 1 — Compute the apparent (kVA) maximum demand

The active demand is 80 kW. PF = 0.8 lagging. Therefore:

$$kVA_{MD} = \frac{kW_{MD}}{pf} = \frac{80}{0.8} = 100 \text{ kVA}$$

The active and reactive components are:

$$kW = kVA \cdot pf = 100 \times 0.8 = 80 \text{ kW (verified)}$$

$$kVAR = kVA \cdot \sin \varphi = 100 \times \sin(\cos^{-1} 0.8) = 100 \times 0.6 = 60 \text{ kVAR}$$

where $\sin \varphi = \sqrt{1 - pf^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.64} = \sqrt{0.36} = 0.6$.

#### Step 2 — Bill under Flat Rate Tariff

$$\text{Bill}_{\text{flat}} = R \times E = 6.00 \times 24{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}44{,}000$$

Average rate = ₹144,000 / 24,000 = **₹6.00/kWh** (by definition).

#### Step 3 — Bill under Two-Part Tariff

The two-part tariff separates the bill into a fixed (demand-based) component and a variable (energy-based) component:

$$\text{Fixed Component} = A \times kW_{MD} = 250 \times 80 = \text{₹}20{,}000$$

$$\text{Energy Component} = B \times kWh = 4.50 \times 24{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}08{,}000$$

$$\text{Bill}_{\text{two-part}} = 20{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}28{,}000$$

Average rate = ₹128,000 / 24,000 = **₹5.333/kWh**.

#### Step 4 — Bill under kVA Maximum Demand Tariff

The fixed component is now based on kVA, not kW:

$$\text{Fixed Component} = A_{kVA} \times kVA_{MD} = 200 \times 100 = \text{₹}20{,}000$$

$$\text{Energy Component} = 4.50 \times 24{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}08{,}000$$

$$\text{Bill}_{\text{kVA-MD}} = 20{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}28{,}000$$

At PF = 0.8, the two-part (kW-based) and kVA-MD bills happen to be *coincidentally equal* — but this is a special numerical case. The kVA tariff becomes *more punitive* if PF is lower than 0.8, and *less punitive* if PF improves.

#### Step 5 — PF Penalty Check

The actual PF is 0.8 lagging. The penalty benchmark is 0.85 lagging. Since 0.8 < 0.85, the penalty applies.

The PF shortfall is:

$$\Delta pf = 0.85 - 0.80 = 0.05$$

The number of penalty units (each 0.01 step) is:

$$N = \frac{\Delta pf}{0.01} = \frac{0.05}{0.01} = 5 \text{ units}$$

The penalty is 2% of the energy bill per unit:

$$\text{Penalty} = 5 \times 2\% \times \text{Energy Bill} = 10\% \times 1{,}08{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}800$$

So under the two-part tariff *with* PF penalty, the total bill is:

$$\text{Bill}_{\text{two-part, with PF}} = 1{,}28{,}000 + 10{,}800 = \text{₹}1{,}38{,}800$$

#### Step 6 — Comparative Summary

| Tariff Scheme | Total Bill (₹) | Average Rate (₹/kWh) | Remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat Rate | 1,44,000 | 6.000 | Most expensive |
| Two-Part (kW) | 1,28,000 | 5.333 | Cheaper; no PF penalty in this case |
| kVA MD Tariff | 1,28,000 | 5.333 | Equal here, but penalizes low PF |
| Two-Part + PF Penalty | 1,38,800 | 5.783 | PF penalty adds 8.4% |

**Conclusion:** The two-part (kW-based) tariff is the cheapest for this consumer *if* PF is above 0.85, but the kVA MD tariff would automatically push them to improve PF, reducing line losses for the utility.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Power Factor Improvement Economics

**Problem:** A consumer draws 100 kW at 0.7 PF lagging. The kVA-MD tariff is ₹150 per kVA + ₹4.00 per kWh. If the consumer installs a capacitor bank to improve PF to 0.95 lagging (without changing kW), find the new fixed charge, monthly savings on fixed charge (assuming MD is captured at the new kVA), and verify the energy bill is unchanged.

#### Step 1 — Initial kVA demand

$$kVA_1 = \frac{100}{0.7} = 142.857 \text{ kVA}$$

#### Step 2 — kVA after capacitor installation

$$kVA_2 = \frac{100}{0.95} = 105.263 \text{ kVA}$$

#### Step 3 — Reduction in kVA

$$\Delta kVA = 142.857 - 105.263 = 37.594 \text{ kVA}$$

Cross-check using the derived identity:

$$\Delta kVA = kW \left(\frac{1}{pf_1} - \frac{1}{pf_2}\right) = 100 \left(\frac{1}{0.7} - \frac{1}{0.95}\right) = 100 (1.4286 - 1.0526) = 37.59 \text{ kVA} \checkmark$$

#### Step 4 — Monthly savings on fixed charge

$$\text{Saving} = 150 \times 37.594 = \text{₹}5{,}639.10 \text{ per month}$$

The energy bill is unchanged because the kW consumption and energy rate are unchanged. **Only the demand charge is reduced.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the *direct economic justification* for capacitor banks in industrial plants. The payback period for the capacitor installation is typically 6–18 months, purely from the demand-charge savings — *before* accounting for the additional benefit of reduced line losses and voltage drop.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tariff Suitability

| Tariff Type | Best Suited For | Promotes | KTU Exam Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat Rate | Small domestic, very simple | Nothing | Rarely asked |
| Block Rate | Domestic (slab-based) | Conservation | Moderate |
| Two-Part (kW) | Medium industries | Bulk consumption but penalizes PF | **Most common (⭐)** |
| Three-Part | Large HT consumers | PF, LF, fuel cost recovery | Moderate |
| kVA MD Tariff | Industries with low PF | PF improvement | High |
| KW + KVAR | Reactive-heavy industries | Direct kVAR penalty | Moderate |
| TOU | Commercial / smart-grid | Load shifting | **Very high (⭐)** |
| RTP | Industrial with flexibility | Real-time response | High |
| CPP | All categories | Peak shaving | High |
| Net-Metering | Rooftop solar prosumers | DER integration | **Very high (⭐)** |
