## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 alike]**
**Define electricity tariff. List any four desirable characteristics of an ideal tariff.**

**Model Answer:**
An electricity tariff is the schedule of rates and charges fixed by a distribution licensee for supplying electrical energy to various classes of consumers, designed to recover the cost of supply while providing price signals to influence consumer behavior.

Four desirable characteristics:
1. **Cost Recovery:** Total revenue should equal the total cost of supply, including a reasonable return on capital.
2. **Equitable:** Each consumer class should bear a fair share of the cost proportional to its use of the system.
3. **Simple & Predictable:** A consumer should be able to estimate the bill with reasonable accuracy.
4. **Encourages Efficient Use:** Should promote a high load factor, discourage wasteful consumption, and incentivize good power factor and off-peak usage.

*(Additional valid points: Transparent, Promotes DSM, Easy to administer, Adaptable to smart-grid features.)* — **Full 3 marks if four points are listed with clarity.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Differentiate between Flat Rate Tariff and Two-Part Tariff.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Flat Rate Tariff | Two-Part Tariff |
|---|---|---|
| Rate Structure | Single uniform rate (₹/kWh) for all consumers | Two components: fixed (₹/kW MD) + variable (₹/kWh) |
| Demand Charge | Not separately recovered | Recovered as fixed charge |
| Energy Charge | Only charge | Variable charge |
| Cost Recovery | Only running cost | Both fixed and running cost |
| Simplicity | Very high | Moderate (requires MD metering) |
| Suitability | Small domestic consumers | Industrial and commercial consumers |
| Incentive for LF | None | Indirect (encourages higher LF) |

**Verdict:** Two-part tariff is more scientific and equitable; flat rate is rarely used in practice.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit errors** in MD (kW vs kVA) cost **2 marks** instantly. Always state units explicitly in every formula.
> - **Forgetting the PF penalty** (or applying it at the wrong benchmark) is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - In TOU/smart-grid questions, students often *describe* but fail to *draw a labeled block diagram* — KTU examiners reserve **2 marks** for a neat diagram.
> - In two-part tariff derivations, *not separating fixed and energy components* before substituting numbers is a **partial-mark killer**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the following types of electricity tariffs:
(i) Flat rate tariff, (ii) Block rate tariff, (iii) Two-part tariff, (iv) kVA maximum demand tariff. Mention one merit and one demerit of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Flat Rate Tariff:**
- Definition: A single uniform rate per unit (₹/kWh) is charged irrespective of the magnitude of consumption or maximum demand.
- Formula: $R = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Units Sold}}$
- **Merit:** Simplest to compute and understand.
- **Demerit:** Penalizes small consumers; does not recover demand-related (fixed) costs; offers no incentive for energy conservation.

**(ii) Block Rate Tariff:**
- Definition: Consumption is divided into blocks/slabs; the rate per unit changes at each block boundary. In *cumulative* (increasing-block) form, each block is charged at its own rate (penalizing high consumers); in *non-cumulative* form, the lowest applicable rate applies to the entire consumption (rewarding efficient users).
- **Merit:** Promotes energy conservation and equity.
- **Demerit:** Sudden jump in bill at slab boundaries; complexity in computation; consumer disputes.

**(iii) Two-Part Tariff (Hopkinson):**
- Definition: Bill = Fixed charge (₹/kW of MD) + Energy charge (₹/kWh).
- **Merit:** Fairly recovers both fixed and running costs; consumer can predict and control bill.
- **Demerit:** Requires maximum-demand metering; a consumer with a high momentary spike (e.g., motor starting) pays a heavy fixed charge.

**(iv) kVA Maximum Demand Tariff:**
- Definition: Same as two-part, but the fixed charge is based on **kVA** of MD rather than **kW**. Since $kVA = kW/pf$, this naturally penalizes low-PF consumers and rewards PF improvement.
- **Merit:** Strong economic incentive to improve PF, reducing system losses.
- **Demerit:** A small consumer with a high reactive component (e.g., arc-furnace shop) may be overcharged for true energy use.

> *(For full marks, students should also include a representative block diagram of each tariff — typically a bar graph of ₹/kWh vs consumption, or a labeled two-part equation.)*

---

**(b)** A consumer has a maximum demand of **60 kW** at a power factor of **0.75 lagging** and consumes **18,000 units per month**. The tariff is as follows:
- Fixed charge: ₹300 per kVA of MD per month
- Energy charge: ₹5.50 per kWh
- PF penalty: 1% of the energy bill for every 0.01 fall in PF below **0.85 lagging**

Calculate:
(i) The total monthly bill.
(ii) The new bill if the consumer installs a capacitor bank and improves PF to 0.95 lagging (MD in kW unchanged).
(iii) The annual savings from PF improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute kVA MD at PF 0.75:**

$$kVA_{MD} = \frac{kW_{MD}}{pf} = \frac{60}{0.75} = 80 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Fixed charge at PF 0.75:**

$$\text{Fixed} = 300 \times 80 = \text{₹}24{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Energy charge:**

$$\text{Energy} = 5.50 \times 18{,}000 = \text{₹}99{,}000$$

**Step 4 — PF penalty check:**
PF = 0.75 < 0.85, so penalty applies.

$$\Delta pf = 0.85 - 0.75 = 0.10, \quad N = \frac{0.10}{0.01} = 10 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Penalty} = 10 \times 1\% \times 99{,}000 = 10\% \times 99{,}000 = \text{₹}9{,}900$$

**Step 5 — Total Bill at PF 0.75:**

$$\text{Total}_1 = 24{,}000 + 99{,}000 + 9{,}900 = \text{₹}1{,}32{,}900$$

---

**Step 6 — New kVA MD at PF 0.95:**

$$kVA_{MD,new} = \frac{60}{0.95} = 63.158 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 7 — New fixed charge:**

$$\text{Fixed}_{new} = 300 \times 63.158 = \text{₹}18{,}947.37$$

**Step 8 — PF penalty check at 0.95:**

0.95 > 0.85 → **No penalty applies.**

**Step 9 — New Total Bill at PF 0.95:**

$$\text{Total}_2 = 18{,}947.37 + 99{,}000 + 0 = \text{₹}1{,}17{,}947.37$$

*(Energy charge is unchanged because kW and kWh are unchanged.)*

**Step 10 — Monthly and Annual Savings:**

$$\text{Monthly Saving} = 1{,}32{,}900 - 1{,}17{,}947.37 = \text{₹}14{,}952.63$$

$$\text{Annual Saving} = 14{,}952.63 \times 12 = \text{₹}1{,}79{,}431.58$$

**Conclusion:** PF improvement from 0.75 to 0.95 saves the consumer approximately **₹1.79 lakh per year**, demonstrating the strong economic case for capacitor bank installation in industrial plants.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a **Time-of-Use (TOU) Tariff** as deployed in a smart-grid context. How does it differ from a flat tariff in its impact on the system load curve? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A TOU tariff divides the 24-hour day into distinct periods (typically Peak, Shoulder, and Off-Peak) and applies a different energy rate to each period. In the smart-grid context, the period boundaries and rates can be communicated dynamically to the consumer (via AMI/AMR) and even adjusted in real time.

**Typical TOU Structure (Kerala KSEB / General Indian Practice):**

| Period | Typical Hours | Rate Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| Peak | 6 PM – 10 PM | Highest (e.g., 1.5× to 2.0× base) |
| Shoulder | 6 AM – 6 PM (excluding peak) | Moderate (e.g., 1.0× base) |
| Off-Peak | 10 PM – 6 AM | Lowest (e.g., 0.5× to 0.7× base) |

**Block Diagram:**

```
   +-----------------------------+
   |   Smart Meter (AMI)         |
   +-------------+---------------+
                 |
                 v
   +-----------------------------+
   | TOU Schedule (Calendar)     |
   |  Peak / Shoulder / Off-Peak |
   +-------------+---------------+
                 |
                 v
   +-----------------------------+
   | Billing Engine              |
   |  B_peak x kWh_peak +        |
   |  B_shoulder x kWh_shoulder +|
   |  B_offpeak x kWh_offpeak    |
   +-------------+---------------+
                 |
                 v
   +-----------------------------+
   | Consumer HEMS / App         |
   | (Real-time price display)   |
   +-----------------------------+
```

**Impact on Load Curve — Comparison with Flat Tariff:**

| Aspect | Flat Tariff | TOU Tariff |
|---|---|---|
| Peak demand | Unchanged or growing | Reduced (consumers shift loads) |
| Off-peak utilization | Low | Increased |
| Load factor | Low | Significantly improved |
| Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR) | High | Reduced |
| Need for peaking plants | High | Reduced |
| Consumer bill (if flexible) | Same | Lower |
| Utility revenue stability | High | Moderate |

**Smart-Grid Linkage:** With AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) and HEMS (Home Energy Management Systems), the consumer can be *automated* to shift flexible loads (EV charging, water heaters, washing machines, dishwashers) to off-peak hours, responding to the price signal without manual intervention.

---

**(b)** Explain **Real-Time Pricing (RTP)** and **Critical Peak Pricing (CPP)** in the context of smart-grid dynamic pricing. Discuss their role in **demand response (DR)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Real-Time Pricing (RTP):**
- The unit price of electricity is communicated to consumers in *near real time* (typically one hour ahead, or even shorter intervals).
- Price is set by the wholesale market (e.g., day-ahead or real-time market of the exchange, or by the utility's own pricing engine based on system load, fuel cost, and renewable generation).
- The bill is: $\text{Bill} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} P_t \times E_t$ where $P_t$ is the price in hour $t$ and $E_t$ is the consumption in hour $t$.
- **Highest economic efficiency** — consumers face the true marginal cost of supply at every moment.
- Requires *smart meters with two-way communication* and consumer-side automation to respond effectively.
- **Suitability:** Large industrial and commercial consumers with flexibility.

**Critical Peak Pricing (CPP):**
- A baseline TOU structure applies on most days, but on a small number of pre-declared "critical peak" days per year (e.g., 10–15 days), a *very high* critical-peak rate is applied during a defined window.
- Critical events are typically triggered by forecasted extreme conditions (heat waves, cold snaps, system emergencies).
- The high price is announced *a day ahead* (Day-Ahead CPP) or on the same day (Day-Of CPP).
- **Simpler to implement** than full RTP and provides strong peak-shaving incentive.

**Role in Demand Response (DR):**

Demand response is the *intentional modification* of consumer load in response to a price signal or reliability event. RTP and CPP are the **two principal price-based DR mechanisms**:

1. **RTP-based DR (Continuous DR):** Consumers continuously respond to the dynamic price. Loads are automated to track price thresholds (e.g., "run the chiller only when price < ₹8/kWh"). Reduces peak demand and *integrates renewables* by absorbing surplus generation when prices are low or negative.

2. **CPP-based DR (Event-driven DR):** Consumers curtail discretionary loads (lighting, HVAC, EV charging, non-critical processes) during a small number of critical events. Provides *capacity adequacy* and *reliability* insurance to the utility at a fraction of the cost of a new peaker plant.

**Key Engineering Benefits:**
- Peak demand reduction of **10–30%** during critical events.
- Improved system **load factor** by 5–15 percentage points.
- Avoided capacity investment → reduced cost of supply for all consumers.
- Better integration of variable renewable energy (solar/wind).
- Empowers consumers to *actively manage* their bills and environmental footprint.

> *(For full marks, students should quote at least one numerical example showing bill reduction under RTP/CPP, mention the role of AMI/HEMS, and conclude with a comparison table.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Electricity Tariff (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Tariff = rate structure that recovers cost of supply and influences consumer behavior; *not* just ₹/kWh.
- **Four main objectives:** Cost recovery, equity, simplicity, DSM.
- **Flat Rate:** $R = C/E$ — simplest, penalizes low users, never used for industry.
- **Block Rate:** Slab-wise rates; cumulative vs non-cumulative; **non-cumulative is incentive-based**.
- **Two-Part (Hopkinson) Tariff:** $\text{Bill} = A \cdot kW_{MD} + B \cdot kWh$ — *most asked formula in KTU*.
- **Three-Part Tariff:** Fixed + semi-fixed + variable (or fixed + energy + fuel adjustment).
- **kVA MD Tariff:** $\text{Fixed} = A \cdot \dfrac{kW}{pf}$ — penalizes low PF automatically.
- **PF Penalty (kW MD + penalty):** 1–2% of energy bill per 0.01 fall below benchmark (typically 0.8 or 0.85 lag).
- **Sliding Scale:** Lower rate if LF or PF is above a threshold.
- **kW + kVAR Tariff:** Most scientific — reactive energy billed directly via kVARh meter.
- **TOU Tariff:** Peak (highest rate), Shoulder (moderate), Off-Peak (lowest). Smart-grid friendly.
- **RTP:** Hourly (or sub-hourly) market-driven price; requires AMI and HEMS.
- **CPP:** High rate on a small number of declared critical days; event-driven DR.
- **Net-Metering:** Prosumer exports solar surplus; import kWh minus export kWh; export feed-in tariff.
- **Key Formulas:** $kVA = kW/pf$, $LF = E / (MD \times T)$, $\text{Bill} = A \cdot kW + B \cdot kWh$, $\text{Penalty} = N \times (\% \text{ rate}) \times \text{Energy Bill}$.
- **Units discipline:** kW *or* kVA (specify!), kWh, ₹/kW/month, ₹/kVA/month, ₹/kWh.
- **PF improvement always reduces kVA → reduces demand charge → annual savings justify capacitor bank.**
- **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Smart-grid tariffs (TOU, RTP, CPP, Net-metering) carry higher weight than legacy tariffs in Part B questions.
- **Always draw a labeled diagram** in any 7-mark sub-question — examiners reserve 2 marks for it.
- **Common errors to avoid:** Mixing kW and kVA; forgetting PF penalty; not computing average rate; units mismatch; missing the demand charge in flat-rate comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Time Pricing, Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Real Time Pricing, Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Real Time Pricing (RTP) ### Formal Academic Definition **Real Time Pricing (RTP)** is a dynamic, time-varying electricity tariff mechanism in which the retail price of electrical energy is continuously updated—typically at intervals of **5 minutes, 15 minutes, or one hour**—in direct response to the prevailing wholesale market clearing price (locational marginal price, LMP), real-time system load, generation availability, and congestion on the transmission/distribution network. RTP is a cornerstone demand-response (DR) program under the smart-grid paradigm that converts the consumer into an active **"prosumer"** (producer + consumer) by exposing them to marginal cost-reflective price signals. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Uber Surge of Electricity"** > Imagine booking a cab during the morning rush hour — the fare dynamically rises. RTP works exactly the same way for electricity. When everyone in your city switches on ACs at 5 PM (peak demand), the grid is "stressed," and the price of every extra unit of electricity rises in real time. Smart meters in your home receive this live price, and your smart appliances (thermostat, water heater, EV charger) automatically **defer or shift** their consumption to cheaper, off-peak hours — saving you money and saving the grid from blackouts. RTP essentially gives the consumer a **"live financial pulse"** of the power grid. ### Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of RTP - **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED):** measures how sensitively consumers respond to price changes. - **Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR):** must be reduced after RTP deployment. - **Customer Baseline Load (CBL):** the reference consumption used to compute DR savings. - **Standard Reference Price:** typically the regulated flat tariff prior to RTP rollout (e.g., **₹6.5/kWh** in Kerala during 2024–25). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > RTP is classified under *Demand-Side Management (DSM) → Pricing-Based DR Programs* in Module 2. Students must distinguish it from **Time-of-Use (ToU) pricing** (predetermined blocks) and **Critical Peak Pricing (CPP)** (event-based spikes). --- ## 1.2 Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) System ### Formal Academic Definition **Automatic Meter Reading (AMR)** is a technology that enables the **one-way (uplink only)** automatic collection of consumption, diagnostic, and status data from utility meters (electrical, water, gas) and its transmission to a central database—via wired or wireless communication channels—**without requiring physical site visits** by meter readers. AMR is the foundational layer of the **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)**, providing utilities with high-resolution consumption data essential for billing, load research, outage management, and the operationalization of RTP. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Self-Reporting Electricity Diary"** > Picture a Fitbit for your house. You don't manually tell it how many steps you walked today — it automatically records and uploads to the cloud every hour. An AMR system does the same for your electricity consumption. The electromechanical meter in your house is replaced (or augmented) with a **smart electronic meter** that records your kWh usage every 15 minutes, and then automatically **texts/ transmits** the reading to the KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) office through cellular/radio signals. The bill arrives in your inbox — no human meter reader, no estimated bills, no tampering. Just like Fitbit transformed health monitoring, AMR transformed utility metering. ### Functional Distinction from AMI > [!NOTE] > - **AMR** = **One-way** communication (meter → utility). It is "talking" only. > - **AMI** = **Two-way** communication (utility ⇄ meter). It is "talking **and** listening." AMI supports remote connect/disconnect, RTP signal reception, and direct load control. > - AMR is therefore a **subset** of AMI. ### Key Performance Indicators of AMR - **Meter Read Accuracy:** ≥ **99.5%** successful reads per billing cycle. - **Data Latency:** ≤ **15 minutes** for hourly reads (per CEA India regulations). - **Communication Range:** **100 m – 10 km** depending on technology (RF mesh vs. cellular). - **Battery Life of Meter:** **≥ 15 years** (IS 16444 / IEC 62056 compliance). - **Tamper Detection:** Logged with timestamp and **≥ 95%** detection rate. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Real Time Pricing — Operational Architecture ### 2.1.1 The Three-Tier RTP Hierarchy RTP in a smart grid operates across three temporally distinct layers, each of which KTU examiners love to test: 1. **Day-Ahead RTP (DAP):** Prices are computed for each of the 24 hours of the next day and published by **06:00 hrs** the previous day. Consumers pre-schedule their loads. 2. **Hour-Ahead RTP (HAP):** Prices are re-calculated every hour for the next hour, using updated wind/solar forecasts and load forecasts. 3. **Real-Time (Balancing) RTP:** Updated every **5–15 minutes** to maintain instantaneous supply-demand balance in the wholesale market (e.g., Indian Energy Exchange — IEX). ### 2.1.2 Mathematical Formulation of the RTP Signal The retail RTP signal received by the consumer is essentially the **wholesale LMP** plus regulated retail components (transmission charge, distribution charge, taxes, cross-subsidy, fixed charge). The simplified form is: $$ P_{\text{RTP}}(t) \;=\; P_{\text{base}} \;+\; \alpha \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{D(t) - \bar{D}}{\bar{D}}}_{\text{Load deviation term}} \;+\; \beta \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{G_{\text{loss}}(t)}{G_{\text{total}}(t)}}_{\text{Loss/cost term}} $$ where the terms are defined in the formula sheet below. ### 2.1.3 Consumer Response Model A consumer's modified demand under RTP, assuming linear price elasticity, is given by: $$ D(t) \;=\; D_0(t) \cdot \left[\,1 \;+\; \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{RTP}}(t) - P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\,\right] $$ - $\varepsilon < 0$ ⇒ demand falls when price rises (typical for residential). - $|\varepsilon|$ is small in the short run (inelastic) and larger in the long run (elastic, when smart appliances automate the response). ### 2.1.4 Why RTP Works — The Underlying "Why" - **Marginal Cost Reflection:** Wholesale prices already reflect the true marginal cost of generation; transmitting this to retail aligns the system. - **Peak Shaving:** The elasticity-induced load shift flattens the load curve, reducing the need for expensive peak plants. - **Renewable Integration:** RTP incentivizes consumption when solar/wind generation is high (prices drop, sometimes go negative). - **Reduced Stranded Asset Risk:** Lower peak demand ⇒ deferral of new generation and T&D capacity. --- ## 2.2 Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) — Operational Architecture ### 2.2.1 Three-Layer AMR Architecture 1. **Field Layer (Smart Meter + Sensors):** Electronic meter with μ-controller, A/D conversion of current/voltage, energy computation chip, and communication module. 2. **Communication Layer (WAN/NAN/LAN):** - **LAN (Local Area Network):** Zigbee, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, M-Bus (in-home display). - **NAN (Neighbourhood Area Network):** RF mesh (868/915 MHz), LoRaWAN, NB-IoT. - **WAN (Wide Area Network):** Cellular (2G/3G/4G/5G), GPRS, PLC on MV lines. 3. **Head-End System + MDMS Layer:** Central server receives, validates, stores, and processes meter data; **Meter Data Management System (MDMS)** is the brain. ### 2.2.2 Communication Technology Comparison (Critical for KTU) | Technology | Range | Data Rate | Topology | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **PLC (Power Line Communication)** | 1–3 km on LV | 10–500 kbps | Bus | Dense urban, India-preferred | | **RF Mesh (Zigbee/6LoWPAN)** | 100 m–2 km | 20–250 kbps | Mesh | Urban/semi-urban | | **Cellular (GPRS/4G/NB-IoT)** | 1–10 km | 10 kbps–100 Mbps | Star | Rural, low density | | **LoRaWAN** | 2–10 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Star | Long-range, low-power | | **Wi-Fi (HAN)** | <50 m | 11–600 Mbps | Star | In-home display | ### 2.2.3 Why AMR Works — The "Why" - Eliminates **human reading errors** and **estimated billing**. - Detects **theft, tamper, and leakages** (AT&C losses in India were **~15–18%** pre-AMR, targeted < 12% post-AMR under UDAY scheme). - Provides **15-minute interval data** essential for RTP billing. - Enables **outage detection** (last gasp message) and **remote disconnect** in AMI. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table before any KTU 2024 ESE question on Module 2.** | # | Formula / Relation | Description of Variables | Unit | Boundary / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{\text{RTP}}(t) = \lambda_{\text{LMP}}(t) + C_T + C_D + C_{\text{fix}}$ | RTP retail price | ₹/kWh | Published every 5–15 min | | 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta D / D_0}{\Delta P / P_0}$ | Price elasticity of demand | dimensionless | Residential: $-0.1$ to $-0.3$ | | 3 | $D(t) = D_0(t)\big[1 + \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{P(t) - P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\big]$ | Demand response to RTP | kW | Linear elasticity regime | | 4 | $\text{PAR} = \dfrac{\max_t\, D(t)}{\bar{D}}$ | Peak-to-Average Ratio | dimensionless | Reduced from 1.8 → 1.4 with RTP | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{DR}} = \dfrac{\text{Load shifted}}{\text{Total load during event}} \times 100\%$ | DR effectiveness | % | 10–25% typical | | 6 | $T_{\text{latency}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{packet}} + H_{\text{overhead}}}{B_{\text{channel}}}$ | AMR communication latency | seconds | ≤ 15 min for hourly reads | | 7 | $N_{\text{bits}} = N_{\text{meters}} \times R \times T \times b$ | Total AMR data volume | bits | 15-min reads → GB/day | | 8 | $C_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{cap}} + N_{\text{yr}} \cdot C_{\text{O\&M}} - S_{\text{salvage}}$ | AMR system lifecycle cost | ₹ | 15-year life assumed | | 9 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{C_{\text{cap}}}{\Delta R_{\text{annual}}}$ | Simple payback period | years | 4–7 years for AMR | | 10 | $\text{AT\&C Loss} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{billed}}}{R_{\text{supplied}}} \times 100\%$ | Aggregate Technical & Commercial loss | % | India target: < 12% | | 11 | $\eta_{\text{success}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{reads received}}}{N_{\text{reads attempted}}} \times 100\%$ | AMR read success rate | % | ≥ 99.5% (IS 16444) | | 12 | $E_{\text{cons}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} V(t) \cdot I(t) \cdot \cos\phi(t) \cdot \Delta t$ | Energy computed by smart meter | kWh | Sampled at 1–4 kHz | **Notation Glossary:** - $\lambda_{\text{LMP}}(t)$: Locational Marginal Price at bus at time $t$ - $C_T, C_D, C_{\text{fix}}$: Transmission, Distribution, Fixed charges (₹/kWh or ₹/month) - $D_0(t)$: Baseline (un-priced) demand at time $t$ - $S_{\text{packet}}$: AMR data packet size (e.g., 200 bytes per interval) - $H_{\text{overhead}}$: Protocol header (e.g., 40 bytes for TCP/IP) - $B_{\text{channel}}$: Channel bandwidth (bits/s) - $N_{\text{meters}}$: Number of meters in the AMI cluster - $R$: Reads per day (e.g., 96 for 15-min interval) - $b$: Bits per reading (typically 16–32) - $R_{\text{billed}}, R_{\text{supplied}}$: Revenue billed, energy supplied > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas directly support utility business cases, regulatory tariff petitions, and the *Central Electricity Authority (CEA) — Smart Meter National Programme (SMNP)* under which 250 million meters are to be deployed in India by **2026**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Consumer Bill Under RTP vs. Flat Tariff **Problem Statement:** A residential consumer in Kerala has the following 24-hour load profile, with RTP prices varying by hour. Compute the bill under **(a)** flat tariff and **(b)** RTP tariff, and determine the **daily saving** if the consumer shifts 30% of peak load to off-peak (elasticity assumed: $\varepsilon = -0.15$). ### Given Data | Time Slot | Load $D_0(t)$ (kWh/h) | Flat Price $P_f$ (₹/kWh) | RTP $P_{\text{RTP}}(t)$ (₹/kWh) | |---|---|---|---| | 00:00 – 06:00 (Off-peak) | 0.5 each hour | **₹6.5** | **₹3.5** | | 06:00 – 18:00 (Normal) | 1.0 each hour | **₹6.5** | **₹7.0** | | 18:00 – 22:00 (Peak) | 2.5 each hour | **₹6.5** | **₹11.0** | | 22:00 – 24:00 (Off-peak) | 0.5 each hour | **₹6.5** | **₹3.5** | ### Step 1 — Compute Total Energy Consumed Sum all hourly loads over 24 hours: $$ E_{\text{total}} = (6 \times 0.5) + (12 \times 1.0) + (4 \times 2.5) + (2 \times 0.5) $$ Expanding each term: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{total}} &= 3.0 + 12.0 + 10.0 + 1.0 \\ &= 26.0 \;\text{kWh/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Bill Under Flat Tariff Direct multiplication of total energy by flat rate: $$ B_{\text{flat}} = E_{\text{total}} \times P_f = 26.0 \times 6.5 = \mathbf{₹169.0} $$ ### Step 3 — Bill Under RTP Without Demand Response Compute hourly cost and sum: $$ B_{\text{RTP}}^{\text{no-DR}} = \sum_{t=1}^{24} D_0(t) \cdot P_{\text{RTP}}(t) $$ Splitting by time slot: $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{RTP}}^{\text{no-DR}} &= (6 \times 0.5 \times 3.5) + (12 \times 1.0 \times 7.0) + (4 \times 2.5 \times 11.0) + (2 \times 0.5 \times 3.5) \\ &= 10.5 + 84.0 + 110.0 + 3.5 \\ &= \mathbf{₹208.0} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Without DR, RTP bill is *higher* (₹208 vs. ₹169) because peak prices are steep. The consumer must shift load to benefit. ### Step 4 — Apply Demand Response (30% Peak → Off-Peak Shift) Total shifted energy: $$ E_{\text{shift}} = 0.30 \times E_{\text{peak}} = 0.30 \times 10.0 = 3.0 \;\text{kWh} $$ Updated demand at each hour: - New peak load: $D_{\text{peak}}^{\text{new}} = 2.5 - (3.0/4) = 1.75$ kWh/h - New off-peak load: $D_{\text{off}}^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + (3.0/8) = 0.875$ kWh/h (3 kWh spread over 8 off-peak hours) ### Step 5 — Bill Under RTP With DR $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{RTP}}^{\text{DR}} &= (8 \times 0.875 \times 3.5) + (12 \times 1.0 \times 7.0) + (4 \times 1.75 \times 11.0) \\ &= 24.5 + 84.0 + 77.0 \\ &= \mathbf{₹185.5} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Compute Daily Savings and Annualized Impact $$ \text{Saving}_{\text{daily}} = B_{\text{flat}} - B_{\text{RTP}}^{\text{DR}} = 169.0 - 185.5 = -16.5 $$ Wait — savings are **negative** even after DR! This is because the elasticity is small and peak prices are too high. The consumer would need to shift **at least 50%** of peak load to break even. This is a **very real finding** in KSEB pilot studies. **Required peak-shift fraction for break-even:** Set $B_{\text{RTP}}^{\text{DR}} = B_{\text{flat}}$ and solve for shift fraction $x$: $$ 169.0 = [8(0.5 + 0.5x/2) \cdot 3.5] + [12 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 7.0] + [4(2.5 - 2.5x) \cdot 11.0] $$ Solving gives $x \approx 0.55$, i.e., consumer must shift **55%** of peak load to off-peak. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AMR Communication Latency **Problem Statement:** A KSEB distribution substation has **5,000 smart meters** reporting 15-minute interval data. Each interval reading is **32 bits**, packet header is **40 bytes**, and the cellular GPRS uplink provides **40 kbps**. Compute (a) total daily data volume, and (b) average transmission latency per meter. ### Step 1 — Number of Intervals per Day $$ N_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{24 \times 60}{15} = 96 \;\text{intervals/day} $$ ### Step 2 — Data Per Reading (Payload) $$ S_{\text{reading}} = 32 \;\text{bits} = 4 \;\text{bytes} $$ ### Step 3 — Total Packet Size $$ S_{\text{packet}} = S_{\text{reading}} + H_{\text{overhead}} = 4 + 40 = 44 \;\text{bytes/reading} $$ ### Step 4 — Total Daily Data Volume $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{daily}} &= N_{\text{meters}} \times N_{\text{int}} \times S_{\text{packet}} \\ &= 5{,}000 \times 96 \times 44 \\ &= 21{,}120{,}000 \;\text{bytes} \\ &= \mathbf{21.12 \;\text{MB/day}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Per-Meter Latency $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{latency}} &= \dfrac{S_{\text{packet}} \times 8}{B_{\text{channel}}} \\ &= \dfrac{44 \times 8}{40 \times 10^3} \\ &= \dfrac{352}{40{,}000} \\ &= \mathbf{8.8 \;\text{ms}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The 8.8 ms latency is negligible, but **throughput** (5,000 meters × 96 intervals = 480,000 packets/day) is the real challenge. Head-end server must handle **bursts of ~5.5 packets/second** at peak. This is why AMI uses **concentrators** (data aggregation at feeder level). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RTP vs. ToU vs. Flat vs. CPP | Attribute | Flat Tariff | Time-of-Use (ToU) | Critical Peak Pricing (CPP) | Real Time Pricing (RTP) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Price Update Frequency** | Once a year | 2–3 blocks/day | Event-based (≤ 15/yr) | Every 5 min – 1 hr | | **Predictability** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | | **DR Signal Strength** | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | **Implementation Cost** | ₹ 0 | ₹ 500/meter | ₹ 1,500/meter | ₹ 2,500/meter | | **Customer Acceptance** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | | **Peak Shaving Potential** | < 3% | 5–10% | 10–20% | 15–30% | | **Consumer Risk** | Utility | Low | Medium | High | | **Data Requirement** | Monthly read | Monthly read | Hourly read | 5-min interval | | **Enabling Tech Required** | Electromechanical meter | Electronic meter + ToU register | AMI | AMI + Home Energy Management | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Compare RTP and ToU pricing. Why is RTP considered a stronger DR tool but harder to implement?"* — Use the matrix above as your answer scaffold. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AMR Communication Technologies | Attribute | PLC | RF Mesh (Zigbee) | Cellular (GPRS/4G/NB-IoT) | LoRaWAN | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Range** | 1–3 km | 100 m–2 km | 1–10 km (cell) | 2–10 km | | **Data Rate** | 10–500 kbps | 20–250 kbps | 10 kbps–100 Mbps | 0.3–50 kbps | | **Topology** | Bus | Mesh | Star | Star | | **Capex/Meter** | ₹ 200 | ₹ 350 | ₹ 150 | ₹ 250 | | **Best For** | Dense urban | Urban cluster | Rural | Sparse rural | | **Limitation** | LV line noise | Interference | SIM cost | Low payload | | **India Adoption (2024)** | **High (BPL, Genus, HPL)** | Medium | **High (Tata Power, BSES)** | Pilot stage | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Real Time Pricing — End-to-End System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wholesale Market
IEX PXIL] --> B[ISO System Operator
LMP Computation] B --> C[Utility Pricing Engine
Retail RTP Calculation] C --> D[AMI Head End System
HES] D --> E[Communication Network
PLC RF Cellular] E --> F[Smart Meter at Consumer
HAN Gateway] F --> G[In Home Display IHD] F --> H[Smart Appliances
HEMS Auto DR] F --> I[Consumer Bill
15 min RTP Tariff] H --> J[Reduced Peak Demand
PAR Improvement] J --> B style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000 style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style J fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The **left side** (yellow → green) is the **price origination** path — wholesale market prices flow to retail. > - The **right side** (pink) is the **consumer response** path — smart appliances react to the price signal. > - The **bottom feedback loop** (green) closes the system: reduced demand in turn reduces wholesale price — this is the **self-stabilizing property** of RTP-enabled smart grids. --- ## 4.2 AMR System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FieldLayer["FIELD LAYER - CONSUMER PREMISES"] M1[Smart Meter Phase A] M2[Smart Meter Phase B] M3[Smart Meter Phase C] M4[Gas and Water Meters] end subgraph CommLayer["COMMUNICATION LAYER"] DC1[Data Concentrator
at Distribution Transformer] NAN[Neighbourhood Area Network
RF Mesh PLC] WAN1[WAN Backhaul
Cellular OFC] end subgraph CenterLayer["CENTRE LAYER - UTILITY CONTROL ROOM"] HES1[Head End System HES] MDMS1[Meter Data Management
System MDMS] OMS1[Outage Management
System] BIS[Billing and IT System] CIS[Customer Information
System] end subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER - ANALYTICS"] RTP1[Real Time Pricing Engine] TAMP1[Tamper and Theft Analytics] LOAD1[Load Research and Forecasting] DSM1[Demand Side Management] end M1 --> DC1 M2 --> DC1 M3 --> DC1 M4 --> DC1 DC1 --> NAN NAN --> WAN1 WAN1 --> HES1 HES1 --> MDMS1 HES1 --> OMS1 MDMS1 --> BIS MDMS1 --> CIS MDMS1 --> RTP1 MDMS1 --> TAMP1 MDMS1 --> LOAD1 MDMS1 --> DSM1 style M1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style M2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style M3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style M4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style DC1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style NAN fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style WAN1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style HES1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style MDMS1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style RTP1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style TAMP1 fill:#F08080,stroke:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"How is AMR data flow structured? Differentiate AMI Head End, MDMS, and Billing System."* > - **HES** = real-time data collection, protocol termination, command dispatch. > - **MDMS** = long-term storage, validation, estimation, aggregation. > - **Billing System** = applies tariffs (flat/ToU/RTP) to validated data, generates invoice. --- ## 4.3 RTP Price Signal Timeline — A 24-Hour View ```mermaid gantt title RTP Price Signal Intensity Over 24 Hours (Kerala Reference Day) dateFormat HH:mm axisFormat %H:%M section Night Valley Off-Peak Low Price (3.5 INR per kWh) :a1, 00:00, 360min section Morning Ramp Moderate Price (7.0 INR per kWh) :a2, 06:00, 720min section Evening Peak High Price RTP (11.0 INR per kWh) :crit, 18:00, 240min section Late Night Off-Peak Low Price (3.5 INR per kWh) :a3, 22:00, 120min ``` > [!NOTE] > The **red (critical)** block from **18:00–22:00** is when RTP peaks. Smart appliances (EV chargers, geysers, dishwashers) should be **scheduled outside this window** for maximum savings. --- ## 4.4 AMR Communication Topology — Cellular Star ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Smart Meter 001] -->|GPRS| BTS1[Cellular Tower BTS] M2[Smart Meter 002] -->|GPRS| BTS1 M3[Smart Meter 500] -->|GPRS| BTS1 M4[Smart Meter 501] -->|GPRS| BTS2[Cellular Tower BTS 2] M5[Smart Meter 1500] -->|GPRS| BTS2 BTS1 -->|OFC MPLS| HESC[HES Cloud Server] BTS2 -->|OFC MPLS| HESC HESC --> MDMSC[MDMS Central DB] style M1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 style M2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 style M3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 style M4 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 style M5 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 style BTS1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style BTS2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style HESC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style MDMSC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Real Time Pricing (RTP). How does it differ from Time-of-Use (ToU) pricing? [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **Model Answer (3 marks):** Real Time Pricing (RTP) is a dynamic electricity tariff where the retail price of energy is updated at very short intervals (typically 5 min to 1 hour) in response to real-time wholesale market conditions — locational marginal price, system load, and generation availability. It is a **demand-response program** that transmits live marginal-cost signals to end consumers via AMI infrastructure. **Difference from ToU (2 marks):** - **ToU:** Predetermined, fixed blocks (e.g., peak 18:00–22:00 ₹10, off-peak ₹5) — updated only seasonally. - **RTP:** Continuous, market-driven, updated every 5–60 minutes; reflects real grid stress and renewable generation. - **ToU =** predictable, low consumer risk; **RTP =** volatile, high consumer risk but high DR signal strength. --- ### Q2. List any **six advantages** of Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) over manual meter reading. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Model Answer (3 marks — list six, ½ mark each):** 1. Eliminates human meter-reading errors. 2. Removes estimated billing — actual consumption is recorded. 3. Enables **time-of-use / RTP billing** using interval data. 4. Faster **outage detection** via "last gasp" alarms. 5. Reduces **AT&C losses** by detecting tamper and theft. 6. Provides high-resolution **load profile data** for planning. 7. (Bonus) Reduces operational cost (no field staff for routine reads). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model] **(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a Real Time Pricing (RTP) system in a smart grid. List the four essential layers involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A residential consumer uses **800 kWh** in a billing month with the following load distribution: | Time Block | Hours | Energy (kWh) | RTP (₹/kWh) | |---|---|---|---| | Off-Peak | 6 hrs/day × 30 = 180 hr | 180 | 4.0 | | Normal | 12 hrs/day × 30 = 360 hr | 380 | 6.5 | | Peak | 6 hrs/day × 30 = 180 hr | 240 | 10.0 | Compute the **monthly bill under RTP** and compare it with a flat tariff of **₹7.0/kWh**. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Q-A **(a) RTP Architecture (7 marks):** The RTP system is structured in **four layers**: 1. **Wholesale Market Layer (LMP Generation):** The system operator (e.g., NLDC, IEX) computes the **Locational Marginal Price** every 15 min based on merit-order dispatch, congestion, and losses. 2. **Utility Pricing Layer (Retail RTP Computation):** The distribution utility receives LMP and adds regulated components: wheeling charges, distribution loss compensation, fixed charge, taxes, and cross-subsidy — producing the **retail RTP signal** $P_{\text{RTP}}(t)$. 3. **Communication Layer (AMI Backbone):** The retail RTP signal is published via AMI — from head-end server to data concentrators, and finally to **smart meters** at consumer premises through PLC / RF Mesh / Cellular. 4. **Consumer Response Layer (HEMS / Auto-DR):** The smart meter displays price on an **In-Home Display (IHD)**. A **Home Energy Management System (HEMS)** receives the price and automatically schedules deferrable loads (EV charging, geysers, washing machines) to low-price windows. The response is fed back into the wholesale market as reduced demand, completing the **closed loop**. > [!NOTE] > **Working summary:** Wholesale LMP → Utility retail RTP calc → AMI broadcast → Smart meter display → HEMS automates response → Reduced demand → Lower LMP (closed loop). **(b) Bill Computation (7 marks):** **RTP Bill (sum of energy × price per block):** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{RTP}} &= (180 \times 4.0) + (380 \times 6.5) + (240 \times 10.0) \\ &= 720 + 2470 + 2400 \\ &= \mathbf{₹5{,}590} \end{aligned} $$ **Flat Tariff Bill:** $$ B_{\text{flat}} = 800 \times 7.0 = \mathbf{₹5{,}600} $$ **Comparison:** $$ \Delta B = B_{\text{flat}} - B_{\text{RTP}} = 5600 - 5590 = \mathbf{₹10} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** The savings are marginal (₹10 / ₹5600 = 0.18%) because the consumer already has a balanced load profile. The real value of RTP emerges when (i) the consumer **shifts peak load** to off-peak (DR), or (ii) the **peak-to-off-peak price ratio is higher** (e.g., ₹15 vs. ₹2). --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) system**. Discuss the role of **HES, MDMS, and the billing system** distinctly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A utility has **50,000 smart meters** each sending **96 readings per day** of **16 bits** each, with a TCP/IP header of **40 bytes**. If the backhaul cellular link is **128 kbps**, compute: - (i) Total daily data volume from the field. - (ii) Effective throughput requirement in **packets per second** during a 1-hour upload window. - (iii) Comment on whether a 128 kbps link is sufficient. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Q-B **(a) AMR System with Block Diagram (7 marks):** *(Draw/describe the architecture shown in Section 4.2.)* **Working Principle:** 1. **Smart meter** samples V and I, computes kWh using DSP/ASIC at intervals (e.g., 15 min). 2. The reading is **packetized** with timestamp, meter ID, and header. 3. **Communication module** (PLC / RF / GPRS) transmits the packet through the **Neighbourhood Area Network (NAN)** to a **Data Concentrator** at the distribution transformer. 4. Concentrator aggregates and forwards to the **Head End System (HES)** via WAN (cellular/OFC). 5. HES performs **protocol termination, decryption, deduplication**. 6. Validated data is pushed to **MDMS (Meter Data Management System)** for storage, validation, VEE (Validation, Estimation, Editing). 7. MDMS hands over aggregated 15-min/30-min data to the **Billing System**, which applies the tariff (flat/ToU/RTP) and generates invoices. **Distinct Roles (3 marks):** | System | Primary Role | Examples | |---|---|---| | **HES** | Real-time collection; protocol termination; command dispatch | Itron OpenWay, Landis+Gyr | | **MDMS** | Long-term storage; VEE; aggregation; analytics | Oracle MDM, Siemens EnergyIP | | **Billing** | Tariff application; invoice generation; collection | SAP IS-U, custom utility IT | **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** **(i) Total Daily Data Volume:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\text{reading}} &= 16 \;\text{bits} = 2 \;\text{bytes} \\ S_{\text{packet}} &= 2 + 40 = 42 \;\text{bytes} \\ V_{\text{daily}} &= 50{,}000 \times 96 \times 42 \\ &= 201{,}600{,}000 \;\text{bytes} \\ &= \mathbf{192.31 \;\text{MB/day}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Throughput in Packets/s During 1-Hour Window:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{packets}} &= 50{,}000 \times 96 = 4{,}800{,}000 \;\text{packets/day} \\ N_{\text{packets/1hr}} &= 4{,}800{,}000 / 24 = 200{,}000 \;\text{packets/hour} \\ \text{Rate} &= 200{,}000 / 3600 = \mathbf{55.6 \;\text{packets/s}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Sufficiency Check:** Required bandwidth if all packets sent in 1 hour: $$ B_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{4{,}800{,}000 \times 42 \times 8}{3600} = \mathbf{448 \;\text{kbps}} $$ Available bandwidth = **128 kbps**. $$ \dfrac{B_{\text{req}}}{B_{\text{avail}}} = \dfrac{448}{128} = 3.5 $$ > [!WARNING] > **The 128 kbps link is INSUFFICIENT by a factor of 3.5.** Either (a) increase backhaul bandwidth to ≥ 512 kbps, (b) use data concentrators to **aggregate and compress** (e.g., delta encoding) at feeder level, or (c) deploy **staggered upload** windows (e.g., 1/3 of meters per hour). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before Writing the Exam:** > 1. **Confusing AMR with AMI** in definitions — AMR is *one-way*, AMI is *two-way* (board examiners deduct 1–2 marks). > 2. **Writing only generic advantages** of RTP ("saves money") without quantifying (PAR reduction, elasticity value) — KTU 2024 scheme demands **numerical/quantitative answers**. > 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** AMR packet size is in **bytes**; bandwidth is in **bits per second**. Multiply by 8 — this single error loses full marks in (b) numericals. > 4. **Not closing the loop in RTP diagrams:** the *feedback path* from consumer response to wholesale market is **the most important pedagogical element** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this. > 5. **Mixing HES and MDMS functions** — HES is real-time, MDMS is analytical. Do not interchange. > 6. **Assuming infinite price elasticity** — residential $\varepsilon$ is only $-0.1$ to $-0.3$; full demand collapse at high price is unrealistic. > 7. **Ignoring fixed charges** — KSEB bills have a fixed component (~₹100/month) independent of consumption; missing this affects the total bill comparison. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 KTU Module 2 — RTP & AMR Quick-Fire Revision:** - ⭐ **RTP** updates price every **5 min – 1 hr**; ToU updates seasonally; CPP is event-triggered. - ⭐ RTP formula (simplified): $P_{\text{RTP}} = P_{\text{base}} + \alpha(D-\bar D)/\bar D + \beta \cdot G_{\text{loss}}/G_{\text{total}}$ - ⭐ **Price elasticity** $\varepsilon$ is the **bridge** between price signal and demand response. - ⭐ RTP **reduces PAR** (Peak-to-Average Ratio) and **shifts load to renewable-rich hours**. - ⭐ RTP requires **AMI** (two-way comms + smart meter + MDMS + HEMS). - ⭐ **AMR = one-way (meter → utility); AMI = two-way** (utility ⇄ meter). AMR ⊂ AMI. - ⭐ AMR components: **Smart meter + Concentrator + NAN/WAN + HES + MDMS + Billing**. - ⭐ Communication techs: **PLC** (urban, Indian-preferred), **RF mesh** (cluster), **Cellular** (rural), **LoRaWAN** (long-range, low-power). - ⭐ AMR data rate (bits/day) $= N_{\text{meters}} \times N_{\text{reads}} \times b$; latency $= S_{\text{packet}}/B_{\text{channel}}$. - ⭐ **Read success rate ≥ 99.5%**, **latency ≤ 15 min** (CEA, IS 16444 standard). - ⭐ **AT&C loss reduction** is the biggest Indian motivation for AMR/AMI rollout (target: < 12%). - ⭐ In Kerala, the pilot RTP-AMI project by **KSEB & IntelliSmart (2023–24)** covered ~ 50,000 consumers in Kochi. - ⭐ KTU exam keyword mappings: **"dynamic pricing"** → RTP; **"interval data"** → AMR; **"two-way communication"** → AMI; **"prosumer"** → RTP-enabled consumer; **"last gasp"** → AMR outage detection; **"VEE"** → MDMS function. - ⭐ Always quantify: **PAR reduction, ₹ savings, kWh shifted, % AT&C loss reduction**. - ⭐ Mention real Indian schemes: **SMNP (Smart Meter National Programme)**, **UDAY**, **RDSS (Revamped Distribution Sector Scheme)** in any Indian-context question. - ⭐ RTP disadvantages to mention for balance: price volatility, consumer risk, requires automation, equity concerns for low-income consumers.

topics.title: Services and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Services and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of a **Smart Grid**, *Services* and *Functions* are two closely related but distinct concepts that define what the grid *delivers to its stakeholders* and *how it operates internally* to deliver those deliverables.

- **Smart Grid Services** are the **value-added capabilities** offered to end-users (consumers, utilities, prosumers, and third-party aggregators) by leveraging bidirectional communication, automation, and information technology. They are *customer-facing* and *market-facing* outputs.
- **Smart Grid Functions** are the **internal operational mechanisms** embedded within the grid's architecture that enable these services. They are the *engine-level capabilities* of sensing, control, protection, computation, and self-management that operate transparently in the background.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Definition Standard:**
> "A *Smart Grid Function* is a foundational operational capability (e.g., self-healing, advanced metering, real-time monitoring) that enables the grid to perform intelligently. A *Smart Grid Service* is the user-visible benefit (e.g., dynamic pricing, demand response, remote connect/disconnect) that is delivered to consumers and utilities as a consequence of one or more functions operating in concert."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Smart Grid as a **modern smartphone**:
- **Functions** are the internal hardware + operating system — the touchscreen, the processor, the cellular modem, the battery management chip, the GPS sensor. You rarely see them directly, but everything depends on them.
- **Services** are the user-facing apps and features — WhatsApp, Google Pay, Uber, Netflix, real-time price tracking. Each service is built on top of multiple functions working together.

If the **GPS sensor** (function) stops working, **Uber** (service) cannot locate your driver. Similarly, if the **Phasor Measurement Unit** (function) fails, the **Wide-Area Real-Time Monitoring Service** cannot warn operators about grid instability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook for KTU Exams:**
> - **F**unctions = **F**oundational (internal, behind-the-scenes, hardware/software level)
> - **S**ervices = **S**urface-level (external, customer/market facing)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Values

The following are the key standard metrics used to evaluate smart grid services/functions:

| Metric | Typical Standard / Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System Average Interruption Duration Index (SAIDI)** | < 90 min/year (best utilities) | Reliability benchmark |
| **System Average Interruption Frequency Index (SAIFI)** | < 1.0 interruptions/year | Outage frequency |
| **Customer Average Interruption Duration Index (CAIDI)** | < 60 min | Average restoration time |
| **Momentary Average Interruption Frequency Index (MAIFI)** | < 5 events/year | Momentary outage count |
| **Smart Meter Data Refresh Rate** | 15 minutes to 1 hour | AMI service latency |
| **PMU Reporting Rate** | **30–60 samples/second** | Wide-area monitoring fidelity |
| **Demand Response Activation Time** | < 5 minutes (automated) | Speed of DR service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently cite **SAIDI, SAIFI, and CAIDI** in the regulation/compliance module. Memorize their formulas:
> - $\text{SAIDI} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Customer Interruption Durations}}{\text{Total Customers Served}}$
> - $\text{SAIFI} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Customer Interruptions}}{\text{Total Customers Served}}$
> - $\text{CAIDI} = \dfrac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Smart Grid Functions — The Internal Engine

Smart grid functions are typically classified into **eight primary functional categories** as defined by the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and the IEEE 2030 standard, which KTU 2024 syllabus adopts.

#### 2.1.1 Self-Healing Function

- **Why it exists:** Conventional grids are passive — faults cause widespread blackouts. Smart grids must *detect, isolate, and restore* autonomously.
- **How it works:**
  1. **Detection:** Sensors (PMUs, smart relays, fault indicators) detect anomalies (overcurrent, voltage sag, frequency deviation).
  2. **Isolation:** Automated switches (reclosers, sectionalizers) open to isolate the faulted section.
  3. **Reconfiguration:** The DMS (Distribution Management System) reroutes power through alternate feeders (network reconfiguration).
  4. **Restoration:** Service is restored to healthy sections within seconds to minutes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The **2003 Northeast Blackout** (U.S./Canada) affected 55 million people for up to 4 days. A self-healing grid with PMU-based wide-area monitoring would have detected the cascading failure within milliseconds and prevented the propagation.

#### 2.1.2 Consumer Participation Function

- Enables bidirectional energy and information flow.
- Consumers become **prosumers** (producer + consumer) via rooftop solar, battery storage, EVs.
- Smart Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS) and IoT-based load scheduling.

#### 2.1.3 Resistance to Attack / Cybersecurity Function

- **Why:** A smart grid is a cyber-physical system — vulnerable to data injection, false-data attacks, DoS, and ransomware.
- **How:** Encryption (AES-256), intrusion detection systems (IDS), public key infrastructure (PKI), blockchain-based energy trading.

#### 2.1.4 Power Quality Function

- Monitors and mitigates harmonics, voltage sags, swells, flicker.
- Uses **FACTS** (Flexible AC Transmission Systems) devices like **STATCOM**, **SVC**, **UPFC**.
- Distributed power quality conditioners at customer premises.

#### 2.1.5 Accommodation of All Generation and Storage Options

- Plug-and-play integration of **DERs** (Distributed Energy Resources): solar PV, wind, micro-turbines, fuel cells.
- Storage: Li-ion batteries, pumped hydro, supercapacitors, hydrogen storage.

#### 2.1.6 Enable New Products, Services, and Markets

- Dynamic Time-of-Use (ToU) pricing, real-time pricing (RTP), critical peak pricing (CPP).
- Peer-to-peer (P2P) energy trading, transactive energy markets.

#### 2.1.7 Optimize Asset Utilization and Operate Efficiently

- **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** using sensor data, thermal imaging, dissolved gas analysis (DGA) for transformers.
- Dynamic Line Rating (DLR) — uses real-time weather and conductor temperature to safely push more power through existing lines.

#### 2.1.8 Enable High Penetration of Renewable Energy

- Advanced inverters with grid-support functions (Volt-VAR, Volt-Watt, frequency ride-through).
- Forecasting (solar irradiance, wind speed) using machine learning.

### 2.2 Smart Grid Services — The User-Visible Deliverables

| Domain | Service | Underlying Function(s) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Consumer-Side** | Time-of-Use Billing | AMI, Smart Metering |
| | Remote Connect/Disconnect | AMI + Communication Network |
| | In-Home Display of Energy Use | HAN, HEMS, Smart Meter |
| | Prepaid Electricity | AMI + Billing System |
| | Electric Vehicle Charging Management | AMI + DERMS + Forecasting |
| **Utility-Side** | Outage Management (OMS) | SCADA + AMI + GIS |
| | Distribution Automation | DMS + Reclosers + Sensors |
| | Asset Health Monitoring | Sensors + Analytics |
| | Workforce Management | GIS + Mobile Workforce |
| | Theft Detection | AMI + Energy Balance Analytics |
| **Grid-Side** | Frequency Regulation | WAMS + PMU + Energy Storage |
| | Voltage/Reactive Power Support | STATCOM/SVC + PMU |
| | Black Start Capability | Microgrid + DERMS |
| **Market/Third-Party** | Demand Response Aggregation | AMI + DRMS + Forecasting |
| | Virtual Power Plant (VPP) | DERMS + Communication + Optimization |
| | Peer-to-Peer Trading | Blockchain + AMI + Smart Contracts |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical**. KTU board questions frequently test these in numerical form (7–14 marks).

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **SAIDI** | $\text{SAIDI} = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i \cdot r_i}{N_T}$ | Reliability metric, minutes/year |
| 2 | **SAIFI** | $\text{SAIFI} = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i}{N_T}$ | Outage frequency |
| 3 | **CAIDI** | $\text{CAIDI} = \dfrac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}}$ | Average restoration time |
| 4 | **MAIFI** | $\text{MAIFI} = \dfrac{\sum N_{mi}}{N_T}$ | Momentary outage index |
| 5 | **ASAIDI** (Adjusted) | $\text{ASAIDI} = \dfrac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{Avg. Load Served}}$ | Weighted reliability |
| 6 | **DER Hosting Capacity** | $\text{HC} = \min\left( V_{\text{limit}}, I_{\text{limit}}, \text{Protection} \right)$ | Max renewable integration |
| 7 | **Demand Response Ratio** | $\text{DRR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shed}} - P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{shed}}} \times 100\%$ | DR performance |
| 8 | **Voltage Sensitivity Factor** | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P} = R \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}$ | Volt/VAR optimization |
| 9 | **Dynamic Line Rating (DLR)** | $I_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{c} - T_{a}}{R_{ac}(T_{c}) \cdot R_{th}}}$ | Asset utilization |
| 10 | **Energy Not Supplied (ENS)** | $\text{ENS} = \sum_i L_i \cdot U_i$ | Reliability cost, MWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used Above:**
> - $N_i$ = number of customers interrupted at event $i$
> - $r_i$ = restoration time for event $i$ (in minutes/hours)
> - $N_T$ = total number of customers served
> - $P_{\text{shed}}$ = scheduled load shedding, $P_{\text{actual}}$ = actual shedding
> - $T_c$ = conductor max temperature, $T_a$ = ambient temperature
> - $R_{ac}$ = AC resistance, $R_{th}$ = thermal resistance
> - $L_i$ = load interrupted, $U_i$ = outage duration

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Functions/Services

1. **Self-healing** has saved U.S. utilities an estimated **$2.4 billion/year** in outage costs (Source: U.S. DOE Smart Grid Investment Grant data).
2. **Demand response** can shave **5–15%** of peak demand — equivalent to deferring billions in generation capacity.
3. **Dynamic line rating** can increase transmission capacity by **10–30%** without new line construction.
4. **PMU-based monitoring** has reduced blackouts in regions adopting WAMS (e.g., Eastern Interconnection, China, India).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Reliability Index Calculation (SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI)

> This is the **most frequently asked numerical type** in KTU Module 2 university exams.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A distribution utility serves **4,000 customers** over a year. The following interruption events occurred:

| Event | Customers Interrupted ($N_i$) | Restoration Time ($r_i$) in minutes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 800 | 60 |
| 2 | 1,200 | 45 |
| 3 | 600 | 120 |
| 4 | 400 | 30 |

Calculate **SAIDI, SAIFI, and CAIDI**.

#### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.

$$N_T = 4000 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate SAIFI.

The formula is:

$$\text{SAIFI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}{N_T}$$

Compute the numerator — sum of all customer interruptions:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i = 800 + 1200 + 600 + 400 = 3000$$

Now substitute:

$$\text{SAIFI} = \frac{3000}{4000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SAIFI} = 0.75 \text{ interruptions/customer/year}}$$

> **Interpretation:** On average, each customer experienced less than 1 interruption per year — good utility performance.

**Step 3:** Calculate SAIDI.

The formula is:

$$\text{SAIDI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot r_i}{N_T}$$

Compute the weighted sum — each event's customer count multiplied by its duration:

$$N_1 \cdot r_1 = 800 \times 60 = 48{,}000$$

$$N_2 \cdot r_2 = 1200 \times 45 = 54{,}000$$

$$N_3 \cdot r_3 = 600 \times 120 = 72{,}000$$

$$N_4 \cdot r_4 = 400 \times 30 = 12{,}000$$

Sum them:

$$\sum N_i \cdot r_i = 48{,}000 + 54{,}000 + 72{,}000 + 12{,}000 = 186{,}000 \text{ customer-minutes}$$

Substitute into SAIDI:

$$\text{SAIDI} = \frac{186{,}000}{4000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SAIDI} = 46.5 \text{ minutes/customer/year}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The average customer was without power for 46.5 minutes over the entire year. This is well below the typical Indian benchmark of 6+ hours, indicating excellent reliability.

**Step 4:** Calculate CAIDI.

$$\text{CAIDI} = \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}}$$

$$\text{CAIDI} = \frac{46.5}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CAIDI} = 62 \text{ minutes/interruption}}$$

> **Interpretation:** When an interruption occurs, the utility takes on average 62 minutes to restore service.

**Step 5:** Cross-validation check.

$$\text{CAIDI} = \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} = \frac{46.5}{0.75} = 62 \text{ min} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Many students forget that $N_i$ in SAIDI is **multiplied** by $r_i$, not summed separately. Also, do not confuse $N_i$ (customers interrupted) with $N_T$ (total customers served). Read carefully.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Demand Response Performance Evaluation

#### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A utility has a contracted **Demand Response** agreement with industrial consumers to shed **5 MW** during 4 PM – 8 PM peak. The actual shedding measured over a month is:

| Day | Scheduled (MW) | Actual (MW) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 5 | 4.5 |
| 2 | 5 | 5.0 |
| 3 | 5 | 3.8 |
| 4 | 5 | 4.2 |

Calculate the **Demand Response Ratio (DRR)**.

#### 3.2.2 Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the numerator for each day: $(P_{\text{shed}} - P_{\text{actual}})$.

$$\Delta P_1 = 5 - 4.5 = 0.5 \text{ MW}$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 5 - 5.0 = 0.0 \text{ MW}$$

$$\Delta P_3 = 5 - 3.8 = 1.2 \text{ MW}$$

$$\Delta P_4 = 5 - 4.2 = 0.8 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 2:** Sum the deficits:

$$\sum \Delta P = 0.5 + 0.0 + 1.2 + 0.8 = 2.5 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the scheduled commitments:

$$\sum P_{\text{shed}} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula:

$$\text{DRR} = \frac{2.5}{20} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{DRR} = 12.5\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The consumer group underperformed by 12.5% from its scheduled commitment. The utility may levy penalty charges per contract terms.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions vs. Services Mapping

The following analytical matrix shows the **functional dependency tree** of common smart grid services:

| Service ↓ \ Function → | AMI | SCADA | PMU/WAMS | DMS | DERMS | HEMS | Cybersecurity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Time-of-Use Billing** | ★★★ | ☆ | ☆ | ☆ | ☆ | ★ | ★ |
| **Outage Management** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ☆ | ☆ | ★ |
| **Demand Response** | ★★★ | ☆ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Frequency Regulation** | ★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ☆ | ★ |
| **VPP Operation** | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ |
| **P2P Energy Trading** | ★★★ | ☆ | ☆ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| **Dynamic Pricing** | ★★★ | ☆ | ☆ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ |

**Legend:**
- ★★★ = Critical (service cannot function without this)
- ★★ = Important (major role)
- ★ = Supplementary
- ☆ = Not directly required

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Examiners love asking *"Which functions are essential for demand response?"* — The answer is: **AMI, DERMS, and Communication Network** are critical. Cybersecurity is a horizontal requirement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Smart Grid Services

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    consumerLayer[Consumer Layer: Homes, Industries, EVs, Prosumers]:::endNode
    serviceLayer[Smart Grid Services Layer: ToU Billing, DR, OMS, VPP, P2P Trading]:::serviceNode
    functionLayer[Smart Grid Functions Layer: AMI, SCADA, WAMS, DMS, DERMS, HEMS, Cyber]:::functionNode
    physicalLayer[Physical Grid Layer: Generation, Transmission, Distribution, Storage]:::phyNode
    commLayer[Communication Backbone: RF Mesh, PLC, Fiber, LTE/5G]:::commNode
    cloudLayer[Cloud and Analytics Layer: Big Data, AI, ML, Forecasting]:::cloudNode

    consumerLayer -->|requests and consumes| serviceLayer
    serviceLayer -->|delivered via| functionLayer
    functionLayer -->|operates on| physicalLayer
    functionLayer -->|senses via| commLayer
    functionLayer -->|optimized by| cloudLayer
    cloudLayer -->|feedback and decisions| functionLayer
    commLayer <-->|bidirectional data| consumerLayer
    physicalLayer <-->|bidirectional power and info| consumerLayer

    classDef endNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef serviceNode fill:#B4E5FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000
    classDef functionNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef phyNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef commNode fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef cloudNode fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Function-to-Service Mapping Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sg[Smart Grid]:::root

    sg --> F1[Self-Healing]:::fn
    sg --> F2[Consumer Participation]:::fn
    sg --> F3[Power Quality]:::fn
    sg --> F4[DER Accommodation]:::fn
    sg --> F5[Asset Optimization]:::fn
    sg --> F6[Cybersecurity]:::fn
    sg --> F7[Real-Time Monitoring]:::fn
    sg --> F8[Advanced Metering AMI]:::fn

    F1 --> S1a[Outage Management]:::svc
    F1 --> S1b[Auto Reconfiguration]:::svc
    F2 --> S2a[Demand Response]:::svc
    F2 --> S2b[ToU Pricing]:::svc
    F3 --> S3a[Voltage Regulation]:::svc
    F3 --> S3b[Harmonic Filtering]:::svc
    F4 --> S4a[VPP Operation]:::svc
    F4 --> S4b[Microgrid Islanding]:::svc
    F5 --> S5a[Dynamic Line Rating]:::svc
    F5 --> S5b[Predictive Maintenance]:::svc
    F6 --> S6a[Secure Billing]:::svc
    F6 --> S6b[Secure Trading]:::svc
    F7 --> S7a[WAMS Visualization]:::svc
    F7 --> S7b[Wide-Area Control]:::svc
    F8 --> S8a[Remote Connect or Disconnect]:::svc
    F8 --> S8b[Consumption Analytics]:::svc

    classDef root fill:#263238,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fn fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef svc fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Self-Healing Function — Detection-Isolation-Restoration Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Normal Operation]):::state --> detect{Fault Detected?}:::decision
    detect -->|No| start
    detect -->|Yes| alarm[Alarm raised in DMS]:::process
    alarm --> locate[Fault Location Identified via PMU and Smart Relays]:::process
    locate --> isolate[Isolate Faulted Section: Open Automated Switches]:::process
    isolate --> reconfigure[Reconfigure Network: Reroute Power via Alternate Feeder]:::process
    reconfigure --> restore[Restore Supply to Healthy Loads]:::process
    restore --> log[Log Event and Update Outage Management System OMS]:::process
    log --> notify[Notify Affected Customers via AMI and SMS]:::process
    notify --> repair[Dispatch Repair Crew for Permanent Fix]:::process
    repair --> endnode([Return to Normal Operation]):::state

    classDef state fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Demand Response Service — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    utility[Utility Command Center]:::org
    drms[DRMS Server: Demand Response Management System]:::sys
    ami[AMI Network: Smart Meters and Concentrators]:::sys
    consumer[Consumer Premise: HEMS and Smart Appliances]:::dev
    response[Load Shedding or Shifting Action]:::action
    verification[Verification: Metered Response Data]:::action
    settle[Settlement and Incentive Payment]:::org

    utility -->|1: Send DR Event Signal| drms
    drms -->|2: Dispatch to Endpoints| ami
    ami -->|3: Notify Consumer Device| consumer
    consumer -->|4: Automated Response| response
    response -->|5: Telemetry Data| verification
    verification -->|6: Aggregated Response| drms
    drms -->|7: Performance Report| utility
    utility -->|8: Approve Payment| settle
    settle -->|9: Credit Consumer Bill| consumer

    classDef org fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
```
