> **Why anti-aliasing is mandatory:** If a **3 kHz** switching transient is sampled at **1 kHz**, the alias appears at $|3 - 1| = 2$ kHz and is wrongly interpreted as a system disturbance. The analog low-pass filter prevents this.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used in this table; all absolute-value and divider notations are replaced with `\vert` or `\mid`.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem | $f_s$ = sampling freq., $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. (Hz) |
| 2 | $f_{s,\,practical} = 32 \times f_{system}$ | Common industrial choice (32 samples/cycle) | Hz; gives **1.6 kHz @ 50 Hz** |
| 3 | $X(k) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j\,2\pi k n / N}$ | Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) | $k = 0, 1, ..., N-1$ |
| 4 | $x(n) = \dfrac{1}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \cdot e^{j\,2\pi k n / N}$ | Inverse DFT | Reconstruction formula |
| 5 | $X_{1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot \left[ \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) - j\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \right]$ | Fundamental phasor extraction | Used in real relays |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{V}{I} = R + jX$ | Apparent impedance seen by relay | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{n} v(n) \, i(n)}{\displaystyle\sum_{n} i(n)^{2}}$ | Resistance from sample correlation | Fault loop resistance |
| 8 | $X = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{n} v\!\left(n - \dfrac{N}{4}\right) i(n)}{\displaystyle\sum_{n} i(n)^{2}}$ | Reactance using 90° shift | Fault loop reactance |
| 9 | $V_{quant} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{b}}$ | Quantization step of an A/D | $b$ = number of bits, $V_{FS}$ = full-scale input |
| 10 | $t_{trip} = t_{sample} + t_{alg} + t_{output}$ | Total relay operating time | ms; typical **< 25 ms** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

DPRs are the backbone of **substation automation**. They enable:
- **Distance protection** of 400 kV EHV transmission lines with mho/quadrilateral characteristics.
- **Transformer differential protection (87T)** using 2nd-harmonic restraint to distinguish inrush from internal faults.
- **Adaptive protection** — settings change automatically as the network topology changes (line outage, DG connection).
- **Wide-Area Monitoring, Protection, and Control (WAMPAC)** via **PMU (Phasor Measurement Unit)** streams at 50–60 messages/second per **IEEE C37.118**.
- **IEC 61850** GOOSE messaging for **< 4 ms** peer-to-peer tripping in a substation LAN.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Sampling Criterion

**Premise:** A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ contains no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz. We want to reconstruct it from discrete samples $x(nT_s)$.

**Step 1 — Define the sampling interval.**
The continuous signal is sampled every $T_s$ seconds. Therefore the sampling frequency is:

$$f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$$

**Step 2 — Express the sampled signal in the time domain.**
Ideal sampling multiplies $x(t)$ by an impulse train $\delta_{T_s}(t)$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Fourier Transform.**
The spectrum of the sampled signal is a periodic repetition of the original spectrum $X(f)$, scaled by $1/T_s$:

$$X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - \dfrac{k}{T_s}\right)$$

**Step 4 — State the no-overlap condition.**
For reconstruction to be perfect, the spectral copies must not overlap. The first copy is centred at $f_s$ and the baseband copy at $0$. The right edge of the baseband copy is at $f_{max}$. The left edge of the first copy is at $f_s - f_{max}$. We require:

$$f_s - f_{max} \geq f_{max}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the inequality.**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

This is the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, proved.

**Step 6 — Apply to a 50 Hz Indian system with 5th-harmonic concern ($f_{max}$ = 250 Hz).**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

A practical relay oversamples by a factor of 4–8, so $f_s = 4 \text{ kHz}$ (80 samples/cycle) is typical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world consequence:** If the anti-aliasing filter is mis-designed, the **250 Hz** 5th harmonic aliases to a false **150 Hz** in a 1 kHz-sampled relay, which may mimic a transformer inrush (2nd harmonic ≈ 100 Hz) and cause **false tripping**. This is a real failure mode reported in field incidents.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) for Phasor Extraction

**Premise:** The protection algorithm needs the **fundamental (50 Hz) phasor** of a sampled waveform so it can compute impedance. We derive the DFT.

**Step 1 — Write the continuous Fourier coefficient at fundamental frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$.**

$$X_1 = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Discretize the integral.**
Replace $dt$ with $T_s$, $T$ with $N T_s$, and $t = n T_s$:

$$X_1 \approx \dfrac{1}{N T_s} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n T_s) \, e^{-j\omega_0 n T_s} \cdot T_s$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $T_s$ and substitute $\omega_0 T_s = 2\pi / N$.**

$$X_1 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi n / N}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the complex exponential using Euler's identity.**

$$X_1 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[ \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) - j \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \right]$$

This is the **fundamental phasor** used by every numerical distance and overcurrent relay. The real part $X_{1,\,R}$ and the imaginary part $X_{1,\,I}$ give the cosine and sine projections, and the magnitude is:

$$\vert X_1 \vert = \sqrt{X_{1,R}^{2} + X_{1,I}^{2}}, \quad \angle X_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{1,I}}{X_{1,R}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner focus area:** Most students lose marks by writing only $|X(k)|$. Always quote the **Cartesian decomposition** $X_1 = a_1 - j b_1$ and then the **polar conversion** $X_1 = A_1 \angle \phi_1$. Marks are awarded separately for the two steps.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Impedance from Sampled Values

**Given:** A digital relay samples 16 points per cycle from a faulted line. The first 4 samples of voltage and current are:

| $n$ | $v(n)$ (V) | $i(n)$ (A) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10.0 | 5.0 |
| 1 | 25.0 | 12.0 |
| 2 | 35.0 | 17.0 |
| 3 | 30.0 | 14.0 |

**Required:** Estimate $R$ and $X$ using the correlation formulas.

**Step 1 — Compute $\sum v(n) i(n)$.**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{3} v(n) i(n) = 10 \times 5 + 25 \times 12 + 35 \times 17 + 30 \times 14$$

$$= 50 + 300 + 595 + 420 = 1365 \text{ V·A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum i(n)^2$.**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{3} i(n)^{2} = 25 + 144 + 289 + 196 = 654 \text{ A}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R$.**

$$R = \dfrac{\sum v(n) i(n)}{\sum i(n)^{2}} = \dfrac{1365}{654} \approx 2.087 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 90°-shifted voltage for $X$.** With 16 samples/cycle, a 90° shift corresponds to a shift of $N/4 = 4$ samples. So $v(n-4) = v(n)$ in this short window, and the sum reduces to the same as Step 1. Hence $X \approx R \approx 2.09 \,\Omega$, indicating a **purely resistive fault** at **Z = 2.087 + j0 $\Omega$**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a simplified pedagogical example. Real relays use all 16 samples and a sliding window, giving sub-cycle accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Digital Protective Relay

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** from the power system primary quantities to the trip coil, including the auxiliary interfaces (HMI, communication) that distinguish a DPR from a static/electromechanical relay.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CT1[Current Transformer CT]
    PT1[Potential Transformer PT]
    SC[Analog Signal Conditioning and Isolation]
    AF[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    SH[Sample and Hold S and H]
    ADC[Analog to Digital Converter A by D]
    DSP[Digital Signal Processor DSP and Microcontroller]
    ALG[Protection Algorithm Library DFT FFT Impedance]
    LG[Logic and Trip Decision Unit]
    TRP[Trip Coil and Breaker Mechanism]
    LED[LED and Local Annunciation]
    HMI[Keypad and LCD HMI for Settings]
    COM[Ethernet Serial IEC 61850 GOOSE]
    MEM[Fault Event Recorder and Datalog]
    PSU[DC Auxiliary Power Supply 110V or 48V]

    CT1 --> SC
    PT1 --> SC
    SC --> AF
    AF --> SH
    SH --> ADC
    ADC --> DSP
    DSP --> ALG
    ALG --> LG
    LG --> TRP
    LG --> LED
    HMI --> DSP
    DSP --> COM
    DSP --> MEM
    PSU --> SC
    PSU --> DSP
    PSU --> TRP
```

**Reading the diagram (top to bottom):**
- The **two left-most blocks** represent the *sensing* boundary.
- The **vertical column AF → SH → ADC** is the *signal acquisition* path — these three blocks are the *unique selling point* of any DPR versus a static relay (no sampling in static relays).
- The **DSP + ALG + LG** cluster is the *decision* brain.
- The **right-side cluster (HMI, COM, MEM)** provides the *intelligence* — event recording, remote tripping, SCADA integration — that a modern Smart Grid demands.
- The **PSU block** branches into the analog side, the digital side, and the trip coil, mirroring the galvanic isolation requirement of IEC 60255.

## 4.2 Internal Memory & Algorithm Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Sampled Input Buffer]
    WIN[Sliding Window of N Samples]
    DFT1[DFT Engine Fundamental Phasor]
    DFT2[DFT Engine Harmonic Components]
    IMP[Impedance Calculator R and X]
    OC[Overcurrent Comparator 50 51]
    DIFF[Differential Comparator 87]
    DIST[Distance Comparator 21]
    SG[Setting Group Active Profile]
    TR[Boolean Trip Logic OR and AND]
    OUT[Trip Output to Breaker]

    INP --> WIN
    WIN --> DFT1
    WIN --> DFT2
    DFT1 --> IMP
    IMP --> DIST
    DFT1 --> OC
    DFT1 --> DIFF
    DFT2 --> DIFF
    SG --> DIST
    SG --> OC
    SG --> DIFF
    DIST --> TR
    OC --> TR
    DIFF --> TR
    TR --> OUT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam illustration tip:** Even if you only have a text answer, drawing this *flow logic* explicitly in your exam sheet gives 1–2 extra marks for "neat and systematic presentation" under KTU's valuation grid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Define a Digital Protective Relay. List any four advantages over an electromechanical relay.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Digital Protective Relay (DPR) is a microprocessor- or DSP-based relay that samples analog current and voltage signals from CTs and PTs, converts them to digital form, processes them through software-implemented protection algorithms (typically using DFT/FFT), and issues a trip output to the circuit breaker when a fault is detected.

**Any four advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. **Faster operating time** — typically 0.5 to 1 cycle versus 0.1–0.4 s for electromechanical.
2. **Self-test and self-diagnostics** — internal watchdog checks memory, A/D, and trip circuit continuously.
3. **Multi-function integration** — a single DPR can implement functions 50, 51, 67, 27, 87, 21 simultaneously.
4. **Communication capability** — supports IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus for SCADA and remote setting changes.
5. **Higher accuracy** — phasor estimation via DFT gives magnitude error < 1 % and angle error < 0.5°.
6. **Event and disturbance recording** — internal COMTRADE-format fault logs aid post-incident analysis.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series B]

**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. Why is an anti-aliasing filter essential in a Digital Protective Relay? Mention the typical sampling rate used in modern numerical relays.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Theorem (1 mark):** *A band-limited signal containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.*

**Anti-aliasing necessity (1 mark):** Without an analog low-pass filter, out-of-band noise and harmonics (e.g., CT saturation transients up to several kHz) fold back into the 0–$f_s/2$ measurement band, producing *alias frequencies* that mimic genuine faults and cause **false or missed trips**.

**Typical sampling rate (1 mark):** Modern numerical relays sample at **32 to 64 samples per cycle**, i.e., **1.6 kHz to 3.2 kHz** at 50 Hz system frequency, well above the theoretical Nyquist minimum of 100 Hz.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule (ESE):** "Answer any one full question from the module, carrying 14 marks, subdivided into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks." Below are two *complete* alternatives.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [Adapted from KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Digital Protective Relay**. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) algorithm** for phasor extraction in numerical relays. Derive the expression for the fundamental-frequency phasor from $N$ samples per cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the same six-stage flow as Section 4.1: CT/PT → Signal Conditioning → Anti-aliasing Filter → Sample-and-Hold → A/D Converter → DSP/Algorithm → Trip Logic → Trip Coil. Include the HMI, Communication, and Event Recorder as auxiliary interfaces.

**Function of each block (4 marks):**

1. **CT and PT (Sensing):** Step down primary current (up to 50 kA) to 1 A/5 A secondary, and primary voltage (say 220 kV) to 110 V/$\sqrt{3}$. They provide galvanic isolation.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Burden resistor + isolation amplifier convert CT/PT secondary to ±5 V or ±10 V analog signal suitable for the A/D.
3. **Anti-aliasing Filter:** Active second-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cut-off around 0.4 × $f_s$ to remove out-of-band noise and harmonics.
4. **Sample-and-Hold:** Freezes the instantaneous value during A/D conversion, typically 1–2 µs acquisition time.
5. **A/D Converter:** Successive-approximation type, 12- to 16-bit, producing one digital sample per channel per $T_s$.
6. **DSP / Microcontroller:** Executes protection algorithm (DFT, impedance calculation, comparator logic) within the sample interval.
7. **Trip Logic and Trip Coil:** Energizes the breaker trip latch via a power semiconductor (SCR/IGBT) within 2–4 ms.
8. **Auxiliaries:** HMI for local setting, communication port for SCADA, COMTRADE memory for fault recording, watchdog timer for self-test.

---

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Algorithm concept (2 marks):** DFT extracts the **fundamental (50 Hz) phasor** by correlating the sampled waveform with cosine and sine reference waves over one full cycle. This yields a magnitude and phase that are insensitive to DC offset and higher harmonics, making it ideal for protection.

**Derivation (5 marks):** See Section 3.2 of these notes. The final compact form is:

$$X_1 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi n / N} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[ \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) - j \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \right]$$

The **real part** $a_1$ corresponds to the in-phase component, and the **imaginary part** $b_1$ to the quadrature component. The polar form is $X_1 = \sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2} \angle \tan^{-1}(b_1 / a_1)$, which is the **phasor** fed to the impedance calculator.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Adapted from KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **signal acquisition and processing chain** in a Digital Protective Relay. Why is the anti-aliasing filter placed *before* the Sample-and-Hold, not after? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital relay samples a current signal at **32 samples per cycle** on a 50 Hz system. Calculate the **sampling interval** and the **sampling frequency**. If the anti-aliasing filter has a 2nd-order Butterworth response with cut-off at **640 Hz**, determine whether **harmonics up to the 13th (650 Hz)** will be adequately suppressed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

**Signal acquisition chain (4 marks):** The chain consists of CT/PT → Isolation and Burden → Anti-aliasing Filter → Sample-and-Hold → Multiplexer → A/D Converter → DSP. The analog blocks (filter, S/H) must precede the A/D because once a signal is digitized at $f_s$, any frequency above $f_s/2$ is **permanently aliased** and cannot be removed in software.

**Why filter is before S/H (3 marks):**
1. The S/H captures the *instantaneous* analog value; if a high-frequency alias were present in the analog waveform at that instant, the S/H would *lock in* the wrong value.
2. A digital filter after A/D operates only on *sampled* data; it cannot remove aliasing that has already occurred at the analog input.
3. An analog low-pass filter with a steep roll-off (e.g., 2nd-order Butterworth at 640 Hz) attenuates the 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th harmonics *before* they reach the S/H, ensuring the samples seen by the A/D are band-limited to $f_s / 2 = 800$ Hz.

---

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Step 1 — Sampling interval and frequency (2 marks).**

$$T_s = \dfrac{1}{32 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{1600} = 0.625 \text{ ms}$$

$$f_s = 32 \times 50 = 1600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist limit (1 mark).**

$$f_{max,\,theory} = \dfrac{f_s}{2} = 800 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Filter attenuation at 650 Hz (13th harmonic) (3 marks).**
For a 2nd-order Butterworth, the magnitude response is:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_c)^{4}}}$$

With $f = 650$ Hz and $f_c = 640$ Hz:

$$\vert H(650) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (650/640)^{4}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (1.0156)^{4}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 1.0637}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2.0637}} \approx 0.696$$

**Result:** The 13th harmonic is only attenuated to **69.6 %** of its original value — i.e., a mere **3.15 dB** suppression. This is **inadequate**. The 13th harmonic (650 Hz) lies inside the Nyquist band (0–800 Hz) and will *not* be filtered, leading to **phasor error in the DFT**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The cut-off must be lowered to at most **~400 Hz** (i.e., 8th harmonic), and the filter order increased to 4th-order, to ensure at least 20 dB attenuation at 650 Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Skipping the assumption line:** Always state *"Assuming a 50 Hz Indian power system, the cycle period is 20 ms"* before writing $T_s = 20/32$. A bare formula without a stated assumption costs one mark.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $T_s$ must carry "ms" or "s"; $f_s$ must carry "Hz". A correct numerical answer without a unit is treated as a *conceptual* error in KTU's strict marking scheme.
> 3. **Confusing filter type with filter order:** "Butterworth" is a *magnitude response shape*; "2nd-order" is the *number of poles*. Writing "Butterworth 2nd order" is correct; "2nd-order Butterworth filter of 640 Hz cut-off and 50 Hz pass band" mixes frequency and order — do not.
> 4. **Not commenting on adequacy:** Simply computing $\vert H(650) \vert = 0.696$ is incomplete. The KTU answer key requires an *engineering verdict* — "this is inadequate, the filter must be redesigned" — to earn the final method mark.
> 5. **In the block diagram, forgetting to show the trip coil's auxiliary DC supply.** Examiners often keep a 1-mark reservation for "completeness of the power-supply path."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A DPR is a *numerical* relay — it samples, quantizes, and digitally processes current/voltage to make a trip decision.
- **Core advantage:** Multifunction, self-test, communication, and sub-cycle trip time.
- **Six-stage signal chain:** CT/PT → Conditioning → Anti-aliasing Filter → Sample-and-Hold → A/D → DSP → Trip Coil.
- **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. In practice, $f_s = 32 \times 50 = 1.6 \text{ kHz}$ (32 samples/cycle).
- **A/D resolution:** 12-bit gives $V_{quant} = V_{FS}/4096$; 16-bit gives 0.0015 % resolution.
- **DFT for phasor extraction:** $X_1 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi n / N}$. Real and imaginary parts yield magnitude and angle.
- **Impedance from samples:** $R = \frac{\sum v(n) i(n)}{\sum i(n)^2}$, $X = \frac{\sum v(n - N/4) i(n)}{\sum i(n)^2}$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Must be *analog* and placed *before* the S/H. Typical: 2nd–4th-order Butterworth at $0.4 \times f_s$.
- **Communication protocols:** IEC 61850 (GOOSE), DNP3, Modbus, IEEE C37.118 (PMU).
- **Standards:** IEEE C37.2 (device numbering), IEC 60255 (protective relay standard), IEC 61850 (substation communication).
- **Trip time budget:** Sample + Algorithm + Output ≈ **< 25 ms** in modern numerical relays.
- **Watchdog and self-test:** Continuous RAM/ROM/A/D/trip-coil check — unique to DPRs.
- **Common exam mistake:** Mixing up "sampling rate" and "sampling interval" — they are reciprocals.
- **Memory aid — "SAD-SALT":** **S**ense, **A**lias-filter, **D**igitize, **S**ample-hold, **A**lgorithm, **L**ogic, **T**rip.
- **One-line answer to "What makes DPR a Smart Grid enabler?"** Adaptive setting groups + IEC 61850 + PMU streaming + event records + remote configuration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Breaker Monitor (CBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circuit Breaker Monitor (CBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Circuit Breaker Monitor (CBM)** is an intelligent electronic device (IED) deployed in smart substations as part of the substation automation system (SAS). It continuously acquires, processes, and records critical mechanical, electrical, and environmental parameters of a High-Voltage (HV) or Medium-Voltage (MV) circuit breaker, enabling **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** and supporting the transition from time-based to predictive maintenance philosophies under the **IEC 61850** standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module-2 (Smart Grid Substation Automation), CBM is treated as a *non-protection* IED that complements protection relays (e.g., numerical overcurrent, distance relays) by focusing purely on the **health, wear, and operational integrity** of the circuit breaker itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" Intuition

Imagine your car. It does not wait for the engine to seize; instead, a **dashboard** constantly tells you the oil level, brake-pad wear, engine temperature, and battery voltage. If any parameter crosses a threshold, it warns you **before** a breakdown occurs.

A **Circuit Breaker Monitor** is the "dashboard" of a circuit breaker. Instead of oil and brake pads, it watches:

- The **contact wear** (how much the tips have eroded after each trip).
- The **opening/closing times** (how fast the contacts separate).
- The **coil currents** of the trip and close coils.
- The **SF₆ gas density** (the "blood" of GIS breakers).
- The **motor current** of the spring-charging mechanism.

When the breaker has opened/closed 2000 times, or the SF₆ pressure has dropped, the CBM raises an alarm so the maintenance crew can act *before* a catastrophic failure during a real short-circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A numerical protection relay tells the breaker *when* to open. The CBM tells the substation engineer *how healthy* the breaker is and *when* it needs servicing. Both are mandatory IEDs in a modern digital substation.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Operating time of a 400 kV SF₆ CB:** typically **20 ms – 40 ms** (2 to 4 cycles of 50 Hz).
- **Rated SF₆ gas pressure (20 °C):** **0.5 – 0.7 MPa** (for 245 kV–420 kV GIS).
- **Permissible contact wear limit:** typically **3 mm – 6 mm** depending on the manufacturer (ABB, Siemens, GE).
- **Communication protocol:** **IEC 61850 (MMS/GSE), DNP3.0, Modbus TCP**.
- **Sampling rate of CBM analog channels:** **1 kHz – 10 kHz** for transient coil-current capture.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a CBM

A modern CBM is internally partitioned into the following sub-modules:

1. **Analog Input Module (AIM):** Conditions signals from CTs (for coil/motor currents) and contact travel transducers (LVDT or resistive).
2. **Binary Input Module (BIM):** Captures status of 52a (breaker-closed), 52b (breaker-open), spring-charged, SF₆ low-pressure interlocks.
3. **Binary Output Module (BOM):** Issues trip/close commands, alarm LEDs, annunciator signals.
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP) / CPU:** Runs the analysis algorithms (curve integration, peak detection, statistical trending).
5. **Communication Module:** Publishes data over **IEC 61850 GOOSE/MMS** to the SCADA/BCU (Bay Control Unit).
6. **Non-Volatile Memory (FRAM/Flash):** Stores **last 512 to 4096 operation records** (Disturbance/Fault Records - DFRs) and **long-term trend logs**.
7. **Real-Time Clock (IRIG-B synchronized):** Time-stamps every event to **±1 ms** accuracy for post-event analysis.

### 2.2 Why Each Parameter is Monitored — The Engineering "Why"

| Parameter | Why it is Monitored | Engineering "Why" (Failure Mechanism) |
|---|---|---|
| **Main contact travel** | Verifies mechanical integrity of operating mechanism | Sticking linkages, worn latches → incomplete closure → contact welding |
| **Opening time (t_open)** | Confirms CB interrupts fault within the design window | Slow opening → arc persists → chamber explosion |
| **Closing time (t_close)** | Confirms autoreclosing readiness | Slow close → instability on power swing |
| **Trip coil current (i_TC)** | Detects coil insulation degradation, burnt coils | Open coil → breaker fails to trip → fault persists |
| **Close coil current (i_CC)** | Detects contact wear via "Knee" analysis | The "knee" in the current waveform disappears when contacts are eroded |
| **Motor current of spring charger** | Detects gear wear, spring fatigue | High mean current → gearbox seizure or broken spring |
| **SF₆ gas density (ρ_SF₆)** | Ensures dielectric strength and arc-quenching ability | Low density → arc reignition, partial discharge |
| **Contact resistance (R_c)** | Indirectly measures contact erosion and oxidation | High R_c → I²R losses, localized heating |
| **Auxiliary contact timing** (52a/52b) | Confirms proper pole independence | Mechanical misalignment between phases |

> [!NOTE]
> **CBM "How" —** For every operation (trip or close), the CBM records a 200 ms–500 ms waveform window for *each* monitored parameter. It then extracts 30–60 diagnostic features per operation and stores them in a cyclically overwritten buffer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{op} = t_{arc} + t_{mech}$ | Total break time = arcing time + mechanical time | ms |
| 2 | $E_{arc} = \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} v_{arc}(t) \cdot i_{fault}(t)\, dt$ | Arc energy dissipated in the interrupting chamber | MJ |
| 3 | $R_{c} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{I_{rated}}$ | Static contact resistance measured by CBM | $\mu\Omega$ |
| 4 | $\rho_{SF_6} = \dfrac{P}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$ | SF₆ gas density (using ideal gas approximation) | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $C_{wear} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \Delta d_k$ | Cumulative contact wear (sum of erosion per operation) | mm |
| 6 | $\Delta d_k = k_{ero} \cdot \int i_{arc}^{2}(t)\, dt$ | Per-operation wear proportional to $I^{2}t$ | mm |
| 7 | $I_{coil}(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R_{coil}} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Trip/close coil current transient | A |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Coil electrical time constant | ms |
| 9 | $f_{sampling} \geq 10 \cdot f_{max\,signal}$ | Nyquist criterion for CBM ADC design | Hz |
| 10 | $T_{interrupting} = T_{relay} + t_{op}$ | Total fault clearing time | ms |
| 11 | $\eta_{cbm} = \dfrac{N_{detected\,anomalies}}{N_{total\,anomalies}} \times 100\%$ | CBM diagnostic efficiency (KTU definition) | % |
| 12 | $MTBF_{CBM} > 100{,}000\ \text{hours}$ | Reliability target of modern CBM IEDs | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Booster:** In KTU derivations, the integral $E_{arc}$ is the *most-frequently* asked expression. The relationship between arc energy, contact wear, and remaining useful life (RUL) is a 14-mark favorite.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Utility Companies (KSEB, NTPC, POWERGRID):** Reduce routine maintenance cost by **up to 40%** and unscheduled outages by **up to 70%**.
- **Industrial Plants (Refineries, Steel):** Avoid $1M+ losses per hour of unplanned shutdown.
- **Railways (Indian Railways 25 kV Traction):** Ensures fault clearance in OHE circuit breakers.
- **Renewable Integration:** Wind farm CBs experience **frequent switching** (100s/day) — CBM is critical to track mechanical wear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Arc Energy and Contact Wear Relationship

**Step 1 — Define Arc Energy.**
The arc energy is the time integral of the instantaneous product of arc voltage and arc current during the arcing interval.

$$
E_{arc} = \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} v_{arc}(t) \cdot i_{arc}(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Assume Sinusoidal Fault Current.**
For a symmetrical short-circuit, the fault current is:
$$
i_{arc}(t) = I_{pk}\, \sin(\omega t + \phi)
$$
where $I_{pk} = \sqrt{2}\, I_{rms,sc}$ is the peak short-circuit current, and $\phi$ is the closing angle.

**Step 3 — Assume Constant Arc Voltage (Quasi-Steady Approximation).**
For an SF₆ breaker, the arc voltage is approximately constant at $V_{arc} \approx V_{0}$ during the current-zero region.

$$
E_{arc} \approx V_{0} \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} I_{pk}\, \sin(\omega t)\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Integration.**

$$
E_{arc} \approx V_{0} \cdot I_{pk} \left[ -\dfrac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_{0}^{t_{arc}}
$$

$$
E_{arc} \approx \dfrac{V_{0} \cdot I_{pk}}{\omega}\, \bigl(1 - \cos(\omega\, t_{arc})\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the Empirical Wear Coefficient.**
Empirically, the contact mass loss per operation is proportional to arc energy through a material constant $k_{ero}$ (units: mm/MJ or $\mu$g/C).

$$
\Delta m_{wear} = k_{ero} \cdot E_{arc}
$$

**Step 6 — Relate Wear to Linear Erosion (Volume-to-Length).**
For uniform contact surface $A_{contact}$ and density $\rho_{cu}$:

$$
\Delta d_{wear} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{wear}}{\rho_{cu} \cdot A_{contact}}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Compact Result.**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta d_{wear} = \dfrac{k_{ero}}{\rho_{cu} \cdot A_{contact}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{0} \cdot I_{pk}}{\omega} \left[1 - \cos(\omega t_{arc})\right]\;}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- Wear is **maximum** when $t_{arc}$ equals a **half cycle** ($\omega t_{arc} = \pi$), where $\cos(\pi) = -1 \Rightarrow 1-(-1) = 2$.
- Wear is **zero** when $t_{arc} = 0$ (ideal interruption) or $\omega t_{arc} = 2\pi, 4\pi, \ldots$ (multiples of full cycle — theoretically no net arc energy).
- This is why SF₆ breakers are designed for **2-cycle** interruption, and the CBM must capture the **first 80 ms** post-trip to compute this correctly.

### 3.2 Derivation: Trip-Coil Current Diagnostic "Knee-Point" Analysis

**Step 1 — Coil Equivalent Circuit.**
Model the trip coil as a series RL circuit driven by the station DC battery $V_{dc}$.

$$
V_{dc} = R_{coil}\, i(t) + L_{coil}\, \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the First-Order ODE.**
Initial condition $i(0) = 0$.

$$
i(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R_{coil}}\, \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = \dfrac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil}}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Value.**
$$
I_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R_{coil}}
$$

**Step 4 — The Diagnostic "Knee".**
When the breaker latch releases, the auxiliary contact opens and inserts a **resistor $R_{aux}$** in series. This causes a **second exponential decay** visible as a "knee" in the current waveform.

$$
i(t > t_{knee}) = I_{ss} \cdot e^{-(t-t_{knee})/\tau_2}, \quad \tau_2 = \dfrac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil} + R_{aux}}
$$

**Step 5 — Knee-Point Slope Used for Diagnostics.**

$$
\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t_{knee}^-} = \dfrac{I_{ss}}{\tau_1}, \qquad
\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t_{knee}^+} = -\dfrac{I_{ss}}{\tau_2}
$$

A **shallow knee** (small $\vert \Delta(di/dt) \vert$) indicates **worn latch** or **weak spring** — a critical CBM alert.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice).**
Given: $V_{dc} = 110\ \text{V}$, $R_{coil} = 11\ \Omega$, $L_{coil} = 0.55\ \text{H}$, $R_{aux} = 33\ \Omega$.

$$
\tau_1 = \dfrac{0.55}{11} = 0.05\ \text{s} = 50\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
I_{ss} = \dfrac{110}{11} = 10\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = \dfrac{0.55}{11 + 33} = \dfrac{0.55}{44} = 12.5\ \text{ms}
$$

At $t = 50\ \text{ms}$ (just before knee):
$$
i(50^-) = 10\,(1 - e^{-1}) = 10 \times 0.6321 = 6.321\ \text{A}
$$

Just after knee:
$$
i(50^+) = 6.321\ \text{A (continuous)}
$$
Slope before: $\dfrac{10}{50} = 0.2\ \text{A/ms}$
Slope after: $-\dfrac{6.321}{12.5} = -0.5057\ \text{A/ms}$

The **magnitude of slope discontinuity** $\vert 0.2 - (-0.5057) \vert = 0.7057\ \text{A/ms}$. This is the **knee signature** stored by the CBM.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — What the CBM Alarms On

| Diagnostic Parameter | Healthy Range | Alarm Threshold (Typical) | Action Triggered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opening time $t_{open}$ | 20 – 40 ms | $\pm 20\%$ drift | Mechanical inspection |
| Closing time $t_{close}$ | 40 – 80 ms | $\pm 15\%$ drift | Latch lubrication |
| Trip coil current peak | 8 – 12 A | $< 5\ \text{A}$ or $> 15\ \text{A}$ | Coil replacement |
| SF₆ pressure @ 20 °C | 0.6 MPa | $< 0.52\ \text{MPa}$ | Gas top-up |
| Contact resistance $R_c$ | $< 25\ \mu\Omega$ | $> 40\ \mu\Omega$ | Contact refurbishment |
| Motor run time | $< 10\ \text{s}$ | $> 20\ \text{s}$ | Spring/gear inspection |
| Wear $\Delta d_{wear}$ | $< 3\ \text{mm}$ | $> 4.5\ \text{mm}$ | Schedule overhaul |
| Operating count $N$ | $< 2000$ | $> 5000$ | Mid-life inspection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a CBM IED

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS["PHYSICAL SENSORS ON CIRCUIT BREAKER"]
        S1[CT on Trip Coil]
        S2[CT on Close Coil]
        S3[CT on Motor]
        S4[LVDT Contact Travel Sensor]
        S5[SF6 Density Transmitter]
        S6[Temperature Sensor PT100]
    end

    subgraph AIM["ANALOG INPUT MODULE - AIM"]
        A1[Anti Aliasing Filter]
        A2[16 bit ADC - 10 kSps]
        A3[Galvanic Isolation]
    end

    subgraph BIM["BINARY INPUT MODULE - BIM"]
        B1[52a Contact Position]
        B2[52b Contact Position]
        B3[Spring Charged Limit Switch]
        B4[SF6 Low Pressure Interlock]
    end

    subgraph DSP["CENTRAL PROCESSING - DSP and CPU"]
        D1[Waveform Capture Buffer]
        D2[Feature Extraction Engine]
        D3[Threshold Comparator]
        D4[Trend Database FRAM]
        D5[Self Diagnostics Watchdog]
    end

    subgraph COMMS["COMMUNICATION MODULE"]
        C1[IEC 61850 MMS Server]
        C2[GOOSE Publisher for Alarms]
        C3[DNP3 over TCP for SCADA]
        C4[IRIG B Time Sync]
    end

    subgraph BOM["BINARY OUTPUT MODULE - BOM"]
        O1[Alarm LED Annunciator]
        O2[Relay to SCADA RTU]
        O3[Local Trip Block Relay]
    end

    S1 --> A1
    S2 --> A1
    S3 --> A1
    S4 --> A1
    S5 --> A1
    S6 --> A1
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> D1
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D5 --> D3
    D3 --> O1
    D3 --> O2
    D3 --> O3
    D4 --> C1
    D4 --> C2
    D4 --> C3
    C4 --> D1
```

### 4.2 CBM Operating Sequence Flowchart (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST([Start - Breaker Operation Initiated]) --> M1{Is it Trip or Close}
    M1 -- Trip --> T1[Trigger CBM Capture on Aux Contact Change]
    M1 -- Close --> T1
    T1 --> T2[Acquire 500 ms Window at 10 kSps per Channel]
    T2 --> T3[Compute: t_open, t_close, t_arc, I_coil peak, E_arc]
    T3 --> T4[Update Cumulative Wear Delta d and Count N]
    T4 --> T5{Any Parameter Outside Threshold}
    T5 -- No --> T6[Store in FRAM with IRIG B Timestamp]
    T5 -- Yes --> T7[Generate Alarm - Class A B or C]
    T7 --> T8[Publish via GOOSE and MMS to SCADA]
    T8 --> T6
    T6 --> T9[Update Long Term Trend Database]
    T9 --> ED([End - Return to Idle Monitoring])
```

### 4.3 CBM Integration Inside a Smart Substation (IEC 61850 Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CB[Circuit Breaker 400 kV] -- Sensors --> CBM[CB Monitor IED]
    CBM -- IEC 61850 MMS --> BCU[Bay Control Unit]
    CBM -- GOOSE Alarms --> PR[Numerical Protection Relay]
    PR -- Trip / Close --> CB
    BCU -- MMS --> SAS[Station Server - HMI]
    SAS -- DNP3 --> NCC[Network Control Center]
    SAS -- ICCP --> RLDC[Regional Load Dispatch Center]
    CBM -- Modbus --> LOCAL[Local Engineering Laptop]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Circuit Breaker Monitor (CBM). List any four parameters it monitors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Circuit Breaker Monitor (CBM)** is an IEC 61850-compliant IED used in digital substations to continuously supervise the mechanical, electrical, and environmental health of high-voltage circuit breakers and to enable **Condition-Based Maintenance**.

Four monitored parameters (1/2 mark each):
1. **Main contact travel** (via LVDT/encoder).
2. **Operating times** (opening time and closing time).
3. **Trip-coil and close-coil currents** (for "knee-point" analysis).
4. **SF₆ gas density / pressure** (for GIS breakers).

*(Plus any valid 4th parameter such as motor current, contact resistance, or auxiliary contact timing — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a protection relay and a Circuit Breaker Monitor in a smart substation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Protection Relay (1.5 marks) | Circuit Breaker Monitor (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Detects faults and issues Trip/Close command. | Monitors health and wear of the circuit breaker itself. |
| **Decision Speed** | Operates in 10 – 30 ms (sub-cycle). | Records and analyses after the operation (post-event). |
| **Output** | Trip signal to breaker coil. | Alarm/data to SCADA, GOOSE, MMS. |
| **Standards** | IEC 60255, IEC 61850. | IEC 62271 (switchgear monitoring). |
| **Time-domain role** | Real-time protection. | Long-term diagnostics, trending. |

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a Circuit Breaker Monitor IED used in a 400 kV digital substation. Clearly state the role of the Analog Input Module, Binary Input Module, and Communication Module. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 245 kV SF₆ circuit breaker has a measured arc voltage of $V_0 = 600\ \text{V}$ and interrupts a fault current of peak value $I_{pk} = 40\ \text{kA}$. The arcing time is $t_{arc} = 12\ \text{ms}$. Given $\omega = 2\pi f$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, contact area $A = 25\ \text{cm}^2$, copper density $\rho_{cu} = 8.96\ \text{g/cm}^3$, and erosion constant $k_{ero} = 0.05\ \text{mm/MJ}$, calculate the **arc energy** and the **per-operation contact wear** $\Delta d_{wear}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution A:**

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

*(Block diagram — 3 marks)*
- **Analog Input Module (AIM):** Conditions and digitises signals from CTs on trip/close coils, motor, contact-travel LVDT, and SF₆ density transmitter. Includes anti-aliasing filter, 16-bit ADC at $\geq 10\ \text{kSps}$, and galvanic isolation. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Binary Input Module (BIM):** Reads 52a / 52b auxiliary contacts, spring-charged limit switch, SF₆ low-pressure interlock, and lock-out relay. Provides start trigger for waveform capture. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Communication Module:** Publishes records over **IEC 61850 MMS** (reports), **GOOSE** (fast alarms), **DNP3 / Modbus** (legacy SCADA), and accepts **IRIG-B** for ±1 ms time synchronisation. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Angular frequency:**
$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos(\omega t_{arc})$:**
$$
\omega t_{arc} = 314.159 \times 0.012 = 3.7699\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
\cos(3.7699) = -0.8090
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket term:**
$$
1 - \cos(\omega t_{arc}) = 1 - (-0.8090) = 1.8090
$$

**Step 4 — Compute arc energy $E_{arc}$:**
$$
E_{arc} = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot I_{pk}}{\omega}\, [1 - \cos(\omega t_{arc})]
$$

$$
E_{arc} = \dfrac{600 \times 40{,}000}{314.159} \times 1.8090
$$

$$
E_{arc} = \dfrac{24{,}000{,}000}{314.159} \times 1.8090
$$

$$
E_{arc} = 76{,}394.4 \times 1.8090 = 138{,}187.5\ \text{J}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{arc} \approx 0.1382\ \text{MJ}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute per-operation contact wear:**

From the derived formula:

$$
\Delta d_{wear} = \dfrac{k_{ero}}{\rho_{cu} \cdot A} \cdot E_{arc}
$$

Unit conversion: $\rho_{cu} \cdot A = 8.96\ \text{g/cm}^3 \times 25\ \text{cm}^2 = 224\ \text{g} = 0.224\ \text{kg}$.

With $k_{ero} = 0.05\ \text{mm/MJ}$:

$$
\Delta d_{wear} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.224} \times 0.1382
$$

$$
\Delta d_{wear} = 0.2232 \times 0.1382 = 0.03085\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta d_{wear} \approx 0.0309\ \text{mm} \approx 30.9\ \mu\text{m per operation}\;}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (extra credit, 0.5 mark):**
If the permissible wear limit is **3 mm**, the breaker can perform:
$$
N_{limit} = \dfrac{3\ \text{mm}}{0.0309\ \text{mm/op}} \approx 97\ \text{fault\ operations}
$$
before requiring contact refurbishment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain, with waveforms, how a Circuit Breaker Monitor uses **trip-coil current analysis** to detect mechanical wear of the latching mechanism. Derive the expression for the coil current and identify the diagnostic "knee point". **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A trip coil has $V_{dc} = 110\ \text{V}$, $R = 11\ \Omega$, $L = 0.55\ \text{H}$. When the auxiliary contact opens, an additional resistor $R_{aux} = 33\ \Omega$ appears in series. Calculate the steady-state current, the time constants $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$, the value of current at $t = 50\ \text{ms}$ (just before knee), and the slope of the current waveform just after the knee. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution B:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

The trip-coil current, when energised by station DC, follows a first-order RL rise:

$$
i(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R_{coil}}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_1}\right), \quad \tau_1 = \dfrac{L}{R_{coil}}
$$

When the breaker latch releases, the auxiliary contact **opens**, inserting $R_{aux}$. The current then **decays** through the new time constant $\tau_2 = L/(R_{coil}+R_{aux})$. The transition between the two exponentials is the **"knee point"** $(t_{knee}, I_{knee})$. *(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for waveform description)*

A **shallow knee** (gentle slope change) indicates a worn latch or weak tripping spring; a **missing knee** indicates an open coil. The CBM digitises this waveform and computes the slope discontinuity $\Delta(di/dt) = (I_{ss}/\tau_1) + (I_{ss}/\tau_2)$ for trending. *(2 marks for diagnostic mapping)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Steady-state current $I_{ss}$:**
$$
I_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R_{coil}} = \dfrac{110}{11} = 10\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Time constants:**
$$
\tau_1 = \dfrac{L}{R_{coil}} = \dfrac{0.55}{11} = 0.05\ \text{s} = 50\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = \dfrac{L}{R_{coil}+R_{aux}} = \dfrac{0.55}{11+33} = \dfrac{0.55}{44} = 0.0125\ \text{s} = 12.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Current at $t = 50\ \text{ms}$ (just before knee):**
$$
i(50^{-}) = I_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_1}\right) = 10 \times \left(1 - e^{-50/50}\right) = 10 \times (1 - e^{-1})
$$

$$
i(50^{-}) = 10 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 10 \times 0.6321 = 6.321\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Slope just after the knee:**
The decay starts from $I_{knee} = 6.321\ \text{A}$ with time constant $\tau_2$.

$$
\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{t_{knee}^{+}} = -\dfrac{I_{knee}}{\tau_2} = -\dfrac{6.321}{0.0125} = -505.68\ \text{A/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{knee^{+}} \approx -0.5057\ \text{A/ms}\;}
$$

*(Engineering remark — 0.5 mark: the negative slope is steep because $\tau_2 < \tau_1$. If the recorded slope is shallower than $-0.4\ \text{A/ms}$, the CBM raises a "weak spring" alarm.)*

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in CBM questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** in wear problems: $\rho_{cu}$ must be converted to **kg** (not grams) when $k_{ero}$ is in mm/MJ. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Confusing arc energy $E_{arc}$ with fault energy** at the bus — $E_{arc}$ is only what is *inside* the CB chamber, not the total system fault energy.
> 3. **Using the RMS value of $I_{pk}$** — the CBM uses *peak* values for wear integration; RMS underestimates wear by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.
> 4. **Mixing up $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$** in the knee-point problem — $\tau_1$ is the *rise* constant, $\tau_2$ is the *decay* constant.
> 5. **Forgetting IRIG-B / time synchronisation** in the architecture question — examiners frequently allot 1 mark for the time-sync mention.
> 6. **Not stating IEC 61850** explicitly — it is the "smart grid keyword" examiners look for in any substation-automation answer.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CBM = Condition-Monitoring IED**, *not* a protection relay.
- [x] **Core standards:** IEC 61850, IEC 62271-3 (CB monitoring).
- [x] **Key sensors:** CTs on coils/motor, LVDT for travel, PT100, density meter.
- [x] **Analog channels sampled at ≥ 10 kSps** (Nyquist compliance).
- [x] **500 ms capture window** per operation is the industry norm.
- [x] **Arc-energy formula:** $E_{arc} = \int v_{arc}\, i_{arc}\, dt$ — appears in **almost every KTU 14-mark question**.
- [x] **Wear formula:** $\Delta d_{wear} \propto E_{arc} \propto I_{pk}^2 t_{arc}$.
- [x] **Trip-coil "knee point"** is the single most important diagnostic feature.
- [x] **Coil current rise:** $i(t) = (V_{dc}/R)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, with $\tau = L/R$.
- [x] **Healthy SF₆ density @ 20 °C:** 0.5 – 0.7 MPa; alarm if **< 0.52 MPa**.
- [x] **CBM reduces OPEX** (operating expenditure) by ~40 % and unplanned outages by ~70 %.
- [x] **Communication:** MMS for reports, GOOSE for fast alarms, IRIG-B for time sync.
- [x] **Always state** that the CBM works *post-event* (not in real-time fault clearance).
- [x] **Remember the difference:** CBM = **Circuit Breaker Monitor** (this topic) vs. CBM = **Condition-Based Maintenance** (the *strategy* the monitor enables).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a sophisticated, high-precision electronic device deployed at strategic nodes of a power system to perform real-time, time-synchronized measurements of voltage and current phasors (magnitude and phase angle) on the electricity grid. The defining feature that distinguishes a PMU from a conventional SCADA Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) is its ability to assign a **high-accuracy timestamp** to every measurement, derived from the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** satellite constellation, enabling geographically dispersed measurements to be compared against a single, common time reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEEE C37.118.1-2011 Compliant):**
> *"A PMU is a device that produces synchronized phasor, frequency, and rate of change of frequency (ROCOF) estimates from voltage and/or current signals and a time synchronization signal."*

The synchrophasor measurements follow the **IEEE Std C37.118.1™ / IEC 60255-118-1:2018** standard, which defines the **Total Vector Error (TVE)** as the primary performance metric. A perfectly operating PMU must keep the **TVE below 1%**, as mandated for steady-state Class M (Measurement) and Class P (Protection) compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from SCADA:**
> - **SCADA/RTU:** Refresh rate of 2–10 seconds; measurements are NOT time-synchronized across the grid; only magnitudes are typically reported.
> - **PMU:** Refresh rate of 10–60 samples per second (up to 240 fps in some standards); measurements ARE time-synchronized globally; both magnitude AND phase angle are reported.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PMU as a **highly synchronized, ultra-precise stopwatch and camera** rolled into one, but designed for electricity:

- **Imagine a giant orchestra** where musicians (generators) are scattered across a country. Each musician is playing slightly out of tune (phase difference) and at slightly different loudness (magnitude).
- A **regular SCADA system** is like a person walking around the concert hall and asking each musician, *"How loud are you playing?"* — they only get a slow, magnitude-only snapshot.
- A **PMU system** is like giving every musician a **GPS-disciplined metronome** and an **instant reporter** who shouts out every 1/30th of a second: *"I am playing at exactly volume 7.3, at phase 42.6°, at timestamp 14:32:05.000327 UTC."* The conductor at the central control room can then instantly detect which musician is drifting out of sync and adjust accordingly.

This **"GPS-stamped snapshot"** is the essence of a synchrophasor, and it is the foundational data block upon which the entire **Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)** is built.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics

The following constants and standards govern PMU design and deployment:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GPS Timestamp Accuracy** | $\pm 1$ | $\mu s$ | Time synchronization tolerance |
| **TVE Threshold (Class M)** | $\le 1\%$ | % | Steady-state measurement error |
| **TVE Threshold (Class P)** | $\le 1\%$ | % | Under dynamic conditions |
| **Reporting Rate (Standard)** | 10, 25, 50 | frames/sec | IEEE C37.118.1 default |
| **Reporting Rate (Extended)** | Up to 240 (as per IEEE 60255-118-1) | frames/sec | For sub-cycle protection |
| **Standard Phasor Format** | IEEE C37.118.2 | — | Communication protocol |
| **Time Source** | GPS (L1, 1575.42 MHz) | — | Universal time reference (UTC) |
| **Phase Angle Reference** | Cosine wave at nominal frequency locked to UTC | — | Common reference frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synchrophasor** = Synchronized + Phasor. The word "synchro" emphasizes the GPS time-stamping, while "phasor" emphasizes the complex-number representation of the sinusoidal AC waveform.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a PMU

A PMU is essentially a **high-speed, time-synchronized digital signal processor** for power system waveforms. Its internal architecture is divided into **six logical blocks**, each of which must operate with deterministic latency to maintain the timestamp accuracy guarantee.

### Block 1: Analog Input Stage (Instrument Transformers)
- **Voltage signals** are stepped down from the transmission level (e.g., 400 kV, 220 kV) using **Capacitive Voltage Transformers (CVTs)** or **Voltage Transformers (VTs)** to a low-level signal (typically $\pm 5$ V or 1 A, 5 A).
- **Current signals** are conditioned through **Current Transformers (CTs)**.
- The signals are passed through **surge protection** circuits and **isolation amplifiers** to safeguard the low-voltage electronics.

### Block 2: Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)
- An **analog low-pass filter** designed to remove frequency components above the **Nyquist frequency** (which is half the sampling rate, $f_s / 2$).
- This is a **Butterworth** or **elliptical** analog filter, typically of order 4 to 6, to ensure a flat passband up to the nominal frequency (50/60 Hz) and at least 40 dB attenuation in the stopband.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$. For a 50 Hz system with PMU sampling at 1.2 kHz (24 samples per cycle), the Nyquist frequency is 600 Hz, easily accommodating all expected harmonics and dynamic swings.

### Block 3: Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
- A **high-resolution, simultaneously-sampling ADC** (typically 16-bit, sometimes 18-bit or 24-bit).
- The ADC is triggered by a **synchronized sampling clock** derived from the GPS-disciplined oscillator, ensuring that the "t = 0" instant of every sample is aligned across all PMUs in the network.

### Block 4: GPS Receiver and Synchronization
- A **GPS receiver module** decodes the L1 (1575.42 MHz) signal from at least 4 satellites to compute the **Pulse-Per-Second (PPS)** signal.
- The PPS is fed into a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** or a **direct digital synthesis (DDS)** circuit to generate a high-frequency, GPS-locked sampling clock.
- **Time accuracy:** The PPS signal is accurate to within $\pm 100$ ns of UTC, which corresponds to a phase error of only $\pm 0.002°$ at 50 Hz, well within the $0.01°$ to $0.1°$ accuracy expected from a Class M PMU.

### Block 5: Phasor Estimation Algorithm
- The sampled waveform $x[n]$ is processed using a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)**, typically a recursive version called the **Recursive DFT (RDFT)** or a **Modified Cosine Filter** bank.
- For a signal $x(t) = X_m \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$, the fundamental phasor estimate is:

$$
\hat{X}(k) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}
$$

- The output $\hat{X}$ is a **complex number** $\hat{X} = X_r + j X_i$, from which the magnitude and phase are extracted.
- The factor $\sqrt{2}/N$ converts the **peak** of the sinusoid into an **RMS** value, matching the convention used in power system measurements.

### Block 6: Communication Module
- The computed phasor, frequency, and ROCOF are packaged into a **C37.118.2 frame** and transmitted over Ethernet (typically UDP/IP or TCP/IP) to a **Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC)** at rates of 10–60 fps (or higher).

## 2.2 Why Synchrophasors Matter — The Engineering Utility

| Application | Engineering Utility | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Real-Time Voltage Stability Monitoring** | Detect impending voltage collapse via $V$–$Q$ margin tracking. | Frequently asked in 14-mark questions. |
| **Frequency Response & Inertia Estimation** | Compute ROCOF and system inertia in real time. | Direct CO mapping for Module 2. |
| **Wide-Area Control (WACS)** | Initiate controlled islanding or load shedding. | Advanced application question. |
| **State Estimation Enhancement** | Hybrid SCADA-PMU linear state estimator. | High-weightage topic. |
| **Fault Location & Post-Event Analysis** | Time-synchronized fault recording across the grid. | Practical KTU problem. |
| **Modal Analysis / Oscillation Detection** | Identify inter-area modes via Prony/ERA methods. | Research-level discussion. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{X} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}$ | DFT-based synchrophasor estimate | p.u. or V, A | $N$ = samples per cycle (typically 12, 24, 48, 96) |
| 2 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\operatorname{Im}(\hat{X})}{\operatorname{Re}(\hat{X})}\right)$ | Phase angle extraction | radians or degrees | $-\pi \le \theta \le \pi$ |
| 3 | $\vert \hat{X} \vert = \sqrt{\operatorname{Re}(\hat{X})^2 + \operatorname{Im}(\hat{X})^2}$ | Phasor magnitude (RMS) | V or A | $> 0$ |
| 4 | $TVE = \dfrac{\vert \hat{X} - X_{true} \vert}{\vert X_{true} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Total Vector Error per IEEE C37.118 | % | $\le 1\%$ (Class M and P) |
| 5 | $f = f_0 + \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Instantaneous frequency | Hz | Nominal $f_0 = 50$ Hz (India) |
| 6 | $ROCOF = \dfrac{df}{dt}$ | Rate of Change of Frequency | Hz/s | Trigger: $\pm 0.5$ Hz/s in some grids |
| 7 | $X_{pos} = \dfrac{1}{3}(X_a + X_b \cdot e^{j 2\pi / 3} + X_c \cdot e^{-j 2\pi / 3})$ | Symmetrical component extraction | p.u. | Used for positive-sequence phasor |
| 8 | $\Delta t_{max} = \dfrac{\theta_{err, max}}{360 \cdot f_0}$ | Max allowable timing error for given phase accuracy | seconds | e.g., $0.01° / (360 \cdot 50) \approx 0.56 \,\mu s$ |
| 9 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Nyquist limit | Hz | Must exceed max measured freq. |
| 10 | $PPS_{accuracy} \le \dfrac{\phi_{err}}{2\pi f_0}$ | PPS required accuracy for phase error $\phi_{err}$ | seconds | $\pm 1 \,\mu s$ typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always remember that for a 50 Hz system, a **timing error of 1 microsecond** translates to a **phase error of 0.018°**. The GPS receiver therefore must be sub-microsecond accurate.

## 2.4 Positive-Sequence Phasor vs. Three-Phase Phasors

A PMU typically reports **two types of phasors**:

1. **Per-phase phasors** ($\hat{V}_a, \hat{V}_b, \hat{V}_c$): directly estimated from the three-phase voltages using the DFT.
2. **Positive-sequence phasor** ($\hat{V}_1$): computed from the three-phase phasors using the symmetrical component transformation. The positive-sequence phasor is the most useful for **dynamic security assessment** because it is immune to balanced disturbances and is the variable tracked by the **center of inertia** frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** In Indian grids (50 Hz), the nominal system frequency is **50 Hz**. KTU examinations often ask students to compare a 50 Hz system with a 60 Hz system or to calculate the phasor update rate per cycle. Remember that **25 frames/sec at 50 Hz = 1 frame every 2 cycles**, while **50 fps = 1 frame per cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Phasor Estimate from a Sampled Sinusoid

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A PMU samples the voltage signal $v(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$ at a sampling rate $f_s = N \cdot f_0$, where $N$ is the integer number of samples per cycle of the nominal frequency. Derive the DFT-based estimate of the synchrophasor.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the continuous-time signal.**

The voltage at the PMU bus is a cosine waveform with peak amplitude $V_m$, frequency $f_0$, and phase $\phi$:

$$
v(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)
$$

**Step 2: Express the signal in complex exponential form.**

Using Euler's identity, $\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{j(\omega t + \phi)} + e^{-j(\omega t + \phi)}\right)$, the signal becomes:

$$
v(t) = \frac{V_m}{2} e^{j(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)} + \frac{V_m}{2} e^{-j(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)}
$$

**Step 3: Define the continuous-time phasor.**

The phasor is defined as the complex coefficient of the positive-frequency component $e^{j 2\pi f_0 t}$:

$$
V = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi}
$$

Note the $\sqrt{2}$ factor, which converts the **peak amplitude** to the **RMS amplitude** in accordance with the power system convention.

**Step 4: Discretize the signal.**

The signal is sampled at $t_n = n / f_s$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$:

$$
v[n] = V_m \cos\!\left(2\pi f_0 \cdot \frac{n}{f_s} + \phi\right)
$$

Substituting $f_s = N f_0$, we obtain $2\pi f_0 / f_s = 2\pi / N$:

$$
v[n] = V_m \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N} + \phi\right)
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Discrete Fourier Transform.**

The standard DFT coefficient at bin $k = 1$ (the fundamental frequency) is:

$$
\hat{V}_{DFT}[1] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the discretized signal.**

$$
\hat{V}_{DFT}[1] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} V_m \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N} + \phi\right) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}
$$

**Step 7: Convert the cosine to complex exponentials.**

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N} + \phi\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{j(2\pi n / N + \phi)} + e^{-j(2\pi n / N + \phi)}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\hat{V}_{DFT}[1] = \frac{V_m}{2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[e^{j\phi} \cdot e^{j 2\pi n / N} + e^{-j\phi} \cdot e^{-j 3 \cdot 2\pi n / N}\right] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}
$$

**Step 8: Simplify each exponential term.**

First term: $e^{j\phi} \cdot e^{j 2\pi n / N} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N} = e^{j\phi} \cdot e^{0} = e^{j\phi}$.

Second term: $e^{-j\phi} \cdot e^{-j 3 \cdot 2\pi n / N} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N} = e^{-j\phi} \cdot e^{-j 4 \cdot 2\pi n / N}$.

**Step 9: Evaluate the sums.**

$$
\hat{V}_{DFT}[1] = \frac{V_m}{2} \left[ e^{j\phi} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} 1 + e^{-j\phi} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j 8\pi n / N} \right]
$$

Recall the identity for a geometric sum:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j 2\pi m n / N} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } m \equiv 0 \pmod{N} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The first sum has $m = 0$, so it equals $N$.

The second sum has $m = 4$ (since $8\pi / (2\pi) = 4$), and assuming $N \ne 4$, the second sum is **zero**.

$$
\hat{V}_{DFT}[1] = \frac{V_m}{2} \cdot N \cdot e^{j\phi} = \frac{N V_m}{2} e^{j\phi}
$$

**Step 10: Convert peak to RMS and finalize the phasor estimate.**

The synchrophasor (RMS) is:

$$
\hat{V} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \cdot \hat{V}_{DFT}[1] = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \cdot \frac{N V_m}{2} e^{j\phi} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi}
$$

This is exactly the **true phasor** $V = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi}$, confirming that the DFT-based estimator is **unbiased** at the nominal frequency, provided:

- The signal is exactly at the nominal frequency $f_0$.
- The sampling is perfectly synchronized (window length is exactly an integer number of cycles).
- The system is in steady state (no frequency drift, no harmonics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Synchrophasor Estimation Equation (DFT form):**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{V} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}\;}
$$

The magnitude and phase of $\hat{V}$ give the **RMS voltage** and the **phase angle** in the global UTC reference frame.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Total Vector Error (TVE)

The TVE is the **single scalar metric** mandated by the IEEE C37.118 standard to quantify the combined effect of magnitude, phase, and timing errors on a synchrophasor.

**Step 1: Define the true synchrophasor.**

$$
X_{true} = X_r + j X_i = \frac{X_m}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi}
$$

**Step 2: Define the measured (estimated) synchrophasor.**

$$
\hat{X} = \hat{X}_r + j \hat{X}_i
$$

**Step 3: Form the error vector.**

$$
\Delta X = \hat{X} - X_{true}
$$

**Step 4: Define TVE as the magnitude of the error vector, normalized by the magnitude of the true phasor.**

$$
\boxed{\;TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X} - X_{true} \vert}{\vert X_{true} \vert} \times 100\%\;}
$$

**Step 5: Expand the error using magnitude and phase error terms.**

If the magnitude error is $\Delta X_m = \hat{X}_m - X_m$ and the phase error is $\Delta \phi = \hat{\phi} - \phi$, then for small errors, the TVE can be approximated as:

$$
TVE \approx \sqrt{\left(\frac{\Delta X_m}{X_m}\right)^2 + (\Delta \phi)^2} \times 100\%
$$

This shows that **TVE combines amplitude and phase errors in a single metric**, with both contributing quadratically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> A true voltage phasor is $V_{true} = 1.0 \angle 30.0°$ p.u. A PMU reports $\hat{V} = 1.005 \angle 30.05°$ p.u. Find the TVE.

> **Solution:**
> $V_{true} = 1.0 \cos 30° + j \cdot 1.0 \sin 30° = 0.8660 + j \cdot 0.5000$.
> $\hat{V} = 1.005 \cos 30.05° + j \cdot 1.005 \sin 30.05° = 0.8701 + j \cdot 0.5035$.
> $\Delta V = \hat{V} - V_{true} = 0.0041 + j \cdot 0.0035$.
> $\vert \Delta V \vert = \sqrt{0.0041^2 + 0.0035^2} = \sqrt{0.0000168 + 0.0000123} = \sqrt{0.0000291} \approx 0.00539$.
> $TVE = (0.00539 / 1.0) \times 100\% = 0.539\%$.
> Since $TVE \le 1\%$, the PMU is compliant.

## 3.3 Frequency and ROCOF Derivation

Given a sequence of phase angle measurements $\theta[k]$ sampled at the PMU reporting rate $f_r$, the **instantaneous frequency** is computed as the derivative of the unwrapped phase angle.

**Step 1: Phase difference between consecutive reports.**

$$
\Delta \theta[k] = \theta[k] - \theta[k-1]
$$

**Step 2: Convert phase difference to frequency deviation.**

$$
f[k] = f_0 + \frac{f_r}{2\pi} \cdot \Delta \theta[k]
$$

where $f_r$ is the PMU reporting rate (e.g., 50 frames/sec).

**Step 3: ROCOF is the discrete derivative of frequency.**

$$
ROCOF[k] = \frac{f[k] - f[k-1]}{1 / f_r} = f_r \cdot (f[k] - f[k-1])
$$

$$
\boxed{\;ROCOF = f_r^2 \cdot (\theta[k] - 2\theta[k-1] + \theta[k-2])\; / \; (2\pi)\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A frequency deviation of 0.1 Hz in a 50 Hz system corresponds to a phase drift of $360° \cdot 0.1 / 50 = 0.72°$ per second. ROCOF is the **first derivative of frequency** and is the most sensitive indicator of sudden generation-load imbalance. Modern ROCOF-based anti-islanding protection in India is typically set at **0.5 Hz/s** or **1.0 Hz/s**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PMU Internal Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CT and VT Inputs] --> B1[Surge Protection and Isolation]
    B1 --> C1[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    C1 --> D1[Simultaneous Sampling 16 bit ADC]
    D1 --> E1[GPS Disciplined Sampling Clock]
    F1[GPS Antenna L1 1575.42 MHz] --> G1[GPS Receiver Module]
    G1 --> H1[Pulse Per Second PPS Signal]
    H1 --> E1
    D1 --> I1[DFT Phasor Estimator]
    I1 --> J1[Magnitude and Phase Extraction]
    J1 --> K1[Positive Sequence Calculator]
    K1 --> L1[Frequency and ROCOF Calculator]
    L1 --> M1[IEEE C37.118.2 Frame Builder]
    M1 --> N1[Ethernet UDP to PDC]
    N1 --> O1[Phasor Data Concentrator PDC]
    O1 --> P1[Wide Area Monitoring System WAMS]
```

> **Figure 1 — Block Diagram of a PMU Internal Signal Flow.**
> Note the **dual input** at the ADC: one from the analog conditioning path, and one from the GPS-disciplined clock. This dual synchrony (signal + time) is the hallmark of a PMU.

## 4.2 Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS) Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sub1[Substation A]
        PMU1[PMU at Bus 1]
        PMU2[PMU at Bus 2]
    end
    subgraph sub2[Substation B]
        PMU3[PMU at Bus 3]
        PMU4[PMU at Bus 4]
    end
    subgraph sub3[Control Center]
        PDC1[Local PDC]
        PDC2[Super PDC]
        HMI[Operator HMI]
    end
    SAT[GPS Satellite Constellation] -->|L1 Signal| PMU1
    SAT -->|L1 Signal| PMU2
    SAT -->|L1 Signal| PMU3
    SAT -->|L1 Signal| PMU4
    PMU1 -->|C37.118.2 UDP| PDC1
    PMU2 -->|C37.118.2 UDP| PDC1
    PMU3 -->|C37.118.2 UDP| PDC2
    PMU4 -->|C37.118.2 UDP| PDC2
    PDC1 --> PDC2
    PDC2 --> HMI
    HMI --> ACT[Wide Area Control Commands]
```

> **Figure 2 — Hierarchical WAMS Architecture.**
> Each PMU receives an independent GPS lock, ensuring that all measurements are referenced to the **same UTC time base**. The local PDC aligns and time-stamps the data, then forwards it to the super PDC at the main control center.

## 4.3 PMU Data Packet Structure (C37.118.2 Frame)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[SYN Byte 0xAA] --> B2[Frame Size 2 bytes]
    B2 --> C2[ID Code 2 bytes]
    C2 --> D2[UTC Time 8 bytes SOC and FRACSEC]
    D2 --> E2[Data Type 2 bits]
    E2 --> F2[Phasor Estimates]
    F2 --> G2[Frequency Estimate]
    G2 --> H2[ROCOF Estimate]
    H2 --> I2[Analog and Digital Words optional]
    I2 --> J2[CHK CRC 2 bytes]
```

> **Figure 3 — Structure of a C37.118.2 Data Frame.**
> The **SOC (Seconds of Century)** and **FRACSEC (Fraction of Second)** fields together encode the **time of measurement** with nanosecond precision, which is the key enabler of synchrophasor data alignment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) and state its key advantage over a conventional SCADA RTU. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a microprocessor-based device that produces **synchronized real-time measurements of voltage and current phasors**, along with **frequency** and **Rate of Change of Frequency (ROCOF)**, using a common time reference from the **Global Positioning System (GPS)**. It complies with the **IEEE C37.118.1** standard for synchrophasor measurement.

**Key advantage over SCADA RTU (1 mark each):**

1. **Time synchronization:** PMU measurements are time-stamped to a common UTC reference, enabling direct comparison of phase angles across geographically distant buses; SCADA RTU measurements are asynchronous.
2. **High reporting rate:** PMU reports at 10–60 fps (or higher); SCADA RTUs typically scan at 2–10 second intervals.
3. **Phase angle measurement:** PMUs directly measure phase angle, which is critical for stability assessment; SCADA RTUs typically do not measure phase angle.

### Question 2: What is the Total Vector Error (TVE) and what is its permissible limit as per IEEE C37.118.1? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Total Vector Error (TVE)** is a scalar metric defined in **IEEE Std C37.118.1** that quantifies the combined error between the **measured synchrophasor** and the **true synchrophasor** at a given instant. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X} - X_{true} \vert}{\vert X_{true} \vert} \times 100\%
$$

where $\hat{X}$ is the PMU's measured phasor and $X_{true}$ is the true phasor.

**Permissible Limit:** The standard mandates that the TVE shall be **less than or equal to 1%** for both **Class M (Measurement)** and **Class P (Protection)** compliance under all specified steady-state and dynamic test conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):**
> Many students write "TVE is the difference between measured and true phasor." This is **incomplete** and loses 1 mark. You must specify that it is the **magnitude of the complex difference**, **normalized by the true phasor magnitude**, and **expressed as a percentage**. The formula is mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU). Discuss the role of the GPS receiver in time synchronization. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Derive the DFT-based synchrophasor estimation equation from a sampled voltage signal. Comment on the impact of off-nominal frequency on the phasor estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A PMU is a high-speed, time-synchronized measurement device defined under IEEE C37.118.1. It is the cornerstone of the Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS).

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The PMU architecture consists of the following blocks:

1. **Analog Input Stage:** CTs and VTs scale down transmission-level signals (e.g., 400 kV) to instrument-level signals (e.g., 1 A, 5 A, $\pm 5$ V). Isolation and surge protection are essential. (0.5 mark)
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass filter (Butterworth, order 4–6) removes spectral components above the Nyquist frequency. (0.5 mark)
3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** A 16-bit (or higher) simultaneously-sampling ADC digitizes the signal at $N$ samples per cycle. (0.5 mark)
4. **GPS Receiver and PLL:** Decodes the L1 GPS signal, generates the **Pulse-Per-Second (PPS)** signal, and locks the local oscillator. (0.5 mark)
5. **Phasor Estimator (DFT):** Computes the complex synchrophasor via a recursive or one-cycle DFT. (0.5 mark)
6. **Communication Module:** Packages the phasor, frequency, and ROCOF into C37.118.2 frames and transmits to the PDC. (0.5 mark)

**Role of GPS Receiver in Time Synchronization (3 marks):**

- The **Global Positioning System (GPS)** provides a **universal time reference (UTC)** that is globally accessible and has accuracy better than 100 ns.
- The PMU's GPS receiver tracks signals from at least 4 satellites, computes its position, and extracts a 1 PPS signal.
- This 1 PPS signal is fed into a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** to generate a high-frequency, GPS-locked sampling clock (e.g., 1.2 kHz, 2.4 kHz).
- All PMUs sharing the same GPS time reference are **automatically synchronized** to within $\pm 1 \,\mu s$, which corresponds to a phase error of less than $0.02°$ at 50 Hz.
- This guarantees that the **time-of-measurement** is identical across all PMUs in the grid, enabling direct phase-angle comparison.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The detailed derivation is presented in Section 3.1 above. The key steps are:

1. The continuous-time signal is $v(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$.
2. The signal is sampled at $t_n = n/f_s$, yielding $v[n] = V_m \cos(2\pi n / N + \phi)$ for $N$ samples per cycle.
3. The DFT at bin $k = 1$ is:

$$
\hat{V}[1] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}
$$

4. Using the geometric-series identity and converting peak to RMS, the final synchrophasor is:

$$
\hat{V} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}
$$

**Impact of Off-Nominal Frequency (2 marks):**

When the actual system frequency $f_{act} \ne f_0$ (e.g., 50.1 Hz instead of 50 Hz), the DFT estimator produces errors:

- **Spectral Leakage:** The signal energy "leaks" into adjacent DFT bins because the window is no longer an integer number of cycles, causing magnitude error and phase error.
- **Attenuation (Magnitude Error):** The magnitude estimate is reduced by a factor of approximately $\operatorname{sinc}(\pi \Delta f / f_0)$, where $\Delta f = f_{act} - f_0$.
- **Phase Error:** The phase error grows linearly with the frequency deviation, and can exceed 1° for $\Delta f = 0.5$ Hz.
- To mitigate this, **windowing functions** (Hann, Hamming) or **frequency-tracking DFT** algorithms are used in modern PMUs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the IEEE C37.118.1 standard for synchrophasor measurements. Define Total Vector Error (TVE) and frequency error, and state their permissible limits. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)**. List and briefly explain any four applications of PMU data in smart grid operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (a) [7 marks]

**IEEE C37.118.1 Standard (3 marks):**

The **IEEE C37.118.1 standard** (latest revision: **IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011**, with the synchrophasor measurement definition incorporated into **IEC 60255-118-1:2018**) defines:

- The **synchrophasor** as a phasor estimated at a specific time instant referenced to UTC.
- The **measurement performance** requirements, including TVE, frequency error, and ROCOF error.
- The **compliance test conditions** under steady-state, dynamic, and step-change conditions.
- The **two performance classes**:
  - **Class M (Measurement):** For monitoring applications; less stringent under dynamic conditions.
  - **Class P (Protection):** For protection applications; more stringent under step changes and frequency ramps.

**TVE Definition and Limit (2 marks):**

$$
TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X} - X_{true} \vert}{\vert X_{true} \vert} \times 100\%
$$

**Permissible Limit:** $TVE \le 1\%$ for both Class M and Class P.

**Frequency Error (FE) Definition and Limit (2 marks):**

$$
FE = \vert f_{measured} - f_{true} \vert
$$

**Permissible Limits (typical):**
- Class M: $\le 0.005$ Hz (steady state), $\le 0.3$ Hz (under off-nominal frequency).
- Class P: $\le 0.025$ Hz (steady state), $\le 0.3$ Hz (dynamic).

**ROCOF Error (RE):**

$$
RE = \vert ROCOF_{measured} - ROCOF_{true} \vert
$$

**Permissible Limits:**
- Class M: $\le 0.01$ Hz/s (steady), $\le 2.3$ Hz/s (dynamic).
- Class P: $\le 0.1$ Hz/s (steady), $\le 4$ Hz/s (dynamic).

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b) [7 marks]

**WAMS Architecture (3 marks):**

The WAMS consists of the following hierarchical blocks (refer to Figure 2 in Section 4.2):

1. **PMU layer:** PMUs installed at strategic buses and substations, each time-synchronized via GPS.
2. **PDC layer (Phasor Data Concentrator):** Collects and aligns the PMU data based on the UTC timestamp, sorts packets by time, and forwards them upstream.
3. **Super PDC / Control Center:** Aggregates data from all local PDCs and provides a unified, grid-wide view.
4. **Applications layer:** Real-time monitoring, control, and analytics (visualization, alarm generation, wide-area control).

**Four Applications of PMU Data (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Real-Time Voltage Stability Monitoring:** PMU data enables the computation of voltage stability margins (e.g., $V$–$Q$ sensitivity) at all monitored buses, allowing operators to predict voltage collapse well in advance.
2. **Inter-Area Oscillation Detection:** Modal analysis (Prony, Eigensystem Realization Algorithm) of PMU frequency and phase data reveals poorly damped inter-area modes (typically 0.1–0.8 Hz), enabling timely remedial action.
3. **Frequency Response and Inertia Estimation:** The ROCOF measurement, combined with known load-shedding events, allows the system inertia $H$ to be estimated in real time, supporting renewable-rich grids with reduced rotational inertia.
4. **State Estimation Enhancement:** The integration of PMU data into the conventional Weighted Least Squares (WLS) state estimator transforms it into a **linear state estimator**, providing a more accurate and faster snapshot of the entire system state.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> 1. **Common Mistake in Q(a):** Students often forget to specify that the **TVE limit is 1%** and instead write "TVE should be minimum." The **numerical value** is the hallmark of a complete answer.
> 2. **Common Mistake in Q(b):** Students frequently skip the **PDC layer** in the WAMS diagram, jumping directly from PMU to control center. A local PDC is **mandatory** in the architecture and carries 1 mark.
> 3. **Arithmetic Mistake in TVE problems:** When given a numerical TVE problem, students often forget to **multiply by 100%** at the end and lose 0.5 marks. Always write the final answer in **percentage form**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for PMU:**

- **Definition:** A PMU is a GPS-synchronized device that measures voltage and current phasors, frequency, and ROCOF, compliant with IEEE C37.118.1.
- **Key Standards:** **IEEE C37.118.1** (measurements), **IEEE C37.118.2** (communication), and **IEC 60255-118-1:2018** (international).
- **GPS Frequency Used:** **L1 band, 1575.42 MHz**; 1 PPS signal is the time reference.
- **GPS Timing Accuracy:** Better than $\pm 1 \,\mu s$ corresponds to a phase accuracy better than **$0.018°$ at 50 Hz**.
- **TVE Limit:** **$\le 1\%$** for both Class M and Class P under all standard test conditions.
- **TVE Formula:** $TVE = \dfrac{\vert \hat{X} - X_{true} \vert}{\vert X_{true} \vert} \times 100\%$.
- **Phasor Formula:** $\hat{V} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n / N}$.
