- **Frequency Formula:** $f = f_0 + \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$.
- **ROCOF Formula:** $ROCOF = \dfrac{df}{dt}$, computed as a discrete second derivative of phase.
- **Reporting Rates:** **10, 25, 50 fps** are standard; **up to 240 fps** in IEC 60255-118-1.
- **Nyquist Rule:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$; sampling at 24 samples/cycle (1200 Hz for 50 Hz) is common.
- **Off-Nominal Frequency Error:** Spectral leakage causes magnitude attenuation ($\operatorname{sinc}$ factor) and linear phase error.
- **Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC):** Time-aligns and de-duplicates PMU packets before forwarding to control center.
- **WAMS Hierarchy:** PMU $\rightarrow$ Local PDC $\rightarrow$ Super PDC $\rightarrow$ Control Center $\rightarrow$ Applications.
- **Positive-Sequence Phasor:** Computed from $X_a, X_b, X_c$ via $X_{pos} = \frac{1}{3}(X_a + a X_b + a^2 X_c)$ where $a = e^{j 2\pi / 3}$.
- **SCADA vs PMU:** SCADA = 2–10 sec scan, no phase, no time sync. PMU = 10–60 fps, phase + magnitude, GPS time-stamped.
- **Key Indian Context:** Indian grid operates at **50 Hz**; CERC has mandated PMU installation in all **400 kV and 220 kV substations** under the **Unified Real-Time Dynamic State Measurement (URTDSM)** project by **POWERGRID**.
- **Applications:** Voltage stability, oscillation monitoring, inertia estimation, post-event analysis, state estimation, and wide-area control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)?

A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a high-speed, synchronized digital instrument deployed at substations and strategic points in a power network that measures the instantaneous voltage and current phasors (magnitude and angle) using a common time reference provided by the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** or equivalent timing sources like the **Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System (IRNSS / NavIC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A PMU is a device that produces **synchronized phasor**, **frequency**, and **Rate of Change of Frequency (ROCOF)** measurements from voltage and current waveforms in real-time, conforming to the **IEEE C37.118 / IEEE 60255-118 series of standards**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Synchronized Stopwatch Network"

Imagine a giant classroom where students (substations) are spread across Kerala, Chennai, and Bengaluru. Each student has an identical, perfectly synchronized stopwatch linked by satellite. The teacher fires a whistle at exactly 12:00 noon. Every student notes the **time elapsed, the amplitude of the whistle sound (magnitude), and the phase (angle) at that exact moment**.

- **GPS signal** = The satellite-provided common stopwatch
- **Voltage/Current waveforms** = The whistle sound waves at each station
- **Synchrophasor** = The amplitude and angle noted at the exact synchronized tick
- **PMU** = The student with the synchronized stopwatch
- **PDC (Phasor Data Concentrator)** = The central teacher collecting all readings

Without synchronization, comparing angles between distant substations is impossible because each substation sees the waveform from a different time reference.

---

## 1.3 The PMU Standards Ecosystem

PMUs do **not** operate in a vacuum — they must strictly follow international standards to ensure **interoperability**, **accuracy**, and **real-time data exchange** between devices from different manufacturers. The two most critical standardization frameworks are:

| Standard | Issuing Body | Scope |
|----------|--------------|-------|
| **IEEE C37.118.1 – 2011** | IEEE Power & Energy Society | Synchrophasor measurement requirements (replaced by IEEE 60255-118-1) |
| **IEEE C37.118.2 – 2011** | IEEE Power & Energy Society | Synchrophasor data transfer protocol (replaced by IEEE 60255-118-2) |
| **IEEE 60255-118-1 – 2018** | IEEE / IEC | Unified synchrophasor, frequency, ROCOF measurement standard |
| **IEEE 60255-118-2 – 2018** | IEEE / IEC | Unified communication protocol (IEEE C37.118.2 + IEEE C37.244 PDC) |
| **IEEE C37.244 – 2013** | IEEE | Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC) standard |

> [!NOTE]
> The **IEEE 60255-118 series** is the most current and authoritative standard as of 2024, harmonizing measurement and communication requirements globally. It supersedes and consolidates the older IEEE C37.118 family.

---

## 1.4 Why Standards Matter in Smart Grids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask, *"Why are PMU standards essential for smart grid operation?"* The answer framework is:
> 1. **Interoperability** — PMUs from Siemens, GE, ABB, SEL must communicate seamlessly.
> 2. **Accuracy benchmarking** — Common **Total Vector Error (TVE)** thresholds.
> 3. **Real-time wide-area monitoring (WAMS)** — Uniform data formats for PDCs.
> 4. **Cybersecurity** — Modern standards mandate secure tunneling (TLS/SSH).
> 5. **Regulatory compliance** — Utilities (e.g., PGCIL, KSEB) require standards for grid synchronization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Synchrophasor Concept (Foundation of PMU Standards)

A standard sinusoidal voltage waveform can be represented as:

$$v(t) = V_{m} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

Where:
- $V_{m}$ = peak amplitude
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency
- $\phi$ = phase angle

The **synchrophasor representation** under PMU standards expresses this waveform as a complex phasor $\mathbf{X} = X_{r} + jX_{i}$ at a specific time tag $t_{k}$ (provided by GPS/IRNSS).

> [!NOTE]
> The **phases of all PMU measurements across the grid are referenced to a single, common time base** — typically **Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)** — even though the power system is geographically distributed. This is the central innovation of the IEEE C37.118 / IEEE 60255-118 standards.

---

## 2.2 Total Vector Error (TVE) — The Core Accuracy Metric

The **Total Vector Error (TVE)** is the defining performance metric of all PMU standards. It quantifies the combined magnitude and phase deviation between the **measured phasor** $\mathbf{X}_{m}$ and the **ideal reference phasor** $\mathbf{X}$ at the same instant.

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Definition

$$\text{TVE}(n) = \frac{\vert \mathbf{X}_{r}(n) - \mathbf{X}(n) \vert}{\vert \mathbf{X}(n) \vert}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{X}_{r}(n) = X_{r}(n) + jX_{i}(n)$ is the **reported (measured) phasor** at sample $n$
- $\mathbf{X}(n) = X(n) + jY(n)$ is the **ideal (true) reference phasor**
- The numerator $\vert \mathbf{X}_{r}(n) - \mathbf{X}(n) \vert$ is the **vector magnitude difference** between measured and true phasors

The percentage form (commonly used in standards documents) is:

$$\text{TVE}\% = \frac{\vert \mathbf{X}_{r}(n) - \mathbf{X}(n) \vert}{\vert \mathbf{X}(n) \vert} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2.2 Expanded Scalar Form

$$\text{TVE}(n) = \sqrt{\frac{(X_{r}(n) - X(n))^{2} + (X_{i}(n) - Y(n))^{2}}{(X(n))^{2} + (Y(n))^{2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TVE Threshold (Maximum Allowable Error) under IEEE C37.118.1 / IEEE 60255-118-1:**
> - **1% TVE** under nominal steady-state conditions
> - **3% TVE** under specific off-nominal frequency and dynamic conditions
> This is the **universally cited benchmark** in KTU exam answers.

---

## 2.3 Frequency Error (FE) and Rate of Change of Frequency Error (RFE)

### 2.3.1 Frequency Error (FE)

$$\text{FE}(n) = \vert f_{r}(n) - f(n) \vert$$

Where:
- $f_{r}(n)$ = frequency reported by PMU at sample $n$
- $f(n)$ = true frequency (from input signal)

### 2.3.2 Rate of Change of Frequency Error (RFE)

$$\text{RFE}(n) = \vert \text{ROCOF}_{r}(n) - \text{ROCOF}(n) \vert$$

Where ROCOF is the time derivative of frequency:

$$\text{ROCOF}(t) = \frac{df(t)}{dt}$$

---

## 2.4 Performance Classes Defined by Standards

The standards explicitly define two distinct performance classes:

### 2.4.1 Class P — "Protection Class"

- Designed for **fast-response applications** (relaying, breaker control).
- **Minimal measurement delay** (low latency).
- **Limited out-of-band rejection** of interfering frequencies.
- **Maximum TVE = 1%** under steady state, **3%** under dynamic conditions.
- Reporting rate up to **120 frames per second** (FPS) in 50 Hz systems, **120/144 FPS** in 60 Hz systems.

### 2.4.2 Class M — "Measurement Class"

- Designed for **high-accuracy, wide-bandwidth analytical applications** (state estimation, oscillation monitoring, post-event analysis).
- **Higher latency** is tolerated for better filtering accuracy.
- **Superior out-of-band interference rejection**.
- **Stricter TVE = 1%** with broader bandwidth requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about the difference between **Class P** and **Class M**, always mention:
> - **Latency** (P < M)
> - **Anti-aliasing filter roll-off** (M has steeper roll-off)
> - **Use-case** (P for protection, M for measurement/visualization)

---

## 2.5 Standard Reporting Rates

The IEEE standards specify a discrete set of permitted **reporting rates** to ensure deterministic, predictable communication traffic.

### For 50 Hz Systems (Used in India / Europe):

| Reporting Rate (FPS) | Application |
|---------------------|-------------|
| **10** | Slow-speed monitoring |
| **25** | Standard steady-state |
| **50** | Nominal sync with fundamental frequency |
| **100** | Fast dynamic monitoring |

### For 60 Hz Systems (Used in USA / parts of Japan):

| Reporting Rate (FPS) | Application |
|---------------------|-------------|
| 10 | Slow-speed |
| 15 | Standard |
| 30 | Nominal sync |
| 60 | Fast dynamic |
| 120 | High-speed capture |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Standard Equation | Allowed Limit |
|--------|------------|-------------------|---------------|
| $\mathbf{X}$ | True complex phasor | $\mathbf{X} = X + jY$ | — |
| $\mathbf{X}_{r}$ | Reported complex phasor | $\mathbf{X}_{r} = X_{r} + jX_{i}$ | — |
| $\text{TVE}$ | Total Vector Error | $\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{X}_{r} - \mathbf{X} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{X} \vert}$ | $\leq 1\%$ (steady), $\leq 3\%$ (dynamic) |
| $\text{FE}$ | Frequency Error | $\vert f_{r} - f \vert$ | $\leq 5\,\text{mHz}$ (Class M, steady) |
| $\text{RFE}$ | ROCOF Error | $\vert \text{ROCOF}_{r} - \text{ROCOF} \vert$ | $\leq 0.1\,\text{Hz/s}$ (steady) |
| $f_{0}$ | Nominal frequency | $50\,\text{Hz}$ (India) | $\pm 2\,\text{Hz}$ range |
| $V_{0}$ | Nominal voltage amplitude | $100\%$ of rated | $10\%$ to $120\%$ range |
| Reporting rate | Frames per second | $F_{s}$ | $10, 25, 50, 100$ FPS (50 Hz) |
| Time sync source | UTC reference | GPS / IRNSS / NavIC | $<1\,\mu\text{s}$ accuracy |

---

## 2.7 Practical Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-world Indian grid deployments, **POWERGRID (PGCIL)** and state utilities like **KSEB** operate one of the largest WAMS (Wide-Area Monitoring Systems) using PMUs compliant with **IEEE C37.118.1/2 and now IEEE 60255-118-1/2**. The data from these PMUs feeds into:
> - **Real-time security assessment**
> - **Oscillation detection (e.g., 0.1–0.7 Hz inter-area modes)**
> - **Adaptive islanding schemes**
> - **Renewable integration and frequency response monitoring**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Vector Error (TVE) — Geometric Origin

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

Let us derive TVE from first principles so KTU students can reproduce this in the examination hall.

Consider an ideal sinusoidal voltage waveform:
$$v(t) = V_{m} \cos(\omega_{0} t + \phi)$$

This can be expressed as a complex phasor using the Euler representation:
$$v(t) = \text{Re}\left[ V_{m} e^{j(\omega_{0} t + \phi)} \right] = \text{Re}\left[ \mathbf{X} \cdot e^{j\omega_{0} t} \right]$$

The complex phasor (a time-invariant representation at the reference instant) is:
$$\mathbf{X} = \frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi} = \frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} (\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$$

In rectangular form:
$$\mathbf{X} = X + jY$$

Where:
- $X = \dfrac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \cos\phi$ is the real component
- $Y = \dfrac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \sin\phi$ is the imaginary component

### 3.1.2 Measured Phasor Definition

A PMU estimates this phasor using sampled data. Due to instrumentation noise, filter delays, and synchronization errors, it produces a **reported (measured) phasor**:
$$\mathbf{X}_{r} = X_{r} + jX_{i}$$

### 3.1.3 Geometric Error Vector

The vector difference between reported and true phasor is:
$$\Delta \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}_{r} - \mathbf{X} = (X_{r} - X) + j(X_{i} - Y)$$

The **scalar magnitude** of this error vector (Euclidean norm) is:
$$\vert \Delta \mathbf{X} \vert = \sqrt{(X_{r} - X)^{2} + (X_{i} - Y)^{2}}$$

### 3.1.4 Normalization by True Phasor Magnitude

To make the error dimensionless (and scale-invariant), we normalize by the magnitude of the true phasor:
$$\vert \mathbf{X} \vert = \sqrt{X^{2} + Y^{2}}$$

Therefore:
$$\text{TVE} = \frac{\sqrt{(X_{r} - X)^{2} + (X_{i} - Y)^{2}}}{\sqrt{X^{2} + Y^{2}}}$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A PMU reports a voltage phasor $\mathbf{X}_{r} = 71.0 + j0.5$ (kV, RMS), while the true reference phasor is $\mathbf{X} = 70.0 + j0.0$. Calculate the TVE in percentage.

**Step 1 — Compute error components:**

$$X_{r} - X = 71.0 - 70.0 = 1.0$$
$$X_{i} - Y = 0.5 - 0.0 = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute squared error magnitudes:**

$$(X_{r} - X)^{2} = (1.0)^{2} = 1.00$$
$$(X_{i} - Y)^{2} = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Sum and take square root (numerator):**

$$\sqrt{1.00 + 0.25} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.1180$$

**Step 4 — Compute true phasor magnitude (denominator):**

$$\sqrt{(70.0)^{2} + (0.0)^{2}} = 70.0$$

**Step 5 — Compute TVE ratio:**

$$\text{TVE} = \frac{1.1180}{70.0} = 0.01597$$

**Step 6 — Convert to percentage:**

$$\text{TVE}\% = 0.01597 \times 100\% = 1.597\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since TVE = 1.597% exceeds the 1% steady-state limit, this PMU **fails** the Class M standard at this instant. It is, however, still within the 3% dynamic-condition limit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Frequency Measurement from Phasor Phase

### 3.2.1 Phase as a Function of Time

The phase angle of a sinusoidal signal with frequency deviation $\Delta f$ from nominal $f_{0}$ evolves linearly with time:
$$\phi(t) = 2\pi \Delta f \cdot t + \phi_{0}$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Differentiation

A PMU estimates the frequency deviation by computing the **change in phase** between two consecutive measurement windows separated by $\Delta T$:
$$\Delta f = \frac{\phi(t + \Delta T) - \phi(t)}{2\pi \cdot \Delta T}$$

Therefore the absolute frequency estimate is:
$$f_{\text{measured}} = f_{0} + \Delta f = f_{0} + \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta T}$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example — Frequency Error

**Problem:** A 50 Hz system. PMU samples at 100 FPS (every $\Delta T = 10\,\text{ms}$). Phase changes from $\phi_{1} = 5.0^{\circ}$ to $\phi_{2} = 7.0^{\circ}$ in one interval. Compute measured frequency and FE.

**Step 1 — Phase change:**

$$\Delta \phi = 7.0^{\circ} - 5.0^{\circ} = 2.0^{\circ} = 2.0 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.03491\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 2 — Apply frequency formula:**

$$f_{m} = 50 + \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{0.03491}{0.010}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$f_{m} = 50 + \frac{0.03491}{0.06283} = 50 + 0.5556 = 50.5556\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Frequency Error:**

If true frequency is 50.5 Hz:

$$\text{FE} = \vert 50.5556 - 50.5000 \vert = 0.0556\,\text{Hz} = 55.6\,\text{mHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This **violates** the Class M steady-state FE limit of **5 mHz**. The PMU would fail compliance testing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PMU Standard Compliance Tests

The IEEE 60255-118-1 standard specifies a rigorous compliance test matrix:

| Test Type | Parameter Varied | Steady-State Limit (TVE / FE / RFE) | Dynamic Limit |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------------------|---------------|
| **Signal magnitude** | Voltage 10%–120% of $V_{0}$ | TVE $\leq 1\%$ | TVE $\leq 3\%$ |
| **Frequency range** | 45 Hz to 55 Hz | TVE $\leq 1\%$, FE $\leq 5\,\text{mHz}$ | TVE $\leq 3\%$ |
| **Harmonic distortion** | Single harmonic up to 50th | TVE $\leq 1\%$ | TVE $\leq 3\%$ |
| **Interharmonic** | Band-limited noise | TVE $\leq 1\%$ | — |
| **Modulation (PMU Class M)** | Phase modulation at $f_{m} = 0.1$ to $5\,\text{Hz}$ | — | TVE $\leq 3\%$ |
| **Frequency ramp** | ROCOF $\leq 1\,\text{Hz/s}$ | — | TVE $\leq 3\%$, RFE $\leq 0.1\,\text{Hz/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Hook:** When asked "List three compliance tests defined by IEEE 60255-118-1," a complete answer must include: **(i) Steady-state magnitude test, (ii) Frequency deviation test, (iii) Harmonic/interharmonic distortion test, (iv) Dynamic modulation/ramp test.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PMU Standards Compliance Workflow (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GPS / IRNSS NavIC Time Source] --> B[PMU Clock Synchronization Module]
    B --> C[Analog Input Conditioning CT and PT]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[ADC 16-bit or higher]
    E --> F[Phasor Estimation DFT or Kalman Filter]
    F --> G[Frequency and ROCOF Computation]
    G --> H[Compliance Check against IEEE 60255-118-1 TVE FE RFE limits]
    H --> I[Data Frame Builder per IEEE 60255-118-2]
    I --> J[Phasor Data Concentrator PDC]
    J --> K[Wide Area Monitoring System WAMS]
    K --> L[Energy Management System EMS and Control Center]
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style H fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style J fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each colored block represents a standardized processing stage. The **pink compliance-check block (H)** is the heart of IEEE 60255-118-1, where the calculated TVE, FE, and RFE are verified against threshold limits. If exceeded, the data frame is **flagged** (not discarded — to preserve observability of degraded conditions).

---

## 4.2 PMU Data Frame Structure (IEEE C37.118.2 / IEEE 60255-118-2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SYNC 2 bytes AA 01] --> B[FRAMESIZE 2 bytes]
    B --> C[IDCODE 2 bytes PMU ID]
    C --> D[SOC 4 bytes Seconds of Century UTC]
    D --> E[FRACSEC 4 bytes Fraction of Second]
    E --> F[STAT 2 bytes Bit-Mapped Status Flags]
    F --> G[PHASORS 4N bytes Real Imaginary]
    G --> H[FREQ 4 bytes]
    H --> I[DFREQ 4 bytes ROCOF]
    I --> J[ANALOG 2M bytes Optional]
    J --> K[DIGITAL Nbytes Optional]
    K --> L[CHK 2 bytes CRC-CCITT]
    
    style A fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style G fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style L fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoding the Frame:**
> - **SYNC byte** indicates frame type (data, header, configuration, command).
> - **SOC + FRACSEC** together form the **UTC timestamp** with microsecond resolution — critical for cross-substation synchronization.
> - **STAT** flags include synchronization status (lock/unlock), PMU health, trigger detection.
> - **CHK** uses **CRC-CCITT** for error detection.

---

## 4.3 PMU Class Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Requirement] --> B{Real-time Protection Relay Control?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Choose Class P]
    B -->|No| D{Wide-Area Monitoring State Estimation?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Choose Class M]
    D -->|No| F{Post-Event Analysis Oscillation Detection?}
    F -->|Yes| E
    F -->|No| G[Re-examine application]
    
    C --> H[Accept higher latency tolerance No]
    E --> H2[Accept higher latency Yes]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c

```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]
**Define Total Vector Error (TVE) as per IEEE C37.118.1 standard and state its maximum allowable limit under steady-state and dynamic conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Total Vector Error (TVE) is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the vector difference between the measured phasor $\mathbf{X}_{r}$ and the true reference phasor $\mathbf{X}$ to the magnitude of the true phasor:

$$\text{TVE} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{X}_{r} - \mathbf{X} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{X} \vert}$$

According to IEEE C37.118.1 / IEEE 60255-118-1:
- **Steady-state maximum allowable TVE = 1%**
- **Dynamic-condition maximum allowable TVE = 3%**

TVE captures both magnitude and phase errors simultaneously and is the **primary compliance metric** for all PMUs.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]
**List any three key requirements specified by the IEEE C37.118.2 / IEEE 60255-118-2 standard for PMU communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The IEEE C37.118.2 / IEEE 60255-118-2 standard specifies:

1. **Structured data frame format** consisting of SYNC, FRAMESIZE, IDCODE, SOC, FRACSEC, STAT, PHASORS, FREQ, DFREQ, and CHK fields.
2. **Deterministic reporting rates** — 10, 25, 50, 100 FPS for 50 Hz systems.
3. **Use of UDP/IP or TCP/IP communication protocols** with optional TLS/SSH secure tunneling for cybersecurity.
4. **CRC-CCITT checksum** for end-to-end data integrity.
5. **Configuration frames (CFG-1, CFG-2, CFG-3)** to convey PMU metadata (channel names, units, scaling factors) to the PDC.

*(Any three well-explained points = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and measurement methodology of a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) as defined by the **IEEE C37.118.1 / IEEE 60255-118-1 standard**. Discuss the role of GPS/IRNSS synchronization in achieving wide-area coherence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 Hz nominal PMU reports the following measured voltage phasor at a 230 kV bus: $\mathbf{X}_{r} = 161.5 + j2.0$ (kV, RMS). The true reference phasor is $\mathbf{X} = 162.0 + j0.0$ (kV, RMS). Calculate the Total Vector Error (TVE) in percentage and determine whether the PMU satisfies the Class M steady-state compliance criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — PMU Architecture & Methodology (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A PMU is a synchronized digital device that measures **voltage and current phasors**, **frequency**, and **Rate of Change of Frequency (ROCOF)** at a sampling rate tied to a common UTC time reference, conforming to the **IEEE C37.118.1 / IEEE 60255-118-1 standard**.

**Step 2 — Functional Architecture (3 marks):**

1. **Analog Front-End (CTs and PTs):** Step down high-voltage/current to instrument-level signals (typically ±10 V or ±1 A).
2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** Band-limits the input to avoid aliasing during sampling (cutoff frequency ≈ half the sampling rate).
3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Samples the conditioned signals at a rate synchronous to the GPS pulse (typically 16-bit resolution or higher).
4. **Phasor Estimation Algorithm:** Applies **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or **Kalman filter** to extract the fundamental-frequency phasor from sampled data.
5. **Frequency and ROCOF Computation:** Computes from the rate of change of phasor phase over consecutive measurement windows.
6. **GPS/IRNSS Time Tagging:** Assigns a **UTC timestamp** (SOC + FRACSEC) to every measurement frame.

**Step 3 — Role of GPS/IRNSS Synchronization (2 marks):**

- All PMUs receive a **1-PPS (one-pulse-per-second)** signal from GPS satellites (or India's **IRNSS / NavIC** for domestic resilience).
- This common reference ensures that phasor angles measured at geographically distant substations (e.g., **Kozhikode and Kasaragod**) are referenced to the **same instant of UTC time**.
- Synchronization accuracy must be **better than 1 μs** to keep phase errors within ±0.018° (0.1 μs ≈ 0.0018° at 50 Hz) — well below the 1% TVE budget.
- This enables **wide-area situational awareness**, **inter-area oscillation detection**, and **real-time stability monitoring**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The IEEE C37.118.1 / IEEE 60255-118-1 standard ensures that PMUs from different manufacturers produce **interoperable, high-accuracy, time-coherent** measurements that form the data backbone of modern WAMS.

---

#### Part (b) — TVE Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify measured and true phasors (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{X}_{r} = 161.5 + j2.0$$
$$\mathbf{X} = 162.0 + j0.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute error components (1 mark):**

$$X_{r} - X = 161.5 - 162.0 = -0.5$$
$$X_{i} - Y = 2.0 - 0.0 = 2.0$$

**Step 3 — Square the error components (1 mark):**

$$(X_{r} - X)^{2} = (-0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$
$$(X_{i} - Y)^{2} = (2.0)^{2} = 4.00$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator (1 mark):**

$$\vert \mathbf{X}_{r} - \mathbf{X} \vert = \sqrt{0.25 + 4.00} = \sqrt{4.25} = 2.0616\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator (true phasor magnitude) (1 mark):**

$$\vert \mathbf{X} \vert = \sqrt{(162.0)^{2} + (0.0)^{2}} = 162.0\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 6 — Compute TVE ratio and percentage (1 mark):**

$$\text{TVE} = \frac{2.0616}{162.0} = 0.01273$$
$$\text{TVE}\% = 1.273\%$$

**Step 7 — Compliance Assessment (1 mark):**

> Since TVE = 1.273% **exceeds the 1% steady-state limit** but remains **below the 3% dynamic-condition limit**, this PMU **fails the Class M steady-state compliance test** but would pass under dynamic-condition acceptance criteria.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **PMU data frame structure** as defined by **IEEE C37.118.2 / IEEE 60255-118-2**. Differentiate between **Data Frame**, **Configuration Frame**, and **Header Frame**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Class P (Protection)** and **Class M (Measurement)** performance classes as defined by the IEEE PMU standards. Justify which class is preferred for **(i)** distance relay applications and **(ii)** post-event oscillation analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Data Frame Structure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Frame Type Overview (1 mark):**
IEEE C37.118.2 defines **four frame types**, identified by the SYNC byte:
- **Data Frame** (SYNC = 0xAA01) — Carries real-time phasor measurements.
- **Header Frame** (SYNC = 0xAA02) — ASCII descriptive text (operator, location, calibration data).
- **Configuration Frame 1** (SYNC = 0xAA31) — Fundamental phasor measurement only.
- **Configuration Frame 2** (SYNC = 0xAA32) — Includes phasors + frequency + ROCOF + analog + digital.

**Step 2 — Data Frame Detailed Structure (4 marks):**

| Field | Size (Bytes) | Function |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| SYNC | 2 | Frame type identifier |
| FRAMESIZE | 2 | Total frame length in bytes |
| IDCODE | 2 | PMU unique identification (0–65534) |
| SOC | 4 | Seconds since UTC midnight, Jan 1, 1970 |
| FRACSEC | 4 | Fraction of second (microseconds resolution) |
| STAT | 2 | Bit-mapped flags (sync, trigger, PMU health) |
| PHASORS | 4N | Real + Imaginary for N phasor channels |
| FREQ | 4 | Measured frequency (Hz) |
| DFREQ | 4 | Measured ROCOF (Hz/s) |
| ANALOG | 2M | Optional analog measurements |
| DIGITAL | Nbytes | Optional digital status words |
| CHK | 2 | CRC-CCITT checksum |

**Step 3 — Configuration vs Header Frame (2 marks):**

- **Configuration Frame** tells the PDC the **numerical characteristics** of the PMU: number of phasors, scaling factors, units, nominal frequency, reporting rate.
- **Header Frame** carries **human-readable text** describing the PMU site, e.g., `"KSEB Substation, Kozhikode, 230 kV Bus-2"`. It does not affect measurement integrity.

---

#### Part (b) — Class P vs Class M Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Tabulated Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Class P (Protection) | Class M (Measurement) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Primary use | Real-time protection, relaying | Post-event analysis, monitoring |
| Latency | Low (fast response) | Higher (better filtering) |
| TVE limit (steady) | 1% | 1% |
| TVE limit (dynamic) | 3% | 3% |
| Out-of-band rejection | Relaxed | Strict (steep filter roll-off) |
| Reporting rate support | Up to 120 FPS (60 Hz) / 100 FPS (50 Hz) | Same |
| Anti-aliasing filter | Wider bandwidth | Narrower, steeper roll-off |
| Data window | Short | Longer for accuracy |
| Frequency error limit | 5 mHz (steady) | 5 mHz (steady), tighter dynamically |

**Step 2 — Application Justification (3 marks):**

**(i) Distance Relay Applications — Prefer Class P:**
Distance relays require **fast, decisive action** within 1–2 cycles (20–40 ms). Class P offers **lower latency** and sufficient accuracy to detect impedance trajectories during faults. The wider filter bandwidth is acceptable because the protective action is event-driven, not steady-state-dependent.

**(ii) Post-Event Oscillation Analysis — Prefer Class M:**
Oscillation modes in large inter-connected grids (e.g., India-South region, 0.2–0.8 Hz modes) require **high-fidelity, low-noise** phasor data over extended time windows. Class M's **steeper filter roll-off** rejects harmonics and interharmonics that would otherwise mask low-frequency electromechanical oscillations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in TVE / FE / RFE answers (always specify % or Hz or Hz/s).
> 2. **Misstating reporting rates** — confusing 50 Hz and 60 Hz system rates.
> 3. **Confusing Class P vs Class M** — many students swap their applications.
> 4. **Not mentioning GPS/IRNSS** when discussing time synchronization — this is a mandatory KTU keyword.
> 5. **Skipping the compliance verdict** in numerical TVE problems — always conclude with "passes/fails Class M steady-state" explicitly.
> 6. **Ignoring the IEEE 60255-118-1 vs IEEE C37.118.1 timeline** — older answers reference only C37.118 and lose modernization marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2: Standards for PMU):**

- ⭐ **Primary standards:** IEEE C37.118.1 (measurement), IEEE C37.118.2 (communication), IEEE 60255-118-1/2 (current unified standard).
- ⭐ **TVE formula:** $\text{TVE} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{X}_{r} - \mathbf{X} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{X} \vert} \times 100\%$.
- ⭐ **TVE limits:** **1% (steady-state)**, **3% (dynamic)** for both Class P and Class M.
- ⭐ **Two performance classes:** Class P (Protection, low latency), Class M (Measurement, high accuracy, steep filter).
- ⭐ **Reporting rates for 50 Hz (India):** **10, 25, 50, 100 FPS** — mandatory discrete set.
- ⭐ **Frequency error (FE) limit:** 5 mHz steady-state, 0.01 Hz dynamic (Class M).
- ⭐ **ROCOF error (RFE) limit:** 0.1 Hz/s steady, 1 Hz/s dynamic.
- ⭐ **Time sync source:** GPS (USA) or **IRNSS / NavIC (India)** with **< 1 μs** accuracy; 1 PPS pulse.
- ⭐ **Phasor formula:** $\mathbf{X} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} (\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$.
- ⭐ **Data frame fields:** SYNC, FRAMESIZE, IDCODE, SOC, FRACSEC, STAT, PHASORS, FREQ, DFREQ, CHK.
- ⭐ **Frame types:** Data (0xAA01), Header (0xAA02), CFG-1 (0xAA31), CFG-2 (0xAA32).
- ⭐ **Indian deployment context:** PGCIL operates one of the largest WAMS installations with IEEE-compliant PMUs; Kerala's KSEB participates through regional load dispatch centres.
- ⭐ **PDC standard:** IEEE C37.244 – 2013 governs Phasor Data Concentrator operation.
- ⭐ **Key compliance tests:** Magnitude sweep, frequency range, harmonic distortion, interharmonic, modulation, frequency ramp.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line exam-ready mnemonic — "**TVE-D-R-P-M**":**
> **T**otal **V**ector **E**rror ≤ 1% | **D**ata frame per 118.2 | **R**eporting rate discrete | **P**rotection vs **M**easurement classes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Synchronization Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time Synchronization Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Synchronization** in the context of smart grids refers to the process of aligning the internal clocks of distributed digital devices — such as **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)**, **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)**, **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)**, protection relays, and smart meters — to a single, globally traceable time reference (typically **Coordinated Universal Time – UTC**) with a stipulated accuracy, usually on the order of **microseconds (μs)** to **nanoseconds (ns)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**
> "Time synchronization is the mechanism of providing a common time base across geographically dispersed measurement, control, and communication devices in the smart grid using protocols such as GPS, IEEE 1588 (PTP), NTP, IRIG-B, and IEC 61850 to enable accurate event correlation, sequence-of-events recording, and synchrophasor measurement."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **orchestra of 1,000 musicians** scattered across a 500 km radius, each in a different city. The conductor is in Delhi. For the symphony to sound like one piece of music, every musician must play their note at **exactly the same instant** — not 0.1 seconds early or late. To achieve this, the conductor broadcasts a reference "click" via satellite (the **GPS time pulse**), and each musician's instrument automatically adjusts its internal tempo to match it.

The smart grid is that orchestra:
- The **musicians** = PMUs, IEDs, meters, relays
- The **conductor's click** = UTC time reference from GPS satellites
- The **tempo adjustment** = time synchronization protocol (PTP, IRIG-B, NTP)
- The **symphony** = a coherent, real-time picture of the entire power system

Without synchronization, a fault that occurred in Kasaragod at 14:32:00.000000 might be recorded by the Kasaragod PMU at 14:32:00.000100 and the Kannur PMU at 14:32:00.000250 — making it impossible to determine the actual sequence of events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024 Smart Grid context:**
> - **Synchrophasor measurement** demands a phase-angle error < **0.01°** for 50 Hz, which translates to a timing error of < **±1 μs**.
> - **Sequence-of-Events (SoE) recorders** need a resolution of **±1 ms**.
> - **Wide-Area Monitoring Systems (WAMS)** become meaningless without tight timing.

### 1.3 Standard Time References Used

| Time Source | Typical Accuracy | Coverage | KTU Relevance |
|-------------|------------------|----------|---------------|
| **GPS (USA)** | ±100 ns to UTC | Global | **Primary** for PMU applications |
| **GLONASS (Russia)** | ±100 ns to UTC | Global | Backup / redundancy |
| **BeiDou (China)** | ±100 ns to UTC | Global | Increasingly used in India |
| **IRIG-B (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group)** | ±1–10 μs | Local distribution | Substation clock distribution |
| **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** | ±1–10 ms | LAN/WAN | Low-precision applications |
| **PTP (IEEE 1588 Precision Time Protocol)** | **±1 μs or better** | LAN | Modern substation automation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant to Remember:**
> For a **50 Hz** power system, **1 μs timing error** → **0.018°** phase error.
> For a **60 Hz** power system, **1 μs timing error** → **0.0216°** phase error.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Smart Grids Need Precise Time Synchronization

The smart grid is a **cyber-physical system** that merges high-voltage power infrastructure with digital sensing, communication, and control. Time synchronization underpins four critical applications:

1. **Synchrophasor Measurement (PMU)** — Real-time angle and magnitude reporting requires synchronized sampling across the grid.
2. **Event Correlation & Fault Analysis** — A disturbance at one location must be time-stamped identically at all substations for post-event reconstruction.
3. **Sequence-of-Events (SoE) Recording** — Protection-grade event logging.
4. **Demand Response & Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariffs** — Billing accuracy at sub-minute granularity.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Time Distribution Architecture

The standard KTU/IEEE-recommended architecture follows a **three-tier cascade**:

- **Tier 1 (Master Clock):** GPS-disciplined oscillator (GPSDO) or atomic clock provides the UTC reference.
- **Tier 2 (Substation Grandmaster):** IEEE 1588 Grandmaster Clock distributes time over the substation LAN.
- **Tier 3 (Slave/Ordinary Clock):** IEDs, PMUs, meters with PTP slave ports that discipline their internal oscillators.

### 2.3 IEEE 1588 Precision Time Protocol (PTP) — Deep Analysis

PTP (also called **Precision Time Protocol**) is the dominant substation time-distribution protocol under **IEC 61850**. It achieves **sub-microsecond** accuracy by using hardware timestamping at the physical layer (PHY).

#### 2.3.1 PTP Message Exchange Mechanism

The protocol works via four timestamps exchanged between **Master** and **Slave**:

- $t_1$ = Master sends **Sync** message
- $t_2$ = Slave receives **Sync**
- $t_3$ = Slave sends **Delay_Req**
- $t_4$ = Master receives **Delay_Req**

#### 2.3.2 Key Derived Quantities

The two fundamental quantities PTP computes are:

**Round-Trip Delay (mean link delay):**

$$
D = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2}
$$

**Offset of Slave Clock relative to Master:**

$$
O = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The PTP algorithm **assumes symmetric path delay** in both directions. Asymmetric links (e.g., different fiber lengths in upstream/downstream) introduce systematic error.

### 2.4 GPS-Based Time Synchronization

GPS satellites carry **atomic clocks** (Rubidium/Cesium) and broadcast a one-pulse-per-second (**1-PPS**) signal. A GPS receiver at a substation:

1. Tracks at least **4 satellites** in view.
2. Computes pseudoranges and corrects for **ionospheric** and **tropospheric** delays.
3. Disciplines a local **OCXO (Oven-Controlled Crystal Oscillator)** or **TCXO** via a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)**.
4. Outputs a **1-PPS** signal aligned to UTC(USNO) within **±100 ns**.

The 1-PPS is then conditioned and distributed via:
- **IRIG-B** (interleaved amplitude-modulated time code, B000 series for 1 kHz carrier)
- **PTP** (over Ethernet)
- **Direct TTL pulse** wiring

### 2.5 IRIG-B Time Code

**IRIG-B** is a serial time code standardized by the **Inter-Range Instrumentation Group**. It transmits:
- **100 bits per second** on a **1 kHz** carrier
- Contains **day-of-year, hour, minute, second**, plus control bits for synchronization
- Two variants: **IRIG-B000** (DC-level shift) and **IRIG-B120** (1 kHz AM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU High-Yield Point: **IRIG-B** is the most common substation time distribution in India for legacy and PGCIL deployments, and is explicitly supported under **IEC 61850-9-2 (Sampled Values)** for substation busbar synchronization.

### 2.6 NTP vs PTP — Engineering Trade-off

| Parameter | NTP (RFC 5905) | PTP (IEEE 1588) |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Typical accuracy | **1–10 ms** | **<1 μs** |
| Hardware timestamping | No (software) | Yes (PHY/MAC) |
| Network | Internet/LAN | LAN (substation) |
| Application | Billing, ToU | PMU, protection, SAS |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | PTP Round-trip delay | $D = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2}$ | seconds | PTP slave computation |
| 2 | PTP Slave offset | $O = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}$ | seconds | Clock correction |
| 3 | Phase error from timing error | $\theta_{\text{err}} = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$ | radians | PMU angle validation |
| 4 | Phase error for 50 Hz | $\theta_{\text{err}}(\deg) = 0.018 \cdot \Delta t(\mu s)$ | degrees | Quick 50 Hz calculation |
| 5 | Allan Deviation (clock stability) | $\sigma_y(\tau)$ | dimensionless | Oscillator characterization |
| 6 | GPS position dilution | $\text{PDOP} \geq 1$ | scalar | Receiver geometry quality |
| 7 | IEC 61850-9-2 timestamp | 64-bit + 32-bit time of day | ns + days | Sampled Values |
| 8 | 1-PPS jitter requirement | < 50 ns (PMU) | ns | GPSDO output spec |
| 9 | C37.118.1 PMU sync tolerance | $\pm 31.25\,\mu s$ (typical $1\,\mu s$ desired) | μs | Synchrophasor standard |
| 10 | Holdover drift (OCXO) | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-10}$ per day | fractional | GPS loss scenario |

> [!NOTE]
> The C37.118.1-2011 (synchrophasor) standard mandates a **TVE (Total Vector Error) of 1 %**, of which timing contributes a major share.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Time synchronization is the **hidden enabler** of:
- **WAMS** (Wide-Area Monitoring Systems) deployed by **POWERGRID** in India
- **Substation Automation Systems (SAS)** built on **IEC 61850**
- **Synchrophasor-based adaptive protection**
- **Disturbance recording** and **post-mortem analysis**
- **Cybersecurity event correlation** in SCADA/EMS systems

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PTP Offset and Delay (IEEE 1588)

We derive the slave offset $O$ and the mean link delay $D$ from the four observed timestamps $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$.

**Step 1 — State the true propagation model.**

Let the **master-to-slave propagation time** be $d_{ms}$ and the **slave-to-master propagation time** be $d_{sm}$. Assume symmetric link so $d_{ms} = d_{sm} = d$.

The **slave clock offset** relative to the master is $O$ (positive means slave is ahead).

$$
t_2 = t_1 + d_{ms} + O
$$

$$
t_4 = t_3 + d_{sm} - O
$$

**Step 2 — Express the two observed differences.**

The master-to-slave measured delay is:

$$
t_2 - t_1 = d_{ms} + O
$$

The slave-to-master measured delay is:

$$
t_4 - t_3 = d_{sm} - O
$$

**Step 3 — Add the two equations.**

$$
(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3) = d_{ms} + d_{sm} + O - O
$$

With $d_{ms} = d_{sm} = d$:

$$
(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3) = 2d
$$

Therefore, the mean one-way delay is:

$$
D = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2} = d
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two equations.**

$$
(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3) = d_{ms} - d_{sm} + 2O
$$

With symmetric path:

$$
(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3) = 2O
$$

Therefore:

$$
O = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}
$$

**Final Result (boxed for exam):**

$$
\boxed{\,D = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2} \quad\text{and}\quad O = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Phase Error Induced by Timing Error

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark setup):** A PMU operating at 50 Hz has a timing error of $\Delta t$ seconds. Derive the resulting phase error.

**Step 1 — Write the phasor definition.**

A sinusoidal voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ has instantaneous phase:

$$
\phi(t) = \omega t + \phi_0
$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$.

**Step 2 — Introduce the timing error.**

If the sampling instant is displaced by $\Delta t$ (positive means late), the measured phase becomes:

$$
\phi_{\text{measured}} = \omega (t - \Delta t) + \phi_0 = \omega t - \omega \Delta t + \phi_0
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the true phase.**

$$
\phi_{\text{err}} = \phi_{\text{measured}} - \phi_{\text{true}} = -\omega \Delta t
$$

The magnitude of phase error is:

$$
\vert \phi_{\text{err}} \vert = \omega \cdot \vert \Delta t \vert = 2\pi f \cdot \vert \Delta t \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees.**

$$
\phi_{\text{err}} (^{\circ}) = \frac{360 \cdot f \cdot \Delta t}{1} = 360 \cdot f \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 5 — Plug in 50 Hz and 1 μs.**

$$
\phi_{\text{err}} = 360 \cdot 50 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.018^{\circ}
$$

**Step 6 — State the IEEE C37.118 limit.**

The PMU total vector error budget is 1 %. A timing error of $\pm 1\,\mu s$ is the typical design target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical example (typical exam):**
> If a PMU has a 5 μs timing error at 50 Hz, $\phi_{\text{err}} = 0.018 \times 5 = 0.09^{\circ}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: GPS Pseudorange and Time Error

A GPS receiver estimates its position and clock bias $b$ (in seconds) from pseudoranges $\rho_i$ to at least four satellites. The pseudorange equation is:

$$
\rho_i = (t_{r,i} - t_{s,i}) \cdot c = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot b_r
$$

where $t_{r,i}$ is the receive time, $t_{s,i}$ is the satellite transmit time, $c$ is the speed of light, $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$ is the receiver position, and $b_r$ is the receiver clock bias.

Solving the four-equation system yields the **3-D position and the time bias** simultaneously. The accuracy of the derived time is therefore directly coupled to the geometric dilution of precision (GDOP), particularly the **Time DOP (TDOP)**.

$$
\sigma_t = \text{TDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\rho}
$$

For a well-designed receiver with good satellite geometry, $\sigma_t \leq 100\,\text{ns}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Synchronization Methods

| Method | Accuracy | Hardware Cost | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|--------|----------|---------------|---------------|------------|
| **GPS** | ±100 ns | Medium–High | PMU, master clock | Antenna placement, jamming |
| **PTP (IEEE 1588)** | < 1 μs | Medium | Substation LAN | Requires transparent clocks |
| **NTP** | 1–10 ms | Low | Billing, ToU | Not for protection |
| **IRIG-B** | 1–10 μs | Low–Medium | Legacy substation | Dedicated wiring |
| **GLONASS / BeiDou** | ±100 ns | Medium | GPS redundancy | Receiver availability |
| **Atomic clock (Rubidium)** | < 1 μs/day | Very High | Holdover for master | Expensive |

---

### 3.5 Holdover Behavior — Clock Drift After GPS Loss

When GPS is unavailable, the disciplined oscillator drifts according to its **Allan deviation**. For an OCXO with $\sigma_y = 10^{-10}$:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{holdover}} = \sigma_y \cdot t_{\text{elapsed}}
$$

After 1 hour (3600 s):

$$
\Delta t = 10^{-10} \cdot 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} = 0.36\,\mu s
$$

This is well within the **1 μs PMU requirement** for several hours, but eventually a TCXO ($10^{-8}$) drifts beyond tolerance in minutes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master–Slave PTP Time Exchange (IEEE 1588)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as Grandmaster Clock
    participant Slave as Slave Clock (PMU/IED)
    Master->>Slave: Sync Message at t1
    Note over Slave: Hardware timestamp t2
    Master->>Slave: Follow_Up with t1
    Slave->>Master: Delay_Req at t3
    Note over Master: Hardware timestamp t4
    Master->>Slave: Delay_Resp with t4
    Note over Slave: Compute Delay D and Offset O
    Slave->>Slave: Adjust internal oscillator
```

**Caption:** The four-timestamp exchange (t1, t2, t3, t4) enables the slave to compute both the one-way delay and the offset of its local clock relative to the grandmaster.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Time Distribution in a Smart Substation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SAT[SATELLITE CONSTELLATION GPS BEIDOU GLONASS]
    GPS[GPS ANTENNA ON ROOFTOP]
    GPSDO[GPS DISCIPLINED OSCILLATOR GPSDO]
    GM[GRANDMASTER CLOCK PTP IEC 61850]
    TC[TRANSPARENT CLOCK SWITCH 1]
    TC2[TRANSPARENT CLOCK SWITCH 2]
    PMU1[PMU 1 BAY 1]
    PMU2[PMU 2 BAY 2]
    IED1[IED BAY 3 RELAY]
    IED2[IED BAY 4 METER]
    BC[BROWNOVER CLOCK GPS FAIL]
    IRIG[IRIG B DISTRIBUTION LEGACY]

    SAT --> GPS
    GPS --> GPSDO
    GPSDO --> GM
    GPSDO --> IRIG
    GM --> TC
    TC --> TC2
    TC --> PMU1
    TC --> PMU2
    TC2 --> IED1
    TC2 --> IED2
    GM -. backup .-> BC
    IRIG --> IED1
    IRIG --> IED2
```

**Caption:** A real substation architecture uses a GPSDO as Tier 1, a PTP Grandmaster as Tier 2, transparent-clock-aware switches as Tier 2.5, and the slave IEDs/PMUs as Tier 3. IRIG-B provides legacy distribution in parallel.

---

### 4.3 Time Synchronization Requirement Matrix by Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[APPLICATION DOMAIN] --> B[PMU SYNCHROPHASOR]
    A --> C[PROTECTION RELAYING]
    A --> D[SEQUENCE OF EVENTS]
    A --> E[SMART METERING TOU]
    A --> F[SCADA DATA LOGGING]
    B --> B1[ACCURACY 1 MICROSECOND]
    C --> C1[ACCURACY 1 TO 4 MILLISECONDS]
    D --> D1[ACCURACY 1 MILLISECOND]
    E --> E1[ACCURACY 1 TO 10 SECONDS]
    F --> F1[ACCURACY 10 TO 100 MILLISECONDS]
    B1 --> R1[GPS PLUS PTP IEC 61850 9 2]
    C1 --> R2[IRIG B OR PTP]
    D1 --> R3[IRIG B OR PTP]
    E1 --> R4[NTP SUFFICIENT]
    F1 --> R5[NTP SUFFICIENT]
```

**Caption:** The required accuracy differs by orders of magnitude across smart grid applications. PMU requires the tightest synchronization — and therefore the most sophisticated (and expensive) infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define time synchronization in the context of smart grids. Why is it essential for synchrophasor measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Time synchronization is the process of aligning the clocks of geographically distributed devices (PMUs, IEDs, meters) in a smart grid to a common reference such as UTC, with microsecond-level accuracy.

It is essential for synchrophasor measurement because:
1. PMUs compute the **phasor** (magnitude and phase angle) of voltage and current waveforms by sampling at precisely timed instants.
2. Phase angle is a **relative quantity** — comparison of angles from two PMUs is only meaningful if both sampled at the same instant.
3. Without synchronization, the **0.01°** angular accuracy mandated by **IEEE C37.118.1** cannot be achieved.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Compare NTP and PTP (IEEE 1588) protocols used for time synchronization. State two advantages of PTP over NTP.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | NTP | PTP (IEEE 1588) |
|-----------|-----|------------------|
| Accuracy | 1–10 ms | < 1 μs |
| Timestamping | Software (OS layer) | Hardware (PHY/MAC layer) |
| Use case | Billing, ToU, SCADA | PMU, protection, SAS |

**Two advantages of PTP over NTP:**
1. **Sub-microsecond accuracy** — sufficient for PMU and protection applications.
2. **Hardware timestamping** eliminates OS jitter and queuing delay variability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Path 1: PTP & GPS*

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **one-way delay (D)** and **slave clock offset (O)** in the IEEE 1588 Precision Time Protocol, clearly stating the assumption made. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **GPS-based time synchronization system** for a smart-grid substation, including the role of the GPS-disciplined oscillator (GPSDO). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the four timestamps. $t_1$ = master sends Sync, $t_2$ = slave receives Sync, $t_3$ = slave sends Delay_Req, $t_4$ = master receives Delay_Req.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the two propagation equations assuming symmetric link with delay $d$ and slave offset $O$:

$$
t_2 = t_1 + d + O \quad\text{and}\quad t_4 = t_3 + d - O
$$

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Add and subtract to obtain:

$$
D = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2}, \quad O = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}
$$

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** State the assumption — **symmetric path delay in both directions**.

**(b) Block Diagram & GPSDO Explanation (7 marks):**

The answer must include the following blocks in a flow diagram (5 marks):
- GPS satellite constellation (atomic clocks on board)
- GPS antenna on the substation roof (with lightning protection and surge arrestor)
- GPS receiver (tracks ≥ 4 satellites)
- GPSDO with internal OCXO and PLL
- 1-PPS output and 10 MHz reference distribution
- Distribution to grandmaster clock and IRIG-B output module
- Distribution to PMU and IEDs

**Textual explanation (2 marks):**
- The GPSDO disciplines a high-stability local oscillator (OCXO) by phase-locking it to the GPS 1-PPS reference.
- When GPS is healthy, the GPSDO outputs 1-PPS aligned to UTC within ±100 ns.
- In **holdover** (GPS unavailable), the OCXO drifts at $10^{-10}$ per day, providing continued timing for several hours.
- The 1-PPS is fanned out to the PTP grandmaster and to the IRIG-B generator for legacy equipment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Path 2: IRIG-B, Standards & Phase Error*

**(a)** Explain the **IRIG-B time code** format. List two advantages and two limitations of using IRIG-B in substations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A PMU operating on a 50 Hz system experiences a **timing error of 2.5 μs**. Compute the resulting **phase angle error** in degrees. Discuss why this matters for **wide-area monitoring**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IRIG-B Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Format (3 marks):** IRIG-B is a serial time code with:
  - 100 bits per second
  - 1 kHz amplitude-modulated carrier (B120 variant) or DC pulse-width modulated (B000 variant)
  - Encodes: seconds, minutes, hours, day-of-year, and control functions
  - Frame repeats every 1 second; bit 0 (position identifier) marks the second boundary
- **Two advantages (2 marks):**
  1. Simple, robust, requires only one twisted pair for distribution
  2. Supported by virtually all legacy and modern substation equipment
- **Two limitations (2 marks):**
  1. Dedicated wiring needed; not IP-routable
  2. Accuracy limited to 1–10 μs; insufficient for advanced PMU applications requiring < 1 μs

**(b) Phase Error Calculation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the phase-error formula:

$$
\phi_{\text{err}} = 360 \cdot f \cdot \Delta t
$$

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ and $\Delta t = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}$:

$$
\phi_{\text{err}} = 360 \cdot 50 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute:

$$
\phi_{\text{err}} = 18000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.018 \times 2.5 = 0.045^{\circ}
$$

- **Step 4 (3 marks):** Discussion on WAMS implications:
  - For a 500 km transmission corridor, a 0.045° error in any one PMU is small, but **cumulative errors across 50+ PMUs** corrupt the WAMS state estimator.
  - Wide-area **oscillation damping controllers** (e.g., POD) react to inter-area modes; even 0.05° errors mis-identify the oscillation frequency and phase.
  - WAMS-based **event location** (fault, loss of generation) relies on angle differences between PMUs; uncorrected timing skew of a few microseconds leads to **fault-location errors of several kilometres**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **PTP derivation**: Students often forget to state the **symmetric path assumption** explicitly. Deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **GPSDO**: Writing only "GPS gives accurate time" loses 2 marks. You must mention **PLL, OCXO discipline, holdover**, and **±100 ns** specification.
> 3. **Phase error calculation**: Using $f$ in Hz with $\Delta t$ in μs without unit conversion (or using $\omega = 2\pi f$ and forgetting to convert rad → deg). Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **IRIG-B**: Writing only "B code" without the carrier frequency (1 kHz) and bit rate (100 bps). Deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing NTP with PTP**: Saying NTP is "as accurate as PTP" is a serious conceptual error. Deduct 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 viva & ESE:**

- **Time synchronization** = alignment of distributed grid clocks to a common UTC reference with μs-to-ns accuracy.
- **GPS** is the most widely used primary time source; accuracy ±100 ns to UTC(USNO).
- **GPSDO** = GPS-disciplined oscillator (OCXO + PLL) — primary grandmaster element.
- **IEEE 1588 PTP** uses four timestamps to compute both **delay** and **offset**; assumes **symmetric path**; accuracy **< 1 μs** with hardware timestamping.
- **PTP formulas:** $D = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2}$ and $O = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}$.
- **IRIG-B** is a 1 kHz AM-modulated, 100 bps serial time code widely used in substations; accuracy 1–10 μs.
- **NTP** (RFC 5905) accuracy 1–10 ms — for **billing and ToU**, **not** for protection or PMU.
- **For 50 Hz, 1 μs timing error → 0.018° phase error.** Phase error formula: $\phi_{\text{err}} = 360 \cdot f \cdot \Delta t$.
- **IEEE C37.118.1** is the synchrophasor standard mandating **TVE ≤ 1 %**; this requires PMU timing within **±1 μs** typical.
- **Holdover** = continued operation after GPS loss; OCXO drifts at $10^{-10}$/day, TCXO at $10^{-8}$/day.
- **IEC 61850-9-2** Sampled Values use 64-bit (time-of-day) + 32-bit (seconds-of-epoch) timestamps.
- **Allan deviation** $\sigma_y(\tau)$ quantifies clock frequency stability.
- **Time sources to know:** GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), BeiDou (China), Galileo (EU).
- **Three-tier architecture:** GPSDO → PTP Grandmaster → Slave IEDs/PMUs.
- **Transparent clocks** in PTP switches correct for residence time — critical for cascaded switch chains.
- **Boundary clocks** in PTP slave a downstream segment to reduce grandmaster load.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line summary for last-minute revision:**
> "Smart-grid time synchronization cascades GPS-disciplined UTC time through PTP grandmasters and IRIG-B to PMUs, ensuring sub-microsecond phase-angle accuracy essential for synchrophasor-based wide-area monitoring and protection."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wide Area Monitoring System (WAMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wide Area Monitoring System (WAMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wide Area Monitoring System (WAMS)** is an advanced, time-synchronized, real-time measurement and monitoring infrastructure deployed across geographically dispersed locations in a power system network. It employs **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** synchronized via the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** to deliver high-speed, simultaneous voltage and current phasor measurements of the power grid, thereby enabling dynamic observability of system behavior well beyond the capabilities of conventional SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> WAMS forms a critical pillar of Smart Grid real-time situational awareness. KTU examiners frequently target the **architecture, role of PMU/PDC, synchrophasor concept, and the WAMS vs SCADA comparison** for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Governing Synchrophasors:** IEEE C37.118.1 (measurement) and IEEE C37.118.2 (communication), later superseded/aligned with **IEEE/IEC 60255-118-1:2018** for synchrophasor measurements in power systems. Reporting rates commonly used: **10, 25, 50, or 120 frames/second** (60 fps in 60 Hz systems).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to understand the traffic flow across an entire country. A traditional **SCADA system** is like having a few police officers on motorcycles who call in their observations every few minutes — you only get a slow, snapshot-like picture of what is happening, and the reports are not perfectly aligned in time.

A **WAMS**, on the other hand, is like having a **fleet of drones** hovering above every major highway, each equipped with a **synchronized atomic clock** (GPS), sending **60 high-resolution video frames per second** to a central command center. Because all drones are time-synchronized to within **1 microsecond**, the central command can overlay the footage perfectly and watch traffic flow dynamics in real time — spotting accidents, jams, or unusual patterns almost instantly.

In this analogy:
- **Drones** = PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units)
- **Synchronized atomic clocks** = GPS time synchronization
- **Central command center** = Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC) + Control Center
- **Highway system** = Power transmission grid

This real-time, time-aligned view is precisely what makes WAMS revolutionary for power system operation.

---

### 1.3 Why WAMS is Indispensable in the Smart Grid

- **SCADA Refresh Rate:** 1 sample every 2–10 seconds (asynchronous, slow).
- **PMU Reporting Rate:** 25–120 samples per second (synchronous, fast).
- **Time Accuracy of PMU:** **±1 microsecond** via GPS synchronization.
- **Measurement Type:** Direct measurement of **positive-sequence voltage and current phasors** (magnitude and angle), frequency, and Rate of Change of Frequency (ROCOF).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** WAMS bridges the gap between traditional steady-state monitoring (SCADA/EMS) and the need for **dynamic, transient, and oscillatory** visibility of modern grids increasingly penetrated by intermittent renewable energy sources.

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology — At a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **PMU** | Phasor Measurement Unit — device measuring synchrophasors |
| **PDC** | Phasor Data Concentrator — aggregates & time-aligns PMU data |
| **GPS Sync** | Common time reference using Global Positioning System |
| **Synchrophasor** | Time-stamped phasor referenced to UTC |
| **WAMS** | Wide Area Monitoring System |
| **WACS** | Wide Area Control System (uses WAMS data for control) |
| **TVE** | Total Vector Error — accuracy metric of PMU |
| **ROCOF** | Rate of Change of Frequency |
| **C37.118** | IEEE standard for synchrophasor measurements |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 WAMS Architecture — Component-Wise Breakdown

A typical WAMS is built as a **hierarchical, multi-layered architecture**. The information flow proceeds from the field level (substations) up to the control center level. Let us dissect each component.

#### Layer 1 — Field Measurement Layer (PMUs)
- Installed at strategic substations and generating stations.
- Samples 3-phase voltages and currents at high speed (typically 48–2400 samples/sec).
- Computes the **positive-sequence phasor** at each reporting instant.
- Each computed phasor is **time-stamped** using a 1-PPS (Pulse Per Second) signal from a **GPS receiver**.
- Phasor data frames are streamed continuously over a communication link (typically Ethernet / fiber / dedicated WAN).

#### Layer 2 — Substation Phasor Data Concentrator (Substation PDC)
- Collects phasor data streams from **all PMUs in the substation**.
- **Time-aligns** the phasors based on their GPS timestamps.
- Eliminates late / out-of-order packets and generates a single aligned output frame.
- Performs data quality checks (e.g., flagging bad data, dropout detection).

#### Layer 3 — Super PDC / Regional PDC
- Receives aligned data from multiple substation PDCs.
- Performs a second level of time alignment and aggregation.
- Forwards consolidated data to the control center PDC.

#### Layer 4 — Control Center PDC + Applications
- The **central PDC** handles the final aggregation.
- Feeds various real-time applications:
  - Real-time visualization (dashboards, geo-synchronized displays)
  - Oscillation detection (e.g., modal analysis)
  - Voltage stability assessment
  - Frequency stability monitoring
  - Event detection and post-event analysis
  - Linear State Estimation
  - Wide-area control (when integrated with WACS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Principle:** Every layer up the stack **adds latency** but **improves data consolidation and quality**. Modern WAMS architectures minimize end-to-end latency to **less than 100 milliseconds** for time-critical control applications.

---

### 2.2 Operational Steps — How WAMS Works

1. **GPS Time Synchronization:** Every PMU receives a 1-PPS (Pulse Per Second) signal and absolute UTC time from satellites. All measurements are referenced to a common, globally identical time base.
2. **High-Speed Sampling:** Analog 3-phase voltages and currents are sampled at rates far exceeding the nominal power-system frequency (oversampling of 8×–64× is common).
3. **Phasor Estimation:** A discrete Fourier transform (DFT) — typically a recursive or non-recursive **DFT/FFT** — extracts the fundamental-frequency phasor. Anti-aliasing filters precede sampling.
4. **Time-Stamping:** The instant at which the phasor is valid is recorded with microsecond precision.
5. **Communication:** Time-stamped phasors are framed (per IEEE C37.118.2) and sent over Ethernet/IP to a PDC.
6. **Aggregation & Alignment:** The PDC aligns phasors from many PMUs by their timestamps, producing a global snapshot.
7. **Application Processing:** Control center applications process aligned data for monitoring, analysis, and (in WACS) closed-loop control.
8. **Storage & Visualization:** Historic data is archived for post-event forensic analysis; real-time dashboards show dynamic system behavior.

---

### 2.3 Synchrophasor Mathematics — Foundation

A pure sinusoid at nominal power-system frequency can be written as:

$$x(t) = X_{m} \cos(2\pi f_{0} t + \phi)$$

The corresponding **complex phasor** in rectangular form is:

$$\mathbf{X} = \frac{X_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi} = X_{r} + j X_{i}$$

where:
- $X_{m}$ = peak amplitude
- $X_{r}, X_{i}$ = real and imaginary components
- $f_{0}$ = nominal system frequency (**50 Hz** in India, **60 Hz** in the US)
- $\phi$ = phase angle relative to a cosine synchronized to UTC

A **synchrophasor** is this complex phasor reported with a **UTC time tag** at the instant of measurement, sampled at a fixed reporting rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** In India, the **nominal frequency is 50 Hz** and the standard PMU reporting rates used in PGCIL/POWERGRID deployments are **25, 50, and 100 frames per second**. KTU questions frequently include this India-specific detail.

---

### 2.4 Total Vector Error (TVE) — The PMU Accuracy Metric

TVE is the most cited quality metric for synchrophasor measurements, defined by IEEE C37.118:

$$TVE(n) = \frac{\mid \mathbf{X}_{true}(n) - \mathbf{X}_{measured}(n) \mid}{\mid \mathbf{X}_{true}(n) \mid} \times 100\%$$

Expanding using the difference of two complex phasors:

$$TVE = \frac{\sqrt{(X_{r,true} - X_{r,meas})^{2} + (X_{i,true} - X_{i,meas})^{2}}}{\sqrt{X_{r,true}^{2} + X_{i,true}^{2}}} \times 100\%$$

- **Maximum allowable TVE under IEEE C37.118.1:** **1%** for protection-class PMUs, with a relaxation to higher values under off-nominal frequency or dynamic conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the **division by the true phasor magnitude**. TVE is a *relative* error, expressed as a percentage. Writing it as a raw vector difference is a guaranteed half-mark loss.

---

### 2.5 Frequency and ROCOF Estimation

PMUs estimate instantaneous system frequency and the rate of change of frequency (ROCOF) as byproducts of synchrophasor measurement.

Frequency estimate:
$$f = f_{0} + \frac{1}{2\pi} \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

ROCOF:
$$ROCOF = \frac{df}{dt}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why ROCOF matters:** In low-inertia grids with high renewable penetration, ROCOF can spike dangerously during a generation-load imbalance. PMU-derived ROCOF enables **fast under-frequency load shedding** and **fast frequency response** from inverter-based resources.

---

### 2.6 WAMS vs. SCADA — Direct Comparison

| Feature | SCADA / EMS | WAMS (PMU-based) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sampling / Reporting Rate** | 1 sample per 2–10 seconds | 25–120 samples/second |
| **Time Synchronization** | None (asynchronous, polled) | GPS-synchronized to ±1 μs |
| **Measurement Type** | RMS magnitude, slow trends | Positive-sequence phasor (magnitude + angle) |
| **Visibility** | Steady-state only | Dynamic, transient, oscillatory |
| **Latency** | Seconds to minutes | Sub-second (typically < 100 ms) |
| **Data Volume** | Low (kB/s) | High (MB/s) |
| **Standards** | IEC 60870-5, DNP3 | IEEE C37.118, IEEE 60255-118-1 |
| **Use Case** | Long-term monitoring & control | Real-time dynamic security assessment |
| **Phasor Angles** | Not directly available | Directly measured |
| **Cost per Node** | Lower | Higher |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **POWERGRID (India)** has deployed one of the world’s largest WAMS — the **Unified Real Time Dynamic State Measurement (URTDSM)** project — covering over 350 PMUs across the Indian grid.
- **Eastern and Western Interconnections in the US** use WAMS from the **BPA, WECC, and Eastern Interconnection** for oscillation detection (e.g., the August 1996 Western Interconnection blackout, post-event analyzed using synchrophasors).
- **Wide-Area Control Systems (WACS)** built atop WAMS have been used to damp inter-area oscillations via **Phasor Power-Controlled FACTS (PSCAD-validated HVDC and SVC modulation)**.
- **Renewable integration:** WAMS is essential for managing the variability and low inertia of wind/solar-penetrated grids.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Time-Domain Sinusoid to Synchrophasor

We start with a clean derivation showing how a real sinusoidal signal is converted into a synchrophasor. This is a frequent derivation-type question in the 14-mark section.

**Given:** A single-phase voltage at the output of a transmission substation is measured as

$$v(t) = 400 \sqrt{2} \cdot \cos(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t + 30^{\circ}) \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the peak amplitude and phase.**

From the given expression:
- Peak amplitude: $V_{m} = 400\sqrt{2}$ V
- Nominal frequency: $f_{0} = 50$ Hz
- Angular frequency: $\omega_{0} = 2\pi f_{0} = 100\pi$ rad/s
- Phase angle: $\phi = 30^{\circ} = \pi/6$ rad

**Step 2 — Express in standard form.**

We rewrite using the cosine form (the reference cosine aligned to the GPS second boundary):

$$v(t) = V_{m} \cos(2\pi f_{0} t + \phi) = 400\sqrt{2} \cdot \cos(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t + 30^{\circ})$$

**Step 3 — Form the analytic signal (complex envelope).**

The analytic signal representation is:

$$z(t) = v(t) + j \cdot \mathcal{H}\{v(t)\}$$

where $\mathcal{H}\{\cdot\}$ denotes the Hilbert transform. For a pure single-tone sinusoid, $\mathcal{H}\{\cos(\omega t + \phi)\} = \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. Hence:

$$z(t) = V_{m}\cos(\omega t + \phi) + j V_{m}\sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's identity.**

Using Euler's formula, $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$:

$$z(t) = V_{m} e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}$$

**Step 5 — Extract the synchrophasor (strip the carrier).**

Dividing by the rotating carrier $e^{j\omega t}$:

$$\mathbf{V} = \frac{z(t)}{e^{j 2\pi f_{0} t}} = V_{m} e^{j\phi}$$

Substituting peak-to-RMS conversion ($V_{rms} = V_{m}/\sqrt{2}$):

$$\mathbf{V}_{rms} = \frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi} = 400 \angle 30^{\circ} \text{ V (RMS)}$$

**Step 6 — Rectangular form.**

$$\mathbf{V}_{rms} = 400\cos(30^{\circ}) + j \cdot 400\sin(30^{\circ}) = 346.41 + j200 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Add the time tag (synchrophasor).**

At the GPS time-stamped instant, say $t_{UTC} = 12:00:00.000$ (00:00 UTC, Jan 1, 2025), the synchrophasor is reported as:

$$\mathbf{V}(t_{UTC}) = 346.41 + j200 \text{ V} \quad \text{at timestamp } t_{UTC}$$

This is precisely the data packet structure under IEEE C37.118.2.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Vector Error (TVE) Worked Example

**Problem:** The true synchrophasor is $\mathbf{X}_{true} = 1.0 \angle 0^{\circ}$ pu. The measured synchrophasor from a PMU is $\mathbf{X}_{meas} = 0.99 \angle 0.5^{\circ}$ pu. Calculate the TVE.

**Step 1 — Convert true phasor to rectangular form.**

$$\mathbf{X}_{true} = 1.0 \cos(0^{\circ}) + j \cdot 1.0 \sin(0^{\circ}) = 1.0 + j0.0$$

So $X_{r,true} = 1.0$, $X_{i,true} = 0.0$.

**Step 2 — Convert measured phasor to rectangular form.**

$$\mathbf{X}_{meas} = 0.99\cos(0.5^{\circ}) + j \cdot 0.99\sin(0.5^{\circ})$$

$$\mathbf{X}_{meas} \approx 0.99 \times 0.99996 + j \cdot 0.99 \times 0.00873$$

$$\mathbf{X}_{meas} \approx 0.98996 + j0.00864$$

So $X_{r,meas} = 0.98996$, $X_{i,meas} = 0.00864$.

**Step 3 — Compute the squared error magnitude.**

$$(\Delta X_{r})^{2} = (1.0 - 0.98996)^{2} = (0.01004)^{2} = 1.008 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$(\Delta X_{i})^{2} = (0.0 - 0.00864)^{2} = (0.00864)^{2} = 7.46 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Sum and take the square root.**

$$\mid \mathbf{X}_{true} - \mathbf{X}_{meas} \mid = \sqrt{1.008 \times 10^{-4} + 7.46 \times 10^{-5}} = \sqrt{1.754 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\mid \mathbf{X}_{true} - \mathbf{X}_{meas} \mid \approx 0.01324$$

**Step 5 — Normalize by the true magnitude.**

$$\mid \mathbf{X}_{true} \mid = \sqrt{1.0^{2} + 0^{2}} = 1.0$$

$$TVE = \frac{0.01324}{1.0} \times 100\% = 1.324\%$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

The TVE is approximately **1.32%**, which is just above the **1% steady-state limit** defined by IEEE C37.118.1 for protection-grade PMUs. This PMU would marginally fail the steady-state TVE test, indicating the need for recalibration or anti-aliasing filter adjustment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** TVE combines the effects of **magnitude error, angle error, time-stamp error, and aliasing** into a single figure of merit. A 1% TVE typically corresponds to an angle error of about **0.57°** at constant magnitude, or equivalently a magnitude error of 1% at constant angle.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — WAMS Performance Equations

| Quantity | Equation | Typical / Acceptable Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phasor (RMS)** | $\mathbf{X} = (X_{m}/\sqrt{2})e^{j\phi}$ | Used in C37.118 reporting |
| **Reporting Rate** | $F_{s}$ (frames/sec) | 25, 50, 100, 120 fps |
| **TVE** | $TVE = \vert \mathbf{X}_{true} - \mathbf{X}_{meas} \vert / \vert \mathbf{X}_{true} \vert$ | $\leq 1\%$ (steady state) |
| **Frequency Estimate** | $f = f_{0} + (1/2\pi) d\phi/dt$ | Within ±0.005 Hz nominal |
| **ROCOF** | $ROCOF = df/dt$ | Used for fast load shedding |
| **End-to-End Latency** | $T_{comm} + T_{PDC} + T_{proc}$ | < 100 ms for control apps |
| **PMU Time Sync Error** | $\Delta t$ (vs UTC) | $\leq \pm 1 \mu s$ |
| **Measurement Bandwidth** | $F_{s}/2$ (Nyquist) | ~50 Hz for 100 fps reporting |
| **Phasor Angle Error vs Time Sync** | $\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_{0} \Delta t$ | 0.018° per μs at 50 Hz |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 1 μs matters at 50 Hz:** A 1 μs timing error translates to a phase-angle error of $\Delta\phi = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1\times 10^{-6} \approx 0.018°$. For wide-area angle-difference measurements across thousands of km, even sub-millisecond timing errors compound to several degrees — justifying the GPS discipline of synchrophasors.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Define Wide Area Monitoring System (WAMS). Mention any two advantages of WAMS over conventional SCADA.**

**Model Answer:**

Wide Area Monitoring System (WAMS) is a real-time, time-synchronized measurement infrastructure that uses **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** synchronized via the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** to measure voltage and current phasors across geographically dispersed locations in a power system.

**Two advantages over SCADA:**
1. **Higher reporting rate** — PMUs report at 25–120 samples/second versus SCADA's 1 sample every 2–10 seconds, enabling observation of **dynamic and transient** phenomena.
2. **GPS time synchronization** — All measurements are aligned to a common UTC reference within ±1 μs, enabling direct comparison of phasor angles across the entire grid.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**What is a Synchrophasor? Mention the IEEE standard that governs synchrophasor measurements.**

**Model Answer:**

A **synchrophasor** is a **time-stamped complex phasor** (with magnitude and angle) of a sinusoidal voltage or current waveform, referenced to a common time base provided by the **Global Positioning System (GPS)**. The timestamp is derived from a 1-PPS signal from the GPS receiver, ensuring that phasors from different locations can be directly compared.

**Governing IEEE Standards:**
- **IEEE C37.118.1-2011** — Synchrophasor measurement requirements (TVE, latency, reporting rate).
- **IEEE C37.118.2-2011** — Communication protocol for synchrophasor data.
- These have been merged into **IEEE/IEC 60255-118-1:2018**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Wide Area Monitoring System (WAMS). Clearly state the role of PMU, PDC, and GPS. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Compare WAMS with conventional SCADA in terms of reporting rate, time synchronization, measurement type, and applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WAMS Architecture and Components (7 Marks):**

The WAMS consists of the following hierarchical layers:

1. **GPS Time Source (1 Mark):**
   - Provides UTC time and a 1-PPS reference to all PMUs.
   - Ensures all phasors are aligned to a common, globally identical time base within ±1 μs.

2. **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) (2 Marks):**
   - Installed at strategic substations and generating stations.
   - Sample 3-phase voltages and currents at high rates, compute the positive-sequence phasor via DFT, and time-stamp each phasor with the GPS clock.
   - Stream phasor data over Ethernet at reporting rates of 25, 50, 100, or 120 fps.

3. **Phasor Data Concentrators (PDCs) (2 Marks):**
   - **Substation PDCs** aggregate and time-align phasors from all PMUs within a substation.
   - **Super/Regional PDCs** combine data from multiple substation PDCs.
   - **Central PDC** at the control center performs final aggregation.
   - PDCs reject out-of-order or late packets, ensuring data integrity.

4. **Control Center Applications (1 Mark):**
   - Real-time visualization, oscillation detection, voltage/frequency stability assessment, linear state estimation, and post-event analysis.

5. **Block Diagram (1 Mark):** [Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of this note for the full hierarchical block diagram.]

**(b) WAMS vs SCADA Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | SCADA / EMS | WAMS (PMU-based) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reporting Rate** (1 Mark) | 1 sample per 2–10 s | 25–120 samples/sec |
| **Time Synchronization** (1 Mark) | Asynchronous, polled | GPS-synchronized ±1 μs |
| **Measurement Type** (2 Marks) | RMS magnitude only | Positive-sequence phasor (mag + angle), frequency, ROCOF |
| **Latency** (1 Mark) | Seconds to minutes | < 100 ms |
| **Application** (1 Mark) | Steady-state monitoring, slow control | Real-time dynamic monitoring, oscillation detection, WACS |
| **Standard** (0.5 Mark) | IEC 60870-5 / DNP3 | IEEE C37.118 / IEEE 60255-118-1 |
| **Data Volume** (0.5 Mark) | Low (kB/s) | High (MB/s) |

**Concluding Statement:** WAMS provides a real-time, dynamic, and time-synchronized view of the power system, making it indispensable for modern smart-grid operation, whereas SCADA is sufficient only for steady-state supervisory control.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU). With a block diagram, describe the functional blocks inside a PMU and the role of GPS synchronization. (7 Marks)
