\left(\frac{PX}{EV}\right)^{2}} - \frac{V^{2}}{X}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply through by $X$ to clear the denominator:** $$Q X = E V \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{PX}{EV}\right)^{2}} - V^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Square both sides to remove the radical:** $$(Q X + V^{2})^{2} = E^{2} V^{2}\left[1 - \left(\frac{PX}{EV}\right)^{2}\right]$$ **Step 5 — Expand the right-hand side:** $$(Q X + V^{2})^{2} = E^{2} V^{2} - P^{2} X^{2}$$ **Step 6 — Expand the left-hand side:** $$Q^{2} X^{2} + 2 Q X V^{2} + V^{4} = E^{2} V^{2} - P^{2} X^{2}$$ **Step 7 — Collect all terms on one side to obtain a quadratic in $V^{2}$:** $$V^{4} + (2 Q X - E^{2})V^{2} + (P^{2} X^{2} + Q^{2} X^{2}) = 0$$ Let $y = V^{2}$. Then the **P–V curve implicit equation** is: $$\boxed{\,y^{2} + (2 Q X - E^{2})\,y + X^{2}(P^{2} + Q^{2}) = 0\,}$$ **Step 8 — Real-solution condition (existence of power-flow equilibrium):** The discriminant of this quadratic in $y$ must be $\geq 0$: $$\Delta = (E^{2} - 2 Q X)^{2} - 4 X^{2}(P^{2} + Q^{2}) \geq 0$$ **Step 9 — Solve for the maximum P (nose-point):** Set $\Delta = 0$ and $Q$ fixed; rearranging: $$E^{4} - 4 E^{2} Q X - 4 X^{2} P^{2} = 0$$ $$\boxed{\,P_{\max} = \frac{\sqrt{E^{4} - 4 E^{2} Q X}}{2 X}\,}$$ For $Q = 0$, this reduces to the well-known $P_{\max} = E^{2}/(2X)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for the student:** The quadratic-in-$V^{2}$ form makes it explicit that **voltage collapse = loss of real solutions** to the power-flow equation. This is a fundamentally different phenomenon from frequency instability (which is a loss of *rotor-angle* equilibrium). ## 3.2 Derivation — L-Index (Kessel & Glavitsch) **Start from the standard power-flow equation in $Y_{bus}$ form:** $$I_{bus} = Y_{bus} \cdot V_{bus}$$ **Partition the system into generator (subscript $G$) and load (subscript $L$) buses:** $$\begin{bmatrix} I_{G} \\ I_{L} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{GG} & Y_{GL} \\ Y_{LG} & Y_{LL} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_{G} \\ V_{L} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Express the load-bus currents using the second row:** $$I_{L} = Y_{LG} V_{G} + Y_{LL} V_{L}$$ **Solve for $V_{L}$ in terms of the $F$ matrix (where $F = - Y_{LL}^{-1} Y_{LG}$):** $$V_{L} = - Y_{LL}^{-1} Y_{LG} V_{G} = F \cdot V_{G}$$ **Substitute back to eliminate load currents:** Apply the relationship $I_{L} = 0$ for a load bus (passive loads only). The voltage at any load bus $j$ becomes: $$V_{j} = \sum_{i \in G} F_{ji} V_{i}$$ **Define the L-index for bus $j$:** $$L_{j} = \left\vert 1 - \frac{\sum_{i \in G} F_{ji} V_{i}}{V_{j}} \right\vert = \left\vert 1 - \sum_{i \in G} F_{ji} \frac{V_{i}}{V_{j}} \right\vert$$ **Stability criterion:** $$0 \leq L_{j} \leq 1 \quad \forall j \in L$$ A value of $L_{j} = 1$ means the bus is at the **point of voltage collapse**. > [!NOTE] > The L-index is **system-wide** (one number per load bus), requires only the $Y_{bus}$ and complex bus voltages, and is therefore ideally suited for **online smart-grid VSA** using PMU streams. ## 3.3 Derivation — Reduced Jacobian Modal Analysis **Start from the linearised power-flow Jacobian:** $$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_{P\theta} & J_{PV} \\ J_{Q\theta} & J_{QV} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta\theta \\ \Delta V \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 1 — Set $\Delta P = 0$ (assumption: real-power injection is held constant by AGC):** $$0 = J_{P\theta} \Delta\theta + J_{PV} \Delta V \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta\theta = -J_{P\theta}^{-1} J_{PV} \Delta V$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the Q row:** $$\Delta Q = J_{Q\theta} \Delta\theta + J_{QV} \Delta V$$ $$\Delta Q = -J_{Q\theta} J_{P\theta}^{-1} J_{PV} \Delta V + J_{QV} \Delta V$$ $$\Delta Q = (J_{QV} - J_{Q\theta} J_{P\theta}^{-1} J_{PV}) \Delta V$$ **Step 3 — Define the Reduced Jacobian:** $$\boxed{\,J_{R} = J_{QV} - J_{Q\theta} J_{P\theta}^{-1} J_{PV}\,}$$ So that $\Delta Q = J_{R} \Delta V$. **Step 4 — Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $J_{R}$:** $$J_{R} = \Phi \, \Lambda \, \Phi^{-1}, \quad \Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n})$$ **Stability criterion:** All eigenvalues must be positive: $\xi_{i} > 0 \, \forall i$. **Voltage collapse condition:** The minimum eigenvalue $\xi_{\min} \to 0$ at the nose. **Step 5 — Participation factors (modal bus involvement):** $$P_{ki} = \Phi_{ki} \cdot \Psi_{ik}$$ Where $\Psi = \Phi^{-1}$. Large $P_{ki}$ means bus $k$ is strongly involved in mode $i$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering interpretation:** The smallest-eigenvalue mode and its dominant participating buses pinpoint **the geographical location of the imminent voltage collapse** — this is the *killer feature* of modal analysis for smart-grid online monitoring. ## 3.4 Derivation — FVSI (Fast Voltage Stability Index) **Start from the two-bus receiving-end active and reactive power for a line with impedance $R + jX$:** $$P_{j} = \frac{V_{i}^{2}}{Z}\cos\theta - \frac{V_{i} V_{j}}{Z}\cos(\theta + \delta)$$ $$Q_{j} = \frac{V_{i}^{2}}{Z}\sin\theta - \frac{V_{i} V_{j}}{Z}\sin(\theta + \delta)$$ **Step 1 — Differentiate the power equations with respect to $V_{j}$ and apply $\partial P / \partial V_{j} = 0$ at the voltage collapse point:** Setting the partial derivative to zero gives the **critical condition**. **Step 2 — After algebraic reduction (Musirin & Rahman, 2007), the index is:** $$\boxed{\,FVSI_{ij} = \frac{4 Z^{2} Q_{j}}{V_{i}^{2} X}\,}$$ **Stability criterion:** $FVSI_{ij} \leq 1$. At $FVSI = 1$, the line is at its **maximum loading**. > [!NOTE] > FVSI is **distribution-system-friendly**: it requires only the line $R$, $X$, sending-end $V_i$, and receiving-end $Q_j$ — all of which are easily SCADA/AMI-measurable in a smart distribution grid. ## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method | Method | Best Suited For | Data Required | Computational Cost | Smart-Grid Suitability | |---|---|---|---|---| | **P–V Curve (CPF)** | Transfer capability, ATC | Full network model | Moderate | Excellent (online-capable) | | **Q–V Curve** | Reactive-reserve planning | Bus admittance + scheduled V | Low | Good (operator advisory) | | **L-Index** | System-wide online VSA | $Y_{bus}$, $V_{bus}$ | Low | Excellent | | **Modal Analysis** | Transmission VSA, mode identification | Full Jacobian | Moderate | Excellent | | **SVD** | Continuation, near-collapse studies | Jacobian | Moderate–High | Good | | **FVSI / LQP** | Distribution VSA | Line parameters, end voltages | Very low | Excellent (edge-of-grid) | | **VCPI** | Quick screening | Active power only | Low | Good | | **Time-domain simulation** | Long-term/dynamic VS | Dynamic models | Very High | Limited (offline) | | **PMU-based WAMS** | Real-time wide-area VSA | Synchrophasor streams | Moderate (stream) | Industry standard | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Online Voltage Stability Assessment in a Smart Grid The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a modern smart-grid Energy Management System (EMS) integrates data, computation, and control to perform **online voltage stability assessment**. ```mermaid flowchart TB SCADA[SCADA Telemetry
Bus Voltages, MW, MVAr]:::input PMUSTRM[PMU Synchrophasor Stream
30-60 samples per second]:::input DERRT[DER Real-Time Output
PV Inverters, Wind, BESS]:::input WEATHER[Weather Forecast API
Solar Irradiance, Wind Speed]:::input DATAHUB[Data Concentrator and
Time-Alignment Engine]:::proc STATEMOD[State Estimator
WLS / PMU-Aided SE]:::proc VSAENG[Online VSA Engine]:::core PVMOD[P-V and Q-V Curve
Generator Module]:::core LSIMOD[L-Index and FVSI
Computation Module]:::core MODMOD[Modal Analysis and SVD
Computation Module]:::core CPFMOD[Continuation Power Flow
Tracer Module]:::core AICORE[AI and ML Forecaster
LSTM or XGBoost Predictor]:::core DECISION[VSA Decision Module]:::output OPALERT[Operator Alarm and
HMI Dashboard]:::output ACTCTRL[Automatic Control Signals
to STATCOM, OLTC, Shunt Cap]:::output MARKTPUB[ATC and Voltage Margin
Publication to Market]:::output LOGDB[Historical Archive
for Post-Event Analysis]:::storage SCADA --> DATAHUB PMUSTRM --> DATAHUB DERRT --> DATAHUB WEATHER --> DATAHUB DATAHUB --> STATEMOD STATEMOD --> VSAENG VSAENG --> PVMOD VSAENG --> LSIMOD VSAENG --> MODMOD VSAENG --> CPFMOD VSAENG --> AICORE PVMOD --> DECISION LSIMOD --> DECISION MODMOD --> DECISION CPFMOD --> DECISION AICORE --> DECISION DECISION --> OPALERT DECISION --> ACTCTRL DECISION --> MARKTPUB DECISION --> LOGDB classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef core fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Logical Flow of the P–V Curve Construction (Analytical Workflow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initialize Base Case
Power Flow at λ=0]:::step B[Increase λ by Δλ
Update P and Q Setpoints]:::step C[Run Newton-Raphson
Power Flow]:::step D{Converged?}:::decision E[Record V and P Point]:::step F[Switch Continuation
Parameter if Near Nose]:::step G[Continue Iterating
until λ_max]:::step H[Identify Nose Point
P_max and V_cr]:::step I[Compute Loading Margin
LM = λ_max x 100 percent]:::step A --> B --> C --> D D -- Yes --> E --> F --> G --> C D -- No --> F G --> H --> I classDef step fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Voltage Stability Index Decision Tree (Choosing the Right Index) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: System to be Assessed]:::root Q1{System Level?}:::decision T1[Use Modal Analysis
or P-V Curve with CPF]:::rec D1[Use FVSI, LQP, or L-Index]:::rec Q2{Real-Time
Constraint?}:::decision Q3{Single Bus or
Line Concern?}:::decision OUT1[L-Index per bus]:::rec OUT2[FVSI or LQP per line]:::rec OFF[Use CPF or time-domain
simulation offline]:::rec START --> Q1 Q1 -- Transmission --> T1 Q1 -- Distribution --> D1 T1 --> Q2 Q2 -- Yes --> MODMOD2[Modal analysis on PMU stream]:::rec Q2 -- No --> OFF D1 --> Q3 Q3 -- Bus --> OUT1 Q3 -- Line --> OUT2 classDef root fill:#d6d8db,stroke:#1b1e21,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef rec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define voltage stability. Distinguish between voltage stability and voltage collapse.** **Model Answer:** **Voltage stability** is the ability of a power system to maintain steady voltages at all buses after being subjected to a disturbance, such that the system reaches a new equilibrium with no loss of integrity. **Voltage collapse** is the *consequent process* by which the voltage instability sequence leads to abnormally low voltages in a significant portion of the system, often resulting in blackout. | Aspect | Voltage Stability | Voltage Collapse | |---|---|---| | Nature | A *capability* of the system | An *event* triggered by instability | | Voltage level | Within acceptable limits | Far below acceptable limits | | System state | Operative equilibrium | Loss of equilibrium | | Duration | Continuous property | Catastrophic, fast or slow | > **Key Phrase to Memorise:** *"Stability is a property; collapse is an outcome of losing that property."* (2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for definitions.) --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is a P–V curve? Explain the term "loading margin" with a neat sketch description.** **Model Answer:** A **P–V curve** is a plot of the receiving-end (load-bus) voltage $V$ as a function of the active power transfer $P$ from a source to that bus, for a fixed power factor and network condition. It has a characteristic "**nose**" shape — the upper half (stable) and lower half (unstable) meet at the **maximum loading point (MLP)** or **point of voltage collapse**. The **loading margin (LM)** is the *horizontal distance* (in MW or as a percentage) from the current operating point to the nose: $$LM = \frac{P_{\max} - P_{0}}{P_{0}} \times 100\%$$ It is the standard metric used by grid operators to declare system security against voltage collapse. A typical smart-grid criterion is **LM ≥ 5 %** under contingency. (2 marks for the P–V curve explanation, 1 mark for the loading margin formula and significance.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum power transfer $P_{\max}$ in a lossless two-bus system. From this, explain how the loading margin is computed in a practical multi-bus system using the continuation power flow (CPF) method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the IEEE 14-bus system under a particular loading condition, the smallest eigenvalue of the reduced Jacobian $J_R$ is $\xi_{\min} = 0.018$ pu. After a contingency (loss of a major line), the new value is $\xi_{\min} = 0.004$ pu. Critically discuss the implications, including the modal interpretation and the role of participation factors. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Start with the two-bus lossless power-flow equation:** $$P = \frac{E V}{X}\sin\delta$$ **Step 2 — Maximum power occurs when $\partial P/\partial \delta = 0$:** $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta} = \frac{E V}{X}\cos\delta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = 90°$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back:** $$P_{\max} = \frac{E V}{X}$$ For a multi-bus system, $X$ becomes the *transfer reactance* (Thevenin equivalent). **Step 4 — Loading margin via CPF:** 1. **Predictor step:** at base case, compute tangent vector of the power-flow equations in $(V, \lambda)$ space. 2. **Step-size selection:** choose $\Delta \lambda$ based on local curvature. 3. **Corrector step:** solve the augmented power-flow with a chosen continuation parameter (e.g., $\lambda$ in flat region, $V$ near the nose). 4. **Repeat** until $\det(J_R) \to 0$ (nose point). 5. **Loading margin:** $$LM = \lambda_{\max} \cdot P_{L0} \quad \text{or} \quad LM(\%) = \lambda_{\max} \times 100$$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** The CPF avoids the Newton–Raphson singularity at the nose, traces *both* the stable and unstable branches, and gives an operator a complete picture of how much load can be added before collapse. #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 — Modal analysis recap:** The reduced Jacobian $J_R$ relates incremental $Q$ to incremental $V$: $$\Delta Q = J_R \Delta V$$ The eigenvalues $\xi_{i}$ of $J_R$ and corresponding right eigenvectors $\Phi$ identify **modes** of voltage-reactive coupling. **Step 2 — Interpretation of given numbers:** - **Base case:** $\xi_{\min} = 0.018$ pu — the system has *positive* margin. Voltage stability is preserved. - **Post-contingency:** $\xi_{\min} = 0.004$ pu — the minimum eigenvalue has dropped by a factor of **4.5×**, indicating a substantial *reduction* in reactive support. **Step 3 — Proximity index:** $$\text{Voltage Stability Index (VSI)} = \min(\xi_{i})$$ A common empirical rule: if $\xi_{\min} < 0.005$ pu, the system is in the **critical zone** and operator action is mandatory. **Step 4 — Role of participation factors:** The participation factor $P_{ki} = \Phi_{ki} \cdot \Psi_{ik}$ identifies which bus $k$ is most involved in the *minimum-eigenvalue mode* $i$. The operator should: 1. Add reactive support (switch in shunt capacitor, raise generator terminal $V$). 2. Reduce import on transmission corridors feeding the participating buses. 3. Curtail or curtailable load in the affected zone. **Step 5 — Smart-grid enhancement:** With PMU streams, the modal calculation can be re-run every 30–60 seconds, giving a **real-time, geographically-resolved VSA**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A** > - Do **not** confuse $\xi_{\min}$ with the *smallest singular value*; they are related but not identical (SVD uses $\sigma_{\min} = \sqrt{\min(\xi_i^2)}$ for symmetric $J_R$). > - Do **not** claim that a single eigenvalue equals the loading margin; the *relationship* is qualitative, not quantitative. > - Always specify the **base** when expressing per-unit values. > - Students often forget to mention the **participation factor formula** — examiners explicitly look for it. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With neat sketches and derivations, explain the construction of **P–V curves** and **Q–V curves** for voltage stability assessment. Highlight the differences in their application. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a two-bus system with $E = 1.05$ pu, $X = 0.4$ pu, and a load drawing $P = 2.5$ pu at unity power factor at bus 2. Compute: (i) the receiving-end voltage $V$, (ii) the maximum transferable power $P_{\max}$, and (iii) the loading margin in %. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **P–V Curve:** - Plots $V$ (receiving end) vs $P$ (active power transfer). - Built by repeated power flow for increasing $\lambda$. - The "nose" gives $P_{\max}$ and $V_{cr}$. - Used for **transfer-capability** and **ATC** studies. **Q–V Curve:** - Plots the *reactive power required* $Q_{inj}$ vs $V$ at a single bus. - The bottom of the curve gives $V_{cr}$ and the reactive margin $\Delta Q$. - Used for **reactive-reserve planning** and **voltage-recovery** studies. **Differences (the KTU-favourite 1-mark table):** | Property | P–V Curve | Q–V Curve | |---|---|---| | Independent variable | $P$ (or $\lambda$) | $V$ | | Dependent variable | $V$ | $Q$ | | Yields | $P_{\max}$, loading margin | $V_{cr}$, reactive margin | | Application | Transfer capability, ATC | Reactive support planning | | Single bus? | No (transfer specific) | Yes (bus specific) | **Construction Method (both):** 1. Establish a base case by power flow. 2. Use either **CPF** (P–V) or successive Q-injection (Q–V) to trace the curve. 3. Identify the nose (P–V) or the bottom vertex (Q–V). 4. Compute the relevant margin. #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** - $E = 1.05$ pu, $X = 0.4$ pu - $P = 2.5$ pu at $pf = 1.0 \Rightarrow Q = 0$ **Step 1 — Write the power-flow equations (lossless):** $$P = \frac{E V}{X}\sin\delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2.5 = \frac{1.05 \cdot V}{0.4}\sin\delta$$ $$Q = \frac{E V \cos\delta - V^{2}}{X} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Solve the Q equation for $\cos\delta$:** $$0 = \frac{1.05 V \cos\delta - V^{2}}{0.4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = 1.05 \cos\delta$$ **Step 3 — Express $\sin\delta$ using $\sin^{2}\delta + \cos^{2}\delta = 1$:** $$\sin\delta = \sqrt{1 - (V/1.05)^{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the P equation:** $$2.5 = \frac{1.05 \cdot V}{0.4}\sqrt{1 - (V/1.05)^{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by 0.4 / 1.05 / V:** $$\frac{2.5 \cdot 0.4}{1.05 V} = \sqrt{1 - (V/1.05)^{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Square both sides:** $$\left(\frac{1.0}{1.05 V}\right)^{2} = 1 - \frac{V^{2}}{1.05^{2}}$$ $$\frac{1.0}{1.1025 V^{2}} = 1 - \frac{V^{2}}{1.1025}$$ Let $u = V^{2}$: $$\frac{1.0}{1.1025 u} = 1 - \frac{u}{1.1025}$$ Multiply through by $1.1025 u$: $$1.0 = 1.1025 u - u^{2}$$ $$u^{2} - 1.1025 u + 1.0 = 0$$ **Step 7 — Solve the quadratic:** $$u = \frac{1.1025 \pm \sqrt{1.1025^{2} - 4}}{2} = \frac{1.1025 \pm \sqrt{1.2155 - 4}}{2}$$ Discriminant is negative ⇒ no real solution ⇒ **the loading $P = 2.5$ pu is infeasible!** **Step 8 — Compute $P_{\max}$ (at $\delta = 90°$, $Q = 0$):** $$P_{\max} = \frac{E^{2}}{2X} = \frac{1.05^{2}}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{1.1025}{0.8} = 1.3781 \text{ pu}$$ **Step 9 — Compute loading margin (the system is already overloaded):** Since $P = 2.5 > P_{\max} = 1.378$, the system is **past the nose**. $$\text{Loading Margin} = \frac{1.378 - 2.5}{2.5} \times 100\% = -44.88\%$$ The negative margin confirms **voltage collapse has already occurred** at this loading — the bus voltage would have collapsed before reaching $P = 2.5$ pu. This is a classic exam result designed to test whether the student notices the inconsistency. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B** > - Many students will mechanically plug into $P = (EV/X)\sin\delta$ and stop. They will not check the **feasibility of the solution** (i.e., the discriminant). Marks are reserved for the *physical interpretation* of the negative loading margin. > - Always state units and pu base in part (b) numericals. > - For Q–V vs P–V, students often mix up which curve gives the reactive margin — examiners check this explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for VSA — KTU 2024 Scheme** ### Core Definitions - ☐ Voltage stability = ability to maintain steady voltages after disturbance. - ☐ Voltage collapse = process of system-wide low-voltage blackout. - ☐ Loading factor $\lambda$ scales $P_L$ and $Q_L$ uniformly. - ☐ Loading margin $LM = (P_{\max}-P_0)/P_0 \times 100\%$. ### Essential Formulae - ☐ $P_{\max} = E^{2}/(2X)$ for lossless line with $Q_L = 0$. - ☐ Quadratic in $V^{2}$: $V^{4} + (2QX - E^{2})V^{2} + X^{2}(P^{2}+Q^{2}) = 0$. - ☐ $L_j = \vert 1 - \sum F_{ji}(V_i/V_j) \vert$, range 0–1. - ☐ $FVSI_{ij} = 4Z^{2}Q_j/(V_i^{2} X)$, range 0–1. - ☐ Reduced Jacobian $J_R = J_{QV} - J_{Q\theta}J_{P\theta}^{-1}J_{PV}$. - ☐ Voltage stability $\Leftrightarrow$ all eigenvalues of $J_R$ positive. - ☐ SVD smallest singular value $\sigma_{\min} \to 0$ at collapse. ### Methods - ☐ **P–V curve** → transfer capability, ATC. - ☐ **Q–V curve** → reactive-reserve planning. - ☐ **CPF** → traces full nose curve; used in online DSA. - ☐ **Modal analysis** → identifies participating buses. - ☐ **L-index** → system-wide online VSA. - ☐ **FVSI / LQP** → distribution-system VSA. ### Smart-Grid Specific Points - ☐ PMU/WAMS feeds real-time VSA engine. - ☐ High renewable penetration reduces system strength → reduces $SCR$ → worsens VS margin. - ☐ STATCOMs, smart inverters (with $Q(V)$ droops) provide *dynamic* reactive support. - ☐ AI/ML models (LSTM, XGBoost) are used to **forecast** VSA index trends. ### Common Pitfalls (Examiner's View) - ☐ Mixing up **per-unit bases** in numericals. - ☐ Forgetting to use **complex arithmetic** in the L-index. - ☐ Confusing $\det(J_R) = 0$ with $\xi_{\min} = 0$ (the latter is correct for symmetric $J_R$ only). - ☐ Treating the **P–V nose** as a "fault" — it is a *mathematical singularity* of the power-flow Jacobian, not a physical short-circuit. - ☐ Forgetting the *continued* in **Continuation Power Flow** — the method is iterative, not single-shot.

topics.title: Frequency Stability Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Stability Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Frequency Stability** is defined (per IEEE/CIGRE Joint Task Force on Stability Terms and Definitions) as:

> The ability of a power system to maintain **steady frequency** following a severe system disturbance, resulting in a significant imbalance between generation and load.

It is a sub-class of **Rotor Angle Stability** that deals with the system's ability to *settle at an acceptable operating frequency* after a disturbance, without tripping of generating units or load-shedding cascades. Frequency stability assessment is the systematic study (offline simulation or real-time monitoring) of how the system frequency evolves following events like generator outages, sudden load additions, islanding, or loss of large renewable infeeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – PEEET861 Module 2**
> Frequency stability in the smart-grid context is *not* the same as classical frequency regulation. It explicitly includes:
> 1. **Low-inertia scenarios** caused by high Renewable Energy Source (RES) penetration (inverter-based resources).
> 2. **Wide-Area Monitoring** via Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs).
> 3. **Adaptive Under-Frequency Load Shedding (UFLS)** schemes.
> 4. **Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)**-based protection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rotating Flywheel" Model

Imagine a giant **turntable (flywheel)** in your kitchen carrying several heavy pots. The motor driving the turntable is *generation*, the weight of pots is *load*, and the turntable's rotation speed is *system frequency* (**50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in the US**).

- When you **add a pot (load ↑)**, the motor momentarily slows down — frequency drops.
- When you **remove a pot (load ↓)**, the motor speeds up — frequency rises.
- The **heaviness (mass) of the turntable** is the *system inertia (H)*. A heavy turntable resists sudden speed changes; a light one spins up/down rapidly.

In a power system, the rotating masses of synchronous generators (turbine + rotor = ~300 tonnes for a 500 MW unit) act as this flywheel. In a *smart grid* dominated by solar PV and wind (no rotating mass), the turntable becomes *lighter* — frequency swings are **faster and deeper**. This is the central problem in modern frequency stability assessment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Limits (India CEA Grid Code):**
> - Nominal Frequency: **50.00 Hz**
> - Normal Band: **49.90 – 50.05 Hz**
> - Emergency Band: **49.50 – 50.20 Hz**
> - Survival Limit (islanding): **47.50 Hz for 0.2 s → instantaneous UFLS trigger**

## 1.3 Why Frequency Stability Matters in Smart Grids

| Driver | Classical Grid | Smart Grid |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia Source | Synchronous generators | Mostly inverter-based (RES) → **low inertia** |
| Disturbance Size | Few hundred MW | Up to GW-scale RES ramps |
| Monitoring | SCADA (2–4 s refresh) | PMU/WAMS (**30–60 samples/s**) |
| Control | Centralized governor | Distributed + adaptive agents |
| Protection | Fixed-step UFLS | ROCOF + adaptive + islanded schemes |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Frequency Deviation

Frequency in an AC system is *the rate of rotation of the equivalent rotating mass* of all synchronized generators:

$$f = \frac{p \cdot \omega_m}{2\pi} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

where $p$ = number of poles and $\omega_m$ = mechanical angular velocity in rad/s.

When **mechanical power input ($P_m$) ≠ electrical power output ($P_e$)**, the kinetic energy stored in the rotating mass is either released (frequency rises) or absorbed (frequency drops).

## 2.2 The Swing Equation — Heart of Frequency Dynamics

The per-unit swing equation, linearized for small deviations ($\Delta f \ll f_0$), is:

$$M \cdot \frac{d(\Delta \omega)}{dt} = P_m - P_e - D \cdot \Delta \omega$$

Substituting $\Delta \omega = 2\pi \cdot \Delta f$ and re-writing in per-unit on system base $S_B$:

$$\frac{2H}{f_0} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta f)}{dt} = \Delta P_m - \Delta P_e - D \cdot \Delta f$$

- $H$ = **Inertia Constant** (seconds) — kinetic energy stored per unit MVA
- $f_0$ = nominal frequency (**50 Hz** in India)
- $D$ = **Damping Coefficient** (load damping, p.u./Hz)
- $\Delta P_m$ = change in mechanical power (governor response)
- $\Delta P_e$ = change in electrical load (the disturbance)
- $\Delta f$ = frequency deviation from nominal

## 2.3 Three-Tier Frequency Control Hierarchy

1. **Primary Control (seconds):** Governor + droop. Acts automatically. Restores balance at a *new* frequency.
2. **Secondary Control (minutes):** Automatic Generation Control (AGC). Restores nominal frequency by adjusting set-points.
3. **Tertiary Control (minutes–hours):** Economic dispatch, unit commitment, reserves reallocation.

## 2.4 Smart-Grid Specifics: Frequency Stability Indices

| Index | Formula | Meaning | Smart-Grid Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Nadir** $f_{nadir}$ | minimum value of $f(t)$ after disturbance | worst-case low frequency | Triggers UFLS |
| **Quasi-Steady-State Frequency** $f_{ss}$ | $f_0 - \Delta P_L / \beta$ | post-primary-control frequency | AGC reference |
| **RoCoF** | $\dfrac{df}{dt}\big|_{t=0^+}$ = $\dfrac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2 H_{sys}}$ | initial slope of $f(t)$ | Anti-islanding, ROCOF relay |
| **Inertia Constant** $H_{sys}$ | $\dfrac{\sum H_i S_i}{S_{sys}}$ | system-wide kinetic energy | inertia monitoring |
| **Frequency Response Characteristic (FRC / β)** | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} + D$ | slope of $P-f$ response | reserve sizing |
| **Critical RoCoF** | $\dfrac{df}{dt}\big|_{crit}$ | max RoCoF tolerated by relays | inertia floor |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = \dfrac{p\,\omega_m}{2\pi}$ | Pole pairs, mech. speed | Hz | $f_0 = 50$ Hz (India) |
| 2 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} J \omega_m^2$ | Stored kinetic energy | MJ | — |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{E_k}{S_{rated}}$ | Inertia constant | seconds (s) | 2 – 8 s (thermal), 2 – 4 s (hydro) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{2H}{f_0}\dot{\Delta f} = \Delta P_m - \Delta P_e - D\Delta f$ | Swing equation (p.u.) | p.u. | Valid for small-signal |
| 5 | $\text{RoCoF} = \dfrac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2 H_{sys}}$ | Initial rate of freq. change | Hz/s | $\le 0.5$ Hz/s for ROCOF relays (Ireland) |
| 6 | $f_{nadir} = f_0 - \dfrac{\Delta P_L}{2 D_s}$ (no gov.) | Min frequency w/o droop | Hz | Triggers UFLS at 49.2 Hz (India) |
| 7 | $f_{ss} = f_0 - \dfrac{\Delta P_L}{D + 1/R_{eq}}$ | Quasi-steady freq. | Hz | AGC target |
| 8 | $\Delta P_L^{max} = \beta (f_0 - f_{nadir,lim})$ | Largest non-UFLS loss | MW | Sizing primary reserve |
| 9 | $H_{sys} = \sum H_i \cdot \dfrac{S_i}{S_B}$ | System inertia | s | Floor ≈ 3 s (ENTSO-E) |
| 10 | $N_s = \dfrac{\Delta P_L}{\sum L_{\text{shed},i}}$ | UFLS stages | — | Typically 4–6 stages in India |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary note for examiners:** For the KTU 2024 numerical problems, the typical values used are: $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $H = 4$ s, $D = 1$ p.u./Hz, $R = 5\%$, $\Delta P_L = 0.1$ p.u. Always state the per-unit base ($S_B$) in your answer.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Frequency stability assessment is essential for:
- **System Operator (e.g., POSOCO/Grid-India)**: Real-time monitoring via **WAMS**.
- **Renewable Integrator**: Computing *minimum inertia floor* for safe operation.
- **Protection Engineer**: Coordinating UFLS/ROCOF relays.
- **Distribution Engineer**: Designing **islanding schemes** for microgrids.
- **Market Operator**: Valuating **Fast Frequency Response (FFR)** services from batteries.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)

Starting from the swing equation at $t = 0^+$ (instant after disturbance, before any governor action, so $\Delta P_m = 0$ and $\Delta f \approx 0$):

$$\frac{2H}{f_0} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta f)}{dt} = -\Delta P_e$$

Here, $\Delta P_e = -\Delta P_L$ (a load increase corresponds to a positive $\Delta P_L$, so electrical power demand rises by $\Delta P_L$ but generation hasn't yet responded).

**Step 1:** Substitute and solve for the derivative:

$$\frac{d(\Delta f)}{dt} = \frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2H}$$

**Step 2:** Re-write in terms of $f$ (since $\Delta f = f - f_0$):

$$\boxed{\;\text{RoCoF} = \frac{df}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0^+} = \frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2H_{sys}} \quad [\text{Hz/s}]\;}$$

**Conversion logic:** $\Delta P_L$ must be expressed in per-unit of the system base. If you are given absolute MW, divide by $S_B$ (MVA) first. Conversely, to find RoCoF in Hz/s, multiply the per-unit disturbance by $f_0/2H$.

> **Worked Example (typical KTU 3-mark style):**
> $S_B = 1000$ MVA, $H = 4$ s, $f_0 = 50$ Hz, Loss of a 250 MW generator.
> - $\Delta P_L = 250/1000 = 0.25$ p.u.
> - $\text{RoCoF} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.25}{2 \times 4} = \dfrac{12.5}{8} = 1.5625$ Hz/s.
> This is **way above** the 0.5 Hz/s RoCoF-relay limit — an islanding risk!

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quasi-Steady-State Frequency

After primary control settles (governors have responded), the system reaches a new equilibrium where $d(\Delta f)/dt = 0$. The swing equation becomes:

$$0 = \Delta P_m - \Delta P_L - D \cdot \Delta f_{ss}$$

The governor droop equation gives the mechanical power change as a function of the frequency deviation:

$$\Delta P_m = -\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R \cdot f_0} \quad \text{(in p.u., with } R \text{ in p.u.)}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\Delta P_m$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R \cdot f_0} - \Delta P_L - D \cdot \Delta f_{ss}$$

**Step 2:** Collect $\Delta f_{ss}$ terms on one side:

$$\Delta f_{ss}\left(\frac{1}{R f_0} + D\right) = -\Delta P_L$$

**Step 3:** Define the **Frequency Response Characteristic (β)**:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{R f_0} + D \quad [\text{p.u./Hz}]$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the steady-state frequency deviation:

$$\Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_L}{\beta} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_{ss} = f_0 - \frac{\Delta P_L}{\beta}$$

$$\boxed{\;f_{ss} = f_0 - \frac{\Delta P_L}{\frac{1}{R f_0} + D} \quad [\text{Hz}]\;}$$

**Conversion logic:** The minus sign appears because we defined $\Delta P_L$ as a load increase (positive), which causes a frequency *drop* (negative $\Delta f_{ss}$). The term $\frac{1}{R f_0}$ is the governor contribution and $D$ is the inherent self-regulation of frequency-dependent loads (motors, resistive loads with thermal inertia, etc.).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Nadir (Worst-Case Swing Without Governor)

For fast-acting disturbances (sub-second), governors have not yet responded, but system inertia is actively absorbing the imbalance. The nadir (minimum frequency) is found by **energy balance** between the kinetic energy released and the disturbance energy.

**Step 1:** Integrate the swing equation from $t = 0$ to $t = t_{nadir}$:

$$\int_0^{t_{nadir}} \frac{2H}{f_0} \cdot \frac{df}{dt}\, dt = \int_0^{t_{nadir}} \left(\Delta P_m - \Delta P_e - D \Delta f\right) dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the assumption that during the inertial phase, $\Delta P_m \approx 0$ and $D \cdot \Delta f$ is small (to first order):

$$\frac{2H}{f_0} (f_{nadir} - f_0) = -\Delta P_L \cdot t_{nadir}$$

**Step 3:** Assume $t_{nadir} \approx \dfrac{f_0 - f_{nadir}}{-\text{RoCoF}_{avg}}$, and for a rough worst-case estimate, use the average RoCoF (half of initial):

$$\boxed{\;f_{nadir} \approx f_0 - \frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{4 H_{sys}}\;\sqrt{\frac{2 H_{sys}}{D \cdot f_0}}\;}$$

A more common engineering approximation used in KTU problems is the **simplified nadir formula** (suitable for Part B numericals):

$$\boxed{\;f_{nadir} \approx f_0 - \frac{\Delta P_L}{2 D_s}\;}\quad \text{where } D_s = D + \frac{1}{R f_0}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of 2 (not 1) in the denominator arises because the average frequency deviation over the inertial period is approximately half the nadir deviation, assuming a triangular swing profile. The KTU 2024 examiner key uses this simplified form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** In all 14-mark derivation/numerical problems, students are expected to:
> 1. State the swing equation.
> 2. Apply the correct boundary condition ($d\Delta f/dt = 0$ for steady state, $t = 0^+$ for RoCoF).
> 3. Show **units** in every line of calculation.
> 4. Convert all quantities to **p.u. on a common base** before substitution.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Assessment Methods

| Method | Time Horizon | Tool | Data Source | Smart-Grid Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor Angle Sweep** | 0 – 10 s | Time-domain simulation (PSCAD, PSS®E) | Full dynamic model | Conventional unit trip |
| **Energy Function (BCU)** | 0 – 5 s | Transient Energy Function | Reduced model | First-swing stability |
| **PMU/WAMS Real-Time** | 30–60 ms | State estimator + RoCoF estimator | Synchrophasors | Adaptive UFLS, inertia monitoring |
| **Statistical / ML** | minutes–hours | LSTM / Random Forest | SCADA + PMU + weather | Frequency stability pre-screening |
| **Inertia Estimation** | 1–5 s | ROCOF-based regression | PMU ringdown | Real-time inertia floor |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define frequency stability in a power system. List any two factors that affect frequency stability in a smart grid.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
Frequency stability is the ability of a power system to maintain *steady frequency* (within statutory limits) following a severe disturbance that creates a significant generation–load imbalance, without uncontrolled load shedding or generator tripping. *Two factors affecting it in a smart grid:* **(i)** Reduced system inertia due to high penetration of inverter-based renewable energy sources (wind/PV), which increases the **Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)**; **(ii)** Variability and uncertainty of RES generation leading to large, unpredictable net-load ramps that strain primary frequency response reserves. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)? How is it used in smart-grid protection?**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
RoCoF is the time derivative of the system frequency, $\dfrac{df}{dt}$, measured at the instant immediately after a disturbance. In a 50 Hz system, it indicates *how fast* the system is decelerating or accelerating. In smart-grid protection, RoCoF is used as a **fast-acting anti-islanding criterion** for distributed generators: if the measured RoCoF exceeds a critical threshold (e.g., **0.5 Hz/s** in Ireland, **1 Hz/s** in Germany), the DG is tripped within ~100–200 ms to prevent out-of-synchronism re-closure. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ▶ Question A (Choice 1) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **initial Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)** following a sudden load increase of $\Delta P_L$ p.u. in a power system having per-unit inertia constant $H$ and nominal frequency $f_0$. Discuss how the RoCoF value changes when renewable energy sources replace conventional synchronous generators. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1000 MVA interconnected system has a total inertia constant $H = 4$ s. The system frequency is 50 Hz. A generator of rating 200 MVA trips suddenly. Calculate: **(i)** the initial RoCoF in Hz/s, **(ii)** the frequency nadir assuming $D = 1$ p.u./Hz, and **(iii)** the steady-state frequency deviation if the governor droop is $R = 5\%$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of RoCoF:**

The per-unit swing equation, linearised around nominal operating point:

$$\frac{2H}{f_0} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta f)}{dt} = \Delta P_m - \Delta P_e - D \cdot \Delta f$$

At $t = 0^+$ (immediately after disturbance, before any governor response), we apply the boundary conditions: $\Delta P_m = 0$ (governor dead time ≈ 0.2–0.5 s), $\Delta f \approx 0$ (frequency has not yet deviated), and $\Delta P_e = \Delta P_L$ (electrical load has stepped up by $\Delta P_L$):

$$\frac{2H}{f_0} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta f)}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = -\Delta P_L$$

Solving for the derivative:

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{df}{dt}\right|_{0^+} = -\frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2H} \quad [\text{Hz/s}]\;}$$

The negative sign indicates a frequency *drop* following a load increase. The magnitude $\vert df/dt \vert$ is the RoCoF.

**Effect of RES penetration on RoCoF:**

The system inertia $H_{sys}$ is the *weighted sum* of individual machine inertias:

$$H_{sys} = \frac{\sum H_i \cdot S_{i,\text{rated}}}{S_B}$$

Synchronous generators (coal, hydro, gas) provide kinetic energy via their rotating mass ($H \approx 3$–$6$ s). Inverter-based resources (solar PV, Type-3/4 wind) **do not inherently store kinetic energy** in the electromagnetic sense; their power electronic interface decouples the source from the grid frequency.

Therefore, as RES penetration increases:
- $H_{sys}$ **decreases** (e.g., from 4 s to 1.5 s).
- For a given $\Delta P_L$, $\vert df/dt \vert$ **increases proportionally** (e.g., a 2.5× reduction in $H$ causes a 2.5× increase in RoCoF).
- The system becomes **more vulnerable** to RoCoF-based anti-islanding relays and frequency-driven cascade events.

*(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for RES discussion, 2 for neatness and units)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $S_B = 1000$ MVA, $H = 4$ s, $f_0 = 50$ Hz, tripped generator = 200 MVA, $D = 1$ p.u./Hz, $R = 5\% = 0.05$ p.u.

**Step 1 — Convert disturbance to per-unit:**
$$\Delta P_L = \frac{200 \text{ MW}}{1000 \text{ MVA}} = 0.20 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial RoCoF:**
$$\text{RoCoF} = \frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2H} = \frac{50 \times 0.20}{2 \times 4} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25 \text{ Hz/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Frequency Nadir (using simplified formula):**

First, calculate the effective system damping including droop:
$$D_s = D + \frac{1}{R \cdot f_0} = 1 + \frac{1}{0.05 \times 50} = 1 + 0.4 = 1.4 \text{ p.u./Hz}$$

Using the simplified nadir formula (Section 3.3):
$$f_{nadir} \approx f_0 - \frac{\Delta P_L}{2 D_s} = 50 - \frac{0.20}{2 \times 1.4} = 50 - 0.0714 = 49.93 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute steady-state frequency deviation:**
$$f_{ss} = f_0 - \frac{\Delta P_L}{D_s} = 50 - \frac{0.20}{1.4} = 50 - 0.1429 = 49.857 \text{ Hz}$$

So $\Delta f_{ss} = -0.143$ Hz (frequency settles 0.143 Hz below nominal, within the 49.9–50.05 Hz normal band, but primary control must return it to 50 Hz via AGC).

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| RoCoF | **1.25 Hz/s** | High — borderline for ROCOF relays |
| Nadir $f_{nadir}$ | **49.93 Hz** | Within statutory limits |
| $f_{ss}$ | **49.86 Hz** | Will be restored by AGC |

*(7 marks — 2 for RoCoF, 2 for nadir, 2 for steady-state, 1 for neatness)*

---

### ▶ Question B (Choice 2) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]*

> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **three-tier hierarchical frequency control** scheme (primary, secondary, tertiary) used in modern smart grids. Compare conventional and smart-grid UFLS schemes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A regional grid has the following parameters: nominal frequency 50 Hz, system inertia constant 3.5 s, total governor droop equivalent 4%, load damping coefficient $D = 0.8$ p.u./Hz. If a sudden load increase of 150 MW occurs on a 500 MVA base, determine: **(i)** the quasi-steady-state frequency $f_{ss}$, **(ii)** the maximum permissible load loss (in MW) that will not violate the 49.5 Hz statutory limit, and **(iii)** the number of UFLS stages if each stage sheds 25 MW. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three-Tier Hierarchical Frequency Control:**

*Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for the visual block diagram. The textual explanation follows.*

**Primary Control (0.5 s – 30 s):**
- **Action:** Automatic governor response.
- **Mechanism:** When frequency deviates, each generator's speed-droop governor changes its mechanical power input by $\Delta P_m = -\Delta f / (R \cdot f_0)$.
- **Outcome:** System reaches a *new* quasi-steady frequency $f_{ss} < f_0$, balancing generation and load.
- **Smart-grid enhancement:** Fast Frequency Response (FFR) from Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and synthetic inertia from wind farms.

**Secondary Control (30 s – 15 min):**
- **Action:** Automatic Generation Control (AGC) / Load Frequency Control (LFC).
- **Mechanism:** Computes the **Area Control Error (ACE)** for each control area: $\text{ACE} = \Delta P_{tie} + B \cdot \Delta f$. AGC sends raise/lower pulses to generators in the area.
- **Outcome:** Frequency is restored to nominal $f_0$, and tie-line schedules are maintained.
- **Smart-grid enhancement:** AGC extended to include aggregated DER aggregators and demand response.

**Tertiary Control (15 min – hours):**
- **Action:** Economic dispatch, reserve reallocation, unit commitment changes.
- **Mechanism:** Operator re-optimises generation mix to restore primary/secondary reserves and minimise cost.
- **Outcome:** System returns to *optimal* state with adequate reserves.
- **Smart-grid enhancement:** Day-ahead and intra-day markets for ancillary services (FCR, FRR, FFR) coordinated across regions.

**Comparison: Conventional vs Smart-Grid UFLS**

| Feature | Conventional UFLS | Smart-Grid UFLS |
|---|---|---|
| Triggering | Fixed frequency thresholds (e.g., 49.2, 48.8, 48.4, 48.0 Hz) | Adaptive, RoCoF-aware, uses PMU data |
| Shedding logic | Pre-set % of load per stage | Optimised for estimated disturbance size |
| Speed | 100–200 ms (electromechanical relays) | < 50 ms (IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging) |
| Selectivity | Whole feeders | Specific DER/non-critical load feeders |
| Restoration | Manual | Automatic, sequenced |
| Adaptability | None — same for all disturbances | Adaptive to RES variability & inertia |

*(7 marks — 3 for control tiers, 2 for block diagram reference, 2 for UFLS comparison)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $H = 3.5$ s, $R = 4\% = 0.04$ p.u., $D = 0.8$ p.u./Hz, $S_B = 500$ MVA, $\Delta P_L = 150$ MW.

**Step 1 — Convert disturbance to per-unit:**
$$\Delta P_L = \frac{150 \text{ MW}}{500 \text{ MVA}} = 0.30 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Frequency Response Characteristic (β):**
$$\beta = D + \frac{1}{R \cdot f_0} = 0.8 + \frac{1}{0.04 \times 50} = 0.8 + 0.5 = 1.3 \text{ p.u./Hz}$$

**(i) Quasi-Steady-State Frequency:**
$$f_{ss} = f_0 - \frac{\Delta P_L}{\beta} = 50 - \frac{0.30}{1.3} = 50 - 0.2308 = 49.77 \text{ Hz}$$

So $f_{ss} \approx 49.77$ Hz. This is *below* the normal 49.9 Hz band but *above* the 49.5 Hz emergency limit — AGC must restore it.

**(ii) Maximum Permissible Load Loss (without violating 49.5 Hz):**

The frequency drop must satisfy $f_0 - f_{nadir,lim} \le \Delta f_{max}$.

$$\Delta f_{max} = 50.0 - 49.5 = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Using the steady-state model (worst case):
$$\Delta P_L^{max} = \beta \cdot \Delta f_{max} = 1.3 \times 0.5 = 0.65 \text{ p.u.}$$

Converting to MW:
$$\Delta P_L^{max} = 0.65 \times 500 = 325 \text{ MW}$$

So **any loss ≤ 325 MW** is acceptable without UFLS tripping. The 150 MW disturbance is well within this envelope.

**(iii) Number of UFLS Stages:**

Each UFLS stage sheds 25 MW. For a worst-case disturbance of 325 MW (the maximum non-tripping loss), the number of stages *available* for emergency would be:
$$N_{stages} = \left\lceil \frac{325}{25} \right\rceil = 13 \text{ stages}$$

However, UFLS design typically uses only 4–6 stages in practice (49.2, 48.8, 48.4, 48.0, 47.6, 47.2 Hz in Indian grid code). If the question intends *minimum* stages to cover the actual 150 MW disturbance:
$$N = \frac{150}{25} = 6 \text{ stages}$$

The standard answer expected is **6 stages** for the actual disturbance of 150 MW.

*(7 marks — 2 for $f_{ss}$, 2 for $\Delta P_L^{max}$, 2 for stage count, 1 for units/neatness)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Frequency Stability Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing **Hz** with **p.u.** in swing-equation substitution. Always state "$\Delta f$ in p.u." or "$\Delta f$ in Hz" *first*.
> 2. **Base Mismatch:** Using machine-rated MVA instead of **system base $S_B$** when computing $H_{sys}$ or per-unit $\Delta P_L$.
> 3. **Sign Errors:** A load *increase* gives $\Delta P_L > 0$ but $\Delta f < 0$ (frequency *drops*). The minus sign in the formula is the most-failed point.
> 4. **Ignoring $D$:** Many students forget the load-damping term in the FRC ($\beta = D + 1/(Rf_0)$). In KTU key answers, $D$ *must* be included.
> 5. **RoCoF vs Nadir:** Students often confuse *RoCoF* (initial slope) with *nadir* (minimum value). They are different quantities computed from different boundary conditions.
> 6. **Forgetting Per-Unit Conversion of $R$:** Droop $R$ must be expressed in p.u. (i.e., 5% = 0.05), *not* as a percentage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Definition:** Frequency stability = ability to maintain *steady* frequency after a severe generation–load imbalance.
- 🔁 **Swing Equation:** $\frac{2H}{f_0}\dot{\Delta f} = \Delta P_m - \Delta P_e - D\Delta f$ — the foundational ODE.
- 📉 **RoCoF** = $\dfrac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_L}{2H_{sys}}$ — depends inversely on system inertia.
- ⬇️ **Nadir** is the *worst* frequency reached; triggers UFLS at 49.2 Hz in India.
- ⚖️ **FRC ($\beta$)** combines droop $\left(\dfrac{1}{Rf_0}\right)$ and load damping $D$ — determines steady-state offset.
- 🏛️ **Three tiers:** Primary (governor, seconds) → Secondary (AGC, minutes) → Tertiary (dispatch, hours).
- 🌞 **Smart-grid challenge:** RES reduces $H_{sys}$, so RoCoF rises, nadir deepens, and conventional UFLS is too slow.
- 🛡️ **Solutions:** Synthetic inertia, Fast Frequency Response (FFR) from BESS, adaptive RoCoF-aware UFLS, real-time WAMS-based inertia monitoring.
- 🛰️ **WAMS/PMU:** Enables real-time, 30–60 samples/s monitoring — the data backbone of smart-grid frequency assessment.
- 🇮🇳 **Indian context:** $f_0 = 50$ Hz, normal band 49.90–50.05 Hz, statutory UFLS at 49.2 Hz, Grid-India (POSOCO) is the operator.
- 📊 **For numericals:** Always state base $S_B$, convert MW → p.u. *first*, include $D$ in FRC, and show units in every line.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Oscillation Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Oscillation Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Oscillation Assessment** is the systematic process of identifying, characterizing, and evaluating electromechanical oscillations in a power system network using real-time measurement data and advanced signal-processing techniques. These oscillations arise due to the dynamic interplay between rotating masses (generators), network topology, and control systems following small or large disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Power Oscillation Assessment refers to the deployment of synchrophasor-based analytics, modal estimation algorithms, and WAMS (Wide Area Monitoring System) infrastructure to extract the damping ratio, frequency, and mode shape of oscillatory modes in the power grid, ensuring system security and stability under the smart-grid paradigm."*

The principal types of oscillations observed in bulk power systems are:

| Oscillation Type | Frequency Band | Geographic Span | Primary Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local Mode** | **0.8 Hz – 3.0 Hz** | Single plant / area | Swing of one machine vs. rest of the system |
| **Inter-Area Mode** | **0.1 Hz – 0.8 Hz** | Multiple control areas | Coherent generator groups oscillating against each other |
| **Global Mode** | < 0.1 Hz | Entire interconnection | Very slow inter-continental swings |
| **Intra-Plant / Torsional** | > 3 Hz | Within a single generator | Shaft-turbine-generator train interactions |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric of concern in oscillation assessment is the **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$)**. A damping ratio below **3% to 5%** is considered a warning threshold, while **$\zeta < 0$** indicates an unstable, growing oscillation (negative damping).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spring–Mass–Damper" of the Grid

Imagine a vast playground where dozens of children are on swings, all connected by elastic ropes. Each child (a generator) pumps their legs at slightly different times. If left alone:
- Some children (generators) will swing together in harmony → **stable mode**.
- A few will swing in the opposite direction to others → **inter-area mode**, potentially growing until a collision (blackout) occurs.

**Power Oscillation Assessment is the playground monitor** standing at the center with a stopwatch and notepad:
1. **Identifies** which swings (modes) are growing in amplitude.
2. **Measures** how fast each swing happens (frequency) and how quickly it dies down (damping).
3. **Foretells** if any swing will become dangerously large (negative damping).

> [!TIP]
> The "ropes" between swings are the **transmission lines** carrying synchronizing power; the "air resistance" on the swings is the **damping torque** in generators. PMUs act as the monitor's eyes and ears.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nominal system frequency:** $f_0 = 50$ Hz (India) or **60 Hz**.
- **Damping threshold for alarm:** $\zeta < 5\%$.
- **PMU reporting rate:** typically **30–120 samples/second**.
- **Maximum data latency requirement:** $< 1$ second (per IEEE C37.118).
- **Mode observability coefficient:** unitless, $0 \le \text{OFC} \le 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Power Oscillations

Power oscillations in a synchronous machine originate from the **swing equation**, which describes the electromechanical dynamics of the rotor:

$$M \frac{d^{2}\delta}{dt^{2}} + D \frac{d\delta}{dt} = P_{m} - P_{e}$$

where:
- $M = \frac{2H}{\omega_s}$ is the **inertia constant** (in seconds · rad),
- $H$ is the **inertia constant** (in seconds, typically 3–6 s for large machines),
- $\omega_s = 2\pi f_0$ is the **synchronous angular frequency**,
- $D$ is the **damping coefficient** (in per unit torque / per unit speed),
- $\delta$ is the **rotor angle** (in electrical radians),
- $P_m, P_e$ are the mechanical and electrical power outputs (in per unit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For small-signal analysis, we **linearize** the swing equation around an operating point. The resulting second-order differential equation has the canonical form of a damped oscillator, whose natural response contains the **mode frequency** and **damping ratio** we seek to assess.

## 2.2 Linearized State-Space Model for Modal Analysis

For an $n$-machine system, the linearized model is written compactly as:

$$\Delta \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A} \Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B} \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

$$\Delta \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C} \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

where:
- $\Delta \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ contains the rotor angle and speed deviations of all generators,
- $\mathbf{A}$ is the **state matrix** of size $2n \times 2n$,
- $\mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}$ are input and output matrices.

The system eigenvalues are obtained by solving:

$$\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$$

Each complex conjugate pair of eigenvalues:

$$\lambda_{i} = \sigma_{i} \pm j\omega_{i}$$

corresponds to one oscillatory mode. From this pair, we extract the **damped frequency** and **damping ratio**:

$$f_{i} = \frac{\omega_{i}}{2\pi}, \qquad \zeta_{i} = \frac{-\sigma_{i}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{i}^{2} + \omega_{i}^{2}}}$$

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \sigma \pm j\omega$ | Eigenvalue (mode) | Modal analysis root |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Mode frequency in Hz | Inter-area mode detection |
| 3 | $\zeta = \dfrac{-\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma^{2}+\omega^{2}}}$ | Damping ratio | Stability assessment |
| 4 | $M \ddot{\delta} + D \dot{\delta} = P_m - P_e$ | Swing equation | Derivation of eigenvalues |
| 5 | $E_{k} = \dfrac{1}{2} M \dot{\delta}^{2}$ | Kinetic energy deviation | Energy-function method |
| 6 | $x(t) = \sum_{i} A_i e^{\sigma_i t}\cos(\omega_i t + \phi_i)$ | Multi-mode response | Prony/Hilbert fit |
| 7 | $\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{z}(t)$ | Modal decomposition (state) | Mode-shape identification |
| 8 | $\text{MODE} = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_i^{T} \mathbf{w}_i}{\mathbf{v}_i^{T} \mathbf{u}_i}$ | Modal observability & energy | Right/left eigenvector mix |
| 9 | $\text{TEF} = -\sum_{i} \sigma_i \mathbf{z}_i^{2}$ | Transient energy function | Out-of-step detection |
| 10 | $a_k = \sum_{i} A_i e^{j \theta_i} p_i^{k}$ | Prony term (sample $k$) | Time-domain mode extraction |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, always present the eigenvalue as $\lambda = \sigma \pm j\omega$ with **explicit units of rad/s for $\omega$** and **per-second for $\sigma$**, and convert to Hz only at the end.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Oscillation Assessment

1. **Disturbance Trigger:** A line trip, generator outage, or load step perturbs the system.
2. **Data Acquisition:** PMUs stream voltage and current phasors to a Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC) at 30–120 fps.
3. **Signal Conditioning:** A frequency/angle/active-power signal is selected (typically a tie-line power or bus-frequency measurement).
4. **Mode Extraction:** A modal-analysis algorithm (Prony, Hankel Total Least Squares, Matrix Pencil, or ESPRIT) decomposes the ringdown into modes.
5. **Mode Identification:** Frequency, damping, amplitude, and phase are computed for each mode.
6. **Stability Decision:** If $\zeta < 5\%$, the system is flagged; if $\zeta < 0$, an alarm is raised.
7. **Control Action:** PSS, FACTS, or HVDC modulation is dispatched to inject positive damping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** A single poorly-damped inter-area mode in 2003 caused the **Northeast blackout** affecting 50 million people. Power Oscillation Assessment, paired with WAMS, is the early-warning system that prevents recurrence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Swing Equation to Damping Ratio

We begin with the linearized swing equation of a single machine connected to an infinite bus:

$$M \frac{d^{2}\Delta\delta}{dt^{2}} + D \frac{d\Delta\delta}{dt} + K_s \Delta\delta = 0$$

where $K_s = \frac{E V}{X} \cos\delta_0$ is the **synchronizing torque coefficient** (the "spring stiffness" of the electrical link).

**Step 1 — Recognize the standard form.** This is a second-order linear homogeneous ODE of the form:

$$\ddot{\Delta\delta} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{\Delta\delta} + \omega_n^{2} \Delta\delta = 0$$

Dividing through by $M$ and comparing terms:

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{D}{M}, \qquad \omega_n^{2} = \frac{K_s}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for natural frequency.**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{E V \cos\delta_0}{X M}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for damping ratio.**

$$\zeta = \frac{D}{2M\omega_n} = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{M K_s}}$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic roots (eigenvalues).** The roots of the characteristic polynomial are:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} = \sigma \pm j\omega_d$$

where:
- $\sigma = -\zeta\omega_n$ is the **real part** (decay/growth rate),
- $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

**Step 5 — Convert to damped frequency in Hz.**

$$f_d = \frac{\omega_d}{2\pi} = \frac{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{2\pi}$$

**Step 6 — General solution.** The time-domain response is:

$$\Delta\delta(t) = A e^{\sigma t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

For stability, we require $\sigma < 0 \Leftrightarrow \zeta > 0$.

### Numerical Demonstration

Consider a 500 MVA machine with $H = 3.5$ s, $D = 2.0$ pu, $E = 1.1$ pu, $V = 1.0$ pu, $X = 0.4$ pu, $\delta_0 = 30^{\circ}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{2H}{\omega_s} = \frac{2 \times 3.5}{2\pi \times 50} = 0.02228 \;\text{s \cdot rad} \\[4pt]
K_s &= \frac{E V \cos\delta_0}{X} = \frac{1.1 \times 1.0 \times \cos 30^{\circ}}{0.4} = \frac{1.1 \times 0.8660}{0.4} = 2.3816 \;\text{pu/rad} \\[4pt]
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.3816}{0.02228}} = \sqrt{106.89} = 10.339 \;\text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
\zeta &= \frac{D}{2M\omega_n} = \frac{2.0}{2 \times 0.02228 \times 10.339} = \frac{2.0}{0.4607} = 4.342 \\[4pt]
f_d &= \frac{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{2\pi} = \frac{10.339 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.04342^{2}}}{2\pi} \approx 1.644 \;\text{Hz} \\[4pt]
\sigma &= -\zeta\omega_n = -0.04489 \;\text{1/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The mode oscillates at **1.644 Hz** with a damping ratio of **4.34%** — a local mode that is **marginally damped**, warranting operator attention.

## 3.2 Derivation: Prony Analysis for Mode Extraction

Given a uniformly sampled ringdown signal $y[k]$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$, the Prony model assumes:

$$y[k] = \sum_{i=1}^{p} A_i e^{j\theta_i} \cdot \lambda_i^{k}$$

Equivalently, in continuous time:

$$y(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} B_i e^{\sigma_i t}\cos(\omega_i t + \phi_i)$$

**Step 1 — Construct the linear prediction matrix.** The samples obey the recurrence:

$$\sum_{m=0}^{p} a_m y[k-m] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the AR coefficients** $a_1, \dots, a_p$ via SVD on the Hankel matrix:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}
y[p] & y[p-1] & \cdots & y[1] \\
y[p+1] & y[p] & \cdots & y[2] \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
y[N-1] & y[N-2] & \cdots & y[N-p]
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The eigenvector corresponding to the smallest singular value gives $\mathbf{a} = [a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p]^{T}$.

**Step 3 — Find the polynomial roots.** The characteristic polynomial:

$$z^{p} + a_1 z^{p-1} + \cdots + a_p = 0$$

yields discrete-time poles $z_i$. Map to continuous time:

$$\lambda_i = \frac{\ln z_i}{\Delta t} = \sigma_i + j\omega_i$$

**Step 4 — Solve for amplitudes and phases** $A_i, \theta_i$ by least-squares fitting:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\lambda_1^{0} & \lambda_2^{0} & \cdots & \lambda_p^{0} \\
\lambda_1^{1} & \lambda_2^{1} & \cdots & \lambda_p^{1} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\lambda_1^{N-1} & \lambda_2^{N-1} & \cdots & \lambda_p^{N-1}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
B_1 \\ B_2 \\ \vdots \\ B_p
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
y[0] \\ y[1] \\ \vdots \\ y[N-1]
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $B_i = A_i e^{j\theta_i}$ are complex residues.

**Step 5 — Compute modal metrics.**

$$f_i = \frac{\omega_i}{2\pi}, \qquad \zeta_i = \frac{-\sigma_i}{\sqrt{\sigma_i^{2} + \omega_i^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Prony?** The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) can identify frequencies, but it cannot directly yield damping — Prony uses a parametric model that captures both, making it the gold standard for oscillation assessment from PMU ringdowns.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Methods of Oscillation Assessment

| Method | Input | Output | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Eigenvalue Analysis** | Full system model | All modes offline | Exhaustive, accurate | Requires full network model |
| **Prony Analysis** | Single PMU ringdown | Modes of the channel | Fast, time-localized | Sensitive to noise; needs order $p$ |
| **Matrix Pencil** | Single PMU ringdown | Modes of the channel | More robust than Prony | Slightly higher computation |
| **Hankel Total LS** | Multiple PMU ringdowns | Multi-channel modes | Filters noise via SVD | Needs stacking of channels |
| **Hilbert–Huang / EMD** | Non-stationary signal | Instantaneous frequency | Handles non-stationary | Mode-mixing artifact |
| **WAMS/PMU Real-Time** | Continuous streaming | Real-time mode index | Operator-decision support | Communication latency |
| **Energy Function (TEF)** | Network topology | Transient stability margin | Fast stability classification | Conservative, complex modeling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Oscillation Assessment — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Disturbance Event] --> B[PMUs at Substations]
    B --> C[Phasor Data Concentrator]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning]
    D --> E[Mode Extraction Algorithm]
    E --> F[Modal Identification]
    F --> G{Damping Check}
    G -->|zeta greater than 5 percent| H[Normal Operation Log]
    G -->|zeta less than 5 percent| I[Warning to Operator]
    G -->|zeta less than 0| J[Alarm and Emergency Control]
    I --> K[Control Action PSS or FACTS]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Improved Damping Feedback]
    L --> B
```

## 4.2 Two-Area Equivalent System for Inter-Area Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AreaA[Area A - Generator G1]
        G1[G1 with M1 and D1]
    end
    subgraph AreaB[Area B - Generator G2]
        G2[G2 with M2 and D2]
    end
    G1 -- Tie line reactance X tie --> G2
    G2 -- Counter swing delta 1 minus delta 2 --> G1
    AreaA --> O[Inter-Area Mode 0.1 to 0.8 Hz]
    AreaB --> O
    O --> P[Mode Shape via Right Eigenvector]
```

## 4.3 Detailed WAMS Signal-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FieldLayer[Field Layer - Substations]
        PMU1[PMU Bus A]
        PMU2[PMU Bus B]
        PMU3[PMU Bus C]
    end
    FieldLayer --> PDC[Phasor Data Concentrator PDC]
    PDC --> Comms[Communication Network C37.118]
    Comms --> ControlCenter[Control Center WAMS Server]
    ControlCenter --> PreProc[Pre-Processing and Outlier Removal]
    PreProc --> AlgoBank{Mode Extraction Suite}
    AlgoBank --> P1[Prony]
    AlgoBank --> P2[Matrix Pencil]
    AlgoBank --> P3[Hilbert Huang EMD]
    AlgoBank --> P4[Eigenvalue Engine]
    P1 --> ModeDB[Mode Database]
    P2 --> ModeDB
    P3 --> ModeDB
    P4 --> ModeDB
    ModeDB --> Dashboard[Operator Dashboard and Alarm Engine]
    Dashboard --> Operator[Decision Support]
    Operator --> FACTS[FACTS and PSS Dispatch]
    FACTS --> FieldLayer
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Frequency Response of the Damped Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[s Domain] --> Hp[Half-Power Bandwidth Filter]
    Hp --> Peak[Resonance Peak omega equals omega_n]
    Peak --> BW[Bandwidth equals 2 zeta omega_n]
    BW --> Y[Output Magnitude and Phase]
    Y --> Z[Quality Factor Q equals 1 over 2 zeta]
```

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU exam, drawing any one of these diagrams and labeling all transfer blocks with damping ratio, mode frequency, and signal flow earns you **2 to 3 marks** of presentation credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define the term 'damping ratio' as used in power oscillation assessment. What is the typical warning threshold for an inter-area mode?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The damping ratio ($\zeta$) is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the rate at which an oscillatory mode decays in a power system. For an eigenvalue $\lambda = \sigma \pm j\omega$, it is defined as:

$$\zeta = \frac{-\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + \omega^{2}}}$$

A damping ratio of **1.0** represents critical damping (no oscillation), while **$\zeta < 0$** represents an unstable, growing oscillation. In KTU-mandated grid codes, an **inter-area mode is considered underdamped and a warning is raised when $\zeta < 5\%$**. If $\zeta$ falls below 0, the mode is unstable and immediate control action is mandatory.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Distinguish between local and inter-area modes of oscillation in a power system, stating their typical frequency ranges."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Local Mode | Inter-Area Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | **0.8 – 3.0 Hz** | **0.1 – 0.8 Hz** |
| **Geographic span** | A single power plant or station | Two or more control areas |
| **Machines involved** | One generator against the rest | Two coherent groups against each other |
| **Typical cause** | Weak tie-line, poor PSS tuning | Heavy inter-area power transfer |
| **Damping** | Usually well-damped | Often poorly damped |
| **Detection difficulty** | Easier | Harder; requires WAMS |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
**(a)** Derive the expressions for **natural frequency $\omega_n$** and **damping ratio $\zeta$** from the linearized swing equation of a single machine connected to an infinite bus. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 555 MVA, 50 Hz, 4-pole synchronous generator has $H = 4.0$ s and damping coefficient $D = 1.5$ pu. The machine delivers power through a reactance $X = 0.5$ pu to an infinite bus. Internal EMF $E = 1.05$ pu, infinite-bus voltage $V = 1.0$ pu, and steady-state rotor angle $\delta_0 = 25^{\circ}$. Compute the mode frequency in Hz and the damping ratio. State whether the mode is stable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Derivation

Starting from the swing equation linearized about an operating point:

$$M \frac{d^{2}\Delta\delta}{dt^{2}} + D \frac{d\Delta\delta}{dt} + K_s \Delta\delta = 0, \qquad K_s = \frac{E V}{X}\cos\delta_0$$

Dividing by $M$:

$$\ddot{\Delta\delta} + \frac{D}{M}\dot{\Delta\delta} + \frac{K_s}{M}\Delta\delta = 0$$

Comparing with the canonical form $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^{2} x = 0$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{M}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{M K_s}}$$

The eigenvalues are $\lambda = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ and the damped frequency is $f_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}/(2\pi)$.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_s &= 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159 \;\text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
M &= \frac{2H}{\omega_s} = \frac{2 \times 4.0}{314.159} = 0.02546 \;\text{s \cdot rad} \\[4pt]
K_s &= \frac{E V \cos\delta_0}{X} = \frac{1.05 \times 1.0 \times \cos 25^{\circ}}{0.5} = \frac{1.05 \times 0.9063}{0.5} = 1.9032 \;\text{pu/rad} \\[4pt]
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{1.9032}{0.02546}} = \sqrt{74.75} = 8.645 \;\text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
\zeta &= \frac{D}{2\sqrt{M K_s}} = \frac{1.5}{2 \times \sqrt{0.02546 \times 1.9032}} = \frac{1.5}{2 \times 0.2201} = \frac{1.5}{0.4402} = 3.408 \\[4pt]
\omega_d &= \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} = 8.645 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.03408^{2}} \approx 8.640 \;\text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
f_d &= \frac{8.640}{2\pi} = 1.375 \;\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The mode oscillates at **1.375 Hz** (a local mode) with a **damping ratio of 3.41%**. Since $\zeta > 0$, the mode is **stable but underdamped** (below the 5% warning threshold). A Power System Stabilizer (PSS) tuning is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
**(a)** Explain the **Prony Analysis** method for extracting oscillatory modes from PMU ringdown data. List the steps involved. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A tie-line power oscillation between two control areas is recorded by a PMU and is found to contain two dominant modes. After Prony analysis, the extracted continuous-time poles are:
- Mode 1: $\lambda_1 = -0.18 + j 2.51$ (1/s)
- Mode 2: $\lambda_2 = -0.05 + j 0.628$ (1/s)

Determine the **frequency, damping ratio, and stability class** (local or inter-area) of each mode. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Prony Analysis Steps

1. **Data acquisition:** Collect uniform samples $y[k]$ of the ringdown signal from a PMU channel (e.g., tie-line active power).
2. **Order selection:** Choose model order $p$ (typically $p = 2N_{modes}$) using criteria such as AIC or singular-value decay.
3. **Build the Hankel matrix** $\mathbf{H}$ of size $(N-p) \times p$ from the samples.
4. **Solve linear prediction coefficients** $a_1, \dots, a_p$ via SVD — the right singular vector of the smallest singular value gives $\mathbf{a}$.
5. **Find the polynomial roots** $z_i$ of $z^{p} + a_1 z^{p-1} + \cdots + a_p = 0$.
6. **Map to continuous-time poles** $\lambda_i = \ln(z_i)/\Delta t = \sigma_i + j\omega_i$.
7. **Solve for residues** $B_i$ by least-squares fitting: $y[k] = \sum B_i \lambda_i^{k}$.
8. **Compute modal metrics:** $f_i = \omega_i/(2\pi)$ and $\zeta_i = -\sigma_i/\sqrt{\sigma_i^{2}+\omega_i^{2}}$.
9. **Validate:** Reconstruct the signal and compare against the original; discard modes with negligible amplitude or low SNR.

#### Model Solution — (b) Modal Metrics

**Mode 1:** $\lambda_1 = -0.18 + j 2.51$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 &= 2.51 \;\text{rad/s} \\
\sigma_1 &= -0.18 \;\text{1/s} \\
f_1 &= \frac{2.51}{2\pi} = 0.3994 \;\text{Hz} \\
\zeta_1 &= \frac{0.18}{\sqrt{0.18^{2} + 2.51^{2}}} = \frac{0.18}{2.5164} = 0.0715 = 7.15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Class:** Frequency in **0.1 – 0.8 Hz** band → **Inter-area mode**, well-damped (above 5% threshold).

**Mode 2:** $\lambda_2 = -0.05 + j 0.628$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_2 &= 0.628 \;\text{rad/s} \\
\sigma_2 &= -0.05 \;\text{1/s} \\
f_2 &= \frac{0.628}{2\pi} = 0.0999 \;\text{Hz} \approx 0.1 \;\text{Hz} \\
\zeta_2 &= \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{0.05^{2} + 0.628^{2}}} = \frac{0.05}{0.6300} = 0.0794 = 7.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Class:** Frequency near the **0.1 Hz** boundary → **Global/inter-area mode**, very slowly damped but still positive. Since $\zeta_2 > 0$, both modes are **stable**, though Mode 2 is borderline and would benefit from **WAMS-based control** if loading increases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\omega_s$ conversion** when computing $M = 2H/\omega_s$ — losing **2 marks**.
> 2. **Reporting frequency in rad/s** instead of converting to Hz — losing **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing damping ratio and damping factor** — remember, $\zeta$ is dimensionless while $\sigma$ has units of 1/s.
> 4. **In Prony problems, skipping the order-selection discussion** — losing **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Omitting the "stability conclusion"** — examiners always allocate the final mark for an explicit statement of stability class.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power oscillations** are categorized by frequency: **local (0.8–3 Hz)**, **inter-area (0.1–0.8 Hz)**, and **global (< 0.1 Hz)**.
- The **swing equation** $M\ddot{\delta} + D\dot{\delta} = P_m - P_e$ is the foundational model.
- The **damping ratio** $\zeta = -\sigma/\sqrt{\sigma^{2}+\omega^{2}}$ must remain **above 5%** to ensure security.
- The **damped frequency** in Hz is $f_d = \omega_d/(2\pi) = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}/(2\pi)$.
- **Prony analysis** is the workhorse for mode extraction from PMU ringdown data; it requires SVD-based order selection and LS residue fitting.
- **WAMS/PMU infrastructure** is the smart-grid enabler for real-time oscillation assessment.
- **Mode shape** is determined by the right eigenvector; **participation** by left/right eigenvector products.
- **FACTS, PSS, and HVDC modulation** are the standard control actions to inject positive damping.
- **Negative damping** ($\zeta < 0$) means an oscillation is growing — this is a system emergency.
- **KTU exam must-haves:** show full derivation, use correct units (rad/s vs Hz), state stability class explicitly, and draw at least one functional block diagram.
- The transient energy function **TEF** complements modal methods for large-disturbance stability classification.
- **Smart-grid integration** ties oscillation assessment to renewable variability, HVDC links, and inverter-based resource damping behavior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Needs of WAMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communication Needs of WAMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of WAMS Communication Needs

**Wide Area Measurement System (WAMS)** is a synchronized, high-speed measurement and monitoring infrastructure that uses **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** placed at geographically dispersed substations to deliver GPS-time-stamped, real-time phasor data (voltage, current, frequency, and rate of change of frequency) to centralized Phasor Data Concentrators (PDCs) and control centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication Needs of WAMS** refers to the specific data-rate, latency, reliability, synchronization, and architectural demands that the underlying telecommunication network must satisfy so that PMU measurements remain useful for real-time monitoring, protection, and control of the power grid.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEET861, Module 2)**, the communication needs of WAMS are defined by four pillars:

1. **High data throughput** (PMUs stream continuous, multi-channel synchronized samples).
2. **Deterministic, low latency** (control loops must close within tight time windows).
3. **Time synchronization accuracy** (typically ±1 µs with respect to UTC via GPS/GNSS).
4. **Robustness, redundancy, and cybersecurity** (failure of comms may cause grid instability).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Grid's Nervous System"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the power grid as a human body. PMUs are the *sensory nerves* located at every joint (substation). The communication network is the *spinal cord* carrying nerve impulses to the brain (control center). If the spinal cord is slow, damaged, or noisy, the brain cannot react in time — and the body collapses. WAMS communication is literally the "nervous system" of the smart grid, and its needs (speed, bandwidth, reliability) are dictated by how fast the brain must react to a stimulus (fault, oscillation, load swing).

### 1.3 Standard Communication Metrics Mandated by WAMS

The IEEE Std **C37.118.2™-2011** (and its successor IEC/IEEE 60255-118-1:2018) define the canonical metrics:

- **PMU nominal reporting rate (frames per second):** 10, 25, 50, or 100 fps for a **50 Hz** system; 10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 60, 120 fps for a 60 Hz system.
- **Synchronization accuracy:** **1 µs UTC** (mandatory).
- **Total Vector Error (TVE):** ≤ **1 %**.
- **Maximum end-to-end latency for protection-class applications:** **≤ 50 ms**.
- **Maximum end-to-end latency for monitoring-class applications:** **≤ 1 s** (often tighter at **100–200 ms**).

### 1.4 Functional Layer Model of WAMS Communication

| Layer | Function | Example |
|-------|----------|---------|
| **Acquisition Layer** | PMUs sample analog signals | ABB, SEL, GE PMUs |
| **Data Link Layer** | Frame formatting, error coding | IEEE C37.118.2 frames |
| **Transport Layer** | Reliable data delivery | TCP/IP, UDP/IP |
| **Network Layer** | Routing across WAN | MPLS, IP-VPN |
| **Application Layer** | Control & visualization | PDC, EMS, WAMS server |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 valuation, a correct mention of *all four pillars* (throughput, latency, synchronization, security) fetches full marks for any 3-mark definition question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why WAMS Has Unique Communication Demands

Conventional SCADA polls RTUs every **2–10 seconds**, which is acceptable for slow energy management functions. WAMS, however, must capture **dynamic, sub-second** phenomena such as:

- **Electromechanical oscillations** (0.1 – 2 Hz inter-area modes)
- **Frequency excursions** during generation/load imbalance
- **Voltage stability margins** evolving over seconds
- **Fault-induced transient swings**

These phenomena evolve faster than any human operator can react, so the comms network itself becomes part of the control loop.

### 2.2 The Five Engineering "Knobs" That Define WAMS Communication Needs

**1. Data Volume per PMU**
Each PMU frame contains:
- 1 *SYNC* word (2 bytes)
- 1 *FRAMESIZE* word (2 bytes)
- 1 *IDCODE* (2 bytes)
- 1 *SOC* (4 bytes — Second Of Century timestamp)
- 1 *FRACSEC* (4 bytes — fractional second)
- *STAT* (2 bytes)
- *PHASORS* (variable: 4 or 8 bytes per phasor)
- *FREQ* & *DFREQ* (4 bytes each)
- *ANALOG* (2 bytes each)
- *DIGITAL* (2 bytes each)
- *CHK* (2 bytes CRC)

**2. Latency Budget**
The **end-to-end latency (τ)** is decomposed as:

$$\tau_{e2e} = \tau_{samp} + \tau_{proc}^{PMU} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{proc}^{PDC}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{samp}$ = sampling/ADC delay
- $\tau_{proc}^{PMU}$ = PMU phasor-estimation processing
- $\tau_{queue}$ = buffering in network switches/routers
- $\tau_{tx}$ = serial transmission time
- $\tau_{net}$ = propagation through the WAN
- $\tau_{proc}^{PDC}$ = PDC sorting/aggregation latency

**3. Synchronization Source**
GPS (or IRNSS, GLONASS, BeiDou, Galileo) gives **UTC** with **±100 ns** typical accuracy. PMUs discipline their internal clocks using **1-PPS** pulses so that phasor angles measured 1000 km apart are aligned.

**4. Reliability & Redundancy**
- Dual Ethernet paths (PRP – Parallel Redundancy Protocol, or HSR – High-availability Seamless Redundancy)
