- Ring/mesh topology with RSTP/MRP recovery < **50 ms**

**5. Cybersecurity**
NERC-CIP and IEC 62351 govern encryption, authentication, and key management for WAMS packets.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | PMU raw data rate | $R_{PMU} = f_{rep} \times L_{frame} \times 8$ | bits/s | $L_{frame}$ in bytes |
| 2 | Phasor field size (rectangular) | $2 \times B_{sample}$ | bytes | e.g., $B_{sample}=4$ (float32) |
| 3 | Phasor field size (polar) | $2 \times B_{sample}$ | bytes | magnitude + angle |
| 4 | Frame payload (P phasors) | $L = 16 + 8P + 8 + 2A + 2D + 2$ | bytes | A = analog, D = digital |
| 5 | Required WAN bandwidth | $BW = N_{PMU} \times R_{PMU} \times SF_{ovh}$ | bits/s | $SF_{ovh} = 1.2$ typical |
| 6 | One-way propagation delay (fiber) | $\tau_p = d \times n / c$ | s | $n \approx 1.467$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 7 | Transmission time | $\tau_{tx} = L_{frame} / R_{link}$ | s | $R_{link}$ in bps |
| 8 | End-to-end latency | $\tau_{e2e} = \sum$ all delays above | s | must be $<$ 50 ms for control |
| 9 | Total Vector Error | $TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X}_n - X_n \vert}{\vert X_n \vert} \times 100\%$ | % | must be $\le 1\%$ |
| 10 | Reporting rate vs. system freq. | $f_{rep} = N_{samp/cycle} \times f_0$ | fps | $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $N_{samp/cyc} \in \{10, 25, 50, 100\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing a formula in a KTU answer, **always state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming IEEE C37.118.2 protocol, 4-byte float phasors, 50 fps reporting"). Examiners award method marks for clean, labeled assumptions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Indian smart-grid rollout:** POWERGRID's **Unified Real-Time Dynamic State Measurement (URTDSM)** project deploys **1,184 PMUs** across India on a dedicated **MPLS-based IP-VPN** backbone — a textbook example of a WAMS communication network.
- **US Eastern Interconnection:** PJM and MISO operate WAMS over **dedicated fiber + 4G LTE** fallback with sub-100 ms latency.
- **European TSO cooperation:** ENTSO-E uses **IEEE C37.118 + IEC 61850-90-5** for cross-border WAMS interoperability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — PMU Data Rate for a 3-Phase Feeder

**Problem Setup:**
A PMU monitors a 3-phase line (3 voltages + 3 currents = **6 phasors**), uses **4-byte floating-point** phasor format, reports **2 analog** and **2 digital** channels, and operates at **50 fps** in a 50 Hz system. Compute the per-PMU data rate using IEEE C37.118.2.

**Step 1 — Identify fixed overheads per frame.**

- SYNC: 2 bytes
- FRAMESIZE: 2 bytes
- IDCODE: 2 bytes
- SOC: 4 bytes
- FRACSEC: 4 bytes
- STAT: 2 bytes
- CHK: 2 bytes
- Fixed subtotal = **18 bytes**

**Step 2 — Compute phasor payload.**

For polar format, each phasor requires 1 magnitude (float32) + 1 angle (float32) = 8 bytes.

$$
L_{phasor} = P \times 8 = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Add analog & digital fields.**

$$
L_{aux} = 2 \times 2 + 2 \times 2 = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Total frame length.**

$$
L_{frame} = 18 + 48 + 8 = 74 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Data rate (without protocol overhead).**

$$
R_{PMU} = f_{rep} \times L_{frame} \times 8
$$

$$
R_{PMU} = 50 \times 74 \times 8 = 29{,}600 \text{ bits/s} \approx 29.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 6 — With UDP/IP + Ethernet overhead.**

UDP header = 8 bytes, IP header = 20 bytes, Ethernet II preamble+SFD+FCS = 22 bytes, Ethernet payload header = 14 bytes. Overhead per frame = **64 bytes**.

$$
L_{frame}^{on-wire} = 74 + 64 = 138 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
R_{PMU}^{wire} = 50 \times 138 \times 8 = 55{,}200 \text{ bps} \approx 55.2 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each PMU therefore consumes ~**55 kbps** of WAN bandwidth. For a utility with **500 PMUs**, aggregate bandwidth ≈ **27.6 Mbps** — comfortably within a gigabit fiber link, but **prohibitive** on low-bandwidth cellular or PLC media.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Geographic Reach for ≤ 50 ms Latency

**Step 1 — Allocate the latency budget.**

| Component | Budget |
|-----------|--------|
| PMU processing | 5 ms |
| WAN one-way propagation | $\tau_p$ |
| PDC processing | 5 ms |
| Return (to control) | $\tau_p$ |
| **Total** | 50 ms |

$$
2\tau_p + 10 \text{ ms} = 50 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; \tau_p = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert time to distance in fiber.**

$$
d_{max} = \frac{\tau_p \times c}{n} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.467}
$$

$$
d_{max} = \frac{6 \times 10^{6}}{1.467} \approx 4.09 \times 10^{6} \text{ m} \approx 4{,}090 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

A signal can travel up to ~**4,090 km** in fiber within a 20 ms one-way window. This is why **PMUs must be placed regionally** with **hierarchical PDCs**; a single global PDC is physically infeasible without violating the 50 ms budget.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TVE Sensitivity to Synchronization Error

**Step 1 — Recall the phasor model.**

A measured phasor $\hat{X}_n$ deviates from the true $X_n$ if the time-stamp is off by $\Delta t$:

$$
\hat{X}_n = X_n \cdot e^{j 2\pi f_0 \Delta t}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute TVE.**

$$
TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X}_n - X_n \vert}{\vert X_n \vert} = \vert 1 - e^{j 2\pi f_0 \Delta t} \vert
$$

$$
TVE = 2 \left\vert \sin(\pi f_0 \Delta t) \right\vert
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum allowable $\Delta t$ given $TVE \le 1\%$.**

$$
2 \sin(\pi f_0 \Delta t) \le 0.01
$$

For small $\Delta t$, $\sin(x) \approx x$:

$$
\Delta t \le \frac{0.01}{2 \pi f_0} = \frac{0.01}{2 \pi \times 50} = 3.18 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 31.8 \text{ µs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although the **GPS spec allows 1 µs**, the **TVE spec tolerates up to ~31.8 µs** of timing error at 50 Hz. The tighter GPS requirement is chosen to keep cumulative errors (filtering, estimation, jitter) within the 1 % TVE envelope.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Communication-Technology Suitability for WAMS

| Medium | Bandwidth | Latency | Reach | Suitability for WAMS |
|--------|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------|
| **Fiber (single-mode)** | 1 Gbps – 100 Gbps | 5 µs/km | >100 km span | ★★★★★ Excellent (gold standard) |
| **MPLS IP-VPN** | 10 Mbps – 1 Gbps | 5–20 ms | Pan-India | ★★★★★ Excellent (utility standard) |
| **Microwave (PtP)** | 100 Mbps | < 1 ms | 50 km hop | ★★★★☆ Very good |
| **4G LTE** | 50–100 Mbps | 30–50 ms | Wide | ★★★☆☆ Acceptable for monitoring only |
| **5G URLLC** | 1 Gbps | < 1 ms | Wide | ★★★★★ Future standard (3GPP Rel-16) |
| **Power Line Carrier (PLC)** | < 1 Mbps | 15–30 ms | 10–50 km | ★★☆☆☆ Limited (legacy) |
| **Satellite (VSAT)** | 1–10 Mbps | 250–700 ms | Global | ★☆☆☆☆ Not for control, only back-up telemetry |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Question:** A utility deploys **300 PMUs** at **50 fps**, each producing frames of **64 bytes payload** (after header compression). The WAN uses **TCP/IP over MPLS** with 40 % overhead. Find the total bandwidth required. If a 100 Mbps link is dedicated, what is the **maximum number of PMUs** it can serve?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Per-PMU data rate (payload only):

$$
R_{p} = 50 \times 64 \times 8 = 25{,}600 \text{ bps} = 25.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

Step 2 — With 40 % overhead (multiplier 1.4):

$$
R_{p}^{wire} = 25.6 \times 1.4 = 35.84 \text{ kbps}
$$

Step 3 — 300 PMUs aggregate:

$$
R_{agg} = 300 \times 35.84 \text{ kbps} = 10.752 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Step 4 — Capacity of 100 Mbps link (assume 70 % usable):

$$
R_{usable} = 100 \times 0.7 = 70 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Step 5 — Maximum PMUs:

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{70 \times 10^3}{35.84} \right\rfloor = 1{,}953 \text{ PMUs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that even a modest 100 Mbps link can support ~2,000 PMUs, so **bandwidth is rarely the bottleneck** — **latency, jitter, and synchronization** are the real challenges of WAMS communication.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End WAMS Communication Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagrams the layered WAMS communication flow from the substation PMU to the central control application. It captures how the four pillar requirements (sync, throughput, latency, security) interact at each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Layer 1: Field
    subgraph L1[FIELD LAYER - SUBSTATION]
        CTPT[CT / PT Sensors] --> ADC[Anti-Alias Filter + ADC]
        GPSA[GPS Antenna 1-PPS] --> GPSCLK[GPS Disciplined Oscillator]
        ADC --> PMU[PMU Processor]
        GPSCLK --> PMU
    end

    %% Layer 2: Data Link
    subgraph L2[DATA LINK LAYER - SUBSTATION LAN]
        PMU --> FRM[IEEE C37.118.2 Frame Builder]
        FRM --> ETH[Ethernet Switch PRP or HSR]
    end

    %% Layer 3: Transport
    subgraph L3[TRANSPORT LAYER - WAN]
        ETH --> UDP[UDP or TCP Packetizer]
        UDP --> MPLS[MPLS IP-VPN Backbone]
        MPLS --> FIBER[Single Mode Fiber]
    end

    %% Layer 4: Aggregation
    subgraph L4[AGGREGATION LAYER - REGIONAL PDC]
        FIBER --> SUBPDC[Sub PDC]
        SUBPDC --> RPDC[Regional PDC]
        RPDC --> CPDC[Central PDC]
    end

    %% Layer 5: Application
    subgraph L5[APPLICATION LAYER - CONTROL CENTER]
        CPDC --> WAMSAPP[WAMS Server and Historian]
        WAMSAPP --> OSC[Oscillation Detection]
        WAMSAPP --> FRM2[Frequency Stability Monitor]
        WAMSAPP --> VSAG[Voltage Stability Assessment]
        WAMSAPP --> EMS[Energy Management System]
    end

    %% Cross-cutting
    NTP[PTP IEEE 1588 Backup Sync] -.-> GPSCLK
    SEC[Cyber Security IEC 62351] -.-> MPLS
```

### 4.2 Protocol Stack Comparison for WAMS Data Delivery

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK1[Legacy SCADA Stack]
        A1[Application DNP3 or IEC 60870-5-104] --> B1[Transport TCP] --> C1[Network IP] --> D1[Link Ethernet] --> E1[Physical Copper or Fiber]
    end
    subgraph STK2[Modern WAMS Stack Option A]
        A2[Application IEEE C37.118.2] --> B2[Transport UDP for speed or TCP for reliability] --> C2[Network IP] --> D2[Link Ethernet PRP or HSR] --> E2[Physical Fiber or MPLS]
    end
    subgraph STK3[Modern WAMS Stack Option B]
        A3[Application IEC 61850-90-5 Routed GOOSE or SV] --> B3[Transport UDP multicast] --> C3[Network IP] --> D3[Link Ethernet] --> E3[Physical Fiber]
    end
    subgraph STK4[Future 5G WAMS Stack]
        A4[Application IEEE C37.118 over 5G] --> B4[Transport 5G URLLC Layer] --> C4[Network 3GPP Core] --> D4[Link 5G NR Radio] --> E4[Physical Wireless]
    end
```

### 4.3 Latency Budget Flow (Mermaid Sankey-style Allocation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[PMU Sample 0 ms] --> P1[PMU Processing 5 ms]
    P1 --> P2[Tx Queue and Serialize 2 ms]
    P2 --> P3[WAN Propagation 15 ms]
    P3 --> P4[PDC Processing 5 ms]
    P4 --> P5[Application Reaction 8 ms]
    P5 --> E[Control Output 35 ms total]
    style E fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#f57c00
```

### 4.4 Pillar-to-Requirement Cross-Reference (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[WAMS Communication Needs] --> B1[High Throughput]
    A --> B2[Low Latency]
    A --> B3[Time Sync]
    A --> B4[Reliability]
    A --> B5[Security]

    B1 --> C1[Frame Size and Reporting Rate]
    B1 --> C2[WAN Bandwidth Allocation]

    B2 --> D1[Sub 50 ms End to End]
    B2 --> D2[Deterministic Switching]

    B3 --> E1[GPS 1 PPS Disciplining]
    B3 --> E2[IEEE 1588 PTP Backup]

    B4 --> F1[PRP and HSR Redundancy]
    B4 --> F2[MPLS Fast Reroute]

    B5 --> G1[IEC 62351 Encryption]
    B5 --> G2[NERC CIP Access Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer sheets, examiners appreciate a **single-page functional block diagram** that maps the four pillars to specific technologies — it instantly signals syllabus mastery and earns 2–3 extra method marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the four fundamental communication requirements of a WAMS and briefly justify each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High data throughput** — PMUs stream continuous phasor frames (e.g., 50 fps × ~64 bytes) that must be delivered in real time.
2. **Deterministic low latency** — control and protection applications require end-to-end delay below 50 ms; conventional SCADA latency of seconds is unacceptable.
3. **Time synchronization** — phasor angles measured at different substations must be aligned to UTC within ±1 µs using GPS/GNSS discipline.
4. **Reliability, redundancy and cybersecurity** — communication failure or attack may cause grid instability; hence PRP/HSR redundancy and IEC 62351 encryption are mandated.

*(Award 0.75 marks per pillar, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the IEEE C37.118.2 standard reporting rates for a 50 Hz system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to IEEE Std C37.118.2, the PMU nominal reporting rates for a **50 Hz** power system are:

$$
f_{rep} \in \{10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 100\} \text{ frames per second}
$$

These correspond to sampling densities of 10, 25, 50, and 100 samples per nominal cycle. The 100 fps rate is the highest mandatory rate and is typically used for **oscillation monitoring and wide-area control**, whereas 25–50 fps suffices for **frequency stability assessment**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Bandwidth and Latency Analysis of WAMS*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A PMU monitors a three-phase line and reports **6 phasors, 4 analog, 4 digital** channels at **50 fps** in a **50 Hz** system using IEEE C37.118.2 protocol with **4-byte floating-point** phasor format. Compute the **per-PMU data rate** (payload only and on-wire with UDP/IP/Ethernet overhead). [7 marks]

**(b)** If the utility has **400 PMUs** connected over an MPLS backbone and must keep **end-to-end latency below 50 ms**, design the communication architecture in terms of (i) bandwidth sizing, (ii) latency budget allocation, and (iii) synchronization strategy. Justify each design choice. [7 marks]

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Per-PMU data rate (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Fixed overhead per frame (SYNC + FRAMESIZE + IDCODE + SOC + FRACSEC + STAT + CHK):

$$
L_{fix} = 2 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 4 + 2 + 2 = 18 \text{ bytes}
$$

Step 2 — Phasor payload: 6 phasors × 8 bytes (polar) = 48 bytes.

Step 3 — Analog + digital payload: $4 \times 2 + 4 \times 2 = 16$ bytes.

Step 4 — Total payload:

$$
L_{payload} = 18 + 48 + 16 = 82 \text{ bytes}
$$

Step 5 — Data rate (payload only):

$$
R_{p} = 50 \times 82 \times 8 = 32{,}800 \text{ bps} \approx 32.8 \text{ kbps}
$$

Step 6 — On-wire overhead = UDP(8) + IP(20) + Ethernet(14 + 22) = 64 bytes.

$$
L_{wire} = 82 + 64 = 146 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
R_{wire} = 50 \times 146 \times 8 = 58{,}400 \text{ bps} \approx 58.4 \text{ kbps}
$$

Step 7 — Per-PMU on-wire = **58.4 kbps** (1 mark for final, 6 marks for steps).

---

**(b) Architecture design (7 marks):**

**(i) Bandwidth sizing (2 marks):**

$$
R_{agg} = 400 \times 58.4 \text{ kbps} = 23.36 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Recommend a **dedicated 100 Mbps MPLS link** with **30 % headroom** for future PMU additions and protocol overhead. The 100/23.36 ≈ 4.3× margin ensures no congestion even at peak reporting.

**(ii) Latency budget (3 marks):**

| Stage | Allocation | Justification |
|-------|------------|---------------|
| PMU processing | 5 ms | DFT window and estimation |
| Tx queue + serialize | 2 ms | At 100 Mbps, 146 bytes ≈ 0.012 ms |
| WAN propagation | 20 ms | Fiber for 4,000 km (worst case) |
| PDC processing | 5 ms | Sort, align, de-duplicate |
| Application | 8 ms | Visualization and alarm |
| **Margin** | 10 ms | Jitter and retransmission buffer |

**(iii) Synchronization strategy (2 marks):**

- **Primary:** GPS L1/L2 disciplined oven-controlled crystal oscillator (OCXO) at every PMU; 1-PPS accuracy ±100 ns; yields TVE < 1 %.
- **Backup:** IEEE 1588 PTP over the MPLS backbone, with transparent clocks at every switch, providing sub-µs accuracy if GPS is jammed.
- **Redundancy:** dual GPS receivers per substation with automatic switchover < 1 s.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Protocols, Standards & Cybersecurity of WAMS*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **IEEE C37.118.2** and **IEC 61850-90-5** protocols for WAMS data exchange. Discuss message types, transport (TCP vs UDP), and interoperability. [7 marks]

**(b)** Explain the **cybersecurity requirements** of a WAMS communication network as per **IEC 62351** and **NERC-CIP**. List at least four specific controls and their engineering implementation. [7 marks]

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) IEEE C37.118.2 vs IEC 61850-90-5 (7 marks):**

| Feature | IEEE C37.118.2 | IEC 61850-90-5 |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| **Origin** | IEEE PSRC, synchrophasor community | IEC TC 57, substation automation |
| **Primary data unit** | Phasor + freq + dfreq + analog + digital | Routed-Sampled-Values (R-SV) + GOOSE over UDP/IP |
| **Transport** | UDP (default) or TCP | UDP multicast (sampled values) and UDP unicast (GOOSE) |
| **Time sync** | Explicit SOC + FRACSEC field | Implicit via IEC 61850-7-2 timestamp |
| **Data rate** | 25–120 fps per PMU | Up to 4,800 fps (4,800 samples/s) for R-SV |
| **Interoperability** | Direct with PDC, EMS, WAMS server | Requires IED configuration tool (SCL files) |
| **Use case** | Wide-area monitoring, oscillation detection | Mixed substation + WAMS, process-bus modernization |
| **Pros** | Simple, lightweight, dedicated to PMUs | Reuses IEC 61850 ecosystem, higher rates |
| **Cons** | Limited metadata, no native multicast | Higher complexity, SCL engineering |

**Verdict (1 mark):** Utilities with legacy PMUs prefer C37.118.2; new digital-substation projects adopt IEC 61850-90-5 to integrate process bus and WAMS in a single stack.

---

**(b) Cybersecurity controls per IEC 62351 / NERC-CIP (7 marks):**

1. **Authentication (IEC 62351-9 / NERC-CIP-005):** Use **X.509 certificates** and **TLS** for all PMU-PDC and PDC-control-center traffic. Each PMU has a unique certificate signed by the utility's Certificate Authority. *Engineering:* deploy Hardware Security Modules (HSM) at PDCs for private-key storage.

2. **Encryption (IEC 62351-6):** Apply **TLS 1.3 / IPsec** to all WAMS packets in transit. *Engineering:* MPLS backbone is logically private, but with end-to-end IPsec tunnel for defense in depth.

3. **Access control and RBAC (NERC-CIP-004 / -007):** Role-Based Access Control at the WAMS server; only authorized operators can change PDC configuration. *Engineering:* integrate with enterprise Active Directory and log all changes to SIEM.

4. **Intrusion detection (NERC-CIP-007-6 R4):** Deploy network IDS (e.g., Snort, Suricata) tuned for **C37.118 signatures** at each PDC ingress. *Engineering:* alert on anomalous reporting rate or rogue IDCODE.

5. **Time-source authentication (IEC 62351-9):** Protect GPS receivers against **spoofing/jamming** using CRPA antennas and multi-constellation GNSS receivers.

6. **Patch and vulnerability management (NERC-CIP-007 R2):** Quarterly firmware audits on every PMU; CVE-tracked software BOM.

7. **Audit and logging (IEC 62351-14):** Tamper-evident logs of every configuration change and PMU-PDC session; 90-day retention minimum.

*(Award 1 mark per control, 1 mark for engineering implementation link.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 2–4 marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "latency" with "bandwidth":** A high-bandwidth link (e.g., 1 Gbps) does NOT guarantee low latency. WAMS needs both.
> 2. **Forgetting to add protocol overhead:** Students often report the *payload* rate as the "data rate" — examiners expect you to add UDP (8) + IP (20) + Ethernet (36) = 64 bytes.
> 3. **Mixing up TVE formula:** Use $TVE = \frac{\vert \hat{X}_n - X_n \vert}{\vert X_n \vert}$, not the angle-difference formula only.
> 4. **Ignoring the GPS-to-PMU discipline path:** A correct synchronization strategy is incomplete without mentioning 1-PPS, OCXO, and PTP backup.
> 5. **Sketching the diagram but not labelling latency components:** A flowchart without numerical budgets loses 2 method marks in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **WAMS = PMUs + PDCs + WAN + Control Center** working in real time.
- **Four communication pillars:** throughput, latency, time-sync, security.
- **Reporting rates (50 Hz):** 10, 25, 50, 100 fps per IEEE C37.118.2.
- **Per-PMU bandwidth** ≈ 30–60 kbps on-wire; 500 PMUs ≈ 30 Mbps aggregate.
- **Latency budget:** 5 ms PMU + ≤ 20 ms WAN + 5 ms PDC + 5–10 ms app ≤ 50 ms.
- **Maximum reach** in fiber for 50 ms: ~**4,090 km** one-way → necessitates **hierarchical PDCs**.
- **TVE ≤ 1 %** ⇒ timing error ≤ 31.8 µs at 50 Hz ⇒ GPS disciplined to ±1 µs.
- **Synchronization:** GPS 1-PPS primary, IEEE 1588 PTP backup.
- **Redundancy protocols:** PRP and HSR; network recovery < 50 ms.
- **Cybersecurity:** IEC 62351 + NERC-CIP; TLS, RBAC, IDS, GPS anti-spoofing.
- **Protocol choice:** IEEE C37.118.2 (simple, PMU-native) vs IEC 61850-90-5 (integrated substation + WAMS).
- **Future:** 5G URLLC and IEC/IEEE 60255-118-1:2018 unified standard.
- **Key real-world reference:** India's **URTDSM** project — 1,184 PMUs on MPLS-VPN.
- **Design mantra:** *Bandwidth is cheap, latency is precious, synchronization is non-negotiable, security is mandatory.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remedial Action Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Remedial Action Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Remedial Action Scheme (RAS)** is a pre-engineered, automated, and deterministic control mechanism deployed within an electric power system that detects predefined abnormal system conditions and takes pre-planned, coordinated corrective actions—distinct from the routine, localized actions of conventional protection systems—to preserve system integrity, prevent cascading outages, and maintain operational reliability. 

According to the **North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC)** and **IEEE C37.2** device number definitions, a RAS is classified under **function number 33** and is often referred to as a **Special Protection Scheme (SPS)** in the Western Interconnection of North America, or as an **Automatic Scheme** under the Indian grid context governed by the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)** Grid Standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A RAS is *not* a replacement for primary protection. It is a **layered defense mechanism** that operates when primary protection cannot, by itself, prevent an abnormal operating condition from escalating into a system-wide disturbance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Circuit Breaker" of the Highway

Imagine a busy **six-lane highway** (the power grid) where traffic normally flows smoothly.

- **Primary Protection** is like a **speed bump** at a toll booth — it slows down individual vehicles (local faults) but does not handle a major pile-up.
- **Remedial Action Scheme** is like a **smart traffic management control room**. When sensors detect a major accident ahead, the control room *simultaneously*:
  1. **Diverts** incoming vehicles to alternate routes (re-routing power flow).
  2. **Stops** additional vehicles from entering the highway (load shedding).
  3. **Opens** specific ramps to relieve congestion (tripping of lines/transformers).

The control room doesn't fix the accident itself — the accident is handled by emergency crews (primary protection). It simply prevents the accident from creating a **city-wide traffic jam** (cascading blackout).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A RAS is a "smart" emergency coordinator that watches the entire grid, anticipates trouble, and takes pre-decided actions faster than any human operator could — all within **milliseconds to a few seconds**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Arming Condition** | The pre-conditions (e.g., line outage, high loading) that must exist for the RAS to be "ready" to act. |
| **Triggering Event** | The actual initiating event (e.g., a fault, frequency deviation) that activates the RAS. |
| **Pre-Planned Action** | A predetermined corrective measure (load shedding, generation tripping, etc.) |
| **Coordination Time** | Typically **100 ms to 5 s**, much slower than primary protection (≤ 80 ms). |
| **Misoperation** | Failure of the RAS to operate (failure-to-operate) or operating when not required (false operation). |

## 1.4 Physical & Engineering Constants in RAS Design

- **Frequency deviation threshold:** typically **49.5 Hz – 50.2 Hz** for the Indian 50 Hz grid.
- **Voltage deviation threshold:** typically **0.9 p.u. – 1.1 p.u.**
- **Operating time window:** **300 ms – 3 s** for most schemes.
- **N-1 / N-2 contingency** is the design baseline.
- **System Operating Limit (SOL)** and **Interconnection Reliability Operating Limit (IROL)** are the boundaries RAS enforces.

> [!TIP]
> Remember the acronym **"ATR"** for any RAS: **A**rm, **T**rigger, **R**espond. Every scheme follows this three-stage logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Architecture of a RAS

A RAS consists of four distinct functional blocks, each serving a critical role:

1. **Measurement / Sensing Block**
   - Continuously monitors system parameters: voltage, frequency, line loading, angle, bus status.
   - Sensors include **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)**, Remote Terminal Units (RTUs), and Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs).
   - In modern smart grids, **Wide Area Measurement Systems (WAMS)** provide synchronized data using **IEEE C37.118** protocol.

2. **Logic / Decision Block**
   - The "brain" of the RAS — typically a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** or a **dedicated microprocessor-based controller**.
   - Executes pre-programmed Boolean logic: `IF (arming condition) AND (trigger), THEN (action)`.
   - In advanced implementations, this can be hosted on a **phasor data concentrator (PDC)** platform.

3. **Communication Block**
   - Carries control signals between sensors, the logic processor, and actuators.
   - May use **SCADA**, **IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging**, **fiber-optic links**, or **microwave communication**.

4. **Actuation Block**
   - Performs the physical corrective action: opening a circuit breaker, shedding load, ramping down a generator.
   - Outputs are typically hardwired to **trip coils** of high-voltage circuit breakers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the architecture of RAS," always draw a **four-block functional diagram**: Sensors → Logic → Communication → Actuators. This is the universally accepted KTU valuation answer.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Classification of RAS

RAS schemes are classified by their **scope of action** and the **type of event** they mitigate.

### 2.2.1 Event-Based Classification

| Type | Triggering Event | Typical Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Generation Rejection Scheme (GRS)** | Loss of a large generator or import corridor | Rapidly trip pre-selected generators |
| **Load Rejection / Load Shedding Scheme (LRS / UFLS)** | Under-frequency, under-voltage, or tie-line overload | Disconnect pre-selected feeders/loads |
| **Under-Frequency Load Shedding (UFLS)** | Frequency drops below threshold (e.g., 49.4 Hz) | Step-wise load disconnection |
| **Under-Voltage Load Shedding (UVLS)** | Voltage collapse imminent | Manual/automatic load shedding |
| **Tie-Line / Corridor Protection** | Overload on interconnecting lines | Trip generation or shift load |
| **Controlled Islanding Scheme** | Imminent system separation | Deliberately split grid into self-sustaining islands |
| **Out-of-Step (OOS) Tripping** | Loss of synchronism detected | Separate system at pre-selected points |
| **Cross-Trip Scheme** | Fault on a critical line | Trip the remote-end breaker as well |

### 2.2.2 Geographic Scope Classification

- **Local RAS** — operates within a single substation (e.g., a transformer overload trip).
- **Wide-Area RAS** — spans multiple substations; uses **PMU/WAMS** data and **fiber/microwave** communication.

## 2.3 Detailed Operational Logic — Step by Step

A RAS does **not** simply "react." It goes through a strict logical sequence:

1. **Continuous Monitoring** — The system reads real-time values $V_i(t)$, $f_i(t)$, $P_{line,i}(t)$ from all monitored buses.
2. **Arming Verification** — Check whether the *current* system topology matches an "armed" condition. For example, `IF Line_2 is OUT OF SERVICE, THEN arm RAS_1`.
3. **Trigger Detection** — Detect whether a triggering event has occurred. For example, `IF (V_bus < 0.85 p.u.) AND (RAS_1 is ARMED), THEN TRIGGER`.
4. **Decision Execution** — Run the logic that decides *what* to do. For example, `THEN trip Generator_G1, shed 200 MW of Load_L1`.
5. **Command Dispatch** — Send a hardwired or GOOSE-messaged trip command to the relevant breakers.
6. **Post-Action Verification** — Confirm via auxiliary contacts that the action was completed. If not, escalate to a backup scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability Principle:** Every RAS action must have a **backup** (secondary logic) — this is a non-negotiable KTU design principle.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state frequency deviation | $\Delta f = f_{actual} - f_{nominal}$ | Hz | UFLS threshold |
| Per-unit voltage | $V_{pu} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{base}}$ | p.u. | UVLS threshold |
| Line loading | $S_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot I_{line}$ | MVA | Corridor overload |
| Real power balance | $\sum P_{gen} = \sum P_{load} + P_{loss}$ | MW | Generation rejection logic |
| Inertia constant (governor response) | $P_{acc} = 2H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}$ | MW | Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF) |
| RoCoF trigger | $\dfrac{df}{dt} \vert > 0.5 \ \mathrm{Hz/s}$ | Hz/s | Inertia-based load shedding |
| Tie-line power deviation | $\Delta P_{tie} = P_{scheduled} - P_{actual}$ | MW | Cross-border RAS |
| Energy not supplied (reliability metric) | $E_{ENS} = \int_{0}^{T} P_{shed}(t) \, dt$ | MWh | RAS performance evaluation |
| Expected outage cost | $C_{out} = E_{ENS} \cdot VOLL$ | ₹/event | Economic justification |
| Composite reliability index | $SAIDI = \dfrac{\sum U_i \cdot N_i}{N_{total}}$ | min/cust·yr | Scheme effectiveness |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark answers, when you write the formula, always write **two lines below it**: (1) what each variable means, and (2) the typical numerical range. Examiners reward definitions generously.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Indian Context:** The **POSOCO (now Grid-India)** operates several RAS schemes, including the **Northern Region** islanding scheme and the **SR (Southern Region)** UFLS scheme designed in stages (49.4, 49.2, 49.0 Hz).
- **Global Context:** The **August 2003 North-East Blackout (USA)** and **July 2012 India Blackout** prompted major investments in RAS to prevent recurrence.
- **Smart Grid Integration:** Modern RAS leverages **AI/ML-based contingency screening** and **synchrophasor data** for predictive arming — a key feature of the KTU Smart Grid syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Load Shedding for Under-Frequency Event

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Numerical Type):**
A 50 Hz grid has a total connected load of $P_L = 4000$ MW. A sudden loss of generation $P_{loss} = 500$ MW occurs. The load damping coefficient is $D = 1.5\% \text{ per Hz}$. Determine the **amount of load to be shed** by a UFLS scheme to restore the frequency to a target value of $f_{target} = 49.0$ Hz.

### Step 1: Identify the Steady-State Frequency Drop

The steady-state frequency deviation in a power system, when generation is lost and damping is neglected initially, is governed by the swing equation simplified form:

$$
\Delta f_{ss} = -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot P_L + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{R_i} \cdot P_{gi}}
$$

For a **first-pass estimate** without governor response, simplify to:

$$
\Delta f_{ss} \approx -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot P_L}
$$

### Step 2: Plug in the Numerical Values

Given:
- $P_{loss} = 500$ MW
- $P_L = 4000$ MW
- $D = 1.5\% = 0.015$ per unit

$$
\Delta f_{ss} = -\dfrac{500}{0.015 \times 4000}
$$

$$
\Delta f_{ss} = -\dfrac{500}{60} = -8.333 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Wait — this is unrealistically large.** This shows that *without* any load-shedding, the system would collapse. This is the **trigger condition** for the RAS.

### Step 3: Set the Target Post-Shed Frequency

We want the post-RAS frequency to be $f_{target} = 49.0$ Hz, so:

$$
\Delta f_{desired} = f_{target} - f_{nominal} = 49.0 - 50.0 = -1.0 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Required Load Shedding

Using the corrected equation that includes the shed load $P_{shed}$:

$$
\Delta f_{desired} = -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot (P_L - P_{shed})}
$$

Solving for $P_{shed}$:

$$
D \cdot (P_L - P_{shed}) = -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{\Delta f_{desired}}
$$

$$
P_L - P_{shed} = -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot \Delta f_{desired}}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = P_L + \dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot \Delta f_{desired}}
$$

### Step 5: Substituting the Numerical Values

$$
P_{shed} = 4000 + \dfrac{500}{0.015 \times (-1.0)}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = 4000 + \dfrac{500}{-0.015}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = 4000 - 33333.3
$$

This negative result tells us the system is **non-recoverable** with UFLS alone at $D = 0.015$. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why UFLS is **stage-based** and combined with **under-voltage load shedding** in real grids. A single 500 MW loss in a 4000 MW system is an **N-1 contingency that UFLS can handle**, but only if the damping is higher ($D \approx 4\text{–}5\%$).

### Step 6: Re-do with Realistic Damping ($D = 4\% = 0.04$)

$$
P_{shed} = 4000 + \dfrac{500}{0.04 \times (-1.0)}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = 4000 - 12500 = -8500 \text{ MW}
$$

**Still negative.** This means $D$ alone cannot save us — we need to **add** generator response.

### Step 7: Add Governor Droop Response (Generalized Equation)

In the modern grid with primary frequency response, total effective power injection after disturbance is:

$$
P_{eff} = P_{loss} - \Delta P_{gov} - P_{shed}
$$

where the governor contribution is:

$$
\Delta P_{gov} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta f}{R_i} \cdot P_{gi,nom}
$$

Assuming $n = 5$ identical generators each of 200 MW with droop $R = 5\% = 0.05$:

$$
\Delta P_{gov} = 5 \times \dfrac{-1.0}{0.05} \times 200 = 5 \times (-20) \times 200
$$

$$
\Delta P_{gov} = -20000 \text{ MW (i.e., 20 GW of power injection capability from governors)}
$$

This is clearly enough. The remaining $P_{shed} = 0$ — the **governors alone can save the system at 1 Hz drop**.

### Step 8: The Correct RAS Design Equation

The **actual KTU-grade** formula for a UFLS scheme:

$$
P_{shed}^{stage,k} = \alpha_k \cdot P_{L,total} \cdot \dfrac{(f_0 - f_{trip,k})}{f_0}
$$

where:
- $\alpha_k$ = percentage of total load shed in stage $k$ (e.g., 10% in stage 1)
- $f_{trip,k}$ = frequency trigger for stage $k$
- $f_0$ = nominal frequency

For a 3-stage Indian UFLS scheme with $\alpha = 10\%$, $f_{trip} = 49.4$ Hz:

$$
P_{shed}^{stage,1} = 0.10 \times 4000 \times \dfrac{(50 - 49.4)}{50}
$$

$$
P_{shed}^{stage,1} = 0.10 \times 4000 \times 0.012 = 4.8 \text{ MW}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This explains the design logic:** Each stage sheds a small portion of load with a small frequency deviation, ensuring that *if the first stage isn't enough*, the second stage (at 49.2 Hz) trips, and so on.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Triggering Time Calculation for a Generator Rejection Scheme

**Given:**
- A 1000 MW generator trips.
- A RAS must reduce the loading on a 765 kV line from $S_1 = 1200$ MVA to $S_2 = 950$ MVA (its thermal limit).
- Two parallel 500 MW generators in the same complex can be modulated; their ramp rate is $R = 50$ MW/min.

**Step 1: Compute load reduction needed**

$$
\Delta P_{need} = S_1 - S_2 = 1200 - 950 = 250 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2: Compute ramp time**

$$
t_{ramp} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{need}}{R} = \dfrac{250}{50} = 5 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3: Verify this is within RAS operating window**

RAS operating window is typically **< 5 seconds**. Five minutes is far too slow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Generation rejection must be **discrete tripping** (full units off), not ramping. This example shows why a RAS uses **trip signals**, not **ramp commands**.

**Step 4: Choose discrete action**

Trip one 250 MW unit instead of ramping → instant action within **100 ms** post-trigger.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Remedial Action Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensA[PMU and RTU Sensors] --> logA[RAS Logic Controller]
    sensB[IED with Frequency and Voltage Inputs] --> logA
    logA --> commA[SCADA IEC 61850 GOOSE Channel]
    logA --> commB[Fiber Optic Backup Link]
    commA --> actA[Trip Coil of Generator Breaker]
    commB --> actB[Trip Coil of Load Feeder Breaker]
    commA --> actC[Substation Bus Coupler Trip]
    actA --> verA[Auxiliary Contact Verification]
    actB --> verA
    actC --> verA
    verA --> repA[SCADA Event Log and Operator HMI]
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic Flowchart of UFLS Stage-Based Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Grid Frequency Monitoring Starts] --> checkA{Is Frequency Below 49.4 Hz}
    checkA -- No --> monA[Continue Monitoring]
    checkA -- Yes --> stage1[Stage 1 Trip: Shed 10 percent of Load]
    stage1 --> checkB{Frequency Recovered to Above 49.5 Hz}
    checkB -- Yes --> okA[Stop RAS and Log Event]
    checkB -- No --> checkC{Is Frequency Below 49.2 Hz}
    checkC -- No --> monA
    checkC -- Yes --> stage2[Stage 2 Trip: Shed Additional 10 percent of Load]
    stage2 --> checkD{Frequency Below 49.0 Hz}
    checkD -- No --> okA
    checkD -- Yes --> stage3[Stage 3 Trip: Shed Additional 10 percent of Load and Alert Operator]
    stage3 --> emergA[Emergency Islanding Activation if Frequency Below 48.5 Hz]
```

## 4.3 Wide-Area RAS Architecture in Smart Grid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph subA[Substation Layer]
        pmuA[PMU at Bus A] --> pdcA[Local Phasor Data Concentrator]
        pmuB[PMU at Bus B] --> pdcA
        pmuC[PMU at Bus C] --> pdcA
    end
    subgraph subB[Control Center Layer]
        pdcA --> wamsA[Wide Area Measurement System Server]
        wamsA --> rasLogic[Central RAS Logic Engine]
        rasLogic --> dbA[Contingency Database]
        dbA --> rasLogic
    end
    subgraph subC[Actuation Layer]
        rasLogic -- Fiber --> trip1[Generator G1 Trip Command]
        rasLogic -- Fiber --> trip2[Feeder F1 Trip Command]
        rasLogic -- Microwave --> trip3[Substation S2 Bus Trip Command]
    end
```

## 4.4 RAS vs. Conventional Protection — Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Conventional Protection] -->|Local and Sub 100 ms| a1[Isolates Faulty Equipment Only]
    p2[Remedial Action Scheme] -->|Wide Area and 0.3 to 5 s| a2[Prevents System Wide Cascading Failure]
    a1 --> outA[No System Stability Guarantee]
    a2 --> outB[System Stability and SOL Preserved]
```

## 4.5 Hierarchical Position of RAS in Power System Defense Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layer1[Layer 1: Primary Protection - 50 to 100 ms - Local Fault Clearing]
    layer2[Layer 2: Remedial Action Scheme - 0.3 to 5 s - Wide Area Stability]
    layer3[Layer 3: Automatic Generation Control - Seconds to Minutes - Economic Dispatch]
    layer4[Layer 4: Operator Manual Intervention - Minutes to Hours - Restoration]
    layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question is 3 marks. Answers should be **3–5 crisp sentences** with a diagram where applicable.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Remedial Action Scheme (RAS). How is it different from a conventional protection scheme?**

**Model Answer:**
A Remedial Action Scheme (RAS) is a pre-engineered, automatic control system that detects abnormal conditions in the power grid and initiates pre-planned corrective actions—such as generator tripping, load shedding, or controlled islanding—to prevent cascading failures.

Unlike conventional protection schemes, which are **localized** and **fast-acting** (operating within 50–100 ms to isolate only the faulty element), a RAS is **wide-area** in scope and operates on a **slower timescale (0.3 to 5 s)** to maintain overall system stability rather than just clear a fault.

Conventional protection is **event-driven** (responds to a fault), whereas a RAS is **condition-driven** (responds to a system state such as overload or low frequency).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any four types of Remedial Action Schemes and state the condition under which each is triggered.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Type of RAS | Triggering Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Generation Rejection Scheme** | Sudden loss of a large in-feed or critical generator |
| 2 | **Under-Frequency Load Shedding (UFLS)** | Grid frequency falls below 49.4 Hz (stage-wise) |
| 3 | **Under-Voltage Load Shedding (UVLS)** | Bus voltage drops below 0.85 p.u. |
| 4 | **Controlled Islanding** | Imminent system separation detected via angle/frequency divergence |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Provide diagrams, equations, and conclusion for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the functional architecture of a Remedial Action Scheme. Describe each block in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A RAS is implemented as a closed-loop control system with four functional blocks:

**Block 1 — Measurement / Sensing Layer:**
- Devices: **PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units)**, **RTUs**, **IEDs**.
- Function: Measure real-time voltage $V$, frequency $f$, line current $I$, and breaker status.
- *Example:* PMUs at 400 kV substations report voltage angle and frequency to a control center every 20–50 ms (synchrophasor data per IEEE C37.118).

**Block 2 — Logic / Decision Layer:**
- Device: **Dedicated PLC or microprocessor-based controller** with redundancy.
- Function: Executes Boolean logic. For example:
  - `IF (Line_AB is OUT) AND (Line_BC loading > 0.9 p.u.) THEN (TRIP Generator G1 AND shed Load L1)`.
- *Example:* A 3-stage UFLS logic with trip frequencies 49.4, 49.2, 49.0 Hz.

**Block 3 — Communication Layer:**
- Channels: **IEC 61850 GOOSE** (substation), **fiber-optic** (inter-substation), **SCADA** (control center).
- Function: Transmit arming status, trigger signals, and verification.
- *Example:* Hardwired copper backup for the GOOSE channel in critical RAS.

**Block 4 — Actuation Layer:**
- Devices: **Trip coils of HV circuit breakers**, **annunciators**.
- Function: Physically disconnect the generator, feeder, or transformer.
- *Example:* Trip coil of a 220 kV feeder breaker shedding 100 MW of load in 80 ms.

**Conclusion:** This four-block architecture ensures **Sensing → Thinking → Talking → Acting** in a coordinated, fail-safe manner.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Under-Frequency Load Shedding (UFLS) scheme. A 50 Hz system has a total load of 5000 MW. A generation loss of 750 MW occurs. Using a damping coefficient $D = 4\%$ per Hz, determine the **load to be shed** if the target post-shed frequency is 49.0 Hz. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of UFLS:**

The UFLS scheme consists of: (i) Frequency relays at every feeder, (ii) Step-wise trip settings at 49.4, 49.2, 49.0 Hz, (iii) Each step sheds a fixed % of total load (e.g., 10% per stage), (iv) Backup logic in the central controller.

**Mathematical Solution:**

Given:
- $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $P_L = 5000$ MW
- $P_{loss} = 750$ MW
- $D = 0.04$ per Hz
- $f_{target} = 49.0$ Hz, so $\Delta f = -1.0$ Hz

Using the load-shedding equation:

$$
\Delta f = -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot (P_L - P_{shed})}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_L - P_{shed} = -\dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot \Delta f}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = P_L + \dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot \Delta f}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
P_{shed} = 5000 + \dfrac{750}{0.04 \times (-1.0)}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = 5000 - \dfrac{750}{0.04}
$$

$$
P_{shed} = 5000 - 18750 = -13750 \text{ MW}
$$

Since $P_{shed}$ cannot be negative, the **system is not stable** at $f_{target} = 49.0$ Hz with UFLS alone at this damping.

**Engineering Interpretation:**
This is the **classic signature of an N-2 contingency or a low-inertia system**. The solution is one of:

1. **Add a third stage** of UFLS at 48.8 Hz, and combine with **under-voltage load shedding**.
2. **Use generator fast valving** or **Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)** injection.
3. **Reduce $P_{loss}$** by tripping a smaller generator or shedding the lost unit's auxiliaries.

**Conclusion:** UFLS design must always include an **assessment of the worst credible contingency** and the system's actual damping constant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare the architecture, operating time, and application of a Remedial Action Scheme and a conventional protection scheme. List any three advantages and two limitations of RAS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Conventional Protection | Remedial Action Scheme |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Local (single equipment) | Wide-area (multiple substations) |
| **Operating time** | 50–100 ms | 0.3 to 5 s |
| **Trigger** | Fault current / impedance | System state (V, f, loading) |
| **Action** | Isolate faulted element | Trip generation / shed load / island |
| **Logic device** | Numerical relay | PLC / WAMS controller |
| **Standard** | IEEE C37.2 device no. 50/51 | IEEE C37.2 device no. 33 |

**Three Advantages of RAS:**
1. **Prevents cascading blackouts** by anticipating system-wide threats.
2. **Maximizes power transfer** by allowing the system to operate closer to its stability limits (i.e., higher utilization).
3. **Fast, deterministic, and auditable** — eliminates human delay in emergencies.

**Two Limitations of RAS:**
1. **Misoperation risk** — failure to operate or false operation can worsen the disturbance. Requires rigorous testing.
2. **Cost and complexity** — needs PMUs, dedicated communication, and periodic maintenance/review.

**Conclusion:** RAS is an *enabler* for modern grid operation; it does not replace protection but complements it as a wide-area, slower, corrective layer.

---

**(b)** Describe the concept of **Controlled Islanding** as a Remedial Action Scheme. What are the criteria for selecting the islanding cut-set? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Controlled Islanding is a RAS in which the grid is **deliberately split into self-sustaining electrical islands** before an unstable condition (such as loss of synchronism) can propagate through the system. Each island must have its own **load-generation balance** and sufficient **inertia** to survive transiently.

**Operational Sequence:**
1. **Detection:** Out-of-step relays or WAMS-based angle monitoring detects angle divergence.
2. **Decision:** Logic engine confirms that separation is imminent and unavoidable.
3. **Action:** Pre-selected breakers along an **optimal cut-set** open simultaneously.
4. **Stabilization:** Each island uses its own governor and AGC to stabilize frequency.

**Criteria for Selecting the Islanding Cut-Set:**
1. **Minimum power flow disruption** — the cut-set should carry the least MW flow under normal conditions to minimize transient.
2. **Generators and loads within each island must be balanced** — generation-load mismatch should be < 5–10%.
3. **Fewest number of breakers to open** — reduces misoperation probability and restoration time.
4. **Geographic and topological simplicity** — avoid splitting critical corridors.
5. **Self-healing capability** — each island must have black-start support.

**Example (KTU-style):**
In the **Indian Northern Region**, the cut-set typically includes 400 kV tie-lines between Punjab and Rajasthan, deliberately opening at Agra–Bhiwani and adjoining substations to form self-sufficient islands during grid stress.

**Conclusion:** Controlled islanding is the **last-resort RAS** — invoked only when all other schemes have failed. It is the grid's emergency parachute.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing RAS with Protection** — Many students write "RAS isolates the fault." Wrong. RAS acts *after* a fault, to maintain stability. A correct statement is "RAS is a wide-area corrective scheme acting on a slower timescale."
> 2. **Forgetting the Architecture Blocks** — A 7-mark architecture question *requires* the four blocks (Sensors, Logic, Communication, Actuation) explicitly named.
> 3. **No Diagram in Numerical Questions** — In Part B numericals, omitting a circuit or block diagram costs 2 marks minimum. Always include at least one figure.
> 4. **Wrong Unit for Frequency Deviation** — Use **Hz**, not % or p.u., for frequency.
> 5. **Skipping the Conclusion** — A 7-mark sub-part without a concluding sentence loses 1 mark. Always end with a one-line summary.
> 6. **Mixing UFLS and UVLS** — UFLS is for *frequency*; UVLS is for *voltage*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam!**

- [x] **RAS = Wide-area, slow (0.3–5 s), corrective scheme.**
- [x] **IEEE C37.2 Device No. 33** is the official designation.
- [x] **Four blocks:** Sensors → Logic → Communication → Actuators.
- [x] **Types:** GRS, UFLS, UVLS, Cross-trip, Islanding, Out-of-step.
- [x] **UFLS trigger frequencies in India:** 49.4 / 49.2 / 49.0 / 48.8 Hz.
- [x] **UVLS trigger voltage:** typically < 0.85 p.u.
- [x] **Key Formula:** $P_{shed} = P_L + \dfrac{P_{loss}}{D \cdot \Delta f}$
- [x] **RoCoF-based UFLS** is the modern approach: $\dfrac{df}{dt} > 0.5$ Hz/s.
- [x] **Controlled Islanding** uses **optimal cut-set** selection.
- [x] **PMU + WAMS** is the modern RAS sensing backbone (IEEE C37.118).
- [x] **RAS does NOT replace protection** — it is a *layered defense*.
- [x] **Misoperation** is the single biggest risk — must have redundancy and backup.
- [x] **Smart grid integration** = AI/ML-based contingency screening + synchrophasor-based RAS.
- [x] **Energy Not Supplied (ENS)** is the standard reliability metric for RAS evaluation.
- [x] **Remember the acronym ATR** — Arm, Trigger, Respond.
- [x] **Always mention N-1 / N-2 contingency** when justifying a RAS design.

> **End of Notes — Remedial Action Scheme | Module 2 | PEEET861**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Substations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Substation (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A **Smart Substation** is an automated, digitally monitored, and intelligently controlled high-voltage electrical substation that integrates **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)**, **substation automation systems (SAS)**, **synchronized phasor measurement units (PMUs)**, **digital sensors**, **fiber-optic communication networks**, and **advanced ICT infrastructure** compliant with the **IEC 61850** standard. It performs real-time **supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA)**, automated **protection**, **metering**, **fault recording**, and **remote asset management** with the goal of achieving **self-healing**, **interoperability**, **reliability**, and **efficiency** in power system operation.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Course Outcome alignment: **CO3 – Analyze the architecture and operation of smart grid subsystems**), a smart substation is positioned as the **"nerve center"** of the smart grid hierarchy. It is the critical interface between transmission/distribution networks and the utility control center.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box – The "Digital Hospital" Analogy**
> Imagine a **conventional substation** as an **old hospital with paper records**, walkie-talkies between doctors, and manual switches for every device. A **smart substation**, by contrast, is a **modern digital hospital** with:
> - **Smart sensors (IoT)** attached to every patient (transformer, breaker, CT/PT) continuously streaming vital signs (voltage, current, temperature).
> - **Electronic patient records (SCADA/DMS database)** accessible to all doctors (operators) in real time.
> - **Standardized communication protocol (IEC 61850)** so that monitors made by Philips, GE, or Siemens all "speak the same language" — like a universal medical record format.
> - **Automatic alarm & protection (IEDs)** that isolate a fault in milliseconds, just like an ICU auto-isolates a critical patient.
> - **Remote specialists (utility control center)** who can tele-diagnose and prescribe switching actions from kilometres away.

## 1.3 Key Functional Pillars of a Smart Substation

| Pillar | Function | Enabling Technology |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| **Sensing** | Real-time measurement of electrical & non-electrical parameters | **Non-conventional Instrument Transformers (NCITs)**, **MU (Merging Units)**, **Optical CTs/VTs** |
| **Communication** | High-speed, deterministic, peer-to-peer data exchange | **IEC 61850**, **GIGABIT ETHERNET**, **MMS, GOOSE, SV protocols**, **Fiber optics** |
| **Automation** | Self-acting protection, control & interlocking | **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)**, **Substation Automation System (SAS)** |
| **Protection** | Ultra-fast, coordinated fault clearance | **Numerical relays**, **Wide-Area Protection (WAPS)** |
| **Monitoring & Diagnostics** | Condition-based asset management | **PMUs**, **DFR/SER**, **DGA sensors**, **Thermal imaging** |
| **Cybersecurity** | Protection against cyber-attacks | **IEC 62351**, **NERC CIP**, **Firewalls, RBAC** |

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember**
> - **Standard Communication Protocol:** **IEC 61850** (published by **IEC TC 57**, current edition: **Edition 2.1 / 2.2**)
> - **Process Bus Sampling Rate:** **4,800 samples/sec** (80 samples/cycle on 60 Hz) for protection; **14,400 samples/sec** for metering
> - **GOOSE Message Latency:** **< 4 ms** (Type 1A – fast trip)
> - **Time Synchronization Standard:** **IEEE 1588 / IEC 61850-9-2 (PTP)** with **±1 μs** accuracy for PMU applications
> - **Substation Voltage Levels (Indian Context):** **11 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV**
> - **Maximum Substation DC Auxiliary Voltage:** **110 V DC / 220 V DC** (battery bank)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution from Conventional to Smart Substation

The transition from a **conventional substation** to a **smart substation** is not merely a hardware upgrade — it represents a paradigm shift from **analog, hard-wired, point-to-point control** to a **digitized, networked, protocol-driven architecture**.

### 2.1.1 Conventional Substation Architecture
- **Copper-wired** control cables from switchyard to control room (often 100s of cores per bay).
- **Electromechanical / Static Relays** operating on analog principles.
- **Single-function IEDs** — one relay per protection function (overcurrent, distance, differential each needing separate hardware).
- **No interoperability** — vendor lock-in.
- **Manual data logging** and **SCADA limited to telemetry**.

### 2.1.2 Smart Substation Architecture
- **Multifunction numerical IEDs** consolidating protection, control, metering, and communication.
- **Fiber-optic Process Bus** replacing copper secondary wiring (IEC 61850-9-2 SV).
- **Station Bus** for inter-IED and SCADA communication (MMS, GOOSE).
- **NCITs** with digital output (IEC 61850-9-2 Light).
- **Centralized or Distributed Busbar Protection**.
- **Condition Monitoring** integrated with **Asset Management Systems**.

## 2.2 IEC 61850 – The Backbone Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61850** is the **single most important concept** for KTU 2024 Smart Grid examinations. The standard is divided into **10 parts**, each prefixed with a topic (e.g., IEC 61850-3, IEC 61850-4 … IEC 61850-10).

### 2.2.1 The Three Core Pillars of IEC 61850

1. **Data Modeling (SCL – Substation Configuration Language)**
   - **SCL** is an **XML-based** language used to describe the **complete substation topology**, IED capabilities, and data flow.
   - Defined in **IEC 61850-6**.
   - File types: **SSD (System Specification Description)**, **ICD (IED Capability Description)**, **SCD (Substation Configuration Description)**, **IID (Instantiated IED Description)**, **CID (Configured IED Description)**.

2. **Communication Services (Abstract Services)**
   - **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification, ISO 9506)** – client-server for SCADA telemetry & configuration.
   - **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event)** – multicast, peer-to-peer, for fast trip/Interlock (latency < 4 ms).
   - **SV (Sampled Values)** – streaming of digitized CT/VT waveforms at 4,800 / 14,400 fps.

3. **Conformance & Interoperability Testing**
   - Defined in **IEC 61850-10**.
   - Ensures IEDs from different vendors interoperate seamlessly.

### 2.2.2 Logical Architecture of IEC 61850 (Three Levels)

| Level | Name | Function | Typical Devices |
|-------|------|----------|-----------------|
| **Level 0** | **Process Level** | Raw data acquisition | **CT, VT, NCIT, Merging Unit (MU), Actuators, Smart sensors** |
| **Level 1** | **Bay Level** | Protection, control, monitoring of a single bay | **Bay Control Unit (BCU)**, **Protection IED** |
| **Level 2** | **Station Level** | Substation-wide control, HMI, SCADA gateway | **Station HMI**, **SCADA Gateway (RTU/Protocol Converter)**, **Engineering Station** |
| **Level 3** | **Network/Enterprise Level** | Utility Control Center (UCC), DMS, EMS | **Energy Management System (EMS)**, **Distribution Management System (DMS)** |

> **Note:** Some texts combine Bay + Station into "Substation Level" and place Process as Level 0. The above is the **KTU-preferred 3-level model**.

## 2.3 Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)

An **IED** is a microprocessor-based device that performs **one or more** of the following: protection, control, metering, monitoring, and communication in an electrical substation.

### 2.3.1 Functional Building Blocks of an IED

- **Analog Input Module (AIM)** – signal conditioning & anti-aliasing filter.
- **A/D Converter** – typically **16-bit or 18-bit, ≥ 4.8 kHz sampling**.
- **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** – executes protection algorithms (Fourier, Walsh, Travelling Wave).
- **Microcontroller / CPU** – runs logic, communication stacks (IEC 61850 MMS, DNP3, Modbus).
- **Binary Input/Output (BIO)** – for status, trip, close commands.
- **Communication Ports** – Ethernet (copper/fiber), serial RS-485, IRIG-B for time sync.
- **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** – local LCD/LED panel.

### 2.3.2 Sampling Theorem Application (Nyquist Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **sampling rate** of the A/D converter in an IED or MU is governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**:
>
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
>
> For a power system with fundamental frequency **f₀ = 50 Hz** (India) and considering the **49th harmonic** for accurate metering (per IEC 61850-9-2):
>
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot 50 \cdot 49 = 4{,}900 \text{ samples/sec}$$

This is why **IEC 61850-9-2 SV** standardizes on **4,800 samples/sec @ 50 Hz** (80 samples/cycle) for protection, and **14,400 samples/sec** (240 samples/cycle) for high-accuracy metering.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | ≥ 4,800 Hz | SV design in Process Bus |
| 2 | GOOSE Trip Latency (Type 1A) | $t_{GOOSE} \leq 4$ ms | 3 – 4 ms | Protection inter-IED |
| 3 | SV Transmission Delay (per packet) | $t_{SV} \approx \frac{1}{f_s} \cdot N_{samples/packet}$ | 250 μs @ 80 sp/cycle | Process Bus latency budget |
| 4 | Total Substation Latency | $t_{total} = t_{MU} + t_{net} + t_{IED} + t_{CB}$ | 15 – 25 ms | Critical for Protection Coordination |
| 5 | PTP Time Sync Accuracy | $\sigma_t \leq 1 \ \mu s$ | μs to ns | PMU / Synchrophasor |
| 6 | Battery Sizing (Hours of Autonomy) | $C_{Ah} = \frac{P_{DC} \cdot T_{reserve}}{V_{DC} \cdot \eta_{inv}}$ | 8 – 10 hours | Station DC auxiliaries |
| 7 | Voltage Drop in Copper Control Cable | $\Delta V = I \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$ | < 5% | Conventional vs Fiber comparison |
| 8 | Optical Fiber Bandwidth–Length Product | $BL = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot BW \cdot \tau_{mod}}$ | MHz·km | Process Bus cabling |
| 9 | Synchrophasor Vector Magnitude | $\underline{X} = X_m \angle \theta$ | 110 V / 5 A nominal | PMU output |
| 10 | IEC 61850 Logical Node Count (typical 220 kV SS) | 50 – 150 LN per IED | ~ 100 LN | SCD file complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **GOOSE latency (4 ms)** with **end-to-end substation protection operating time (15–25 ms)**. GOOSE is only the *communication* leg; the breaker operating time (~ 2–3 cycles = 40–60 ms for 50 Hz) dominates in some schemes.

## 2.5 Substation Communication Network Topologies

| Topology | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage | KTU Use |
|----------|-------------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| **Star** | All IEDs connect to a central switch | Simple, easy fault isolation | Single point of failure at switch | Small 11/33 kV SS |
| **Ring (PRP/HSR)** | Dual counter-rotating fiber ring | Zero-loss redundancy | Complex, more cost | Critical 400/220 kV SS |
| **Mesh** | Multiple interconnects | Highest availability | Complex configuration | Future 765 kV |
| **Daisy-Chain** | Sequential connection | Minimum cable | Accumulated latency | Tap-offs, PMU chains |

> **PRP (Parallel Redundancy Protocol, IEC 62439-3)** and **HSR (High-availability Seamless Ring, IEC 62439-3)** are the **two key redundancy protocols** for IEC 61850 substations and are **favourite KTU questions**.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

Smart substations are deployed across India by **POWERGRID, NTPC, and state DISCOMs** in projects like the **"Unified Real-Time Dynamic State Measurement (URTDSM)"** scheme, where **1,184 PMUs** were installed at **400 kV and 765 kV substations**. They enable:
- **Real-time congestion management**.
- **Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)**.
- **Automated Demand Response (ADR)** integration.
- **Renewable integration** through **FACTS** coordinated control.
- **Reduced SAIDI / SAIFI** indices for DISCOMs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nyquist Sampling Rate for IEC 61850-9-2 SV

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A merging unit samples the secondary output of a 1100 V / $\sqrt{3}$ Voltage Transformer. To accurately measure up to the **49th harmonic** (as per IEC 61850-9-2), determine the minimum sampling frequency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum frequency of interest**

The highest harmonic considered is the **49th harmonic** of the fundamental power frequency. In India, the nominal fundamental frequency is $f_0 = 50$ Hz.

$$f_{max} = 49 \times f_0 = 49 \times 50 = 2{,}450 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**

To reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency must be **at least twice the maximum signal frequency**:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the value**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 2{,}450 = 4{,}900 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Choose the standardized IEC 61850 rate**

The IEC 61850-9-2 standard (and the UCA Implementation Guideline for SV) defines the **standardized rate of 4,800 samples/sec at 50 Hz**, which corresponds to **80 samples per fundamental cycle**:

$$f_s^{std} = 50 \times 80 = 4{,}000 \text{ Hz (per phase) per stream} \to \text{4,800 sps for 50 Hz with reservation}$$

> **Final Answer:** The minimum required sampling rate is **4,900 Hz**, and the standard prescribes **80 samples/cycle (4,000 sps nominal, 4,800 sps effective)** for protection-class SV streams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Trip Command Latency Budget

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A numerical distance relay (IED-A) detects a Zone-1 fault and sends a GOOSE trip message to a Breaker Control IED (IED-B). Given the communication latency components, compute the **total operating time**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Decompose the latency budget**

The total operating time $t_{op}$ is the sum of four sequential components:

$$t_{op} = t_{detect} + t_{GOOSE} + t_{process} + t_{breaker}$$

**Step 2 — Insert standard values from KTU reference**

$$t_{detect} = 0.5 \text{ cycle} = 0.5 \times 20 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_{GOOSE} = 3 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(Type 1A, IEC 61850-5)}$$

$$t_{process} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(IED-B output contact processing)}$$

$$t_{breaker} = 2 \text{ cycles} = 40 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(typical 50 Hz SF₆ breaker)}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components**

$$t_{op} = 10 + 3 + 1 + 40 = 54 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with conventional architecture**

In a **hard-wired conventional substation**, $t_{GOOSE}$ is replaced by a **2 ms copper-cable propagation delay** (negligible), and the dominant term is still $t_{breaker} = 40$ ms. Hence, the IEC 61850 smart substation preserves the **same critical operating time envelope (~ 50–60 ms)** while providing the flexibility of **peer-to-peer, vendor-independent communication**.

> **Final Answer:** Total trip operating time = **54 ms** (well within the standard 80–100 ms critical clearing time for 220 kV systems).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Station Battery Sizing (Auxiliary DC Supply)

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A 220 kV smart substation has a connected DC load of **15 kW**. The battery bank voltage is **110 V DC**. The reserve time is **8 hours** and inverter efficiency is **85%**. Calculate the required battery capacity in **Ah**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the capacity formula**

The ampere-hour capacity of a lead-acid / VRLA battery bank is given by:

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{P_{DC} \cdot T_{reserve}}{V_{DC} \cdot \eta_{inv}}$$

where $P_{DC}$ is the connected DC load in watts, $T_{reserve}$ is the autonomy in hours, $V_{DC}$ is the battery bank nominal voltage, and $\eta_{inv}$ is the combined efficiency of charger + inverter (when AC loads are also supplied).

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{15{,}000 \text{ W} \times 8 \text{ h}}{110 \text{ V} \times 0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 15{,}000 \times 8 = 120{,}000 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 110 \times 0.85 = 93.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Final division**

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{120{,}000}{93.5} \approx 1{,}283.4 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 5 — Apply design margin (1.2×) for battery aging**

$$C_{Ah}^{design} = 1.2 \times 1{,}283.4 = 1{,}540.1 \text{ Ah} \approx 1{,}600 \text{ Ah}$$

> **Final Answer:** The battery bank must be rated at **≈ 1,600 Ah @ 110 V** for 8-hour autonomy (typical configuration: 2 × 800 Ah strings in parallel, each string of 55 cells × 2 V).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Smart vs Conventional Substation

| **Parameter** | **Conventional Substation** | **Smart Substation (IEC 61850)** | **Quantitative / Qualitative Impact** |
|---------------|-----------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Control Wiring** | ~ 30–50 km of copper cable/bay | < 5 km of fiber + Ethernet | **> 80% reduction in cabling** |
| **CT/VT** | Conventional iron-core, analog | NCIT (Optical/Rogowski) | **Wider dynamic range, no saturation** |
| **Data Refresh Rate** | ~ 2–4 sec (SCADA polling) | 1–2 cycles for protection, 40 ms for control | **> 50× faster** |
| **Time Sync** | Not synchronized | PTP / IEEE 1588, 1 μs | Enables PMU/WAMS |
| **Interoperability** | Vendor-locked | Open standard (IEC 61850) | **Lower LCC, multi-vendor bays** |
| **Asset Monitoring** | Periodic manual | Continuous (DGA, thermal, vibration) | **30% life extension of transformers** |
| **Fault Recording** | Standalone DFR | Distributed, IED-resident COMTRADE | **Faster root-cause analysis** |
| **Cybersecurity** | Air-gapped, physical | IEC 62351, RBAC, signed GOOSE | **Attack-surface increased; mitigation essential** |

## 3.5 Comparative Latency Stack (Analytical)

A **complete numerical example** for a Process Bus trip (MU → Switch → IED → GOOSE → BCU → Breaker):

$$t_{total} = \underbrace{0.250 \text{ ms}}_{t_{MU\,A/D}} + \underbrace{0.125 \text{ ms}}_{t_{net\,propagation}} + \underbrace{1 \text{ ms}}_{t_{IED\,process}} + \underbrace{3 \text{ ms}}_{t_{GOOSE}} + \underbrace{0.5 \text{ ms}}_{t_{BCU\,output}} + \underbrace{40 \text{ ms}}_{t_{breaker}}$$

$$t_{total} \approx 44.875 \text{ ms}$$

> **Note:** When deploying **Process Bus (IEC 61850-9-2 SV)**, additional care is needed for **time-stamping accuracy** and **network load**. The total bandwidth per merging unit is approximately:

$$\text{BW}_{MU} = f_s \times N_{channels} \times N_{bits/sample} = 4{,}800 \times 4 \times 16 = 307{,}200 \text{ bps per MU} \approx 0.31 \text{ Mbps}$$

For an 8-bay substation with **2 MUs per bay (one for current, one for voltage)**, total Process Bus traffic $\approx 8 \times 2 \times 0.31 = 4.96$ Mbps — well within gigabit Ethernet capacity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smart Substation — Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEVEL0["LEVEL 0  PROCESS LEVEL"]
        CT["Conventional CT 1100A to 1A"]
        VT["Conventional VT 110 by sqrt 3 kV to 110 by 3"]
        NCIT["Non Conventional Instrument Transformer NCIT"]
        MU["Merging Unit MU IEC 61850 9 2 SV"]
        SA1["Smart Actuator Breaker Coil"]
    end

    subgraph LEVEL1["LEVEL 1  BAY LEVEL"]
        PIED["Protection IED Distance O C Differential"]
        BCU["Bay Control Unit BCU"]
        PMI["Phasor Measurement Unit PMU"]
        CMR1["Condition Monitoring Relay"]
    end

    subgraph LEVEL2["LEVEL 2  STATION LEVEL"]
        HMI["Station HMI Touchscreen"]
        GATE["SCADA Gateway IEC 61850 to DNP3 IEC 60870 5 104"]
        ENG["Engineering Station SCL Tool"]
        DC1["Station DC Battery 110 V"]
    end

    subgraph LEVEL3["LEVEL 3  ENTERPRISE LEVEL"]
        EMS["Utility EMS DMS WAMS"]
        NMS["Asset Management System"]
        CYBR["Cyber Security Module IEC 62351"]
    end

    PBUS(("Process Bus\nIEC 61850-9-2 SV"))
    SBUS(("Station Bus\nIEC 61850 MMS GOOSE"))

    CT --> MU
    VT --> MU
    NCIT --> MU
    MU --> PBUS
    PBUS --> PIED
    PBUS --> PMI
    PIED --> SBUS
    BCU --> SBUS
    CMR1 --> SBUS
    PMI --> SBUS
    SBUS --> HMI
    SBUS --> GATE
    SBUS --> ENG
    PIED --> SA1
    BCU --> SA1
    GATE --> EMS
    HMI --> NMS
    ENG --> NMS
    SBUS --> CYBR
    PBUS --> CYBR
    DC1 -.Aux Power.-> PIED
    DC1 -.Aux Power.-> BCU
    DC1 -.Aux Power.-> HMI
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** The architecture is built around the **Process Bus** (sampled values from MUs) and the **Station Bus** (MMS, GOOSE). Level 0 devices stream raw digitized waveforms; Level 1 IEDs perform protection and control; Level 2 aggregates data for SCADA/EMS; Level 3 connects to the wider utility enterprise. The **DC battery bank** is the lifeline for all electronic subsystems.

## 4.2 IEC 61850 Logical Node Hierarchy (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LND["Logical Device LD"] --> LNA["LNA Phase A Measurement"]
    LND --> LNB["LNB Phase B Measurement"]
    LND --> LNC["LNC Phase C Measurement"]
    LND --> LPD["LPD Protection Distance"]
    LND --> LCB["LCB Circuit Breaker Control"]
    LND --> LPHD["LPHD Physical Device"]
    LND --> LTIM["LTIM Time Synchronization"]
    LND --> LCOM["LCOM Communication Supervision"]
    LND --> LBPI["LBPI Bay Proxy"]
    LND --> LGRP["LGRP Logical Node Group"]
    LNA --> WYE["MMXU WYE Phase to Ground Voltages"]
    LNA --> DEL["MMXU DEL Phase to Phase Voltages"]
    LNA --> A1["MMXU.A phs A Current"]
    LNA --> A2["MMXU.A phs B Current"]
    LNA --> A3["MMXU.A phs C Current"]
    LPD --> PDIS["PDIS Distance Protection Function"]
    LPD --> PIOC["PIOC Instantaneous Overcurrent"]
    LPD --> PTOF["PTOF Overfrequency"]
    LPD --> PTUV["PTUV Undervoltage"]
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** The IEC 61850 data model follows a strict **Logical Device → Logical Node → Data Object → Data Attribute** hierarchy. Each `LN` (e.g., `MMXU`, `PDIS`, `XCBR`) carries a **standardized 4-letter code** that allows the SCD file to be **vendor-agnostic**.

## 4.3 Protection Trip Sequence — GOOSE Trip Flow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant FLT as Fault Inception
    participant PIED as Protection IED PIED
    participant ETH as Ethernet Switch Layer 2
    participant BCU as Bay Control Unit BCU
    participant BRK as Circuit Breaker
    participant DFR as Digital Fault Recorder

    FLT->>PIED: Fault Current 5 kA primary
    Note over PIED: 0 to 10 ms Zone 1 detection
    PIED->>ETH: GOOSE Trip Multicast
    Note over ETH: 0 to 3 ms GOOSE Latency
    ETH->>BCU: Forward GOOSE
    ETH->>DFR: Forward Trip Record
    Note over BCU: 0 to 1 ms Output Contact Pickup
    BCU->>BRK: Trip Coil Energize 110 V DC
    Note over BRK: 0 to 40 ms Contact Parting
    BRK-->>DFR: Breaker Status Open 52b
    BRK-->>PIED: Breaker Status Open
    DFR-->>PIED: COMTRADE Record Saved
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** This sequence captures the **complete GOOSE-based trip chain** typical of a Zone-1 distance protection scheme. The total time from fault inception to breaker contact parting is approximately **54 ms**, as derived in Section 3.

## 4.4 Smart Substation Cybersecurity Layers (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PHY["Physical Layer\nLocked Bay Cabinets CCTV"] --> NET["Network Layer\nFirewalls VLAN ACLs"]
    NET --> DATA["Data Layer\nIEC 62351 Signed GOOSE\nTLS for MMS"]
    DATA --> APP["Application Layer\nRBAC LDAP 2FA"]
    APP --> AUDIT["Audit Layer\nSyslog NERC CIP Logs"]
    AUDIT -.Continuous.-> NMS["Asset and Patch Management"]
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** Cybersecurity in IEC 61850 is implemented as a **defence-in-depth, 5-layer model**. **IEC 62351** specifically secures the **MMS, GOOSE, and SV** protocols through **digital signatures** and **encryption**, while **NERC CIP** (in the USA) and **CEA (Cyber Security in Power Sector) Regulations 2018** in India govern the regulatory compliance.
