# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Smart Substation. List any three functional benefits it provides over a conventional substation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Smart Substation** is an automated electrical substation that employs **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)**, **digital communication networks compliant with IEC 61850**, and **advanced sensors** to perform **real-time protection, control, monitoring, and asset management** of the substation equipment with minimal human intervention.

**Three benefits over conventional substations:**

1. **Reduced copper cabling** — Fiber-optic Process Bus replaces thousands of km of control cables, cutting installation and maintenance cost by up to **80%**.
2. **Multi-vendor interoperability** — IEC 61850 standard allows IEDs from different OEMs to communicate seamlessly, eliminating vendor lock-in.
3. **Enhanced situational awareness** — Synchronized PMUs and continuous condition monitoring enable **self-healing** operation and **wide-area monitoring (WAMS)**.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition 1M, Benefit 1 1M, Benefit 2 0.5M, Benefit 3 0.5M.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is an Intelligent Electronic Device (IED)? State its main functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **IED (Intelligent Electronic Device)** is a microprocessor-based controller used in substations to perform **protection, control, metering, monitoring, and communication** functions within a single hardware platform.

**Main functions:**

1. **Protection** — distance, overcurrent, differential, etc.
2. **Control** — breaker close/trip, tap-changer control.
3. **Metering & Monitoring** — voltage, current, power, energy, harmonics.
4. **Communication** — IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus, IEC 60870-5-104.
5. **Event recording** — Sequence of Event (SOE), Disturbance/Fault recording (COMTRADE).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers **internal choice** between two alternatives (e.g., Q. 7 (a) or 7 (b)). Both alternatives are provided below; only **one** is to be answered in the exam.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q7 (a)]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-level architecture** of a smart substation as per **IEC 61850**. Discuss the role of the **Process Bus** and **Station Bus**, listing at least **two protocols** used on each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the function and significance of the **GOOSE message** in IEC 61850. How does it differ from **MMS** and **Sampled Values (SV)**? Discuss the **latency class** of GOOSE for a fast trip. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The IEC 61850 standard organizes the smart substation into three logical levels: **Process Level (Level 0)**, **Bay Level (Level 1)**, and **Station Level (Level 2)**, interconnected by the **Process Bus** and **Station Bus**.

**Process Level (1 Mark):**
- Houses the **primary equipment** — Circuit Breakers, Disconnectors, Instrument Transformers (CT, VT), and the new-generation **Non-Conventional Instrument Transformers (NCITs)**.
- **Merging Units (MUs)** digitize the analog CT/VT signals and publish them on the **Process Bus** as **Sampled Values (SV)**.

**Bay Level (1 Mark):**
- One IED per bay.
- Performs **protection, control, and metering** of the assigned bay.
- Examples: **BCU (Bay Control Unit)**, **Distance relay (PDIS)**, **Differential relay (PIOC)**.

**Station Level (1 Mark):**
- Substation-wide control, HMI, gateway, engineering station.
- Connects to **Utility SCADA / EMS / DMS** at the enterprise level.

**Process Bus (1.5 Marks):**
- Transports **Sampled Values (SV)** per **IEC 61850-9-2** between MUs and IEDs.
- Protocols: **(i) IEC 61850-9-2 SV (Sampled Values)**, **(ii) IEC 61869 (NCIT data)**.
- Physical medium: **fiber optic** (100/1000 Mbps Ethernet).

**Station Bus (1.5 Marks):**
- Transports **MMS, GOOSE, and time-sync (PTP / SNTP)** between IEDs and station HMI/SCADA.
- Protocols: **(i) MMS (ISO 9506)** for client-server SCADA traffic, **(ii) GOOSE** for peer-to-peer trip/interlock, **(iii) PTP (IEEE 1588)** for time synchronization.

> **Mark split:** 7 marks for clear architecture description and 2 protocol-level mentions.

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**GOOSE Function (2 Marks):**
- **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event)** is a **multicast, peer-to-peer, layer-2 Ethernet** message used for **fast, event-driven transfer of critical data** such as **trip commands, interlocking, and breaker status** between IEDs.
- It **bypasses the TCP/IP stack** to achieve **sub-4 ms latency**.

**Comparison with MMS and SV (3 Marks):**

| Feature | MMS | GOOSE | SV |
|---------|-----|-------|-----|
| Standard | ISO 9506 | IEC 61850-8-1 | IEC 61850-9-2 |
| Layer | Application (TCP/IP) | Layer 2 (Direct Ethernet) | Layer 2 (Multicast) |
| Direction | Client–Server | Publisher–Subscriber (Multicast) | Publisher–Subscriber (Multicast) |
| Latency | 100 ms – 1 s | **< 4 ms** (Type 1A) | **0.25 ms/packet** (continuous stream) |
| Use | SCADA telemetry, config | Trip, Interlock, Annunciation | Digitized CT/VT waveforms |

**Latency Class (2 Marks):**
IEC 61850-5 defines **performance classes**:
- **Type 1A (Fast Trip)** — Transfer time **≤ 4 ms** (used for **Zone-1 distance trip** and **busbar differential trip**).
- **Type 1B** — ≤ 10 ms.
- **Type 2, 3, 4, 5, 6** — for control, monitoring, file transfer (up to 1000 ms).

> **Final answer:** A **GOOSE Type 1A message** has a transfer time of **≤ 4 ms**, making it suitable for **fast protection trips** in IEC 61850-based smart substations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q7 (b)]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Substation Automation (SA)**. Draw the **functional architecture** and list the **hardware and software elements** of a typical SAS. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of PMUs and WAMS** in a smart substation. How does **synchrophasor technology** enhance **real-time situational awareness**? Mention the relevant **IEEE standard**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a)

**Concept of SA (2 Marks):**
**Substation Automation (SA)** refers to the use of **IEDs, communication networks, and SCADA software** to perform **automatic protection, control, monitoring, and data acquisition** within a substation, replacing the manual / hard-wired logic of conventional substations.

**Functional Architecture (3 Marks):**
The three-tier architecture (Bay → Station → Enterprise) is reproduced here in textual form:
- **Tier 1 (Bay level):** BCU, Protection IEDs, PMU, MUs.
- **Tier 2 (Station level):** Station HMI, Redundant SCADA Gateway, Engineering Station, Time Sync Server (GPS / PTP Grandmaster).
- **Tier 3 (Enterprise / Control Center):** EMS, DMS, WAMS, Asset Management.

**Hardware Elements (1 Mark):**
- IEDs, MUs, Ethernet Switches (Managed, IEC 61850-3 compliant), GPS antenna, PTP Grandmaster, GPS Clock, Station DC Battery, NCITs, Smart actuators.

**Software Elements (1 Mark):**
- **SCL Configuration Tool** (e.g., IEDScout, PCM600), **SCADA Suite** (e.g., WinCC, Power Factory), **HMI**, **Firmware**, **Asset Management**, **Cyber Security Suite (IEC 62351)**.

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b)

**PMU Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a device that measures the **synchronized voltage and current phasors** (magnitude and angle) at a substation bus using a **common time reference** derived from **GPS** or **PTP** (IEEE 1588). The output is a **synchrophasor**.

**WAMS Definition (1 Mark):**
**Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)** is the **centralized platform** that aggregates PMU data from geographically dispersed substations to provide **real-time visualization, oscillation detection, and post-event analysis** of the power system.

**Role in Smart Substation (2 Marks):**
- **Real-time voltage stability assessment**.
- **Oscillation monitoring** (inter-area modes 0.1 – 1 Hz).
- **Fast fault-location** using synchronized measurements.
- **Model validation** for offline studies.
- **Wide-Area Protection (WAPS)** — preventive control actions.

**IEEE Standard (1.5 Marks):**
- **IEEE C37.118.1** — Synchrophasor data transfer format.
- **IEEE C37.118.2** — Synchrophasor measurement requirements.
- **IEEE 1588** — Precision Time Protocol (PTP).
- **IEEE C37.244** — PMU application guide.
- **Reporting rate:** 25, 50, 100 samples/sec (in India: **50 fps** is most common in URTDSM).

**Real-time Situational Awareness (1 Mark):**
By providing **30–60 measurements per second** (versus 1 sample per 2–4 seconds in conventional SCADA), WAMS allows the **control center operator to visualize the dynamic state** of the grid, detect stressed conditions, and trigger **corrective action within seconds** rather than minutes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Errors**
> 1. **Confusing IEC 61850-9-2 SV (Sampled Values) with IEC 61869-9 (NCIT standard).** They are different — 9-2 is the protocol, 61869-9 is the instrument. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 2. **Stating GOOSE latency as "≤ 4 cycles"** instead of "≤ 4 ms". KTU expects **ms**, not cycles.
> 3. **Forgetting PTP (IEEE 1588)** when explaining time synchronization — students often mention only GPS, which is incomplete.
> 4. **Drawing GOOSE as a client–server model.** It is **publisher–subscriber, multicast, layer-2**, not TCP/IP. (Loses 1.5 marks.)
> 5. **Not specifying India's 50 Hz fundamental** when calculating sampling rate. (The same architecture in the USA uses 60 Hz → 14,400 sps at 240 samples/cycle.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Smart Substation** = IEDs + IEC 61850 + SCADA + Condition Monitoring.
- [x] **IEC 61850** is the **backbone** standard; 10 parts, XML-based **SCL** for configuration.
- [x] **Three-level architecture:** Process (Level 0) → Bay (Level 1) → Station (Level 2) → Enterprise (Level 3).
- [x] **Process Bus** uses **IEC 61850-9-2 SV** (Sampled Values) at **4,800 sps** for protection, **14,400 sps** for metering.
- [x] **Station Bus** uses **MMS (client–server) + GOOSE (multicast, < 4 ms)**.
- [x] **GOOSE Type 1A** transfer time = **≤ 4 ms**; runs directly on **Layer 2 Ethernet** (no TCP/IP).
- [x] **Sampling rate from Nyquist** = $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$; for 49th harmonic at 50 Hz → **≥ 4,900 Hz**, standardized at **4,800 Hz**.
- [x] **PMU standard:** **IEEE C37.118.1 / 118.2**; reporting rate **25/50/100 fps**; time sync via **GPS + IEEE 1588 PTP (1 μs accuracy)**.
- [x] **Cybersecurity standard:** **IEC 62351** (for IEC 61850); Indian regulation — **CEA (Cyber Security in Power Sector) Regulations, 2018**.
- [x] **Redundancy protocols:** **PRP** and **HSR** (both from **IEC 62439-3**).
- [x] **IED functions:** Protection + Control + Metering + Monitoring + Communication (in one box).
- [x] **Logical Node codes:** `MMXU` (metering), `PDIS` (distance), `PIOC` (instantaneous OC), `XCBR` (circuit breaker), `PTRC` (protection trip conditioning), `LPHD` (physical device).
- [x] **NCIT** = Non-Conventional Instrument Transformer = Optical CT / Rogowski Coil; no saturation, wide dynamic range.
- [x] **Total trip time** ≈ **54 ms** (10 ms detect + 3 ms GOOSE + 1 ms process + 40 ms breaker).
- [x] **DC Battery bank** = **110 V (or 220 V) DC**, sized typically for **8 hours autonomy**, design margin **1.2×**.
- [x] **Indian context:** URTDSM project deployed **1,184 PMUs** on **400/765 kV** grid; 220 kV is the most common smart substation voltage.
- [x] **Key benefit numbers:** **> 80% cabling reduction**, **> 50× faster data refresh**, **30% life extension** of transformer.

> **End of Module 3 — Smart Substations Notes**

# ⚡ Substation Automation — Module 3 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Substation Automation (SA)** refers to the integrated deployment of Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs), protection relays, communication networks, sensors, and supervisory software (SCADA/EMS) to perform real-time **protection, control, monitoring, metering, and data acquisition** functions within an electrical substation — with minimal or zero manual human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Substation Automation is an integrated system that uses IEDs, sensors, and standardized communication protocols (predominantly **IEC 61850**) to automate the operation, protection, and remote management of substations, enabling them to function as intelligent nodes within the **Smart Grid** ecosystem.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a traditional **traffic intersection** controlled by a *human traffic police officer* — he sees cars (data), makes decisions (logic), and signals (control). If he falls sick, traffic collapses.
Now replace him with an **AI-driven smart traffic system** containing:
- **Cameras/Sensors** → see vehicles and detect jams
- **Local Processors (IEDs)** → make quick decisions locally
- **A Central Control Room (SCADA)** → monitors everything remotely
- **A Common Communication Language (IEC 61850)** → so every device "talks" the same way

That is exactly what **Substation Automation** does for the power grid! The human operator in the control room is replaced by intelligent, networked devices that **protect, control, and report** continuously — **24/7, in milliseconds**.

### Key Components (Bolded Standard Metrics)
The core building blocks of a modern SAS include:
- **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)** — multifunction digital relays, BCUs, PMUs
- **Remote Terminal Units (RTUs)** — legacy interface devices (typically replaced by IEDs in modern SAS)
- **Bay Controllers (BCUs)** — handle bay-level interlocking and control
- **Merging Units (MUs)** — digitize analog CT/PT signals for the process bus
- **SCADA / EMS Master Station** — central supervisory software
- **Station Bus & Process Bus** — fiber-optic Ethernet LANs operating at **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps**
- **GPS Time Synchronization** — accuracy of **±1 μs** for synchrophasor / IEC 61850-9-2 SV
- **HMI (Human Machine Interface)** — operator workstation

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU 2024, the focus of Module 3 is on the **architecture, communication protocol (IEC 61850), automation functions, and benefits of SAS** in enabling Smart Grid objectives.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Functional Architecture of a Substation Automation System

A modern SAS operates across **three hierarchical levels**:

### Level 1 — Process Level (Bay/Field Level)
- Contains **Current Transformers (CTs)**, **Potential Transformers (PTs)**, and primary switchgear (breakers, isolators).
- Analog signals are digitized by **Merging Units (MUs)** and transmitted over the **Process Bus** using **IEC 61850-9-2 Sampled Values (SV)**.
- Sampling rate is typically **80 samples/cycle (4000 Hz for 50 Hz system)** or **256 samples/cycle** for protection-class MUs.

### Level 2 — Bay Level
- Houses **IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices)** which perform:
  - Protection functions (overcurrent, distance, differential — ANSI 50/51, 67, 87)
  - Bay-level control (breaker open/close, interlocking)
  - Metering and event recording
- IEDs communicate with each other and the station level over the **Station Bus** using **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event)** messaging for trip/close commands.
- GOOSE latency is **< 4 ms** (Type 1A) per IEC 61850.

### Level 3 — Station Level
- Comprises the **SCADA gateway**, **Engineering Workstation**, and **HMI**.
- Communicates with the **Control Centre (Utility Dispatch)** via **DNP3 / IEC 60870-5-104 / IEC 61850**.
- Hosts the **Substation Server** which runs the **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification)** protocol for client-server reporting.

## Communication Protocol Stack — IEC 61850
IEC 61850 is the **gold standard** for SAS communication. It is structured as a 10-part standard:

| Part | Title | Function |
|------|-------|----------|
| IEC 61850-1 | Introduction & Overview | Concepts |
| IEC 61850-3 | General Requirements | Environmental & EMC |
| IEC 61850-4 | System & Project Mgmt | Engineering workflow |
| IEC 61850-5 | Communication Requirements | Performance classes |
| IEC 61850-6 | SCL (Substation Config Language) | XML-based config |
| IEC 61850-7-x | Basic Info Structure | Data models, LN classes |
| IEC 61850-8-1 | MMS Mapping | Client-server comms |
| IEC 61850-8-2 | GOOSE | Fast peer-to-peer events |
| IEC 61850-9-2 | SV (Sampled Values) | Process bus streaming |
| IEC 61850-10 | Conformance Testing | Compliance verification |

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Equation / Standard Value | Meaning |
|--------------------|--------------------------|---------|
| GOOSE Latency | $T_{GOOSE} \leq 4 \text{ ms}$ | Type 1A performance class |
| SV Sampling Rate | $f_{sv} = 80 \times f_{nom}$ (Protection) | 4000 Hz @ 50 Hz |
| SV Sampling Rate | $f_{sv} = 256 \times f_{nom}$ (Metering) | 12800 Hz @ 50 Hz |
| Time Sync Accuracy | $\Delta t \leq \pm 1 \text{ μs}$ | GPS / IEEE 1588 |
| Throughput (Process Bus) | $\geq 100 \text{ Mbps}$ | Fiber-optic Ethernet |
| Reporting Latency | $T_{rep} \leq 1 \text{ s}$ | MMS to SCADA |
| Phasor Data Rate | $50 \text{–} 60 \text{ frames/sec}$ | PMU output to PDC |
| Bayesian Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | IED Mean Time Between Failures |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | SAS uptime index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Substation Automation is the **backbone of every Smart Grid utility**. Without SAS, it is impossible to achieve DER (Distributed Energy Resource) integration, FLISR (Fault Location Isolation & Service Restoration), Volt-VAR optimization, or wide-area situational awareness.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: GOOSE Transmission Time & Performance Class

IEC 61850 defines three message performance classes — Type 1 (fast), Type 2 (medium), Type 3 (low). The GOOSE message is Type **1A**.

$$
T_{total} = T_{enc} + T_{net} + T_{dec}
$$

Where:
- $T_{enc}$ = Encoding time in IED publisher ≈ 0.1 ms
- $T_{net}$ = Network transit time over fiber ≈ 0.5 ms per switch hop
- $T_{dec}$ = Decoding & processing in IED subscriber ≈ 0.1 ms

**For a 2-switch network hop:**

$$
T_{total} = 0.1 + (2 \times 0.5) + 0.1 = 1.2 \text{ ms}
$$

Since the IEC 61850 budget for Type 1A is **≤ 4 ms**, the design is well within limits. The remaining **2.8 ms margin** is reserved for retransmissions (in case of packet loss, GOOSE retransmits with exponentially increasing backoff).

## Derivation 2: Process Bus Bandwidth Calculation

A single MU streams **8 current + 8 voltage channels** as per IEC 61850-9-2 LE.

Per-sample size (with 16-bit ADC) for 1 ASDU frame:

$$
\text{Frame Size} = (\text{Channels} \times 16 \text{ bits}) + \text{Header (48 bits)} + \text{Eth overhead (256 bits)}
$$

$$
\text{Frame Size} = (16 \times 16) + 48 + 256 = 256 + 48 + 256 = 560 \text{ bits}
$$

For protection rate of 4000 Hz:

$$
\text{Bit Rate} = 560 \times 4000 = 2{,}240{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 2.24 \text{ Mbps per MU}
$$

For 10 bays (10 MUs):

$$
B_{total} = 10 \times 2.24 = 22.4 \text{ Mbps}
$$

This easily fits in a **100 Mbps** Ethernet link, leaving **77.6% bandwidth** for other traffic.

## Derivation 3: SAS Availability Calculation

Using the standard availability formula:

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

Assume an IED has $MTBF = 100{,}000$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours.

$$
A = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}000 + 4} = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}004} = 0.99996
$$

$$
A_{\%} = 99.996\% \approx \text{"Six Nines" availability}
$$

Downtime per year:

$$
D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 8760 = (4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 8760 \approx 0.35 \text{ hours/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** A well-designed SAS achieves **five-to-six nines** of availability, which is critical for mission-critical grid infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Three-Level SAS Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC["PROCESS LEVEL - Field"]
        CT["Current Transformer CT"]
        PT["Potential Transformer PT"]
        CB["Circuit Breaker"]
        ISO["Isolator Switch"]
        MU["Merging Unit MU - IEC 61850-9-2"]
    end

    subgraph BAY["BAY LEVEL - Protection and Control"]
        IEDP["Protection IED - ANSI 50 51 87"]
        IEDC["Bay Control Unit BCU"]
        IEDM["Metering and PMU IED"]
    end

    subgraph STAT["STATION LEVEL - Supervisory"]
        SCADA["SCADA Gateway - MMS"]
        HMI["Operator HMI"]
        ENGWS["Engineering Workstation - SCL"]
        GPS["GPS Time Sync - IEEE 1588"]
    end

    PROC -- "Analog CT PT signals" --> MU
    MU -- "Sampled Values SV" --> BAY
    BAY -- "GOOSE Trip Close" --> PROC
    IEDP -- "Protection trip" --> CB
    IEDC -- "Control commands" --> ISO

    IEDP -- "MMS Reports" --> SCADA
    IEDC -- "Status Events" --> SCADA
    IEDM -- "PMU Stream" --> SCADA

    SCADA --> HMI
    SCADA --> ENGWS
    GPS -- "1 microsec pulse" --> IEDP
    GPS -- "1 microsec pulse" --> IEDC
    GPS -- "1 microsec pulse" --> MU
```

## Diagram 2: IEC 61850 Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Application Layer - MMS GOOSE SV"]
    L2["Presentation - ASN.1 BER Encoding"]
    L3["Session Transport - TCP UDP"]
    L4["Network - IP"]
    L5["Data Link - Ethernet MAC - IEEE 802.1Q VLAN"]
    L6["Physical - 100BASE-FX Fiber or 1000BASE-LX"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6

    TIME["IEEE 1588 PTP - Time Sync Layer"]
    L5 -.-> TIME
```

## Diagram 3: SAS Functional Flow During a Fault Event

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Fault on Line
    participant MU as Merging Unit
    participant IED as Protection IED
    participant BCU as Bay Controller
    participant CB as Circuit Breaker
    participant SC as SCADA Server

    F->>MU: Fault current 5000 A flows
    MU->>IED: SV stream 4000 Hz digital samples
    IED->>IED: ANSI 50 51 element picks up
    IED->>CB: GOOSE Trip Command type 1A less than 4 ms
    CB->>CB: Opens contacts in 50 ms
    IED->>BCU: Status Event breaker open
    IED->>SC: MMS Report event record COMTRADE
    SC->>SC: Displays fault on HMI and notifies control center
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above schematics follow KTU's recommended approach of presenting SAS as a **multi-layered, event-driven, peer-to-peer networked system**, rather than a centralized RTU-polled architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Substation Automation System (SAS). List any four of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**
A Substation Automation System (SAS) is an integrated assembly of IEDs, sensors, communication networks, and supervisory software that performs real-time **protection, control, monitoring, and data acquisition** within a substation with minimal human intervention.

Four primary functions:
1. **Protection** — fault detection and isolation (ANSI 50/51, 67, 87)
2. **Control** — breaker/isolator switching, interlocking
3. **Monitoring** — status, alarms, event logging (COMTRADE)
4. **Metering** — energy measurement (Wh, VArh), PMU synchrophasors
5. **Communication** — IEC 61850 GOOSE, SV, MMS reporting

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the role of an Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) in a modern SAS? Name two communication protocols used by IEDs.**

**Model Answer:**
An **IED** is a microprocessor-based device that integrates protection, control, metering, and communication functions into a single unit. It acquires analog/digital signals from the process level, executes protection logic, and communicates with peer IEDs and the SCADA master.

Two communication protocols:
- **IEC 61850** — modern standard (GOOSE, SV, MMS)
- **DNP3 / IEC 60870-5-104** — legacy protocol for SCADA gateway

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **three-level hierarchical architecture** of a Substation Automation System with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of each level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the salient features of the **IEC 61850 standard** and explain **GOOSE and Sampled Values (SV)** messaging in detail. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The SAS architecture is divided into **three levels**:

**Level 1 — Process Level:**
- Houses CTs, PTs, breakers, isolators.
- Analog signals converted to digital by **Merging Units (MUs)**.
- Communicates to bay level via **Process Bus** using IEC 61850-9-2 SV.

**Level 2 — Bay Level:**
- Contains **IEDs** (Protection, Control, Metering).
- Performs local decisions, breaker tripping via **GOOSE**.
- Inter-bay interlocking executed by **Bay Controllers (BCUs)**.

**Level 3 — Station Level:**
- Hosts **SCADA gateway, HMI, Engineering Workstation**.
- Aggregates substation data and forwards to **Control Centre** using **IEC 60870-5-104 / DNP3**.
- Contains **GPS clock** for time synchronization.

Communication between levels is over **Station Bus** (100 Mbps / 1 Gbps Ethernet with VLAN prioritization per IEEE 802.1Q).

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Salient Features of IEC 61850:**
- **Object-oriented data modelling** using Logical Nodes (e.g., XCBR for breaker, PTRC for trip logic).
- **Standardized SCL** (Substation Configuration Language) for vendor-independent engineering.
- **Three messaging services**: MMS (client-server reporting), GOOSE (fast peer-to-peer), SV (digitized CT/PT streaming).
- **High speed & interoperability** — supports multi-vendor IED integration.

**GOOSE Messaging:**
- Used for **critical trip/close commands**.
- Direct publisher-subscriber model (no IP/TCP overhead), mapped directly over Ethernet.
- Latency **≤ 4 ms (Type 1A)**.
- Built-in retransmission with exponential backoff to ensure reliability.

**Sampled Values (SV):**
- Streams digitized CT/PT measurements from MU to IEDs.
- IEC 61850-9-2 supports **80 samples/cycle (protection)** and **256 samples/cycle (metering)**.
- Eliminates copper wiring; uses **fiber-optic Process Bus**.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Describe the various **functional benefits** of Substation Automation in Smart Grid. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a sequence diagram, explain the **sequence of operations during a fault in an automated substation** using IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

1. **Improved Reliability** — sub-cycle fault clearing (≤ 80 ms) via GOOSE.
2. **Reduced Operating Cost (OPEX)** — fewer field staff; remote operation possible.
3. **Enhanced Power Quality** — real-time VAR compensation, voltage regulation.
4. **Better Asset Management** — condition monitoring of transformers/breakers.
5. **Faster Restoration** — auto-reclosing, FLISR.
6. **DER Integration** — seamless handling of solar/wind variability.
7. **Cybersecurity** — IEC 62351 adds authentication and encryption.
8. **Centralized Data** — single source of truth for SCADA/EMS/WAMS.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Sequence of operations during a fault:**

1. **T = 0 ms:** Fault occurs on the transmission line.
2. **T + 0.5 ms:** CT senses fault current → MU digitizes → SV stream reaches IED.
3. **T + 1 ms:** Protection IED algorithm (ANSI 50/51) trips.
4. **T + 2 ms:** IED publishes GOOSE Trip message to breaker IED.
5. **T + 3 ms:** Breaker IED receives GOOSE → issues trip coil energization.
6. **T + 50 ms:** Breaker contacts separate → fault cleared.
7. **T + 60 ms:** IED sends MMS event report → SCADA HMI displays fault.
8. **T + 1 s:** Sequence-of-Events (SOE) log written; COMTRADE file generated.

The entire **detect-to-clear cycle** takes **< 80 ms** — meeting IEC 61850 Type 1A performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing Station Bus with Process Bus** — Station Bus = IED-to-Scada/IED-to-IED (MMS/GOOSE). Process Bus = MU-to-IED (SV). Many students interchange them.
> 2. ❌ **Wrong GOOSE timing** — Always state **≤ 4 ms (Type 1A)**, not 100 ms.
> 3. ❌ **Missing SCL mention** — IEC 61850's **Substation Configuration Language (SCL)** is a high-yield topic; examiners expect it.
> 4. ❌ **Skipping GOOSE retransmission logic** — Always mention that GOOSE *retransmits on packet loss* — this is a favorite 2-mark sub-question.
> 5. ❌ **Writing RTU-centric answers** — Modern SAS is **IED-centric**, not RTU-centric. Avoid legacy architecture descriptions.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **SAS = IEDs + IEC 61850 + SCADA** — three pillars.
- ⭐ **3 Levels**: Process (CT/PT/MU) → Bay (IED/BCU) → Station (SCADA/HMI).
- ⭐ **Process Bus** carries **Sampled Values (SV)** at 80/256 samples/cycle.
- ⭐ **Station Bus** carries **GOOSE (≤ 4 ms)** and **MMS (≤ 1 s)**.
- ⭐ **GOOSE = Type 1A, peer-to-peer, publisher-subscriber, retransmits on loss**.
- ⭐ **IEC 61850 supports 10 parts**; key ones: -7-x (data model), -8-1 (MMS), -8-2 (GOOSE), -9-2 (SV), -6 (SCL).
- ⭐ **Time Sync** via **IEEE 1588 PTP** or **GPS**, accuracy **±1 μs**.
- ⭐ **PMU streams** at **50–60 frames/sec** to PDC for wide-area monitoring.
- ⭐ **Availability of SAS** ≈ **99.996% (six nines)**.
- ⭐ **Benefits**: Faster fault clearing, OPEX reduction, DER integration, cybersecurity (IEC 62351).
- ⭐ **Cybersecurity** uses **IEC 62351** for authentication, encryption, role-based access.
- ⭐ **Engineering Workflow**: SCL → Configure → Bind → Test → Commission using IEDScout.
- ⭐ **Migration Path**: Conventional (Hardwired) → RTU-based → Hybrid → Full IEC 61850 SAS.
- ⭐ **For KTU 2024 exams**: Always draw a **block diagram of the 3-level architecture** — it fetches easy 4–5 marks.
- ⭐ **Distinguish MMS vs GOOSE vs SV** clearly in any 7-mark question — examiners specifically test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feeder Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feeder Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Feeder Automation (FA)** is a critical subsystem of the broader Distribution Automation (DA) framework within the Smart Grid ecosystem. It refers to the automatic monitoring, control, and protection of the **medium-voltage (MV) distribution feeders** — the radial or looped power lines that carry electricity from the primary substation (11 kV / 33 kV bus) to the secondary distribution transformers serving end customers.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Feeder Automation is defined as:

> *"The set of hardware, software, and communication technologies that enable the remote or autonomous detection, isolation, and restoration of faults on distribution feeders, while continuously optimizing voltage profile and reactive power flow along the feeder."*

Mathematically, the scope of feeder automation can be abstracted as a control problem over a distribution feeder graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ represents the set of switching devices (reclosers, sectionalizers, RMUs) and $E$ represents the line segments connecting them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Core Outcomes:**
> After studying Feeder Automation, students must be able to:
> 1. Differentiate between **localized**, **distributed**, and **centralized** feeder automation architectures.
> 2. Explain the **FDIR (Fault Detection, Isolation, and Restoration)** sequence.
> 3. Identify the role of **reclosers**, **sectionalizers**, and **RMUs** in self-healing grids.
> 4. Evaluate reliability indices (**SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, ASAI, ENS**) before and after automation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Immune System of the Distribution Network"

Imagine the human body. When you cut your finger, your nervous system (sensors) instantly detects the pain, your platelets (switches) rush to clot the blood (isolate the damage), and your skin heals (restoration). **You do not consciously think about it — it happens automatically.**

Now consider an electrical distribution feeder running through a city. A tree falls on a wire (fault). Without feeder automation:
- Thousands of customers remain in darkness for hours.
- A lineman must drive to the location, identify the fault, manually open switches, and restore supply.

**With feeder automation:**
- Sensors detect the fault in **milliseconds**.
- Intelligent switches **isolate** only the faulty section.
- An automated algorithm **reconfigures** the network (through tie-switches) to restore supply to healthy sections via an alternate feeder.

This is the essence of the **"Self-Healing Grid"** — the network diagnoses its own injury, contains it, and heals itself with minimal human intervention.

## 1.3 Why Feeder Automation is Indispensable

| Driver | Quantified Impact |
|---|---|
| Reliability | Reduces **SAIDI** by **30%–60%** |
| Operational Efficiency | Reduces field crew dispatch by **up to 70%** |
| Power Quality | Maintains voltage within ±**5%** of nominal |
| Asset Life | Reduces peak loading on transformers by **15%–20%** |
| Customer Satisfaction | Directly improves utility regulatory scorecards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Standard Reference:**
> The **IEEE Std 1547-2018** and **IEC 61850** protocols govern the communication and interoperability requirements for feeder automation devices globally. In India, the **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Regulations 2010** mandate reliability metrics that drive the adoption of FA in DISCOMs.

## 1.4 Scope of Feeder Automation

Feeder automation encompasses **five functional pillars**:

1. **Fault Detection, Isolation & Restoration (FDIR)** — the cornerstone function.
2. **Volt/VAR Optimization (VVO)** — dynamic control of voltage regulators and capacitor banks.
3. **Feeder Reconfiguration (FR)** — topology switching for loss minimization.
4. **Load Balancing** — phase-wise load redistribution.
5. **Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) / AMI integration** — bidirectional data flow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Feeder Automation — The Three Tiers

Feeder automation is implemented across **three hierarchical tiers**, each with distinct intelligence and communication requirements:

### Tier 1: Field Level (Process Level)
- **Devices:** Reclosers, Sectionalizers, Ring Main Units (RMUs), Fault Passage Indicators (FPIs), Remote Terminal Units (RTUs), Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs).
- **Function:** Real-time fault detection (current/voltage sensing), local protection (time-overcurrent curves), data acquisition.
- **Time Frame:** **Milliseconds to seconds.**
- **Intelligence:** Localized (autonomous) logic.

### Tier 2: Station Level (Bay Control Level)
- **Devices:** Bay Control Units (BCUs), Distribution Substation Automation Controllers, SCADA Remote Terminal Units.
- **Function:** Supervisory control of multiple feeders, event logging, alarm management, communication protocol translation (DNP3 / IEC 61850 / Modbus).
- **Time Frame:** **Seconds to minutes.**

### Tier 3: Control Center Level (Enterprise Level)
- **Devices:** SCADA Master Station, DMS (Distribution Management System), OMS (Outage Management System, ADMS (Advanced DMS).
- **Function:** Wide-area optimization, FLISR (Fault Location, Isolation, Service Restoration), Volt/VAR control across entire utility.
- **Time Frame:** **Minutes to hours.**
- **Intelligence:** Centralized, algorithmic, operator-supervised.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> - **Local Automation** = Tier 1 only (recloser + sectionalizer coordination).
> - **Distributed Automation** = Tier 1 + Tier 2 (peer-to-peer IED communication).
> - **Centralized Automation** = All 3 tiers (SCADA-driven FLISR).

## 2.2 The FDIR (Fault Detection, Isolation & Restoration) Process

This is the **most critical and exam-favorite** process in feeder automation. It executes in a strict sequential logic:

**Step 1: Fault Detection**
- The substation circuit breaker (or feeder recloser) senses overcurrent.
- Protective relays (overcurrent, distance, differential) identify the fault.
- Fault type classification: **L-G**, **L-L**, **L-L-G**, **3-Φ**.

**Step 2: Fault Localization**
- Multiple techniques:
  - **Impedance-based** measurement.
  - **Travelling wave** analysis.
  - **Synchrophasor (PMU)**-based localization.
  - **FPIs (Fault Passage Indicators)** lighting up at sectionalizer points.

**Step 3: Isolation**
- The faulty zone (bounded by two adjacent automated switches) is electrically disconnected by opening sectionalizing switches.
- **Goal:** Isolate the **smallest possible** section to minimize affected customers.

**Step 4: Restoration (Self-Healing)**
- Healthy upstream section: Closed main switch restores supply.
- Healthy downstream section: Closed tie-switch connects to an adjacent (backup) feeder.
- **Goal:** Restore supply to **maximum** customers within **< 1 minute**.

## 2.3 Recloser — Sectionalizer Coordination

A **Recloser** is a self-contained device with the ability to sense overcurrent, trip, and automatically reclose a preset number of times (typically 3 or 4 shots) before locking out. It is installed at the substation feeder exit or at strategic mid-feeder points.

A **Sectionalizer** is a load-break switch that **does not interrupt fault current**. It counts the number of fault-current passages (set on a counter) and opens when the recloser upstream goes into lockout, thereby isolating the downstream faulted section.

**Standard Coordination Sequence:**

| Shot | Device Action | Recloser Time | Sectionalizer Counter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shot 1 | Recloser trips → fast curve | **0.05–0.1 s** | +1 |
| Shot 2 | Recloser recloses → trips slow curve | **0.3–0.5 s** | +2 |
| Shot 3 | Recloser recloses → trips slow curve | **1.0–2.0 s** | +3 |
| Lockout | Recloser locks out | — | Sectionalizer opens |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Scheme numerical questions. Memorize these for guaranteed marks in any feeder automation problem.

$$
\text{SAIFI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\text{SAIDI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\text{CAIDI} = \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}}
$$

$$
\text{ASAI} = \frac{8760 \cdot N_{\text{total}} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \cdot N_i}{8760 \cdot N_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\text{ASAI} = 1 - \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{8760}
$$

$$
\text{ASUI} = 1 - \text{ASAI} = \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{8760}
$$

$$
\text{ENS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i \cdot U_i \quad \text{[kWh]}
$$

$$
\text{AENS} = \frac{\text{ENS}}{N_{\text{total}}} \quad \text{[kWh/customer]}
$$

$$
\text{MAIFI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i^{\text{momentary}} \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

### Variable Legend & Units

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda_i$ | Failure rate of zone $i$ | failures / year |
| $N_i$ | Number of customers in zone $i$ | customers |
| $N_{\text{total}}$ | Total customers served | customers |
| $U_i$ | Outage duration of zone $i$ | hours |
| $L_i$ | Average load interrupted in zone $i$ | kW |
| $8760$ | Total hours in a year | hours/year |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These indices are the **regulatory benchmarks** used by electricity regulatory commissions worldwide. In India, the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC)** and **APERC** mandate annual SAIDI/SAIFI reporting. Utilities that fail to meet standards face **financial penalties** — directly incentivizing the deployment of feeder automation. Globally, the **IEEE P1366** standard provides a refined methodology to handle major event days (MEDs), ensuring reliability comparisons are statistically fair across utilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Reliability Indices Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Numerical:** A 3-zone feeder serves the following customers. Each zone experiences one fault per year. Compute **SAIFI, SAIDI, CAIDI, ASAI, ASUI, and ENS**. Assume Zone loads are at rated capacity.

### Given Data Table

| Zone | Customers ($N_i$) | Average Load $L_i$ (kW) | Outage Duration $U_i$ (hours/year) | Failures/Year $\lambda_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zone A | 500 | 1200 | 3.0 | 1 |
| Zone B | 800 | 2000 | 5.0 | 1 |
| Zone C | 1200 | 3000 | 2.0 | 1 |
| **Total** | $N_{\text{total}} = 2500$ | — | — | — |

### Step 1: Compute SAIFI

$$
\text{SAIFI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

Substituting each zone contribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{(1 \times 500) + (1 \times 800) + (1 \times 1200)}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{500 + 800 + 1200}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2500}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= 1.0 \;\; \text{interruptions/customer-year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute SAIDI

$$
\text{SAIDI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{(3.0 \times 500) + (5.0 \times 800) + (2.0 \times 1200)}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1500 + 4000 + 2400}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7900}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= 3.16 \;\; \text{hours/customer-year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute CAIDI

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIDI} &= \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.16}{1.0} \\[4pt]
&= 3.16 \;\; \text{hours/customer-interruption}
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the **average restoration time per interruption**, i.e., once an interruption occurs, it takes on average **3.16 hours** to restore service.

### Step 4: Compute ASAI (Availability)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASAI} &= 1 - \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{8760} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{3.16}{8760} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.000361 \\[4pt]
&= 0.999639 \;\; \text{(dimensionless fraction)} \\[4pt]
&= 99.9639\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute ASUI (Unavailability)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASUI} &= 1 - \text{ASAI} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{8760} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.16}{8760} \\[4pt]
&= 0.000361 \;\; \text{(dimensionless fraction)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0361\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compute ENS (Energy Not Supplied)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ENS} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i \cdot U_i \\[4pt]
&= (1200 \times 3.0) + (2000 \times 5.0) + (3000 \times 2.0) \\[4pt]
&= 3600 + 10000 + 6000 \\[4pt]
&= 19600 \;\; \text{kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Compute AENS (Average Energy Not Supplied per customer)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AENS} &= \frac{\text{ENS}}{N_{\text{total}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{19600}{2500} \\[4pt]
&= 7.84 \;\; \text{kWh/customer-year}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison — Before vs. After Feeder Automation

> [!NOTE]
> This type of **comparative table** is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU Module 3 exams.

Now suppose the same feeder is upgraded with **automated sectionalizers and a tie-switch** to an adjacent backup feeder. The post-automation scenario changes as follows:

- Zone A (healthy upstream): Isolation time reduces from 3.0 hr → **0.083 hr (5 min)** for switching.
- Zone B (fault zone, isolated): Still 5.0 hr for repair crew.
- Zone C (healthy downstream, restored via tie-switch): 2.0 hr → **0.083 hr (5 min)**.

| Index | Before FA | After FA | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| SAIFI | 1.00 | 1.00 | **No change** (same faults) |
| SAIDI | 3.16 hr | **1.764 hr** | **44.2% reduction** |
| CAIDI | 3.16 hr | **1.764 hr** | Improved |
| ASAI | 99.9639% | **99.9799%** | Improved |
| ENS | 19,600 kWh | **11,000 kWh** | **43.9% reduction** |

This numerical comparison **proves** the engineering value proposition of feeder automation — a direct quantitative answer KTU examiners expect.

## 3.3 Key Takeaway Equations for Derivations

$$
\Delta \text{SAIDI}_{\text{saved}} = \text{SAIDI}_{\text{before}} - \text{SAIDI}_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
\Delta \text{ENS}_{\text{saved}} = \text{ENS}_{\text{before}} - \text{ENS}_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
\text{Customer Minutes Saved (CMS)} = \Delta \text{SAIDI} \times 60 \times N_{\text{total}}
$$

The **CMS** is the industry-standard metric used to justify capital expenditure on feeder automation projects to regulatory commissions.
