# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Feeder Automation. List any four functional pillars of feeder automation in a smart grid.**

**Model Answer:**

**Feeder Automation (FA):** It is a subsystem of Distribution Automation that involves the automatic monitoring, control, and protection of medium-voltage distribution feeders using remote terminal units, intelligent electronic devices, reclosers, sectionalizers, and advanced communication infrastructure to enable rapid fault detection, isolation, and restoration.

**Four Functional Pillars:**
1. **FDIR** — Fault Detection, Isolation, and Restoration (self-healing).
2. **Volt/VAR Optimization (VVO)** — Dynamic control of voltage and reactive power.
3. **Feeder Reconfiguration** — Topology switching for loss minimization.
4. **Automatic Metering / AMI Integration** — Bidirectional data exchange.

*(Examiner Note: Any four valid functions accepted.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a Recloser and a Sectionalizer in a distribution feeder automation scheme.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Recloser | Sectionalizer |
|---|---|---|
| Function | **Interrupts fault current** by tripping and reclosing | **Does not interrupt fault current**; only counts and opens on command |
| Operating Principle | Independent time-current characteristic (fast & slow curves) | Operates based on **predetermined count** of upstream recloser operations |
| Self-Containment | Has built-in CT, PT, and protection logic | Usually requires an upstream protective device |
| Lockout | Yes — after preset number of shots | No — opens only when upstream locks out |
| Typical Installation | Substation feeder exit or major junctions | Mid-feeder at sectionalization points |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (1 × 14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the FDIR (Fault Detection, Isolation, and Restoration) process in a distribution feeder with a neat block diagram. How does this contribute to the self-healing capability of a smart grid?**

**Model Answer:**

The FDIR process is the heart of feeder automation. It comprises **four sequential phases**:

**1. Fault Detection (Time: < 50 ms):**
- Overcurrent / distance relays at the substation detect abnormal current/voltage.
- The fault type (L-G, L-L, 3-Φ) is classified using phasor measurement.
- A fault event is generated and broadcast via IEC 61850 GOOSE messages to all peer IEDs.

**2. Fault Localization (Time: < 200 ms):**
- Methods include:
  - **Travelling-wave-based detection** (high-frequency transient analysis).
  - **Impedance-based measurement** using V/I phasors.
  - **Fault Passage Indicators (FPIs)** at sectionalizer locations.
- The faulty segment between two adjacent automated switches is identified.

**3. Isolation (Time: 1–5 seconds):**
- Automated switches immediately upstream and downstream of the fault **OPEN simultaneously**.
- The smallest possible zone is electrically isolated.
- All other healthy sections remain energized.

**4. Restoration (Time: 10–60 seconds):**
- The upstream healthy zone: Main switch closes — supply restored immediately.
- The downstream healthy zone: **Tie-switch** closes, connecting to an alternate feeder (backup path).
- The faulty zone remains isolated until repair crew arrives.

**Self-Healing Contribution:**
The FDIR process transforms the passive radial distribution network into an **active, intelligent, self-reconfiguring system**. It reduces restoration time from **hours to seconds**, achieving the smart grid's primary reliability goal.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A 4-zone radial feeder serves the following loads. Compute SAIFI, SAIDI, CAIDI, ASAI, and ENS.**

| Zone | Customers | Failure Rate (λ/yr) | Outage Time (hr/yr) | Average Load (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 2 | 4 | 500 |
| 2 | 400 | 1 | 3 | 800 |
| 3 | 600 | 1 | 6 | 1200 |
| 4 | 800 | 3 | 2 | 1500 |

**Step 1: Total Customers**
$$
N_{\text{total}} = 200 + 400 + 600 + 800 = 2000 \;\; \text{customers}
$$

**Step 2: SAIFI Calculation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{\sum \lambda_i \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(2)(200) + (1)(400) + (1)(600) + (3)(800)}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{400 + 400 + 600 + 2400}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3800}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= 1.9 \;\; \text{interruptions/customer-year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: SAIDI Calculation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{\sum U_i \cdot N_i}{N_{\text{total}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(4)(200) + (3)(400) + (6)(600) + (2)(800)}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{800 + 1200 + 3600 + 1600}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7200}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= 3.6 \;\; \text{hours/customer-year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: CAIDI Calculation**
$$
\text{CAIDI} = \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} = \frac{3.6}{1.9} = 1.895 \;\; \text{hours/interruption}
$$

**Step 5: ASAI Calculation**
$$
\text{ASAI} = 1 - \frac{3.6}{8760} = 0.999589 = 99.9589\%
$$

**Step 6: ENS Calculation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ENS} &= \sum L_i \cdot U_i \\[4pt]
&= (500 \times 4) + (800 \times 3) + (1200 \times 6) + (1500 \times 2) \\[4pt]
&= 2000 + 2400 + 7200 + 3000 \\[4pt]
&= 14600 \;\; \text{kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:**
- SAIFI = **1.9** int/customer-yr
- SAIDI = **3.6** hr/customer-yr
- CAIDI = **1.895** hr/int
- ASAI = **99.9589%**
- ENS = **14,600 kWh/year**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Describe the three-tier architecture of Feeder Automation. Compare centralized vs distributed feeder automation in terms of intelligence, communication requirement, and speed.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three-Tier Architecture:**

1. **Tier 1 — Field Level:**
   - Components: Reclosers, sectionalizers, RMUs, RTUs, IEDs.
   - Function: Real-time protection and local data acquisition.

2. **Tier 2 — Substation (Station) Level:**
   - Components: BCUs, substation RTUs.
   - Function: Local supervisory control and inter-device coordination.

3. **Tier 3 — Control Center Level:**
   - Components: SCADA, DMS, OMS, ADMS.
   - Function: Wide-area monitoring, FLISR, Volt/VAR optimization.

**Centralized vs. Distributed FA Comparison:**

| Parameter | Centralized FA | Distributed FA |
|---|---|---|
| **Intelligence Location** | Single control center (ADMS) | Local at each IED (peer-to-peer) |
| **Communication Need** | Continuous SCADA telemetry | Peer-to-peer GOOSE messaging only |
| **Decision Speed** | Slow (minutes) — depends on latency | Fast (sub-second) |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes — control center outage halts FA | No — fully autonomous |
| **Algorithm Complexity** | High — global optimization | Low — local logic |
| **Cost** | High (centralized servers + comm) | Moderate |
| **Example** | SCADA-driven FLISR | IEC 61850 GOOSE peer-to-peer scheme |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain with a diagram the Recloser–Sectionalizer coordination in a 3-shot scheme. Why is sectionalizer count setting critical?**

**Model Answer:**

In a typical **3-shot coordination scheme**, the recloser operates on two time-current curves:

**Shot 1 (Fast Curve):**
- Trip time: **0.05–0.1 s**
- Recloser opens to clear **temporary faults** (e.g., tree branch, lightning).
- Recloses after **2 seconds**.

**Shot 2 (Slow Curve):**
- Trip time: **0.3–0.5 s**
- Recloses after **5 seconds**.

**Shot 3 (Slow Curve, Final):**
- Trip time: **1.0–2.0 s**
- After this shot, recloser enters **lockout** (permanent open).

**Sectionalizer Operation:**
- Counts fault-current passages during recloser operations.
- Counter is preset (e.g., **2 or 3 shots**).
- When upstream recloser locks out, sectionalizer **opens automatically**, isolating the downstream faulted section from the rest of the feeder.

**Why Sectionalizer Count Setting is Critical:**
- **Too low:** Sectionalizer opens before recloser can clear a temporary fault → unnecessary outage for downstream customers.
- **Too high:** Faulty section remains connected during multiple shots → worsens equipment damage and customer outage.
- **Optimal setting** = (Number of recloser shots − 1), ensuring sectionalizer only opens on a **permanent fault**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** SAIDI is in **hours**, SAIFI is **dimensionless** (interruptions/customer). Many students interchange them — instant loss of 2 marks.
> 2. **Formula Confusion:** CAIDI ≠ SAIDI. CAIDI = SAIDI/SAIFI (gives hours per interruption).
> 3. **Forgetting ASUI:** ASAI + ASUI = 1. Examiners test whether you remember both.
> 4. **Recloser-Sectionalizer Misconception:** A sectionalizer **never interrupts fault current** — it only counts. Mention this clearly to score full marks.
> 5. **Diagram Omission:** Always draw a **block diagram** even if not explicitly asked — KTU awards 1–2 marks for diagrams.
> 6. **Mixing Up FDIR Steps:** Always write the order — **Detection → Isolation → Restoration**, not arbitrary sequence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feeder Automation (FA)** = automatic monitoring, control, and protection of MV distribution feeders.
- **Core Function:** **FDIR** (Fault Detection, Isolation, Restoration) — the self-healing heart.
- **Three-Tier Architecture:** Field → Substation → Control Center.
- **Key Devices:** Recloser (interrupts fault current), Sectionalizer (counts and opens on lockout), RMU, FPI, RTU, IED.
- **Reliability Indices:**
  - **SAIFI** = Σ(λᵢ × Nᵢ) / N_total — interruptions/customer/year
  - **SAIDI** = Σ(Uᵢ × Nᵢ) / N_total — hours/customer/year
  - **CAIDI** = SAIDI / SAIFI — hours per interruption
  - **ASAI** = 1 − (SAIDI/8760) — dimensionless availability fraction
  - **ASUI** = 1 − ASAI
  - **ENS** = Σ(Lᵢ × Uᵢ) — kWh/year
  - **MAIFI** = momentary average interruption frequency index
- **Recloser-Sectionalizer Coordination:** Recloser operates on fast+slow curves with 3–4 shots; sectionalizer counts and opens only at lockout.
- **Communication Protocols:** IEC 61850 (substation), DNP3 (SCADA), Modbus (RTU), MQTT (IoT sensors).
- **Centralized FA:** SCADA/ADMS-driven, high latency but global optimization.
- **Distributed FA:** Peer-to-peer IED communication, sub-second response, no single point of failure.
- **Standards:** IEEE 1547, IEC 61850, IEEE P1366 (reliability indices).
- **Key Benefits:** 30–60% SAIDI reduction, 70% fewer field crew dispatches, ±5% voltage regulation, improved customer satisfaction.
- **Volt/VAR Optimization (VVO)** is the second major FA function — minimizes losses and maintains voltage profile using capacitor banks and voltage regulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Detection, Isolation, and Service Restoration (FDISR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault Detection, Isolation, and Service Restoration (FDISR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Detection, Isolation, and Service Restoration (FDISR)** is a self-healing function of the smart grid distribution automation system that, upon the occurrence of a fault in the distribution network, automatically detects the faulted section, electrically isolates it through the operation of switching devices, and reconfigures the network topology to restore supply to the maximum number of healthy (de-energized but unfaulted) load points using alternative power sources.

FDISR is formally recognized as the cornerstone of **Distribution Automation (DA)** under the IEEE 1547 series and the **IEC 61850** communication standard, enabling **self-healing grids**. It is also internationally referred to as **FLISR** (Fault Location, Isolation, and Service Restoration) in North American utility practice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Immune System**
> Imagine your body as a distribution grid. When a virus (fault) attacks a specific organ (feeder section), the immune system (FDISR system) first **detects** the infection via symptoms (current/voltage sensors), then **isolates** the affected area by surrounding it with white blood cells (open switches/reclosers), and finally **restores** function to the rest of the body by rerouting blood flow through alternative vessels (tie-switches connecting to adjacent healthy feeders). Just as you don't shut down the whole body, FDISR does not de-energize the entire substation — it surgically operates on the faulted zone only.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Mandatory Values)

- **Reliability Index — SAIDI (System Average Interruption Duration Index):** measured in **minutes/customer-year**.
- **Reliability Index — SAIFI (System Average Interruption Frequency Index):** measured in **interruptions/customer-year**.
- **Reliability Index — CAIDI (Customer Average Interruption Duration Index):** measured in **minutes/interruption**.
- **Restoration Time Objective:** typically **< 1 minute** for automated FDISR; manual restoration may take **45–180 minutes**.
- **Fault Clearing Time:** **50–100 ms** for primary protection; **300–500 ms** for backup.

## 1.4 Primary Components of an FDISR System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs):** numerical relays, recloser controllers.
> - **Sensors:** CTs (Current Transformers), PTs (Potential Transformers), **PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units)**.
> - **Communication Backbone:** fiber optics, 4G/5G, PLC, IEC 61850 / DNP3 protocols.
> - **Master Station:** DMS (Distribution Management System) / SCADA control center.
> - **Switching Devices:** Reclosers, Sectionalizers, **Tie Switches**, Load Break Switches.
> - **Decision Engine:** heuristic / expert system / AI-based optimizer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — The Three Functional Pillars

### 2.1.1 Fault Detection
- **Mechanism:** Overcurrent (50/51 function), distance (67 function), or differential (87 function) protection relays sense abnormal currents/voltages exceeding pickup thresholds.
- **Why:** Smart grid feeders have radial topology (mostly) where load current is well-defined; any deviation signals a fault.
- **How:** IEDs sample waveforms at **4.8 kHz–3.84 MHz** (per IEC 61850-9-2 SV stream) and compare against pickup values:
$$I_{pickup} = k_s \cdot I_{load,max}$$
where $k_s$ is the **safety factor**, typically **1.2 to 1.5**.

### 2.1.2 Fault Isolation
- **Mechanism:** Trip commands are sent to the recloser immediately upstream of the faulted zone. Adjacent reclosers and sectionalizers perform coordinated opening to bound the faulted segment.
- **Why:** Confirms single isolation zone and prevents back-feeding from alternate sources.
- **How:** Automated switch sequence (Open-Open-Open) along the fault path; closing of tie-switch is **inhibited** until isolation is confirmed.

### 2.1.3 Service Restoration
- **Mechanism:** Network reconfiguration algorithm identifies the optimal set of normally-open **tie switches (N.O. TS)** to close, supplying the de-energized healthy zone from adjacent feeders without overloading them.
- **Why:** Minimizes **ENS (Energy Not Supplied)** and improves SAIDI/SAIFI.
- **How:** Graph-search and load-flow-based algorithm validates thermal and voltage limits before issuing the close command.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $SAIFI = \dfrac{\sum \lambda_i \cdot N_i}{\sum N_i}$ | System Average Interruption Frequency | $\text{int/cust-yr}$ |
| 2 | $SAIDI = \dfrac{\sum U_i \cdot N_i}{\sum N_i}$ | System Average Interruption Duration | $\text{min/cust-yr}$ |
| 3 | $CAIDI = \dfrac{SAIDI}{SAIFI}$ | Customer Average Interruption Duration | $\text{min/int}$ |
| 4 | $ASAI = 1 - \dfrac{SAIDI}{8760 \cdot 60}$ | Average Service Availability Index | $\text{p.u.}$ |
| 5 | $ENS = \sum_{i} L_i \cdot U_i$ | Energy Not Supplied | $\text{kWh}$ |
| 6 | $I_{pickup} = k_s \cdot I_{L,\max}$ | Relay pickup current | $\text{A}$ |
| 7 | $V_{pu,\min} \geq 0.95$ | Voltage constraint for restoration | $\text{p.u.}$ |
| 8 | $S_{k,alt} \geq S_{L,transfer}$ | Feeder thermal capacity constraint | $\text{kVA}$ |
| 9 | $Z_{bus} \cdot I = V$ | Load flow nodal equation (matrix form) | — |
| 10 | $t_{op} = TMS \cdot \dfrac{k}{(M^p - 1)}$ | IDMT overcurrent relay operating time | $\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are the contractual KPIs between utilities and regulatory commissions (e.g., KSERC in Kerala). FDISR directly reduces SAIDI/SAIFI, saving utilities penalty charges and improving customer satisfaction.

## 2.3 Self-Healing Decision Architecture

The smart grid FDISR operates in a hierarchical control structure:

1. **Local Level (Bay Controller):** Performs instantaneous fault detection and isolation using IED logic (< 100 ms).
2. **Substation Level (Substation Server / RTU):** Coordinates multiple feeders, performs back-up detection.
3. **Control Center Level (DMS / ADMS):** Runs reconfiguration algorithms for service restoration (1–5 s timescale).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Kerala KSEB Scenario:** During the 2018 Kerala floods, automated FDISR in pilot feeders of Thiruvananthapuram reduced outage time from 4 hours to under 12 minutes.
- **Global:** EPRI studies indicate FLISR reduces SAIDI by **30%–50%** and SAIFI by **20%–35%**.
- **Microgrids:** FDISR enables seamless islanding and resynchronization in grid-tied microgrids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem Context

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
> A 11 kV radial distribution feeder supplies three loads through reclosers R1, R2, R3 and is provided with a tie-switch TS to an adjacent feeder. The relay coordination pickup currents and loading data are given. Compute the SAIDI and SAIFI before and after FDISR deployment, and verify the thermal limit of the alternate feeder.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

- Number of customers on feeder: $N_1 = 200$, $N_2 = 150$, $N_3 = 100$.
- Fault rate per section: $\lambda_1 = 0.5$, $\lambda_2 = 0.3$, $\lambda_3 = 0.2$ (faults/year).
- Average restoration time (manual): $r_{manual} = 90$ minutes.
- Restoration time (FDISR automated): $r_{auto} = 1$ minute.
- Adjacent feeder thermal capacity: $S_{alt,\max} = 5\text{ MVA}$.
- Load to be transferred: $S_{transfer} = 3.5\text{ MVA}$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation — SAIFI (Before FDISR)

**Step 1:** Write the general SAIFI formula.

$$
SAIFI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i \cdot N_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values section-wise.

$$
SAIFI_{manual} = \frac{(0.5)(200) + (0.3)(150) + (0.2)(100)}{200 + 150 + 100}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$
(0.5)(200) = 100
$$
$$
(0.3)(150) = 45
$$
$$
(0.2)(100) = 20
$$
$$
\text{Numerator} = 100 + 45 + 20 = 165
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$
\text{Denominator} = 200 + 150 + 100 = 450
$$

**Step 5:** Final result for SAIFI (before FDISR).

$$
SAIFI_{manual} = \frac{165}{450} = 0.3667 \text{ int/cust-yr}
$$

**Step 6:** SAIFI remains unchanged by FDISR (frequency is not eliminated, only duration is reduced).

$$
SAIFI_{FDISR} = 0.3667 \text{ int/cust-yr}
$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation — SAIDI (Before FDISR)

**Step 1:** Write the SAIDI formula.

$$
SAIDI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \cdot N_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i} = \frac{\sum \lambda_i \cdot r_i \cdot N_i}{\sum N_i}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute with $r = 90$ min (manual).

$$
SAIDI_{manual} = \frac{(0.5 \cdot 90 \cdot 200) + (0.3 \cdot 90 \cdot 150) + (0.2 \cdot 90 \cdot 100)}{450}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator expansion.

$$
(0.5 \cdot 90 \cdot 200) = 9000
$$
$$
(0.3 \cdot 90 \cdot 150) = 4050
$$
$$
(0.2 \cdot 90 \cdot 100) = 1800
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = 9000 + 4050 + 1800 = 14850
$$

**Step 4:** Final SAIDI (manual).

$$
SAIDI_{manual} = \frac{14850}{450} = 33.0 \text{ min/cust-yr}
$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation — SAIDI (After FDISR, $r_{auto} = 1$ min)

**Step 1:** Recompute numerator with $r_{auto} = 1$ min.

$$
SAIDI_{FDISR} = \frac{(0.5)(1)(200) + (0.3)(1)(150) + (0.2)(1)(100)}{450}
$$

**Step 2:** Numerator simplification.

$$
100 + 45 + 20 = 165
$$

**Step 3:** Final result.

$$
SAIDI_{FDISR} = \frac{165}{450} = 0.3667 \text{ min/cust-yr}
$$

### 3.1.5 Derivation — Percentage Improvement

**Step 1:** Percentage reduction in SAIDI.

$$
\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{SAIDI_{manual} - SAIDI_{FDISR}}{SAIDI_{manual}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
= \frac{33.0 - 0.3667}{33.0} \times 100 = 98.89\%
$$

### 3.1.6 Thermal Constraint Verification (Alternate Feeder)

**Step 1:** Restoration Constraint.

$$
S_{transfer} \leq S_{alt,\max}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
3.5 \text{ MVA} \leq 5.0 \text{ MVA} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** Load factor after transfer.

$$
\text{Loading} = \frac{3.5}{5.0} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

Acceptable under IEEE 1547-2018 loading limits ($\leq 80\%$ for emergency transfer).

### 3.1.7 ENS (Energy Not Supplied) Calculation

Assume each customer draws 1.5 kW average.

$$
ENS = \sum L_i \cdot U_i = (1.5 \text{ kW/cust}) \cdot (SAIDI \cdot \sum N_i) / 60
$$

**Step 2:** Manual case.

$$
ENS_{manual} = 1.5 \times 33.0 \times 450 / 60 = 371.25 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3:** FDISR case.

$$
ENS_{FDISR} = 1.5 \times 0.3667 \times 450 / 60 = 4.125 \text{ kWh}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Manual Restoration | FDISR | % Reduction |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| SAIFI (int/cust-yr) | 0.3667 | 0.3667 | 0% |
| SAIDI (min/cust-yr) | 33.0 | 0.3667 | **98.89%** |
| ENS (kWh) | 371.25 | 4.125 | **98.89%** |
| Loading of alt feeder | — | 70% | Within limits |
| CAIDI (min/int) | 90.0 | 1.0 | 98.89% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FDISR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Sensors: CT, PT, PMU] --> B[IED Bay Controller]
    B --> C{Protection Logic}
    C -- Trip --> D[Recloser / Sectionalizer]
    D --> E[Isolated Faulted Zone]
    C -- Status Data --> F[RTU Substation Server]
    F --> G[Distribution Management System DMS]
    G --> H[Reconfiguration Algorithm]
    H --> I{Load Flow Check}
    I -- Valid --> J[Close Tie Switch]
    I -- Invalid --> K[Alert Operator]
    J --> L[Restored Healthy Zone]
    G --> M[SCADA HMI Display]
    G --> N[Historical Database]
```

## 4.2 FDISR Decision State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> FaultDetected: Overcurrent sensed
    FaultDetected --> Trip: Pickup exceeded
    Trip --> Isolation: Open reclosers
    Isolation --> FaultConfirmed: Sectionalizer OK
    Isolation --> FalseTrip: No fault
    FalseTrip --> Normal: Reclose
    FaultConfirmed --> Reconfiguration: Run algorithm
    Reconfiguration --> LoadFlow: Validate
    LoadFlow --> TieClose: Within limits
    LoadFlow --> ManualOverride: Exceeds limits
    TieClose --> Restored
    ManualOverride --> OperatorAction
    Restored --> Normal
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Communication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1: Field Devices - Recloser, Switchgear]
    L2[Level 2: Bay Controller - IED]
    L3[Level 3: Substation Server - RTU]
    L4[Level 4: Control Center - ADMS]
    L1 -- IEC 61850 MMS GOOSE --> L2
    L2 -- DNP3 / IEC 60870 --> L3
    L3 -- ICCP / VPN Fiber --> L4
    L4 -- 4G 5G PLC --> L1
```

## 4.4 Radial Feeder with FDISR Switching Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Substation Bus 11kV] --> R1[Recloser R1]
    R1 --> R2[Recloser R2]
    R2 --> R3[Recloser R3]
    R3 --> L1[Load Section A]
    R2 --> L2[Load Section B]
    R1 --> L3[Load Section C]
    TS[Tie Switch N.O.] -. Normally Open .-> R2
    ALT[Adjacent Feeder] --> TS
    FAULT[Faulted Zone here]:::fault
    classDef fault fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define FDISR. List its three functional stages.**

**Model Answer:**
FDISR stands for **Fault Detection, Isolation, and Service Restoration**. It is the self-healing function of a smart distribution grid.
- **Detection:** IEDs sense abnormal overcurrent/voltage.
- **Isolation:** Reclosers/Sectionalizers open to isolate the faulted segment.
- **Restoration:** DMS re-routes supply through tie-switches to healthy zones.

*(3 marks — definition 1, three stages 2)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between SAIDI and SAIFI.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SAIDI | SAIFI |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Full form | System Average Interruption Duration Index | System Average Interruption Frequency Index |
| Unit | min/cust-yr | int/cust-yr |
| Measures | Average outage duration | Average outage frequency |
| Affected by FDISR | Yes (drastically reduced) | No (frequency unchanged) |

*(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often confuse **SAIDI with CAIDI** — CAIDI = SAIDI / SAIFI.
> - Many forget the units of reliability indices (write them explicitly).
> - In numerical problems, do not skip the substitution step — examiners award 1 mark for the substitution line.
> - When asked to **list**, do not write paragraphs — use bullet points for 1/2 mark per point.
> - For tie-switch restoration, you **must** validate thermal and voltage constraints before closing.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of a self-healing smart grid FDISR system. List the major components.

**Model Answer:**

The FDISR architecture consists of four functional layers (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

**Layer 1 — Sensing Layer:**
- CTs, PTs, PMUs, smart meters.
- Function: Real-time measurement of currents, voltages, phase angles.

**Layer 2 — Protection Layer (Bay Level):**
- Numerical IEDs, recloser controllers.
- Function: Trip command generation within 50–100 ms.

**Layer 3 — Communication Layer:**
- IEC 61850, DNP3, fiber optics, 4G/5G.
- Function: Secure bidirectional data transfer.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (DMS/ADMS):**
- SCADA + reconfiguration algorithm.
- Function: Service restoration via tie-switch closure.

**Major Components:** IEDs, RTU, SCADA, DMS, Reclosers, Sectionalizers, Tie Switches, Communication Network.

*(7 marks — diagram 3, explanation 2, components 2)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A radial feeder with 500 customers experiences 4 sustained faults per year. Average manual restoration time is 2 hours. After installing FDISR, restoration time reduces to 90 seconds. Calculate SAIDI and CAIDI before and after FDISR.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $N = 500$ customers
- $\lambda = 4$ faults/year (total sustained interruptions)
- $r_{manual} = 120$ min
- $r_{FDISR} = 1.5$ min (90 sec)

**Step 2:** SAIFI (same in both cases).

$$
SAIFI = \frac{\lambda \cdot N}{N} = 4 \text{ int/cust-yr}
$$

**Step 3:** SAIDI (manual).

$$
SAIDI_{manual} = \frac{\lambda \cdot r \cdot N}{N} = (4)(120) = 480 \text{ min/cust-yr}
$$

**Step 4:** SAIDI (FDISR).

$$
SAIDI_{FDISR} = (4)(1.5) = 6 \text{ min/cust-yr}
$$

**Step 5:** CAIDI (manual).

$$
CAIDI_{manual} = \frac{480}{4} = 120 \text{ min/int}
$$

**Step 6:** CAIDI (FDISR).

$$
CAIDI_{FDISR} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \text{ min/int}
$$

**Step 7:** Percentage improvement.

$$
\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{480 - 6}{480} \times 100 = 98.75\%
$$

*(7 marks — formula 2, substitution 2, answer 2, improvement 1)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of intelligent electronic devices (IEDs) and IEC 61850 communication in FDISR.

**Model Answer:**

**Role of IEDs:**
- IEDs are microprocessor-based controllers integrating protection, measurement, recording, and communication.
- In FDISR, IEDs perform:
  - Fault detection (50/51, 67 functions).
  - Trip signal generation to breakers/reclosers.
  - Sequence-of-events (SOE) recording with 1 ms resolution.
  - GOOSE message broadcasting per IEC 61850.

**Role of IEC 61850:**
- Standard for substation communication ensuring interoperability between multivendor IEDs.
- Key services in FDISR:
  - **GOOSE (Generic Object-Oriented Substation Event):** < 4 ms latency for trip signals.
  - **Sampled Values (SV):** Streamed CT/PT waveforms at 4.8 kHz.
  - **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification):** Reporting to SCADA.
- Enables horizontal (peer-to-peer IED) communication eliminating single point of failure.

*(7 marks — IED role 3, IEC 61850 features 4)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the service restoration algorithm in a distribution feeder with tie switches. Mention the constraints checked.

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1 — Pre-fault Network Snapshot:**
Store the open/closed status of all switches and the load on each section.

**Step 2 — Fault Isolation Confirmation:**
Verify isolation of faulted zone (status feedback from IEDs).

**Step 3 — Candidate Tie Switches Enumeration:**
Identify normally-open tie switches whose closing can supply the de-energized healthy zone.

**Step 4 — Load Flow Validation (Constraints):**

- **Constraint 1 — Thermal (Capacity):**
$$
S_{transfer} \leq S_{alt,max}
$$

- **Constraint 2 — Voltage Profile:**
$$
V_{pu} \geq 0.95 \text{ p.u. (steady)}; V_{pu} \geq 0.90 \text{ p.u. (transient)}
$$

- **Constraint 3 — Protection Coordination:**
Alternate feeder's relay settings must not be violated.

- **Constraint 4 — Radiality:**
The restored network must remain radial (no loops).

**Step 5 — Optimal Selection:**
Among all feasible tie switches, select the one with minimum switching cost and minimum loss.

**Step 6 — Issue Close Command:**
SCADA/DMS sends close command to the chosen tie switch.

**Step 7 — Post-restoration Monitoring:**
Continuous monitoring via PMUs for 5 minutes to confirm stability.

*(7 marks — algorithm 4, constraints 3)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FDISR**

- **FDISR = Detection + Isolation + Restoration** (FLISR is the same thing, US terminology).
- **Self-healing time objective:** < 1 minute (full automation).
- **Primary sensors:** CT, PT, PMU, smart meter, FPI (Fault Passage Indicator).
- **Switchgear:** Recloser (auto reclosing), Sectionalizer (auto load-break), Tie Switch (N.O. inter-feeder link).
- **Communication:** IEC 61850 (GOOSE < 4 ms), DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, IEEE C37.118 (PMU).
- **Reliability Indices (must remember):** SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, ASAI, ENS, AENS.
- **Master Formulae (must memorize):**
  - $SAIFI = \sum \lambda_i N_i / \sum N_i$
  - $SAIDI = \sum \lambda_i r_i N_i / \sum N_i$
  - $CAIDI = SAIDI / SAIFI$
  - $ASAI = 1 - SAIDI/(8760 \times 60)$
- **Restoration constraints:** thermal (S), voltage (V pu ≥ 0.95), radiality, protection coordination.
- **Hierarchical control:** Field → Bay → Substation → Control Center (ADMS).
- **Kerala KSEB example:** Smart grid pilot in Thrissur using Schneider ADMS reduced SAIDI by 40%.
- **FDISR is essential for:** Smart Grid KPI improvement, microgrid islanding, DER integration, regulatory compliance.
- **Do NOT confuse:** SAIFI (frequency, unchanged) vs SAIDI (duration, drastically reduced by FDISR).
- **AI/ML in FDISR:** Modern systems use ANN, fuzzy logic, and graph-search (Dijkstra/A*) for optimal reconfiguration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geographic Information System (GIS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geographic Information System (GIS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌍 Geographic Information System (GIS) — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geographic Information System (GIS)** is a computer-based information system designed to **capture, store, manipulate, analyze, manage, and present spatial or geographic data**. In the context of smart grid engineering, GIS serves as the foundational geospatial platform that maps, models, and manages every physical asset of the electrical power network — from generation plants and transmission towers to distribution feeders, transformers, and end-consumer meters.

According to the **National Research Council (US)**, GIS is formally defined as a *"system of hardware, software, data, procedures, and people used for the acquisition, storage, analysis, and management of spatial data."*

For KTU 2024 Scheme Power & Energy Engineering contexts, the working definition used in Board Examinations is:

> A GIS integrates **hardware, software, geospatial data, spatial analysis tools, and trained personnel** to enable the visualization, querying, and analysis of geographically referenced information about electrical grid assets.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of GIS as "Google Maps on Steroids for Power Engineers."**

Imagine you are an electricity distribution engineer in Kerala. You have:
- A physical map showing every **electric pole** in your district
- Layers of information attached to each pole: its **transformer rating**, **connected load**, **maintenance history**, **age**, and **last fault date**
- A tool that lets you click on a pole and instantly see: "If this pole fails, **how many customers** will lose power, what is the **shortest alternate route**, and what is the **estimated restoration time**?"

**That is GIS.** It is essentially a **smart, layered, geo-referenced digital twin** of the physical electrical network.

A simpler analogy:

| Familiar System | What it Stores | GIS Equivalent for Power |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Google Maps** | Roads, buildings, traffic | Electrical lines, substations, load points |
| **Zomato** | Restaurant locations + ratings | Transformer locations + health ratings |
| **Wikipedia infobox** | Text + image per topic | Asset data + spatial coordinates |

---

## 1.3 Five Pillars (Components) of GIS

The **Burrough's Five Components Model** is the universally accepted GIS architecture and is **directly tested in KTU exams**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Components of GIS** (Mandatory KTU Definition):
> 1. **Hardware** — Computers (workstations/servers), GPS receivers, digitizers, plotters, scanners
> 2. **Software** — GIS engine (e.g., ESRI ArcGIS, QGIS, GE Smallworld), DBMS, spatial analysis tools
> 3. **Data** — The most critical component (~80% of project cost); includes spatial + non-spatial (attribute) data
> 4. **People** — GIS analysts, power engineers, database administrators, field surveyors
> 5. **Methods (Procedures)** — Standard operating protocols for data collection, updating, analysis, and dissemination

---

## 1.4 Why GIS is Indispensable in Smart Grids

> [!NOTE]
> A Smart Grid without GIS is like a **car without a dashboard** — you have the engine (sensors, AMI, SCADA) but no way to **see, navigate, or plan** with it.

**Key Functional Roles of GIS in Smart Grid:**
- **Asset Management** — pole-top transformer, switchgear, conductor inventory
- **Outage Management System (OMS)** integration
- **Distribution Network Planning** — feeder routing, load forecasting geographically
- **Consumer Indexing** — mapping every HT/LT consumer to a feeder
- **Right-of-Way (RoW)** documentation for transmission lines
- **Renewable Integration** — solar/wind siting based on terrain and grid proximity
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Architecture of GIS

GIS functions on a layered model where each layer represents a thematic category of information. In smart grid applications, typical layers include:

- **Layer 1:** Base map (roads, rivers, terrain)
- **Layer 2:** Substations and switching stations
- **Layer 3:** Transmission lines (EHV) and towers
- **Layer 4:** Distribution feeders (11 kV / 22 kV / 33 kV)
- **Layer 5:** LT network and consumer connections
- **Layer 6:** Demand hot-spots, load density heat-maps

---

## 2.2 Two Fundamental Spatial Data Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction — Always tested as 3 or 14 mark question:**

| Feature | **Vector Data Model** | **Raster Data Model** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Structure | Points, Lines, Polygons | Grid of cells (pixels) |
| Storage | Coordinates (x, y) | Cell values in a matrix |
| Best For | Discrete assets (poles, lines) | Continuous phenomena (load density, solar irradiance) |
| Resolution | Scale-dependent | Cell-size dependent |
| Example in Smart Grid | Pole, transformer location | Heat-map of consumption per km² |
| File Format | Shapefile (.shp), DWG | GeoTIFF, IMG |
| Database | Object-oriented / Relational | Matrix / Array-based |

---

## 2.3 Coordinate Systems & Map Projections

> [!NOTE]
> **The Earth is a 3D ellipsoid (oblate spheroid) — but maps are 2D planes. A map projection is the mathematical transformation that converts curved Earth surface to flat paper.**

Two primary coordinate frameworks used in GIS for Indian power systems:

1. **Geographic Coordinate System (GCS)** — Latitude (φ) and Longitude (λ) in degrees, referenced to **WGS-84** (World Geodetic System 1984) datum.
2. **Projected Coordinate System (PCS)** — Uses linear units (meters) on a flat plane. For India, the standard is **UTM (Universal Transverse Mercator)** Zone 44N/45N and the Survey of India uses **Lambert Conformal Conic** projections.

**Indian Standard Datum:** The **Everest-1830 spheroid** with **Kalianpur-1880 datum** is used by Survey of India. Modern GIS systems use **WGS-84**.

---

## 2.4 🧮 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking table syntax in markdown rendering.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Application in Smart Grid |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Haversine Distance (Great-Circle) | $d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$ | $R$ = 6,371 km (Earth radius), $\phi$ = latitude (rad), $\lambda$ = longitude (rad) | Calculate shortest line distance between two substations |
| 2 | Euclidean 2D Distance | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | $x, y$ in meters | Pole-to-pole span calculation |
| 3 | Area of a Spherical Polygon | $A = R^2 \cdot \vert E \vert$ | $R$ in km, $E$ = spherical excess (sr) | Area of electrical zone / circle |
| 4 | Spherical Excess (for triangles) | $E = A + B + C - \pi$ | Angles in radians | Triangulation-based survey |
| 5 | Raster Resolution (Spatial) | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Ground Distance}}{\text{Number of Pixels}}$ | meters/pixel | Resolution of satellite load map |
| 6 | Map Scale | $\text{Scale} = \dfrac{\text{Map Distance}}{\text{Ground Distance}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Scale Denominator}}$ | Unitless ratio | 1:50,000 survey maps |
| 7 | Power Line Sag Approx. | $S \approx \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8T}$ | $w$ = weight/unit length, $L$ = span, $T$ = tension | Line length estimation for GIS buffer |
| 8 | Buffer Zone Width (RoW) | $W_{RoW} = k \cdot V_{kV} + C$ | $k$ ≈ 0.25–0.5 m/kV, $C$ = clearance (m) | Right-of-Way around transmission lines |
| 9 | Voltage Drop (radial feeder) | $\Delta V = I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \cdot L$ | $V$, $A$, $\Omega$/km | Geo-correlation of voltage complaints |
| 10 | Solar Irradiance (tilted surface) | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d \left(\tfrac{1+\cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \rho \left(\tfrac{1-\cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | W/m² | Solar farm siting using GIS layers |

---

## 2.5 Spatial Analysis Functions (Operationally Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the "verbs" of GIS — every smart grid use case combines one or more of these:**

- **Buffering** — Creating a zone of specified width around an asset (e.g., **Right-of-Way** around an EHV line)
- **Overlay** — Combining two layers (e.g., flood-prone map + substation map → risk assessment)
- **Proximity Analysis** — Finding nearest feeder to a new consumer
- **Network Analysis** — Shortest path, connectivity tracing, load flow visualization
- **Spatial Interpolation** — IDW (Inverse Distance Weighting), Kriging for load density mapping
- **Reclassification** — Re-grouping zones (e.g., high/medium/low consumer density)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application Domain | Specific Use of GIS |
|:---|:---|
| **Distribution Utilities (KSEB, Tata Power, BSES)** | Consumer indexing, asset mapping, O&M |
| **Disaster Management (Kerala 2018 floods)** | Substation inundation risk mapping |
| **EV Charging Infrastructure Planning** | Site selection based on demand density & grid capacity |
| **Rooftop Solar (PM-Surya Ghar Yojana)** | Rooftop area estimation using satellite imagery |
| **Smart City Mission** | Multi-utility mapping (power, water, gas) on a single platform |
| **Right-of-Way (RoW) Disputes** | Geometric documentation of corridor for legal records |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation: Haversine Formula for Great-Circle Distance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Haversine"?** — The word is a contraction of "half-versed-sine." The function is preferred over the basic law of cosines for small distances on a sphere because it avoids floating-point precision loss.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with the spherical law of cosines**
For two points on a sphere of radius $R$ with central angle $\Delta\sigma$ between them:

$$
\cos\!\left(\Delta\sigma\right) = \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos\!\left(\Delta\lambda\right)
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate the angle**
This is the great-circle (arc) distance formula:

$$
\Delta\sigma = \arccos\!\left[\sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos\!\left(\Delta\lambda\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3: Multiply both sides by $R$ for arc length**

$$
d = R \cdot \arccos\!\left[\sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos\!\left(\Delta\lambda\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4: Apply the half-angle identity to convert to Haversine form**
Using the identity:

$$
\cos\!\left(\Delta\sigma\right) = 1 - 2\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\sigma}{2}\right)
$$

And similarly for the longitudinal difference, we substitute and after algebraic rearrangement obtain the **Haversine formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)\,}
$$

where $\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$ and $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Two grid substations are located at:
> - **Substation A:** $\phi_1 = 10.0261°$ N, $\lambda_1 = 76.3125°$ E (Kochi)
> - **Substation B:** $\phi_2 = 11.2588°$ N, $\lambda_2 = 75.7804°$ E (Kozhikode)
>
> Calculate the great-circle distance using the Haversine formula. Take $R = 6371$ km.

### Solution

**Step 1: Convert degrees to radians**
Use $\text{rad} = \text{deg} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$

$$
\phi_1 = 10.0261 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.17500 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\phi_2 = 11.2588 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.19651 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\lambda_1 = 76.3125 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 1.33200 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\lambda_2 = 75.7804 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 1.32258 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Compute differences**

$$
\Delta\phi = 0.19651 - 0.17500 = 0.02151 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\Delta\lambda = 1.32258 - 1.33200 = -0.00942 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the half-difference trigonometric terms**

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \sin^2\!\left(0.010755\right) = (0.010755)^2 \approx 1.1567 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\cos\phi_1 = \cos(0.17500) \approx 0.98473
$$
$$
\cos\phi_2 = \cos(0.19651) \approx 0.98078
$$
$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) = \sin^2(-0.00471) \approx 2.218 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the term under square root**

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)
$$
$$
= 1.1567 \times 10^{-4} + (0.98473 \times 0.98078) \times 2.218 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
= 1.1567 \times 10^{-4} + (0.96578) \times 2.218 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
= 1.1567 \times 10^{-4} + 2.1421 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
= 1.3709 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5: Square root and arcsine**

$$
\sqrt{1.3709 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.01171
$$
$$
\arcsin(0.01171) \approx 0.01171 \text{ rad} \quad (\text{since } \arcsin(x) \approx x \text{ for small } x)
$$

**Step 6: Final distance**

$$
d = 2 \times 6371 \times 0.01171
$$
$$
\boxed{\,d \approx 149.21 \text{ km}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The straight-line distance between Kochi and Kozhikode is widely reported as ~150 km. The Haversine result is consistent. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Right-of-Way (RoW) Width for Transmission Line

The RoW width is a critical spatial buffer in GIS for power line corridors.

**Step 1: Define the empirical relationship**
For voltages above 66 kV, the RoW width is given empirically as:

$$
W_{RoW} = k \cdot V_{kV} + C
$$

where:
- $V_{kV}$ = line voltage in kV
- $k$ = empirical coefficient (~0.3 to 0.5 m/kV for vertical swing, doubled for horizontal RoW)
- $C$ = base clearance (m)

**Step 2: Apply the CEA (Central Electricity Authority) norms for India**

| Voltage Level | Statutory RoW Width |
|:---:|:---:|
| 11 kV | 7 m |
| 33 kV | 15 m |
| 66 kV | 18 m |
| 110 kV | 22 m |
| 220 kV | 35 m |
| 400 kV | 46 m |
| 765 kV | 67 m |

**Step 3: Worked example**
For a 220 kV line, $k = 0.15$ m/kV, $C = 2$ m:

$$
W_{RoW} = 0.15 \times 220 + 2 = 33 + 2 = 35 \text{ m}
$$

This matches the CEA standard of **35 m**. ✓

> [!TIP]
> The GIS Buffer tool uses this width to generate a polygon corridor around each transmission line, enabling the utility to identify affected land parcels, encroachments, and safety zones.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GIS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a GIS** as deployed in a smart grid distribution utility, showing the data flow from field assets to decision-making dashboards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Field Assets: Poles, Transformers, Feeders] --> B1[Field Survey & GPS Data Collection]
    A2[Consumer Meters & AMI Data] --> B2[Smart Metering Layer]
    A3[Satellite & Drone Imagery] --> B3[Remote Sensing Ingest]
    
    B1 --> C1[Spatial Data Input]
    B2 --> C2[Attribute Data Input]
    B3 --> C3[Image Processing & Raster Ingest]
    
    C1 --> D1[GIS Database Server]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    
    D1 --> E1[Spatial Analysis Engine]
    D1 --> E2[Network Analysis Module]
    D1 --> E3[Asset Management Module]
    
    E1 --> F1[Load Density Heat Map]
    E2 --> F2[Outage Management System OMS]
    E3 --> F3[Asset Health Dashboard]
    
    F1 --> G1[Planning Engineer]
    F2 --> G2[Field Crew Dispatch]
    F3 --> G3[Maintenance Scheduler]
    
    G1 --> H1[Decision Output]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
```

---

## 4.2 GIS Integration Architecture with Smart Grid Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIELD [Field Layer]
        F1[Smart Meters]
        F2[Sensors on Lines]
        F3[Drones and Field Crews]
    end
    
    subgraph COMM [Communication Layer]
        C1[RF Mesh and PLC]
        C2[4G and 5G]
        C3[IoT Gateways]
    end
    
    subgraph GIS [GIS Platform Core]
        G1[Spatial Database]
        G2[Vector Layer Assets]
        G3[Raster Layer Imagery]
        G4[Network Topology Engine]
    end
    
    subgraph APPS [Smart Grid Applications]
        A1[Outage Management OMS]
        A2[Distribution Management DMS]
        A3[Consumer Indexing]
        A4[Load Forecasting]
    end
    
    F1 --> C1 --> G1
    F2 --> C2 --> G1
    F3 --> C3 --> G3
    
    G1 --> G2
    G1 --> G3
    G2 --> G4
    G3 --> G4
    
    G4 --> A1
    G4 --> A2
    G2 --> A3
    G1 --> A4
```

---

## 4.3 Raster vs Vector Representation (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GIS Data Types] --> B[Raster Data]
    A --> C[Vector Data]
    
    B --> B1[Grid of Pixels]
    B --> B2[Suitable for Continuous Data]
    B --> B3[Example: Solar Irradiance Map]
    B --> B4[Format: GeoTIFF and IMG]
    
    C --> C1[Points: Pole Locations]
    C --> C2[Lines: Power Feeders]
    C --> C3[Polygons: Substation Boundary]
    C --> C4[Format: Shapefile and DWG]
```

---

## 4.4 Spatial Analysis Workflow in GIS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: Pole with Fault at Location A] --> S2[Step 1: Buffer of 500m around A]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Overlay with Consumer Layer]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Count Affected Consumers]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Network Trace to Find Alternate Path]
    S5 --> S6[Output: Estimated Restoration Time and Crew Dispatch]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For examiners:** These diagrams are the **highest-scoring visual answers** in KTU 2024 Scheme. Always include at least one block diagram in a 14-mark question on GIS architecture.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Geographic Information System (GIS). List any four components of GIS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Geographic Information System (GIS) is a computer-based system consisting of hardware, software, geospatial data, procedures, and trained personnel that is used for the input, storage, manipulation, analysis, management, and visualization of spatially-referenced (geo-referenced) data.

**Four Components (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
1. **Hardware** — Workstations, servers, GPS receivers, plotters
2. **Software** — GIS engine (e.g., ArcGIS, QGIS), DBMS, spatial analysis tools
3. **Data** — Spatial and non-spatial (attribute) data of real-world features
4. **People** — GIS analysts, operators, and end-users (power engineers)
5. **Methods (Procedures)** — Standard protocols for data collection, updating, and analysis

*(Any four correct components fetch full 2 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Raster and Vector data models in GIS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | **Raster Data Model** | **Vector Data Model** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Structure | Grid of cells (pixels) with values | Points, lines, polygons with coordinates |
| 2 | Best Suited For | Continuous phenomena (load density, solar irradiance) | Discrete features (poles, lines, substations) |
| 3 | Storage | Matrix of cell values | X, Y coordinate pairs |
| 4 | Resolution | Determined by cell size | Determined by scale |
| 5 | Example in Power System | Heat-map of consumption per sq. km | Network of 11 kV feeders |
| 6 | File Formats | GeoTIFF, IMG | Shapefile (.shp), DWG |

*(Any 3 distinguishing points with clear comparison fetch 3 marks)*

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **📌 Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) Explain the five components of GIS in detail with reference to a power distribution utility. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five components of GIS, as defined by **Burrough (1986)**, are:

**1. Hardware (1.5 marks):**
Hardware forms the physical platform on which GIS operates. In a power distribution utility, this includes:
- High-end workstations and database servers for processing large vector and raster data
- GPS receivers for pole-top and substation geo-tagging
- Digitizers for converting paper-based feeder maps into digital form
- High-resolution plotters and printers for printing network diagrams
- Scanners for rasterizing legacy paper records

**2. Software (1.5 marks):**
The GIS software ecosystem for utilities includes:
- Core GIS engine (e.g., **ESRI ArcGIS**, **GE Smallworld**, **QGIS** open source)
- Database Management System (DBMS) — **Oracle Spatial**, **PostgreSQL/PostGIS**
- Spatial analysis toolkits (Network Analyst, Spatial Analyst extensions)
- Specialized utility modules — **ArcFM**, **Smallworld Core**
- Web-GIS server for browser-based distribution utility access

**3. Data (2 marks):**
Data is the most critical and expensive component. In a distribution utility:
- **Spatial data:** Geographic coordinates of every pole, transformer, switch, conductor span
- **Non-spatial (attribute) data:** Rating (kVA) of transformer, age, last maintenance date, conductor type
- **Topological data:** Connectivity (which pole connects to which feeder)
- **Consumer data:** Each consumer's billing info linked to the nearest pole
- Data can be obtained via field GPS survey, satellite imagery, or drone flights

**4. People (1 mark):**
- **GIS Analysts** — build and maintain the spatial database
- **Power Engineers** — provide domain expertise on electrical network
- **Field Surveyors** — collect geo-tagged data
- **Database Administrators** — manage data integrity
- **Decision-Makers** — utility managers, planners who consume GIS outputs

**5. Methods (1 mark):**
- Standard operating procedures for data capture, validation, update cycles
- Quality control checklists
- Standard symbology for power assets (e.g., red for HT, blue for LT)
- Workflows for integrating GIS with **SCADA, OMS, ERP, and AMI systems**

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the role of GIS in Smart Grid. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

GIS acts as the **spatial backbone** of the smart grid, integrating with multiple utility IT/OT systems:

**1. Asset Management (1.5 marks):**
GIS maintains a complete geo-referenced inventory of all grid assets — substations, transformers, feeders, poles, conductors, and consumer meters. Each asset is linked to its attribute data (rating, age, maintenance history).

**2. Consumer Indexing & Billing (1 mark):**
Every consumer is geo-tagged to the nearest pole and feeder. This enables accurate billing, disconnection/reconnection, and theft analysis.

**3. Outage Management System (OMS) (1.5 marks):**
When a fault occurs, GIS identifies the affected feeder section, counts impacted consumers, and locates the nearest field crew. **AM/SCADA** sends the fault signal, and GIS provides the geographic context for restoration.

**4. Distribution Network Planning (1 mark):**
For new connections, load additions, or renewable integration, GIS performs **buffer analysis**, **proximity analysis**, and **load density mapping** to plan new feeders and substations.

**5. Integration with SCADA / DMS / AMI (1 mark):**
Modern smart grid **Distribution Management Systems (DMS)** rely on GIS for the network topology. Real-time sensor data from **SCADA** is overlaid on GIS to visualize voltage, current, and power flow geographically.

**6. Disaster Management & Right-of-Way (1 mark):**
GIS overlays flood, cyclone, and landslide risk maps on substation/line data to assess vulnerability. It also maintains the **Right-of-Way (RoW)** corridor documentation for legal and safety purposes.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn):**

```
[Field Assets] → [GIS Platform] → [OMS / DMS / AMI / SCADA] → [Decision Layer]
         ↑                              ↓
    [GPS Survey]              [Outage / Planning / Consumer]
```

*(Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 as reference)*

---

### **📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the application of GIS in distribution network planning and load forecasting. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Base Network Creation (2 marks):**
GIS serves as the **single source of truth** for the entire distribution network. A geo-referenced base map containing all substations, feeders, transformers, and consumers is created. This base map supports both radial and ring-main network planning.

**2. Feeder Routing & Optimization (2 marks):**
For a new housing colony or industrial park, GIS performs:
- **Shortest path analysis** — finds optimal feeder route minimizing conductor length
- **Terrain analysis** — uses DEM (Digital Elevation Model) to avoid steep slopes
- **Buffer analysis** — keeps lines away from environmentally sensitive zones (forests, water bodies)
- **Right-of-Way planning** — uses voltage-based corridor widths to compute land area required

**3. Load Density Mapping (1.5 marks):**
By overlaying consumer billing data on geographic zones, GIS creates a **load density heat map**. This identifies high-demand areas suitable for:
- New substation siting
- Additional transformer installation
- HT/LT network reinforcement

**4. Load Forecasting (Geographic Component) (1.5 marks):**
GIS assists load forecasting through:
- **Spatial interpolation** (IDW, Kriging) of past consumption data across un-surveyed areas
- **Demographic overlay** — population density + per-capita consumption → future load
- **EV & Solar penetration projection** — identifying likely future load addition zones

---

**(b) A grid substation A is located at latitude 9.97°N, longitude 76.30°E and substation B is at 10.02°N, 76.36°E. Calculate the great-circle (Haversine) distance between them. Take R = 6371 km. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Convert to radians** (1 mark)

$$
\phi_1 = 9.97 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17404 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\phi_2 = 10.02 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17488 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\lambda_1 = 76.30 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33178 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\lambda_2 = 76.36 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33282 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Differences** (1 mark)

$$
\Delta\phi = 0.17488 - 0.17404 = 0.00084 \text{ rad}
$$
$$
\Delta\lambda = 1.33282 - 1.33178 = 0.00104 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3: Trigonometric computations** (2 marks)

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \sin^2(0.00042) \approx 1.764 \times 10^{-7}
$$
$$
\cos\phi_1 = \cos(0.17404) \approx 0.98488
$$
$$
\cos\phi_2 = \cos(0.17488) \approx 0.98473
$$
$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) = \sin^2(0.00052) \approx 2.704 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 4: Combine inside square root** (1 mark)

$$
a = 1.764 \times 10^{-7} + (0.98488)(0.98473)(2.704 \times 10^{-7})
$$
$$
a = 1.764 \times 10^{-7} + 2.625 \times 10^{-7} = 4.389 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5: Apply arcsine and multiply by $2R$** (1.5 marks)

$$
\sqrt{a} = 6.624 \times 10^{-4}
$$
$$
\arcsin(6.624 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 6.624 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$
$$
d = 2 \times 6371 \times 6.624 \times 10^{-4}
$$
$$
\boxed{\,d \approx 8.44 \text{ km}\,}
$$

**Step 6: Engineering interpretation** (0.5 mark)
This ~8.4 km distance, combined with terrain and load data in GIS, can be used to plan an interconnecting transmission line, estimate conductor length, voltage drop, and cost.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on GIS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing GIS with GPS** — GIS is a system; GPS is **only a data input tool** (a sensor). Many students wrongly equate the two. (*Penalty: 1–2 marks*)
> 2. **Forgetting the "five components" terminology** — Writing only 3 or 4 components without naming them properly. The **5 components by Burrough** is the standardized KTU answer.
> 3. **Mixing Raster/Vector definitions** — Stating "Raster is for discrete, Vector is for continuous" — this is **exactly reversed** and is a common trap.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners explicitly state *"neat diagram"* in the question paper. A missing diagram = **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 5. **Wrong unit handling in Haversine** — Mixing degrees and radians without conversion is a fatal error. Always convert to radians first.
> 6. **Ignoring datum** — Writing coordinates without specifying the **datum (WGS-84)** loses technical accuracy marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for GIS (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3):**

- 🔹 **GIS = Computer-based system for capturing, storing, analyzing, managing, and presenting spatial data.** Do not confuse with GPS.
- 🔹 **5 Components:** Hardware, Software, **Data (most expensive ~80%)**, People, Methods (Burrough's Model).
- 🔹 **Two Data Models:** **Vector** (Points, Lines, Polygons — for discrete assets) vs **Raster** (Grid of pixels — for continuous data like solar irradiance, load density).
- 🔹 **Standard Indian Datums:** WGS-84 (modern), Everest-1830 (legacy), Kalianpur-1880.
- 🔹 **Key Spatial Operations:** Buffering, Overlay, Proximity, Network Analysis, Interpolation (IDW, Kriging).
- 🔹 **Haversine Formula** — for great-circle distance on Earth's curved surface; remember the small-angle approximation $\arcsin(x) \approx x$ for short distances.
- 🔹 **CEA RoW widths** — 7 m (11 kV) to 67 m (765 kV); used in GIS buffer generation.
- 🔹 **GIS in Smart Grid** = integrates with SCADA, OMS, DMS, AMI for real-time grid visualization.
- 🔹 **Must-remember applications:** Asset management, Consumer indexing, Outage Management, Load forecasting, EV charging siting, Rooftop solar planning, Right-of-Way documentation, Disaster resilience mapping.
- 🔹 **Key Software for KTU awareness:** ArcGIS, QGIS (open-source), GE Smallworld, ArcFM — mention in exam answers for extra credit.
- 🔹 **Geographic Coordinates** are always written as **(Latitude, Longitude)** in degrees, with N/S and E/W indicators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outage Management System (OMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Outage Management System (OMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Outage Management System (OMS)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Outage Management System (OMS)** is an advanced, computer-based operational platform employed by electric utility companies to efficiently detect, analyze, predict, coordinate, and resolve power outages across the distribution network. The OMS acts as the central nervous system for outage handling, integrating real-time data from SCADA, AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure), Customer Information Systems (CIS), Geographic Information Systems (GIS), and customer trouble calls to provide a unified operational view of network health and restoration status.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEET861 Module 3):**
> "The Outage Management System (OMS) is a comprehensive software platform that processes outage calls, predicts fault locations using call clustering and network topology analysis, recommends optimal switching operations, dispatches field crews, tracks restoration progress, and computes reliability indices in compliance with regulatory standards such as IEEE 1366."

The OMS essentially closes the loop between **customer-reported interruptions**, **field operations**, and **system-level reliability performance**, transforming what was historically a manual, paper-based utility function into a real-time, data-driven, automated workflow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are managing a **large hospital complex** with 50 buildings, each housing hundreds of patients. When a patient presses the emergency call button in Building 12, the central nursing station receives a call. Now, if 10 patients from Buildings 10, 11, and 12 all call within 5 minutes, the nurse instantly knows that there is a common issue — perhaps a fire alarm in the corridor, or a power cut in the transformer feeding all three buildings. The nurse dispatches an electrician to a specific sub-station, monitors progress via the hospital information system, and notifies affected patients. Once power is restored, the system logs the event for compliance and future improvements.

The **Outage Management System** works exactly like this digital hospital command centre, but for the **electrical distribution grid**:

| Hospital Analogy | Smart Grid Equivalent (OMS) |
|---|---|
| Patient emergency call | Customer trouble call |
| Hospital Information System | CIS + AMI Database |
| Building wiring map | GIS Network Model |
| Nurse call clustering | Call Clustering Algorithm |
| Electrician dispatch | Field Crew Dispatch |
| Restoration confirmation | SCADA/AMI status feedback |
| Incident log | Outage Database / Audit Trail |
| Hospital performance report | SAIDI / SAIFI Reliability Indices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> Think of OMS as the **"Uber-for-Power-Outages"**. When lights go out somewhere in the city, the OMS receives electronic alerts, figures out *where* the fault is, *who* is affected, sends *the nearest qualified crew*, tracks them via GPS, and tells customers when power will return — all happening in **seconds to minutes**, not hours.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Standardized for OMS

The OMS is the **primary computational engine** for the following standard reliability indices defined by **IEEE Standard 1366-2012**:

- **SAIDI** (System Average Interruption Duration Index) — measured in **minutes/customer/year**
- **SAIFI** (System Average Interruption Frequency Index) — measured in **interruptions/customer/year**
- **CAIDI** (Customer Average Interruption Duration Index) — measured in **minutes/interruption**
- **CAIFI** (Customer Average Interruption Frequency Index) — measured in **interruptions/affected customer/year**
- **MAIFI** (Momentary Average Interruption Frequency Index) — measured in **interruptions/customer/year**
- **ASAI** (Average Service Availability Index) — dimensionless, expressed as a percentage

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "How does OMS help improve reliability?" — the answer ALWAYS culminates in numerical improvement of **SAIDI** and **SAIFI**. Always end your OMS answers with the reliability index impact.

---

## 1.4 Why OMS is Indispensable in Smart Grids

The smart grid philosophy demands **self-healing, observable, and resilient** distribution networks. The OMS provides three fundamental smart-grid properties:

1. **Self-Healing Capability** — Automated fault location, isolation, and service restoration (FLISR) using OMS predictions.
2. **Situational Awareness** — Real-time visualization of the network state using GIS + SCADA + AMI data fusion.
3. **Customer Centricity** — Proactive outbound notifications via SMS, IVR, and web portals during outages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Outcome Mapping (CO3):**
> This topic directly addresses **CO3: Design and analyze distribution automation, outage management, and demand response systems in a smart grid context**, evaluated at Bloom's Level **Apply / Analyze** in End Semester Examinations.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of an OMS

An OMS is not a monolithic software; it is a **federated architecture** integrating multiple utility subsystems. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the architectural awareness of students.

### 2.1.1 Core Subsystem Inventory

| Subsystem | Role in OMS | Data Exchange Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** | Real-time status of breakers, switches, and substations | Bi-directional: SCADA → OMS (telemetry), OMS → SCADA (control commands) |
| **AMI / Smart Meter System** | Last-gasp outage notifications, restoration confirmation | Bi-directional: AMI head-end ↔ OMS via middleware |
| **GIS (Geographic Information System)** | Network connectivity model, asset location, feeder topology | Read-only by OMS; OMS queries topology dynamically |
| **CIS (Customer Information System)** | Customer-to-transformer-to-feeder mapping, billing, contact info | Read-only by OMS for customer grouping |
| **IVR / Call Center System** | Customer trouble call intake, ticket creation | Push-based: Trouble tickets enter OMS event queue |
| **DMS (Distribution Management System)** | Switching recommendations, load flow analysis | Bi-directional: DMS runs what-if switching scenarios |
| **WMS (Workforce Management System)** | Crew scheduling, dispatch, and tracking | Bi-directional: OMS → WMS (work orders), WMS → OMS (ETAs) |
| **Historical Outage Database** | Past outage patterns, predictive analytics | Read-write: Long-term storage of all OMS events |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Alert:** In KTU 2024 questions on OMS architecture, examiners expect the student to draw/describe the **integration map** between OMS and at least three of the above subsystems. Memorize this table for the Part A 3-mark question.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step OMS Operational Workflow

The OMS processes an outage through **six distinct logical phases**. Understanding these phases is mandatory for all 14-mark KTU Part B questions.

### Phase 1: Event Detection (Latency Target: < 60 seconds)
Outage events are detected through three parallel channels:
- **Channel A — Customer Trouble Calls**: Humans phone the utility 1-800 number. The IVR captures the address and creates a trouble ticket.
- **Channel B — AMI Last-Gasp Alerts**: Smart meters send a "last-gasp" message within 5-10 seconds of power loss via the AMI backhaul (RF mesh, PLC, or cellular).
- **Channel C — SCADA Alarms**: A breaker trip, fuse operation, or protective relay action generates an unsolicited SCADA alarm.

### Phase 2: Event Correlation & Confirmation (Latency Target: 2-5 minutes)
The OMS uses the **network connectivity model** to confirm whether a real outage exists and groups related calls. If 30 customers on the same feeder call within 10 minutes, the OMS infers a single feeder outage rather than 30 individual incidents.

### Phase 3: Fault Location Prediction (Call Clustering Algorithm)
Using **spatial clustering of trouble call addresses** plotted on the GIS map, the OMS identifies the **probable protection device** (fuse, recloser, breaker) that has operated. The clustering algorithm uses **graph traversal of the radial feeder tree** — the lowest common ancestor node in the feeder tree is the predicted fault location.

### Phase 4: Switching Analysis & Recommendation
The OMS, in conjunction with the DMS, runs a **switching optimization** to:
- **Isolate** the faulted section
- **Restore** upstream healthy customers via the primary source
- **Transfer** downstream customers to an adjacent feeder (load transfer) if alternate paths exist
- All while respecting **thermal limits, voltage limits, and radiality constraints**

### Phase 5: Crew Dispatch
The optimal field crew is identified based on:
- **Proximity to the fault** (using GIS routing)
- **Skill certification** (e.g., hot-stick trained, substation authorized)
- **Current workload** (no other open ticket)
- **Travel time minimization** (using real-time traffic feeds)

### Phase 6: Restoration Tracking & Closing
The OMS tracks crew progress, receives SCADA status updates as switches are operated, receives AMI restoration-confirmed messages from smart meters, and automatically **closes the outage ticket** when all affected customers report restoration. The event is then archived in the historical database and the corresponding SAIDI/SAIFI values are updated.

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often confuse OMS with DMS. **DMS = real-time network control (SCADA on steroids with load flow & Volt-VAR).** **OMS = outage-specific workflow management.** They overlap, but OMS is the higher-level orchestrator that *uses* DMS for its switching recommendations.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Reliability Indices)

The following six equations are the **mathematical backbone** of OMS. Every KTU 2024 question on OMS expects students to compute and interpret these.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention (IEEE 1366-2012):**
> - $N_T$ = Total number of customers served
> - $N_i$ = Number of customers interrupted for outage event $i$
> - $r_i$ = Restoration time for outage event $i$ (in minutes)
> - $N_{mi}$ = Number of customers experiencing momentary interruption $i$ (less than 5 minutes)
> - $T$ = Total time period under analysis (8760 hours = 1 year)

### 2.3.1 SAIDI — System Average Interruption Duration Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i \cdot N_i}{N_T} \quad \text{[minutes / customer / year]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** The average outage duration that **each customer** would have experienced over the year. It is the *primary* KPI used by Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and SERC regulators.

### 2.3.2 SAIFI — System Average Interruption Frequency Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i}{N_T} \quad \text{[interruptions / customer / year]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** How many times, on average, a customer lost power in the year. Notice the absence of $r_i$ — SAIFI counts *frequency*, not *duration*.

### 2.3.3 CAIDI — Customer Average Interruption Duration Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIDI} &= \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i \cdot N_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i} \quad \text{[minutes / interruption]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** Average time to restore service *for those who actually experienced an outage*. CAIDI is a *crew-efficiency* metric, whereas SAIDI is a *system-impact* metric.

### 2.3.4 CAIFI — Customer Average Interruption Frequency Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIFI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i}{C_N} \quad \text{[interruptions / affected customer / year]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C_N$ is the **total number of distinct customers who experienced at least one outage**. CAIFI gives equal weight to each affected customer, unlike SAIFI which weighs by the total customer base.

### 2.3.5 MAIFI — Momentary Average Interruption Frequency Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAIFI}_E &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_{mi}}{N_T} \quad \text{[momentary events / customer / year]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** Counts short-duration interruptions (< 5 minutes) caused by recloser operations, equipment switching, or transient faults. Critical for industrial customers with sensitive process loads.

### 2.3.6 ASAI — Average Service Availability Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASAI} &= \frac{8760 \times 60 - \text{SAIDI}}{8760 \times 60} \quad \text{[dimensionless, as percentage]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** The fraction of time that service was available to the average customer. A typical target for Indian urban feeders is **ASAI $\geq$ 99.9 %** (the "three-nines" benchmark, equivalent to $\leq$ 526 minutes/year of outage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Insight:** The two indices **CAIDI = SAIDI / SAIFI** and **ASAI = 1 - SAIDI/(8760×60)** are *derived* relationships. In a 14-mark question, you can save derivation effort by invoking these identities directly. The first three indices (SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI) are the **primary** indices; the last three (CAIFI, MAIFI, ASAI) are **secondary** derived/extension indices.

### 2.3.7 Master Formula Cheat-Sheet Table

| Index | Formula (using $N_T, N_i, r_i$) | Units | Performance Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SAIDI** | $\sum r_i N_i / N_T$ | min/cust/yr | Duration Impact |
| **SAIFI** | $\sum N_i / N_T$ | int/cust/yr | Frequency Impact |
| **CAIDI** | SAIDI / SAIFI | min/int | Restoration Speed |
| **CAIFI** | $\sum N_i / C_N$ | int/cust/yr | Affected Group |
| **MAIFI** | $\sum N_{mi} / N_T$ | mom/cust/yr | Momentary Events |
| **ASAI** | $(8760 \times 60 - \text{SAIDI}) / 525600$ | ratio | Availability % |
| **ENS** | $\sum L_i \cdot r_i$ | kWh/yr | Energy Not Supplied |
| **AENS** | ENS / $N_T$ | kWh/cust/yr | Per-Capita Energy Loss |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for ENS Calculation:** When a question provides feeder load data (say, 5 MW for 2 hours), compute ENS = 5 MW × 2 h = **10,000 kWh**. ENS is often the bridge between OMS and **regulatory penalty calculations** in India.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of OMS

| Domain | OMS Application |
|---|---|
| **Indian Regulatory Compliance (CERC / SERC)** | Performance-based rate-making uses SAIDI/SAIFI/ASAI for incentives/penalties. Lower SAIDI = higher tariff approval. |
| **Disaster Management** | OMS is repurposed during cyclones (e.g., Cyclone Ockhi, 2017 in Kerala) for rapid damage assessment. |
| **Distributed Energy Resources (DER) Integration** | OMS must coordinate with rooftop solar, BESS, microgrids during outages to ensure crew safety and **anti-islanding** compliance. |
| **Electric Vehicle (EV) Charging Infrastructure** | OMS predicts the load impact of EV charging stations and plans **load shedding** equitably. |
| **Predictive Outage Analytics** | Machine learning models trained on OMS historical data predict equipment failures 24-72 hours in advance. |
