# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Problem: Reliability Index Computation

This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark Part B numerical problem** on OMS. We will work through every algebraic step with zero shortcuts.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A radial distribution feeder in the KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) network serves a total of **5,000 customers** ($N_T = 5000$) in a district. During the year 2024, the following four major outage events were recorded in the OMS database:

| Event # | Date | Customers Affected ($N_i$) | Restoration Time ($r_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 14 Jan 2024 | 800 | 90 minutes |
| 2 | 22 May 2024 | 1,200 | 45 minutes |
| 3 | 08 Aug 2024 (Cyclone) | 2,500 | 240 minutes |
| 4 | 25 Dec 2024 | 500 | 30 minutes |

Additionally, the OMS logged **three momentary events**:
- Event A: 600 customers affected, duration 2 minutes
- Event B: 1,000 customers affected, duration 3 minutes
- Event C: 400 customers affected, duration 4 minutes

A total of **2,300 distinct customers** experienced at least one sustained interruption during the year.

**Required:** Calculate (a) SAIDI, (b) SAIFI, (c) CAIDI, (d) CAIFI, (e) MAIFI, and (f) ASAI for the year.

---

### 3.1.2 Part (a): SAIDI Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the SAIDI formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i \cdot N_i}{N_T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerator term-by-term, i.e., the customer-minutes of interruption for each event.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= r_1 \cdot N_1 = 90 \text{ min} \times 800 \text{ cust} = 72{,}000 \text{ cust-min} \\
E_2 &= r_2 \cdot N_2 = 45 \text{ min} \times 1{,}200 \text{ cust} = 54{,}000 \text{ cust-min} \\
E_3 &= r_3 \cdot N_3 = 240 \text{ min} \times 2{,}500 \text{ cust} = 600{,}000 \text{ cust-min} \\
E_4 &= r_4 \cdot N_4 = 30 \text{ min} \times 500 \text{ cust} = 15{,}000 \text{ cust-min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the customer-minutes across all four sustained events.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum r_i N_i &= 72{,}000 + 54{,}000 + 600{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\
&= 126{,}000 + 600{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\
&= 726{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\
&= 741{,}000 \text{ cust-min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Divide by total customers to obtain SAIDI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{741{,}000 \text{ cust-min}}{5{,}000 \text{ cust}} = 148.20 \text{ min/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** SAIDI = **148.20 minutes/customer/year**. This means the average customer on this feeder experienced 148.2 minutes (≈ 2 hours 28 minutes) of outage in 2024.

---

### 3.1.3 Part (b): SAIFI Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the SAIFI formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i}{N_T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum the customers interrupted across all four sustained events.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i &= 800 + 1{,}200 + 2{,}500 + 500 \\
&= 2{,}000 + 2{,}500 + 500 \\
&= 4{,}500 + 500 \\
&= 5{,}000 \text{ customer-interruptions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Divide by total customers.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{5{,}000 \text{ cust-int}}{5{,}000 \text{ cust}} = 1.00 \text{ int/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** SAIFI = **1.00 interruption/customer/year**. On average, each customer lost power exactly once in 2024.

---

### 3.1.4 Part (c): CAIDI Derivation

**Step 1: Use the derived identity CAIDI = SAIDI / SAIFI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIDI} &= \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} = \frac{148.20 \text{ min/cust/yr}}{1.00 \text{ int/cust/yr}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Perform the unit cancellation (minutes/customer ÷ interruptions/customer = minutes/interruption).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIDI} &= 148.20 \text{ min/int}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** CAIDI = **148.20 minutes/interruption**. For every sustained outage, the utility needed an average of 148.2 minutes (≈ 2 hours 28 minutes) to restore service.

---

### 3.1.5 Part (d): CAIFI Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the CAIFI formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIFI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i}{C_N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

- Numerator $\sum N_i = 5{,}000$ (from Part b)
- Denominator $C_N = 2{,}300$ (distinct affected customers, given)

**Step 3: Compute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIFI} &= \frac{5{,}000}{2{,}300} = 2.1739 \text{ int/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** CAIFI = **2.17 interruptions/affected customer/year**. Among the 2,300 customers who experienced an outage, the average number of interruptions was 2.17. CAIFI > SAIFI here indicates the same customers are being repeatedly affected — a **reliability hotspot** that OMS should flag for capital investment.

---

### 3.1.6 Part (e): MAIFI Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the MAIFI formula (excluding events $\leq$ 5 minutes).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAIFI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} N_{mi}}{N_T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum the customers affected by momentary events.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum N_{mi} &= 600 + 1{,}000 + 400 = 2{,}000 \text{ cust-events}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Divide by total customers.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAIFI} &= \frac{2{,}000}{5{,}000} = 0.40 \text{ mom/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** MAIFI = **0.40 momentary events/customer/year**. Less than one momentary interruption per customer per year, indicating good recloser coordination.

---

### 3.1.7 Part (f): ASAI Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the ASAI formula (using minutes for consistency).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASAI} &= \frac{525{,}600 - \text{SAIDI}}{525{,}600}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the total available minutes per year.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
8760 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} = 525{,}600 \text{ min/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Subtract SAIDI and divide.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASAI} &= \frac{525{,}600 - 148.20}{525{,}600} = \frac{525{,}451.80}{525{,}600} = 0.999718
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASAI}_\text{(\%)} &= 99.9718 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** ASAI ≈ **99.97 %**. This corresponds to the "three-nines" reliability benchmark, a respectable target for an Indian urban feeder.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: OMS vs. Competing Concepts

A frequent KTU question type is "Differentiate OMS from SCADA / DMS / EMS." The following matrix provides examiner-ready comparison points.

| Feature | SCADA | EMS (Energy Management System) | DMS | OMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Generation + Transmission | Generation + Transmission | Distribution | Distribution |
| **Time Horizon** | Real-time (sec) | Real-time (sec–min) | Real-time (min) | Event-driven (min–hr) |
| **Primary Function** | Supervisory control & data acquisition | Economic dispatch, AGC | Volt-VAR, load flow, switching | Outage detection, crew dispatch |
| **Customer Visibility** | None | None | Limited | Full (per-customer) |
| **Crew Management** | No | No | Limited | **Yes — core feature** |
| **Call Handling** | No | No | No | **Yes — core feature** |
| **Reliability Indices** | No | No | No | **Yes — core feature** |
| **GIS Integration** | Optional | No | Yes | **Yes — core feature** |
| **AMI Integration** | Optional | No | Yes | **Yes — core feature** |
| **Scope of Optimization** | None | Economic dispatch | Volt-VAR, loss minimization | Restoration time minimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** OMS is **event-driven**, not time-driven. SCADA is time-driven (continuous polling). DMS is time-driven (continuous state estimation). OMS *activates* only when an outage event is detected — this distinguishes it from SCADA/DMS which run continuously.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Problem: Energy Not Supplied (ENS)

**Problem Extension:** If the average feeder load during Event 3 (Cyclone, 240 min) was **5 MW**, compute the ENS and AENS.

**Step 1: Convert load to kW.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 5 \text{ MW} = 5{,}000 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Multiply by restoration time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ENS}_3 &= L \times r_3 = 5{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 240 \text{ min} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ kW-min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert kW-min to kWh (divide by 60).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ENS}_3 &= \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{60} = 20{,}000 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: AENS = ENS / N_T.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AENS}_3 &= \frac{20{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{5{,}000 \text{ cust}} = 4.0 \text{ kWh/cust}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** ENS = **20,000 kWh**, AENS = **4.0 kWh/customer** for the cyclone event alone. At a typical industrial tariff of ₹8/kWh, this represents ₹1,60,000 in lost revenue per event — a strong economic case for OMS investment.

---

## 3.4 Call Clustering Algorithm (Conceptual Walkthrough)

The OMS uses a **graph-based call clustering** algorithm to localize faults. The procedure is:

**Step 1: Build the Feeder Connectivity Tree.**
The GIS provides a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where nodes $V$ are devices (transformers, switches, fuses) and edges $E$ are line segments. A radial feeder is a **directed tree** rooted at the substation.

**Step 2: Map Each Trouble Call to a Leaf Node.**
Each customer address is mapped to its service transformer (a leaf or near-leaf node) via the CIS-GIS join. This produces a set of $K$ customer nodes $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_K\}$ that reported outages.

**Step 3: Find the Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA).**
The OMS computes the **LCA of all $K$ trouble call nodes** in the feeder tree. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Predicted Fault Device} &= \text{LCA}(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_K)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Validate with SCADA Telemetry.**
The OMS cross-checks whether any device in the path from the substation root to the predicted LCA has a tripped status flag. If yes, the prediction is confirmed. If multiple events occur, the algorithm runs iteratively.

**Step 5: Confidence Scoring.**
The OMS computes a **clustering confidence** based on:
- Density of calls within a 1 km radius
- Time-of-day correlation
- Historical outage frequency of the device

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidence} &= \frac{K_{\text{in-cluster}}}{K_{\text{total calls}}} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When asked "How does OMS predict fault location?", answer in three sentences: "By clustering trouble calls geographically, mapping each call to the network tree, and finding the lowest common ancestor (LCA) device. SCADA confirms. The algorithm runs in O(log n) for balanced feeder trees." This 3-line answer is worth full marks.

```mermaid
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM
    stepL --> stepN
    stepK --> stepH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Process Insight:** The loop from stepK (Restoration Confirmation) back to stepH (Crew Dispatch) represents the *partial restoration* scenario. If upstream customers are restored via switching but the faulted section requires repair, the OMS dispatches a *second* crew action for full restoration. This iterative loop is what distinguishes a mature OMS from a basic SCADA alarm logger.

---

## 4.3 OMS State Transition Diagram (Outage Ticket Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    sN1 --> sN2 : Trouble Call Received
    sN2 --> sN3 : Call Aggregated
    sN3 --> sN4 : Fault Predicted
    sN4 --> sN5 : SCADA Confirmed
    sN5 --> sN6 : Crew Dispatched
    sN6 --> sN7 : Crew On Site
    sN7 --> sN8 : Fault Isolated
    sN8 --> sN9 : Restoration Complete
    sN9 --> sN10 : OMS Ticket Closed
    sN10 --> sN11 : Archived in Database
    sN5 --> sN12 : No Crew Available
    sN12 --> sN6 : Crew Assigned Later
    sN8 --> sN13 : Partial Restoration
    sN13 --> sN9 : Full Restoration
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State Machine Significance:** The "Detected → Confirmed → Dispatched → On-Site → Isolated → Restored → Closed → Archived" lifecycle mirrors the **ITIL incident management** framework. In KTU 2024 scheme, the question may ask to draw the state diagram of an outage ticket. Memorize the state names and transitions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Outage Management System (OMS). List its four primary functional modules.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

An **Outage Management System (OMS)** is a computer-based utility software platform that integrates real-time data from SCADA, AMI, GIS, and customer trouble calls to detect, analyze, predict, and resolve power distribution outages while optimizing crew dispatch and computing reliability indices.

The four primary functional modules of an OMS are:

1. **Outage Detection & Confirmation Module** — Ingests AMI last-gasp alerts, SCADA alarms, and customer trouble calls to detect outage events in real time.

2. **Fault Location Prediction Module** — Uses call clustering on the GIS network model to predict the most probable protection device that has operated (the Lowest Common Ancestor device on the feeder tree).

3. **Crew Dispatch & Management Module** — Selects the optimal field crew based on proximity, skill, and workload; issues work orders and tracks progress via GPS.

4. **Restoration Tracking & Reporting Module** — Monitors restoration via SCADA status and AMI restoration confirmations, then computes SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, MAIFI, and ASAI for regulatory reporting.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between SAIDI and SAIFI. State the IEEE standard that defines these indices.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

| Aspect | SAIDI | SAIFI |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | System Average Interruption Duration Index | System Average Interruption Frequency Index |
| **Measures** | Total duration of interruptions | Total frequency of interruptions |
| **Formula** | $\sum r_i N_i / N_T$ | $\sum N_i / N_T$ |
| **Units** | Minutes per customer per year | Interruptions per customer per year |
| **What it tells you** | "How long was the average customer without power?" | "How many times did the average customer lose power?" |
| **Optimization Lever** | Reduce restoration time (faster crews) | Reduce outage occurrences (better equipment) |

Both indices are defined by **IEEE Standard 1366-2012** ("IEEE Guide for Electric Power Distribution Reliability Indices"). The standard specifies the data collection methodology, the rules for including/excluding major event days (MEDs), and the normalization procedures for benchmarking across utilities.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: (a) Describe the architecture of a modern Outage Management System (OMS). Explain how the OMS integrates with SCADA, AMI, GIS, and CIS to manage power outages. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

A modern OMS is a **federated, service-oriented architecture** with the OMS core acting as the central orchestrator that queries and exchanges data with peripheral utility systems.

**1. Integration with SCADA (2 Marks):**
- The SCADA system continuously polls field devices (breakers, reclosers, switches) and generates alarms on status change.
- The OMS **subscribes** to these alarms via a middleware message bus (typically DNP3 or IEC 61850 over TCP/IP).
- On receiving a breaker-trip alarm, the OMS uses the **feeder connectivity model** to determine which customers are downstream and immediately creates an outage event.
- The OMS can also **issue control commands** back to SCADA (e.g., open a specific switch for isolation), which SCADA then relays to the field RTU.

**2. Integration with AMI (2 Marks):**
- Smart meters emit a **"last-gasp" message** within 5-10 seconds of losing power, sent via the AMI backhaul (RF mesh, PLC, or cellular).
- The AMI head-end system forwards these alerts to the OMS event queue.
- Conversely, when power is restored, meters send a **"power-on" message** that the OMS uses to **automatically close outage tickets** for those customers, even before a crew confirms restoration on-site.
- AMI provides *granular* outage visibility down to the individual customer, which SCADA cannot.

**3. Integration with GIS (1.5 Marks):**
- The GIS maintains the **network connectivity model** — the directed graph of devices, conductors, transformers, and customer service points.
- The OMS uses GIS to: (i) spatially cluster trouble calls, (ii) trace the radial feeder upstream/downstream of a given switch, and (iii) visualize outage extents on a geo-map for the operator dashboard.
- The GIS data is treated as **read-only by the OMS**; updates flow from a separate asset management workflow.

**4. Integration with CIS (1.5 Marks):**
- The CIS provides the **customer-to-transformer-to-feeder mapping** (the "connectivity bill of materials").
- The OMS uses CIS to: (i) identify the set of customers affected by an outage, (ii) retrieve contact information for proactive notifications (SMS, IVR, email), and (iii) correlate billing addresses with reported trouble call locations.
- Like GIS, CIS is read-only for OMS purposes.

The integration enables a **closed-loop workflow**: SCADA detects → AMI confirms → GIS locates → CIS identifies customers → OMS dispatches → SCADA confirms restoration → AMI confirms at customer level → OMS closes ticket.

---

**(b) Compute SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, and ASAI for the following distribution feeder data. Total customers $N_T = 10{,}000$. During the year, the following sustained interruptions occurred: (i) 2,000 customers out for 60 min, (ii) 3,000 customers out for 120 min, (iii) 1,000 customers out for 30 min. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Tabulate the data.**

| Event | $N_i$ | $r_i$ (min) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,000 | 60 |
| 2 | 3,000 | 120 |
| 3 | 1,000 | 30 |

**Step 2: Compute $r_i \cdot N_i$ for each event (customer-minutes).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 60 \times 2{,}000 = 120{,}000 \\
E_2 &= 120 \times 3{,}000 = 360{,}000 \\
E_3 &= 30 \times 1{,}000 = 30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the customer-minutes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum r_i N_i &= 120{,}000 + 360{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 510{,}000 \text{ cust-min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the customers interrupted.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i &= 2{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ cust-int}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute SAIDI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{510{,}000}{10{,}000} = 51.0 \text{ min/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute SAIFI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{6{,}000}{10{,}000} = 0.60 \text{ int/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute CAIDI (using the derived identity).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIDI} &= \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} = \frac{51.0}{0.60} = 85.0 \text{ min/int}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Compute ASAI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASAI} &= \frac{525{,}600 - 51.0}{525{,}600} = \frac{525{,}549.0}{525{,}600} = 0.999903 \\
\text{ASAI}_\text{(\%)} &= 99.9903\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results Summary (1 Mark):**

- **SAIDI = 51.0 minutes/customer/year**
- **SAIFI = 0.60 interruptions/customer/year**
- **CAIDI = 85.0 minutes/interruption**
- **ASAI ≈ 99.99 %**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:**
> Students often confuse **SAIDI's numerator** as simply "sum of $r_i$" (ignoring $N_i$). The correct numerator is $\sum r_i N_i$ — a *weighted* sum where each event's duration is weighted by the number of customers it impacted. Writing SAIDI = (60+120+30) / 10000 will earn **zero marks** for the calculation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #2:**
> CAIDI is **NOT** (sum of $r_i$) / (number of events). It is SAIDI / SAIFI. The formula CAIDI = $\sum r_i N_i / \sum N_i$ is equivalent and may be used, but writing CAIDI = average of $r_i$ values (e.g., 70 min) is **incorrect** and will cost 1-2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: (a) Explain the step-by-step operational workflow of an OMS, from outage detection to ticket closure. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The OMS workflow consists of **six sequential phases**:

**Phase 1 — Outage Detection (1 Mark):**
Outages are detected through three independent channels: (i) customer trouble calls received via IVR/call center, (ii) AMI last-gasp messages from smart meters, and (iii) SCADA alarms on breaker/recloser operation. These events flow into the OMS event queue within seconds.

**Phase 2 — Event Aggregation & Confirmation (1 Mark):**
The OMS deduplicates multiple trouble calls from the same address, groups calls by transformer and feeder, and confirms whether a real sustained outage exists. A 2-3 minute window is used to allow AMI and SCADA confirmation before declaring an event.

**Phase 3 — Fault Location Prediction (1.5 Marks):**
The OMS maps each caller's address to a service transformer (leaf node) using CIS-GIS join. It then computes the **Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)** of all affected leaf nodes on the feeder tree. The LCA device (typically a fuse, recloser, or breaker) is the predicted fault location. SCADA status is used to confirm.

**Phase 4 — Switching Optimization (1.5 Marks):**
The OMS consults the DMS to recommend switching actions that: (i) isolate the faulted section by opening appropriate switches, (ii) restore upstream customers via the primary source, and (iii) transfer downstream customers to an alternate feeder if load capacity permits. All recommendations respect thermal, voltage, and radiality constraints.

**Phase 5 — Crew Dispatch (1 Mark):**
The OMS Work Order Management module selects the optimal crew based on GPS proximity, skill certification, current workload, and travel time. The crew receives a digital work order via mobile tablet, containing location, fault prediction, and switching instructions.

**Phase 6 — Restoration & Closure (1 Mark):**
As the crew operates switches and completes repairs, SCADA reports new device statuses and AMI reports power restoration at customer meters. The OMS updates the ticket in real time, sends proactive customer notifications (SMS/IVR), and upon full restoration, closes the ticket and updates the SAIDI/SAIFI database.

The complete cycle, from detection to closure, typically takes **30 minutes to 4 hours** in a well-implemented OMS, compared to **6-12 hours** in a manual utility.

---

**(b) A 3-feeder distribution zone has $N_T = 20{,}000$ customers. The following outages occurred: Feeder A — 4,000 customers out for 90 min; Feeder B — 6,000 customers out for 45 min; Feeder C — 2,000 customers out for 180 min. Compute SAIDI, SAIFI, and CAIDI. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Tabulate.**

| Feeder | $N_i$ | $r_i$ (min) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4,000 | 90 |
| B | 6,000 | 45 |
| C | 2,000 | 180 |

**Step 2: Compute $r_i N_i$ products.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_A &= 90 \times 4{,}000 = 360{,}000 \\
E_B &= 45 \times 6{,}000 = 270{,}000 \\
E_C &= 180 \times 2{,}000 = 360{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the products.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum r_i N_i &= 360{,}000 + 270{,}000 + 360{,}000 = 990{,}000 \text{ cust-min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the customers interrupted.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i &= 4{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 12{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute SAIDI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIDI} &= \frac{990{,}000}{20{,}000} = 49.5 \text{ min/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute SAIFI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAIFI} &= \frac{12{,}000}{20{,}000} = 0.60 \text{ int/cust/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute CAIDI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAIDI} &= \frac{49.5}{0.60} = 82.5 \text{ min/int}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results (1 Mark):**

- **SAIDI = 49.5 min/cust/yr**
- **SAIFI = 0.60 int/cust/yr**
- **CAIDI = 82.5 min/int**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #3:**
> When the problem involves **multiple feeders or substations**, students often incorrectly compute SAIDI per feeder and then average the indices. **NEVER DO THIS.** SAIDI is a *system-wide* metric. Sum the customer-minutes across ALL feeders, then divide once by the total customers served. Averaging is mathematically wrong and will cost full marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #4:**
> Some students express CAIDI in "minutes per customer per year" — this is wrong. CAIDI's units are **minutes per interruption** because the customer-base dimension cancels out (SAIDI's customer dimension is divided by SAIFI's customer dimension). Writing wrong units does not lose marks numerically, but it loses the **understanding** mark (1 mark out of 7).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision — Memorize This 12-Point Checklist Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **OMS is event-driven**, SCADA is time-driven, DMS is state-driven — know the difference.
- [ ] **Six IEEE 1366 indices** you must know cold: SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, CAIFI, MAIFI, ASAI.
- [ ] **SAIDI numerator is $\sum r_i N_i$** (weighted by customers), NOT just $\sum r_i$.
- [ ] **SAIFI numerator is $\sum N_i$** (no time term).
- [ ] **CAIDI = SAIDI / SAIFI** (derived identity, no separate memory needed).
- [ ] **ASAI = 1 - SAIDI / 525,600** where 525,600 = 8760 hours × 60 min.
- [ ] **OMS has six core functions**: Detect, Confirm, Predict, Switch, Dispatch, Close.
- [ ] **Fault prediction uses LCA (Lowest Common Ancestor)** in the radial feeder tree.
- [ ] **AMI provides per-customer visibility**; SCADA provides per-device visibility.
- [ ] **GIS is read-only** for OMS; **CIS is read-only** for OMS; SCADA and AMI are **bidirectional**.
- [ ] **ENS = Load × Restoration Time** (in kWh) — the bridge to regulatory penalty calculations.
- [ ] **The "three-nines" ASAI target** (99.9 %) corresponds to ≤ 526 min/cust/yr of SAIDI — Indian urban benchmark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Sentence Definition to Memorize for 2-Mark Questions:**
> "An Outage Management System is a utility software platform that integrates SCADA, AMI, GIS, and CIS to detect power outages, predict fault locations via call clustering, optimize crew dispatch, and compute IEEE 1366 reliability indices (SAIDI, SAIFI) in real time."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Smart Distributed Energy Resources and Their Grid Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Smart Distributed Energy Resources and Their Grid Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Energy Resources (DERs)** are small-to-medium scale power generation, storage, and demand-side management assets that are interconnected to the electric distribution grid (typically at the medium or low voltage level) at or near the point of consumption. When equipped with **digital intelligence, bidirectional communication, and autonomous control capabilities**, they are termed **Smart Distributed Energy Resources (Smart DERs)**.

According to the **IEEE 1547-2018** standard, DERs encompass the **"Source of electric power that is not directly connected to a bulk power transmission system,"** including both generation and storage resources.

### KTU 2024 Categorization of DERs

DERs are broadly classified under the KTU 2024 Scheme into **four functional families**:

| Family | Typical Examples | Power Range |
|--------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Distributed Generation (DG)** | Solar PV, Wind Turbines, Micro-turbines, Fuel Cells, Biomass | **1 kW – 10 MW** |
| **Distributed Storage (DS)** | Li-ion Batteries, Flywheels, Supercapacitors, Pumped Hydro, CAES | **1 kW – 100 MW** |
| **Demand Response (DR) Assets** | Smart Loads, HVAC, EVs, Smart Appliances | Variable |
| **Hybrid/Coupled Systems** | PV+Battery, CHP, Microgrid Clusters | Aggregated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart DERs** are distinguished from conventional DERs by their embedded **smart inverters (IEEE 1547-2018 Category III)**, **advanced metering infrastructure (AMI)**, and adherence to **grid-supportive functions** such as **Volt-VAR, Volt-Watt, and Frequency-Watt** autonomous responses.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plug-and-Play Power Neighbourhood"

Imagine the **traditional power grid** as a **giant water pipeline** where one massive reservoir (the central power plant) pushes water one-way through huge pipes (transmission lines) to every tap (your home). If the reservoir falters, every tap goes dry. There is no way for a homeowner to "share back" or "store" excess.

Now imagine a **Smart Grid with DERs** as a **modern apartment complex**:
- **Each apartment installs its own rooftop solar (PV)**, a small battery (storage), and a smart meter.
- On a sunny afternoon, the apartment actually **produces more energy than it consumes** and can "donate" the excess to the building.
- A **smart energy management system (EMS)** decides *when* to store, *when* to sell back to the grid, and *when* to draw from the central utility.
- The building can even **island itself** (run independently) if the central pipeline breaks, using its own battery + solar.

This is precisely the paradigm shift that **Smart Distributed Energy Resources and Their Grid Integration** addresses: a **bidirectional, decentralized, intelligent, and resilient** power ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition for KTU Exams:** The fundamental shift in modern grids is the transition from a **radial, unidirectional, passive network** to a **multi-source, bidirectional, active network** where intelligence resides at the **distribution edge**, not just the transmission core.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Reference Parameters

The following **standard engineering metrics and constants** are universally referenced in KTU 2024 evaluation of DER integration problems:

| Symbol / Constant | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|-------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| **PCC** | Point of Common Coupling | Bus where DER meets utility grid |
| **V\_LL** | Line-to-Line Voltage at PCC | **415 V (LV)**, **11 kV (MV)** |
| **f** | Nominal Grid Frequency | **50 Hz (India)**, 60 Hz (US) |
| **THD** | Total Harmonic Distortion | **< 5% (IEEE 519)** |
| **PF** | Power Factor at PCC | **0.95 lag – 0.95 lead** |
| **IEEE 1547-2018** | Interconnection Standard | Mandatory compliance |
| **V_deadband** | Voltage Deadband for Volt-VAR | **±5% of nominal** |
| **f_deadband** | Frequency Deadband | **±0.036 Hz (59.964–60.036 Hz)** |
| **LVRT** | Low Voltage Ride-Through | Mandatory in modern codes |
| **kVA_rating** | DER Inverter Apparent Power | Per equipment nameplate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Acceptance Threshold:** Modern grid codes (CEA India, IEEE 1547-2018, IEC 61727) require DER inverters to **trip offline only under extreme abnormal conditions**; otherwise, they must **remain grid-connected and actively support** voltage and frequency.

---

## 1.4 Why Smart DERs Matter — The KTU-Mandated Motivation

The penetration of Smart DERs is driven by **four converging global pressures** that the KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to articulate:

1. **Decarbonization Mandate** — Replacement of fossil-fuel central generation with renewable DG.
2. **Grid Resilience** — Resilience against natural disasters, cyber-physical attacks, and cascading failures.
3. **Economic Efficiency** — Reduction in Transmission & Distribution (T&D) losses by **local generation at load centres**.
4. **Electrification of Transport** — Rapid growth of **EVs** as mobile storage and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Questions in this module frequently test the student's ability to **differentiate between grid-connected mode and islanded (autonomous) mode** of DER operation, and to **map specific technical challenges** (voltage rise, reverse power flow, protection coordination) to **specific grid codes (IEEE 1547-2018, IEC 61727, CEA Technical Standards for Connectivity)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Hierarchy of Smart DER Integration

The integration of Smart DERs into the existing power grid operates on **three architectural layers**, each with distinct technical roles and communication protocols.

### Layer 1 — The Physical Power Layer

This is the **hardware interface** where real electrical energy is exchanged. It includes:
- **Power Electronic Interfaces** (DC-AC inverters, AC-AC converters, DC-DC converters)
- **Protection Equipment** (circuit breakers, fuses, anti-islanding relays)
- **Synchronization Units** (PLL — Phase Locked Loop)
- **Step-up/Step-down Transformers** at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC)

### Layer 2 — The Smart Control & Communication Layer

This is the **"intelligence" layer** that distinguishes Smart DERs from legacy DERs. It includes:
- **Smart Inverters** with embedded DSP controllers
- **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** using protocols like **IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus, IEEE 2030.5**
- **Energy Management Systems (EMS)** at the DER, microgrid, and utility levels
- **SCADA-based Distribution Management System (DMS)**

### Layer 3 — The Market & Optimization Layer

This is the **economic intelligence** layer:
- **Net-metering and Feed-in Tariff (FiT)** settlement
- **Demand Response (DR) signals** from utility to consumer
- **Aggregator platforms** for Virtual Power Plants (VPPs)
- **Real-time pricing** via OpenADR 2.0b protocol

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** A "Smart DER" is **not just a renewable source**; it is the **combination of a physical asset + a smart inverter + a bidirectional communication link + a grid-supportive control algorithm**. Examiners expect all four components in any definition.

---

## 2.2 The Core Integration Challenges — "The Five Vs"

When Smart DERs are integrated into a distribution feeder, **five fundamental technical challenges** emerge. These are the most heavily tested concepts in the KTU 2024 Scheme for this module.

### Challenge 1 — **Voltage Rise (V↑)**

In a **radial distribution feeder**, power flows from the substation to the load. When a DER injects real power **P_DER** at a downstream node, the local voltage **rises** due to the **reverse active power flow** through the feeder impedance.

The simplified voltage rise equation at the PCC is:

$$\Delta V \approx \frac{R \cdot P_{DER} + X \cdot Q_{DER}}{V_{PCC}}$$

where:
- **R, X** = feeder resistance and reactance
- **P_DER, Q_DER** = active and reactive power injected by the DER
- **V_PCC** = nominal voltage at the Point of Common Coupling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Consequence:** On a sunny midday, a heavily loaded rooftop PV feeder can push PCC voltage **above the statutory upper limit (+10% in India)**, causing inverter **anti-islanding trips** and curtailment of renewable energy.

### Challenge 2 — **Reverse Power Flow (RPF)**

When **local generation exceeds local load**, power flows **backwards** from the LV side to the MV side. Distribution transformers and protection devices were originally **designed for unidirectional flow**, leading to:
- **Incorrect operation of directional overcurrent relays**
- **Tap changer mal-operation** in OLTC transformers
- **Bi-directional energy meter compatibility issues**

### Challenge 3 — **Frequency Stability Deviation**

In a low-inertia system with high DER penetration, the **system inertia constant (H)** decreases because inverters do not provide the rotating-mass inertia of synchronous generators. The Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF) becomes very high:

$$RoCoF = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \cdot H \cdot S_{base}}$$

where:
- **ΔP** = power imbalance after a disturbance
- **H** = system inertia constant (seconds)
- **S_base** = system base apparent power (MVA)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly assume that **inverter-interfaced DERs provide inertia**. They do not — unless equipped with **synthetic (virtual) inertia control**, which is a smart inverter function. RoCoF is therefore a **first-order KTU concern** for high-DER grids.

### Challenge 4 — **Harmonics & Power Quality**

Power electronic inverters inject **switching harmonics** into the grid. The Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) at PCC is governed by:

$$THD_v = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

$$\vert V_h \vert = \text{RMS value of the } h^{th} \text{ harmonic voltage}$$

Compliance is measured against **IEEE 519-2014** limits:
- **Voltage THD < 5%** at PCC
- **Individual harmonic < 3%**

### Challenge 5 — **Protection Coordination Failure**

Conventional distribution protection uses **overcurrent relays** graded along the feeder. When a DER injects fault current from the downstream side, it can cause:
- **Sympathetic tripping** of the main feeder breaker
- **Loss of coordination** between fuses and relays
- **Blind spot in protection reach** for faults between the DER and the substation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution Mandated by IEEE 1547-2018:** All DERs must implement **anti-islanding protection** that disconnects the DER within **2 seconds** of an unintentional island formation. Modern codes also mandate **fault ride-through (FRT)** so that DERs **support the grid** during faults rather than simply tripping.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **all critical equations** for this topic. Every variable is defined; every equation is **Kerala board exam-ready**.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend & Units | Application in DER Integration |
|---|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{R \cdot P_{DER} + X \cdot Q_{DER}}{V_{PCC}}$ | R, X in Ω; P, Q in W, VAr; V in V | Voltage rise at PCC due to DER injection |
| 2 | $RoCoF = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \cdot H \cdot S_{base}}$ | ΔP in MW; H in s; S\_base in MVA | Frequency stability in low-inertia systems |
| 3 | $THD_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | V\_h in V; dimensionless % | Power quality compliance per IEEE 519 |
| 4 | $THD_i = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | I\_h in A; dimensionless % | Current harmonic compliance |
| 5 | $I_{sc,DER} = k \cdot I_{rated,DER}$ | k = 1.0–1.5 (typical), dimensionless | Fault current contribution by inverter DERs |
| 6 | $P_{max,DER} = V_{PCC} \cdot I_{rated} \cdot \cos\phi$ | P in W; V in V; I in A | Maximum active power export at PCC |
| 7 | $P_{VAr}^{loss} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot \frac{Q^2}{P^2 + Q^2}$ | P, Q in p.u.; R in p.u. | Loss minimization via PF correction |
| 8 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot V^2$ | E in J; C in F; V in V | Energy in supercapacitor DER storage |
| 9 | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{Ah} \cdot \eta_{coul}$ | E in Wh; Q in Ah; η dimensionless | Battery DER state of energy |
| 10 | $f_{res,LC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}}$ | L in H; C in F; f in Hz | LCL filter resonance for grid-tie inverter |
| 11 | $Q_{Volt-VAR} = m \cdot (V_{PCC} - V_{ref})$ | m = droop slope VAr/V; Q in VAr | Smart inverter Volt-VAR response |
| 12 | $P_{Freq-Watt} = P_{rated} \cdot \left[1 - k_f \cdot (f - f_{ref})\right]$ | k\_f droop coefficient Hz⁻¹; P in W | Smart inverter frequency support |
| 13 | $V_{th,island} = \dfrac{V_{DER}}{Z_{grid} + Z_{load}}$ | V\_th in V; Z in Ω | Thevenin voltage for islanding detection |
| 14 | $P_{V2G,max} = \dfrac{V_{bat}^2}{R_{line}} \cdot \sin^2\delta$ | δ power angle; R in Ω | Vehicle-to-Grid power transfer limit |
| 15 | $CF = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{P_{peak}} = \dfrac{\int_0^T P(t)\,dt}{T \cdot P_{peak}}$ | CF dimensionless; P in W | Capacity factor of renewable DER |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Special Note:** Equations **(1), (2), (3), (11), and (12)** are the **"Big Five"** most likely to appear in numerical problems or derivations. The other ten appear in conceptual/descriptive questions.

---

## 2.4 Smart Inverter Functions — The Heart of Smart DERs

A **Smart Inverter** is the digital brain of a modern DER. Per **IEEE 1547-2018**, a smart inverter must autonomously perform the following **grid-supportive functions**:

| Function | Input Variable | Output Action | Benefit |
|----------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| **Volt-VAR** | V\_PCC | Inject/Absorb reactive power (Q) | Voltage regulation |
| **Volt-Watt** | V\_PCC | Curtail active power (P) | Over-voltage prevention |
| **Freq-Watt** | f\_grid | Adjust active power (P) | Frequency stabilization |
| **LVRT / HVRT** | V\_PCC under fault | Ride through or curtail | Grid stability during faults |
| **Anti-islanding** | V, f, THD | Trip within 2 s | Personnel & equipment safety |
| **Soft-start reconnection** | Reclosure signal | Ramp P, Q over 3–300 s | Prevents inrush on reclose |
| **Power factor control** | P output | Maintain PF at PCC | Loss minimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** In any 14-mark question on Smart DERs, **always include a labelled block diagram of a smart inverter architecture** (DC source → DC-DC boost → DC-link capacitor → DC-AC VSI → LCL filter → PCC) to **secure 2–3 free marks** even if the rest of the answer is partially correct.

---

## 2.5 Microgrid Architecture — The Operational Endpoint of Smart DER Integration

A **Microgrid** is a localized grouping of DERs, storage, and loads that operates as a **single controllable entity** with respect to the main grid. It is the **terminal architectural form** of smart DER integration.

### Two Modes of Microgrid Operation

**Mode 1 — Grid-Connected Mode:**
- The microgrid PCC circuit breaker is **closed**
- Power exchange with the main grid is bidirectional
- The **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** permits import/export
- Voltage and frequency are **slaves** to the main grid

**Mode 2 — Islanded (Autonomous) Mode:**
- The PCC circuit breaker is **open**
- The microgrid operates as a **self-sufficient electrical island**
- One DER (typically a battery or micro-turbine) acts as the **Master** providing voltage and frequency reference using **droop control**
- All other DERs operate as **Slaves** in P-f and Q-V droop

### Droop Control Equations (Master-Slave)

$$f = f_0 - m_p \cdot (P - P_0)$$

$$V = V_0 - n_q \cdot (Q - Q_0)$$

where:
- **m_p** = P-f droop coefficient (Hz/W)
- **n_q** = Q-V droop coefficient (V/VAr)
- **P\_0, Q\_0** = nominal setpoints
- **f\_0, V\_0** = nominal frequency and voltage

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Droop control is the **analogue of synchronous generator governor action**, implemented entirely in software within the smart inverter. It is the cornerstone of **autonomous microgrid operation** and is a **guaranteed question topic** in Module 3.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Rigor Note:** This section provides **fully expanded, multi-step derivations** for the four most critical analytical relationships in Smart DER grid integration. Each step includes a verbal explanation suitable for direct transcription into the KTU answer booklet.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voltage Rise at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC)

### Problem Statement

A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 11 kV distribution feeder with impedance **Z = R + jX = 1.2 + j0.8 Ω/phase** supplies a load at the far end. A customer installs a **rooftop solar PV DER** rated **500 kW** at the load end. The PCC voltage is maintained at **V\_PCC = 11 kV (line-to-line)**. The DER operates at **unity power factor**.

**Task:** Derive the voltage rise at the PCC due to DER injection.

### Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the Simplifying Assumptions**

We assume:
- The feeder is **radial** (single laterals only)
- The DER is at the **far end** of the feeder (worst case)
- Voltage at the substation end is **fixed at 11 kV** (stiff source)
- Per-phase analysis is used; the system is **balanced 3-phase**

> **Explanation:** These assumptions are standard in KTU-level feeder analysis and isolate the voltage-rise effect from other dynamic phenomena.

**Step 2 — Write the Per-Phase Voltage Equation**

For a feeder with sending-end voltage **V\_s** and receiving-end (PCC) voltage **V\_r**, carrying current **I**, the per-phase voltage drop is:

$$V_s = V_r + I \cdot (R + jX)$$

**Step 3 — Express the DER Injected Current**

The DER injects active power P_DER at unity power factor. The per-phase current magnitude is:

$$I_{DER} = \frac{P_{DER}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos\phi}$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

$$I_{DER} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 11 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.0}$$

$$I_{DER} = \frac{500{,}000}{1.732 \times 11{,}000} = \frac{500{,}000}{19{,}052}$$

$$I_{DER} \approx 26.24 \text{ A (per phase)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Voltage Drop Across the Feeder**

The complex voltage drop is:

$$\Delta V = I_{DER} \cdot (R + jX)$$

$$\Delta V = 26.24 \times (1.2 + j0.8)$$

$$\Delta V = (31.49 + j20.99) \text{ V (per phase)}$$

**Step 5 — Extract the In-Phase (Real) Component of the Voltage Drop**

The voltage rise at PCC is dominated by the **in-phase component** of the drop:

$$\Delta V_{rise} = I_{DER} \cdot R \cos\phi + I_{DER} \cdot X \sin\phi$$

For unity power factor ($\cos\phi = 1$, $\sin\phi = 0$):

$$\Delta V_{rise} = I_{DER} \cdot R$$

$$\Delta V_{rise} = 26.24 \times 1.2 = 31.49 \text{ V (per phase)}$$

**Step 6 — Convert Per-Phase Value to Line-to-Line Value**

The line-to-line voltage rise is:

$$\Delta V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \Delta V_{phase}$$

$$\Delta V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \times 31.49 = 54.55 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Percentage Voltage Rise**

$$\% V_{rise} = \frac{\Delta V_{LL}}{V_{LL}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% V_{rise} = \frac{54.55}{11{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$\% V_{rise} \approx 0.496\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The DER injection causes a **per-phase voltage rise of 31.49 V** and a **line-to-line rise of 54.55 V (0.496%)** at the PCC. This is below the 5% statutory limit, so the DER can operate without curtailment. However, if the DER rating were increased to **5 MW**, the rise would scale linearly to **~5%**, triggering inverter over-voltage protection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF) in a Low-Inertia System

### Problem Statement

A microgrid has a total base apparent power **S_base = 10 MVA** and aggregate system inertia constant **H = 1.5 s** (very low, typical of a 100% inverter-based microgrid). A sudden load increase of **ΔP = 2 MW** occurs.

**Task:** Derive the RoCoF using the swing equation.

### Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the Swing Equation**

The simplified swing equation for small-signal frequency behaviour is:

$$\frac{2H}{f_0} \cdot \frac{df}{dt} = \frac{P_m - P_e}{S_{base}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Variables**

- **P\_m** = mechanical power input (in p.u.) — becomes the new steady-state target
- **P\_e** = electrical power output (in p.u.) — initially the pre-disturbance value
- **ΔP = P\_m - P\_e** = power imbalance in per unit
- **df/dt** = RoCoF in Hz/s

**Step 3 — Convert ΔP to Per Unit**

$$\Delta P_{pu} = \frac{\Delta P}{S_{base}} = \frac{2 \text{ MW}}{10 \text{ MVA}} = 0.2 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Swing Equation for df/dt**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{df}{dt} = \frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_{pu}}{2H}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values (50 Hz system)**

$$RoCoF = \frac{50 \times 0.2}{2 \times 1.5} = \frac{10}{3}$$

$$RoCoF \approx 3.33 \text{ Hz/s}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Result**

> **Conclusion:** A RoCoF of **3.33 Hz/s** is **catastrophically high**. The grid code limit (IEC 61727, IEEE 1547) is typically **RoCoF < 0.5 Hz/s**. This confirms the KTU principle: **low-inertia systems need synthetic inertia** from smart inverters to stabilize frequency.

**Step 7 — Compare with a Conventional System**

For a conventional synchronous-generator-dominated system, **H ≈ 5.0 s**. Recomputing:

$$RoCoF_{conv} = \frac{50 \times 0.2}{2 \times 5.0} = 1.0 \text{ Hz/s}$$

The **low-inertia inverter-based system is ~3.3× worse**, validating the need for **synthetic inertia control** in smart inverters.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volt-VAR Droop Characteristic of a Smart Inverter

### Problem Statement

A smart inverter at a PCC is programmed with the following Volt-VAR characteristic:
- **V1 = 0.95 p.u.**: Q = +100% Q\_rated (inject VAr)
- **V2 = 1.00 p.u.**: Q = 0% (no VAr)
- **V3 = 1.05 p.u.**: Q = -100% Q\_rated (absorb VAr)

The inverter has Q\_rated = **50 kVAr** and V\_base = **415 V**. The measured PCC voltage is **V\_PCC = 425 V**.

**Task:** Determine the reactive power output of the smart inverter.

### Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert V\_PCC to Per Unit**

$$V_{pu} = \frac{V_{PCC}}{V_{base}} = \frac{425}{415} \approx 1.0241 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Operating Region**

Since **1.00 p.u. < V\_pu = 1.0241 < 1.05 p.u.**, the operating point lies in the **linear droop region between V2 and V3**, where the inverter must **absorb reactive power** (negative Q).

**Step 3 — Define the Linear Droop Equation**

The droop slope m (in %Q\_rated per p.u. voltage) is:

$$m = \frac{0\% - (-100\%)}{1.00 - 1.05} = \frac{+100\%}{-0.05} = -2000 \text{ %Q/p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Linear Interpolation Formula**

The reactive power in %Q\_rated is:

$$Q_{\%} = 0\% + m \cdot (V_{pu} - V_2)$$

$$Q_{\%} = 0 + (-2000) \cdot (1.0241 - 1.00)$$

$$Q_{\%} = -2000 \cdot 0.0241 = -48.19 \text{ %Q\_rated}$$

**Step 5 — Convert %Q to kVAr**

$$Q = -0.4819 \times 50 \text{ kVAr}$$

$$Q \approx -24.1 \text{ kVAr}$$

> **Conclusion:** The smart inverter must **absorb 24.1 kVAr** to push the local voltage down. The negative sign confirms **inductive (absorbing) operation**, which raises the local line current and drops the voltage — a **self-regulating grid-supportive action**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Conventional vs Smart DER Integration

| Parameter | Conventional DER (Legacy) | Smart DER (IEEE 1547-2018) |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Inverter Type** | Grid-following, P/Q control only | Grid-forming with Volt-VAR, Volt-Watt, Freq-Watt |
| **Response to Voltage Rise** | Trips offline (over-voltage protection) | Autonomously absorbs VAr / curtails P |
| **Response to Frequency Deviation** | Disconnects | Adjusts active power via Freq-Watt droop |
| **Fault Ride-Through** | None — immediate trip | LVRT/HVRT compliant, supports grid during fault |
| **Anti-islanding** | Passive detection only | Active + passive (within 2 s) |
| **Communication** | None (standalone) | Two-way AMI, IEC 61850, IEEE 2030.5 |
| **Reconnection** | Hard reconnect | Soft-start ramp over 3–300 s |
| **Grid Support** | None | Voltage, frequency, harmonic, damping |
| **Reactive Power** | PF locked to 1.0 | Dynamically controlled per V at PCC |
| **Cyber-Security** | None | IEC 62351, encrypted protocols |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** This matrix is a **high-scoring 14-mark answer framework**. The examiner expects students to contrast **at least 6 of these 10 parameters** with specific IEEE 1547-2018 clause references.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Capacity Factor of a Wind DER

A 1.5 MW wind turbine generates the following daily energy output:
- 00:00–06:00 → 0.0 MWh
- 06:00–10:00 → 0.9 MWh
- 10:00–14:00 → 1.8 MWh
- 14:00–18:00 → 1.2 MWh
- 18:00–24:00 → 0.3 MWh

**Task:** Calculate the daily **Capacity Factor (CF)**.

**Step 1 — Sum the Total Energy**

$$E_{day} = 0.0 + 0.9 + 1.8 + 1.2 + 0.3 = 4.2 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Peak Possible Energy**

$$E_{peak} = P_{rated} \times 24 \text{ h} = 1.5 \times 24 = 36 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Capacity Factor**

$$CF = \frac{E_{day}}{E_{peak}} = \frac{4.2}{36} = 0.1167$$

$$CF = 11.67\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A capacity factor of **~12%** is typical for on-shore wind DERs in Kerala, consistent with ISRO–NIWE data for the Western Ghats region.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Smart DER Volt-VAR Response

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GRID as Utility Grid
    participant PCC as PCC Bus
    participant V as Voltage Sensor
    participant DSP as Smart Inverter DSP
    participant INV as Power Stage
    participant LOG as Data Logger

    GRID->>PCC: Feeder voltage rises due to upstream event
    PCC->>V: Sense V_PCC continuously at 4 kHz
    V->>DSP: Transmit V_PCC_raw to DSP
    DSP->>DSP: Compute V_PCC_pu and compare with V_ref
    DSP->>DSP: Check if V_PCC_pu > 1.05 p.u. deadband
    alt Voltage above upper threshold
        DSP->>INV: Command Q = -100 percent Q_rated absorb VAr
        INV->>PCC: Inject inductive reactive current
        PCC->>V: Local voltage begins to fall
        DSP->>LOG: Log event time stamp and Q dispatched
    else Voltage within deadband 0.95 to 1.05
        DSP->>INV: Command Q = 0 percent idle
    end
    LOG-->>EMS: Transmit log to Microgrid EMS via IEC 61850
```

---

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — DER Islanding Detection & Response

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GC: Initial grid connected
    GC --> MON: Continuous monitoring V f THD
    MON --> GC: All parameters within IEEE 1547 window
    MON --> ALERT: V or f or THD out of window
    ALERT --> GC: Transient recovers within 160 ms
    ALERT --> ISLAND: Sustained anomaly beyond 2 s
    ISLAND --> TRIP: Open PCC breaker within 2 s
    TRIP --> STDBY: Wait 5 minutes for grid restoration
    STDBY --> SYNC: Verify V f phase match with grid
    SYNC --> RAMP: Soft start P Q ramp over 3 to 300 s
    RAMP --> GC: Return to grid connected mode
    TRIP --> STDBY: No grid return yet
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Interpretation Note:** The state transition diagram above captures the **mandatory sequence** of a compliant smart DER per **IEEE 1547-2018 Clause 8**. The **2-second islanding trip** and the **5-minute reclose wait** are the two most frequently asked numericals in board examinations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Smart Distributed Energy Resources (Smart DERs). List any four categories with one example each.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Smart Distributed Energy Resource (Smart DER)** is a small-to-medium scale electrical asset (generator, storage, or controllable load) that is connected to the distribution network near the point of consumption and is equipped with **digital intelligence**, **bidirectional communication**, and **autonomous grid-supportive control** capabilities as mandated by **IEEE 1547-2018**.

**Four categories (1 mark each, brief):**

1. **Distributed Generation (DG):** Solar PV rooftop system producing DC → AC at the customer premises.
2. **Distributed Storage (DS):** Lithium-ion battery bank providing time-shifting and frequency support.
3. **Demand Response (DR):** Smart HVAC system that reduces consumption in response to a utility signal.
4. **Electric Vehicle (EV) with V2G:** EV battery that can inject power back to the grid via a bidirectional charger.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the Point of Common Coupling (PCC) in a smart DER installation? Why is it important for IEEE 1547 compliance?**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** is the **electrical node** at which a DER is interfaced with the **Area Electric Power System (EPS)** — typically the busbar of the customer or the secondary of the distribution transformer. (1 mark)

> IEEE 1547-2018 mandates that all compliance tests for **voltage, frequency, harmonics, anti-islanding, ride-through, and power quality** be measured **at or referred to the PCC**. (1 mark)

> The PCC is important because it is the **boundary of responsibility** between the DER owner and the utility — beyond the PCC, the utility guarantees power quality; on the DER side, the owner must meet interconnection standards. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **INTERNAL CHOICE RULE (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a smart inverter** as per **IEEE 1547-2018**. List the **seven mandatory grid-supportive functions** it must implement. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 11 kV radial distribution feeder has impedance **Z = (1.2 + j0.8) Ω/phase**. A solar PV DER of rating **800 kW** is installed at the far end of the feeder operating at **unity power factor**. Calculate the **per-phase voltage rise** and the **% voltage rise at the PCC**. State whether the DER must be curtailed if the statutory voltage limit is **+5% of nominal**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
A labelled block diagram showing:
- **DC Source (PV array)** → **DC-DC Boost Converter** → **DC-Link Capacitor** → **DC-AC Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** → **LCL Filter** → **Isolation Transformer** → **PCC Bus**

**Smart Inverter Control Layer (3 marks):**
- **Phase Locked Loop (PLL):** Synchronizes inverter output with grid voltage angle and frequency.
- **Current Controller:** Inner loop regulates inverter output current via d-q axis decoupled control.
- **Voltage Controller:** Outer loop regulates PCC voltage via d-q reference frame.

**Seven IEEE 1547-2018 Grid-Supportive Functions (2 marks):**
1. **Volt-VAR** (reactive power support with voltage)
2. **Volt-Watt** (active power curtailment with voltage)
3. **Freq-Watt** (active power adjustment with frequency)
4. **Low/High Voltage Ride-Through (LVRT/HVRT)**
5. **Anti-Islanding Detection and Disconnection**
6. **Soft-Start Reconnection with Ramp**
7. **Power Factor / Reactive Power Control**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the per-phase current injected by the DER (1 mark):**

$$I_{DER} = \frac{P_{DER}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos\phi}$$

$$I_{DER} = \frac{800 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3 \times 1.0} = \frac{800{,}000}{19{,}052}$$

$$I_{DER} \approx 41.99 \text{ A (per phase)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the in-phase voltage drop (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V_{phase} = I_{DER} \cdot R \cos\phi + I_{DER} \cdot X \sin\phi$$

At unity power factor ($\cos\phi = 1$, $\sin\phi = 0$):

$$\Delta V_{phase} = 41.99 \times 1.2 = 50.39 \text{ V (per phase)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to line-to-line voltage rise (1 mark):**

$$\Delta V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \times 50.39 = 87.28 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the % voltage rise (1 mark):**

$$\% V_{rise} = \frac{87.28}{11{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 0.793\%$$

**Step 5 — Decision and Recommendation (2 marks):**

> The computed % voltage rise of **0.793%** is well **below the +5% statutory limit**. The DER does **not** need to be curtailed under present conditions. However, if the rating were increased beyond **~5 MW**, or if multiple DERs were aggregated on the same feeder, the voltage rise would exceed the limit and the smart inverter's **Volt-VAR or Volt-Watt function** would have to be enabled for autonomous mitigation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> *(a)* Define a **Microgrid**. With a neat diagram, explain the **two operating modes** of a microgrid — **grid-connected and islanded** — highlighting the role of the **PCC static switch** and the **droop control equations** for autonomous operation. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A microgrid has **S\_base = 5 MVA** and aggregate inertia constant **H = 1.2 s**. A sudden load increase of **ΔP = 1.5 MW** occurs. The system frequency is **50 Hz**. Calculate the **Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)** and comment on whether **synthetic inertia control** must be activated in the smart inverters. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
> A **Microgrid** is a localized cluster of distributed energy resources, storage systems, and loads that operates as a **single controllable entity** with a clearly defined **electrical boundary** and is capable of operating in both **grid-connected and islanded** modes.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
A diagram showing:
- **DER Cluster:** PV + Wind + Battery + Micro-turbine
- **PCC Static Switch** connecting the microgrid to the main grid
- **Loads** (critical and non-critical)
- **Microgrid Energy Management System (MEMS)**

**Two Operating Modes (3 marks):**

| Feature | Grid-Connected Mode | Islanded Mode |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| **PCC Switch** | Closed | Open |
| **Voltage/Frequency Reference** | Provided by main grid | Provided by Master DER (droop) |
| **Power Flow** | Bidirectional | Internal only |
| **Control Strategy** | P/Q dispatch to grid | P-f / Q-V droop |

**Droop Equations (1 mark):**

$$f = f_0 - m_p \cdot (P - P_0)$$

$$V = V_0 - n_q \cdot (Q - Q_0)$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State the Swing Equation (1 mark):**

$$RoCoF = \frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta P_{pu}}{2H}$$

**Step 2 — Convert ΔP to Per Unit (1 mark):**

$$\Delta P_{pu} = \frac{1.5 \text{ MW}}{5 \text{ MVA}} = 0.3 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Compute (1 mark):**

$$RoCoF = \frac{50 \times 0.3}{2 \times 1.2} = \frac{15}{2.4} = 6.25 \text{ Hz/s}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Grid Code Compliance (2 marks):**

> The computed RoCoF of **6.25 Hz/s** is **far above** the typical grid code limit of **0.5 Hz/s** (IEC 61727, IEEE 1547-2018 default). Within 100 ms of the disturbance, frequency would deviate by **0.625 Hz**, potentially causing under-frequency load shedding and equipment damage.

**Step 5 — Recommendation (2 marks):**

> **Synthetic inertia control MUST be activated** in the smart inverters. The recommended technique is the **virtual synchronous machine (VISMA)** or **synchronverter** approach, which mimics the inertial response of a synchronous generator by adding a **proportional-derivative frequency-droop term** to the inverter's active power control loop:

$$\Delta P_{synth} = K_i \cdot \frac{df}{dt} + K_d \cdot (f_0 - f)$$

> where **K\_i** is the inertia emulation gain and **K\_d** is the damping gain. This reduces the effective RoCoF to within acceptable limits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing DER with Renewable Energy Source (RES):**
> DER is a **functional category** (small, grid-edge, bidirectional). RES is a **generation technology** (solar, wind, hydro). All RESs at distribution level are DERs, but not all DERs are RESs (e.g., a battery is a DER but not a RES).
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting the "Smart" qualifier:**
> A DER without smart inverter functions is a **legacy DER** and is **not** eligible for IEEE 1547-2018 compliance. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for omitting the autonomous control aspect.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Wrong Per-Phase / Line-to-Line Conversion:**
> When using $\Delta V = IR$, students often forget to multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ to convert per-phase to line-to-line. This is the **single most common** numerical error in KTU DER papers.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Assuming Inverters Provide Physical Inertia:**
> Inverter-interfaced DERs **do not have rotating mass**; therefore, they do not provide physical inertia. The "synthetic inertia" in smart inverters is a **software emulated response**, and must be explicitly stated.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Misquoting the Anti-Islanding Trip Time:**
> The standard is **2 seconds** for unintentional island (IEEE 1547-2018), not 0.2 s or 20 s. Wrong values will cost **1 mark**.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Omitting the Soft-Start Ramp:**
> After a trip, the DER must **NOT reconnect instantly**. A **3–300 second ramp** is mandatory to avoid transformer inrush and protection re-trip. This is a favourite **1-mark bonus question** for the KTU examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### 1. Definition Anchors
- **DER** = Distributed Energy Resource (generation, storage, or controllable load) at distribution level.
- **Smart DER** = DER + smart inverter + bidirectional comms + grid-supportive functions.
- **PCC** = Point of Common Coupling (boundary of utility–customer responsibility).
- **Microgrid** = Localized cluster of DERs operating as a single controllable entity.

### 2. Numerical Anchors (Memorize These)
- Indian grid frequency: **50 Hz**
- Indian LV: **415 V**; MV: **11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV**
- Statutory voltage tolerance: **±10% of nominal**
- IEEE 1547 anti-islanding trip time: **≤ 2 seconds**
- Reconnection wait time: **≥ 5 minutes**
- Reconnection ramp: **3–300 seconds**
- Voltage THD limit (IEEE 519): **< 5%**

### 3. The "Big Five" Equations
1. Voltage rise: $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{R P + X Q}{V_{PCC}}$
2. RoCoF: $\dfrac{df}{dt} = \dfrac{f_0 \Delta P_{pu}}{2H}$
3. THD: $\dfrac{\sqrt{\sum V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$
4. Droop (P-f): $f = f_0 - m_p (P - P_0)$
5. Droop (Q-V): $V = V_0 - n_q (Q - Q_0)$

### 4. IEEE 1547-2018 Seven Smart Inverter Functions
**Volt-VAR · Volt-Watt · Freq-Watt · LVRT/HVRT · Anti-Islanding · Soft-Start Reconnection · PF/Q Control**

### 5. The Five Integration Challenges ("Five Vs")
**Voltage Rise · Reverse Power Flow · Frequency Deviation · Harmonics · Protection Coordination Failure**

### 6. Microgrid Operational Modes
- **Grid-Connected** = PCC closed, main grid sets V & f, bidirectional power.
- **Islanded** = PCC open, Master DER sets V & f via droop, unidirectional internal flow.

### 7. Standard References for KTU 2024
- **IEEE 1547-2018** — Interconnection standard.
- **IEEE 519-2014** — Harmonic limits.
- **IEC 61850** — Substation communication.
- **IEC 61727** — PV interconnection.
- **CEA Technical Standards for Connectivity (India, 2019)** — Indian regulation.

### 8. Common Synonyms (Use in KTU Answers for Marks)
- "Bidirectional" = "Two-way"
- "Anti-islanding" = "Loss-of-Mains protection"
- "Smart inverter" = "Grid-supportive inverter" = "IEEE 1547-2018 inverter"
- "Volt-VAR" = "Reactive power compensation with voltage"
- "Microgrid" = "Mini-grid" (colloquial)

### 9. Common Mnemonics
- **"Five Vs"** = Voltage rise, V/f control, Volt-VAR/Volt-Watt, Verification by IEEE 1547, VPP integration.
- **"R-H-F-P-C"** for Integration Challenges = Reverse Power Flow, Harmonics, Frequency, Protection, Coordination.

### 10. Final Mantra for KTU Exam
> **"A Smart DER is defined by its SMART INVERTER, governed by IEEE 1547-2018, connected at the PCC, communicating via IEC 61850, and operating in a microgrid that can GRID-CONNECT or ISLAND seamlessly."**

---
