topics.title: Smart Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Inverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Smart Inverter** (also termed *Advanced Grid-Support Inverter* or *Grid-Interactive Inverter*) is a sophisticated power-electronic energy-conversion device that performs the fundamental function of DC-to-AC conversion (or AC-to-DC/AC-to-AC bidirectional conversion) while simultaneously providing autonomous, intelligent, and communicative **grid-supportive ancillary services** to the distribution network. Unlike conventional inverters which are passive, dumb, and operate strictly in a "fit-and-forget" current-source mode, smart inverters embed microprocessor-based control intelligence, real-time sensing, and standardized bidirectional communication interfaces to dynamically respond to grid conditions such as voltage and frequency excursions, dispatch reactive power, curtail active power, and coordinate with utility-level Distribution Management Systems (DMS).

According to the **IEEE 1547-2018** standard (the KTU-referenced benchmark for DER interconnection), smart inverters are mandated to perform a minimum suite of functions including **Volt-VAR control, Volt-Watt control, Frequency-Watt (droop) response, dynamic reactive current injection during voltage sags, anti-islanding protection, and ride-through capability** (Low/High Voltage Ride-Through — LVRT/HVRT, and Low/High Frequency Ride-Through — LFRT/HFRT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Board-Exam Friendly):**
> *"A smart inverter is a microprocessor-controlled, grid-interactive power-electronic converter that converts DC power from Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) into grid-synchronized AC power while autonomously executing grid-support functions such as voltage regulation, frequency response, reactive power compensation, and bidirectional communication in accordance with IEEE 1547-2018 standards."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intelligent Translator & Diplomat"

Imagine the electrical grid as a **multilingual international organization** where:
- **DERs (solar PV, batteries, fuel cells)** are foreign delegates that "speak" only in DC.
- A **traditional inverter** is a basic translator who simply converts the message into the local AC language and shouts it onto the floor — but has **no idea what is being discussed, who is listening, or whether the assembly is in chaos**.
- A **smart inverter** is a **diplomat-translator** who not only translates the message but also:
  1. **Listens** to the moderator (grid voltage/frequency) using its sensors (microphone).
  2. **Adjusts tone** (reactive power injection) to maintain order if voices get too loud or too soft.
  3. **Coordinates** with other diplomats (other inverters) via a secure channel (communication protocol like IEEE 2030.5, DNP3, or Modbus).
  4. **Knows emergency exits** (anti-islanding — disconnects safely when the grid fails to protect line workers).
  5. **Reports back** to headquarters (utility DMS) about its status, generation, and grid health.

This diplomat-translator analogy captures the **three pillars** of smart inverter functionality: **Sensing + Intelligence + Communication**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Grid Frequency | **50 Hz** (India/Kerala), 60 Hz (US) | CEA Grid Standards |
| Nominal Grid Voltage (LV) | **230 V (1-φ), 415 V (3-φ)** | IS 12360 |
| Standard THD Limit (Current) | **< 5%** | IEEE 519-2014 |
| DC Injection Limit | **< 0.5% of rated output current** | IEEE 1547-2018 |
| Power Factor Range | **0.85 lead to 0.85 lag** (adjustable) | IEEE 1547-2018 |
| Voltage Ride-Through Range | **0.65 p.u. to 1.1 p.u.** | IEEE 1547-2018 |
| Anti-Islanding Detection Time | **≤ 2 seconds** | IEEE 1547-2018 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Smart" is a Technical Distinction:** A device must satisfy **at least one of the following** to qualify as "smart" in KTU/regulatory parlance: (i) Autonomous grid-support function execution, (ii) External communication capability, (iii) Remote firmware updateability, (iv) Coordinated dispatch capability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Four-Layer Stack

A smart inverter is best understood as a four-layer hierarchical system:

**Layer 1 — Power Stage (Hardware):**
- IGBT/MOSFET-based full-bridge (H-bridge) or three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) topology.
- DC-link capacitor bank for energy buffering.
- LCL output filter (Inductor–Capacitor–Inductor) to attenuate switching harmonics and meet IEEE 519 THD limits.
- Grid-side electromagnetic interference (EMI) filter.

**Layer 2 — Sensing & Measurement Layer:**
- Voltage transducers (LVDTs, Hall-effect sensors) at PCC (Point of Common Coupling).
- Current transducers for output current monitoring.
- Temperature sensors for thermal derating.
- DC-side voltage and current sensors for MPPT.

**Layer 3 — Control & Intelligence Layer (DSP/FPGA/Microcontroller):**
- **Inner Current Control Loop** — fast (kHz range), typically using Proportional-Resonant (PR) controllers in αβ-frame or PI controllers in dq-frame (Synchronous Reference Frame).
- **Outer Power Control Loop** — slower (tens of Hz), generates current references for inner loop.
- **Grid-Support Function Modules** — Volt-VAR, Volt-Watt, Frequency-Watt, anti-islanding.
- **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** algorithm (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) for PV sources.

**Layer 4 — Communication & Coordination Layer:**
- Wired: RS-485, Ethernet, Power Line Communication (PLC).
- Wireless: Wi-Fi, Zigbee, cellular (4G/5G).
- Protocols: **IEEE 2030.5 (SEP 2.0), DNP3, Modbus TCP, SunSpec, OpenADR**.
- Gateway to utility DMS/ADMS (Advanced Distribution Management System).

### 2.2 Core Smart Inverter Functions (The "Smart" Capabilities)

#### A. Volt-VAR Control (Reactive Power Compensation)
- The inverter dynamically absorbs or injects **reactive power (VARs)** in response to local PCC voltage deviation from nominal.
- **Operating Principle:** If V_PCC > V_nominal → absorb VARs (behave as inductor). If V_PCC < V_nominal → inject VARs (behave as capacitor).
- Helps mitigate voltage rise caused by high PV penetration (the "PV-induced voltage rise" problem in Kerala's distribution feeders).

#### B. Volt-Watt Control (Active Power Curtailment)
- When PCC voltage exceeds a threshold, the inverter **curtails real (active) power output** to prevent over-voltage.
- Provides a "last resort" mechanism if VAR support is insufficient.

#### C. Frequency-Watt (Droop) Control
- Mimics the behavior of conventional synchronous generators in primary frequency response.
- Active power is reduced when frequency rises above nominal (and vice versa) following a configurable droop characteristic.

#### D. Low/High Voltage Ride-Through (LVRT/HVRT)
- The inverter must **remain connected** and support the grid during voltage disturbances (instead of immediate tripping as older inverters did).
- Critical for maintaining grid stability during faults.

#### E. Anti-Islanding Protection
- Detects unintentional islanding (when the grid is de-energized but the inverter continues to energize a local section) and disconnects within **≤ 2 seconds**.
- Methods: Passive (under/over voltage, under/over frequency), Active (Sandia Frequency Shift, active impedance injection), or communication-based (transfer trip).

#### F. Dynamic Reactive Current Injection
- During voltage sags, the inverter injects a proportional reactive current to support grid voltage (similar to STATCOM behavior).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | $S$ in VA, $P$ in W, $Q$ in VAR | Power triangle foundation |
| 2 | Reactive Power | $Q = V_{PCC} \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $\phi$ in rad | Volt-VAR dispatch |
| 3 | Power Factor | $pf = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | dimensionless | Grid-code compliance |
| 4 | Frequency-Watt Droop | $\Delta P = -\dfrac{1}{R} \cdot \Delta f$ | $R$ in Hz/p.u., $f$ in Hz | Primary frequency response |
| 5 | Volt-VAR Characteristic | $Q(V) = \dfrac{Q_{\max}}{2}\left[1 - \text{sgn}(V-V_{ref}) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V-V_{ref}}{V_{range}}\right)\right]$ | piecewise linear, see Section 3 | Voltage regulation |
| 6 | DC-AC Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC,out}}{P_{DC,in}} \times 100\%$ | dimensionless % | Performance metric |
| 7 | Total Harmonic Distortion | $THD_i = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | $I_1$ fundamental, $I_h$ harmonic | IEEE 519 compliance |
| 8 | DC Injection | $I_{DC,inj} < 0.005 \cdot I_{rated}$ | dimensionless ratio | Transformer saturation safety |
| 9 | Per-Unit Voltage | $V_{pu} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{base}}$ | dimensionless | Ride-through boundary |
| 10 | Anti-Islanding Detection Time | $t_{detect} \leq 2$ s | seconds | IEEE 1547-2018 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Smart inverters transform a **passive distribution network** into an **active, self-healing, and self-regulating system**. They are the cornerstone enabler of **high-penetration renewable integration** in India's ambitious 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030, particularly relevant for Kerala's solar rooftop mission and floating PV projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Volt-VAR Characteristic Equation (Piecewise Linear Form)

The IEEE 1547-2018 standard specifies a piecewise linear Volt-VAR curve. We will derive the slope and the operational quadrants.

**Given:** 
- $V_1$ = voltage at which VAR injection begins (typically 0.95 p.u.)
- $V_2$ = voltage at maximum VAR injection (typically 0.92 p.u.)
- $V_3$ = voltage at which VAR absorption begins (typically 1.05 p.u.)
- $V_4$ = voltage at maximum VAR absorption (typically 1.08 p.u.)
- $Q_{max}$ = maximum reactive power capability of the inverter

**Step 1:** Define the dead-band region where $Q = 0$:

$$V_2 \leq V_{PCC} \leq V_3 \implies Q(V) = 0$$

**Reasoning:** In this range, grid voltage is acceptable; no reactive support is required, and the inverter operates at unity power factor to maximize active power delivery.

**Step 2:** Define the VAR injection region (voltages below $V_2$):

$$Q(V) = Q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{V_1 - V}{V_1 - V_2}, \quad \text{for } V < V_2$$

**Reasoning:** When voltage sags (e.g., due to a heavy load or fault), the inverter injects VARs proportionally to the deviation. The slope $\dfrac{Q_{max}}{V_1 - V_2}$ has units of VAR/p.u. and represents inverter "stiffness".

**Step 3:** Define the VAR absorption region (voltages above $V_3$):

$$Q(V) = -Q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{V - V_3}{V_4 - V_3}, \quad \text{for } V > V_3$$

**Reasoning:** When voltage swells (typical for PV-induced over-voltage on a lightly loaded feeder), the inverter absorbs VARs (behaves inductively) to push voltage back down. The negative sign indicates absorption (leading current from grid perspective becomes lagging).

**Step 4:** Saturation limits:

$$-Q_{max} \leq Q(V) \leq +Q_{max}$$

**Reasoning:** The inverter's VAR output is bounded by its nameplate apparent power rating and the prevailing active power output through the constraint $Q_{max} = \sqrt{S_{rated}^2 - P_{actual}^2}$.

**Step 5:** Complete compact Volt-VAR function:

$$
Q(V) =
\begin{cases}
+Q_{max}, & V \leq V_1 \\
+Q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{V_1 - V}{V_1 - V_2}, & V_1 < V < V_2 \\
0, & V_2 \leq V \leq V_3 \\
-Q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{V - V_3}{V_4 - V_3}, & V_3 < V < V_4 \\
-Q_{max}, & V \geq V_4
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, students often forget that $Q_{max}$ is **not constant** — it decreases as $P$ increases because of the apparent power ceiling: $Q_{max}(P) = \sqrt{S_{rated}^2 - P^2}$. Always apply this constraint before plotting the Volt-VAR curve.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Volt-VAR Dispatch

**Problem:** A 100 kVA three-phase smart inverter is operating at $P = 60$ kW. The PCC voltage is $V_{PCC} = 0.94$ p.u. The Volt-VAR curve parameters are: $V_1 = 0.95$ p.u., $V_2 = 0.92$ p.u., $V_3 = 1.05$ p.u., $V_4 = 1.08$ p.u.

**Step 1:** Compute the dynamic $Q_{max}$ at $P = 60$ kW:

$$Q_{max}(P) = \sqrt{S_{rated}^2 - P^2} = \sqrt{100^2 - 60^2} = \sqrt{10000 - 3600} = \sqrt{6400} = 80 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the operating region. Since $V_{PCC} = 0.94$ p.u. lies between $V_2 = 0.92$ and $V_1 = 0.95$, we are in the **VAR injection linear region**.

**Step 3:** Apply the Volt-VAR equation:

$$Q(V) = Q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{V_1 - V}{V_1 - V_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$Q = 80 \cdot \dfrac{0.95 - 0.94}{0.95 - 0.92} = 80 \cdot \dfrac{0.01}{0.03} = 80 \cdot 0.3333 = 26.67 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the new operating power factor:

$$S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 26.67^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 711.1} = \sqrt{4311.1} = 65.66 \text{ kVA}$$

$$pf = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{60}{65.66} = 0.914 \text{ (leading)}$$

**Step 6:** Verify the S-rating constraint: $S = 65.66 \text{ kVA} < S_{rated} = 100 \text{ kVA}$ ✓ (Operation is feasible).

### 3.3 Derivation: Frequency-Watt Droop Characteristic

**Given:** A smart inverter rated $P_{rated} = 100$ kW is connected to a 50 Hz grid. The droop setting is $R = 5\% = 0.05$ p.u./Hz.

**Step 1:** Define the droop equation in per-unit form:

$$P = P_{rated} \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{R} \cdot (f_{nom} - f)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $f = 50.5$ Hz (over-frequency event):

$$P = 100 \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{0.05} \cdot (50 - 50.5)\right] = 100 \cdot \left[1 + 20 \cdot (-0.5)\right] = 100 \cdot [1 - 10] = -900 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Clamp to the physical limits $0 \leq P \leq P_{rated}$:

$$P = \max(0, -900) = 0 \text{ kW}$$

**Reasoning:** The droop specification says the inverter must reduce its output by $1/R = 20$ p.u. per Hz of frequency rise, but the result is clamped to zero (it cannot generate negative power).

**Step 4:** More realistic example — $f = 50.1$ Hz:

$$P = 100 \cdot \left[1 + 20 \cdot (50 - 50.1)\right] = 100 \cdot [1 + 20 \cdot (-0.1)] = 100 \cdot [1 - 2] = -100 \text{ kW} \to 0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** $f = 49.9$ Hz (under-frequency event):

$$P = 100 \cdot \left[1 + 20 \cdot (50 - 49.9)\right] = 100 \cdot [1 + 20 \cdot 0.1] = 100 \cdot 3 = 300 \text{ kW} \to 100 \text{ kW} \text{ (clamped)}$$

**Reasoning:** Under-frequency event — the inverter tries to increase output, but is limited to $P_{rated} = 100$ kW.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The droop coefficient $R$ determines how aggressively the inverter participates in primary frequency control. Smaller $R$ = stiffer response = more grid-supportive. Kerala's islanding/weak-grid scenarios particularly benefit from such controllable frequency response.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Smart Inverter Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC1[DC Source PV Battery FuelCell] -->|Vdc Idc| DCStage
    DCStage[DC Link Capacitor Bank + MPPT]
    DCStage -->|Filtered DC| InverterBridge
    InverterBridge[IGBT MOSFET H-Bridge VSI]
    InverterBridge -->|PWM Switched AC| LCLFilter
    LCLFilter[LCL Output Filter]
    LCLFilter -->|Sinusoidal AC| PCC[Point of Common Coupling PCC]
    PCC --> Grid[LV Distribution Grid 415V 50Hz]
    
    SenseV[Voltage Sensor Hall Effect LVDT]
    SenseI[Current Sensor CT Rogowski]
    SenseT[Temperature Sensor NTC PT100]
    SenseDC[DC Side V I Sensor]
    
    PCC --> SenseV
    LCLFilter --> SenseI
    InverterBridge --> SenseT
    DCStage --> SenseDC
    
    SenseV --> DSP
    SenseI --> DSP
    SenseT --> DSP
    SenseDC --> DSP
    DSP[DSP FPGA Controller Core]
    
    DSP -->|Current Reference Id Iq| InverterBridge
    DSP -->|MPPT Perturb Observe| DCStage
    
    Functions[Grid Support Function Library]
    Functions --> VoltVAR[Volt VAR Control]
    Functions --> VoltWatt[Volt Watt Curtailment]
    Functions --> FreqWatt[Frequency Watt Droop]
    Functions --> LVRT[Low High Voltage Ride Through]
    Functions --> AntiIsland[Anti Islanding Protection]
    Functions --> DynReactive[Dynamic Reactive Current Injection]
    
    DSP --> Functions
    Functions --> DSP
    
    Comms[Communication Interface Layer]
    Comms --> RS485[RS485 Modbus]
    Comms --> Eth[Ethernet IEEE 2030.5]
    Comms --> PLC[Power Line Communication]
    Comms --> Wireless[WiFi Zigbee Cellular]
    
    DSP --> Comms
    Comms -->|Telemetry Setpoints| ADMS[Utility ADMS DMS SCADA]
    ADMS -->|DER Dispatch Commands| Comms
```

### 4.2 Volt-VAR Operational Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[V less than V1 0.95pu] -->|Inject Qmax| B[VAR Injection Mode]
    C[V between V1 and V2] -->|Linear Ramp Up| B
    D[V between V2 and V3 Deadband] -->|Q equals 0| E[Unity Power Factor Mode]
    F[V between V3 and V4] -->|Linear Ramp Down| G[VAR Absorption Mode]
    H[V greater than V4 1.08pu] -->|Absorb Qmax| G
    E --> C
    E --> F
    B --> C
    F --> G
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Control Coordination with Utility

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Utility[Utility Control Center SLDC ALDC] -->|Setpoint Dispatch| DMS[Distribution Management System DMS]
    DMS -->|Volt VAR Curve Settings| Gateway[Smart Inverter Gateway Aggregator]
    Gateway -->|IEEE 2030.5 Protocol| SI1[Smart Inverter 1 Rooftop PV]
    Gateway --> SI2[Smart Inverter 2 Rooftop PV]
    Gateway --> SI3[Smart Inverter 3 Battery Storage]
    Gateway --> SI4[Smart Inverter 4 Community PV]
    SI1 -->|Telemetry Status| Gateway
    SI2 -->|Telemetry Status| Gateway
    SI3 -->|Telemetry Status| Gateway
    SI4 -->|Telemetry Status| Gateway
    Gateway -->|Aggregated Data| DMS
    DMS -->|Grid Analytics| Utility
```

### 4.4 Anti-Islanding Detection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Grid Connected Mode] --> Measure[Measure V and f at PCC]
    Measure --> Check1{V within 0.88 to 1.10 pu?}
    Check1 -->|No| Trip1[Trip Instantaneous OVR UVR]
    Check1 -->|Yes| Check2{f within 49.5 to 50.5 Hz?}
    Check2 -->|No| Trip2[Trip OFR UFR with Delay]
    Check2 -->|Yes| Check3{Active Sandia Frequency Shift Drift?}
    Check3 -->|No Drift Detected| Stay[Remain Connected]
    Check3 -->|Drift Detected| Trip3[Confirm Islanding Trip within 2 sec]
    Stay --> Measure
    Trip1 --> Disconnect[Open AC Contactor]
    Trip2 --> Disconnect
    Trip3 --> Disconnect
    Disconnect --> Alarm[Send Alarm to Utility DMS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a smart inverter. List any four grid-support functions it performs as per IEEE 1547-2018.*

**Model Answer:**

A smart inverter is a microprocessor-controlled, grid-interactive power electronic converter that converts DC power from Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) into grid-synchronized AC power while autonomously providing voltage and frequency support to the distribution network.

Four grid-support functions as per IEEE 1547-2018:

1. **Volt-VAR Control** — dynamic reactive power injection/absorption based on PCC voltage.
2. **Volt-Watt Control** — active power curtailment to prevent over-voltage.
3. **Frequency-Watt (Droop) Control** — primary frequency response through active power modulation.
4. **Low/High Voltage Ride-Through (LVRT/HVRT)** — remain connected during voltage disturbances to support grid stability.

*(Auxiliary valid answers: Anti-islanding protection, dynamic reactive current injection, ramp-rate control, frequency ride-through.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a traditional inverter and a smart inverter in the context of distributed generation.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Traditional Inverter | Smart Inverter |
|---|---|---|
| Grid Interaction | Passive — operates in current-source mode, disconnects on any disturbance | Active — performs grid-supportive functions autonomously |
| Voltage/Frequency Response | Trip on first deviation (no ride-through) | LVRT/HVRT/LFRT/HFRT compliant per IEEE 1547-2018 |
| Reactive Power | Operates at unity PF (or fixed PF) | Dynamically dispatches VARs via Volt-VAR curve |
| Communication | Typically none | Bidirectional (IEEE 2030.5, DNP3, Modbus) to utility DMS |
| Anti-Islanding | Basic passive detection | Multi-method (passive + active + communication-based) |
| Firmware Update | Not field-updatable | Remote firmware upgrade capability |
| Coordination with other DERs | Standalone | Coordinated dispatch via aggregator/gateway |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat block diagram the functional architecture of a smart inverter, clearly identifying the power stage, sensing layer, control/intelligence layer, and communication layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 kVA three-phase smart inverter operates at $P = 180$ kW with unity power factor initially. The PCC voltage drops to 0.93 p.u. Calculate: (i) the dynamic $Q_{max}$ available, (ii) the reactive power dispatched using the Volt-VAR curve with $V_1 = 0.95$ p.u., $V_2 = 0.90$ p.u., $V_3 = 1.05$ p.u., $V_4 = 1.10$ p.u., and (iii) the resulting operating power factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The functional architecture of a smart inverter consists of four hierarchical layers:

**1. Power Stage (Hardware Layer):**
- DC-link capacitor bank for energy buffering and ripple suppression.
- IGBT/MOSFET-based Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) using Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) or Space Vector Modulation (SVM).
- LCL output filter (L1, C, L2) for harmonic attenuation to meet IEEE 519 THD < 5% limit.
- Grid-side EMI filter and AC contactor/relay for grid disconnection.

**2. Sensing & Measurement Layer:**
- Hall-effect voltage and current transducers at PCC.
- DC-side V/I sensors for MPPT feedback.
- Temperature sensors (NTC thermistors) for thermal derating.
- Anti-islanding dedicated frequency sensor with high bandwidth.

**3. Control & Intelligence Layer (DSP/FPGA-based):**
- **Inner Loop:** Current control using Proportional-Resonant (PR) controller in αβ-frame (or PI in dq-frame using Park transformation). Bandwidth ~1–5 kHz.
- **Outer Loop:** Power control with bandwidth ~10–100 Hz, generates current references.
- **Grid-Support Modules:** Volt-VAR, Volt-Watt, Frequency-Watt droop, LVRT/HVRT, anti-islanding, dynamic reactive current injection.
- **MPPT Algorithm:** Perturb & Observe (P&O) or Incremental Conductance (IncCond) for PV sources.

**4. Communication & Coordination Layer:**
- Physical layer: RS-485, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, cellular (4G/5G), PLC.
- Protocols: **IEEE 2030.5 (SEP 2.0)**, DNP3, Modbus TCP, SunSpec Modbus.
- Interface to utility ADMS/DMS for setpoint dispatch and telemetry reporting.

**Block Diagram (Verbal Reconstruction for Exam):**
```
[DERS PV/Battery] → [DC Link] → [Inverter Bridge VSI] → [LCL Filter] → [PCC] → [GRID]
                          ↑              ↑                    ↑
                          |              |                    |
                   [DC Sensors]   [DSP/FPGA Controller]   [PCC Sensors]
                                          |
                          ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
                          |               |               |
                  [MPPT Algo]  [Grid-Support Library]  [Comm Interface]
                                                          |
                                                     [Utility ADMS]
```

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $S_{rated} = 250$ kVA, $P = 180$ kW
- $V_{PCC} = 0.93$ p.u.
- $V_1 = 0.95$ p.u., $V_2 = 0.90$ p.u., $V_3 = 1.05$ p.u., $V_4 = 1.10$ p.u.

**Part (i):** Dynamic $Q_{max}$:

$$Q_{max} = \sqrt{S_{rated}^2 - P^2} = \sqrt{250^2 - 180^2} = \sqrt{62500 - 32400} = \sqrt{30100} \approx 173.49 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Part (ii):** Volt-VAR dispatch. Since $V_2 = 0.90 < V_{PCC} = 0.93 < V_1 = 0.95$, we are in the **linear VAR injection region**:

$$Q = Q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{V_1 - V_{PCC}}{V_1 - V_2} = 173.49 \cdot \dfrac{0.95 - 0.93}{0.95 - 0.90} = 173.49 \cdot \dfrac{0.02}{0.05} = 173.49 \cdot 0.4 = 69.40 \text{ kVAR (injected)}$$

**Part (iii):** New operating apparent power and power factor:

$$S_{new} = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{180^2 + 69.40^2} = \sqrt{32400 + 4816.36} = \sqrt{37216.36} \approx 192.92 \text{ kVA}$$

$$pf_{new} = \dfrac{P}{S_{new}} = \dfrac{180}{192.92} \approx 0.933 \text{ (leading — since Q is injected)}$$

**Feasibility check:** $S_{new} = 192.92 \text{ kVA} < S_{rated} = 250 \text{ kVA}$ ✓

**Final Answer:** $Q_{max} = 173.49$ kVAR, $Q_{dispatched} = 69.40$ kVAR (injected), $pf = 0.933$ leading.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe in detail the IEEE 1547-2018 anti-islanding protection requirements for smart inverters. Explain passive, active, and communication-based detection methods with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a frequency-watt droop characteristic, explain how a smart inverter participates in primary frequency control. A 500 kW inverter is operating at full output when the grid frequency rises to 50.2 Hz. If the droop setting is $R = 4\%$ (0.04 p.u./Hz), calculate the new active power output. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**IEEE 1547-2018 Anti-Islanding Requirements:**
- The inverter must detect the formation of an unintentional island and cease to energize the area within **2 seconds**.
- Voltage window: Disconnect if $V_{PCC} < 0.88$ p.u. or $V_{PCC} > 1.10$ p.u.
- Frequency window: Disconnect if $f < 49.5$ Hz or $f > 50.5$ Hz (for 50 Hz systems, may vary by region).
- Anti-islanding must operate for both balanced and unbalanced islanding conditions.

**Detection Methods:**

**1. Passive Methods:**
- **Under/Over Voltage (UVR/OVR):** A real power mismatch between the inverter and local load causes voltage drift.
- **Under/Over Frequency (UFR/OFR):** A reactive power mismatch causes frequency drift.
- **Phase Jump Detection (PJD):** Sudden phase shift at PCC indicates loss of grid reference.
- *Limitation:* Large Non-Detection Zone (NDZ) when P-Q mismatch is small.

**2. Active Methods:**
- **Sandia Frequency Shift (SFS):** Inverter perturbs frequency slightly each cycle; grid holds frequency stable, but an island causes frequency to drift, triggering detection.
- **Sandia Voltage Shift (SVS):** Inverter perturbs output power proportional to voltage deviation; amplified in island condition.
- **Active Impedance / Impedance Measurement:** Inverter injects a current pulse and measures voltage response to detect grid impedance change.
- *Advantage:* Smaller NDZ compared to passive methods.

**3. Communication-Based Methods:**
- **Transfer Trip:** Utility sends dedicated trip signal via SCADA when grid breaker opens. Most reliable; no NDZ.
- **Power Line Carrier (PLC) Communication:** Continuous pilot signal injection; absence = island.
- *Disadvantage:* Requires utility infrastructure investment.

**Recommendation:** Modern smart inverters deploy a **hybrid approach** (e.g., SFS + UVR/OVR + PLC for critical feeders).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Frequency-Watt Droop Concept:**
The frequency-watt droop characteristic allows the smart inverter to mimic the inertia response of conventional synchronous generators. The droop curve linearly reduces active power output as frequency rises above nominal, providing primary frequency response to stabilize the grid.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$P = P_{rated} \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{R} \cdot (f_{nom} - f)\right], \quad \text{clamped to } [0, P_{rated}]$$

**Given Data:**
- $P_{rated} = 500$ kW
- $f_{nom} = 50$ Hz, $f_{actual} = 50.2$ Hz
- $R = 0.04$ p.u./Hz

**Step 1:** Compute the frequency deviation:

$$\Delta f = f_{nom} - f_{actual} = 50 - 50.2 = -0.2 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the droop equation:

$$P = 500 \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{0.04} \cdot (-0.2)\right] = 500 \cdot \left[1 + 25 \cdot (-0.2)\right] = 500 \cdot [1 - 5] = 500 \cdot (-4) = -2000 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the physical clamp $0 \leq P \leq P_{rated}$:

$$P_{new} = \max(0, -2000) = 0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: The droop coefficient is so aggressive ($1/R = 25$ p.u./Hz) that a mere 0.2 Hz over-frequency event would command the inverter to completely curtail its output to zero (which it physically cannot go below).

**Step 5:** More realistic — let us verify with a milder event $f = 50.05$ Hz:

$$P = 500 \cdot \left[1 + 25 \cdot (50 - 50.05)\right] = 500 \cdot [1 + 25 \cdot (-0.05)] = 500 \cdot [1 - 1.25] = 500 \cdot (-0.25) = -125 \text{ kW} \to 0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6:** With $f = 49.95$ Hz (under-frequency):

$$P = 500 \cdot \left[1 + 25 \cdot (50 - 49.95)\right] = 500 \cdot [1 + 25 \cdot 0.05] = 500 \cdot [1 + 1.25] = 500 \cdot 2.25 = 1125 \text{ kW} \to 500 \text{ kW (clamped)}$$

**Final Answer:** For the given problem, $P_{new} = 0$ kW (fully curtailed). The example demonstrates that aggressive droop settings lead to rapid curtailment even for small frequency deviations, which is why KSU (Kerala State Electricity Board) typically configures milder droop settings (~5% per Hz) in field deployments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $Q_{max}$ dynamic constraint:** $Q_{max} = \sqrt{S_{rated}^2 - P^2}$ — using $S_{rated}$ as $Q_{max}$ directly is the most common error. (Penalty: -2 marks)
> 2. **Incorrect identification of the Volt-VAR region:** Always map $V_{PCC}$ against the four voltage breakpoints $V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4$ before applying the equation. (Penalty: -2 marks)
> 3. **Missing the clamp on P or Q:** Both active and reactive power outputs must be clamped within physical limits. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting power factor sign convention:** "Leading" pf corresponds to VAR injection (capacitive), "lagging" pf corresponds to VAR absorption (inductive). Mixing these up is a frequent error. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 5. **Drawing block diagram without labeling signal flow:** In architecture questions, examiners expect arrows with signal/variable names (e.g., "I_d, I_q references", "V_PCC feedback"). Unlabeled diagrams fetch only partial credit. (Penalty: -2 to -3 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Inverters — KTU 2024 Quick-Recall Bullets:**

- **Definition (Board-Worthy):** Microprocessor-controlled, grid-interactive converter with autonomous grid-support and communication per IEEE 1547-2018.
- **Three Pillars:** **Sensing** (V, I, f, T) + **Intelligence** (DSP-based control) + **Communication** (IEEE 2030.5 / DNP3 / Modbus).
- **Key Topology:** Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) with IGBT/MOSFET bridge, LCL filter, DC-link capacitor.
- **Volt-VAR Function:** Inject VARs when V < V_nominal, absorb VARs when V > V_nominal; piecewise linear curve with four voltage breakpoints.
- **Volt-Watt Function:** Curtail P when V exceeds threshold; last-resort over-voltage mitigation.
- **Frequency-Watt Droop:** $P = P_{rated}[1 + (1/R)(f_{nom} - f)]$; clamped to $[0, P_{rated}]$.
- **Ride-Through:** LVRT/HVRT/LFRT/HFRT — stay connected during disturbances per IEEE 1547 curve.
- **Anti-Islanding:** Trip within 2 seconds; methods = passive (UVR/OVR/UFR/OFR) + active (SFS/SVS) + comm-based (transfer trip).
- **DC Injection Limit:** < 0.5% of rated output current (transformer safety).
- **THD Limit:** < 5% per IEEE 519.
- **Key Constraint:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2 \leq S_{rated}^2$ — limits $Q_{max}$ as $P$ rises.
- **Indian Context:** CEA (Central Electricity Authority) 2019 regulations mandate smart inverter functions for all DERs > 10 kW; Kerala's KSEB rooftop solar program aligns with this.
- **Exam Frequency:** Smart Inverters appear in **every KTU ESE cycle** as Part A (3-mark definition) and Part B (14-mark numerical on Volt-VAR or Frequency-Watt droop).
- **Frequently Confused Terms:** *Grid-tie inverter* (may be dumb) vs *Smart inverter* (must have grid-support + comms); *Microinverter* (small, module-level, may or may not be smart) vs *String inverter* (larger, often smart in modern designs).
- **Communication Protocols Hierarchy:** Field level (Modbus, DNP3) → Enterprise level (IEEE 2030.5, IEC 61850) → Control center (ICCP, OpenADR 2.0b for demand response).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Microgrid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Microgrid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Microgrid** is a localized, self-contained electrical distribution system that integrates multiple **Distributed Energy Resources (DERs)**, energy storage systems, and controllable loads, operating as a single controllable entity with respect to the main utility grid. According to the **U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)** and modern smart grid literature, a microgrid can operate in two distinct modes:

- **Grid-Connected Mode** – Coupled to the upstream utility grid via a *Point of Common Coupling (PCC)*.
- **Islanded (Autonomous) Mode** – Disconnected from the main grid, functioning as a self-sustained electrical island.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A microgrid is *not* a smaller version of the main grid — it is a **cyber-physical energy system** capable of seamless mode transitions, bidirectional power flow, and localized optimization of generation, storage, and demand.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Microgrid as a "Community Kitchen" inside a large hotel.**

| Element | Analogy | Real Microgrid Component |
|---|---|---|
| The big hotel | Centralized national grid | Utility grid |
| Community kitchen | Local energy hub | **Microgrid** |
| Gas, solar heaters, ovens | Multiple cooking methods | **DERs (PV, Wind, Diesel, Microturbine)** |
| Cold storage room | Backup battery | **Energy Storage System (BESS)** |
| Diner's appetite (adjustable) | Demand-side control | **Controllable/Shiftable Loads** |
| Lockable serving window | Circuit breaker at PCC | **Static/Mode Transfer Switch** |

When the hotel's main supply works, the community kitchen supplements it (grid-connected). If the main supply fails, the kitchen locks its window to the hotel and feeds its own diners (islanded mode). The kitchen decides *when to cook, what to use, and how much to share* — just like a microgrid's Energy Management System (EMS).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **PCC (Point of Common Coupling):** Electrical node where the microgrid interfaces with the main utility.
- **IEEE Std 1547:** Benchmark for interconnecting DERs with the grid.
- **IEEE Std 2030:** Standard for smart grid interoperability.
- **Frequency stability band:** Typically $\pm 0.5 \text{ Hz}$ around nominal 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA).
- **Voltage stability band:** Typically $\pm 5\%$ of nominal.
- **Typical Microgrid size range:** **5 kW to 10 MW** (campus / community scale).

---

## 1.4 Visual Snapshot of a Microgrid

> [!TIP]
> A microgrid contains **five mandatory functional layers**:
> 1. **Generation Layer** (DERs: PV, Wind, Fuel Cell, Diesel Genset, Microturbine)
> 2. **Storage Layer** (Batteries, Flywheels, Supercapacitors, Pumped Hydro)
> 3. **Load Layer** (Critical, Non-critical, Shiftable, Curtailable)
> 4. **Control & Communication Layer** (SCADA, EMS, Smart Meters, IEDs)
> 5. **Grid Interface Layer** (PCC switch, protective relays, power converters)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

A microgrid functions through **three tightly-coupled operational subsystems**:

### A. Generation Subsystem (DERs)
- **Renewable DERs:** Solar PV, Wind Turbines, Small Hydro.
- **Conventional DERs:** Diesel Generator, Natural Gas Microturbine, Gas Engine.
- **Power Electronic Interface:** Each DER is coupled through an **inverter** (DC-AC) or **rectifier** (AC-DC) for grid compatibility.

### B. Storage Subsystem
- Provides **ride-through** during source/load transients.
- Performs **peak shaving** and **load leveling**.
- Enables **renewable firming** (smoothing PV/wind intermittency).

### C. Control Subsystem (EMS)
- Performs **economic dispatch** among DERs.
- Maintains **frequency and voltage** in islanded mode (droop control).
- Manages **seamless islanding** and **re-synchronization**.

---

## 2.2 Modes of Operation — Logical Flow

1. **Grid-Connected Mode**
   - PCC switch **CLOSED**.
   - Microgrid can **import / export** power from/to main grid.
   - Voltage and frequency are **slaves** to the main grid (no autonomous control needed).
   - Objective: Minimize cost, maximize renewable self-consumption.

2. **Transition to Islanded Mode**
   - Triggered by: Grid fault, voltage sag, frequency excursion, scheduled outage.
   - **PCC switch opens** in $< 100 \text{ ms}$ (for critical loads).
   - Master DG or BESS becomes **grid-forming** and sets voltage/frequency reference.

3. **Islanded Mode**
   - Microgrid operates **autonomously**.
   - **Droop control** (P-f, Q-V) shares load proportionally among inverters.
   - Storage compensates for instantaneous power mismatch.

4. **Re-synchronization & Re-connection**
   - Phase, frequency, and voltage of microgrid are **matched** with main grid.
   - PCC switch **closes** after synchronization check.
   - Control transfers back to grid-following mode.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power balance equation | $P_{gen} + P_{import} = P_{load} + P_{export} + P_{losses}$ | Energy conservation in the microgrid at PCC |
| 2 | State of Charge (SoC) | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) + \dfrac{\int_{t_0}^{t} \eta \cdot P_{bat}(t)\, dt}{E_{rated}}$ | Battery storage level ($\eta$ = round-trip efficiency) |
| 3 | Droop control (frequency) | $f = f_0 - m_p \cdot (P - P_0)$ | Active power vs. frequency droop ($m_p$ = droop coefficient in Hz/kW) |
| 4 | Droop control (voltage) | $V = V_0 - n_q \cdot (Q - Q_0)$ | Reactive power vs. voltage droop ($n_q$ = droop coefficient) |
| 5 | Droop coefficient (frequency) | $m_p = \dfrac{\Delta f_{max}}{P_{max}}$ | Hz per kW; typically 0.5–3 % droop |
| 6 | Reactive droop coefficient | $n_q = \dfrac{\Delta V_{max}}{Q_{max}}$ | V per kVAR |
| 7 | Inverter rating | $S_{inv} = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | Apparent power capacity of converter |
| 8 | Renewable penetration | $\eta_{RE} = \dfrac{P_{RE}}{P_{load}} \times 100\%$ | % of load met by renewables |
| 9 | Cost minimization (dispatch) | $\min \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i(P_i)$ subject to $\sum P_i = P_{demand}$ | Economic dispatch objective |
| 10 | PV power output | $P_{PV} = P_{STC} \cdot G \cdot [1 + \alpha (T_{cell} - 25)]$ | $G$ = irradiance (kW/m²), $\alpha$ = temperature coeff. |
| 11 | Wind power output | $P_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | $\rho$ = air density, $v$ = wind speed, $C_p$ = power coeff. (Betz limit 0.593) |
| 12 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$ | Battery cycle efficiency |
| 13 | Voltage deviation | $\Delta V \% = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{nom} \vert}{V_{nom}} \times 100\%$ | Must be $\leq 5\%$ per IEEE |
| 14 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = \vert f_{actual} - f_{nom} \vert$ | Must be $\leq 0.5$ Hz per grid code |
| 15 | Islanding detection time | $t_{island} \leq 100 \text{ ms}$ | For critical microgrids (IEEE 1547) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In droop equations, **$m_p$** must be expressed in Hz/kW, *not* in pu, unless normalized correctly. Examiner strictly checks units.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Resilience:** Hospitals, military bases, data centers ride through grid outages.
- **Rural Electrification:** Remote villages powered by solar + battery microgrids (e.g., **Aapni Yojana**, India; minigrids in Africa).
- **Industrial Parks:** Reduce demand charges via peak shaving.
- **Renewable Integration:** Manages high PV/wind penetration without violating grid codes.
- **Carbon Reduction:** Replaces diesel generation in islanded areas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Balance at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC)

**Statement:** At any instant, the net power injected at the PCC equals the algebraic sum of microgrid internal generation, internal consumption, and losses.

**Step 1 — Define power components:**
Let:
- $P_{DG}$ = Total active power from all DERs (kW)
- $P_{bat,dis}$ = Battery discharging power (kW)
- $P_{bat,ch}$ = Battery charging power (kW)
- $P_{L}$ = Total load demand (kW)
- $P_{loss}$ = System losses (kW)
- $P_{PCC}$ = Power exchanged with main grid (+ve = import, −ve = export)

**Step 2 — Write conservation equation inside the microgrid:**

$$
P_{DG} + P_{bat,dis} = P_{L} + P_{loss} + P_{bat,ch} + P_{PCC}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for PCC power exchange:**

$$
P_{PCC} = P_{DG} + P_{bat,dis} - P_{L} - P_{loss} - P_{bat,ch}
$$

**Step 4 — Sign convention interpretation:**
- If $P_{PCC} > 0$ → microgrid **imports** from main grid.
- If $P_{PCC} < 0$ → microgrid **exports** to main grid.
- If $P_{PCC} = 0$ → microgrid is **self-sufficient** (net-zero exchange).

**Step 5 — Sample numerical evaluation:**

Given: $P_{DG} = 120$ kW (PV + DG), $P_{bat,dis} = 30$ kW, $P_{L} = 100$ kW, $P_{loss} = 5$ kW, $P_{bat,ch} = 0$ kW.

$$
P_{PCC} = 120 + 30 - 100 - 5 - 0 = 45 \text{ kW}
$$

Since $P_{PCC} = +45$ kW $> 0$, the microgrid **imports 45 kW** from the main grid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Droop Control Equations from First Principles

**Statement:** In an islanded microgrid with multiple inverter-based DERs, the active and reactive powers are shared without any communication link by using frequency-active power (P-f) and voltage-reactive power (Q-V) droop characteristics.

**Step 1 — Active power flow in a line:**

$$
P \approx \dfrac{V \cdot E}{X} \sin \delta
$$

where $E$ = sending-end voltage, $V$ = receiving-end voltage, $X$ = line reactance, $\delta$ = load angle.

**Step 2 — Reactive power flow in a line:**

$$
Q \approx \dfrac{V^2 - V \cdot E}{X}
$$

**Step 3 — Linearize for small $\delta$:** $\sin \delta \approx \delta$ (in rad).

$$
P \approx \dfrac{V \cdot E}{X} \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \approx \dfrac{P \cdot X}{V \cdot E}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate $\delta$ to frequency:** Phase angle $\delta$ is the integral of frequency deviation:

$$
\delta = 2\pi \int (f - f_0)\, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Apply steady-state approximation:** If we control frequency $f$ directly with $P$, we get the **P-f droop**:

$$
f = f_0 - m_p (P - P_0)
$$

Solving for droop coefficient:

$$
m_p = -\dfrac{\Delta f}{\Delta P} = -\dfrac{f - f_0}{P - P_0}
$$

**Step 6 — Similar derivation for Q-V droop:**

$$
V = V_0 - n_q (Q - Q_0)
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example:**
A microgrid has two inverters:
- Inverter 1: rated 100 kW, 50 Hz nominal, allowed freq drop = 0.5 Hz at full load.
- Inverter 2: rated 200 kW, same droop percentage.

Droop coefficient for Inverter 1:

$$
m_{p1} = \dfrac{0.5 \text{ Hz}}{100 \text{ kW}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Hz/kW}
$$

Droop coefficient for Inverter 2 (same % droop = 0.5 % of 50 Hz = 0.25 Hz):

$$
m_{p2} = \dfrac{0.25 \text{ Hz}}{200 \text{ kW}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Hz/kW}
$$

If total load = 180 kW, then by droop sharing:
- Inverter 1 supplies: $P_1 = \dfrac{1/m_{p1}}{1/m_{p1} + 1/m_{p2}} \cdot 180 = \dfrac{200}{400} \cdot 180 = 90 \text{ kW}$ ... but actually since $m_{p1} > m_{p2}$, Inverter 1 is more "stiff" and gets less incremental share. Correct proportional share uses inverse of droop coefficient: Inverter with smaller $m_p$ picks up **more** load for the same $\Delta f$. Final shared frequency:

$$
f = 50 - 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P_1 = 50 - 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P_2
$$

Solving with $P_1 + P_2 = 180$:

$$
P_1 = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3} + 1.25 \times 10^{-3}} \times 180 = \dfrac{1.25}{6.25} \times 180 = 36 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_2 = 180 - 36 = 144 \text{ kW}
$$

Operating frequency:

$$
f = 50 - 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 36 = 49.82 \text{ Hz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Both equations must yield the same $f$. Check: $f = 50 - 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 144 = 49.82$ Hz. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Renewable Penetration Limit in Islanded Mode

**Statement:** In islanded mode, the maximum instantaneous renewable penetration $P_{RE}^{max}$ is limited by the **grid-forming source's ramp capability** and the storage's SoC headroom.

$$
P_{RE}^{max} = P_{load} - P_{grid-forming,min} - P_{curtailable}
$$

where:
- $P_{grid-forming,min}$ = minimum output of the dispatchable DER (DG, BESS) for stable voltage/frequency reference.
- $P_{curtailable}$ = load that can be shed if renewable generation exceeds demand.

**Numerical evaluation:**
- $P_{load} = 50$ kW
- $P_{grid-forming,min} = 10$ kW (diesel gen must run for voltage reference)
- $P_{curtailable} = 0$ kW

$$
P_{RE}^{max} = 50 - 10 - 0 = 40 \text{ kW}
$$

Hence maximum renewable penetration:

$$
\eta_{RE}^{max} = \dfrac{40}{50} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Pushing renewable penetration beyond this forces the grid-forming DG below its stable operating point, causing frequency collapse.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Microgrid Types

| Parameter | AC Microgrid | DC Microgrid | Hybrid AC/DC Microgrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bus voltage type | AC (400/230 V, 50 Hz) | DC (48 V, 380 V, ± 10 %) | Both AC and DC |
| Power conversion stages | Fewer for AC loads; many for DC loads | Fewer for DC loads; many for AC loads | Optimized — fewer conversions |
| Losses | Higher (multiple AC-DC-AC) | Lower (single conversion) | Lowest |
| Compatibility with PV/Battery | Needs DC-AC inverter | Direct DC connection | Native |
| Protection complexity | High (AC protection gear) | Low (DC breakers emerging) | Moderate |
| Suitability | Conventional loads, motor loads | Data centers, telecom, EVs | Mixed residential + commercial |
| Example application | IIT campus, military base | Telecom tower, EV charging station | Smart village, green building |

---

## 3.5 Key Operational Standards (Reference Matrix)

| Standard | Scope | Relevance to Microgrid |
|---|---|---|
| **IEEE 1547** | Interconnection of DERs | Defines islanding, voltage/freq ride-through |
| **IEEE 2030** | Smart grid interoperability | Communication protocol framework |
| **IEC 61850** | Substation automation | IED-to-IED communication |
| **IEEE 519** | Harmonic limits | Power quality in islanded mode |
| **UL 1741** | Inverter testing | Anti-islanding & grid support |
| **IS 1692 / CEA Reg.** | India grid code | Indian context, frequency = 50 Hz |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Microgrid Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef source fill:#FCE570,stroke:#A07A00,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#7FD1AE,stroke:#0A6E3F,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FFA577,stroke:#7A2E0E,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#9CC2F0,stroke:#0E3A75,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#5A1F5A,color:#000

    subgraph M1[Microgrid Domain]
        A1[PV Solar Array]:::source
        A2[Wind Turbine]:::source
        A3[Diesel Generator]:::source
        A4[Fuel Cell]:::source
        B1[Battery Energy Storage]:::storage
        B2[Flywheel]:::storage
        L1[Critical Load]:::load
        L2[Non-critical Load]:::load
        L3[Shiftable Load]:::load
    end

    C1[Microgrid Central Controller]:::control
    C2[Energy Management System]:::control
    C3[SCADA]:::control

    PCC[Point of Common Coupling + Static Switch]:::grid
    G1[Utility Main Grid]:::grid

    A1 --> PCC
    A2 --> PCC
    A3 --> PCC
    A4 --> PCC
    B1 --> PCC
    B2 --> PCC
    L1 --> PCC
    L2 --> PCC
    L3 --> PCC

    PCC <--> G1
    C1 --> A1
    C1 --> A2
    C1 --> A3
    C1 --> A4
    C1 --> B1
    C1 --> B2
    C2 --> C1
    C3 --> C2
```

---

## 4.2 Microgrid Operating Mode Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GridConnected
    GridConnected --> IslandingDetected: Grid fault / Outage
    IslandingDetected --> Islanded: PCC switch opens
    Islanded --> ReSync: Grid restored
    ReSync --> GridConnected: Phase match + PCC closes
    Islanded --> BlackStart: Total shutdown
    BlackStart --> Islanded: BESS forms grid
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Control Architecture of a Microgrid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef tier0 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#7A4A00,color:#000
    classDef tier1 fill:#80C8FF,stroke:#0A4470,color:#000
    classDef tier2 fill:#A0E57C,stroke:#1F4D0E,color:#000
    classDef tier3 fill:#FF9999,stroke:#7A1F1F,color:#000

    T0[Primary Control - Local - Droop P-f and Q-V]:::tier3
    T1[Secondary Control - Microgrid - Frequency and Voltage Restoration]:::tier2
    T2[Tertiary Control - EMS - Economic Dispatch and Power Exchange]:::tier1
    T3[Quaternary Control - Market Layer - Grid Transactions]:::tier0

    T0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** Always draw the **3-tier control structure** (Primary / Secondary / Tertiary) — questions worth 7–14 marks often require it. State the **time-scale** of each layer: Primary (ms), Secondary (s), Tertiary (min–hr).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a microgrid. List any four key components of a microgrid.**

**Model Answer:**
A microgrid is a localized group of distributed energy resources (DERs), energy storage systems, and loads that can operate **autonomously (islanded)** or **interconnected with the main utility grid** through a Point of Common Coupling (PCC), functioning as a single controllable entity.

**Four key components:**
1. Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) — PV, wind, diesel genset, fuel cell.
2. Energy Storage System (BESS / flywheel / supercapacitor).
3. Microgrid Energy Management System (MEMS / EMS).
4. Point of Common Coupling (PCC) with mode transfer switch.
5. Communication & SCADA infrastructure.

> [!TIP]
> 3-mark answers require *one definition sentence* + *bullet list* — keep it under 80 words for 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between grid-connected and islanded modes of microgrid operation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Grid-Connected Mode | Islanded Mode |
|---|---|---|
| PCC switch | Closed | Open |
| Voltage/frequency reference | Main grid | Internal grid-forming source (DG/BESS) |
| Power exchange | Bidirectional | None with main grid |
| Renewable share | High (exportable) | Limited by grid-forming capacity |
| Control strategy | Grid-following (PQ control) | Grid-forming (V/f or droop) |
| Stability | Maintained by grid | Self-stabilization required |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question 3 — Choice A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of an AC microgrid**, listing the functions of the **Microgrid Central Controller (MCC)** and the **Energy Management System (EMS)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **P-f and Q-V droop control equations** for load sharing among parallel inverters in an islanded microgrid. A microgrid has two inverters rated **50 kW and 100 kW** with a common droop setting of **3%** (i.e., allowed freq drop = 1.5 Hz at full load). If the total load is **120 kW**, find the **load shared** by each inverter and the **operating frequency**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- The AC microgrid architecture diagram includes DERs (PV, Wind, DG, FC), BESS, AC loads, PCC switch, and the **two-layer control**:
  - **MCC** (Microgrid Central Controller) – supervises voltage/frequency, handles mode transitions (grid-tied ↔ islanded), and coordinates DERs.
  - **EMS** (Energy Management System) – performs economic dispatch, unit commitment, demand-side management, forecasting, and market participation.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks are awarded separately for: (i) labelled diagram — 2 marks, (ii) MCC functions — 2 marks, (iii) EMS functions — 2 marks, (iv) mode transition explanation — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Step 1:** Droop coefficient for Inverter 1 (rated 50 kW, $\Delta f_{max} = 1.5$ Hz):

$$
m_{p1} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ Hz}}{50 \text{ kW}} = 0.03 \text{ Hz/kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Droop coefficient for Inverter 2 (rated 100 kW, $\Delta f_{max} = 1.5$ Hz):

$$
m_{p2} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ Hz}}{100 \text{ kW}} = 0.015 \text{ Hz/kW}
$$

**Step 3:** In steady state, both inverters operate at the **same frequency**:

$$
f_0 - m_{p1} \cdot P_1 = f_0 - m_{p2} \cdot P_2
$$

$$
m_{p1} \cdot P_1 = m_{p2} \cdot P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = \dfrac{m_{p1}}{m_{p2}} \cdot P_1
$$

**Step 4:** Apply power balance:

$$
P_1 + P_2 = 120 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{m_{p1}}{m_{p2}} \cdot P_1 = 120
$$

$$
P_1 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{0.03}{0.015}\right) = 120 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_1 \cdot 3 = 120
$$

$$
\boxed{P_1 = 40 \text{ kW}, \quad P_2 = 80 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 5:** Operating frequency:

$$
f = 50 - 0.03 \times 40 = 50 - 1.2 = \boxed{48.8 \text{ Hz}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert the **% droop to Hz** before computing $m_p$. If droop is given as 3%, the actual frequency deviation is $0.03 \times 50 = 1.5$ Hz. **This is the #1 mark-losing mistake.**

---

### Question 3 — Choice B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **four hierarchical control levels** of a microgrid (primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary). State the time-scale and the controller of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microgrid has total load demand of **200 kW**, supplied by: PV = **80 kW**, Wind = **40 kW**, Diesel Generator = **60 kW**, and BESS (discharging) = **30 kW**. Compute the **power exchange at the PCC** and state whether the microgrid is importing or exporting. Assume losses = **5 kW**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
| Level | Function | Time-scale | Controller |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Local current/voltage control, droop-based P-f & Q-V | $<\!1$ ms to 100 ms | Inverter local controller |
| **Secondary** | Restores nominal voltage and frequency; compensates droop deviation | 0.1 s to few seconds | Microgrid Central Controller (MCC) |
| **Tertiary** | Power flow control, economic dispatch, mode management | Seconds to minutes | Energy Management System (EMS) |
| **Quaternary** | Market participation, demand response, grid transactions | Minutes to hours | Aggregator / Market operator |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
Given:
- $P_{PV} = 80$ kW, $P_{wind} = 40$ kW, $P_{DG} = 60$ kW, $P_{bat,dis} = 30$ kW
- $P_L = 200$ kW, $P_{loss} = 5$ kW, $P_{bat,ch} = 0$ kW

**Step 1:** Total internal generation:

$$
P_{gen}^{total} = 80 + 40 + 60 + 30 = 210 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Power balance at PCC:

$$
P_{PCC} = P_{gen}^{total} - P_L - P_{loss} - P_{bat,ch}
$$

$$
P_{PCC} = 210 - 200 - 5 - 0 = +5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: $P_{PCC} = +5$ kW $> 0$ → Microgrid **imports 5 kW** from the main grid.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Choice B):** Some students treat $P_{PCC} > 0$ as export. **Remember: +ve = import, −ve = export** (when import is defined as positive). State the **sign convention explicitly** in the first line of the answer to score full marks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in Microgrid questions:**
> 1. **Confusing grid-following vs grid-forming inverters** — grid-following needs an external reference; grid-forming can create its own.
> 2. **Sign convention error at PCC** — always declare it.
> 3. **Forgetting losses** in power balance — 1 mark deduction guaranteed.
> 4. **Mixing up AC/DC microgrid protection** — AC uses CB; DC needs solid-state or hybrid breakers.
> 5. **Skipping the time-scale** while explaining hierarchical control — KTU explicitly tests this.
> 6. **Drawing droop graph as straight line from $P_0$** instead of through $(0, f_0)$ — the droop equation is $f = f_0 - m_p (P - P_0)$.
> 7. **Not labelling units** in droop coefficient — Hz/kW must be mentioned.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Microgrid = local cluster of DERs + storage + loads operating as a single controllable entity.
- **Two operating modes:** Grid-connected (PCC closed) and Islanded (PCC open).
- **Mandatory components:** DERs, Storage, EMS, PCC, Communication, Protection.
- **AC vs DC vs Hybrid microgrid** — selection based on load type and conversion efficiency.
- **Droop equations:** $f = f_0 - m_p (P - P_0)$ and $V = V_0 - n_q (Q - Q_0)$ — basis of decentralized load sharing.
- **Inverter control modes:** Grid-forming (V/f), grid-following (PQ), and grid-supporting (droop).
- **Hierarchical control:** Primary (ms) → Secondary (s) → Tertiary (min) → Quaternary (hr).
- **Power balance:** $P_{gen} + P_{import} = P_{load} + P_{export} + P_{losses}$.
- **Key standards:** IEEE 1547 (interconnection), IEEE 2030 (interop), UL 1741 (inverters), IEEE 519 (harmonics), IEC 61850 (comms).
- **Islanding detection time:** $< 100$ ms for critical microgrids.
- **Renewable penetration limit:** Bounded by minimum grid-forming DG output.
- **Betz limit:** $C_p \leq 0.593$ for wind turbines.
- **Frequency band:** 49.5 – 50.5 Hz in India (CEA grid code).
- **Voltage band:** $\pm 5\%$ of nominal.
- **Seamless transition:** Requires fast switching (static transfer switch) and pre-synchronization.
- **Re-synchronization:** Match phase, frequency, and voltage before closing PCC.
- **Black start capability:** Must be present in storage for full islanded operation.
- **Real-world examples:** IIT campus microgrids, smart village projects, Aapni Yojana (Rajasthan), Brooklyn Microgrid (USA), Kythnos Island (Greece).
- **Common exam keywords:** PCC, DER, BESS, EMS, droop, grid-forming, islanding, black start, hierarchical control, anti-islanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need and Application of Microgrid, Energy Management, Role of Technology in Demand Response, Demand Side Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need and Application of Microgrid, Energy Management, Role of Technology in Demand Response, Demand Side Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microgrid — Formal Definition

A **Microgrid** is a localized, semi-autonomous group of interconnected distributed energy resources (DERs), energy storage systems (ESS), and controllable loads that operate as a single, dispatchable entity. It can function either:
- **Grid-connected mode** — synchronized with the main utility grid, or
- **Islanded mode** — operating independently when disconnected from the upstream grid.

According to the **U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)** and the **IEC 62898** standard, a microgrid possesses clearly defined electrical boundaries, a single point of common coupling (PCC), and an internal Microgrid Energy Management System (MEMS) that ensures stable, economic, and reliable operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A microgrid is *not* merely a collection of solar panels and batteries. The defining feature is its ability to **seamlessly island and re-synchronize** with the main grid while maintaining local frequency and voltage stability (typically **50 Hz ± 0.5 Hz** and **400 V / 11 kV** in Indian distribution standards).

## 1.2 Need for Microgrids — The "Why"

The modern power grid faces structural weaknesses that microgrids directly address:

| Challenge in Conventional Grid | How a Microgrid Solves It |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Centralized generation, long transmission losses (typically **5–8%** technical loss) | Local generation near load → reduced T&D losses |
| Poor reliability during faults / natural disasters | Islanding capability → **self-healing** distribution |
| High penetration of variable RES causes stability issues | Internal EMS balances generation and demand |
| Rural / remote electrification economically unviable | Off-grid microgrids with solar + battery storage |
| Carbon emissions from thermal generation | High share of **renewable** DERs (PV, wind, micro-hydro) |

## 1.3 Applications of Microgrids

- **Campus / Institutional Microgrids** — universities, hospitals, military bases (e.g., IIT-Kanpur microgrid, IIT-Bombay smart grid lab).
- **Remote Village Electrification** — *e.g., the Chhattisgarh and Assam microgrid projects in India under the Ministry of Power's Smart Grid Mission.*
- **Industrial / Commercial Microgrids** — factories, data centers, IT parks.
- **Military Forward Operating Bases** — require zero-interruption power.
- **Utility Distribution Microgrids** — part of *smart city* infrastructure.
- **Community Microgrids** — Brooklyn Microgrid (USA), Kerala's **Kannur KSEB pilot** project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Students):**
> Imagine the main power grid as a *single large water tank on a hill* feeding an entire city. If the main pipe bursts, the whole city goes dry. A **microgrid is like a local water tank with its own pump and rooftop rainwater harvesting** in each neighborhood. Normally it draws from the main pipe, but during an emergency (a burst main), it disconnects and runs on its own harvested water. The **Microgrid Energy Management System (MEMS)** is the *automatic valve controller* that decides when to draw from the main, when to use stored water, and when to disconnect — all in real time.

## 1.4 Demand Response (DR) — Formal Definition

**Demand Response (DR)** is a tariff or control mechanism that motivates end-use electricity customers to **modify their consumption patterns** — in terms of timing, level of instantaneous demand, or total energy use — in response to grid signals (price, incentives, or reliability events).

## 1.5 Demand Side Management (DSM) — Formal Definition

**Demand Side Management (DSM)** is the broader umbrella that includes *all* activities undertaken by utilities or system operators to influence consumer energy use, including:
- Energy efficiency (EE)
- Demand Response (DR)
- Time-of-Use (ToU) tariffs
- Strategic load growth (e.g., electric vehicle charging at off-peak)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DSM ⊃ DR** — Demand Response is a *subset* of Demand Side Management. DSM encompasses long-term energy efficiency programs, while DR focuses on **short-term load shifting** (minutes to hours) in response to dynamic grid conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microgrid Architecture — The Three-Layer Model

A microgrid can be understood in **three logical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (Generation, Storage, Loads)**
- Distributed Energy Resources (DERs): Solar PV, wind, micro-turbines, fuel cells, diesel gensets.
- Energy Storage Systems (ESS): Lithium-ion batteries, supercapacitors, pumped hydro, flywheels.
- Controllable + Non-controllable Loads: HVAC, lighting, EV chargers, industrial processes.

**Layer 2 — Control & Communication Layer**
- **Primary Control** — local droop control of inverters (millisecond response).
- **Secondary Control** — restores nominal frequency/voltage after islanding (seconds).
- **Tertiary Control** — economic dispatch, grid synchronization (minutes to hours).
- Communication: IEC 61850, IEEE 2030.5, DNP3, Modbus, MQTT.

**Layer 3 — Energy Management Layer (MEMS)**
- Performs unit commitment, economic dispatch, load forecasting, and market participation.
- Optimizes a real-time cost function subject to power balance and operational constraints.

## 2.2 Microgrid Energy Management — Mathematical Formulation

The **central optimization problem** of any Microgrid Energy Management System (MEMS) is:

$$
\min_{P_G, P_{ESS}, P_{load}^{shed}} \quad
J = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N_G} C_i(P_{G,i}(t)) + C_{grid}(t) \cdot P_{grid}(t) \right]
$$

subject to constraints:

**Power Balance Constraint**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N_G} P_{G,i}(t) \;+\; P_{grid}(t) \;+\; P_{ESS}^{dis}(t) \;-\; P_{ESS}^{ch}(t) \;=\; P_{L}(t) \;-\; P_{LS}(t)
$$

**Generator Capacity Limits**

$$
P_{G,i}^{\min} \le P_{G,i}(t) \le P_{G,i}^{\max}
$$

**Battery State-of-Charge Dynamics**

$$
SOC(t+1) = SOC(t) + \frac{\eta_{ch} P_{ESS}^{ch}(t)\,\Delta t - \tfrac{1}{\eta_{dis}} P_{ESS}^{dis}(t)\,\Delta t}{E_{rated}}
$$

**Battery SOC Limits**

$$
SOC^{\min} \le SOC(t) \le SOC^{\max}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world meaning of the cost function:** The MEMS picks the **cheapest mix of available energy sources every 15 minutes** — just like you might choose between a coupon, a credit card, or your savings account to pay a bill. The penalty term on load shedding ($C_{LS} \cdot P_{LS}$) ensures the optimizer never willingly cuts power to a critical load (hospital ICU) before exhausting cheaper options.

## 2.3 Demand Response — Categories & Formulas

### 2.3.1 Classification of DR Programs

| Type | Mechanism | Customer Action |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **Price-Based DR** | Time-of-Use (ToU), Real-Time Pricing (RTP), Critical Peak Pricing (CPP) | Voluntary shift based on price signal |
| **Incentive-Based DR** | Direct Load Control (DLC), Interruptible Load, Emergency DR, Capacity Market | Contracted curtailment in exchange for payment |

### 2.3.2 Key DR Formulas

**Price Elasticity of Demand**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}
$$

where $Q$ is quantity (kWh) and $P$ is price (₹/kWh).

**Customer Bill Savings under RTP**

$$
S = \sum_{t=1}^{T} P_{price}(t) \cdot \left[ L_{baseline}(t) - L_{actual}(t) \right]
$$

**Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR) — a DR Performance Metric**

$$
PAR = \frac{\max_{t} L(t)}{\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} L(t)}
$$

**Demand Response Event Energy Reduction**

$$
E_{DR} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ L_{baseline}(t) - L_{actual}(t) \right] dt
$$

## 2.4 Demand Side Management — The Six Pillars

The **three "E"** classical framework of DSM consists of:
1. **Energy Efficiency** — reduce energy use for the same service.
2. **Energy Conservation** — reduce consumption via behavior change.
3. **Demand Response** — shift load in time.

KTU 2024 also recognizes **six DSM strategies**:

1. Peak Clipping
2. Valley Filling
3. Load Shifting
4. Strategic Conservation
5. Strategic Load Growth
6. Flexible Load Shape

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|-----------------|------------|-------|-----------------|
| Power balance | $\sum P_{G} + P_{grid} = P_{L}$ | kW | Energy management |
| Battery SOC update | $SOC(t+1) = SOC(t) + \tfrac{\eta \cdot P \cdot \Delta t}{E_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | Storage scheduling |
| Price elasticity | $\varepsilon = \tfrac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}$ | unitless | DR program design |
| Peak-to-Average Ratio | $PAR = \tfrac{L_{peak}}{\bar{L}}$ | unitless | Load profile analysis |
| Levelized Cost of Energy | $LCOE = \tfrac{\sum_{t} (CAPEX_t + OPEX_t) / (1+r)^t}{\sum_{t} E_t / (1+r)^t}$ | ₹/kWh | DER investment decisions |
| Load Factor | $LF = \tfrac{\bar{L}}{L_{peak}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Grid utilization |
| DG penetration ratio | $DPR = \tfrac{P_{DER}^{total}}{P_{peak}^{load}}$ | pu or % | Microgrid sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in engineering practice:** A microgrid designer must size the battery (using the SOC equation), pitch a DR program to a regulator (using elasticity), and justify ROI (using LCOE). All three are KTU-exam favorites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation: Battery State-of-Charge Update Equation

**Starting premise:** A battery has rated energy $E_{rated}$ (in kWh). During a time interval $\Delta t$, it either absorbs charging power $P_{ch}$ or delivers discharging power $P_{dis}$.

**Step 1 — Energy delivered to battery during charging:**

$$
E_{stored}^{ch} = P_{ch} \cdot \Delta t \cdot \eta_{ch}
$$

This is the energy input multiplied by charging efficiency $\eta_{ch}$ (typically **0.90 to 0.95** for Li-ion).

**Step 2 — Energy removed from battery during discharging:**

$$
E_{removed}^{dis} = \frac{P_{dis} \cdot \Delta t}{\eta_{dis}}
$$

The division by $\eta_{dis}$ accounts for round-trip inefficiencies.

**Step 3 — Express SOC as a ratio of remaining energy to rated energy:**

$$
SOC(t) = \frac{E_{battery}(t)}{E_{rated}}
$$

**Step 4 — Update SOC for the next time step:**

$$
SOC(t+1) = SOC(t) + \frac{\eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch}(t) \cdot \Delta t}{E_{rated}} - \frac{P_{dis}(t) \cdot \Delta t}{\eta_{dis} \cdot E_{rated}}
$$

This is the discrete-time form used by every microgrid EMS in commercial software (e.g., HOMER Pro, DER-CAM, GridLAB-D).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Microgrid Dispatch Problem

**Problem Statement:**

A 50 kW peak microgrid has:
- **PV generation** (forecast): 20 kW available from 10:00–16:00, 0 kW otherwise.
- **Diesel generator**: 0 to 30 kW, fuel cost = **₹10 / kWh**.
- **Battery**: 50 kWh rated, SOC limits [0.2, 0.9], initial SOC = 0.5, $\eta_{ch} = \eta_{dis} = 1.0$.
- **Grid import cost**: ₹6 / kWh.
- **Load** at 13:00 = **35 kW**, at 22:00 = **10 kW**.
- Time step $\Delta t = 1$ hour.

Find the optimal dispatch at **13:00** and at **22:00**, and update the battery SOC.

### 3.2.1 Solution at t = 13:00 (Daytime, PV available)

**Step 1 — Identify available resources:**
- PV: 20 kW (free, but non-dispatchable beyond 20 kW)
- Diesel: 0 to 30 kW at ₹10/kWh
- Grid: any amount at ₹6/kWh
- Battery: SOC = 0.5, can discharge up to 30 kW

**Step 2 — Apply cheapest-first priority:**

Since grid at ₹6/kWh is **cheaper** than diesel at ₹10/kWh, the EMS will first use the free PV (20 kW), then import from grid.

**Step 3 — Cover the load:**

$$
P_{PV} + P_{grid} = 35 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
20 + P_{grid} = 35 \implies P_{grid} = 15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Cost at 13:00:**

$$
C_{13:00} = 15 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ h} \times \text{₹}6/\text{kWh} = \text{₹}90
$$

### 3.2.2 Solution at t = 22:00 (Night, no PV)

**Step 1 — Available resources:**
- PV: 0 kW
- Diesel: ₹10/kWh
- Grid: ₹6/kWh
- Battery: SOC = 0.5 → can supply up to 25 kW (to maintain SOC ≥ 0.2)

**Step 2 — Optimal choice:**

Grid import at ₹6/kWh is the cheapest. Since the load is only 10 kW, the battery is **not discharged** (discharging would be wasteful without higher price benefit).

**Step 3 — Cover the load:**

$$
P_{grid} = 10 \text{ kW}
$$

**Cost at 22:00:**

$$
C_{22:00} = 10 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ h} \times \text{₹}6/\text{kWh} = \text{₹}60
$$

**Step 4 — Update battery SOC (assuming battery not used):**

$$
SOC(23:00) = 0.5 + \frac{1 \cdot 0 \cdot 1}{50} - \frac{0}{1 \cdot 50} = 0.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world interpretation:** At ₹6/kWh grid price, diesel is too expensive to run unless grid fails. This is a *grid-connected* microgrid maximizing economic savings — the *exact* optimization the KTU question setter loves.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Microgrid Control Strategies

| Criterion | Grid-Forming Inverter | Grid-Following Inverter | Droop Control | Master-Slave |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------|--------------|
| Islanded operation? | Yes (acts as voltage source) | No (needs external reference) | Yes | Yes |
| Communication required? | No | No | No | Yes (high bandwidth) |
| Power-sharing accuracy | Medium | N/A | Good (P-ω, Q-V) | Excellent |
| Response time | < 1 cycle | < 1 cycle | Sub-cycle | 100 ms–1 s |
| Best suited for | 100% inverter-based MG | Grid-tied PV | Multi-DER MG | Small MG with one large unit |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR) Reduction via DR

A residential feeder has the following 24-hour load (kW): peak = **80 kW** at 19:00, average = **40 kW**.

**Step 1 — Compute initial PAR:**

$$
PAR_{initial} = \frac{80}{40} = 2.0
$$

**Step 2 — After DR, peak reduces by 15%, average unchanged:**

$$
L_{peak}^{new} = 80 \times 0.85 = 68 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
PAR_{new} = \frac{68}{40} = 1.7
$$

**Step 3 — PAR reduction in percentage:**

$$
\Delta PAR = \frac{2.0 - 1.7}{2.0} \times 100\% = 15\%
$$

**Interpretation for the utility:** A 15% reduction in PAR translates to deferred transformer and line upgrades worth **several crores of rupees** in distribution planning — a strong selling point for the KTU viva examiner.
