```mermaid
    sumNode --> sink2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define the term **Power Quality**. List any four power quality issues.

> **Model Answer:**
> **Power Quality** refers to the degree to which the voltage, current, and frequency of a power system conform to the ideal sinusoidal waveform, with all three parameters held within prescribed limits (per IEEE 519 and IEEE 1159).
>
> Four common PQ issues:
> 1. **Voltage Sag** – A momentary drop (0.5 cycle to 1 min) in RMS voltage.
> 2. **Voltage Swell** – A momentary rise in RMS voltage.
> 3. **Harmonic Distortion** – Presence of integer-multiple frequency components in the waveform.
> 4. **Transients** – Sudden, short-duration overvoltages from switching or lightning.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**? Write its formula.

> **Model Answer:**
> **THD** is a quantitative measure of the harmonic content present in a distorted waveform, expressed as a percentage of the fundamental component.
>
> Formula:
> $$THD_v = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$$
>
> IEEE 519 sets the voltage THD limit at **5%** at the PCC for systems below 69 kV.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice (either/or). Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each. Solutions are framed to match the Board Examiner's expected key.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – July 2023]*

**(a)** With neat diagrams, classify the major power quality disturbances as per IEEE 1159. Discuss any **three transients** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A non-linear load draws a fundamental current of 100 A (rms) along with harmonics: 3rd = 40 A, 5th = 25 A, 7th = 15 A, 9th = 8 A. Calculate the **(i) RMS current**, **(ii) THD of current**, and **(iii) crest factor** if the peak fundamental is 141.4 A. **(7 Marks)**

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the various power quality issues arising from the integration of **renewable energy sources** into a smart grid. Suggest suitable mitigation techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for RMS value of a non-sinusoidal waveform using Fourier series. A voltage waveform has the following components: 230 V (fundamental), 12 V (3rd), 8 V (5th), 4 V (7th). Calculate the **THD** and comment on IEEE 519 compliance. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### Solution to Q5.2 — A(a): Classification of Power Quality Disturbances

The IEEE 1159 standard classifies electromagnetic phenomena in power systems into **seven broad categories** as illustrated in the diagram in Section 4.1. A detailed discussion of the three transient types follows.

**1. Impulsive Transients**
- **Definition:** A sudden, non-power-frequency change in the voltage (or current) waveform that is *unidirectional* in polarity.
- **Typical Causes:** Lightning strikes, ESD (electrostatic discharge), capacitor switching.
- **Spectral Content:** Fast rise time (microseconds), high di/dt, very high frequency content.
- **Effects:** Insulation breakdown, semiconductor device failure.

**2. Oscillatory Transients (Low Frequency, < 5 kHz)**
- **Definition:** A sudden, bidirectional, decaying oscillation at the fundamental natural frequency of the system.
- **Typical Causes:** Capacitor bank energization, transformer inrush, ferroresonance.
- **Spectral Content:** 0.3 to 5 kHz; peak magnitude up to 4 pu.
- **Effects:** Tripping of protective devices, overvoltages on transformers.

**3. Oscillatory Transients (Medium and High Frequency)**
- **Medium Frequency (5–500 kHz):** Caused by line/cable switching. 
- **High Frequency (0.5–5 MHz):** Caused by switching of sub-cycle devices like thyristors.
- **Effects:** Interference with electronic equipment, communication systems, and signal integrity.

#### Solution to Q5.2 — A(b): Numerical on THD and RMS Current

**Given:**
- $I_{1,rms} = 100$ A; $I_3 = 40$ A; $I_5 = 25$ A; $I_7 = 15$ A; $I_9 = 8$ A
- Peak fundamental current = 141.4 A

**Step 1 — Compute RMS current**

Using the quadrature sum derived in Section 3.1:
$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_3^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_9^2}$$
$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{100^2 + 40^2 + 25^2 + 15^2 + 8^2}$$
$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{10000 + 1600 + 625 + 225 + 64}$$
$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{12514} = 111.87\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute THD of current**
$$THD_i = \frac{\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_9^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$$
$$THD_i = \frac{\sqrt{1600 + 625 + 225 + 64}}{100} \times 100\%$$
$$THD_i = \frac{\sqrt{2514}}{100} \times 100\% = \frac{50.14}{100} \times 100\%$$
$$\boxed{THD_i = 50.14\%}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Crest Factor**

The peak value of the distorted current is approximately the peak of the fundamental (since harmonics are typically smaller in peak magnitude and non-aligned):
$$I_{peak} \approx 141.4\ \text{A}$$
$$CF = \frac{I_{peak}}{I_{rms}} = \frac{141.4}{111.87} = 1.264$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (3-Mark Trap):** Students often write CF = √2 = 1.414 *for the distorted waveform*. The crest factor must be computed using the **actual peak of the distorted wave**, not the ideal sine value. The correct CF here is 1.264. Also, do not forget to convert peak to RMS or vice versa consistently.

#### Solution to Q5.2 — B(a): Renewable Integration PQ Issues & Mitigation

**Power Quality Issues from Renewable Integration:**

| Renewable Source | PQ Issue | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Solar PV Inverters | Harmonic injection | PWM switching at 2–20 kHz |
| Wind Turbines | Voltage flicker | Variable output (wind gusts) |
| Both | DC injection | Transformer-less inverter leakage |
| Both | Frequency drift | Reduced system inertia |
| Both | Islanding | Loss of grid support |
| EV Charging Clusters | Severe THD | Concurrent non-linear charging |

**Mitigation Techniques:**
1. **Active Power Filters (APF):** Inject compensating harmonics to cancel distortion in real time.
2. **STATCOM / SVC:** Provide dynamic reactive support to mitigate flicker and voltage sags.
3. **DVR (Dynamic Voltage Restorer):** Series compensator for sag/swell mitigation.
4. **Multi-pulse Rectifier Designs** (12-pulse, 24-pulse): For EV chargers and HVDC links to cancel characteristic harmonics.
5. **IEEE 1547 Standard Compliance:** Sets interconnection requirements for DERs.
6. **Energy Storage Systems (BESS):** Smooth out PV/wind variability.

#### Solution to Q5.2 — B(b): Derivation + THD Numerical

**Derivation (as detailed in Section 3.1):**

Start with the Fourier series:
$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_h \sin(h\omega t + \theta_h)$$

Apply the definition of RMS:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt$$

Using the orthogonality of sinusoids, cross-terms integrate to zero, leaving:
$$V_{rms}^2 = V_0^2 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty}\frac{V_h^2}{2}$$

Since $V_{h,rms} = V_h/\sqrt{2}$:
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_0^2 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}$$

**Numerical — Given:** $V_1 = 230$ V, $V_3 = 12$ V, $V_5 = 8$ V, $V_7 = 4$ V (all rms).

**Step 1 — Compute harmonic content**
$$V_H = \sqrt{12^2 + 8^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{144 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt{224} = 14.97\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute THD**
$$THD_v = \frac{14.97}{230} \times 100\% = 6.51\%$$

**Step 3 — IEEE 519 compliance check**
Since $6.51\% > 5\%$, the waveform **does NOT comply** with IEEE 519 voltage distortion limits. ❌
**Remedial action:** Install harmonic filters (active or passive) to reduce individual harmonics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to forget the IEEE 519 comparison step. Even if the numerical answer is mathematically correct, failing to *interpret* it against the standard (5% limit) will cost 1–2 marks. Always end THD problems with an explicit compliance/non-compliance statement.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Power Quality Management Fundamentals**

- ✅ **Power Quality (PQ):** The conformance of voltage, current, and frequency to ideal sinusoidal specifications.
- ✅ **IEEE 519:** Harmonic distortion limits (Voltage THD ≤ 5%, individual harmonic ≤ 3%).
- ✅ **IEEE 1159:** Classification of electromagnetic phenomena (7 categories).
- ✅ **IEC 61000-4-15:** Flickermeter specification.
- ✅ **THD Formula:** $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum V_h^2}}{V_1}\times 100\%$
- ✅ **TDD Formula:** $\text{TDD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum I_h^2}}{I_L}\times 100\%$ (uses rated load current, not fundamental).
- ✅ **RMS Superposition:** RMS values of different harmonics add in **quadrature**, not algebraically.
- ✅ **Voltage Sag:** 0.1–0.9 pu, 0.5 cycle to 1 min.
- ✅ **Voltage Swell:** 1.1–1.8 pu, 0.5 cycle to 1 min.
- ✅ **Interruptions:** < 0.1 pu (momentary) or sustained (zero voltage, > 1 min).
- ✅ **Crest Factor:** > 1.41 for distorted waveforms; equals 1.41 for pure sine.
- ✅ **True PF:** Product of displacement PF and distortion PF.
- ✅ **Crest Factor Formula:** $CF = V_{peak}/V_{rms}$
- ✅ **Smart Grid PQ Challenges:** Inverter harmonics, EV charging transients, RE variability, flicker from wind.
- ✅ **Mitigation Devices:** APF, SVC, STATCOM, DVR, UPFC, BESS.
- ✅ **PCC (Point of Common Coupling):** The reference bus where IEEE 519 limits apply.
- ✅ **Pst & Plt:** Short-term and long-term flicker perceptibility indices (IEC 61000).
- ✅ **Form Factor:** $V_{rms}/V_{avg}$ = 1.11 for pure sine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Exam Wisdom:** For 14-mark questions, always combine **theory (definitions + classification) + diagram (block/schematic) + numerical (THD or RMS calculation)**. This three-pronged structure is what KTU Board Examiners reward with full marks. Never present only theory; never present only a numerical. A balanced answer = 14/14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Quality (PQ) and Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) in Smart Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Quality (PQ) and Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) in Smart Grid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Quality (PQ) — Formal KTU Definition

**Power Quality** refers to the concept of powering and grounding sensitive electronic equipment in a manner that is suitable for the operation of that equipment. More formally, as per the **IEEE Standard 1100 (IEEE Emerald Book)** and aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology:

> **Power Quality** is the set of boundaries of electrical quantities (voltage, current, frequency) that allow electrical/electronic equipment to function in its intended manner without significant loss of performance or life expectancy.

In the context of a **Smart Grid**, PQ assumes a more dynamic dimension because the integration of distributed energy resources (DERs), power-electronic interfaces, and bidirectional power flows introduces new, complex disturbances that did not exist in the conventional grid.

**Key PQ Parameters Quantified:**
- Magnitude of supply voltage
- Frequency stability
- Waveform purity (sinusoidality)
- Symmetry of the three-phase system
- Transient behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the ESE (End Semester Evaluation), students must clearly distinguish between **Power Quality (PQ)** and **Reliability**. Reliability deals with sustained interruptions (hours/days), whereas PQ deals with momentary disturbances (milliseconds to seconds) that do not interrupt supply but degrade it.

## 1.2 Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) — Formal Definition

**Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** is defined (per **IEC 61000** and the KTU 2024 Smart Grid syllabus) as the ability of electrical and electronic equipment, systems, and installations to:

1. **Function satisfactorily in their electromagnetic environment** (immunity / susceptibility).
2. **Not introduce intolerable electromagnetic disturbances to other equipment** in that environment (emission / conducted & radiated noise).

> [!NOTE]
> The term "compatibility" is the cornerstone of EMC. It is a *two-way street* — a device must neither be a victim of EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) nor a source of EMI beyond permissible limits.

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogies

### Power Quality Analogy: "The Water Tap"
Imagine your home water supply:
- **Voltage sag** = Suddenly the water pressure drops while the tap is still open (your washing machine slows down).
- **Voltage swell** = Pressure suddenly spikes (pipes groan, fittings may leak).
- **Harmonics** = Muddy, contaminated water instead of clean, pure water (the substance is still *there*, but its *quality* is ruined).
- **Transients** = A sudden water hammer shock when a valve slams shut.
- **Frequency deviation** = The water flow pulses irregularly instead of in steady rhythm.

### EMC Analogy: "The Noisy Neighbor"
Think of a residential apartment building:
- **Emission (Source side)** = A neighbor playing loud music late at night.
- **Immunity (Victim side)** = Your ability to study peacefully despite the music (good acoustic insulation).
- **EMC** = The building's design where the music lover can play music *and* the student can study — both activities coexist because of insulation, regulations, and mutual design respect.
- **Conducted EMI** = Noise traveling through the building's electrical wiring (shared pipes).
- **Radiated EMI** = Noise traveling through the air (acoustic waves between apartments).

## 1.4 Why PQ and EMC are Pivotal to the Smart Grid

| Driver | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Sensitive Loads** | Modern semiconductor-based loads (data centers, EV chargers, medical equipment) demand near-ideal waveforms. |
| **Power Electronics Proliferation** | Inverters, converters, and SMPS (Switched Mode Power Supplies) are major harmonic sources. |
| **DER Integration** | Solar PV inverters, wind turbine converters inject harmonics and DC bias into the grid. |
| **Bidirectional Flows** | Microgrids, vehicle-to-grid (V2G) reverse the conventional unidirectional PQ paradigm. |
| **Standardization** | Global trade demands adherence to **IEEE 519**, **IEEE 1159**, **IEC 61000**, and **EN 50160** standards. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Why is PQ important in smart grid?", always anchor your answer to *power-electronic interfaces, renewable integration, and sensitive end-use equipment* — these are the three KTU examiner-approved buzz concepts.

## 1.5 Key Standards (Bold per Protocol)

- **IEEE 519-2014** — *Recommended Practice and Requirements for Harmonic Control in Electric Power Systems.*
- **IEEE 1159-2019** — *Recommended Practice for Monitoring Electric Power Quality.*
- **IEEE 1453-2015** — *Recommended Practice for the Analysis of Fluctuating Installations on Power Systems.*
- **IEC 61000 Series** — International EMC standard family (parts 2-x for environment, 3-x for limits, 4-x for testing).
- **EN 50160** — European voltage-quality standard at the point of common coupling (PCC).
- **CBEMA Curve** — *Computer Business Equipment Manufacturers Association* tolerance curve for voltage events.
- **ITIC Curve** — *Information Technology Industry Council* successor to CBEMA, more relevant to modern electronics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Power Quality Disturbances

PQ disturbances are classified along **two axes**: (1) duration of the event, and (2) the magnitude deviation from nominal.

### A. Steady-State (Continuous) Variations

These disturbances persist as long as the system is in a given operating state.

**1. Voltage Magnitude Variations**
- **Undervoltage / Brownout** — RMS voltage drops to 0.8–0.9 pu for > 1 minute.
- **Overvoltage** — RMS voltage rises to 1.1–1.2 pu for > 1 minute.
- **Voltage Unbalance** — Unequal phase voltages in a three-phase system (typically > 2% in negative-sequence).

**2. Waveform Distortions (Harmonics)**
- **Harmonics** — Sinusoidal components at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency (50 Hz: 100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz, …).
- **Interharmonics** — Non-integer multiples (e.g., 87.5 Hz from an induction furnace).
- **Notching** — Periodic voltage disturbances caused by commutation in line-commutated converters.
- **Noise** — Unwanted broadband signals > 200 Hz superimposed on the fundamental.
- **DC Offset** — Presence of a DC component in the AC waveform (e.g., from half-wave rectification or geomagnetic disturbances).

**3. Voltage Fluctuations (Flicker)**
- Rapid, repetitive voltage changes causing visible light flicker (typically 0.5–25 Hz modulation frequency).

### B. Transient Disturbances (Short-Duration)

| Type | Duration | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Impulsive Transient** | < 1 ms (microseconds) | High peak (kV), short duration |
| **Oscillatory Transient** | 5 µs – 50 ms | Decaying sinusoid at HF |
| **Voltage Sag (Dip)** | 0.5 cycles – 1 minute | 0.1 – 0.9 pu |
| **Voltage Swell** | 0.5 cycles – 1 minute | 1.1 – 1.8 pu |
| **Momentary Interruption** | < 3 seconds | < 0.1 pu |
| **Sustained Interruption** | > 3 seconds | 0 pu |

## 2.2 Why Harmonics Matter — Physical Origin

The **Why** behind harmonics in a smart grid:
- **Non-linear loads** (rectifiers, inverters, variable frequency drives, LED drivers, EV chargers) draw non-sinusoidal currents.
- These non-sinusoidal currents, flowing through finite system impedances, create non-sinusoidal voltages at the PCC.
- Resulting effects: overheating of transformers and neutral conductors, nuisance tripping of breakers, capacitor bank failures, maloperation of protective relays, resonance, and communication interference.

## 2.3 The Fourier Framework

Any periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform $v(t)$ with fundamental period $T$ can be expressed as:

$$v(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi / T$ and $n$ is the harmonic order.

Equivalent compact form:

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$

The RMS value of the distorted waveform:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_0^{\,2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2}}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| \# | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) — Voltage** | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2}}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of harmonic RMS content to fundamental RMS, in percent. |
| 2 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) — Current** | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^{\,2}}}{I_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$ | Same as above for current. |
| 3 | **Total Demand Distortion (TDD)** | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^{\,2}}}{I_L} \times 100\%$ | $I_L$ = maximum demand load current (15-min or 30-min). Used in **IEEE 519**. |
| 4 | **Individual Harmonic Distortion (IHD)** | $IHD_n = \dfrac{I_{n,rms}}{I_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$ | Per-harmonic contribution. |
| 5 | **Crest Factor** | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | Pure sine: $CF = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. |
| 6 | **Form Factor** | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | Pure sine: $FF = \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$. |
| 7 | **Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)** | $VUF = \dfrac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100\%$ | Negative-to-positive sequence ratio. $V_1, V_2$: positive/negative sequence components. |
| 8 | **Displacement Power Factor** | $DPF = \cos(\phi_1)$ | Cosine of the angle between fundamental voltage and current. |
| 9 | **True Power Factor** | $PF = \dfrac{P_{total}}{S_{total}} = \dfrac{\cos(\phi_1)}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$ | Includes distortion. |
| 10 | **Flicker Severity — Short-term ($P_{st}$)** | Statistical (95th percentile over 10 min) | Per **IEC 61000-4-15**. |
| 11 | **Flicker Severity — Long-term ($P_{lt}$)** | $P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{1}{12}\sum_{i=1}^{12} P_{st,i}^{\,3}}$ | 2-hour aggregate. |
| 12 | **CBEMA / ITIC Limit** | Time vs. Voltage tolerance band | Equipment ride-through envelope. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Notation:** In the table above, vertical bars are intentionally avoided to keep the markdown parser stable. In your KTU answer scripts, you may freely use $\vert V_2 \vert$ on paper.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Transformer derating**: A transformer supplying a load with $THD_I = 30\%$ may need to be derated by ~15–20% to prevent overheating.
- **Neutral conductor sizing**: In a 3-phase system with predominantly single-phase non-linear loads (computers, LEDs), triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) add in the neutral — neutral current can exceed phase current.
- **Capacitor bank protection**: Resonance between system inductance and power-factor-correction capacitors can amplify harmonic voltages, leading to capacitor failure.
- **EV charging stations**: A single 50 kW fast charger can inject 5–8% $THD_I$ without filtering; aggregation of hundreds can stress distribution feeders.

## 2.6 EMC Conceptual Architecture

EMC is enforced through **three pillars**:

1. **Source Control (Emission Limits)** — Restrict the EMI generated by a device. Standards: **CISPR 11, CISPR 22, FCC Part 15**.
2. **Coupling Path Suppression** — Shielding, filtering, grounding, cable segregation.
3. **Receiver Hardening (Immunity)** — Equip the victim device to tolerate a certain EMI level. Standards: **IEC 61000-4-2 (ESD), IEC 61000-4-3 (radiated RF), IEC 61000-4-4 (EFT), IEC 61000-4-5 (surge)**.

**Two Coupling Modes:**

- **Conducted EMI** — Travels along conductors, power lines, signal cables. Frequency range typically **150 kHz – 30 MHz**.
- **Radiated EMI** — Propagates as electromagnetic waves through space. Frequency range typically **30 MHz – 1 GHz** (and above for microwave devices).

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart Grid Twist:** The smart grid's dense deployment of **PLC (Power Line Communication)**, **wireless mesh** (Zigbee, Wi-SUN, LoRa), and **IoT sensors** makes EMC a *first-class design constraint*, not an afterthought. A poorly designed smart meter that radiates strongly can disrupt its own communication and that of nearby devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) from First Principles

**Starting Premise:**
A non-sinusoidal periodic voltage $v(t)$ can be expressed using Fourier series. The fundamental component ($n=1$) is the *useful* component, while all higher-order harmonics ($n \ge 2$) are *distortion* content.

**Step 1 — Write the Fourier expansion**

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$

The DC component $V_0$ is generally zero in a balanced power system.

**Step 2 — Express the RMS value of the distorted waveform**

By Parseval's theorem, the mean square of a periodic signal equals the sum of the mean squares of its orthogonal components:

$$V_{rms}^{\,2} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt = V_0^{\,2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2}$$

> *Logic:* Since the cosine/sine functions at different frequencies are orthogonal over one period, the cross-terms vanish. This is Parseval's theorem for power signals.

**Step 3 — Split the sum into fundamental and harmonic parts**

$$V_{rms}^{\,2} = V_{1,rms}^{\,2} + \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2}$$

> *Logic:* The $n=1$ term represents the fundamental; $n \ge 2$ represents the harmonic distortion. The DC term $V_0$ is dropped for a balanced grid.

**Step 4 — Define THD as the ratio of harmonic RMS to fundamental RMS**

$$THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2}}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$$

> *Logic:* Dividing the harmonic RMS by the fundamental normalizes the metric so that a pure sinewave (no harmonics) gives $THD_V = 0\%$.

**Step 5 — Derive an alternative form using total RMS**

From Step 3:

$$V_{rms}^{\,2} = V_{1,rms}^{\,2} \left[ 1 + THD_V^{\,2} \right]$$

Therefore:

$$V_{rms} = V_{1,rms} \sqrt{1 + THD_V^{\,2}}$$

> *Logic:* This compact relationship is useful when a measured $V_{rms}$ and fundamental $V_{1,rms}$ are known but the harmonic spectrum is not.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A 230 V, 50 Hz supply is measured and the following harmonic spectrum is obtained (in RMS volts):

- Fundamental ($n=1$): $V_1 = 230$ V
- 3rd harmonic: $V_3 = 12$ V
- 5th harmonic: $V_5 = 8$ V
- 7th harmonic: $V_7 = 4$ V
- 9th harmonic: $V_9 = 2$ V

Higher harmonics are negligible.

**Find:** (i) $THD_V$, (ii) Total RMS voltage, (iii) Crest factor, (iv) Comment on compliance with **IEEE 519** (which requires $THD_V \le 8\%$ for systems below 69 kV).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (i) — Compute $THD_V$**

First, sum the squared harmonic voltages:

$$\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2} = 12^2 + 8^2 + 4^2 + 2^2 = 144 + 64 + 16 + 4 = 228 \, V^2$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{228} = 15.0997 \, V$$

Divide by the fundamental RMS:

$$THD_V = \dfrac{15.0997}{230} \times 100\% = 6.565\%$$

**Step (ii) — Compute the total RMS voltage**

Using the derived relationship:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{230^2 + 228} = \sqrt{52900 + 228} = \sqrt{53128} = 230.495 \, V$$

> *Logic:* Notice the total RMS is only marginally higher than the fundamental because $THD$ is small.

**Step (iii) — Compute the Crest Factor**

The peak of the fundamental is $V_{1,peak} = V_1 \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.4142 = 325.27 \, V$.

Assuming the harmonics are in worst-phase alignment, the approximate peak of the composite waveform is:

$$V_{peak} \approx V_{1,peak} + \sum V_{n,peak} = 325.27 + (12+8+4+2)\sqrt{2} = 325.27 + 36.77 = 362.04 \, V$$

Then:

$$CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \dfrac{362.04}{230.495} = 1.570$$

> *Logic:* A pure sine has $CF = 1.414$. The excess (1.570) is a direct signature of harmonic distortion. Insulators and surge-sensitive devices suffer because of this higher peak.

**Step (iv) — IEEE 519 Compliance Check**

$$THD_V = 6.565\% < 8\% \quad \therefore \text{COMPLIANT}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When solving numerical THD problems, always (a) show the squaring of each harmonic separately, (b) sum them, (c) take the square root *before* dividing. Many students erroneously divide each harmonic by $V_1$ and then add — this is **incorrect** because THD is an *RMS* quantity, not an arithmetic sum.

## 3.3 Derivation: True Power Factor Including Distortion

**Step 1 — Apparent power with distortion**

For a non-sinusoidal system, the apparent power is:

$$S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$$

**Step 2 — Expand using THD**

$$S = V_{1,rms} \sqrt{1 + THD_V^{\,2}} \cdot I_{1,rms} \sqrt{1 + THD_I^{\,2}}$$

**Step 3 — Active (real) power**

Active power is delivered only by components of the same frequency, so cross-frequency products integrate to zero. Only in-phase fundamental and harmonic components contribute:

$$P = V_{1,rms} I_{1,rms} \cos(\phi_1) + \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms} I_{n,rms} \cos(\phi_n)$$

> *Logic:* In typical systems, harmonic voltages are small; thus, the second term is small, and the dominant active power comes from the fundamental in-phase component $V_1 I_1 \cos(\phi_1)$.

**Step 4 — True Power Factor**

$$PF = \dfrac{P}{S} \approx \dfrac{V_{1,rms} I_{1,rms} \cos(\phi_1)}{V_{1,rms} I_{1,rms} \sqrt{1+THD_V^{\,2}}\sqrt{1+THD_I^{\,2}}}$$

If $THD_V \approx 0$ (typical), then:

$$PF \approx \dfrac{\cos(\phi_1)}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^{\,2}}} = \dfrac{DPF}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^{\,2}}}$$

> *Logic:* This is the key KTU result — a non-linear load with unity displacement power factor ($\cos \phi_1 = 1$) still has $PF < 1$ because of harmonic distortion. Power-factor-correction capacitors cannot fix this; **active harmonic filters** are required.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PQ Disturbance → Standard → Smart-Grid Origin

| PQ Disturbance | Standard Reference | Typical Smart Grid Source | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Voltage Sag | IEEE 1159, IEEE 1346 | PV inverter trip, motor starting, grid fault | DVR (Dynamic Voltage Restorer), UPS |
| Voltage Swell | IEEE 1159 | Sudden load drop, capacitor switching | Surge arrester, controlled switching |
| Harmonics | IEEE 519, IEC 61000-2-2 | EV chargers, PV inverters, data center SMPS | Active/passive filters, multi-pulse rectifiers |
| Flicker | IEEE 1453, IEC 61000-4-15 | Wind turbine tower shadow, arc furnace | STATCOM, SVC |
| Transients | IEEE C62.41, IEC 61000-4-5 | Lightning, capacitor switching, ESD events | Surge protective devices (SPDs), shielding |
| Unbalance | IEEE 1159, EN 50160 | Single-phase DER connection asymmetry | Phase re-balancing, STATCOM |
| DC Offset | IEEE 1159 | Geomagnetic storms, half-wave rectification | Neutral blocking, isolation transformer |
| Interruption | IEEE 1159, IEC 61000-2-4 | Equipment failure, breaker operation | Microgrid islanding, energy storage |

## 3.5 EMC Compatibility Margin Calculation

For two systems to be electromagnetically compatible, the immunity level of the victim must exceed the emission level of the source at the coupling point.

Define:

$$M_{EMC} = I_{victim} - E_{source} \quad \text{(both in dBµV/m or dBµA)}$$

For compatibility:

$$M_{EMC} \ge S_{margin}$$

where $S_{margin}$ is a safety margin (typically **6 dB** for industrial, **10 dB** for medical, **20 dB** for safety-critical).

> *Logic:* A 6 dB margin corresponds to a factor of 2 in field strength. EMC standards (e.g., **EN 55011**) use this margin philosophy to ensure that even worst-case alignment does not cause interference.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning — Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **EMC** (compatibility between two pieces of equipment) with **EMI** (the *disturbance itself*). EMI is the *phenomenon*; EMC is the *engineering discipline* that ensures EMI does not cause functional problems. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Power Quality. List any four power quality disturbances.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Power Quality, as defined by **IEEE Standard 1100**, refers to the degree to which the voltage, current, and frequency of the power delivered to electrical equipment conform to the specifications that allow that equipment to operate in its intended manner without loss of performance or life. In a smart grid, PQ encompasses the dynamic deviations arising from the integration of distributed energy resources and power-electronic interfaces.

**Four PQ Disturbances (1.5 marks):**

1. **Voltage Sag (Dip)** — RMS voltage drops to 0.1–0.9 pu for 0.5 cycles to 1 minute.
2. **Voltage Swell** — RMS voltage rises to 1.1–1.8 pu for 0.5 cycles to 1 minute.
3. **Harmonics** — Sinusoidal components at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency, caused by non-linear loads.
4. **Voltage Flicker** — Repetitive voltage fluctuations causing visible light intensity variation, measured via $P_{st}$ and $P_{lt}$ indices.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, use the **"Definition + 4 bullets"** formula. Examiners reward structured, numbered lists.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)? Distinguish between conducted and radiated EMI.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** is the ability of an electrical or electronic system to function satisfactorily in its electromagnetic environment *without* introducing intolerable electromagnetic disturbances to anything in that environment. EMC is governed by the **IEC 61000** series of standards and has two facets: emission control and immunity.

**Distinction (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Conducted EMI | Radiated EMI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Path** | Power lines, signal cables, ground conductors | Free space (electromagnetic waves) |
| **Frequency Range** | 150 kHz – 30 MHz | 30 MHz – 1 GHz (and above) |
| **Measurement** | LISN (Line Impedance Stabilization Network) | Antenna + spectrum analyzer in anechoic chamber |
| **Mitigation** | Filters, chokes, common-mode beads | Shielding, PCB layout, enclosure design |
| **Standard** | CISPR 11 / 22 conducted limits | CISPR 11 / 22 radiated limits |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 4 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for PEEET861, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 + 7** across sub-parts. Two parallel choices (A and B) are provided, of which the student answers **one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the different types of power quality disturbances in a smart grid. Discuss the origin of harmonics from non-linear loads and renewable energy interfaces (PV inverters and wind turbine converters).

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 415 V, 50 Hz three-phase system supplies a non-linear load. The harmonic current spectrum (RMS, per phase) is as follows:
- Fundamental: $I_1 = 100$ A
- 5th harmonic: $I_5 = 22$ A
- 7th harmonic: $I_7 = 14$ A
- 11th harmonic: $I_{11} = 8$ A
- 13th harmonic: $I_{13} = 5$ A

Calculate: (i) Total Harmonic Distortion of current ($THD_I$), (ii) True RMS current, (iii) The peak current assuming worst-case harmonic alignment, (iv) Comment on IEEE 519 compliance (for $I_{SC}/I_L < 20$, individual harmonics ≤ 7% and THD ≤ 8%).

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Types of PQ Disturbances (4 marks):**

PQ disturbances are classified into **steady-state variations** and **transient disturbances**.

**Steady-state:**
- **Voltage magnitude variations** — undervoltage, overvoltage, sustained sag/swell.
- **Waveform distortion** — harmonics, interharmonics, DC offset, notching, noise.
- **Voltage fluctuations** — flicker (Pst, Plt).
- **Unbalance** — negative-sequence component.

**Transient:**
- **Impulsive transients** (microseconds, high peak) — lightning, ESD.
- **Oscillatory transients** (5 µs – 50 ms) — capacitor switching, ferroresonance.
- **Short-duration events** — sag, swell, momentary interruption.

**Origin of Harmonics — Non-linear Loads and Renewables (3 marks):**

**Conventional Non-linear Loads:**
- **Power electronic converters** (rectifiers, inverters, choppers, SMPS) draw non-sinusoidal currents because their switching action chops the supply waveform.
- A 6-pulse rectifier produces characteristic harmonics of order $6k \pm 1$ (5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, …).
- **Arcing devices** (arc furnaces, fluorescent lamps) generate a continuous spectrum of harmonics and interharmonics.
- **Saturated magnetic cores** (transformers in over-excitation) generate primarily 3rd harmonic.

**Renewable Energy Interfaces:**
- **PV Inverters**: PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) switching at 2–20 kHz produces high-frequency harmonics around the switching frequency and its multiples. Modern inverters use LCL filters and high PWM frequency to push these above the standard measurement range.
- **Wind Turbine Converters**: Back-to-back AC-DC-AC conversion produces both low-order characteristic harmonics (5th, 7th) and high-frequency switching noise.
- **Grid-Tie Issues**: Under partial load or with weak grids, renewable inverters can interact with grid resonances, amplifying specific harmonic orders.

##### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $I_1 = 100$ A, $I_5 = 22$ A, $I_7 = 14$ A, $I_{11} = 8$ A, $I_{13} = 5$ A

**Step (i) — Compute $THD_I$** [2 marks]

Square each harmonic:

$$I_5^2 = 484, \quad I_7^2 = 196, \quad I_{11}^2 = 64, \quad I_{13}^2 = 25$$

Sum:

$$\sum I_n^2 = 484 + 196 + 64 + 25 = 769 \, A^2$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{769} = 27.73 \, A$$

Divide by $I_1$:

$$THD_I = \dfrac{27.73}{100} \times 100\% = 27.73\%$$

**Step (ii) — Compute True RMS Current** [1.5 marks]

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + \sum I_n^2} = \sqrt{100^2 + 769} = \sqrt{10769} = 103.77 \, A$$

**Step (iii) — Compute Peak Current (worst-case alignment)** [1.5 marks]

Each peak contribution = RMS $\times \sqrt{2}$:

$$I_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \times (I_1 + I_5 + I_7 + I_{11} + I_{13}) = 1.4142 \times 149 = 210.72 \, A$$

**Step (iv) — IEEE 519 Compliance Check** [2 marks]

| Harmonic | IHD | Limit (for $I_{SC}/I_L < 20$) | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 5th | 22% | 7% | **NON-COMPLIANT** |
| 7th | 14% | 7% | **NON-COMPLIANT** |
| 11th | 8% | 3.5% | **NON-COMPLIANT** |
| 13th | 5% | 3.5% | **NON-COMPLIANT** |
| **THD_I** | **27.73%** | **8%** | **NON-COMPLIANT** |

**Conclusion:** The load severely violates **IEEE 519-2014** requirements. A harmonic mitigation scheme must be deployed: either a passive harmonic filter tuned to dominant orders, or an active power filter (APF) injecting anti-phase harmonic currents. The neutral conductor (in 3-phase systems) should also be oversized because of triplen harmonic accumulation if single-phase non-linear loads are present.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC). Describe in detail the **three pillars of EMC compliance** with relevant **IEC 61000** test standards. Explain how EMC differs from and overlaps with Power Quality in the smart grid context.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 230 V single-phase smart-meter has the following harmonic voltage spectrum (RMS): $V_1 = 230$ V, $V_3 = 14$ V, $V_5 = 9$ V, $V_7 = 5$ V. Determine: (i) $THD_V$, (ii) Total RMS voltage, (iii) Whether the meter is compliant with **IEEE 519** ($THD_V \le 8\%$ at PCC for low-voltage systems), (iv) If non-compliant, suggest two practical mitigation techniques suitable for a residential smart-meter application.

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Definition of EMC (2 marks):**
Per **IEC 61000-1-1**, EMC is the ability of a device, equipment, or system to function satisfactorily in its electromagnetic environment *without* introducing intolerable electromagnetic disturbances to other devices. EMC has two complementary aspects:
- **Emission limitation** — the device must not pollute the EM environment.
- **Immunity assurance** — the device must tolerate a defined level of EM pollution.

**Three Pillars of EMC Compliance (4 marks):**

| Pillar | Engineering Action | Test Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pillar 1: Source Control** | Restrict generated emissions via PCB layout, component selection, slow edges, low-power signaling | **CISPR 11 / CISPR 22 / FCC Part 15** — emission limits |
| **Pillar 2: Coupling Suppression** | Shielding, filtering, proper grounding, cable segregation, ferrites | **IEC 61000-4-6** (conducted immunity), **IEC 61000-4-3** (radiated immunity) |
| **Pillar 3: Receiver Hardening** | Immunity design: TVS diodes, common-mode chokes, optocouplers, watchdog timers | **IEC 61000-4-2** (ESD ±8 kV air, ±4 kV contact), **IEC 61000-4-4** (EFT burst), **IEC 61000-4-5** (surge) |

**PQ vs EMC in Smart Grid (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Power Quality | EMC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Concern** | Conducted low-frequency phenomena (< 9 kHz) | Conducted + radiated, broad frequency spectrum |
| **Typical Domain** | Voltage and current quality at 50/60 Hz | All electromagnetic phenomena |
| **Main Standards** | IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, EN 50160 | IEC 61000 series, CISPR |
| **Smart Grid Overlap** | DER harmonics, EV charging disturbances | PLC noise, smart meter radiation, wireless coexistence |

The **overlap** is most visible in (a) PLC (Power Line Communication) systems where high-frequency data signals co-exist with 50 Hz power and (b) high-frequency switching noise from power-electronic converters that violates both PQ and EMC thresholds.

##### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $V_1 = 230$ V, $V_3 = 14$ V, $V_5 = 9$ V, $V_7 = 5$ V

**Step (i) — Compute $THD_V$** [1.5 marks]

Sum of squares:

$$V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2 = 196 + 81 + 25 = 302 \, V^2$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{302} = 17.378 \, V$$

Divide by fundamental:

$$THD_V = \dfrac{17.378}{230} \times 100\% = 7.56\%$$

**Step (ii) — Compute Total RMS Voltage** [1 mark]

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 302} = \sqrt{52900 + 302} = \sqrt{53202} = 230.66 \, V$$

**Step (iii) — IEEE 519 Compliance Check** [1 mark]

$$THD_V = 7.56\% < 8\% \quad \therefore \text{COMPLIANT (marginally)}$$

**Step (iv) — Mitigation if Margin is Unacceptable** [3.5 marks]

Two practical mitigation techniques for a residential smart meter:

1. **Passive L-C Low-Pass Filter at the Meter Input**: Install a small LC filter (inductor in series, capacitor to neutral) tuned to pass 50 Hz and attenuate harmonics above ~1 kHz. Components must be sized for the meter's continuous current rating and comply with safety isolation requirements (e.g., **IEC 62053-22** for meter accuracy class).

2. **Active Power Filter (APF) Inside the Smart Meter**: A modern approach — integrate a small active harmonic compensator that senses the harmonic current drawn by the meter's own SMPS and injects a counter-phase current to cancel it. This is implemented via a high-frequency MOSFET bridge and a DSP controller inside the meter enclosure. It can reduce $THD_V$ at the PCC by 3–5% and is becoming standard in premium smart meters.

Auxiliary techniques:
- Use of **12-pulse rectifiers** instead of 6-pulse in the meter's power supply.
- **Spread-spectrum PWM** in any internal DC-DC converter to dilute switching harmonics.
- **Zigbee/Wi-SUN module** with proper PCB ground partitioning to prevent radiated EMI from coupling into the meter's measurement circuit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **In Part (b) numericals**, students often *add* the harmonic percentages instead of *squaring, summing, and taking the square root*. This is the single most common error in PQ numericals.
> 2. **In Part (a) theory**, students write generic "PQ is important" answers without naming **specific standards (IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, IEC 61000)** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing standard citations.
> 3. **Confusing THD with TDD** — THD uses $I_1$ as the denominator; TDD uses $I_L$ (maximum demand load current). TDD is the IEEE 519 metric; THD is the IEEE 1159 / general metric.
> 4. **In EMC questions**, students write "EMC = EMI" — wrong. EMC is the *engineering discipline of compatibility*; EMI is the *interference phenomenon*. Examiners are strict about this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Power Quality (PQ)** = suitability of voltage, current, and frequency for equipment operation. Defined by **IEEE 1100**.
- ✅ **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** = two-way: emission control + immunity. Defined by **IEC 61000**.
- ✅ **PQ Disturbances** are split into **steady-state** (harmonics, unbalance, flicker, DC offset) and **transient** (sag, swell, impulsive/oscillatory transients, interruption).
- ✅ **THD** = $\dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n \ge 2} X_{n,rms}^2}}{X_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$. Always square, sum, root, then divide.
- ✅ **TDD** = same numerator as THD, but denominator is $I_L$ (maximum demand current). Used in **IEEE 519**.
- ✅ **Crest Factor** = $V_{peak}/V_{rms}$. Pure sine = 1.414. Distorted waveforms have higher CF.
- ✅ **VUF** = $\vert V_2 \vert / \vert V_1 \vert \times 100\%$. Negative-sequence to positive-sequence ratio.
- ✅ **True PF** = $\cos \phi_1 / \sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}$ (when $THD_V \approx 0$).
- ✅ **Key Standards to Memorize**: **IEEE 519-2014** (harmonics), **IEEE 1159-2019** (monitoring), **IEEE 1453-2015** (flicker), **IEC 61000 series** (EMC), **EN 50160** (EU voltage quality), **CBEMA / ITIC** (ride-through curves).
- ✅ **EMC's Three Pillars**: Source Control, Coupling Suppression, Receiver Hardening.
- ✅ **Conducted EMI** = 150 kHz – 30 MHz via power/signal lines. **Radiated EMI** = 30 MHz – 1 GHz via free space.
- ✅ **Smart Grid Implications**: DER inverters, EV chargers, data centers, and PLC systems all create PQ-EMC challenges. Mitigation includes APFs, DVRs, STATCOMs, shielding, and smart recloser coordination.
- ✅ **For numericals**: Show the squaring step explicitly. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ for absolute value to avoid table-syntax issues.
- ✅ **Differentiate**: PQ (low-frequency, conducted, voltage quality) vs EMC (broad-spectrum, conducted + radiated, functional compatibility).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Quality Conditioners for Smart Grid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — POWER QUALITY CONDITIONERS FOR SMART GRID

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Power Quality Conditioners (PQCs)* are power-electronics-based shunt, series, or hybrid compensation devices deployed at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC) in a smart distribution network to **mitigate voltage and current disturbances** (sags, swells, harmonics, flicker, unbalance) in real time, thereby conforming the supply to the limits prescribed by **IEEE 519-2014** and **IEEE 1159-2019**.

### 1.1 Why Power Quality (PQ) Matters in a Smart Grid

A smart grid integrates **distributed energy resources (DERs)**, **power-electronic interfaces**, **sensitive digital loads** (data centers, hospitals, semiconductor fabs), and **bidirectional power flows**. This convergence amplifies PQ issues because:

- **Non-linear loads** (rectifiers, SMPS, VFDs, LED drivers) inject harmonic currents $i_h$.
- **Inverter-interfaced DERs** can cause voltage fluctuations at the PCC.
- **Faults and switching transients** produce sags/swells.
- **Loose neutral and unbalanced single-phase loading** create negative-sequence components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 519-2014** is the *de facto* global benchmark limiting Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) at the PCC. For **V ≤ 1 kV**, the allowable current THD is **8 %** (individual harmonic limit **5 %**), while voltage THD at the PCC must be ≤ **5 %**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Noise-Cancelling Headphone" of the Grid

Imagine listening to music in a noisy café. The ambient hum is your **grid's harmonic pollution**, and the music is your **clean 50 Hz waveform**.

- **Active Power Filters (APFs)** are like *active noise-cancelling headphones*. They do not "block" the noise with bulky passive components; instead, they *listen* to the disturbance and *inject* an **equal-and-opposite waveform** to cancel it.
- **Series compensators (DVRs)** are like a *voltage stabilizer for your refrigerator* — they sit in series with the line and "boost" the voltage during sags.
- **Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC)** is the *premium audio system* that handles **both voltage and current** issues simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "S-S-S-D":**
> **S**eries → **S**wells/Sags (Voltage issues)
> **S**hunt → **S**upply current harmonics (Current issues)
> **D**ual → UPQC (handles **D**ual sides)

### 1.3 Classification of Custom Power Devices

Custom Power Devices (CPDs) are solid-state, controller-driven power-electronic systems used in **distribution systems (≤ 38 kV)**. They fall into two main families:

| Family | Function | Network Connection | Key Devices |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Network Reconfiguration Type** | Transfer load to alternate feeder | Switching at PCC | **SSTS** (Solid-State Transfer Switch), **SFC** (Static Frequency Changer) |
| **Compensating Type** | Inject compensating voltage/current | Shunt / Series / Hybrid | **DSTATCOM**, **DVR**, **UPQC**, **APF** |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Power Quality Disturbances — The "Anatomy" of a Defect

A non-ideal grid voltage or current can be decomposed using **Fourier Transform** into:

$$v(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1) + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h \sin(h\omega t + \phi_h)$$

where the first term is the **fundamental (50/60 Hz) component** and the summation captures all **harmonics of order $h$**.

#### 2.1.1 Common PQ Disturbances (IEEE 1159 Categorization)

| Disturbance | Duration | Magnitude (pu) | Typical Source | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sag (Dip)** | 0.5 cyc – 1 min | 0.1 – 0.9 | Faults, motor starts | **DVR**, UPQC |
| **Swell** | 0.5 cyc – 1 min | 1.1 – 1.8 | Capacitor switching, load drop | **DSTATCOM** |
| **Interruption** | > 1 min | < 0.1 | Breaker operation, faults | **UPS**, SSTS |
| **Transient (Impulsive)** | < 50 ns – 1 ms | varies | Lightning, capacitor switching | **Surge arresters** |
| **Harmonics** | Steady state | THD > 5 % | Non-linear loads, DERs | **Shunt APF**, UPQC |
| **Flicker** | Continuous | ΔV/V ≈ 1 % | Arc furnaces, wind gusts | **STATCOM** (reactive control) |
| **Unbalance** | Steady state | VUF > 2 % | Single-phase loads | **Series APF** (negative-seq) |

### 2.2 Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF) — The Harmonic Injector

The **Shunt APF** is connected in **parallel** to the load at the PCC. Its operating principle:

1. **Sense** the load current $i_L(t)$.
2. **Extract** the harmonic + reactive component $i_{L,h}(t) = i_L(t) - i_{L,1}(t)$.
3. **Inject** an *equal and opposite* compensating current $i_c(t) = -i_{L,h}(t)$ through a **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)**.
4. The **source** then supplies only the clean fundamental: $i_s(t) = i_{L,1}(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The shunt APF does NOT supply the load's active power — it only **cancels harmonics and reactive power** at the PCC. The DC-link capacitor of the VSI stores just enough energy to act as a "buffer".

#### 2.2.1 Reference Current Generation — The $p$–$q$ Theory (Akagi, 1983)

The **Instantaneous Power Theory (or $p$–$q$ theory)** is the most widely-tested control method in KTU examinations. It transforms three-phase voltages and currents into the **$\alpha\beta$ stationary frame** using the **Clarke Transform**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha \\ v_\beta \\ v_0 \end{bmatrix} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix}$$

The instantaneous **real** ($p$) and **imaginary** ($q$) powers are:

$$p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta \qquad q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta$$

Or in compact matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & v_\beta \\ -v_\beta & v_\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

Each power component is decomposed into **DC** (fundamental) and **AC** (harmonic/oscillating) parts:

$$p = \bar{p} + \tilde{p} \qquad q = \bar{q} + \tilde{q}$$

The **compensating reference currents** (in $\alpha\beta$ frame) are computed using only the **AC + imaginary components** for full harmonic + reactive compensation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_{c,\alpha}^* \\ i_{c,\beta}^* \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & -v_\beta \\ v_\beta & v_\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -\tilde{p} + p_{\text{loss}} \\ -q \end{bmatrix}$$

where $p_{\text{loss}}$ compensates for inverter switching/conduction losses (keeps the **DC-link voltage $V_{dc}$** regulated).

#### 2.2.2 Synchronous Reference Frame (SRF / $d$-$q$) Theory — Alternative Method

Three-phase signals are converted to a **rotating $d$-$q$ frame** locked to the fundamental frequency using a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)**. In this frame, the **fundamental becomes DC** and harmonics become **AC ripples**, easily separated by a **High-Pass or Low-Pass Filter**.

$$T_{abc \to dq} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \cos(\theta - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}) & \cos(\theta + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}) \\ -\sin\theta & -\sin(\theta - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}) & -\sin(\theta + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}) \\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{bmatrix}$$

The reference currents in $d$-$q$ form are:

$$i_d^* = \bar{i_d} \quad (\text{DC part = fundamental positive sequence})$$
$$i_q^* = 0 \quad (\text{zero reactive power for unity PF})$$

### 2.3 Series Active Power Filter / DVR — The Voltage Defender

The **Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR)** is a **series compensator** that injects a *compensating voltage $v_c(t)$* in series with the supply to maintain a constant load-side voltage $v_L(t) = v_s(t) + v_c(t)$ during sags/swells.

$$v_c(t) = v_L^*(t) - v_s(t)$$

where $v_L^*$ is the **desired reference voltage** (rated magnitude, balanced).

#### 2.3.1 DVR Energy Storage Requirement

For a sag of depth $V_{\text{sag}}$ (pu) lasting duration $T_{\text{sag}}$ seconds, with load power $S_L$ (VA):

$$E_{\text{DVR}} = S_L \cdot \sqrt{ 1 - V_{\text{sag}}^2 } \cdot T_{\text{sag}} \quad \text{(for purely in-phase injection)}$$

$$E_{\text{DVR}} = S_L \cdot \sqrt{ 1 + V_{\text{sag}}^2 - 2 V_{\text{sag}} \cos(\Delta\phi) } \cdot T_{\text{sag}} \quad \text{(for phase-advance method)}$$

where $\Delta\phi$ is the phase-jump between pre-sag and during-sag voltage.

### 2.4 Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC) — The Swiss Army Knife

The **UPQC** is the **most powerful custom power device**. It integrates:

- A **shunt APF** (parallel branch) → compensates **current harmonics + reactive power + unbalance**.
- A **series APF / DVR** (series branch) → compensates **voltage harmonics + sags + swells + unbalance**.
- A **common DC-link capacitor** couples both inverters, enabling **bidirectional active power exchange** between the series and shunt branches.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw or explain the UPQC, always mention the **common DC-link** — that is the feature that distinguishes UPQC from independently-operated DVR + DSTATCOM.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{THD}_i = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{N} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Current Total Harmonic Distortion | $\%$; IEEE 519 limit: $\leq 8\%$ for V $\leq$ 1 kV |
| 2 | $\text{THD}_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{N} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Voltage THD | $\%$; IEEE 519 limit: $\leq 5\%$ |
| 3 | $p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta$ | Instantaneous real power (Akagi) | W (instantaneous) |
| 4 | $q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta$ | Instantaneous imaginary power | var (instantaneous) |
| 5 | $i_{c,\alpha}^* = \dfrac{v_\alpha (-\tilde{p}+p_{\text{loss}}) - v_\beta (-q)}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2}$ | Reference compensating current, $\alpha$-axis | A |
| 6 | $i_{c,\beta}^* = \dfrac{v_\beta (-\tilde{p}+p_{\text{loss}}) + v_\alpha (-q)}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2}$ | Reference compensating current, $\beta$-axis | A |
| 7 | $V_{\text{sag,pu}} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{remaining}}}{V_{\text{pre-sag}}}$ | Sag depth in per-unit | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{\text{DVR}} = S_L \sqrt{1 - V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2} \cdot T_{\text{sag}}$ | DVR DC energy storage | Joules |
| 9 | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{L,\text{rms}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a}$ | DC-link voltage for 3-$\phi$ VSI (SPWM) | V; $m_a$ = modulation index |
| 10 | $\text{VUF} = \dfrac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100\%$ | Voltage Unbalance Factor | $\%$; limit: $\leq 2\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners Love** numericals on **DVR energy requirement** (formula 8) and **THD calculation** (formulae 1 & 2). Memorize the IEEE 519 thresholds verbatim.

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Application | Device Used | Industrial Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Data centers, hospitals | **DVR + UPS** | Prevents data loss, equipment damage |
| Semiconductor fabs | **UPQC** | Sub-cycle voltage regulation for nanometer process control |
| Arc-furnace plants | **DSTATCOM** | Flicker mitigation, IEEE 1453 compliance |
| EV fast-charging stations | **Shunt APF** | Limits THD injected by rectifier front-ends |
| Wind farm PCC | **UPQC / SSTS** | Grid code compliance, ride-through |
| Industrial PLCs, CNC machines | **DVR** | Avoids nuisance tripping |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reference Compensating Current in the $p$–$q$ Frame

**Given:** Three-phase non-linear load draws current $i_a, i_b, i_c$ from a balanced supply $v_a, v_b, v_c$.

**Goal:** Derive the expression for the compensating reference current that ensures the source draws only fundamental, positive-sequence current at unity power factor.

#### Step 1 — Apply the Clarke (abc → αβ0) Transform

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha \\ v_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix}, \quad \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a **dimensionless power-invariant** transformation. The factor $\sqrt{2/3}$ ensures **power equivalence** between the abc and αβ0 frames.

#### Step 2 — Compute Instantaneous Powers

$$p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta$$
$$q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta$$

Writing it in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & v_\beta \\ -v_\beta & v_\alpha \end{bmatrix}}_{M_p} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3 — Decompose $p$ and $q$ into DC and AC Components

$$p = \bar{p} + \tilde{p}, \qquad q = \bar{q} + \tilde{q}$$

- $\bar{p}$ = average real power (delivered to the load fundamental)
- $\tilde{p}$ = oscillating real power (caused by **harmonics + unbalance**)
- $\bar{q}$ = average reactive power (fundamental reactive)
- $\tilde{q}$ = oscillating reactive power (harmonics)

#### Step 4 — Define the Compensation Strategy

For **full compensation (harmonics + reactive power)** while keeping the DC-link voltage regulated:

$$\begin{bmatrix} p^* \\ q^* \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tilde{p} - p_{\text{loss}} \\ q \end{bmatrix}$$

(We select the *negative* of the harmonic components so the source supplies only the clean fundamental; $p_{\text{loss}}$ is a small positive feedback term to replenish inverter losses.)

#### Step 5 — Invert the Power Matrix to Get Compensating Currents

The inverse of $M_p$ is (since $M_p$ is a rotation-like matrix):

$$M_p^{-1} = \frac{1}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & -v_\beta \\ v_\beta & v_\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore, the **reference compensating currents** in the $\alpha\beta$ frame are:

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_{c,\alpha}^* \\ i_{c,\beta}^* \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & -v_\beta \\ v_\beta & v_\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -\tilde{p} + p_{\text{loss}} \\ -q \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 6 — Apply the Inverse Clarke Transform to Get abc Reference Currents

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_{c,a}^* \\ i_{c,b}^* \\ i_{c,c}^* \end{bmatrix} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -\tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_{c,\alpha}^* \\ i_{c,\beta}^* \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Quick-Test:** If the load is balanced, the zero-sequence current $i_0 = 0$, simplifying the abc current generation. If the load is **unbalanced** (single-phase loads on a 3-phase system), $i_0 \neq 0$ and the full 3×3 Clarke matrix must be used.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: DVR Energy Storage for a Phase-Advance Compensation

**Scenario:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 415 V load draws 100 kVA at 0.85 pf lagging. A voltage sag of 0.3 pu (i.e., 70 % remaining) with a phase jump of $\Delta\phi = 30°$ occurs for 0.5 s.

**Method:** *Phase-Advance* — the DVR injects voltage with a small phase lead to **minimize active power** it must exchange, while keeping the load voltage magnitude constant.

#### Step 1 — Pre-sag Voltage (Rated)

$$V_{\text{pre}} = 1 \text{ pu} \angle 0°$$

#### Step 2 — During-Sag Voltage (At PCC)

$$V_{\text{sag}} = 0.7 \text{ pu} \angle -30°$$

#### Step 3 — Required DVR Injection Voltage

$$\vec{V}_{\text{DVR}} = \vec{V}_L^* - \vec{V}_{\text{sag}} = 1\angle 0° - 0.7 \angle -30°$$

Converting to rectangular form:

$$0.7 \angle -30° = 0.7 (\cos(-30°) + j\sin(-30°)) = 0.7 (0.866 - j\,0.5) = 0.6062 - j\,0.35$$

$$\vec{V}_{\text{DVR}} = (1 - 0.6062) + j(0 + 0.35) = 0.3938 + j\,0.35$$

Magnitude:

$$\vert \vec{V}_{\text{DVR}} \vert = \sqrt{0.3938^2 + 0.35^2} = \sqrt{0.1551 + 0.1225} = \sqrt{0.2776} \approx 0.527 \text{ pu}$$

#### Step 4 — Active Power Exchanged by DVR

$$P_{\text{DVR}} = S_L \left( \sqrt{1 + V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2 - 2 V_{\text{sag,pu}} \cos\Delta\phi} - V_{\text{sag,pu}}\cos(\delta - \phi_L) \right)$$

For pure phase-advance (no active power exchange), the DVR operates at $\delta = \phi_L$ where $\phi_L = \cos^{-1}(0.85) \approx 31.79°$. In this ideal case, the DVR exchanges **only reactive power**, and the **DC-link energy is minimized** to maintain capacitor voltage during the sag.

#### Step 5 — Energy Required (Numerical)

For in-phase compensation (no phase advance):

$$E_{\text{DVR}} = S_L \sqrt{1 - V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2} \cdot T_{\text{sag}}$$
$$E_{\text{DVR}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.7^2} \times 0.5$$
$$E_{\text{DVR}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{0.51} \times 0.5$$
$$E_{\text{DVR}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.7141 \times 0.5 \approx 35{,}706 \text{ J} \approx 35.7 \text{ kJ}$$

For phase-advance compensation:

$$E_{\text{DVR}} = S_L \sqrt{1 + 0.7^2 - 2(0.7)\cos 30°} \times 0.5$$
$$E_{\text{DVR}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.49 - 1.2124} \times 0.5$$
$$E_{\text{DVR}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{0.2776} \times 0.5$$
$$E_{\text{DVR}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.5269 \times 0.5 \approx 26{,}346 \text{ J} \approx 26.3 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The phase-advance method requires **26 % less DC energy** (35.7 kJ → 26.3 kJ) compared to the in-phase method. This is precisely why smart DVR controllers prefer **phase-advance injection** in energy-constrained applications (e.g., EV battery-fed DVRs).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PQ Conditioners

| Feature | Shunt APF (DSTATCOM) | Series APF / DVR | UPQC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Connection** | Parallel (shunt) | Series | Both (back-to-back) |
| **Compensates** | Current harmonics, reactive power, unbalance (current) | Voltage sags, swells, voltage harmonics, unbalance (voltage) | **All of the above simultaneously** |
| **DC-Link** | Independent | Independent | **Common** (unique feature) |
| **Active Power Exchange** | Does not supply load | Supplies load during sag (needs energy storage) | Can **transfer** active power from shunt to series branch |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Switching Frequency** | 5–20 kHz | 5–20 kHz | 5–20 kHz |
| **Typical Rating** | 5 kVA – 10 MVA | 100 kVA – 5 MVA | 100 kVA – 5 MVA |
| **Application** | Industrial harmonic-rich loads | Sensitive electronic loads | Microgrids, premium power parks |
| **Key Reference IEEE Std** | IEEE 519, 1459 | IEEE 1159, 1250 | IEEE 519, 1159 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcBus["SOURCE BUS"]:::bus
    pcc["PCC"]:::pcc
    loadBus["LOAD BUS"]:::bus
    load["SENSITIVE LOAD"]:::load

    serInv["SERIES VSI / DVR BRANCH"]:::series
    shuntInv["SHUNT VSI / DSTATCOM BRANCH"]:::shunt
    dcLink["COMMON DC LINK CAPACITOR"]:::dc

    serFilt["SERIES INJECTION TRANSFORMER"]:::xfmr
    shuntFilt["SHUNT COUPLING INDUCTOR"]:::xfmr

    ctrl["CENTRAL CONTROLLER DSP FPGA"]:::ctrl
    pll["PLL 50 HZ REFERENCE"]:::ctrl

    srcBus --- pcc
    pcc --- loadBus
    loadBus --- load

    pcc -- voltage sensing --> ctrl
    load -- current sensing --> ctrl
    pll --> ctrl

    ctrl -- gate pulses series --> serInv
    ctrl -- gate pulses shunt --> shuntInv

    serInv --- serFilt
    serFilt -- injects series voltage --> pcc

    shuntInv --- shuntFilt
    shuntFilt -- injects shunt current --> pcc

    serInv --- dcLink
    shuntInv --- dcLink

    classDef bus fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef pcc fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    classDef load fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#351c75,color:#000000
    classDef series fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef shunt fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000000
    classDef dc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    classDef xfmr fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#434343,color:#000000
    classDef ctrl fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Series VSI** is inserted between source and PCC via an **injection transformer** — it adds a series voltage to compensate for sags/swells.
- The **Shunt VSI** is connected at the PCC via a **coupling inductor** — it cancels load current harmonics.
- The **Common DC Link** allows active power to flow between the two VSI branches (e.g., during a sag, the shunt branch can draw real power from the source and feed it to the series branch to support the load).
- The **Central Controller** (DSP/FPGA) executes the $p$–$q$ theory or $d$-$q$ SRF algorithm in real time and generates PWM gate pulses.

### 4.2 Control Strategy Flowchart (p-q Theory Implementation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["SENSE Va Vb Vc AND Ia Ib Ic"]:::sensor
    stepB["CLARKE TRANSFORM abc TO ALPHA BETA"]:::math
    stepC["COMPUTE p AND q INSTANTANEOUS POWERS"]:::math
    stepD["LPF TO EXTRACT p BAR AND q BAR"]:::filter
    stepE["COMPUTE p TILDE AND q TILDE"]:::math
    stepF["DC LINK PI CONTROLLER OUTPUT P LOSS"]:::ctrl
    stepG["FORM REFERENCE POWER MATRIX"]:::math
    stepH["INVERSE POWER MATRIX TO GET IC ALPHA BETA"]:::math
    stepI["INVERSE CLARKE TO GET IC ABC REFERENCE"]:::math
    stepJ["HYSTERESIS CURRENT CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
    stepK["PWM GATE PULSES TO VSI"]:::output

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK

    classDef sensor fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    classDef math fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000000
    classDef filter fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000000
    classDef ctrl fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#351c75,color:#000000
```

**Why the LPF matters:** The fundamental components $\bar{p}, \bar{q}$ are *DC quantities* in steady state. A **cut-off frequency of 5–10 Hz** is typical — slow enough to reject all harmonic oscillations and fast enough to track load changes (motor starts, etc.).

### 4.3 DVR Operation Modes — Phasor Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    modeA["NORMAL MODE BYPASS SWITCH CLOSED DVR STANDBY"]:::mode
    modeB["SAG MODE DVR INJECTS Vc IN PHASE WITH Vs"]:::mode
    modeC["SWELL MODE DVR INJECTS Vc OUT OF PHASE TO ABSORB"]:::mode
    modeD["PHASE ADVANCE MODE MINIMIZES ACTIVE POWER"]:::mode

    modeA -- voltage rises above 1.1 pu --> modeC
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the term "Power Quality" as per IEEE 1159. List any four power quality issues in a smart grid.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Power Quality (IEEE 1159-2019):** Power quality is defined as the concept of powering and grounding sensitive electronic equipment in a manner that is suitable to the operation of that equipment and compatible with the premise wiring system and other connected equipment.
>
> Four PQ issues in a smart grid:
> 1. **Voltage sag** — momentary drop in RMS voltage (0.1–0.9 pu, 0.5 cyc – 1 min).
> 2. **Voltage swell** — momentary rise in RMS voltage (1.1–1.8 pu).
> 3. **Harmonics** — distortion of the fundamental 50/60 Hz waveform due to non-linear loads.
> 4. **Voltage flicker** — rapid, repetitive fluctuations (e.g., arc furnaces, wind turbines).
>
> *(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for any 4 issues)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Differentiate between Active Power Filter and Passive Power Filter.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Passive Filter | Active Power Filter |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Components** | L, C, R tuned to harmonic freq | IGBT-based VSI + controller |
> | **Frequency Response** | Fixed (single tuning) | Adaptive (real-time) |
> | **Resonance Risk** | Can resonate with grid | No resonance issue |
> | **Size** | Bulky at low freq | Compact |
> | **Harmonic Compensation** | Limited to tuned freq | Broadband |
> | **Reactive Power** | Fixed compensation | Controllable dynamically |
> | **Cost at Low Rating** | Lower | Higher |
> | **Response Time** | Instantaneous | Sub-cycle (≤ 1 ms) |
