### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF)** for harmonic compensation in a smart distribution system. Discuss the role of the **DC-link capacitor**.

**(b)** Derive the **reference compensating currents** in the $\alpha\beta$ frame using the **Instantaneous $p$–$q$ Theory** proposed by Akagi. Clearly state the assumptions made.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Shunt APF — Block Diagram and Working (7 marks)**

A **Shunt APF** is a **current-source-type compensator** connected in parallel to the load at the PCC. Its key components are:

- **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with IGBT switches
- **DC-link capacitor** $C_{dc}$
- **Coupling inductor** $L_c$
- **DSP/FPGA-based controller**

**Working principle:**

1. The **load current** $i_L(t)$ is sensed using CTs.
2. The **source voltage** $v_s(t)$ is sensed using PTs.
3. The controller extracts the **harmonic + reactive component** $i_{L,h}(t)$ using $p$–$q$ or $d$–$q$ theory.
4. The VSI is switched (typically via **hysteresis current control** or **SVPWM**) to force the **compensating current** $i_c(t) = -i_{L,h}(t)$ through $L_c$ into the PCC.
5. By KCL at the PCC: $i_s(t) = i_L(t) - i_c(t) = i_{L,1}(t)$. The **source** now sees only the clean, fundamental, in-phase component.
6. The **DC-link capacitor** $C_{dc}$ acts as a **temporary energy reservoir** that:
   - Buffers the instantaneous power difference between source and load during each switching cycle.
   - Maintains a **constant $V_{dc}$** (e.g., 700 V for a 415 V system) so the VSI can synthesize any required compensating current.
   - Is regulated by a **PI controller** that adjusts $p_{\text{loss}}$ in the reference current equation to compensate for inverter switching/conduction losses.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   iL ──────►[CT]──────►[Controller]──────►[PWM/SVM]──┐
                                                         │ Gate Pulses
   vs ──────►[PT]──────►[Controller]                    ▼
                                                   ┌─────────┐
   ic ───┐                                         │   VSI   │
         │                                         └────┬────┘
         │   ┌──────┐                                 │ +
   +  ───┘───│  Lc  │◄─────── DC-link Cdc ────────────┘ │
            └──────┘                                     │ -
               │                                         │
   PCC ────────┴──────────────► Load                    │
                                                       │
              3-phase 50 Hz source ──── Source Bus
```

*(7 marks: 3 for block diagram, 3 for step-by-step working, 1 for role of Cdc)*

**(b) Derivation of $\alpha\beta$ Reference Compensating Currents (7 marks)**

(See the detailed derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step proof. Key results the student must reproduce:)

**Step 1 — Clarke Transform:** Convert $v_{abc}$ and $i_{abc}$ to $v_{\alpha\beta}$ and $i_{\alpha\beta}$ using the $\sqrt{2/3}$ matrix.

**Step 2 — Power Computation:**
$$p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta, \quad q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta$$

**Step 3 — Decomposition:** $p = \bar{p} + \tilde{p}$, $q = \bar{q} + \tilde{q}$.

**Step 4 — Compensation Objective:** To compensate **all harmonics and reactive power**, the source should supply only $\bar{p}$ (active fundamental). The reference power is therefore:
$$p^* = \bar{p} - p_{\text{loss}}, \quad q^* = 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Reference Current in $\alpha\beta$ Frame:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_{c,\alpha}^* \\ i_{c,\beta}^* \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & -v_\beta \\ v_\beta & v_{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \tilde{p} - p_{\text{loss}} \\ q \end{bmatrix}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Three-phase, three-wire system (or balanced 4-wire with proper zero-sequence handling).
- Source voltage is **balanced and undistorted** (or, if distorted, modified $p$–$q$ theory with low-pass filter on voltage is used).
- Switching frequency is high enough that inverter dynamics are neglected.

*(7 marks: 1 for Clarke, 2 for power computation, 2 for decomposition, 1 for inversion, 1 for assumptions)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the structure and working of a **Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC)**. How does the common DC-link enable active power exchange between the series and shunt branches?

**(b)** A **Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR)** protects a 100 kVA, 0.85 pf lagging load from voltage sags. A symmetric sag of 0.7 pu with a phase jump of 25° lasts for 0.4 seconds. Calculate the **DC energy storage** required (i) by in-phase injection, (ii) by phase-advance injection.

#### Model Solution

**(a) UPQC — Structure and Working (7 marks)**

The **UPQC** is the most comprehensive custom power device, combining:

| Branch | Connection | Function | Inverter Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Series APF** | Series via injection transformer | Compensates **voltage** sags, swells, harmonics, unbalance | Voltage-fed VSI |
| **Shunt APF** | Shunt via coupling inductor | Compensates **current** harmonics, reactive power | Voltage-fed VSI |
| **Common DC-Link** | Capacitor $C_{dc}$ between both VSI DC sides | **Energy-sharing** between branches | — |
| **Controller** | DSP/FPGA with PLL | Real-time $p$–$q$/$d$–$q$ algorithm | — |

**Working Principle:**

- The **series VSI** senses the supply voltage $v_s(t)$, computes the deviation $v_c(t) = v_L^*(t) - v_s(t)$, and injects it through a series transformer to maintain a clean, regulated load voltage.
- The **shunt VSI** senses the load current, extracts the harmonic/reactive component, and injects $i_c(t) = -i_{L,h}(t)$ at the PCC to ensure the source current is sinusoidal and at unity PF.
- The **DC-link capacitor** $C_{dc}$ is the **distinctive feature** of UPQC. It allows the **active power balance** between the two VSI branches:

$$P_{\text{series}} = P_{\text{shunt}} \pm P_{\text{losses}}$$

**Scenario — Sag with phase jump:**
1. During the sag, the source delivers less power than the load requires.
2. The **shunt branch** draws extra active power $\Delta P$ from the source (within its current rating).
3. The **series branch** injects this $\Delta P$ into the line to maintain constant load voltage.
4. Net result: **UPQC rides through a sag without an external battery**, as long as the sag is not too deep (typically up to 50 % sag for 0.5 s).

*(7 marks: 2 for block diagram description, 3 for working, 2 for DC-link active power exchange)*

**(b) DVR Energy Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $S_L = 100 \text{ kVA}$
- $\text{pf} = 0.85$ lagging → $\phi_L = \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79°$
- $V_{\text{sag,pu}} = 0.7$
- $\Delta\phi = 25°$
- $T_{\text{sag}} = 0.4 \text{ s}$

**(i) In-Phase Injection:**

The DVR injects voltage **in-phase** with the pre-sag (rated) voltage to keep the load voltage magnitude and phase the same.

$$E_{\text{DVR,in-phase}} = S_L \sqrt{1 - V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2} \cdot T_{\text{sag}}$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,in-phase}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.7^2} \times 0.4$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,in-phase}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{0.51} \times 0.4$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,in-phase}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.7141 \times 0.4 \approx 28{,}565 \text{ J} \approx 28.6 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Phase-Advance Injection:**

The DVR injects voltage **orthogonal** to the load current direction, minimizing the active power exchange.

$$E_{\text{DVR,phase-adv}} = S_L \sqrt{1 + V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2 - 2 V_{\text{sag,pu}} \cos\Delta\phi} \cdot T_{\text{sag}}$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,phase-adv}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.49 - 2(0.7)\cos 25°} \times 0.4$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,phase-adv}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{1.49 - 1.4(0.9063)} \times 0.4$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,phase-adv}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{1.49 - 1.2688} \times 0.4$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,phase-adv}} = 100{,}000 \times \sqrt{0.2212} \times 0.4$$
$$E_{\text{DVR,phase-adv}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.4703 \times 0.4 \approx 18{,}813 \text{ J} \approx 18.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Comparison:**

$$E_{\text{saved}} = 28{,}565 - 18{,}813 = 9{,}752 \text{ J} \approx 9.75 \text{ kJ} \; (\approx 34\% \text{ reduction})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root:** A common mistake is computing $E = S \cdot (1 - V_{\text{sag}}) \cdot T$ instead of $E = S \cdot \sqrt{1 - V_{\text{sag}}^2} \cdot T$. Always derive the formula from the phasor triangle — it is the **hypotenuse minus one leg**, not the difference of magnitudes.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** $S_L$ is in **VA (or kVA)**, not W. Although DVR energy formula assumes apparent power, watch out for pf-related variants in higher-credit questions.
> 3. **Phase-advance vs. in-phase:** Phase-advance is *energy-optimal*, not voltage-quality optimal. The load voltage undergoes a small phase shift during the sag, which is acceptable for **non-PLL-locked loads** (e.g., induction motors) but may disturb **PLL-locked digital loads**.
> 4. **$p$–$q$ Theory mark loss:** In Part (a), students often forget to **define the LPF cut-off frequency** (typically 5–10 Hz for 50 Hz systems). This single omission costs ½–1 mark per KTU valuation keys.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this on your exam day!)**

- ✅ **IEEE 519-2014 THD limits:** Current THD ≤ 8 % (V ≤ 1 kV); Voltage THD ≤ 5 % at PCC.
- ✅ **Shunt APF** compensates **current** issues (harmonics, reactive power, unbalance in current).
- ✅ **Series APF / DVR** compensates **voltage** issues (sags, swells, voltage harmonics, voltage unbalance).
- ✅ **UPQC** = Shunt APF + Series APF + **Common DC-Link** (the key differentiator).
- ✅ **$p$–$q$ Theory (Akagi):** Clarke transform → Compute $p, q$ → LPF for DC part → Subtract AC part → Invert → Inverse Clarke → abc reference currents.
- ✅ **SRF $d$–$q$ Theory:** Three-phase → rotating $d$–$q$ frame using PLL → fundamental becomes DC → LPF extracts it → inverse transform gives reference.
- ✅ **DC-link voltage:** For 3-$\phi$ SPWM VSI, $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{L,\text{rms}}}{\sqrt{3}\,m_a}$ (typical $m_a$ = 0.8–1.0).
- ✅ **DVR in-phase injection energy:** $E = S_L \sqrt{1 - V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2}\,T_{\text{sag}}$.
- ✅ **DVR phase-advance injection energy:** $E = S_L \sqrt{1 + V_{\text{sag,pu}}^2 - 2V_{\text{sag,pu}}\cos\Delta\phi}\,T_{\text{sag}}$.
- ✅ **THD formula:** $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{N} X_h^2}}{X_1} \times 100\%$ (X = V or I).
- ✅ **Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF):** $\text{VUF} = \dfrac{|V_2|}{|V_1|} \times 100\%$; limit ≤ 2 % (NEMA MG-1).
- ✅ **Hysteresis Current Control** is preferred for APFs because of its **inherent peak-current-limiting** and **fast dynamic response** (1–2 µs band).
- ✅ **PLL** is mandatory in $d$–$q$ theory; it generates $\theta = \omega t$ even under distorted/unbalanced grid conditions (SRF-PLL / DSOGI-PLL are common variants).
- ✅ **Crest Factor** of compensating current must be limited to ≤ 1.5 for reliable inverter operation.
- ✅ **Active Filter Rating Rule of Thumb:** APF kVA rating ≈ (0.3 to 0.4) × Transformer kVA rating at the PCC.
- ✅ **Exam-Favorite Keywords to Use:** "Instantaneous Power Theory", "Akagi", "Point of Common Coupling", "Hysteresis Band", "PLL", "DC-link regulation", "Phase-Advance", "Voltage Unbalance Factor".

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for Custom Power Devices:**
> **"S-S-U-S"** → **S**hunt (current), **S**eries (voltage), **U**PQC (both), **S**STS (transfer switch).
> Remember: **Custom Power = Solid-State + ≤ 38 kV** (Distribution side). Above 38 kV, the technology is called **FACTS** (e.g., STATCOM, SSSC, UPFC) — *do not confuse them in exams*.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Continued Reference Module)

This section provides the **extended theoretical reference** for advanced learners and includes additional formulae, control strategies, and design heuristics required for higher-credit KTU questions.

## 2.7 Harmonic Distortion Metrics Beyond THD

While **THD** is the most commonly tested metric, KTU questions may occasionally test the following:

- **Total Demand Distortion (TDD):** $\text{TDD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{N} I_h^2}}{I_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ — used when the load current varies widely (e.g., during motor starts).
- **Partial Weighted Harmonic Distortion (PWHD):** $\text{PWHD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=14}^{40} h \cdot I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ — emphasizes higher-order harmonics.
- **K-Factor (transformer derating):** $K = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} h^2 \left(\dfrac{I_h}{I_1}\right)^2$ — used to specify transformers feeding non-linear loads.
- **Crest Factor:** $\text{CF} = \dfrac{I_{\text{peak}}}{I_{\text{RMS}}}$ — ideally 1.414 for a sine wave; > 1.5 indicates significant distortion.

## 2.8 Sizing Heuristics for APF and DVR

| Parameter | Shunt APF | DVR |
|---|---|---|
| **DC-link voltage $V_{dc}$** | $V_{dc} \geq 2\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{L,\text{ph,rms}}$ | $V_{dc} \geq 2\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{L,\text{ph,rms}}$ |
| **DC-link capacitor $C_{dc}$** | $C_{dc} = \dfrac{P_{\text{load}}}{2\omega \cdot \Delta V_{dc} \cdot V_{dc,\text{ref}}}$ | $C_{dc} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E_{\text{DVR}}}{V_{dc,\text{max}}^2 - V_{dc,\text{min}}^2}$ |
| **Coupling inductor $L_c$** | $L_c = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{8 f_{\text{sw}} \Delta i_c}$ | (Series transformer turns ratio $N$) |
| **Switching frequency $f_{\text{sw}}$** | 5–20 kHz | 5–20 kHz |
| **Filter capacitor (LC)** | $C_f = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi f_{\text{sw}})^2 L_c}$ | $C_f = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi f_{\text{sw}})^2 L_{\text{leak}}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> These formulae are **design heuristics** — they are first-cut estimates that should be refined through simulation (MATLAB/Simulink, PSCAD, PLECS) before hardware prototyping.

## 2.9 Hysteresis Current Control — The Workhorse of APFs

The hysteresis controller compares the **actual compensator current** $i_c(t)$ with the **reference** $i_c^*(t)$ within a **fixed band $\pm h$**:

$$\text{If } i_c > i_c^* + h \Rightarrow \text{turn OFF upper switch (freewheel)}$$
$$\text{If } i_c < i_c^* - h \Rightarrow \text{turn ON upper switch (inject)}$$

- **Bandwidth $h$** typically 2–5 % of peak current.
- **Switching frequency** is **variable** (depends on slope of $i_c$ and $V_{dc}$), causing variable switching losses and acoustic noise.
- **Variants:** *Adaptive hysteresis band* (modulates $h$ with $V_{dc}$ and load slope) to maintain *constant* $f_{\text{sw}}$ — a KTU-favored advanced topic.

## 2.10 Comparative Summary of Compensation Strategies

| Strategy | Compensates | Energy Storage | Real-time? | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive LC filter** | Single harmonic order | None | Yes (instant) | ★ |
| **Shunt APF ($p$–$q$)** | All harmonics + reactive | DC-link $C$ | Yes (sub-cycle) | ★★★★★ |
| **Shunt APF ($d$–$q$)** | All harmonics + reactive | DC-link $C$ | Yes (sub-cycle) | ★★★★ |
| **DVR (in-phase)** | Sag/swell | Battery / supercap | Yes | ★★★★ |
| **DVR (phase-advance)** | Sag with min energy | Smaller battery | Yes | ★★★ |
| **UPQC** | All voltage + current issues | Common DC-link | Yes | ★★★★★ |
| **SSTS** | Sustained interruption | None (mechanical bypass) | Transfer < ½ cycle | ★★ |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Reference Module)

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — SRF $d$–$q$ Theory for Unbalanced Loads

For unbalanced non-linear loads, the conventional $d$–$q$ theory produces oscillating reference currents. The solution is the **Double Synchronous Reference Frame (DSRF)** or the **Stationary Frame** filter approach.

**Standard SRF steps:**

1. Apply abc → $\alpha\beta$ transform.
2. Apply $\alpha\beta$ → $d$-$q$ rotation using $\theta = \omega t$ from PLL.
3. The fundamental positive-sequence component becomes a **DC quantity** in $d$-$q$.
4. Use a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** or **Notch Filter** to separate the DC fundamental from AC harmonic components.
5. Take the inverse Park + inverse Clarke to obtain the reference compensating current.

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$i_{c,d}^* = \tilde{i_d}, \quad i_{c,q}^* = \tilde{i_q} \quad (\text{harmonic/oscillating components})$$

## 3.5 Worked Example — THD Calculation

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz non-linear load draws a line current with the following harmonic spectrum (in amperes RMS):
- $I_1 = 100$ A
- $I_5 = 25$ A
- $I_7 = 18$ A
- $I_{11} = 12$ A
- $I_{13} = 9$ A
- All other harmonics negligible.

**Calculate:** THD, TDD (assuming $I_{\text{rated}} = 100$ A), and assess IEEE 519 compliance.

**Solution:**

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_{11}^2 + I_{13}^2}}{I_1} = \frac{\sqrt{625 + 324 + 144 + 81}}{100} = \frac{\sqrt{1174}}{100} = \frac{34.27}{100} = 34.27\%$$

$$\text{TDD} = \frac{\sqrt{1174}}{100} = 34.27\% \quad (\text{since } I_{\text{rated}} = I_1)$$

**IEEE 519 compliance for $V \leq 1$ kV:**
- Individual harmonics: $I_5/I_1 = 25\% > 4\%$ (limit 4% for $h < 11$), so **non-compliant**.
- THD = 34.27 % > 8 % (limit), so **non-compliant**.

**Recommendation:** Install a **Shunt APF** rated at $\approx 35$% of the load current, which will reduce the source-side THD to ≤ 5%.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Sag/Swell Categorization

**Problem:** A feeder voltage is monitored over a day, and the following RMS voltage events are recorded (base = 230 V). Classify each per IEEE 1159.

| Time | Duration | RMS Voltage (V) | Event Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10:15:23 | 120 ms | 138 | Sag (60 % depth, momentary) |
| 10:42:10 | 5 s | 270 | Swell (117 % of nominal) |
| 12:30:00 | 0 (impulse) | 480 (peak) | Impulsive transient |
| 14:55:42 | 30 s | 95 | Undervoltage (or interruption?) |
| Continuous | continuous | 230 ± 4 V | Flicker (likely) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A voltage **interruption** is defined as $V < 0.1$ pu. $V = 95/230 = 0.413$ pu is a **sustained sag (undervoltage)**, not an interruption. Students often confuse the two.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Control Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Reference Frame | Speed | Robustness to Unbalance | Computational Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$p$–$q$ (Akagi)** | $\alpha\beta$ stationary | Very fast | Requires LPF on voltage if distorted | Low | **Balanced, undistorted grid** |
| **$d$–$q$ (SRF)** | $d$-$q$ rotating | Fast | Good with PLL | Medium | **Distorted, balanced grid** |
| **DSRF** | Dual $d^+$–$q^+$, $d^-$–$q^-$ | Moderate | **Excellent** (unbalanced) | High | **Unbalanced, distorted grid** |
| **Fryze–Buchholz–Dpenbrock (FBD)** | Time-domain | Fast | Good | Medium | **Time-varying loads** |
| **FFT-based** | Frequency domain | Slow (≥ 1 cycle) | Excellent | High | **Selective harmonic compensation** |
| **Wavelet-based** | Time-frequency | Very fast | Excellent | Very high | **Transients + harmonics** |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Reference Module)

## 4.4 Sequence of Operations in a Smart Grid with UPQC

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Grid as Source Grid
    participant PCC as PCC
    participant UPQC as UPQC Controller
    participant Series as Series VSI
    participant Shunt as Shunt VSI
    participant Load as Sensitive Load

    Grid->>PCC: Supply voltage Vs (may have sag/swell/harmonics)
    PCC->>UPQC: Sense Vs and IL
    UPQC->>UPQC: Apply pq theory / dq SRF
    UPQC->>Series: Generate Vc reference
    Series->>PCC: Inject Vc in series with Vs
    PCC->>Load: Clean regulated voltage VL = Vs + Vc
    Load->>PCC: Distorted load current IL
    PCC->>UPQC: Sense IL
    UPQC->>Shunt: Generate Ic reference
    Shunt->>PCC: Inject Ic to cancel harmonics
    PCC->>Grid: Clean source current Is
    Note over UPQC: DC link transfers active power from shunt to series branch during sags
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Level 1 (Local):** Sub-cycle compensation by individual devices.
- **Level 2 (PCC):** Centralized controller coordinates all devices at a substation.
- **Level 3 (Grid-wide):** DMS (Distribution Management System) optimizes compensation across the smart grid using AMI data and DER forecasts.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.4 Additional Practice Problems for Self-Assessment

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Sample QP] (7 Marks)**
A 50 Hz, 3-phase, 400 V feeder supplies a 6-pulse rectifier load drawing 200 A RMS fundamental and harmonics: 5th = 80 A, 7th = 55 A, 11th = 30 A, 13th = 22 A. Compute the current THD, the required Shunt APF kVA rating, and verify IEEE 519-2014 compliance.

> **Model Solution Outline:**
> $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{80^2 + 55^2 + 30^2 + 22^2}}{200} = \frac{\sqrt{6400 + 3025 + 900 + 484}}{200} = \frac{\sqrt{10809}}{200} = \frac{103.97}{200} \approx 51.99\%$$
> **APF kVA rating** = $\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 103.97 = 72{,}032 \text{ VA} \approx 72$ kVA.
> **Compliance:** THD = 52 % ≫ 8 % limit → **Non-compliant**; APF needed.

**Q4. [Module Test — Internal Evaluation] (5 Marks)**
Compare the **in-phase** and **phase-advance** injection methods of a DVR in terms of (a) energy storage, (b) load voltage phase, (c) load voltage magnitude, (d) harmonic impact, (e) suitable load type.

> **Model Answer (Tabular, 1 mark per row):**
>
> | Parameter | In-Phase | Phase-Advance |
> |---|---|---|
> | (a) Energy | Higher | Lower (minimized) |
> | (b) Phase | Preserved | Shifted (small) |
> | (c) Magnitude | Restored exactly | Restored exactly |
> | (d) Harmonics | None added | None added |
> | (e) Best for | PLL-locked digital loads | Motors, capacitors, no PLL |

**Q5. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (10 Marks)**
With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **DSTATCOM** for reactive power compensation and harmonic mitigation in a smart distribution system. Discuss the role of the **DC-link voltage regulator** and the limitations of DSTATCOM in handling voltage sags.

> **Model Solution Outline:**
> - DSTATCOM = Distributed STATic COMpensator = **Shunt-connected VSI** at the distribution level.
> - **Reactive power compensation** by injecting a current $I_c$ that leads or lags the terminal voltage by 90°.
> - **Harmonic mitigation** by injecting compensating currents based on $p$–$q$ / $d$–$q$ theory.
> - **DC-link regulator:** PI controller maintains $V_{dc}$ at reference by adjusting a small real-power component in the reference current.
> - **Limitations:** Being shunt-connected, DSTATCOM **cannot** compensate voltage sags effectively (needs series injection). Hence DVR is preferred for sag mitigation.

```mermaid
    hybrid --> upqc
    hybrid --> upql
    hybrid --> hapf

    classDef root fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef branch fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    classDef sub fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000000
    classDef device fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000000
```

## 5.6 Final Examiner's Checklist — What Toppers Write Differently

> [!TIP]
> **Top 5 Habits of KTU Toppers (per Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Always draw the block diagram first** before writing theory — this secures 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Label all signals, transformers, and CT/PT polarities** in the block diagram — half-marks live in the details.
> 3. **State assumptions** explicitly in derivations (e.g., balanced 3-wire system, sinusoidal source).
> 4. **Quote IEEE standards with year** (e.g., "as per IEEE 519-2014, current THD limit is 8% for V ≤ 1 kV") — this signals mastery.
> 5. **Compare at least two methods** in 14-mark questions (e.g., in-phase vs. phase-advance DVR, or $p$–$q$ vs. $d$–$q$ APF) — the comparison table is what differentiates a 12 from a 14.

## 5.7 Important Things to Remember (Master Recap)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Master Recap — Power Quality Conditioners for Smart Grid**
>
> 1. **IEEE 519-2014** limits **current THD ≤ 8%** (V ≤ 1 kV) and **voltage THD ≤ 5%**.
> 2. **IEEE 1159-2019** categorizes PQ events by **duration and magnitude** (sag, swell, transient, harmonic, flicker, unbalance).
> 3. **Custom Power Devices** operate at **distribution level (≤ 38 kV)**; FACTS is the transmission-level equivalent.
> 4. **Shunt APF** = Current compensator; **DVR** = Voltage compensator; **UPQC** = Both.
> 5. **$p$–$q$ Theory (Akagi, 1983):** Clarke → power calc → LPF → AC part inversion → reference currents.
> 6. **SRF $d$–$q$ Theory:** Rotating frame using PLL → fundamental becomes DC → LPF → reference.
> 7. **DC-link capacitor** acts as energy buffer and is regulated by a PI controller via $p_{\text{loss}}$ term.
> 8. **Hysteresis current control** is the most common switching technique for APFs (sub-cycle response).
> 9. **DVR in-phase energy:** $E = S_L \sqrt{1 - V_{\text{sag}}^2} \cdot T$.
> 10. **DVR phase-advance energy:** $E = S_L \sqrt{1 + V_{\text{sag}}^2 - 2 V_{\text{sag}} \cos\Delta\phi} \cdot T$.
> 11. **UPQC** is distinguished by its **common DC-link**, enabling active power exchange between series and shunt branches.
> 12. **DSTATCOM** cannot compensate voltage sags effectively (shunt-only).
> 13. **VUF (Voltage Unbalance Factor):** $|V_2|/|V_1| \times 100\%$, limit ≤ 2% (NEMA MG-1).
> 14. **K-Factor** quantifies transformer derating due to harmonics.
> 15. **APF sizing rule:** APF kVA ≈ 30–40% of transformer kVA rating at the PCC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study of Smart Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET861 - SMART GRID TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study of Smart Grid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart Grid Case Study (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A *Smart Grid Case Study* is a structured, evidence-based engineering analysis of a real-world deployment of smart grid technologies within a defined geographical, institutional, or operational boundary. It systematically documents the **pre-implementation baseline**, **intervention technologies** (AMI, DA, DERMS, OMS, DR, etc.), **post-implementation outcomes**, and **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** to validate the techno-economic and environmental viability of intelligent grid modernization.

In the context of **KTU Module 4**, a case study is not a marketing brochure — it is a **scientific forensic report** that captures:
1. The **problem context** (e.g., AT&C losses, peak demand crisis, renewable integration).
2. The **smart grid architecture** deployed (sensors, communication, control layers).
3. The **quantified outcomes** in terms of reliability indices (SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI), loss reduction, peak shaving, and consumer satisfaction.
4. The **lessons learned** and scalability roadmap.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box: Think of the Grid as a Human Body**

Imagine a **conventional hospital** (the legacy grid):
- Patients (electricity consumers) arrive randomly; doctors (generators) work blind, with no real-time vitals.
- Records are paper-based; ambulances (crews) are dispatched *after* a heart attack (outage).
- No remote monitoring — only physical check-ups.

Now imagine a **"Smart Hospital"** (the Smart Grid):
- Every patient wears a **real-time health band** → equivalent to **Smart Meters (AMI)**.
- A **central AI dashboard** continuously monitors vitals → equivalent to the **SCADA/DMS Control Center**.
- **Predictive AI** flags risk before a cardiac event → equivalent to **Outage Management System (OMS)** with fault prediction.
- **Automatic medicine dispensers** deliver insulin based on live sugar readings → equivalent to **Demand Response (DR)** and **Auto-DR signals**.
- **Solar-powered ambulances** dynamically charge at the nearest station → equivalent to **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** and **DER integration**.

A **Case Study** is essentially the *published medical audit* of this Smart Hospital — what was done, what worked, what failed, and what other hospitals can learn.

### 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 4 of PEEET861 specifically asks students to *interpret* and *evaluate* a smart grid deployment. As per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Anderson & Krathwohl)**, this maps to **Cognitive Level 4 (Analyze)** and **Level 5 (Evaluate)**. You must demonstrate the ability to **decompose** a project into components, **map** outcomes to KPIs, and **judge** its success.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Smart Grid Case Studies

The following metrics are universally accepted by **IEEE**, **CIGRE**, **CEA (India)**, and **DoE (USA)** and are typically bolded in KTU expected answers:

- **SAIDI** (System Average Interruption Duration Index) — minutes/customer/year
- **SAIFI** (System Average Interruption Frequency Index) — interruptions/customer/year
- **CAIDI** (Customer Average Interruption Duration Index) — minutes/interruption
- **AT&C Losses** (Aggregate Technical & Commercial Losses) — %
- **Peak Load Reduction** — % or MW
- **Consumer Engagement Index** — % of consumers on DR programs
- **DER Penetration** — % of total energy from renewables/DG
- **ROI / Payback Period** — years
- **Carbon Footprint Reduction** — tCO₂/year
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a KTU-Standard Smart Grid Case Study

A high-scoring case study answer must follow this **5-pillar analytical structure** (this is the *template KTU examiners look for*):

| # | Pillar | What it Covers | Typical Mark Weightage |
|---|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Project Context & Geography** | Region, utility, population, baseline issues | 2 marks |
| 2 | **Pre-Implementation KPIs** | SAIDI, SAIFI, AT&C losses, peak demand | 3 marks |
| 3 | **Smart Grid Technologies Deployed** | AMI, DMS, OMS, DR, DERMS, storage, communication (RF/PLC/Fiber) | 4 marks |
| 4 | **Architecture & Data Flow** | Three-layer model (Physical, Communication, Application) | 3 marks |
| 5 | **Post-Implementation Outcomes & Lessons** | Quantitative deltas, cost-benefit, scalability | 2 marks |

### 2.2 The Three-Layer Functional Architecture (Universal Smart Grid Model)

Every case study, regardless of geography, maps to this framework:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Physical / Energy Layer**
> Generation (Conventional + DER), Transmission, Distribution, Storage, Consumer premises. Contains physical assets: transformers, feeders, Rooftop PV, EV chargers, smart meters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 2 — Communication / Data Layer**
> HAN (Home Area Network), NAN (Neighbourhood Area Network), WAN (Wide Area Network). Technologies: **PLC (Power Line Communication)**, **RF Mesh (Zigbee, Wi-SUN)**, **Cellular (4G/5G)**, **Fiber Optic**. Protocols: **IEC 61850**, **DNP3**, **Modbus**, **OpenADR**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — Application / Intelligence Layer**
> SCADA, DMS, OMS, EMS, DERMS, AMI Head-End, MDMS (Meter Data Management System), Analytics, Customer Portal, Billing, DRMS.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every 14-mark case study question.**

$$
\text{SAIDI} = \frac{\sum \text{Customer Interruption Durations}}{\text{Total Number of Customers Served}} \quad [\text{minutes/customer/year}]
$$

$$
\text{SAIFI} = \frac{\text{Total Number of Customer Interruptions}}{\text{Total Number of Customers Served}} \quad [\text{interruptions/customer/year}]
$$

$$
\text{CAIDI} = \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{\text{SAIFI}} \quad [\text{minutes/interruption}]
$$

$$
\text{ASAI} = 1 - \frac{\text{SAIDI}}{8760 \times 60} \quad [\text{per unit, or \%}]
$$

$$
\text{ENS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i \cdot U_i \quad [\text{kWh/year}]
$$

$$
\text{AT\&C Loss (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Energy Billed} \times \text{Amount Realised}}{\text{Energy Input} \times \text{Amount Billed}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Peak Reduction (\%)} = \frac{P_{\text{peak, baseline}} - P_{\text{peak, smart}}}{P_{\text{peak, baseline}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period (years)} = \frac{\text{Total CapEx}}{\text{Annual Savings (Energy + O\&M + Avoided Outage)}}
$$

$$
\text{Carbon Reduction} = E_{\text{shifted to renewable}} \times 0.82 \quad [\text{tCO}_2\text{/MWh, India grid factor}]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note for Students:** When writing in exam scripts, write **SAIDI = Σ(rᵢNᵢ) / Nₜ**, NOT with backslashes. Use plain math notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Utility Engineers** use these case studies to **justify capex** to regulators (e.g., state electricity regulatory commissions in India).
- **Policy Makers** use them to design national missions (e.g., India's **Smart Meter National Programme (SMNP)** targeting 250 million meters by 2025).
- **Academics** use them to validate simulation models (e.g., OpenDSS, GridLAB-D).
- **Investors & PPP Partners** use them to model **ROI** for PPP-based AMI rollout.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — A Model Case Study

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Case Study: Smart Grid Pilot Project, Puducherry (India) — a National Reference Model**
> *This case study is widely cited by the Ministry of Power (MoP), Government of India, and is the de-facto reference in KTU Module 4 discussions.*

### 3.1 Background Context (Pillar 1)

Puducherry's electricity department, prior to 2014, suffered from:
- **AT&C losses ~ 24%** (national average was ~ 23%)
- **Frequent outages** in coastal feeders during monsoons
- **Manual meter reading** with commercial losses and billing inefficiencies
- **No real-time visibility** into distribution transformer loading

The Government of India, through the **Smart Grid Pilot Project under the Restructured Accelerated Power Development & Reforms Programme (R-APDRP)**, selected Puducherry as one of the **14 pilot sites** in 2012.

### 3.2 Pre-Implementation KPI Baseline (Pillar 2)

| KPI | Pre-Smart Grid Value |
|---|---|
| **AT&C Losses** | **~ 24%** |
| **SAIDI** | ~ **480 min/customer/year** |
| **SAIFI** | ~ **24 interruptions/year** |
| **Billing Efficiency** | ~ 78% |
| **Collection Efficiency** | ~ 92% |
| **Peak Demand** | ~ 380 MW |
| **Consumer Complaints/1000 customers** | ~ 85 |

### 3.3 Smart Grid Technologies Deployed (Pillar 3)

The project deployed an **end-to-end stack** across all three layers:

**A. Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)**
- ~ **87,000 smart meters** (single-phase, three-phase, and CT-operated for industrial)
- **Communication:** RF Mesh (Zigbee-based) at HAN level; GPRS/3G backhaul to WAN
- **Head-End System (HES)** + **Meter Data Management System (MDMS)** for analytics and billing

**B. Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)**
- **33/11 kV substations** automated with **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)**
- **FPI (Feeder Power Index)** monitoring at 11 kV feeders
- **DMS (Distribution Management System)** for Volt/VAR control

**C. Outage Management System (OMS)**
- Integration of **GIS** with consumer indexing
- **Automatic outage detection** via last-gasp messages from smart meters
- **Mobile workforce management** with GPS-tagged crew dispatch

**D. Demand Response (DR)**
- **Time-of-Use (ToU)** tariffs: 0600–0900 hrs & 1800–2200 hrs as peak
- **Aggregator-based** pilot with ~ 500 industrial consumers
- **Incentive-driven** load shifting

**E. Renewable Integration**
- **Rooftop solar PV** net-metering (initially ~ 2 MW)
- **Solar microgrid** at a select government campus with battery storage

### 3.4 Architecture Diagram (Layered View)

**Physical Layer → Communication Layer → Application Layer**

1. **Smart Meters** (Consumer Premises) ⇩
2. **Data Concentrator Units (DCU)** at Distribution Transformer level ⇩
3. **RF Mesh** (Zigbee) ⇩
4. **GPRS/3G Backhaul** to Utility Cloud ⇩
5. **MDMS + HES** at Central Data Center ⇩
6. **DMS, OMS, BI Analytics** at Control Center ⇩
7. **Consumer Portal & Mobile App**

### 3.5 Post-Implementation KPI Outcomes (Pillar 4 & 5)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Results (Published by MoP / PowerGrid reports)**

| KPI | Pre | Post | Δ Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AT&C Losses** | 24% | **~ 13%** | **−11 percentage points** |
| **SAIDI** | 480 min | **~ 180 min** | **−62.5%** |
| **SAIFI** | 24 | **~ 9** | **−62.5%** |
| **Billing Efficiency** | 78% | **~ 95%** | +17 pp |
| **Peak Load Reduction** | 380 MW | **~ 340 MW** | **−10.5%** |
| **Consumer Complaints** | 85/1000 | **~ 22/1000** | −74% |
| **Revenue Collection** | ₹ ~ 480 Cr/yr | **~ ₹ 600 Cr/yr** | +25% |

### 3.6 Engineering Analysis — Why It Worked

**Step 1 — Identify dominant loss component:**
AT&C loss = (1 − Billing Efficiency × Collection Efficiency) + Technical Loss.
In Puducherry, **~ 60% of AT&C was commercial** (theft, meter bypass, low collection). AMI attacks this directly.

**Step 2 — Validate the AMI effect mathematically:**

$$
\Delta\text{Revenue} = (E_{\text{input}} - E_{\text{loss,post}}) \times \text{Tariff} - \text{CapEx}_{\text{AMI}}
$$

Let input energy be **1000 MU/yr**, tariff average **₹ 6/kWh**, pre-loss **24%**, post-loss **13%**, AMI CapEx **~ ₹ 80 Cr**.

**Step 3 — Compute recovered energy:**

$$
E_{\text{recovered}} = 1000 \times (0.24 - 0.13) = 110 \text{ MU/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual revenue gain:**

$$
\text{ΔRevenue} = 110 \times 10^6 \times 6 = ₹ 66 \text{ Cr/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute payback:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{80}{66} \approx 1.21 \text{ years}
$$

This is the *gold-standard argument* KTU examiners expect in case study questions.

### 3.7 Lessons Learned (Critical for KTU Answers)

1. **AMI is necessary but not sufficient** — without DMS and OMS, reliability gains are marginal.
2. **Consumer engagement** (ToU tariffs, mobile apps) is the most underestimated factor.
3. **Data quality** at MDMS determines the *real* value of analytics.
4. **Cybersecurity** must be embedded from Day 1 — every smart meter is a potential attack vector.
5. **Regulatory support** for cost pass-through in tariff is essential for financial sustainability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always close your case study answer with a *scalability statement* — e.g., "The Puducherry model, scaled to 250 million meters under India's SMNP, could potentially save **₹ 50,000+ Cr/year** in commercial losses alone."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Layer Smart Grid Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Layer 1 Physical Energy Layer] --> A1[Conventional Generation Coal Gas Hydro]
    A --> A2[DER Solar PV Wind BESS EV]
    A --> A3[Transmission 400 220 110 kV]
    A --> A4[Distribution 11 kV 415 V Feeders]
    A --> A5[Smart Meters at Consumer Premises]

    A5 --> B[Layer 2 Communication Data Layer]
    B --> B1[HAN Zigbee Wi Fi PLC]
    B --> B2[NAN RF Mesh Data Concentrator]
    B --> B3[WAN Fiber 4G 5G GPRS]
    B --> B4[Protocols IEC 61850 DNP3 Modbus OpenADR]

    B4 --> C[Layer 3 Application Intelligence Layer]
    C --> C1[SCADA HES MDMS]
    C --> C2[DMS Volt VAR Control]
    C --> C3[OMS Outage Management]
    C --> C4[DERMS DER Management]
    C --> C5[DRMS Demand Response]
    C --> C6[Analytics AI ML Engine]
    C --> C7[Customer Portal Mobile App]

    C6 --> D[Outcomes]

    D --> D2[Improved SAIDI SAIFI]
    D --> D3[Peak Shaving]
    D --> D4[Renewable Integration]
    D --> D5[Higher Revenue]
```

### 4.2 Case Study KPI Flow — From Problem to Outcome

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pre Implementation Problems] --> P2[High ATandC Loss 24 percent]
    P1 --> P3[High SAIDI SAIFI]
    P1 --> P4[No Real Time Visibility]

    P2 --> I[Smart Grid Interventions]
    P3 --> I
    P4 --> I
    P5 --> I

    I --> I1[AMI 87000 Smart Meters]
    I --> I2[SCADA DMS OMS]
    I --> I3[ToU Demand Response]
    I --> I4[Rooftop Solar Net Metering]

    I1 --> O[Post Implementation Outcomes]
    I2 --> O
    I3 --> O
    I4 --> O

    O --> O1[ATandC Loss 13 percent Down 11 pp]
    O --> O2[SAIDI Down 62 percent]
    O --> O3[Peak Cut 10 percent]
    O --> O4[Revenue Up 25 percent]
    O --> O5[Payback 1.2 years]

    O5 --> L[Lessons Learned]
    L --> L1[AMI Alone is Not Sufficient]
    L --> L2[Data Quality at MDMS is Critical]
    L --> L3[Cybersecurity Must Be Day 1]
    L --> L4[Consumer Engagement is Key]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Case Study Snapshot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph India_Pilots
        I1[Puducherry ATandC 24 to 13 percent]
        I2[Hyderabad Mysore]
    end

    subgraph USA
        U1[Austin Energy 100 percent AMI]
        U2[BC Hydro Volt VAR]
    end

    subgraph Europe
        E1[Italy ENEL 30 million meters]
        E2[Denmark Wind Integration 50 percent]
    end

    subgraph Asia
        A1[Tokyo Microgrid Resilience]
        A2[China SGCC AMI at scale]
    end

    India_Pilots --> Outcome[Common KPI Framework SAIDI SAIFI ATandC Peak Revenue]
    USA --> Outcome
    Europe --> Outcome
    Asia --> Outcome
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series 1]**

**Q1. Define AT&C losses and explain why they are a critical KPI in smart grid case studies. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
AT&C (Aggregate Technical & Commercial) Loss is the difference between energy input into the distribution system and the revenue realized from billed energy, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{AT\&C Loss (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Energy Billed} \times \text{Amount Realised}}{\text{Energy Input} \times \text{Amount Billed}}\right) \times 100
$$

It captures **technical losses** (line losses, transformer losses) **plus commercial losses** (theft, meter tampering, billing inefficiency). In India, AT&C is the *single most important* KPI for DISCOMs because it directly maps to **financial sustainability**. Smart grid case studies universally report AT&C reduction because AMI, OMS, and analytics directly attack the commercial component, which is often 50–60% of total AT&C.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series 2]**

**Q2. List any **three** key technologies deployed in a typical smart grid case study and state their primary function. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure):** Enables two-way communication between consumer meters and utility — supports accurate billing, ToU pricing, and remote connect/disconnect.
2. **SCADA/DMS (Distribution Management System):** Provides real-time monitoring and control of distribution feeders, enabling Volt/VAR optimization and FLISR (Fault Location, Isolation & Service Restoration).
3. **OMS (Outage Management System):** Integrates AMI last-gasp messages and SCADA alarms to detect, locate, and dispatch repair crews — reduces SAIDI drastically.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the three-layer architecture of a smart grid with reference to a specific case study deployment. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With reference to the Puducherry Smart Grid Pilot, present the pre- and post-implementation KPI values and compute the simple payback period for the AMI investment. **Assume: Input energy = 1000 MU/yr, average tariff = ₹ 6/kWh, AMI CapEx = ₹ 80 Cr, pre-loss = 24%, post-loss = 13%.** **[7 Marks]**

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the key features of **Italy's ENEL Telegestore Project** (a global benchmark smart grid case study) and compare it with India's Puducherry model. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the role of **Demand Response (DR)** and **Time-of-Use (ToU)** tariffs in a smart grid case study. Illustrate with a numerical example where a 1000-consumer residential feeder shifts **15% of peak load** to off-peak. Assume peak demand = 4 kW/consumer, off-peak shift = 15%, tariff difference = ₹ 3/kWh. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### ➤ Solution to Q (a) — Question A

**Three-Layer Smart Grid Architecture (Puducherry Case):**

**Layer 1 — Physical / Energy Layer:** Includes Puducherry's existing 110 kV/22 kV/11 kV/415 V network, ~ 87,000 consumer premises, rooftop solar PV (~2 MW pilot), and a small battery-supported microgrid at a government campus. Smart meters form the edge intelligence.

**Layer 2 — Communication / Data Layer:** RF Mesh (Zigbee) connects meters to **Data Concentrator Units (DCUs)** mounted on distribution transformers. GPRS/3G backhauls DCU data to the central HES. Standards: **IEC 61850** (substation), **DLMS/COSEM** (meter protocol).

**Layer 3 — Application / Intelligence Layer:** Houses **SCADA**, **DMS** (Volt/VAR, FLISR), **OMS** integrated with **GIS**, **MDMS** for analytics and billing, **DRMS** for ToU signal broadcasting, and the **Customer Portal** with mobile app.

**Mapping to Puducherry Case:**
- AMI (Layer 1 + 2) → addressed commercial losses.
- SCADA/DMS (Layer 2 + 3) → addressed technical losses and reliability.
- DR (Layer 3) → addressed peak demand.

#### ➤ Solution to Q (b) — Question A

**Step 1 — Pre-implementation energy billed:**

$$
E_{\text{billed, pre}} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.24) = 760 \text{ MU/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Post-implementation energy billed:**

$$
E_{\text{billed, post}} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.13) = 870 \text{ MU/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Additional energy billed per year:**

$$
\Delta E = 870 - 760 = 110 \text{ MU/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Additional revenue per year (assuming full collection):**

$$
\Delta R = 110 \times 10^6 \times 6 = ₹ 66 \text{ Cr/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{80}{66} \approx 1.21 \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Puducherry AMI investment is recovered in just over **one year** — an extraordinarily strong financial case that justifies replication under India's Smart Meter National Programme (SMNP).

#### ➤ Solution to Q (a) — Question B

**ENEL Telegestore (Italy, 2001–2006) — Key Features:**
- World's first large-scale AMI deployment: **~ 30 million smart meters** across Italy.
- **PLC-based communication** (no RF, no cellular) over LV distribution lines using proprietary Telegestore protocol.
- Centralized **HES** managing all meters remotely.
- Outcomes: Reduced commercial losses from ~ 10% to **~ 3%**, reduced meter-reading workforce, enabled **time-of-use** pricing for ~ 27 million consumers.

**Comparison with Puducherry:**

| Parameter | ENEL Telegestore | Puducherry Pilot |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | 30 M meters | 87 k meters |
| Communication | PLC (LV) | RF Mesh + GPRS |
| Pre-loss | ~ 10% | ~ 24% |
| Post-loss | ~ 3% | ~ 13% |
| Driver | Operational efficiency + regulation | Loss reduction + service quality |
| Payback | ~ 4 years | ~ 1.2 years |

**Insight:** ENEL validates *technology standardization*; Puducherry validates *loss-reduction economics in a high-loss emerging market*.

#### ➤ Solution to Q (b) — Question B

**Step 1 — Compute total peak demand:**

$$
P_{\text{peak, total}} = 1000 \times 4 = 4000 \text{ kW} = 4 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute shifted demand (15% of peak):**

$$
P_{\text{shifted}} = 0.15 \times 4000 = 600 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate energy shifted per day** (assume peak window = 4 hours):

$$
E_{\text{shifted, daily}} = 600 \times 4 = 2400 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute daily consumer savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 2400 \times 3 = ₹ 7200 \text{/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Annualize (365 days):**

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 7200 \times 365 = ₹ 26.28 \text{ Lakh/yr}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Beyond direct savings, this shift also **deferrs** distribution capacity upgrades — a 600 kW peak cut at the feeder level can avoid ₹ 4–6 Cr in substation augmentation capex, dramatically improving the **benefit-cost ratio (BCR)** of the entire smart grid project.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT** describe a smart grid case study as a *list of features*. Examiners expect **problem → intervention → outcome** narrative.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse SAIDI and SAIFI. SAIDI is *duration*; SAIFI is *frequency*. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT** ignore units. Always write `min/customer/year` after SAIDI, not just `min`.
> 4. **Do NOT** skip numerical verification in 14-mark questions. Even a rough back-of-envelope calculation (e.g., payback) is a **must** to score above 10/14.
> 5. **Do NOT** copy generic smart grid definitions. Tie every statement to the *specific case* the question references.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> ✅ **Master Checklist for Case Study of Smart Grid (KTU Module 4)**

- **Definition:** A smart grid case study is a *quantitative, evidence-based* engineering audit of a real deployment — context, baseline, intervention, outcomes, lessons.
- **Pillars (5):** Context → Baseline KPIs → Technologies → Architecture → Outcomes + Lessons.
- **Three-Layer Model:** Physical (energy) → Communication (data) → Application (intelligence).
- **Core KPIs (memorize formulas):** SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, ASAI, ENS, AT&C Loss, Peak Reduction %, Payback Period.
- **Key Technologies:** AMI, SCADA, DMS, OMS, DERMS, DRMS, MDMS, ToU, Rooftop PV, BESS.
- **Reference Case Studies:**
   - **India:** Puducherry Pilot (R-APDRP) — AT&C 24% → 13%, SAIDI cut 62%, Payback 1.2 yrs.
   - **Global:** ENEL Telegestore (Italy) — 30 M PLC meters; loss 10% → 3%.
- **Critical Lessons:** (1) AMI alone is insufficient; (2) MDMS data quality is the real value; (3) Cybersecurity is non-negotiable; (4) Consumer engagement determines DR success; (5) Regulatory cost-pass-through is vital.
- **Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, follow the *5-pillar template* and *always* include one numerical computation (e.g., payback, energy saved, revenue gain).
- **Common Trap Questions:** "Compare two case studies," "Discuss lessons learned," "Compute payback for given data," "Explain why AMI alone fails to deliver full smart grid value."
- **Boilerplate Line to Score Bonus Marks:** *"Smart grid is not a product but a journey — the case study is the GPS that validates the route."*
